_README: v2 corpus manifest (graph-conjectures beyond OPG; plan meta/V2_FULL_CORPUS_PLAN.md). Built by meta/build_v2_manifest.py from the upstream graph-conjectures clone — deterministic; regenerate + diff to review changes. Status uses the exact-type-gate vocabulary (unclear->open, raw kept in review_status; 3 Aboulker errata applied). b1_candidate rows are OPG-alias CANDIDATES pending per-record adjudication. source_text currently holds the extractor's statement; REC/implementation replaces it with our normalized statement (licensing: LICENSE-DATA.md). Legs merge in from meta/v2_legs_state.json (the source of truth). Classifier fields (bucket/topic/formalizability/repo/phase/formal_name) are filled by the X0b classifier pass and wave planning, recorded via meta/apply_v2_classification.py.

rows.title: Open problem on lower bound for concentration interval
rows.paper_title: On the concentration of the chromatic number of random graphs
rows.statement_text: For $p$ fixed, nothing is known from below concerning concentration: it has not even been shown that the chromatic number cannot be concentrated in an interval of constant length.
rows.context_text: After establishing that $\chi(G)$ for $G \in \mathcal{G}(n,p)$ with $p$ fixed is concentrated in an interval of length $\omega(n)\sqrt{n}/\log n$, the author notes that all known results give only upper bounds on the concentration width. Whether $\chi(G)$ could in fact be concentrated on a bounded (or even constant) number of values remains completely open. Bollobás [4, 6] is cited for further discussion of this question.
rows.source_text: For $p$ fixed, nothing is known from below concerning concentration: it has not even been shown that the chromatic number cannot be concentrated in an interval of constant length.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:0806.0178 — Open problem on lower bound for concentration interval
rows.status_semantics: Heckel (2019, arXiv:1906.11808) proved that the chromatic number of G(n,1/2) is not concentrated on fewer than n^{1/4-epsilon} consecutive values, directly resolving the open problem by showing chi(G) cannot be confined to any constant-length interval. Heckel (2021, arXiv:2103.14014, JLMS) further improved the lower bound to at least n^{1/2-o(1)}, nearly matching the Alon-Scott upper bound of O(sqrt(n)/log n).
rows.class_notes: Underlying proposition: chi(G(n,p)) with p fixed is not concentrated on an interval of constant length (solved by Heckel-Riordan); record phrases it as a knowledge gap but the proposition is extractable.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.4
rows.paper_title: Three-coloring triangle-free graphs on surfaces V. Coloring planar graphs with distant anomalies
rows.statement_text: There exists an absolute constant $d \geq 2$ with the following property. Let $G$ be a plane triangle-free graph, let $S$ be a set of vertices of $G$ and let $\psi : S \to \{1, 2, 3\}$ be an arbitrary function. If the distance between every two vertices of $S$ is at least $d$, then $\psi$ extends to a 3-coloring of $G$.
rows.context_text: This is a natural extension of Theorem 1.3 to the case of precolored single vertices, where forbidding separating 4-cycles should not be necessary. The paper shows in Theorem 5.1 that Conjecture 1.4 is implied by the seemingly simpler Conjecture 1.5.
rows.source_text: There exists an absolute constant $d \geq 2$ with the following property. Let $G$ be a plane triangle-free graph, let $S$ be a set of vertices of $G$ and let $\psi : S \to \{1, 2, 3\}$ be an arbitrary function. If the distance between every two vertices of $S$ is at least $d$, then $\psi$ extends to a 3-coloring of $G$.
rows.class_notes: Precise precoloring-extension statement for plane triangle-free graphs with pairwise-distant precolored vertices.

rows.title: Open Problem: polynomial-time 4-colorability on fixed surfaces
rows.paper_title: Three-coloring triangle-free graphs on surfaces I. Extending a coloring to a disk with one triangle
rows.statement_text: Is there a polynomial-time algorithm for testing 4-colorability of graphs drawn in $\Sigma$ when $\Sigma$ is a fixed surface other than the sphere?
rows.context_text: The authors note that while 5-colorability on any fixed surface can be decided in polynomial (even linear) time using the finiteness of 6-critical graphs (Theorem 1), the analogous question for 4-colorability is open, and the techniques currently available do not give much hope for a positive resolution in the near future.
rows.source_text: Is there a polynomial-time algorithm for testing 4-colorability of graphs drawn in $\Sigma$ when $\Sigma$ is a fixed surface other than the sphere?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1010.2472 — Open Problem: polynomial-time 4-colorability on fixed surfaces
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no algorithmic question: polytime 4-colorability testing on a fixed surface.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.7
rows.paper_title: Three-coloring triangle-free graphs on surfaces II. 4-critical graphs in a disk
rows.statement_text: For every integer $k \geq 5$, there exists an integer $K$ with the following property. Let $G$ be a planar graph of girth at least five, let $C_1, C_2$ be two cycles in $G$ of lengths at most $k$, and for every $v \in V(G)$ let $L(v)$ be a set such that $|L(v)| = 1$ if $v \in V(C_1 \cup C_2)$ and $|L(v)| \geq 3$ otherwise. If there exists no proper coloring $\phi$ of $G$ such that $\phi(v) \in L(v)$ for every $v \in V(G)$, then $G$ has a subgraph $H$ on at most $K$ vertices such that $C_1$ and $C_2$ are subgraphs of $H$ and there exists no proper coloring $\psi$ of $H$ such that $\psi(v) \in L(v)$ for every $v \in V(H)$.
rows.context_text: The conjecture is a list-coloring generalisation of the paper's main result (Theorem 1.6). The authors note that an affirmative answer would imply an analogue of Theorem 1.4 for graphs of girth at least five in the list-coloring setting, and that Luke Postle (private communication) believes he has a proof, though it had not yet been written down at the time of submission.
rows.source_text: For every integer $k \geq 5$, there exists an integer $K$ with the following property. Let $G$ be a planar graph of girth at least five, let $C_1, C_2$ be two cycles in $G$ of lengths at most $k$, and for every $v \in V(G)$ let $L(v)$ be a set such that $|L(v)| = 1$ if $v \in V(C_1 \cup C_2)$ and $|L(v)| \geq 3$ otherwise. If there exists no proper coloring $\phi$ of $G$ such that $\phi(v) \in L(v)$ for every $v \in V(G)$, then $G$ has a subgraph $H$ on at most $K$ vertices such that $C_1$ and $C_2$ are subgraphs of $H$ and there exists no proper coloring $\psi$ of $H$ such that $\psi(v) \in L(v)$ for every $v \in V(H)$.
rows.class_notes: Self-contained bounded-critical-subgraph statement for list coloring of planar girth-5 graphs.

rows.title: 4-colorability on Fixed Surfaces (Open Step in Thomassen's Program)
rows.paper_title: Three-coloring triangle-free graphs on surfaces IV. Bounding face sizes of 4-critical graphs
rows.statement_text: Whether 4-colorability of graphs embedded in a fixed surface can be tested in polynomial time remains open; the authors remark that prospects for its resolution in the near future are not very bright.
rows.context_text: After announcing that Theorem 1.3 completes one of two missing steps in Thomassen's polynomial-time colorability program, the authors identify the remaining open step as the analogous question for 4-coloring graphs on a fixed surface.
rows.source_text: Whether 4-colorability of graphs embedded in a fixed surface can be tested in polynomial time remains open; the authors remark that prospects for its resolution in the near future are not very bright.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1404.6356 — 4-colorability on Fixed Surfaces (Open Step in Thomassen's Program)
rows.class_notes: Same open question as axv_1010_2472_00 (polytime 4-colorability on fixed surfaces).

rows.title: VC-Dimension Dichotomy for Identifying Codes (Approximation)
rows.paper_title: Identifying codes in hereditary classes of graphs and VC-dimension
rows.statement_text: For any hereditary class of graphs $\mathcal{C}$, either (1) the minimum identifying code size has a logarithmic lower bound and Min Id Code is log-APX-hard in $\mathcal{C}$, or (2) the minimum identifying code size has a polynomial lower bound and Min Id Code admits a constant factor approximation algorithm in $\mathcal{C}$.
rows.context_text: Surveying known results in Table 1, the authors observe that hereditary graph classes appear to split into two regimes according to their VC-dimension: infinite VC-dimension corresponds to a logarithmic lower bound and log-APX-hardness, while finite VC-dimension corresponds to a polynomial lower bound and (conjecturally) constant-factor approximability. The paper aims to shed light on the validity of this dichotomy for all hereditary classes.
rows.source_text: For any hereditary class of graphs $\mathcal{C}$, either (1) the minimum identifying code size has a logarithmic lower bound and Min Id Code is log-APX-hard in $\mathcal{C}$, or (2) the minimum identifying code size has a polynomial lower bound and Min Id Code admits a constant factor approximation algorithm in $\mathcal{C}$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1407.5833 — VC-Dimension Dichotomy for Identifying Codes (Approximation)
rows.class_notes: Dichotomy is stated as a single proposition but mixes asymptotic code-size bounds with log-APX-hardness/approximability, so a complexity foundation is required.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (diameter bound improvement)
rows.paper_title: Random graphs from a block-stable class
rows.statement_text: The extra factor $\sqrt{\log n}$ (compared with the random tree $T_n$) in the diameter bound $5\sqrt{n \log n}$ for the block tree $BT(R_n)$ could be replaced by any function tending to $\infty$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.2 establishes that, for $R_n$ sampled uniformly from the connected graphs in a block-stable class, the block forest $BF(R_n)$ has diameter at most $5\sqrt{n \log n}$ whp. Random labelled trees have diameter of order $\sqrt{n}$, so a gap of $\sqrt{\log n}$ remains between the two bounds. The authors conjecture this gap can be closed down to an arbitrarily slowly growing function.
rows.source_text: The extra factor $\sqrt{\log n}$ (compared with the random tree $T_n$) in the diameter bound $5\sqrt{n \log n}$ for the block tree $BT(R_n)$ could be replaced by any function tending to $\infty$.
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that the $\sqrt{\log n}$ factor in the diameter bound $5\sqrt{n\log n}$ for $BT(R_n)$ can be replaced by any function tending to infinity is resolved by Addario-Berry and Donderwinkel (arXiv:2201.11773). Their paper explicitly states it resolves 'a conjecture by McDiarmid and Scott on the block-diameter of a family of random graphs called block-graphs,' and the result is in fact stronger: they prove sub-Gaussian tail bounds for the block-diameter, going beyond the asymptotic tightness that was conjectured.
rows.class_notes: Relies on paper-local machinery (block-stable class, block tree BT(R_n)) not defined in the record.

rows.title: Open problem: ordered Ramsey number of matchings vs triangles
rows.paper_title: Ordered Ramsey numbers
rows.statement_text: What is the correct order of magnitude of $r_<(M, K_3)$ for an ordered matching $M$ on $n$ vertices? The paper can only prove the trivial upper bound $r_<(M, K_3) \leq r_<(K_n, K_3) = O\!\left(\frac{n^2}{\log n}\right)$, while the lower bound of Theorem 1.8 gives $r_<(M, K_3) \geq c\left(\frac{n}{\log n}\right)^{4/3}$ for some ordered matching $M$ on $n$ vertices.
rows.context_text: The classical off-diagonal Ramsey number $r(K_n, K_3)$ is known up to an asymptotic factor of 4 thanks to Kim's result and improvements via triangle-free processes. For ordered matchings the authors identify the analogous ordered problem but can only close a gap between an exponent of $4/3$ (lower) and $2$ (upper) in the logarithmic scale.
rows.source_text: What is the correct order of magnitude of $r_<(M, K_3)$ for an ordered matching $M$ on $n$ vertices? The paper can only prove the trivial upper bound $r_<(M, K_3) \leq r_<(K_n, K_3) = O\!\left(\frac{n^2}{\log n}\right)$, while the lower bound of Theorem 1.8 gives $r_<(M, K_3) \geq c\left(\frac{n}{\log n}\right)^{4/3}$ for some ordered matching $M$ on $n$ vertices.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1410.5292 — Open problem: ordered Ramsey number of matchings vs triangles
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture asks for the correct order of magnitude of $r_<(M, K_3)$ for an ordered matching $M$ on $n$ vertices, with the original gap between a lower bound of $\Omega((n/\log n)^{4/3})$ and the trivial upper bound $O(n^2/\log n)$. Balko and Poljak (2024) made significant partial progress: for almost all $n$-vertex ordered matchings with interval chromatic number 2, they establish $r_<(M^<, K^<_3) \in \Omega((n/\log n)^{4/3}) \cap O(n^{7/4})$, improving the upper-bound exponent from 2 to 7/4 for this class. The correct order of magnitude for general ordered matchings remains open.
rows.defer_reason: Asks for the correct order of magnitude of r_<(M,K_3); a determine-the-value question, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Open problem: true ordered Ramsey number of matchings
rows.paper_title: Ordered Ramsey numbers
rows.statement_text: What is the true ordered Ramsey number of an ordered matching $M$ on $n$ vertices? Theorem 1.1 gives the lower bound $r_<(M) \geq n^{c\log n/\log\log n}$ for some labeling, while Theorem 1.2 gives $r_<(M) \leq n^{\lceil \log n \rceil}$; the authors describe the upper bound as 'almost matching' but the gap remains open.
rows.context_text: For ordinary matchings the Ramsey number is clearly linear, but for ordered matchings the paper shows the ordered Ramsey number can be superpolynomial. The precise exponent (between $c\log n/\log\log n$ and $\log n$) is not determined.
rows.source_text: What is the true ordered Ramsey number of an ordered matching $M$ on $n$ vertices? Theorem 1.1 gives the lower bound $r_<(M) \geq n^{c\log n/\log\log n}$ for some labeling, while Theorem 1.2 gives $r_<(M) \leq n^{\lceil \log n \rceil}$; the authors describe the upper bound as 'almost matching' but the gap remains open.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1410.5292 — Open problem: true ordered Ramsey number of matchings
rows.defer_reason: Asks for the true ordered Ramsey number of matchings (close the exponent gap); determine-the-value, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Question 1.7
rows.paper_title: Colouring graphs with constraints on connectivity
rows.statement_text: For fixed $k \geq 4$, is there a polynomial-time algorithm that, given a $k$-connected graph $G$ with maximal local connectivity $k$, finds a $k$-colouring of $G$, or determines that none exists?
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.2 gives a polynomial-time algorithm for $k$-colouring when restricted to $\hat{\mathcal{C}}^k_1$ (k-connected graphs with maximal local edge-connectivity $k$). The complexity for the more general class $\hat{\mathcal{C}}^k_2$ (k-connected graphs with maximal local connectivity $k$) remains open for $k \geq 4$.
rows.source_text: For fixed $k \geq 4$, is there a polynomial-time algorithm that, given a $k$-connected graph $G$ with maximal local connectivity $k$, finds a $k$-colouring of $G$, or determines that none exists?
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no polytime-algorithm question for k-coloring k-connected graphs with maximal local connectivity k.

rows.title: Open Problem: Expansion vs. Corruption Detection
rows.paper_title: Distributed Corruption Detection in Networks
rows.statement_text: There is still a significant gap between the expansion properties that suffice for solving the detection problem (Theorem 1.4) and the conditions in Proposition 1.6 that are necessary for such a solution. It will be interesting to obtain tighter relations between expansion and corruption detection.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.4 shows that strong spectral expansion (Ramanujan-type $(n,d,\lambda)$-graphs with $\lambda \leq 2\sqrt{d-1}$) suffices for identifying most truthful and corrupt vertices. Proposition 1.6 shows that if removing at most $\epsilon n$ vertices splits the graph into components of size at most $\epsilon n$, then no corruption detection is possible. The gap between these two regimes is noted by the authors as an open research direction.
rows.source_text: There is still a significant gap between the expansion properties that suffice for solving the detection problem (Theorem 1.4) and the conditions in Proposition 1.6 that are necessary for such a solution. It will be interesting to obtain tighter relations between expansion and corruption detection.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1505.05637 — Open Problem: Expansion vs. Corruption Detection
rows.defer_reason: 'It will be interesting to obtain tighter relations' between expansion and corruption detection — a research direction, not a proposition.

rows.title: Open Problem: Reducing l to 1 in the Set Mapping Theorem
rows.paper_title: Short proofs of some extremal results II
rows.statement_text: Determine whether $l$ can be decreased to $1$ for all $k$; that is, whether $p(m,k,1)=\Theta(m^{1/k})$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 2.1 establishes $p(m,k,(k-1)!)=\Theta(m^{1/k})$. The case $l=(k-1)!$ is noted to be best possible when $k=2$ (independently solved earlier by Füredi). The authors remark it would be very interesting to determine whether $l$ can be decreased to $1$ for all $k$.
rows.source_text: Determine whether $l$ can be decreased to $1$ for all $k$; that is, whether $p(m,k,1)=\Theta(m^{1/k})$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1507.00547 — Open Problem: Reducing l to 1 in the Set Mapping Theorem
rows.defer_reason: Set-mapping/set-system extremal problem, not graph/digraph/hypergraph theory.
rows.class_notes: Erdos-Hajnal-style set-mapping free-set problem (set systems, not graph theory); p(m,k,l) also undefined in the record.

rows.title: Queue Number of Planar Graphs
rows.paper_title: Sparsity and dimension
rows.statement_text: A challenging open problem in the area of queue layouts is to decide whether planar graphs have constant queue number.
rows.context_text: Mentioned in the applications section where the authors note that graphs with bounded queue number form a class with bounded expansion (Dujmović and Wood), so Theorem 1 implies that posets whose cover graphs have bounded queue number have dimension bounded by a function of the queue number and the height. The queue-number problem for planar graphs is cited as independently interesting.
rows.source_text: A challenging open problem in the area of queue layouts is to decide whether planar graphs have constant queue number.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1507.01120 — Queue Number of Planar Graphs
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that planar graphs have bounded (constant) queue number was resolved affirmatively by Dujmović, Joret, Micek, Morin, Ueckerdt, and Wood in a 2019 arXiv preprint (published in J. ACM 2020), proving that every planar graph has queue number at most 49. The proof introduces layered partitions as a key structural tool and also shows that every proper minor-closed class of graphs has bounded queue number. Notably, Joret and Micek, two of the three authors of the source paper 1507.01120, are among the co-authors of the solving paper.
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no (solved by Dujmovic et al.: planar graphs have bounded queue number); queue number is a buildable linear-layout primitive.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.2
rows.paper_title: Edge-partitioning a graph into paths: beyond the Barát-Thomassen conjecture
rows.statement_text: There is a function $f$ such that, for any fixed tree $T$ with maximum degree $\Delta_T$, every $f(\Delta_T)$-edge-connected graph with its number of edges divisible by $|E(T)|$ and minimum degree at least $f(|E(T)|)$ can be $T$-decomposed.
rows.context_text: The authors propose this as a refinement of the Barát-Thomassen Conjecture (Conjecture 1.1), arguing that the large edge-connectivity required in Conjecture 1.1 can be replaced by a constant depending only on the maximum degree of $T$, provided the host graph has sufficiently large minimum degree. The present paper proves Conjecture 1.2 in the case $\Delta_T \leq 2$, i.e., when $T$ is a path.
rows.source_text: There is a function $f$ such that, for any fixed tree $T$ with maximum degree $\Delta_T$, every $f(\Delta_T)$-edge-connected graph with its number of edges divisible by $|E(T)|$ and minimum degree at least $f(|E(T)|)$ can be $T$-decomposed.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.2 from arXiv:1507.08208, which posits a function $f$ such that $f(\Delta_T)$-edge-connectivity and minimum degree $f(|E(T)|)$ suffice for $T$-decomposition for any tree $T$, is disproved by Klimošová and Thomassé (arXiv:1803.03704): for trees of maximum degree 3 (specifically complete binary trees of arbitrary depth), no such function $f$ exists. The path case ($\Delta_T \leq 2$, already proved in the source paper) was subsequently strengthened by arXiv:1907.11600, establishing that 3-edge-connectivity is optimal for paths.
rows.class_notes: Disproved refinement of Barat-Thomassen; needs an 'exists f' wrapper over edge-connectivity/min-degree thresholds.

rows.title: Open Question on 3-Choosability for Forbidden Cycles 4–7 and 4–6
rows.paper_title: Correspondence coloring and its application to list-coloring planar graphs without cycles of lengths 4 to 8
rows.statement_text: It is not known whether excluding cycles of lengths 4 to 7, or even 4 to 6, forces a planar graph to be 3-choosable. The latter question is wide open even for ordinary coloring.
rows.context_text: After establishing 3-choosability for planar graphs without cycles of lengths 4 to 8, the authors remark that the analogous questions for shorter forbidden cycle ranges remain open. They note that the case of forbidden lengths 4 to 7 might be approachable with the methods of the paper, whereas the case 4 to 6 is wide open even for ordinary 3-colorability.
rows.source_text: It is not known whether excluding cycles of lengths 4 to 7, or even 4 to 6, forces a planar graph to be 3-choosable. The latter question is wide open even for ordinary coloring.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1508.03437 — Open Question on 3-Choosability for Forbidden Cycles 4–7 and 4–6
rows.class_notes: Two clean sibling propositions in one record (3-choosability of planar graphs without cycles of lengths 4-7 resp. 4-6); split at formalization time.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture on Correspondence Chromatic Number of Planar Graphs Without Cycles 4–8
rows.paper_title: Correspondence coloring and its application to list-coloring planar graphs without cycles of lengths 4 to 8
rows.statement_text: Every planar graph $G$ without cycles of lengths 4 to 8 has correspondence chromatic number at most 3, i.e., $G$ is $C$-colorable for every 3-correspondence assignment $C$ (without the additional assumption of consistency on closed walks of length 3).
rows.context_text: Theorem 6 is proved only for 3-correspondence assignments that are consistent on every closed walk of length 3, a restriction needed to carry out the main reduction. The authors remark that they do not know any counterexample to the stronger claim that the full correspondence chromatic number is at most 3.
rows.source_text: Every planar graph $G$ without cycles of lengths 4 to 8 has correspondence chromatic number at most 3, i.e., $G$ is $C$-colorable for every 3-correspondence assignment $C$ (without the additional assumption of consistency on closed walks of length 3).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1508.03437 — Informal Conjecture on Correspondence Chromatic Number of Planar Graphs Without Cycles 4–8
rows.status_semantics: Jin, Kang, and Zhu (arXiv:2412.19059, December 2024) proved the stronger Theorem 1.4: every planar graph with no cycle of length 4, 6, or 8 is DP-3-colorable. Since any planar graph without cycles of lengths 4 to 8 is a special case (having no 4-, 6-, or 8-cycles), the conjecture of Dvořák and Postle follows as an immediate corollary. The paper explicitly identifies this as resolving the conjecture from arXiv:1508.03437, describing it as a ‘more natural and stronger claim’ that such graphs are DP-3-colorable without the consistency assumption.
rows.class_notes: Also needs the correspondence/DP-coloring primitive on top of planarity; solved.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (generalization of 1.3 to bounded clique number)
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IV. Consecutive holes
rows.statement_text: The corresponding result to 1.3 is true for graphs with bounded clique number rather than just triangle-free graphs: for all integers $\nu > 0$ and $k \geq 3$ there exists $n$ such that if $G$ has no clique of cardinality $k$ and has chromatic number at least $n$, then for some $t$, $G$ has a hole of length $t+i$ for $1 \leq i \leq \nu$.
rows.context_text: Immediately after the statement of the main result (1.3), which establishes $\nu$ consecutive hole lengths for triangle-free graphs, the authors conjecture the same conclusion holds under the weaker assumption that $G$ has bounded clique number. They note they have made no progress in proving this.
rows.source_text: The corresponding result to 1.3 is true for graphs with bounded clique number rather than just triangle-free graphs: for all integers $\nu > 0$ and $k \geq 3$ there exists $n$ such that if $G$ has no clique of cardinality $k$ and has chromatic number at least $n$, then for some $t$, $G$ has a hole of length $t+i$ for $1 \leq i \leq \nu$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1509.06563 — Informal Conjecture (generalization of 1.3 to bounded clique number)
rows.class_notes: Precise: bounded clique number + large chromatic number forces nu consecutive hole lengths.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.5
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IV. Consecutive holes
rows.statement_text: Let $F$ be an infinite set of positive integers with bounded gaps. Then $F$ is constricting.
rows.context_text: A set $F$ of integers is $k$-constricting if there exists $n$ such that every graph with chromatic number at least $n$ contains either a clique with $k$ vertices or a hole with length in $F$; $F$ is constricting if it is $k$-constricting for every $k$. The authors prove (1.4) that every infinite set with bounded gaps is 3-constricting, and conjecture 1.5 as the natural generalization to all $k$.
rows.source_text: Let $F$ be an infinite set of positive integers with bounded gaps. Then $F$ is constricting.
rows.class_notes: 'Constricting' is fully defined in context_text; bounded-gaps sets of integers are standard vocabulary.

rows.title: Problem 1.6
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IV. Consecutive holes
rows.statement_text: Is there an infinite set $F$ of positive integers such that $F$ is constricting and has density $0$?
rows.context_text: The authors discuss which infinite sets are constricting, noting that not every infinite set is constricting (not even sets with upper density one), while sets with bounded gaps are 3-constricting. Lower density seems closer to the truth, and the authors conjecture the answer to Problem 1.6 is positive.
rows.source_text: Is there an infinite set $F$ of positive integers such that $F$ is constricting and has density $0$?
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no; density 0 of an integer set needs a limit wrapper on top of the constricting definition given in context.

rows.title: Informal Question (on 2.1 for ℓ = 4)
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IV. Consecutive holes
rows.statement_text: Statement 2.1 might be true when $\ell = 4$: let $\varphi : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ be a non-decreasing function; does there exist $n$ such that every $(2,\varphi)$-controlled triangle-free graph with chromatic number more than $n$ has a $4$-hole?
rows.context_text: Statement 2.1 is proved in the paper for all $\ell \geq 5$ (with $\ell = 7$ handled by a prior paper). The authors remark that 2.1 might also hold for $\ell = 4$ but that they have not been able to decide this.
rows.source_text: Statement 2.1 might be true when $\ell = 4$: let $\varphi : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ be a non-decreasing function; does there exist $n$ such that every $(2,\varphi)$-controlled triangle-free graph with chromatic number more than $n$ has a $4$-hole?
rows.class_notes: Depends on the paper-local '(2,phi)-controlled' definition and internal Statement 2.1, neither defined in the record.

rows.title: Open case: WSK algorithm on the triangular lattice for q=5
rows.paper_title: On a conjecture of Mohar concerning Kempe equivalence of regular graphs
rows.statement_text: Determine whether the Wang-Swendsen-Koteck\'{y} algorithm for $q = 5$ colourings of the triangular lattice (with periodic boundary conditions) is valid (i.e., whether the set of $5$-colourings of the triangular lattice is a Kempe class).
rows.context_text: Theorem 2.2 establishes that the WSK algorithm on the triangular lattice is valid for $q \geq 6$ (via Theorem 1.1 and a degeneracy lemma) and invalid for $q \leq 4$ (Mohar and Salas). The authors explicitly note that $q = 5$ is the single remaining open case.
rows.source_text: Determine whether the Wang-Swendsen-Koteck\'{y} algorithm for $q = 5$ colourings of the triangular lattice (with periodic boundary conditions) is valid (i.e., whether the set of $5$-colourings of the triangular lattice is a Kempe class).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1510.06964 — Open case: WSK algorithm on the triangular lattice for q=5
rows.status_semantics: The question of whether all 5-colourings of the triangular lattice (with periodic boundary conditions) form a single Kempe class — equivalently whether the WSK algorithm is ergodic for q=5 — was explicitly left open in arXiv:1510.06964. A 2022 preprint by Salas and Sokal (arXiv:2206.13126) proves WSK ergodicity for q≥5 on Eulerian triangulations of the torus satisfying certain structural conditions; since the triangular lattice is a 6-regular Eulerian triangulation of the torus, this is a significant adjacent result, but the abstract does not explicitly confirm that the standard triangular lattice satisfies the paper's conditions for q=5. No paper was found that unambiguously and completely settles the conjecture.
rows.class_notes: Needs Kempe-class and toroidal-triangular-lattice primitives, both buildable finitely (family of quotient lattice graphs).

rows.title: Problem 1
rows.paper_title: Minimal normal graph covers
rows.statement_text: How large can a $(c, s)$-normal graph be?
rows.context_text: The authors define $N(c,s)$ as the maximum number of vertices of a $(c,s)$-normal graph, where a graph is $(c,s)$-normal if it admits a normal cover with cliques of sizes at most $c$ and stable sets of sizes at most $s$. They prove $N(c,s) \leq 2^{c+s}$ and a lower bound of roughly $\sqrt{5}^{\,c}$ for $N(c,c)$, but describe the results as 'far from conclusive,' with the exact asymptotics analogous to open questions in Ramsey theory.
rows.source_text: How large can a $(c, s)$-normal graph be?
rows.defer_reason: 'How large can a (c,s)-normal graph be?' — determine-the-value of N(c,s), not a single proposition.

rows.title: Open Question (normality of random graphs)
rows.paper_title: Minimal normal graph covers
rows.statement_text: Are random graphs $G(n,p)$ normal with high probability?
rows.context_text: The authors note that partial motivation for studying normal graphs with small clique and independence numbers came from this question. They explicitly state that as of the time of writing this question 'is still unresolved.'
rows.source_text: Are random graphs $G(n,p)$ normal with high probability?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1601.01129 — Open Question (normality of random graphs)
rows.class_notes: Underspecified in the record: 'normal graph' is not defined here and the range of p is not stated.

rows.title: Open Problem (linear-time 3-coloring output)
rows.paper_title: Three-coloring triangle-free graphs on surfaces VII. A linear-time algorithm
rows.statement_text: Does there exist a linear-time algorithm to output a 3-coloring of a triangle-free graph embedded in a fixed surface (with a bounded number of precolored boundary vertices), using ideas similar to those of Theorem 1.3?
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.4 gives a quadratic-time algorithm to output a 3-coloring in the affirmative case. The authors believe a linear-time algorithm for the output step exists via ideas analogous to those of the linear-time decision algorithm (Theorem 1.3), but note significant technical challenges in realising it.
rows.source_text: Does there exist a linear-time algorithm to output a 3-coloring of a triangle-free graph embedded in a fixed surface (with a bounded number of precolored boundary vertices), using ideas similar to those of Theorem 1.3?
rows.class_notes: Core question is a clean linear-time-algorithm existence question; the 'ideas similar to Theorem 1.3' clause is flavor, not part of the proposition.

rows.title: Optimality of factor 2 in Lemma 4
rows.paper_title: Pathwidth and nonrepetitive list coloring
rows.statement_text: We do not know whether the factor 2 is unavoidable in the bound of Lemma 4, which states that every tree of pathwidth $k$ has a path-partition of height at most $2k$.
rows.context_text: Lemma 4 is a key structural tool used in the proof of Theorem 2. The authors note that they made no effort to optimise the bound and leave open whether the factor 2 can be reduced.
rows.source_text: We do not know whether the factor 2 is unavoidable in the bound of Lemma 4, which states that every tree of pathwidth $k$ has a path-partition of height at most $2k$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1601.01886 — Optimality of factor 2 in Lemma 4
rows.defer_reason: Asks whether the factor 2 in a lemma can be improved, with no target constant — improve-the-bound meta-question.
rows.class_notes: 'Path-partition height' is also paper-local and undefined in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 5
rows.paper_title: On the difference between the Szeged and Wiener index
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a 2-connected graph of order $n \geq 10$ not isomorphic to $K_n$, $K^2_n$, or $K^{n-2}_n$. Then $\eta(G) \geq 2n$.
rows.context_text: The authors note that while $\eta(K_n)=0$ and $\eta(K^2_n)=\eta(K^{n-2}_n)=2n-6$, computer search found only finitely many 2-connected graphs with $\eta(G)<2n$ and none on 10 or more vertices, motivating this stronger bound beyond the three exceptional families.
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a 2-connected graph of order $n \geq 10$ not isomorphic to $K_n$, $K^2_n$, or $K^{n-2}_n$. Then $\eta(G) \geq 2n$.
rows.class_notes: eta (Szeged-Wiener difference, inferable only from the paper title) and the exceptional families K^2_n, K^{n-2}_n are paper-local notation undefined in the record.

rows.title: Characterization of Social Graphs
rows.paper_title: On the number of cliques in graphs with a forbidden minor
rows.statement_text: Characterize social graphs, i.e., graphs $G$ such that contracting any edge of $G$ does not increase the number of cliques of $G$.
rows.context_text: Social graphs are introduced as a key structural tool in the proof of Theorem 1.2: the authors show that any very dense social graph is, apart from a small number of vertices, the complement of a (not necessarily perfect) matching. The paper explicitly notes that a full characterization of social graphs appears to be a challenging problem.
rows.source_text: Characterize social graphs, i.e., graphs $G$ such that contracting any edge of $G$ does not increase the number of cliques of $G$.
rows.defer_reason: 'Characterize social graphs' — open-ended characterization request, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (vertex-disjoint paths, stability number two)
rows.paper_title: Disjoint paths in unions of tournaments
rows.statement_text: We suspect the $k$ vertex-disjoint paths problem might be NP-complete for digraphs with stability number two.
rows.context_text: The authors discuss whether their polynomial-time result for semicomplete digraphs (Theorem 1.2) can be extended to digraphs with bounded stability number. The edge-disjoint version is known to be polynomial-time solvable in this setting, but the vertex-disjoint version remains out of reach. The authors remark that they suspect NP-completeness already at stability number two.
rows.source_text: We suspect the $k$ vertex-disjoint paths problem might be NP-complete for digraphs with stability number two.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1604.02317 — Informal Conjecture (vertex-disjoint paths, stability number two)
rows.class_notes: Precise NP-completeness conjecture for k vertex-disjoint paths in digraphs with stability number two.

rows.title: Conjecture 1
rows.paper_title: Smaller Extended Formulations for the Spanning Tree Polytope of Bounded-genus Graphs
rows.statement_text: If $G = (V, E)$ is a connected graph embedded in a fixed surface, then $\mathrm{xc}(P_{\mathrm{sp.trees}}(G)) = O(|V|)$.
rows.context_text: The authors prove an $O(g^{1/2}|V|^{3/2} + g^{3/2}|V|^{1/2})$ upper bound for graphs embedded in a surface of genus $g$ (Theorem 3). They conjecture that this can be improved to match the linear bound known for planar graphs (Theorem 2, due to Williams).
rows.source_text: If $G = (V, E)$ is a connected graph embedded in a fixed surface, then $\mathrm{xc}(P_{\mathrm{sp.trees}}(G)) = O(|V|)$.
rows.class_notes: Precise O(|V|) claim but extension complexity of the spanning tree polytope needs real polyhedral machinery beyond graph vocabulary.

rows.title: Conjecture 2
rows.paper_title: Smaller Extended Formulations for the Spanning Tree Polytope of Bounded-genus Graphs
rows.statement_text: If $\mathcal{C}$ is a proper minor-closed family of graphs and $G = (V, E)$ is a connected graph in $\mathcal{C}$, then $\mathrm{xc}(P_{\mathrm{sp.trees}}(G)) = O(|V|)$.
rows.context_text: Following Conjecture 1, the authors suggest the linear bound may hold even more generally for all proper minor-closed families. They note the conjecture is known to hold when graphs in $\mathcal{C}$ have bounded treewidth, and they prove it for $k$-apex graphs (Theorem 4) as additional supporting evidence.
rows.source_text: If $\mathcal{C}$ is a proper minor-closed family of graphs and $G = (V, E)$ is a connected graph in $\mathcal{C}$, then $\mathrm{xc}(P_{\mathrm{sp.trees}}(G)) = O(|V|)$.
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that $\mathrm{xc}(P_{\mathrm{sp.trees}}(G)) = O(|V|)$ for every proper minor-closed family $\mathcal{C}$ and connected $G \in \mathcal{C}$ remains open. The main post-2017 progress is due to Aprile, Fiorini, Huynh, Joret, and Wood (2021), who proved an $O(n^{3/2})$ upper bound for any proper minor-closed class (and more generally $O(n^{1+\beta})$ for classes with $O(n^\beta)$-size balanced separators), improving the previously known $O(n^2)$ bounds. The linear $O(n)$ bound has been established for planar graphs and for graphs of bounded treewidth, but the full conjecture for all proper minor-closed families is unresolved.
rows.class_notes: Minor-closed generalization of Conjecture 1 (axv_1604_07976_00); same polytope/reals machinery.

rows.title: Question 1
rows.paper_title: Token Sliding on Chordal Graphs
rows.statement_text: For any integers $k$, $D$, for any chordal graph $G$ of clique-tree degree at most $D$, can the connectivity of $TS_k(G)$ be decided in polynomial time?
rows.context_text: Motivated by the contrast between the polynomial time result for interval graphs (clique-tree degree 1) and the co-NP-hardness for split graphs, the authors introduce the clique-tree degree of a chordal graph $G$ as the smallest maximum degree of a clique-tree of $G$, and ask whether bounding this parameter restores polynomial tractability.
rows.source_text: For any integers $k$, $D$, for any chordal graph $G$ of clique-tree degree at most $D$, can the connectivity of $TS_k(G)$ be decided in polynomial time?
rows.status_semantics: Bonamy and Bousquet asked whether bounding the clique-tree degree D of a chordal graph restores polynomial tractability for deciding connectivity of TS_k(G). Adak, Nanoti, and Tale (arXiv:2502.12749, 2025) directly and negatively answer this: TS-Connectivity is co-NP-hard on chordal graphs of maximum clique-tree degree 4, and TS-Reachability is NP-hard already at degree 3. Together these results show that bounded clique-tree degree does not make the problem polynomial-time tractable.
rows.class_notes: Clean polytime yes/no (disproved); TS_k is the standard token-sliding reconfiguration graph, clique-tree degree defined in context.

rows.title: Conjecture 2
rows.paper_title: $χ$-bounded families of oriented graphs
rows.statement_text: $\mathrm{Forb}(H)$ is $\chi$-bounded if and only if $H$ is a forest.
rows.context_text: The paper notes that an easy argument shows this conjecture is equivalent to the Gyárfás–Sumner Conjecture (Conjecture 1). It generalises the 'only if' direction (which follows from Erdős's girth theorem) to a complete characterisation.
rows.source_text: $\mathrm{Forb}(H)$ is $\chi$-bounded if and only if $H$ is a forest.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 2 from arXiv:1605.07411 — that $\mathrm{Forb}(H)$ is $\chi$-bounded if and only if $H$ is a forest in the directed/oriented setting — is explicitly stated in the source paper to be equivalent to the Gyárfás–Sumner conjecture and remains open in full generality. Substantial partial progress has been made: the conjecture is proved for every orientation of $P_4$ (Cook et al., 2022/2023), for $(\overrightarrow{P}_6, \text{triangle})$-free digraphs (Aboulker et al., 2022), and for locally out-transitive oriented graphs, i.e., the $H = S_2^+$ case (Aboulker et al., 2021). No counterexample has been found and the full conjecture remains open.
rows.class_notes: Full iff form of Gyarfas-Sumner (context says equivalent to it); Forb(H) presumably induced-H-free undirected graphs — worth confirming against the oriented-graphs paper.

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: $χ$-bounded families of oriented graphs
rows.statement_text: For any oriented star $S$, $\mathrm{Forb}(S)$ is $\chi$-bounded.
rows.context_text: This is the paper's central new conjecture, extending the Gyárfás–Sumner framework to oriented graphs. The authors note it suffices to prove it for $S_{k,k}$ for all $k$, and prove several special cases: $k=0$, $k=\ell=1$, and the $TT_3$-free case (Theorem 15).
rows.source_text: For any oriented star $S$, $\mathrm{Forb}(S)$ is $\chi$-bounded.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 4 from arXiv:1605.07411 — that Forb(S) is χ-bounded for every oriented star S — remains open in full generality. The source paper proved the cases k=0, k=ℓ=1, and the TT_3-free setting; subsequent work (arXiv:2103.07886) proved the S_2^+ special case (locally out-transitive oriented graphs have dichromatic number at most 2). The related Aboulker–Charbit–Naserasr conjecture for oriented trees has seen additional progress (P4 orientations in arXiv:2209.06171, P6-free triangle-free in arXiv:2212.02272), but the full oriented-star conjecture appears open for general k ≥ 2.
rows.class_notes: Oriented Gyárfás–Sumner analogue; Forb(S) = oriented graphs with no induced copy of S, standard in this framework.

rows.title: Conjecture 5
rows.paper_title: $χ$-bounded families of oriented graphs
rows.statement_text: Let $\mathcal{P}$ be a non-empty subset of $\mathrm{Or}(P_4)$. If $\mathcal{P} \neq \{P^+(3)\}$ and $\mathcal{P} \neq \{P^+(1,1,1)\}$, then $\mathrm{Forb}(\mathcal{P})$ is $\chi$-bounded.
rows.context_text: Among the four non-isomorphic orientations of $P_4$, the paper shows $\mathrm{Forb}(P^+(3))$ and $\mathrm{Forb}(P^+(1,1,1))$ are not $\chi$-bounded. The conjecture asserts these are the only obstructions; the paper proves it when $P^+(3) \in \mathcal{P}$ (Corollary 35).
rows.source_text: Let $\mathcal{P}$ be a non-empty subset of $\mathrm{Or}(P_4)$. If $\mathcal{P} \neq \{P^+(3)\}$ and $\mathcal{P} \neq \{P^+(1,1,1)\}$, then $\mathrm{Forb}(\mathcal{P})$ is $\chi$-bounded.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 5 asserts that $\mathrm{Forb}(\mathcal{P})$ is $\chi$-bounded for every non-empty $\mathcal{P} \subseteq \mathrm{Or}(P_4)$ with $\mathcal{P} \neq \{P^+(3)\}$ and $\mathcal{P} \neq \{P^+(1,1,1)\}$. The source paper itself proved the case $P^+(3) \in \mathcal{P}$ (Corollary 35). Cook, Masařík, Pilipczuk, Reinald, and Souza (EJC 2023) subsequently proved the directed analogue of the Gyárfás–Sumner conjecture for all orientations of $P_4$, establishing $\chi$-boundedness of $\mathrm{Forb}(F)$ for each non-exceptional orientation $F$ of $P_4$; together with Corollary 35 this resolves all remaining cases of Conjecture 5.
rows.class_notes: Relies on paper-local notation P^+(3), P^+(1,1,1) for specific orientations of P4, not defined in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 2.2
rows.paper_title: A new class of graphs that satisfies the Chen-Chvátal Conjecture
rows.statement_text: There is a finite set of graphs $F_0$ such that every connected graph $G \notin F_0$ either has a pendant edge or satisfies $\ell(G) + \mathrm{br}(G) \geq |G|$.
rows.context_text: The authors observe that replacing a bridge by a path of arbitrary length in a counter-example to $\ell(G)+\mathrm{br}(G)\geq|G|$ yields infinitely many counter-examples, so finitely many minimal ones cannot account for all exceptions; they propose that graphs with a pendant edge should be excluded and that all remaining counter-examples come from a finite family. The three known minimal counter-examples with a bridge are depicted in Figure 2.
rows.source_text: There is a finite set of graphs $F_0$ such that every connected graph $G \notin F_0$ either has a pendant edge or satisfies $\ell(G) + \mathrm{br}(G) \geq |G|$.
rows.class_notes: l(G) (number of metric lines) and br(G) are not defined in the record; Chen–Chvátal line vocabulary needed.

rows.title: Open question on counter-examples to ℓ(G)+br(G)≥|G|
rows.paper_title: A new class of graphs that satisfies the Chen-Chvátal Conjecture
rows.statement_text: It remains unknown whether all counter-examples to $\ell(G) + \mathrm{br}(G) \geq |G|$ can be obtained from a finite set of graphs by replacing a bridge by a path of arbitrary length.
rows.context_text: The authors note (on the basis of an observation by Yori Zwols) that any counter-example containing a bridge generates infinitely many counter-examples by path-elongation. They ask whether this operation accounts for all counter-examples.
rows.source_text: It remains unknown whether all counter-examples to $\ell(G) + \mathrm{br}(G) \geq |G|$ can be obtained from a finite set of graphs by replacing a bridge by a path of arbitrary length.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1606.06011 — Open question on counter-examples to ℓ(G)+br(G)≥|G|
rows.class_notes: Same undefined l(G)/br(G) notation as axv_1606_06011_00; near-duplicate finite-generation question of that row.

rows.title: Fractional Chromatic Number Bound for Planar Triangle-Free Graphs
rows.paper_title: Triangle-free planar graphs with the smallest independence number
rows.statement_text: The fractional chromatic number of $n$-vertex planar triangle-free graphs might be bounded by $3 - 3/(n+1)$.
rows.context_text: Since $\alpha(G) \geq |V(G)|/\chi_f(G)$, the bound $\alpha(G) \geq (n+1)/3$ from Steinberg–Tovey and Theorem 2 indicate this potential upper bound on $\chi_f$. Dvořák et al. [6] confirmed it for planar triangle-free graphs of maximum degree at most 4; in general only the weaker bound $3 - 3/(3n+1)$ is known.
rows.source_text: The fractional chromatic number of $n$-vertex planar triangle-free graphs might be bounded by $3 - 3/(n+1)$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1606.06265 — Fractional Chromatic Number Bound for Planar Triangle-Free Graphs
rows.class_notes: Single clean inequality chi_f(G) <= 3 - 3/(n+1) for planar triangle-free G; fractional chi is rational-valued, fine.

rows.title: Conjecture on maximum cliques with no $K_t$-immersion
rows.paper_title: On the number of cliques in graphs with a forbidden subdivision or immersion
rows.statement_text: The maximum number of cliques in a graph on $n \geq t-2$ vertices with no $K_t$-immersion is $2^{t-2}(n-t+3)$.
rows.context_text: The authors construct a graph on $n$ vertices with no weak $K_t$-immersion achieving $2^{t-2}(n-t+3)$ cliques: begin with a clique $K$ on $t-2$ vertices and attach $n-t+2$ additional vertices each with neighborhood $K$. Since every vertex outside $K$ has degree $t-2 < t-1$, no $K_t$-immersion exists. The authors conjecture this construction is optimal.
rows.source_text: The maximum number of cliques in a graph on $n \geq t-2$ vertices with no $K_t$-immersion is $2^{t-2}(n-t+3)$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1606.06810 — Conjecture on maximum cliques with no $K_t$-immersion
rows.class_notes: Both said B2/needs-primitive; repo resolved to minor-theory since the blocking primitive is K_t-immersion (edge-disjoint paths containment); minor immersion-vs-weak-immersion ambiguity between statement and construction.

rows.title: Conjecture on optimal exponential constant for $K_t$-subdivision clique count
rows.paper_title: On the number of cliques in graphs with a forbidden subdivision or immersion
rows.statement_text: The maximum number of cliques in an $n$-vertex graph with no $K_t$-subdivision is $3^{2t/3+o(t)}n$, i.e., the optimal exponential constant in the exponent is $\frac{2}{3}\log_2 3 \approx 1.06$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.2 establishes an upper bound of $2^{1.817t+o(t)}n$, improving on Lee and Oum. Wood's lower bound construction (which also forbids clique subdivisions) shows the constant cannot be reduced below $\frac{2}{3}\log_2 3 \approx 1.06$. The authors proved $c(t) = 3^{2t/3+o(t)}$ for $K_t$-minors in earlier work [10] and conjecture the same bound holds for $K_t$-subdivisions.
rows.source_text: The maximum number of cliques in an $n$-vertex graph with no $K_t$-subdivision is $3^{2t/3+o(t)}n$, i.e., the optimal exponential constant in the exponent is $\frac{2}{3}\log_2 3 \approx 1.06$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1606.06810 — Conjecture on optimal exponential constant for $K_t$-subdivision clique count
rows.class_notes: Asymptotic 3^{2t/3+o(t)}n form requires o(t) wrapper.

rows.title: Conjecture 2
rows.paper_title: Majority Colourings of Digraphs
rows.statement_text: Every digraph has a majority 3-colouring.
rows.context_text: This conjecture naturally arises from Theorem 1, which shows every digraph has a majority 4-colouring. It would be best possible: odd directed cycles require 3 colours (since each vertex has outdegree 1, a majority colouring is a proper colouring), and circulant digraphs with large odd outdegree also witness this lower bound.
rows.source_text: Every digraph has a majority 3-colouring.
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that every digraph has a majority 3-colouring remains open in full generality as of 2026. Anastos, Lamaison, Steiner, and Szabó (arXiv:1911.01954, EJC 2021) proved it for digraphs with chromatic number at most 6 or dichromatic number at most 3 (covering all planar digraphs), and proved the stronger majority 3-choosability for digraphs with maximum out-degree at most 4 or maximum degree at most 7. Their probabilistic argument achieves a fractional bound of K ≤ 3.9602, approaching but not reaching the conjectured value of 3. A 2025 paper in Acta Mathematicae Applicatae Sinica reports further new partial results, and the conjecture is still described as 'far from being resolved' in recent literature.
rows.class_notes: Majority colouring is standard vocabulary (introduced by this well-known paper); statement is complete despite recovery flag.

rows.title: Conjecture 9
rows.paper_title: Majority Colourings of Digraphs
rows.statement_text: For $k \geq 2$, every digraph has a vertex $(k+1)$-colouring such that for each vertex $v$, at most $\frac{1}{k}\deg^+(v)$ out-neighbours of $v$ receive the same colour as $v$.
rows.context_text: This is the natural generalisation of Conjecture 2 to arbitrary $k$. The proof of Theorem 1 generalises to give an upper bound of $k^2$ colours; whether $O(k)$ colours suffice is explicitly stated as open. The conjecture is best possible, as shown by the $k$-th power of a directed $n$-cycle with $n \not\equiv 0 \pmod{k+1}$.
rows.source_text: For $k \geq 2$, every digraph has a vertex $(k+1)$-colouring such that for each vertex $v$, at most $\frac{1}{k}\deg^+(v)$ out-neighbours of $v$ receive the same colour as $v$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 9 from arXiv:1608.03040 asserts that for k≥2, every digraph can be (k+1)-coloured so that each vertex shares its colour with at most 1/k of its out-neighbours (i.e. the generalised majority chromatic number m(k) equals k+1). Girão, Kittipassorn, and Popielarz (arXiv:1701.03780, CPC 2017) proved that m(k) ∈ {2k−1, 2k}, establishing in particular a lower bound m(k) ≥ 2k−1; for k≥3 this exceeds k+1, disproving the conjecture for those values of k. The special case k=2 (the majority 3-colouring conjecture) remains open; Anastos, Lamaison, Steiner, and Szabó (arXiv:1911.01954, 2019) proved it for digraphs with chromatic number at most 6 or dichromatic number at most 3, and established NP-completeness of majority 2-colourability.
rows.class_notes: Fully self-defined 1/k-majority (k+1)-colouring statement; disproved, feeds right-polarity.

rows.title: Open Problem 1
rows.paper_title: Majority Colourings of Digraphs
rows.statement_text: Is there a constant $\beta < 1$ for which every digraph has a 3-colouring such that for every vertex $v$, at most $\beta \deg^+(v)$ out-neighbours receive the same colour as $v$?
rows.context_text: Theorems 3 and 4 establish majority 3-colourings (i.e., $\beta = \frac{1}{2}$) under extra degree assumptions (logarithmic minimum outdegree, or bounded minimum/maximum degree ratio). Whether any $\beta < 1$ holds universally for 3-colourings without degree conditions is open.
rows.source_text: Is there a constant $\beta < 1$ for which every digraph has a 3-colouring such that for every vertex $v$, at most $\beta \deg^+(v)$ out-neighbours receive the same colour as $v$?
rows.status_semantics: Open Problem 1 asks whether a universal constant β < 1 exists such that every digraph admits a 3-colouring where at most β·deg⁺(v) out-neighbours of each vertex v share its colour. Knox and Šámal (EJC 2018) resolve this affirmatively with β = 2/3: they prove that for any list assignment of size k, every digraph has a 2/k-majority list-colouring; specialising to k = 3 with all lists identical gives an ordinary 3-colouring with β = 2/3 < 1. The stronger majority 3-colouring conjecture (β = 1/2) remains open; Anastos, Lamaison, Steiner, and Szabó (2019) established it for digraphs with chromatic number ≤ 6 or dichromatic number ≤ 3, and for digraphs with maximum out-degree ≤ 4.
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no with existential rational constant beta<1; solved.

rows.title: Open Problem 2
rows.paper_title: Majority Colourings of Digraphs
rows.statement_text: Does every tournament have a majority 3-colouring?
rows.context_text: Ilya Bogdanov proved (in response to the original MathOverflow question [7]) that tournaments admit a majority colouring with a bounded number of colours, but whether 3 colours suffice for all tournaments is open.
rows.source_text: Does every tournament have a majority 3-colouring?
rows.class_notes: Tournament special case of Conjecture 2 (axv_1608_03040_00).

rows.title: Open Problem 3
rows.paper_title: Majority Colourings of Digraphs
rows.statement_text: Does every Eulerian digraph have a majority 3-colouring?
rows.context_text: For Eulerian digraphs $G$ where every vertex $v$ has equal in- and out-degree $\deg(v)$, Lovász's result for undirected graphs gives a majority 4-colouring, and an analogous argument yields a 3-colouring in which each vertex $v$ has at most $\frac{2}{3}\deg(v)$ same-colour in-or-out-neighbours, proving a special case of Open Problem 1. Whether a full majority 3-colouring (with $\frac{1}{2}\deg^+(v)$) exists for all Eulerian digraphs is open.
rows.source_text: Does every Eulerian digraph have a majority 3-colouring?
rows.class_notes: Eulerian-digraph special case of Conjecture 2.

rows.title: Open Problem 5
rows.paper_title: Majority Colourings of Digraphs
rows.statement_text: Is there a characterisation of digraphs that have a majority 2-colouring, or a polynomial-time algorithm to recognise such digraphs?
rows.context_text: Majority 2-colourability is not closed under taking induced subdigraphs (an example with four vertices is given in the introduction), making a simple hereditary characterisation impossible; no structural characterisation or efficient recognition algorithm is known.
rows.source_text: Is there a characterisation of digraphs that have a majority 2-colouring, or a polynomial-time algorithm to recognise such digraphs?
rows.status_semantics: The algorithmic half of Open Problem 5 is settled: Anastos, Lamaison, Steiner, and Szabó (arXiv:1911.01954) proved that deciding whether a given digraph is majority 2-colorable is NP-complete (Theorem 9), ruling out a polynomial-time recognition algorithm unless P=NP. The structural-characterisation half of the question remains open, as no combinatorial characterisation of majority 2-colorable digraphs is known.
rows.defer_reason: Asks for a characterisation or a polynomial-time recognition algorithm, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Algorithm half would additionally need a computation model.

rows.title: Open Problem 7
rows.paper_title: Majority Colourings of Digraphs
rows.statement_text: A digraph $G$ is majority $c$-choosable if for every function $L : V(G) \to \mathcal{Z}$ with $|L(v)| \geq c$ for each vertex $v \in V(G)$, there is a majority colouring of $G$ with each vertex $v$ coloured from $L(v)$. Is every digraph majority $c$-choosable for some constant $c$?
rows.context_text: Acyclic digraphs are shown to be majority 2-choosable (from the proof of Theorem 1), and Theorems 3 and 4 extend to the choosability setting; whether a universal constant $c$ covers all digraphs is open.
rows.source_text: A digraph $G$ is majority $c$-choosable if for every function $L : V(G) \to \mathcal{Z}$ with $|L(v)| \geq c$ for each vertex $v \in V(G)$, there is a majority colouring of $G$ with each vertex $v$ coloured from $L(v)$. Is every digraph majority $c$-choosable for some constant $c$?
rows.status_semantics: Open Problem 7 (whether every digraph is majority $c$-choosable for some constant $c$) was answered affirmatively by Anholcer, Bosek, and Grytczuk (EJC 2017), who proved that every digraph is majority 4-choosable, establishing $c=4$ as a valid universal constant. Subsequently, Anastos, Lamaison, Steiner, and Szabó (2019) proved majority 3-choosability for sparse classes (list chromatic number at most 6, dichromatic number at most 3, or max out-degree at most 4) and showed that deciding majority 2-colorability is NP-complete; whether $c=3$ suffices for all digraphs remains open.
rows.class_notes: Choosability version fully defined in the statement; existential constant c, solved.

rows.title: Open Problem 8
rows.paper_title: Majority Colourings of Digraphs
rows.statement_text: Let $S(G)$ be the set of all stable sets of a digraph $G$ and $S(G,v)$ the stable sets containing $v$. A fractional majority colouring assigns each $T \in S(G)$ a weight $x_T \geq 0$ such that $\sum_{T \in S(G,v)} x_T \geq 1$ for each vertex $v$. What is the minimum $k$ such that every digraph $G$ has a fractional majority colouring with $\sum_{T \in S(G)} x_T \leq k$? Perhaps $k < 3$.
rows.context_text: This fractional relaxation of majority colouring is introduced in Section 5 as a natural variant. The informal conjecture that the minimum total weight is less than 3 would constitute a fractional analogue of Conjecture 2.
rows.source_text: Let $S(G)$ be the set of all stable sets of a digraph $G$ and $S(G,v)$ the stable sets containing $v$. A fractional majority colouring assigns each $T \in S(G)$ a weight $x_T \geq 0$ such that $\sum_{T \in S(G,v)} x_T \geq 1$ for each vertex $v$. What is the minimum $k$ such that every digraph $G$ has a fractional majority colouring with $\sum_{T \in S(G)} x_T \leq k$? Perhaps $k < 3$.
rows.status_semantics: The minimum total weight k for fractional majority colourings of all digraphs remains unresolved below 3 in full generality. Anastos, Lamaison, Steiner, and Szabó (arXiv:1911.01954, EJC 2021) proved k ≤ 3.9602 for all digraphs (Theorem 6), and that digraphs with minimum out-degree Ω((1/ε)² ln(1/ε)) admit fractional majority (2+ε)-colourings (Theorem 7), placing dense digraphs below the conjectured threshold. The general question of whether k < 3 for every digraph remains open.
rows.defer_reason: Primarily a determine-the-minimum-value question.
rows.class_notes: The embedded 'perhaps k<3' fragment could be split off as a B2 target (fractional majority weight < 3).

rows.title: Open Problem: error term for longest rainbow cycle
rows.paper_title: Random subgraphs of properly edge-coloured complete graphs and long rainbow cycles
rows.statement_text: Determine the correct order of the error term in the maximum length of a rainbow cycle guaranteed in every properly edge-coloured $K_n$; currently the deficit is known to lie between $-1$ and $-O(n^{3/4})$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.2 establishes a rainbow cycle of length at least $n - 24n^{3/4}$, while Andersen's Conjecture 1.1 posits a rainbow path of length $n-2$. The authors note that the constant $24$ in front of $n^{3/4}$ is not optimised, and leave as an open problem the task of pinning down the true order of the error term.
rows.source_text: Determine the correct order of the error term in the maximum length of a rainbow cycle guaranteed in every properly edge-coloured $K_n$; currently the deficit is known to lie between $-1$ and $-O(n^{3/4})$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1608.07028 — Open Problem: error term for longest rainbow cycle
rows.status_semantics: The open problem asks for the exact order of the error term $n - \ell^*(n)$, where $\ell^*(n)$ is the length of the longest rainbow cycle guaranteed in every properly edge-coloured $K_n$. Alon, Pokrovskiy, and Sudakov (2016, arXiv:1608.07028) established $\ell^*(n) \ge n - O(n^{3/4})$. Balogh and Molla (arXiv:1706.04950, published 2019) subsequently improved this to $\ell^*(n) \ge n - O(\log n \cdot \sqrt{n})$, so the deficit now lies between $\Omega(1)$ and $O(\log n \cdot \sqrt{n})$; the exact order remains open.
rows.defer_reason: Asks to determine the correct order of an error term, i.e. improve bounds, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 1
rows.paper_title: Graphic TSP in cubic graphs
rows.statement_text: Every 2-connected subcubic $n$-vertex graph with $n_2$ vertices of degree two has a TSP walk of length at most $\frac{5}{4}n + \frac{1}{4}n_2 - 1$.
rows.context_text: The authors construct a 2-connected cubic $n$-vertex graph with no TSP walk shorter than $\frac{5}{4}n - 2$ (Proposition 31) and a 2-connected subcubic $n$-vertex graph with $n_2 = \Theta(n)$ degree-two vertices and no TSP walk shorter than $\frac{5}{4}n_2 - 1$ (Proposition 29); the cubic construction was found independently by Maz\'ak and Lukot'ka. The authors believe these two constructions are tight and that the conjecture represents the correct improvement of Theorem 1 (which achieves the weaker bound $\frac{9}{7}n + \frac{2}{7}n_2 - 1$). They also remark that the stronger form of their reduction inequality (equation (1)) was kept precisely because it may be useful in an eventual proof of this conjecture.
rows.source_text: Every 2-connected subcubic $n$-vertex graph with $n_2$ vertices of degree two has a TSP walk of length at most $\frac{5}{4}n + \frac{1}{4}n_2 - 1$.
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture is fully resolved. Wigal, Yoo, and Yu (arXiv:2112.06278, December 2021) prove that every simple 2-connected subcubic graph on n vertices with n_2 vertices of degree two has a TSP walk of length at most (5n+n_2)/4 - 1, confirming Conjecture 1 of Dvořák, Král', and Mohar. The bound is shown to be tight, and they also provide a quadratic-time combinatorial algorithm, yielding a 5/4-approximation for the graphic TSP on cubic graphs (improving the prior 9/7 ratio). The result was subsequently published in the Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series B.
rows.class_notes: TSP walk = closed spanning walk; exact length bound for 2-connected subcubic graphs, solved.

rows.title: Problem 1
rows.paper_title: The crossing number of the cone of a graph
rows.statement_text: For each $k \geq 0$, find the smallest integer $f(k)$ for which there is a graph $G$ with crossing number at least $k$ and its cone has $\operatorname{cr}(CG) = f(k)$.
rows.context_text: Motivated by the negative answer to Richter's question for $n=6$, the authors reformulate the problem as finding the minimum possible crossing number of a cone over all graphs of a given crossing number. Equivalently, $f(k)$ is the largest integer such that every graph with $\operatorname{cr}(G) \geq k$ satisfies $\operatorname{cr}(CG) \geq f(k)$. The paper proves $f(k) = k + \Theta(\sqrt{k})$ for multigraphs.
rows.source_text: For each $k \geq 0$, find the smallest integer $f(k)$ for which there is a graph $G$ with crossing number at least $k$ and its cone has $\operatorname{cr}(CG) = f(k)$.
rows.status_semantics: The problem asks for a general formula for f(k), the smallest crossing number achievable by the cone of any graph with crossing number at least k. The source paper (published as SIAM J. Discrete Math. 32 (2018) 2080–2093) establishes f(k) = k + Θ(√k) for multigraphs and computes the simple-graph version φ_s(3)=3, φ_s(4)=4, φ_s(5)=5. Ding and Huang (Graphs Combin. 2021) extend this table by proving φ_s(6)=5 and φ_s(7)=6. A closed-form formula for all k remains open.
rows.defer_reason: Determine-the-value problem (find smallest f(k)), not a single proposition.

rows.title: Problem 4
rows.paper_title: The crossing number of the cone of a graph
rows.statement_text: Given a 1-page drawing of a graph $G$ with $k$ crossings, find an upper bound on the number of crossings of an optimal 2-page drawing of $G$ while having the order of vertices of $G$ on the spine unchanged.
rows.context_text: This problem asks: if all vertices of $G$ are incident to the outer face (equivalently, $G$ admits a 1-page drawing), what is the most efficient way to redraw some edges in a new page to reduce crossings? It is intimately related to bounding the crossing number of the cone, and the Edwards max-cut bound (Lemma 5) is applied to answer it partially.
rows.source_text: Given a 1-page drawing of a graph $G$ with $k$ crossings, find an upper bound on the number of crossings of an optimal 2-page drawing of $G$ while having the order of vertices of $G$ on the spine unchanged.
rows.defer_reason: Asks to 'find an upper bound', an open-ended optimization task, not a proposition.
rows.class_notes: 1-page/2-page book drawing vocabulary would also be needed.

rows.title: Conjecture on $f_s(k)$ asymptotics
rows.paper_title: The crossing number of the cone of a graph
rows.context_text: Section 5 proves $f_s(k) = k + o(k)$ for simple graphs, improving on the upper bound $f_s(k) \leq 2k$ suggested by small cases ($f_s(1)=3,\, f_s(2)=5,\, f_s(3)=6,\, f_s(4)=8,\, f_s(5)=10$). The authors provide additional justification for this more precise asymptotic conjecture on the simple-graph version of $f$.
rows.class_notes: f_s(k) (cone crossing function for simple graphs) is not defined in this record; also needs o(1) asymptotics.

rows.title: Conjecture: every forest of lanterns is pervasive
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. V. Chandeliers and strings
rows.statement_text: Every forest of lanterns $H$ is pervasive; that is, for all $\nu, \ell \geq 0$ there exists $c$ such that every graph $G$ with $\omega(G) \leq \nu$ and $\chi(G) > c$ contains an induced subgraph isomorphic to an $(\geq \ell)$-subdivision of $H$.
rows.context_text: Results of Chalopin, Esperet, Li and Ossona de Mendez (via the Burling/Pawlik graphs) show that every pervasive graph must be a forest of lanterns. The authors state 'perhaps the converse is true, that every forest of lanterns is pervasive', and the abstract notes 'it is not known whether every forest of lanterns is pervasive in the class of all graphs.' The paper proves pervasiveness of all forests of lanterns in every $\rho$-controlled ideal ($\rho \geq 2$) and in the class of string graphs, but the question for the ideal of all graphs remains open.
rows.source_text: Every forest of lanterns $H$ is pervasive; that is, for all $\nu, \ell \geq 0$ there exists $c$ such that every graph $G$ with $\omega(G) \leq \nu$ and $\chi(G) > c$ contains an induced subgraph isomorphic to an $(\geq \ell)$-subdivision of $H$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1609.00314 — Conjecture: every forest of lanterns is pervasive
rows.class_notes: 'Forest of lanterns' is paper-local (Scott–Seymour) and not defined in the record; pervasiveness itself is defined inline.

rows.title: Informal Problem (projective plane structure in near-extremal constructions)
rows.paper_title: A note on intersecting hypergraphs with large cover number
rows.statement_text: It would be interesting either to find a different type of construction of near-extremal intersecting $r$-partite hypergraphs $H$ with $\tau(H)$ close to $r$, or to show that near-extremal constructions must contain large pieces from a projective plane.
rows.context_text: The authors observe that except for a few sporadic small examples, all known constructions of intersecting $r$-partite hypergraphs $H$ with $\tau(H)$ close to $r$ are based in some way on finite projective planes.
rows.source_text: It would be interesting either to find a different type of construction of near-extremal intersecting $r$-partite hypergraphs $H$ with $\tau(H)$ close to $r$, or to show that near-extremal constructions must contain large pieces from a projective plane.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1609.05458 — Informal Problem (projective plane structure in near-extremal constructions)
rows.defer_reason: 'It would be interesting to find a construction or show structure' — vague meta-task.
rows.class_notes: Meta-question about structure of near-extremal examples for Ryser's bound, not a restatement or variant of Ryser's conjecture itself.

rows.title: Question 1.1
rows.paper_title: Gallai's path decomposition conjecture for graphs of small maximum degree
rows.statement_text: Does every connected graph $G$ that is not an odd semi-clique admit a path decomposition into $\lfloor \frac{|V(G)|}{2} \rfloor$ paths?
rows.context_text: An odd semi-clique is a graph obtained from a clique on $2k+1$ vertices by deleting at most $k-1$ edges; a simple counting argument shows such a graph cannot be decomposed into $k$ paths. The authors ask whether odd semi-cliques are the only obstructions to a stronger, ceiling-free version of Gallai's conjecture.
rows.source_text: Does every connected graph $G$ that is not an odd semi-clique admit a path decomposition into $\lfloor \frac{|V(G)|}{2} \rfloor$ paths?
rows.status_semantics: Question 1.1 — whether every connected graph that is not an odd semi-clique admits a path decomposition into \lfloor |V(G)|/2 \rfloor paths — remains open in full generality. It has been settled positively for planar graphs (Blanché, Bonamy, Bonichon 2021: every connected planar graph except K_3 and K_5^- decomposes into \lfloor n/2 \rfloor paths) and for 2-degenerate graphs (Anto, Basavaraju 2022: every connected 2-degenerate graph except the triangle decomposes into \lfloor n/2 \rfloor paths). Search results also indicate partial progress for 3-degenerate graphs (2024) but those could not be verified within the call budget.
rows.class_notes: Ceiling-free strengthening of Gallai's path decomposition conjecture excluding odd semi-cliques (defined in context).

rows.title: Conjecture 2.4
rows.paper_title: Optimal compression of approximate inner products and dimension reduction
rows.statement_text: Under the assumptions of Theorem 1.2, the conclusion holds together with the further requirement that $\|x_i\| \leq O(1)$ and $\|y_i\| \leq O(1)$ for all $1 \leq i \leq n$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.2 establishes a bipartite Johnson-Lindenstrauss reduction to dimension $t = \lfloor C\log(2+\varepsilon^2 n)/\varepsilon^2 \rfloor$ that approximates inner products up to additive error $\varepsilon$, but does not control the norms of the output vectors. The authors conjecture that the output vectors can simultaneously be taken to have bounded Euclidean norm $O(1)$. If true, this would close the gap between the upper and lower bounds in the first bullet of Theorem 2.2 for all $\varepsilon \geq 2/\sqrt{n}$.
rows.source_text: Under the assumptions of Theorem 1.2, the conclusion holds together with the further requirement that $\|x_i\| \leq O(1)$ and $\|y_i\| \leq O(1)$ for all $1 \leq i \leq n$.
rows.defer_reason: Johnson–Lindenstrauss dimension reduction / inner-product compression; discrete geometry, not graph theory.
rows.class_notes: Also references 'assumptions of Theorem 1.2' internally, but B5 dominates.

rows.title: Conjecture 3
rows.paper_title: Subdivisions in digraphs of large out-degree or large dichromatic number
rows.statement_text: There exists a least integer $\mathrm{mader}_{\delta^0}(TT_k)$ such that every digraph $D$ with $\delta^0(D) \geq \mathrm{mader}_{\delta^0}(TT_k)$ contains a subdivision of $TT_k$.
rows.context_text: The paper shows Conjecture 3 is equivalent to Mader's Conjecture 2: if transitive tournaments are $\delta^0$-maderian, then $\mathrm{mader}_{\delta^+}(TT_k) \leq \mathrm{mader}_{\delta^0}(TT_{2k})$ for all $k$. It is stated separately to make this equivalence explicit.
rows.source_text: There exists a least integer $\mathrm{mader}_{\delta^0}(TT_k)$ such that every digraph $D$ with $\delta^0(D) \geq \mathrm{mader}_{\delta^0}(TT_k)$ contains a subdivision of $TT_k$.
rows.class_notes: Minimum-semidegree (delta^0) form, proven equivalent to Mader's out-degree conjecture matched by opg_match.

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: Subdivisions in digraphs of large out-degree or large dichromatic number
rows.statement_text: Every oriented tree is $\delta^+$-maderian.
rows.context_text: Oriented trees are known to be $\delta^0$-maderian with $\mathrm{mader}_{\delta^0}(T) = |T|-1$ via a greedy procedure, but whether they are $\delta^+$-maderian is open. This is posed as a natural weaker step towards Mader's Conjecture 2. The paper provides evidence by proving in-arborescences are $\delta^+$-maderian (Theorem 23) and all oriented paths are $\delta^+$-maderian (Corollary 20).
rows.source_text: Every oriented tree is $\delta^+$-maderian.
rows.class_notes: 'delta^+-maderian' is paper-local and not defined in the record; weaker step toward the matched Mader conjecture (oriented trees instead of TT_k).

rows.title: Conjecture 7
rows.paper_title: Subdivisions in digraphs of large out-degree or large dichromatic number
rows.statement_text: If $F_1$ and $F_2$ are $\delta^+$-maderian, then the disjoint union of $F_1$ and $F_2$ is also $\delta^+$-maderian.
rows.context_text: Alon's Conjecture 6 would imply this. Partial positive answers follow from the Erdős–Pósa property: Theorem 8 shows that if $F$ is $\delta^+$-maderian and has the Erdős–Pósa property, then $k \times F$ is also $\delta^+$-maderian for all positive integers $k$.
rows.source_text: If $F_1$ and $F_2$ are $\delta^+$-maderian, then the disjoint union of $F_1$ and $F_2$ is also $\delta^+$-maderian.
rows.class_notes: Closure of delta^+-maderian under disjoint union; framework companion, not a variant of the TT_k conjecture. 'Maderian' undefined in record.

rows.title: Conjecture 11
rows.paper_title: Subdivisions in digraphs of large out-degree or large dichromatic number
rows.statement_text: If $T$ is an oriented tree of order $k$, then $\mathrm{mader}_{\chi}(T) \leq 2k-2$.
rows.context_text: Stated as an interesting step towards Burr's Conjecture 10, of which it is a direct consequence. The $\chi$-maderian digraphs are exactly the oriented forests, but determining $\mathrm{mader}_{\chi}$ precisely for each oriented tree is still open.
rows.source_text: If $T$ is an oriented tree of order $k$, then $\mathrm{mader}_{\chi}(T) \leq 2k-2$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 11 states that for every oriented tree T of order k, mader_chi(T) <= 2k-2. The 2020 paper arXiv:2008.09888 (Gishboliner, Steiner, Szabó) proves the stronger result mader_chi(F) = v(F) for all orientations of cactus graphs (Corollary 8 per internal references); since every tree is a cactus graph (trees have no cycles, so the cactus condition is vacuously satisfied), this implies mader_chi(T) = k <= 2k-2 for every oriented tree T of order k >= 2, resolving Conjecture 11. The abstract of 2008.09888 confirms results for orientations of cactus graphs and bioriented forests, consistent with this claim.
rows.class_notes: Chromatic-parameter (Burr-type) bound mader_chi(T) <= 2k-2; different parameter than the matched out-degree conjecture. mader_chi undefined in record; solved.

rows.title: Problem 12
rows.paper_title: Subdivisions in digraphs of large out-degree or large dichromatic number
rows.statement_text: What is $\mathrm{mader}_{\vec{\chi}}(\vec{K}_n)$?
rows.context_text: The paper proves in Section 3 that every digraph is $\vec{\chi}$-maderian. Since every digraph of order $n$ is a subdigraph of $\vec{K}_n$, bounding $\mathrm{mader}_{\vec{\chi}}(\vec{K}_n)$ controls all cases. The paper establishes $\mathrm{mader}_{\vec{\chi}}(\vec{K}_n) \leq 4^{n^2-2n+1}(n-1)+1$ (Corollary 36), but the exact value is unknown.
rows.source_text: What is $\mathrm{mader}_{\vec{\chi}}(\vec{K}_n)$?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the value of mader(K_n)?' — value-determination question.
rows.class_notes: Also uses paper-local mader_{dichromatic} notation.

rows.title: Problem 16
rows.paper_title: Subdivisions in digraphs of large out-degree or large dichromatic number
rows.statement_text: Are all digraphs $\kappa$-maderian? $\kappa'$-maderian?
rows.context_text: Since $k$-connected graphs have minimum degree at least $k$, Mader's undirected theorem implies graphs with connectivity at least $g(k)$ contain a $K_k$ subdivision. The analogous question for strong connectivity $\kappa(D)$ and strong arc-connectivity $\kappa'(D)$ of digraphs is open. Thomassen's Conjecture 17 would imply a positive answer for $\kappa$-maderianness.
rows.source_text: Are all digraphs $\kappa$-maderian? $\kappa'$-maderian?
rows.status_semantics: Problem 16 asks two questions: whether all digraphs are $\kappa$-maderian (large strong connectivity forces subdivisions) and whether all digraphs are $\kappa'$-maderian (large strong arc-connectivity forces subdivisions). The $\kappa'$-maderian question was answered negatively by Gishboliner, Steiner, and Szabó (arXiv:2008.13224, Propositions 10--11), who showed that neither $\overleftrightarrow{K_4}$ nor $\overleftrightarrow{S_4}$ is $\kappa'$-maderian. The $\kappa$-maderian question remains open; Thomassen's Conjecture 17 from the source paper would imply a positive answer for that part.
rows.class_notes: Yes/no pair on kappa-/kappa'-maderianness, but 'maderian' is paper-local and undefined in the record; distinct from the matched TT_k out-degree conjecture.

rows.title: Open Problem (better query complexity bounds for property testing)
rows.paper_title: Fast property testing and metrics for permutations
