rows.statement_text: It remains a major open problem if better bounds hold for the various property testing results.
rows.context_text: General results in combinatorial property testing show that natural properties can be tested with constant query complexity depending only on $\varepsilon$ and the property, but the upper bounds arising from proofs are often enormous: wowzer-type or Ackermann-type in $1/\varepsilon$, or established only via compactness with no explicit bound at all. The paper makes progress on this problem for permutations by establishing a universal polynomial-in-$1/\varepsilon$ query complexity bound for two-sided testing of hereditary properties with respect to the rectangular distance.
rows.source_text: It remains a major open problem if better bounds hold for the various property testing results.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1611.01270 — Open Problem (better query complexity bounds for property testing)
rows.defer_reason: Query-complexity meta-problem about property testing of permutations; not graph theory.
rows.class_notes: Also hopelessly vague ('better bounds for various results'), but subject matter (permutation property testing) already puts it out of scope.

rows.title: Open Problem (interference worsens with depth)
rows.paper_title: A Graph-Theoretic Approach to Multitasking
rows.statement_text: We believe that it is also the case that interference gets worst with $r$ (namely that interference worsens as $r$ increases to $r+1$ for $r > 2$), although whether this is indeed the case is an open problem.
rows.context_text: The paper proves that for $d$-regular networks of depth $r$ the multitasking capacity for task sets of size $n$ is at most $O\!\left(\frac{r}{d^{1-1/r}}\right)$, showing depth-$r$ networks suffer strictly more interference than depth-2 networks when $2 < r \ll d$. The authors then conjecture that interference continues to worsen monotonically as depth increases beyond 2.
rows.source_text: We believe that it is also the case that interference gets worst with $r$ (namely that interference worsens as $r$ increases to $r+1$ for $r > 2$), although whether this is indeed the case is an open problem.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1611.02400 — Open Problem (interference worsens with depth)
rows.defer_reason: Vague monotonicity belief without a precise quantitative statement.
rows.class_notes: Graph-based multitasking/interference model, but the claim 'interference gets worse as r increases' has no precise formulation in the record.

rows.title: Open Problem (multitasker at average degree $\Theta(\log n)$)
rows.paper_title: A Graph-Theoretic Approach to Multitasking
rows.statement_text: It is an interesting question whether there exists a multitasker with $\alpha > 0$ independent of $n$, for average degree $\Theta(\log n)$, which, if true is the largest average degree possible. This is left as an open problem.
rows.context_text: The paper shows there exist multitaskers of average degree $\Omega(\log\log n)$ with $\alpha > 1/3 - \varepsilon$, and that for the multitasking capacity to decay with average degree $d$ one must assume $d$ grows faster than $\log\log n$. The question is whether the threshold can be pushed up to $\Theta(\log n)$.
rows.source_text: It is an interesting question whether there exists a multitasker with $\alpha > 0$ independent of $n$, for average degree $\Theta(\log n)$, which, if true is the largest average degree possible. This is left as an open problem.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1611.02400 — Open Problem (multitasker at average degree $\Theta(\log n)$)
rows.class_notes: 'Multitasker' and capacity alpha are paper-local definitions never given in context_text; question is also inherently asymptotic (Theta(log n)).

rows.title: Conjecture 1.6
rows.paper_title: Fair representation by independent sets
rows.statement_text: Given a partition of the vertex set of a path into sets $V_1, \ldots, V_m$ there exists an independent set $S$ and integers $b_i$, $i \leq m$, such that $|S \cap V_i| \geq \frac{|V_i|}{2} - b_i$ for all $i$, and 1. $\sum_{i \leq m} b_i \leq \frac{m}{2}$ and 2. $b_i \leq 1$ for all $i \leq m$.
rows.context_text: The paper proves (Theorem 1.7) that either condition alone holds for the independence complex of a path, but not necessarily both simultaneously. Conjecture 1.6 asserts both conditions can be satisfied at once. The matching complex of a path is the independence complex of a path one vertex shorter.
rows.source_text: Given a partition of the vertex set of a path into sets $V_1, \ldots, V_m$ there exists an independent set $S$ and integers $b_i$, $i \leq m$, such that $|S \cap V_i| \geq \frac{|V_i|}{2} - b_i$ for all $i$, and 1. $\sum_{i \leq m} b_i \leq \frac{m}{2}$ and 2. $b_i \leq 1$ for all $i \leq m$.
rows.class_notes: Fully self-contained: independent sets in a path with fair-representation deficiencies b_i.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.9
rows.paper_title: Fair representation by independent sets
rows.statement_text: For any partition $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_m$ of $E(K_{n,n})$ and any $j \leq m$ there exists a perfect matching $F$ in $K_{n,n}$ satisfying $|F \cap E_i| \geq \left\lfloor \frac{|E_i|}{n} \right\rfloor$ for all $i \neq j$, and $|F \cap E_j| \geq \left\lfloor \frac{|E_j|}{n} \right\rfloor - 1$.
rows.context_text: Central conjecture of the paper: the matching complex of $K_{n,n}$ admits almost fair representation of any partition of its edge set. The paper proves the conjecture for $m = 2$ and $m = 3$ (Theorem 1.10) using topological methods including Sperner's lemma.
rows.source_text: For any partition $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_m$ of $E(K_{n,n})$ and any $j \leq m$ there exists a perfect matching $F$ in $K_{n,n}$ satisfying $|F \cap E_i| \geq \left\lfloor \frac{|E_i|}{n} \right\rfloor$ for all $i \neq j$, and $|F \cap E_j| \geq \left\lfloor \frac{|E_j|}{n} \right\rfloor - 1$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.9 from arXiv:1611.03196 remains open in full generality. The source paper established the conjecture for m=2 and m=3 via topological methods (Sperner's lemma). A 2024 follow-up by Othman and Berger (Discrete Mathematics) provides combinatorial reproofs for m=2 and m=3 and shows for m=4 that a perfect matching M always exists in K_{n,n} with total shortfall s(M)≤11, constituting partial progress on the m=4 case but not a full resolution. The conjecture for m≥4 remains open.
rows.class_notes: Central fair-representation conjecture for perfect matchings of K_{n,n}; fully stated.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.14
rows.paper_title: Fair representation by independent sets
rows.statement_text: If the edge set of a graph $H$ is partitioned into sets $E_1, \ldots, E_m$ then there exists a matching $M$ satisfying $|M \cap E_i| \geq \left\lfloor \frac{|E_i|}{\Delta(H)+2} \right\rfloor$ for all $i \leq m$.
rows.context_text: Proposed as a stronger version of Conjecture 1.12 (the rainbow matching conjecture), extending almost fair representation to arbitrary graphs via the parameter $\Delta(H)+2$, which mirrors the connectivity bound for independence complexes of line graphs.
rows.source_text: If the edge set of a graph $H$ is partitioned into sets $E_1, \ldots, E_m$ then there exists a matching $M$ satisfying $|M \cap E_i| \geq \left\lfloor \frac{|E_i|}{\Delta(H)+2} \right\rfloor$ for all $i \leq m$.
rows.class_notes: Fair matching representation via Delta(H)+2; standard vocabulary only.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.15
rows.paper_title: Fair representation by independent sets
rows.statement_text: For every $m$ there exists a number $c(m)$ for which the following is true: if $G$ is a bipartite graph and $E_1, \ldots, E_m$ are any sets of edges, then there exists a matching $S$ in $G$ of size at least $\frac{|E(G)|}{\Delta(G)} - c(m)$ such that $|S \cap E_i| \leq \left\lceil \frac{|E_i|}{\Delta(G)} \right\rceil$ for all $i \leq m$.
rows.context_text: An under-representation formulation of the fair representation theme, motivated by the observation that over-representation fails when the sets do not form a partition. The authors suggest $c(m) = m/2$ may suffice. When the $E_i$ form a partition, condition (2) implies all but $c(m)$ sets are fairly represented.
rows.source_text: For every $m$ there exists a number $c(m)$ for which the following is true: if $G$ is a bipartite graph and $E_1, \ldots, E_m$ are any sets of edges, then there exists a matching $S$ in $G$ of size at least $\frac{|E(G)|}{\Delta(G)} - c(m)$ such that $|S \cap E_i| \leq \left\lceil \frac{|E_i|}{\Delta(G)} \right\rceil$ for all $i \leq m$.
rows.class_notes: Exists-c(m) under-representation statement; fully stated with standard vocabulary.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture on Tight Constant for Clique Chromatic Number
rows.paper_title: Clique coloring of dense random graphs
rows.statement_text: It seems plausible that $\chi_c(G(n, 1/2)) = (1/2 + o(1)) \log n$ whp.
rows.context_text: The paper establishes $\chi_c(G(n,1/2)) = \Theta(\log n)$ whp, with the upper bound $(1/2+o(1))\log_2 n$ coming from McDiarmid–Mitsche–Pratat. The authors note that the constant $\frac{1}{2000}$ in their lower bound can certainly be improved but that the method as presented does not suffice to pin down the tight constant.
rows.source_text: It seems plausible that $\chi_c(G(n, 1/2)) = (1/2 + o(1)) \log n$ whp.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1612.06539 — Informal Conjecture on Tight Constant for Clique Chromatic Number
rows.status_semantics: Demidovich and Zhukovskii (arXiv 2020, JGT 2023) fully resolve the conjecture, proving that $\chi_c(G(n,1/2)) = (1/2)\log_2 n - \Theta(\ln \ln n)$ whp. This confirms the conjectured leading constant of $1/2$ and in fact establishes a tight concentration result that sharply pins down the second-order term as well.
rows.class_notes: Solved; clique chromatic number of G(n,1/2) whp with (1/2+o(1))log n — needs random-graph and asymptotic wrappers; log base implicitly base 2.

rows.title: Open problem: dimension of posets with planar cover graphs vs. height
rows.paper_title: Planar posets have dimension at most linear in their height
rows.statement_text: It is not known whether the dimension of posets with planar cover graphs is bounded by a linear function of their height (or any polynomial function for that matter).
rows.context_text: The paper's main result (Theorem 1) establishes a linear bound $\dim(P) \leq 192h + 96$ for planar posets. The Streib–Trotter theorem guarantees only that some (exponential) function $f(h)$ bounds the dimension for the broader class of posets with planar cover graphs. Whether a polynomial — let alone linear — bound holds in this more general setting is explicitly left open by the authors, who also provide an improved lower bound of $2h - 2$ (Theorem 3) for this class.
rows.source_text: It is not known whether the dimension of posets with planar cover graphs is bounded by a linear function of their height (or any polynomial function for that matter).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1612.07540 — Open problem: dimension of posets with planar cover graphs vs. height
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture asks whether the dimension of posets with planar cover graphs is bounded by a polynomial (or linear) function of their height. Kozik, Micek, and Trotter (arXiv:1907.00380, 2019) resolved the polynomial part affirmatively by proving an O(h^6) upper bound; Gorsky and Seweryn (arXiv:2103.15920, 2021) subsequently improved this to O(h^3). Whether a linear bound holds—analogous to the main theorem of the source paper for genuinely planar posets—remains open.
rows.class_notes: Single proposition (exists polynomial bound on dim in height for posets with planar cover graph) but needs poset-dimension primitive plus planarity layer.

rows.title: Problem 1
rows.paper_title: List coloring with requests
rows.statement_text: Does there for every integer $d \geq 0$ exist $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every $d$-degenerate graph with an assignment of lists of size $d + 1$ is weighted $\varepsilon$-flexible? Or at least $\varepsilon$-flexible?
rows.context_text: Theorem 2 shows that lists of size $d+2$ suffice for weighted $\varepsilon$-flexibility of $d$-degenerate graphs. The question is whether one extra color (size $d+1$, matching choosability) also guarantees flexibility. For $d=0$ trivial; for $d=1$ (forests with lists of size 2) weighted $1/2$-flexibility is easy; the first open case is $d=2$.
rows.source_text: Does there for every integer $d \geq 0$ exist $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every $d$-degenerate graph with an assignment of lists of size $d + 1$ is weighted $\varepsilon$-flexible? Or at least $\varepsilon$-flexible?
rows.status_semantics: Problem 1 remains open in full generality: no paper has proved epsilon-flexibility for all d-degenerate graphs with (d+1)-lists for every d >= 2. Substantial partial progress exists: the d=2 case has been confirmed for planar graphs of girth >= 6 (lists of size 3) and for all graphs of maximum average degree < 3; the d=3 case has been confirmed for triangle-free planar graphs (lists of size 4); and epsilon-flexibility has been established for graphs of treewidth 2, treedepth k, and bipartite d-degenerate graphs. The general conjecture for arbitrary d-degenerate graphs with (d+1)-lists remains open for d >= 2.
rows.class_notes: '(Weighted) epsilon-flexible' is a paper-local notion not defined in this record.

rows.title: Motivating Question (Introduction)
rows.paper_title: Many cliques in $H$-free subgraphs of random graphs
rows.statement_text: For which values of $p$ is it true that $\mathrm{ex}(G(n,p), K_m, H) = (1+o(1))\binom{k-1}{m}\left(\frac{n}{k-1}\right)^m p^{\binom{m}{2}}$ w.h.p.?
rows.context_text: For any fixed $H$ with $\chi(H)=k>m$ the deterministic extremal result gives $\mathrm{ex}(n,K_m,H)=(1+o(1))\binom{k-1}{m}(n/(k-1))^m$. Analogous to the edge case characterised by Theorem 1.1, the authors ask for which values of $p$ this extremal count is achieved with high probability in $G(n,p)$. The paper answers the question fully when $m_2(H)\geq m_2(K_m)$ (Theorem 1.2) and only partially when $m_2(H)<m_2(K_m)$ (Theorem 1.4), leaving the exact transition threshold in the latter case open; the paper closes with a dedicated Section 6 of open problems not included in the provided text.
rows.source_text: For which values of $p$ is it true that $\mathrm{ex}(G(n,p), K_m, H) = (1+o(1))\binom{k-1}{m}\left(\frac{n}{k-1}\right)^m p^{\binom{m}{2}}$ w.h.p.?
rows.status_semantics: The source paper resolves the question when $m_2(H) \geq m_2(K_m)$ (Theorem 1.2) but only partially when $m_2(H) < m_2(K_m)$ (Theorem 1.4), leaving the exact transition threshold in the harder regime open. Samotij and Shikhelman (arXiv:1806.06609, 2018) generalise the problem to arbitrary pattern graphs $T$ in place of $K_m$: for the case $m_2(H) \leq m_2(T)$ they identify that threshold locations are governed by densities of coverings of $H$ by copies of $T$, reducing the question to deterministic hypergraph Tur\'{a}n problems that remain unsolved in full generality. The full question --- in particular the exact threshold when $m_2(H) < m_2(K_m)$ --- remains open.
rows.defer_reason: 'For which values of p' — a characterization/threshold-location question, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Antisymmetric variant of Jaeger's weak 3-flow conjecture
rows.paper_title: Additive bases and flows in graphs
rows.statement_text: There is a constant $k$ such that every directed $k$-edge-connected graph has a $\mathbb{Z}_5$-antisymmetric flow.
rows.context_text: The authors present this as the natural antisymmetric analogue of Jaeger's weak 3-flow conjecture (which was solved by Thomassen and later improved to 6-edge-connectivity by Lovász et al.). The note $\mathbb{Z}_5$ is best possible since $\mathbb{Z}_2$, $\mathbb{Z}_3$, and $\mathbb{Z}_4$ cannot support antisymmetric flows in general.
rows.source_text: There is a constant $k$ such that every directed $k$-edge-connected graph has a $\mathbb{Z}_5$-antisymmetric flow.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1701.03366 — Antisymmetric variant of Jaeger's weak 3-flow conjecture
rows.class_notes: Exists-constant-k statement; antisymmetric Z_5-flow is definable flow vocabulary.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.8
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. VI. Banana trees
rows.statement_text: Every multigraph is widespread.
rows.context_text: Scott [6] conjectured that every multigraph is pervasive in the ideal of all graphs, but this was disproved by a construction in [5]. The authors introduce the notion of 'widespread' and present Conjecture 1.8 as a resuscitated version of Scott's conjecture. They note they are very far from proving it, cannot verify it even for every chandelier, and that all multigraphs proved widespread so far are subdivisions of outerplanar graphs.
rows.source_text: Every multigraph is widespread.
rows.class_notes: 'Widespread' is a paper-local definition not reproduced in context_text (recovery flagged).

rows.title: Conjecture 1.10
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. VI. Banana trees
rows.statement_text: For all graphs $J$ and for all integers $\tau \geq 0$, there exists $c$ such that if $G$ is a graph with chromatic number more than $c$, then either some induced subgraph of $G$ is a subdivision of $J$ or $\chi_2(G) > \tau$.
rows.context_text: This conjecture is shown to be equivalent to Conjecture 1.8 (every multigraph is widespread), using the result from [3] that for all $\rho \geq 2$ and every multigraph $J$, every $\rho$-controlled class of $J$-subdivision-free graphs is $2$-controlled. The authors present 1.10 as a nicer reformulation of 1.8, though they do not use it directly in the paper.
rows.source_text: For all graphs $J$ and for all integers $\tau \geq 0$, there exists $c$ such that if $G$ is a graph with chromatic number more than $c$, then either some induced subgraph of $G$ is a subdivision of $J$ or $\chi_2(G) > \tau$.
rows.class_notes: Relies on paper-local parameter chi_2(G) (local chromatic control) undefined here; stated equivalent to Conjecture 1.8.

rows.title: Open Question: first pervasiveness characterisation
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. VI. Banana trees
rows.statement_text: Which multigraphs $H$ have the property that for all $\kappa$ there exists $c$ such that every $H$-subdivision-free graph with clique number at most $\kappa$ has chromatic number at most $c$?
rows.context_text: The paper poses two bullet-point questions about characterising multigraphs $H$ by a pervasiveness-type condition. For the second (stronger) question, which involves all subdivisions of $H$, the answer is known for controlled classes; the first question, which asks about $H$-subdivision-free graphs directly, is explicitly stated to remain open.
rows.source_text: Which multigraphs $H$ have the property that for all $\kappa$ there exists $c$ such that every $H$-subdivision-free graph with clique number at most $\kappa$ has chromatic number at most $c$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1701.05597 — Open Question: first pervasiveness characterisation
rows.defer_reason: 'Which multigraphs H ...' — a grand characterization question, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Problem 1
rows.paper_title: Do triangle-free planar graphs have exponentially many 3-colorings?
rows.statement_text: Is there a positive real number $\alpha$ such that every planar triangle-free request graph admits a 3-coloring satisfying $\alpha$-fraction of its requests?
rows.context_text: This problem arises from the work of Asadi et al. [1] and is introduced as a reformulation vehicle for Thomassen's exponential-coloring conjecture. A request graph $(G, R_=, R_{\neq}, w)$ consists of a planar triangle-free graph $G$ together with degree-two vertices partitioned into equality requests $R_=$ and inequality requests $R_{\neq}$ with positive rational weights; a coloring satisfies a request if the two neighbors receive equal (resp. different) colors.
rows.source_text: Is there a positive real number $\alpha$ such that every planar triangle-free request graph admits a 3-coloring satisfying $\alpha$-fraction of its requests?
rows.status_semantics: The source paper (1702.00588) proves in Theorem 1 that Problem 1 (RGEN: existence of \alpha>0 such that every planar triangle-free request graph admits a 3-coloring satisfying an \alpha-fraction of its requests) is equivalent to Thomassen's conjecture (EXP: every planar triangle-free graph on n vertices has at least 2^{\beta n} 3-colorings for some \beta>0). EXP has since been disproved: Dvo\v{r}\'{a}k (arXiv:2108.12669, 2021) constructs planar triangle-free graphs with at most 2^{6n^{0.731}} 3-colorings (sub-exponential), improving on Thomassen's own earlier counterexample to his conjecture. By the equivalence RGEN \Leftrightarrow EXP, no positive real \alpha of the type sought in Problem 1 can exist.
rows.class_notes: Disproved; request-graph machinery is fully defined in context_text but the statement needs a planarity layer plus weighted-fraction wrapper.

rows.title: Question (rainbow hole with $s$ consecutive rainbow vertices)
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IX. Rainbow paths
rows.statement_text: Is it true that for all fixed $s$, $\kappa$, if $G$ is a graph with $\omega(G)\leq\kappa$ and $\chi(G)$ sufficiently large, then in every colouring of $G$ there is a hole in which some set of $s$ consecutive vertices is rainbow?
rows.context_text: The authors show that the shift graph of triples (coloured by middle elements) rules out a fully rainbow hole and even a hole in which every three consecutive vertices are rainbow. The weaker question of $s$ consecutive rainbow vertices in a hole is left open.
rows.source_text: Is it true that for all fixed $s$, $\kappa$, if $G$ is a graph with $\omega(G)\leq\kappa$ and $\chi(G)$ sufficiently large, then in every colouring of $G$ there is a hole in which some set of $s$ consecutive vertices is rainbow?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1702.01094 — Question (rainbow hole with $s$ consecutive rainbow vertices)
rows.class_notes: Rainbow-consecutive-vertices-in-a-hole question; standard finite vocabulary (holes, proper colourings, clique number).

rows.title: Question (uniquely-covered vertices in an induced path)
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IX. Rainbow paths
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a triangle-free graph with very large chromatic number, and let $\mathcal{A}$ be a set of stable subsets (not necessarily pairwise disjoint) of $V(G)$ with union $V(G)$. Does there necessarily exist an $s$-vertex induced path $P$ of $G$ such that for each $v\in V(P)$, some $X\in\mathcal{A}$ satisfies $X\cap V(P)=\{v\}$?
rows.context_text: The authors pose this as an additional open question in the Conclusion, asking whether a large chromatic number forces an induced path whose vertices are each 'uniquely covered' by some member of an arbitrary stable-set cover.
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a triangle-free graph with very large chromatic number, and let $\mathcal{A}$ be a set of stable subsets (not necessarily pairwise disjoint) of $V(G)$ with union $V(G)$. Does there necessarily exist an $s$-vertex induced path $P$ of $G$ such that for each $v\in V(P)$, some $X\in\mathcal{A}$ satisfies $X\cap V(P)=\{v\}$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1702.01094 — Question (uniquely-covered vertices in an induced path)
rows.class_notes: 'Very large chromatic number' is informal but the standard for-all-s exists-c quantification is unambiguous from the series' conventions.

rows.title: Problem 3
rows.paper_title: Coloring tournaments: from local to global
rows.statement_text: For every integer $i \geq 1$, there exists $f(i)$ such that every tournament $T$ with dominating number at least $f(i)$ contains an isomorphic copy of $S_i$.
rows.context_text: The tournaments $S_i$ are defined recursively: $S_1$ is a single vertex, and for $i > 1$, $S_i$ is the tournament on $2^i - 1$ vertices obtained by blowing up two vertices of an oriented triangle into two copies of $S_{i-1}$; one can check $\vec{\chi}(S_i) \geq i$. The problem is trivial for $i \leq 2$, verified for $i = 3$ in [7], and open for all $i \geq 4$.
rows.source_text: For every integer $i \geq 1$, there exists $f(i)$ such that every tournament $T$ with dominating number at least $f(i)$ contains an isomorphic copy of $S_i$.
rows.class_notes: S_i tournaments are fully defined recursively in context_text; dominating number standard.

rows.title: Problem 4
rows.paper_title: Coloring tournaments: from local to global
rows.statement_text: For every integer $k \geq 1$, there exist integers $K$ and $\ell$ such that every tournament $T$ with dominating number at least $K$ contains a subtournament with $\ell$ vertices and dominating number at least $k$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 2 shows that sufficiently large dominating number forces a bounded-size subtournament of large chromatic number. The analogous statement with dominating number in place of chromatic number in the conclusion remains open; the note observes that replacing dominating-number hypothesis by large chromatic number alone fails for any $k \geq 3$.
rows.source_text: For every integer $k \geq 1$, there exist integers $K$ and $\ell$ such that every tournament $T$ with dominating number at least $K$ contains a subtournament with $\ell$ vertices and dominating number at least $k$.

rows.title: Open Problem (independence number of planar graphs)
rows.paper_title: Triangle-free planar graphs with small independence number
rows.statement_text: It is an intriguing open problem to describe planar graphs on $n$ vertices whose independence number is exactly $n/4$, and we do not even know any polynomial-time algorithm to decide whether an $n$-vertex planar graph has an independent set larger than $n/4$.
rows.context_text: By the Four Colour Theorem every planar graph is 4-colourable, giving an independent set of size at least $n/4$, and there are infinitely many planar graphs for which this bound is tight. The paper studies the easier related problem for triangle-free planar graphs (where Grötzsch's theorem raises the bound to $n/3$) as a tractable surrogate for this harder general question.
rows.source_text: It is an intriguing open problem to describe planar graphs on $n$ vertices whose independence number is exactly $n/4$, and we do not even know any polynomial-time algorithm to decide whether an $n$-vertex planar graph has an independent set larger than $n/4$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1702.02888 — Open Problem (independence number of planar graphs)
rows.defer_reason: 'Describe planar graphs with alpha = n/4' — a characterization/meta problem, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Bundles a 'describe the extremal graphs' request with an algorithmic question.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.1
rows.paper_title: The Interactive Sum Choice Number of Graphs
rows.statement_text: If any connected component of $G$ is not a complete graph, then $\chi_{ISC}(G) < \chi_{SC}(G)$.
rows.context_text: The authors show that the interactive sum choice number satisfies $\chi_{ISC}(G) \leq \chi_{SC}(G)$ for all graphs $G$, and that equality holds when $G$ is a disjoint union of complete graphs (since Bob can force every vertex to receive colours $1, 2, \ldots$ in order). They believe this is the only case of equality, motivating the conjecture. The paper provides evidence by confirming it for sc-greedy graphs, trees, unbalanced complete bipartite graphs, and grids.
rows.source_text: If any connected component of $G$ is not a complete graph, then $\chi_{ISC}(G) < \chi_{SC}(G)$.
rows.class_notes: Interactive sum choice number chi_ISC is a paper-local game-defined parameter not defined in this record.

rows.title: Conjecture 3
rows.paper_title: Burling graphs, chromatic number, and orthogonal tree-decompositions
rows.statement_text: There exists a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f(k)$ for every $k \geq 1$ and every graph $G$ admitting a spaghetti tree-decomposition $(T, \{B_t\}_{t \in V(T)})$ and a path-decomposition $(P, \{B_p\}_{p \in V(P)})$ such that $|B_t \cap B_p| \leq k$ for every $t \in V(T)$ and $p \in V(P)$.
rows.context_text: The authors conclude the paper with this conjecture as an open problem in the spirit of exploring how the Asplund–Grünbaum result could be extended. A spaghetti tree-decomposition is a tree-decomposition rooted at some vertex $r$ such that, orienting edges away from $r$, the subtree induced by bags containing any fixed vertex $v$ forms a directed path. The authors remark that the conjecture could even hold with two spaghetti tree-decompositions.
rows.source_text: There exists a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f(k)$ for every $k \geq 1$ and every graph $G$ admitting a spaghetti tree-decomposition $(T, \{B_t\}_{t \in V(T)})$ and a path-decomposition $(P, \{B_p\}_{p \in V(P)})$ such that $|B_t \cap B_p| \leq k$ for every $t \in V(T)$ and $p \in V(P)$.
rows.class_notes: Spaghetti tree-decomposition is fully defined in context_text; needs tree/path-decomposition primitives.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (Section 1.2): dropping bounded-degree assumption
rows.paper_title: Thin graph classes and polynomial-time approximation schemes
rows.statement_text: It seems plausible that this additional assumption [bounded maximum degree] could be dropped, leading to a much more general result strengthening majority of the algorithms mentioned in the previous subsection.
rows.context_text: The paper proves that subgraph-closed graph classes with strongly sublinear separators admit thin systems of overlays under the additional assumption that their maximum degree is bounded. The authors conjecture this extra condition is unnecessary, which would subsume a much wider class of results.
rows.source_text: It seems plausible that this additional assumption [bounded maximum degree] could be dropped, leading to a much more general result strengthening majority of the algorithms mentioned in the previous subsection.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1704.00125 — Informal Conjecture (Section 1.2): dropping bounded-degree assumption
rows.class_notes: The intended proposition (thin systems of overlays without bounded degree) rests on 'thin system of overlays' and 'strongly sublinear separators', neither defined in this record.

rows.title: Open Problem (Section 1.2): APX-hardness on classes without sublinear separators
rows.paper_title: Thin graph classes and polynomial-time approximation schemes
rows.statement_text: It would be interesting to see whether this intuition can be made precise and for example show that the maximum independent set problem is APX-hard on any subgraph-closed class of graphs that does not have sublinear separators (or at least has exponential expansion).
rows.context_text: The paper notes that maximum independent set is APX-hard on bounded-degree graphs, so results cannot extend to classes containing all 3-regular graphs. Classes studied all have strongly sublinear separators; the authors ask whether APX-hardness can be proved for any subgraph-closed class lacking this property.
rows.source_text: It would be interesting to see whether this intuition can be made precise and for example show that the maximum independent set problem is APX-hard on any subgraph-closed class of graphs that does not have sublinear separators (or at least has exponential expansion).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1704.00125 — Open Problem (Section 1.2): APX-hardness on classes without sublinear separators
rows.defer_reason: 'It would be interesting to see whether this intuition can be made precise' — vague meta request; even the sample APX-hardness claim needs a complexity foundation.

rows.title: Open Problem: Regularity-free proof for the ordered removal lemma
rows.paper_title: Testing hereditary properties of ordered graphs and matrices
rows.statement_text: It will be interesting to try to obtain a proof for the ordered graph removal lemma that does not go through the strong regularity lemma needed in the current proof.
rows.context_text: The proofs of the ordered removal lemmas rely on strong variants of the graph regularity lemma, which impose a wowzer-type (tower-of-towers) dependence between the parameters $\delta^{-1}$ and $\varepsilon^{-1}$. Fox gave the first regularity-free proof of the unordered graph removal lemma, though still with tower-type dependence; an analogous approach for the ordered setting remains open.
rows.source_text: It will be interesting to try to obtain a proof for the ordered graph removal lemma that does not go through the strong regularity lemma needed in the current proof.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1704.02367 — Open Problem: Regularity-free proof for the ordered removal lemma
rows.status_semantics: The open problem of finding a regularity-free proof of the ordered graph removal lemma (analogous to Fox's 2011 proof for unordered graphs) remains open in general. Partial progress has been made: the paper arXiv:2110.03577 characterizes exactly which ordered graphs F admit a polynomial bound on delta_F(epsilon) in the induced ordered removal lemma; and arXiv:2307.01652 proves a polynomial removal lemma for ordered matchings via a novel nested-partition technique that avoids the regularity lemma for that special case. A full regularity-free proof for arbitrary ordered graphs has not been found.
rows.defer_reason: Asks for a regularity-free proof technique, a meta goal about proof methods rather than a proposition.

rows.title: Open Problem: Better parameter dependence for ordered binary matrix removal
rows.paper_title: Testing hereditary properties of ordered graphs and matrices
rows.statement_text: It will be interesting to combine the ideas from the current proof with the binary matrix regularity lemma of Alon, Fischer and Newman to obtain a removal lemma for finite families of ordered binary matrices with better dependence between the parameters $\delta^{-1}$ and $\varepsilon^{-1}$; ideally polynomial dependence, but even exponential dependence would be interesting.
rows.context_text: For ordered binary matrices, Alon, Fischer and Newman obtained an efficient conditional regularity lemma in which $\delta^{-1}$ is polynomial in $\varepsilon^{-1}$. The general ordered removal lemma proved in this paper inherits a wowzer-type dependence from the strong regularity lemma, and improving this for finite families of ordered binary matrices is posed as an open problem.
rows.source_text: It will be interesting to combine the ideas from the current proof with the binary matrix regularity lemma of Alon, Fischer and Newman to obtain a removal lemma for finite families of ordered binary matrices with better dependence between the parameters $\delta^{-1}$ and $\varepsilon^{-1}$; ideally polynomial dependence, but even exponential dependence would be interesting.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1704.02367 — Open Problem: Better parameter dependence for ordered binary matrix removal
rows.status_semantics: The open problem asks to improve the wowzer-type parameter dependence in the ordered binary matrix removal lemma (for finite families) to polynomial or at least exponential dependence between \delta^{-1} and \varepsilon^{-1}. Follow-up work has made partial progress: arXiv:2110.03577 characterises exactly when polynomial removal lemmas exist for ordered graphs (only for 2-vertex graphs or one specific 3-vertex configuration, up to symmetry), explicitly building on the Alon--Ben-Eliezer--Fischer framework and suggesting polynomial bounds for general families are rare; arXiv:2307.01652 proves a polynomial removal lemma for ordered matchings, a special subclass of ordered binary matrix patterns. The conjecture for arbitrary finite families of ordered binary matrices appears unresolved.
rows.defer_reason: 'Better dependence between the parameters; ideally polynomial, but even exponential would be interesting' — an improve-the-bounds meta problem.

rows.title: Question 1.1
rows.paper_title: Lines in Euclidean Ramsey theory
rows.statement_text: For which subsets $K \subset E^n$ does every red/blue-coloring of $E^n$ contain a red pair of points of distance one or a blue isometric copy of $K$?
rows.context_text: This is the central question studied throughout the paper. The notation $E^n \to (\ell_2, K)$ means every red/blue-coloring of $E^n$ contains a red copy of $\ell_2$ or a blue copy of $K$, and $E^n \not\to (\ell_2, K)$ means there is a coloring avoiding both. The paper says it follows Erdős et al. [4] in studying this question.
rows.source_text: For which subsets $K \subset E^n$ does every red/blue-coloring of $E^n$ contain a red pair of points of distance one or a blue isometric copy of $K$?
rows.status_semantics: Question 1.1 asks for which K every red/blue-coloring of E^n contains a red unit-distance pair or a blue isometric copy of K; the source paper resolves this for K = ℓ_m (m collinear unit-spaced points) up to the constant in the exponent 2^(cn). Conlon–Wu (2022) extends the analysis to asymmetric variants involving ℓ_3 vs. ℓ_m colorings, and Führer–Tóth (2024) sharpens those explicit bounds from m = 10^50 down to m = 1177. The question for general K beyond collinear configurations remains open.
rows.defer_reason: Euclidean Ramsey theory on E^n (isometric copies, unit distances) — discrete geometry, not graph/hypergraph theory; also a 'for which K' characterization.

rows.title: Open problem: $E^n \not\to (\ell_3, \ell_m)$ for large $m$
rows.paper_title: Lines in Euclidean Ramsey theory
rows.statement_text: For every natural number $m$, does there exist a natural number $n$ such that $E^n \not\to (\ell_3, \ell_m)$?
rows.context_text: In the concluding remarks the authors say they were unable to decide this question. They consider it unlikely to hold for large $m$ but were unable to exhibit a coloring confirming their suspicion. Erdős et al. [3] showed that $E^n \to (\ell_6, \ell_6)$ for all $n$, providing related context.
rows.source_text: For every natural number $m$, does there exist a natural number $n$ such that $E^n \not\to (\ell_3, \ell_m)$?
rows.status_semantics: Conlon and Fox (arXiv:1705.02166) asked whether for every $m$ there exists $n$ with $E^n \not\to (\ell_3, \ell_m)$, while suspecting this fails for large $m$. Conlon and Wu (arXiv:2208.13513) gave a dimension-independent coloring showing $E^n \not\to (\ell_3, \ell_{1177})$ for all $n$, partially answering the question in the positive direction for a specific $m$. A subsequent paper (arXiv:2404.19233) improved the bound dramatically to $m = 20$. The question for all $m$, in particular whether the non-arrow relation can hold for arbitrarily large $m$, remains open.
rows.defer_reason: Euclidean Ramsey theory (colorings of E^n, isometric copies of arithmetic progressions) — discrete geometry, out of scope.

rows.title: Open problem: removing diameter dependence from Theorem 1.1
rows.paper_title: Lines in Euclidean Ramsey theory
rows.statement_text: Can the dependence on the diameter $R$ be removed from Theorem 1.1? That is, does there exist a function $f(n)$, independent of the diameter of $K$, such that $E^n \not\to (\ell_2, K)$ for every $1$-separated set $K \subset E^n$ with $|K| > f(n)$?
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 requires $|K| > 10^4 n \log R$ where $R$ is the diameter of $K$. The authors note in the concluding remarks that a complete answer to the Erdős et al. problem on a universal two-coloring bound would follow from resolving this problem, and describe it as interesting in its own right.
rows.source_text: Can the dependence on the diameter $R$ be removed from Theorem 1.1? That is, does there exist a function $f(n)$, independent of the diameter of $K$, such that $E^n \not\to (\ell_2, K)$ for every $1$-separated set $K \subset E^n$ with $|K| > f(n)$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1705.02166 — Open problem: removing diameter dependence from Theorem 1.1
rows.defer_reason: Euclidean Ramsey theory (1-separated point sets in E^n, diameter dependence) — discrete geometry, out of scope.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.8
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. X. Holes of specific residue
rows.statement_text: For all integers $\kappa, \ell \geq 0$, there exists $c \geq 0$ such that every graph with chromatic number greater than $c$ contains either a complete subgraph on $\kappa$ vertices or holes of $\ell$ consecutive lengths.
rows.context_text: The authors proved in [14] (a prior paper in this series) that every triangle-free graph with sufficiently large chromatic number contains holes of $\ell$ consecutive lengths (result 1.7 of the present paper). Conjecture 1.8 extends this to graphs with clique number bounded by any $\kappa$. The conjecture remains open; this paper makes a partial step by proving 1.9, which guarantees holes of two consecutive lengths both greater than $\ell$.
rows.source_text: For all integers $\kappa, \ell \geq 0$, there exists $c \geq 0$ such that every graph with chromatic number greater than $c$ contains either a complete subgraph on $\kappa$ vertices or holes of $\ell$ consecutive lengths.
rows.class_notes: Clique of size kappa or holes of ell consecutive lengths; entirely standard finite vocabulary.

rows.title: Problem 3
rows.paper_title: Triangle-free graphs of tree-width t are ceil((t + 3)/2)-colorable
rows.statement_text: For integers $k \geq 4$ and $t \geq k - 1$, what is the maximum chromatic number of $K_k$-free graphs of tree-width at most $t$?
rows.context_text: Theorem 2 establishes that $g(t,k) > \bigl(1 - \frac{1}{2^{k-2}}\bigr)t$ for all $t \geq 0$ and $k \geq 2$, providing a lower bound on the maximum chromatic number. The case $k = 3$ (triangle-free) is completely resolved by Theorem 1, which gives the tight bound $\lceil(t+3)/2\rceil$, but the question remains open for $k \geq 4$.
rows.source_text: For integers $k \geq 4$ and $t \geq k - 1$, what is the maximum chromatic number of $K_k$-free graphs of tree-width at most $t$?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the maximum chromatic number' — determine-the-value question, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Could be salvaged as a bounded statement once a target value g(t,k) is conjectured, but none is given.

rows.title: Problem 1.2
rows.paper_title: Embedding Quartic Eulerian Digraphs on the Plane
rows.statement_text: Determine the complete set of obstructions for diplanar quartic Eulerian digraphs.
rows.context_text: The paper studies the partial order on quartic Eulerian digraphs defined by cycle removal followed by suppression of degree-2 vertices. Since diplanarity is hereditary under this order (Lemma 1.1), it is natural to seek the minimal non-diplanar elements (obstructions). The paper makes partial progress, classifying all obstructions containing parallel arcs, but the complete list remains open.
rows.source_text: Determine the complete set of obstructions for diplanar quartic Eulerian digraphs.
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the complete set of obstructions' — an open-ended classification task, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (error term in Proposition 1.4)
rows.paper_title: $H$-free subgraphs of dense graphs maximizing the number of cliques and their blow-ups
rows.statement_text: We believe that the error term $o(n^2)$ in Proposition 1.4 can be improved to $O(n^{2-\delta})$ for some $\delta := \delta(H)$.
rows.context_text: Proposition 1.4 shows that for every graph $H$ with $\chi(H) = k$ (not necessarily edge critical), any $H$-free subgraph of a sufficiently dense graph $G$ maximizing copies of $K_m(t)$ can be made $(k-1)$-colorable by deleting $o(n^2)$ edges. The authors note that Theorems 1.2 and 1.3 cannot be directly generalized to non-edge-critical $H$ since one can add an edge to any $(k-1)$-partite graph without creating a copy of such $H$, but they believe the $o(n^2)$ deletion bound in Proposition 1.4 is not tight.
rows.source_text: We believe that the error term $o(n^2)$ in Proposition 1.4 can be improved to $O(n^{2-\delta})$ for some $\delta := \delta(H)$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1706.05642 — Informal Conjecture (error term in Proposition 1.4)
rows.class_notes: Refers to internal Proposition 1.4 whose exact setup (K_m(t)-maximizing H-free subgraphs of dense G) is only summarized, not fully specified; recovery flagged.

rows.title: Open Question on Number of Tight Skew Partitions
rows.paper_title: Colouring perfect graphs with bounded clique number
rows.statement_text: We do not know how many tight skew partitions there can really be; the authors do not know of a graph with more than linearly many tight skew partitions.
rows.context_text: The paper shows there are at most $n^3 \log n$ tight skew partitions in an $n$-vertex graph (improving the $n^4$ bound from the Kennedy–Reed list), because for every tight skew partition $(A,B)$ the set $B$ appears at least $k_2^2$ times in the Kennedy–Reed output, where $k_2$ is the size of the second-largest anticomponent of $G[B]$. The true maximum is unknown.
rows.source_text: We do not know how many tight skew partitions there can really be; the authors do not know of a graph with more than linearly many tight skew partitions.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1707.03747 — Open Question on Number of Tight Skew Partitions
rows.defer_reason: Meta statement of ignorance ('we do not know how many'); no single proposition, and 'tight skew partition' is paper-local Kennedy-Reed machinery.
rows.class_notes: An extractable candidate is 'every n-vertex graph has O(n) tight skew partitions' but the record does not commit to it.

rows.title: Open question: additive approximation for triangle-free graphs
rows.paper_title: Additive non-approximability of chromatic number in proper minor-closed classes
rows.statement_text: Does there exist $\alpha \geq 0$ such that for every proper minor-closed class $\mathcal{G}$, the chromatic number of triangle-free graphs in $\mathcal{G}$ can be approximated in polynomial time within additive error $\alpha$?
rows.context_text: Forbidding triangles makes coloring more tractable for embedded graphs (all planar graphs are 3-colorable and 3-colorability in any fixed surface is linear-time decidable). The authors show forbidding cliques of size 4 is insufficient (Theorem 6), but explicitly state the triangle-free case remains open.
rows.source_text: Does there exist $\alpha \geq 0$ such that for every proper minor-closed class $\mathcal{G}$, the chromatic number of triangle-free graphs in $\mathcal{G}$ can be approximated in polynomial time within additive error $\alpha$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1707.03888 — Open question: additive approximation for triangle-free graphs
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no question but requires poly-time approximation-algorithm vocabulary over minor-closed classes.

rows.title: Open question: better additive approximation for $K_{4k_0+1}$-minor-free graphs
rows.paper_title: Additive non-approximability of chromatic number in proper minor-closed classes
rows.statement_text: Is there a polynomial-time additive approximation algorithm for the chromatic number of $K_{4k_0+1}$-minor-free graphs with additive error strictly between $k_0/4$ (the lower bound of Corollary 4) and $k_0 - 2$ (the upper bound of Kawarabayashi et al.)?
rows.context_text: Kawarabayashi et al. showed the chromatic number of $K_k$-minor-free graphs can be approximated additively up to $k-2$. Corollary 4 of this paper establishes that an additive error of $k_0 - 1$ is NP-hard to achieve, leaving a gap of roughly a factor of 4 between the best known algorithm and the hardness bound.
rows.source_text: Is there a polynomial-time additive approximation algorithm for the chromatic number of $K_{4k_0+1}$-minor-free graphs with additive error strictly between $k_0/4$ (the lower bound of Corollary 4) and $k_0 - 2$ (the upper bound of Kawarabayashi et al.)?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1707.03888 — Open question: better additive approximation for $K_{4k_0+1}$-minor-free graphs
rows.defer_reason: Close-the-gap question between an internal Corollary 4 lower bound and a cited upper bound; paper-local constant k_0 undefined in the record.

rows.title: Complexity of Independent Feedback Vertex Set for H-free graphs (linear forest cases)
rows.paper_title: Independent Feedback Vertex Set for $P_5$-free Graphs
rows.statement_text: Only the cases where $H$ is a linear forest, that is, a disjoint union of paths, remain open for the complexity classification of \textsc{Independent Feedback Vertex Set} on $H$-free graphs. In particular, the case where $H$ is a single path $P_h$ with $h \geq 6$ has not yet been resolved.
rows.context_text: The authors prove NP-completeness when $H$ contains a claw or cycle, and polynomial-time solvability for $P_4$-free and $P_5$-free graphs, completing those cases. This leaves all linear-forest cases open toward a full complexity dichotomy for $H$-free graphs.
rows.source_text: Only the cases where $H$ is a linear forest, that is, a disjoint union of paths, remain open for the complexity classification of \textsc{Independent Feedback Vertex Set} on $H$-free graphs. In particular, the case where $H$ is a single path $P_h$ with $h \geq 6$ has not yet been resolved.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1707.09402 — Complexity of Independent Feedback Vertex Set for H-free graphs (linear forest cases)
rows.defer_reason: Asks to complete a complexity dichotomy (P vs NP-complete for each linear forest H), not a single proposition with a stated answer.

rows.title: Conjecture 3.5
rows.paper_title: Domination and fractional domination in digraphs
rows.statement_text: There is an integer $\ell$ such that if $G$ is a directed-triangle-free digraph with $\alpha(D) = \alpha$, then $\gamma(G) \leq \alpha^{\ell}$.
rows.context_text: The same authors previously conjectured in [8] that the dichromatic number $\vec{\chi}(G)$ of a directed-triangle-free digraph can be bounded as a polynomial function of $\alpha(G)$. Because $\gamma(G) \leq \alpha(G) \cdot \vec{\chi}(G)$ (Observation 3.4), Conjecture 3.5 is presented as a necessary consequence: if the dichromatic-number conjecture holds, so must this domination bound.
rows.source_text: There is an integer $\ell$ such that if $G$ is a directed-triangle-free digraph with $\alpha(D) = \alpha$, then $\gamma(G) \leq \alpha^{\ell}$.
rows.class_notes: Standard digraph vocabulary (domination number, independence number, directed-triangle-free); existential polynomial exponent needs a bounded wrapper.

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: Clustered Colouring in Minor-Closed Classes
rows.statement_text: For every graph $H$, $\chi^*(\mathcal{M}_H) \leq 2\,\mathrm{td}(H) - 2$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 3 establishes the matching lower bound $\chi^*(\mathcal{M}_{H_k}) \geq 2k-2$ for graphs $H_k$ with $\mathrm{td}(H_k) = k$, showing that defective and clustered chromatic numbers can differ. The authors conjecture this lower bound is tight, and note the conjecture is proved in Section 4 for the special case $\mathrm{td}(H) = 3$.
rows.source_text: For every graph $H$, $\chi^*(\mathcal{M}_H) \leq 2\,\mathrm{td}(H) - 2$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 4 from arXiv:1708.02370 — that $\chi^*(\mathcal{M}_H) \leq 2\,\mathrm{td}(H) - 2$ for every graph $H$ — has been proved for the special cases of minor-closed classes with bounded treedepth and bounded pathwidth by Norin, Scott, and Wood (arXiv:2012.05554, 2022), with the pathwidth bound shown to be best possible. The general statement remains open. The internal reference arXiv:2508.14332 is a false match and concerns the unrelated weak coarse Menger conjecture.
rows.class_notes: Needs clustered chromatic number of a minor-closed class and treedepth as primitives; both buildable finite notions.

rows.title: Question 1.1
rows.paper_title: Ramsey-nice families of graphs
rows.statement_text: Is it true that for any finite family of graphs $\mathcal{F}$ that contains at least one forest, there exists a constant $k_0 = k_0(\mathcal{F})$ such that $\mathcal{F}$ is $k$-nice for all $k \geq k_0$?
rows.context_text: It is easy to see that if $\mathcal{F}$ contains no forest then it is not $k$-nice for any $k$. The question asks whether a converse holds: every family containing at least one forest is eventually $k$-nice. The paper proves this for all families containing a graph with at most 2 edges and for several two-graph families of 3-edge connected graphs.
rows.source_text: Is it true that for any finite family of graphs $\mathcal{F}$ that contains at least one forest, there exists a constant $k_0 = k_0(\mathcal{F})$ such that $\mathcal{F}$ is $k$-nice for all $k \geq k_0$?
rows.class_notes: Precise question but the key notion 'k-nice family' is never defined in the record; recoverable from the paper.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.5
rows.paper_title: Ramsey-nice families of graphs
rows.statement_text: Any finite family of graphs $\mathcal{F}$ that contains at least one forest is $k$-nice for infinitely many integers $k$.
rows.context_text: This is posed as a weaker version of Question 1.1. Theorem 1.4 shows that $\mathcal{F}_7 = \{K_3, P_4, S_3\}$ is $k$-nice for infinitely many $k$, and Theorem 1.6 gives further support for families containing a star $K_{1,r+1}$.
rows.source_text: Any finite family of graphs $\mathcal{F}$ that contains at least one forest is $k$-nice for infinitely many integers $k$.
rows.class_notes: Weaker sibling of Question 1.1 in the same paper; 'k-nice' undefined in the record.

rows.title: Informal Question (correct exponential constant in r(n,m))
rows.paper_title: Popular progression differences in vector spaces II
rows.statement_text: Is $3$, the lower bound on $r(n,m)$ given by considering a random set, the right exponential constant? That is, does $r(n,m) = N^{1-\varepsilon_m}$ hold with the same exponential base $3$ as in the random lower bound $r(n,m) \geq N^{1-(m+1)3^{-m}}$, rather than the base $C \approx 13.901$ appearing in the upper bound $r(n,m) \leq (1+o(1))N^{1-C^{-m}}$?
rows.context_text: The lower and upper bounds for $r(n,m)$ (the maximum size of a subset of $\mathbb{F}_3^n$ containing no affine $m$-dimensional subspace) both have the form $N^{1-\varepsilon_m}$ with $\varepsilon_m \to 0$ exponentially fast in $m$, but with different exponential constants: $3$ in the random lower bound and $C \approx 13.901$ in the upper bound derived from the cap set supersaturation result. The authors note that resolving this question is closely tied to whether $n_3(\alpha,\beta)$ is small enough for $\beta = \alpha^{3+o(1)}$, and their main result provides evidence that the approach via popular differences likely cannot resolve it.
rows.source_text: Is $3$, the lower bound on $r(n,m)$ given by considering a random set, the right exponential constant? That is, does $r(n,m) = N^{1-\varepsilon_m}$ hold with the same exponential base $3$ as in the random lower bound $r(n,m) \geq N^{1-(m+1)3^{-m}}$, rather than the base $C \approx 13.901$ appearing in the upper bound $r(n,m) \leq (1+o(1))N^{1-C^{-m}}$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1708.08486 — Informal Question (correct exponential constant in r(n,m))
rows.defer_reason: Additive combinatorics over F_3^n (affine-subspace-free sets / cap-set constants), not graph theory.

rows.title: Conjecture (Theorem 2 extends to primes p < 19)
rows.paper_title: Popular progression differences in vector spaces II
rows.statement_text: The bounds on the tower height of $n_p(\alpha,\beta)$ given in Theorem 2 should also hold for all primes $p < 19$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 2 is proved only for primes $p \geq 19$. The authors note that the upper bounds in Theorem 2 hold for all primes, and the lower bound holds when $\varepsilon = \alpha^3 - \beta$ is small. The obstacle for $p < 19$ in the large-$\varepsilon$ regime is that the best known bounds on the largest subset of $\mathbb{F}_p^n$ without a three-term arithmetic progression are not strong enough to imply the required lower bound estimate in that case.
rows.source_text: The bounds on the tower height of $n_p(\alpha,\beta)$ given in Theorem 2 should also hold for all primes $p < 19$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1708.08486 — Conjecture (Theorem 2 extends to primes p < 19)
rows.defer_reason: Additive combinatorics (popular progression differences in F_p^n); also leans on internal Theorem 2.

rows.title: Open Question on s((Z/kZ)^n) for fixed k
rows.paper_title: Erdős-Ginzburg-Ziv constants by avoiding three-term arithmetic progressions
rows.statement_text: It remains an interesting question to estimate $s((\mathbb{Z}/k\mathbb{Z})^n)$ when $k$ is fixed and $n$ is large.
rows.context_text: Alon and Dubiner proved $s((\mathbb{Z}/k\mathbb{Z})^n) \leq (cn\log n)^n k$ for some absolute constant $c$, showing that for any fixed $n$ the quantity grows linearly in $k$. Elsholtz obtained lower bounds $s((\mathbb{Z}/k\mathbb{Z})^n) \geq 2.08^n$ for odd $k\geq 3$ and large $n$, but a large gap remains between these bounds and the upper bounds of Corollary 2 for fixed odd $k\geq 3$ and large $n$.
rows.source_text: It remains an interesting question to estimate $s((\mathbb{Z}/k\mathbb{Z})^n)$ when $k$ is fixed and $n$ is large.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1708.09100 — Open Question on s((Z/kZ)^n) for fixed k
rows.defer_reason: Zero-sum additive combinatorics (Erdos-Ginzburg-Ziv constants), not graph theory; also an 'estimate' request rather than a proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.1
rows.paper_title: Induced 2-degenerate Subgraphs of Triangle-free Planar Graphs
rows.statement_text: Every triangle-free planar graph contains an induced 2-degenerate subgraph on at least $\frac{7}{8}$ of its vertices.
rows.context_text: The authors introduce this as the central open problem motivating the paper, noting it would be tight for the cube (the unique 3-regular triangle-free planar graph on 8 vertices). The paper's main theorems (Theorems 1.2 and 1.3) prove weaker bounds of $\frac{4}{5}$ and $\frac{7}{8}n - 18n_3$ respectively as partial progress.
rows.source_text: Every triangle-free planar graph contains an induced 2-degenerate subgraph on at least $\frac{7}{8}$ of its vertices.
rows.class_notes: Standard vocabulary (triangle-free, planar, induced 2-degenerate subgraph); tight for the cube.

rows.title: Informal belief: 5/6 bound attainable
rows.paper_title: Induced 2-degenerate Subgraphs of Triangle-free Planar Graphs
rows.statement_text: We believe the argument we use can be strengthened to give a bound $\frac{5}{6}$, however the technical issues are substantial and since we do not see this as a viable way to prove Conjecture 1.1 in full, we prefer to present the easier argument giving the bound $\frac{4}{5}$.
rows.context_text: Immediately after stating Theorem 1.2 (the $\frac{4}{5}$ bound), the authors remark that their method is likely improvable to $\frac{5}{6}$ but that pursuing this is not a practical route to proving the full $\frac{7}{8}$ conjecture.
rows.source_text: We believe the argument we use can be strengthened to give a bound $\frac{5}{6}$, however the technical issues are substantial and since we do not see this as a viable way to prove Conjecture 1.1 in full, we prefer to present the easier argument giving the bound $\frac{4}{5}$.
rows.defer_reason: Meta remark about strengthening the authors' own argument; the implicit 5/6 bound is anyway subsumed by the 7/8 conjecture recorded as axv_1709_04036_00.
rows.class_notes: If desired, the 5/6 bound could be extracted as a clean B2 weakening of Conjecture 1.1.

rows.title: Question 1.4
rows.paper_title: A polynomial bound for the arithmetic $k$-cycle removal lemma in vector spaces
rows.statement_text: What is the smallest possible exponent $C_{p,k}$ for which Theorem 1.3 is true?
rows.context_text: The paper proves Theorem 1.3 with exponent $C_{p,k} = \frac{k-2}{c_{p,3}} + 1$, and shows via constructions of $k$-colored sum-free sets that the smallest possible exponent must be at least $\frac{k-2}{c_{p,k}} + 1$. The achieved exponent is within a factor of $O(\log p)$ from this lower bound (independently of $k$), but the exact optimum remains open.
rows.source_text: What is the smallest possible exponent $C_{p,k}$ for which Theorem 1.3 is true?
rows.status_semantics: Question 1.4 asks for the exact optimal exponent $C_{p,k}$ in Theorem 1.3. The source paper achieves $C_{p,k} = \frac{k-2}{c_{p,3}}+1$ and shows the lower bound is at least $\frac{k-2}{c_{p,k}}+1$, leaving a gap of $O(\log p)$. A direct follow-up by L.M. Lovász (arXiv:1804.08837, 2019) tightens the k-colored sum-free bound in $\mathbb{Z}_m^n$—showing the lower-bound construction is essentially sharp—thereby pinning down $c_{p,k}$ precisely and sharpening the lower bound on $C_{p,k}$. However, closing the remaining gap between the upper bound (which uses $c_{p,3}$) and the now-exact lower bound (using $c_{p,k}$) remains open.
rows.defer_reason: Arithmetic removal lemma in vector spaces (additive combinatorics), and a 'what is the optimal exponent' question tied to internal Theorem 1.3.

rows.title: Classification of planar graphs by cop number
rows.paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers
rows.statement_text: Classify which planar graphs have cop number $i$, for $1 \leq i \leq 3$.
rows.context_text: By Theorem 1, every connected planar graph has cop number 1, 2, or 3. Cop-win graphs are precisely the dismantlable ones, which may eventually aid in classifying planar cop-win graphs, but a complete classification for all three cop number values remains open.
rows.source_text: Classify which planar graphs have cop number $i$, for $1 \leq i \leq 3$.

rows.title: Classification of outerplanar graphs by cop number

rows.statement_text: Classify which outerplanar graphs are $1$-cop-win and which are $2$-cop-win.
rows.context_text: By Theorem 3, every outerplanar graph has cop number at most 2. The smallest outerplanar graph with cop number 2 is the four cycle, but a full classification of outerplanar graphs by cop number remains open.

rows.title: Tightness of linear capture time bound for planar graphs
rows.paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers
rows.statement_text: Is the bound $\mathrm{capt}_3(G) \leq 2n$ tight for planar graphs of order $n$? Does there exist a planar graph $G$ of order $n$ with $\mathrm{capt}(G) > n$?
rows.context_text: Theorem 5 establishes $\mathrm{capt}_3(G) \leq 2n$ for planar graphs of order $n$, improving the earlier $O(n^2)$ bound of Theorem 4. Whether this linear bound is tight is unknown; even finding a single planar graph with capture time exceeding $n$ remains open.
rows.source_text: Is the bound $\mathrm{capt}_3(G) \leq 2n$ tight for planar graphs of order $n$? Does there exist a planar graph $G$ of order $n$ with $\mathrm{capt}(G) > n$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1709.09050 — Tightness of linear capture time bound for planar graphs

rows.title: Capture time bounds for higher genus graphs
rows.paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers
rows.statement_text: What are the bounds on the capture time for graphs of higher genus? If Schroeder's conjecture holds and $\gamma(G) = g$, is it true that $\mathrm{capt}_k(G) = O(n^{g+4})$ for every $k \geq g+3$?
rows.context_text: The paper identifies bounding the capture time of graphs embedded on surfaces as an interesting open question. If Schroeder's conjecture holds, giving $c(G) \leq g+3$ for genus-$g$ graphs, then a natural bound on $\mathrm{capt}_k(G)$ would follow for $k \geq g+3$.
rows.source_text: What are the bounds on the capture time for graphs of higher genus? If Schroeder's conjecture holds and $\gamma(G) = g$, is it true that $\mathrm{capt}_k(G) = O(n^{g+4})$ for every $k \geq g+3$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1709.09050 — Capture time bounds for higher genus graphs
rows.defer_reason: Open-ended 'what are the bounds' question, conditional on Schroeder's conjecture, with a loose O(n^{g+4}) target.

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: Islands in minor-closed classes. I. Bounded treewidth and separators
rows.statement_text: A minor-closed class of graphs $\mathcal{G}$ satisfies $\mathrm{col}^\star(\mathcal{G}) \leq t$ if and only if there exists $m \geq 1$ such that $K_{t,m} \notin \mathcal{G}$ and $I_{t-1}+P_m \notin \mathcal{G}$.
rows.context_text: Observation 3 shows that any class $\mathcal{G}$ with $\mathrm{col}^\star(\mathcal{G}) \leq t$ can contain only finitely many graphs of the form $K_{t,m}$ or $I_{t-1}+P_m$; the conjecture asserts that this necessary condition is also sufficient for minor-closed classes. Theorem 7 confirms the conjecture for the special case of minor-closed classes of bounded treewidth, and Conjecture 4 would imply $\chi^\star_{\ell}(\mathcal{G}) = \mathrm{col}^\star(\mathcal{G})$ for every minor-closed class.
rows.source_text: A minor-closed class of graphs $\mathcal{G}$ satisfies $\mathrm{col}^\star(\mathcal{G}) \leq t$ if and only if there exists $m \geq 1$ such that $K_{t,m} \notin \mathcal{G}$ and $I_{t-1}+P_m \notin \mathcal{G}$.
rows.class_notes: Precise iff statement but the parameter col*(G) (island/coloring-number variant) and I_{t-1}+P_m notation are paper-local and undefined in the record.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (constant-size M)
rows.paper_title: On classes of graphs with strongly sublinear separators
rows.statement_text: I conjecture that there actually always exists a balanced separator $C \cup M$ of $G$ with $q(C) \leq q(V(G))/\ell$ for some set $M$ of constant size (dependent on the class and $\ell$, but not on $|V(G)|$).
rows.context_text: Theorem 5 guarantees a weighted balanced separator $C \cup M$ with $q(C) \leq q(V(G))/\ell$, but the bound on $|M|$ is only polylogarithmic in $n$ for graphs from a class with polynomial $\omega$-expansion. The author conjectures this polylogarithmic dependence on $n$ can be eliminated entirely, yielding a set $M$ of size depending only on the graph class and $\ell$.
rows.source_text: I conjecture that there actually always exists a balanced separator $C \cup M$ of $G$ with $q(C) \leq q(V(G))/\ell$ for some set $M$ of constant size (dependent on the class and $\ell$, but not on $|V(G)|$).
rows.class_notes: Relies on the paper's Theorem 5 setup (weight q, class with polynomial omega-expansion, balanced separator C u M) not fully defined in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.2
rows.paper_title: A tight Erdős-Pósa function for wheel minors
rows.statement_text: For every planar graph $H$, the Erdős-Pósa property holds for $H$-models with a $O(k \log k)$ bounding function.
rows.context_text: Chekuri and Chuzhoy established a $O(k \log^c k)$ bounding function for planar $H$-models with $c$ at least a double-digit integer; their approach necessarily yields $c \geq 2$. Since a $\Omega(k \log k)$ lower bound already holds for any planar $H$ containing a cycle, proving this conjecture would completely settle the growth rate of the Erdős-Pósa function for all planar graphs $H$. The paper proves the conjecture for $H = W_t$ (wheels) as its main result.
rows.source_text: For every planar graph $H$, the Erdős-Pósa property holds for $H$-models with a $O(k \log k)$ bounding function.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.2 was resolved by Cames van Batenburg, Huynh, Joret, and Raymond in arXiv:1807.04969, submitted just eight days after the journal publication date of the source paper. They prove that for every planar graph H, the Erdős-Pósa property holds for H-models with a c(H)·k log k bounding function, which is best possible up to the constant for any H containing a cycle. The result was published in Advances in Combinatorics in 2019, fully settling the growth rate of the Erdős-Pósa function for all planar graphs H.
rows.class_notes: Standard Erdos-Posa vocabulary for H-minor models; O(k log k) needs a constant-wrapped bound. Marked solved (right-polarity target).

rows.title: Conjecture 1.4
rows.paper_title: A tight Erdős-Pósa function for wheel minors
rows.statement_text: There is a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for all integers $r, k \geq 1$, every graph $G$ of treewidth at least $f(r) \cdot k \log(k + 1)$ has $k$ vertex-disjoint subgraphs $G_1, \ldots, G_k$, each of treewidth at least $r$.
rows.context_text: This is the treewidth-level analogue of Conjecture 1.2: it asserts that $c' = 1$ suffices in Theorem 1.3 of Chekuri and Chuzhoy (at least ignoring the precise dependence on $r$), whereas their proof only gives $c' \geq 1$. The authors note it is implied by Conjecture 1.2 by taking $H$ to be the $r \times r$ grid.
rows.source_text: There is a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for all integers $r, k \geq 1$, every graph $G$ of treewidth at least $f(r) \cdot k \log(k + 1)$ has $k$ vertex-disjoint subgraphs $G_1, \ldots, G_k$, each of treewidth at least $r$.
rows.status_semantics: Errata (Aboulker ranked list): treewidth version follows from Conjecture 1.2 (Cames van Batenburg–Huynh–Joret–Raymond) + the grid-minor theorem.
rows.class_notes: Fully standard vocabulary (treewidth, vertex-disjoint subgraphs); implied by Conjecture 1.2 of the same paper (axv_1710_06282_00) via grids.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture on exact asymptotics of maxcode_L for even L
rows.paper_title: List-decodable zero-rate codes
rows.statement_text: We believe that in fact $\mathrm{maxcode}_L(\varepsilon) = c_L \varepsilon^{-1} + O(1)$ for even $L$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 2 establishes the two-sided bound $c_L \varepsilon^{-1} + O(1) \leq \mathrm{maxcode}_L(\varepsilon) = O(\varepsilon^{-1})$ for every even $L \geq 2$, where $c_L = 2^{-\lfloor L/2 \rfloor}\binom{L}{\lfloor L/2 \rfloor}$ comes from Theorem 1. The authors conjecture that the lower bound is tight up to an additive constant, i.e., that the exact leading coefficient is $c_L$.
rows.source_text: We believe that in fact $\mathrm{maxcode}_L(\varepsilon) = c_L \varepsilon^{-1} + O(1)$ for even $L$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1710.10663 — Informal Conjecture on exact asymptotics of maxcode_L for even L
rows.defer_reason: List-decodable zero-rate codes (coding theory over Hamming space), not graph/hypergraph theory.

rows.title: Conjecture 8
rows.paper_title: Notes on Cops and Robber game on graphs
rows.statement_text: $c(g) = g^{\frac{1}{2}+o(1)}$ and $ec(g) = g^{\frac{1}{2}+o(1)}$. In other words, for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $g_0$ such that for every $g \geq g_0$, $$g^{\frac{1}{2}-\varepsilon} < c(g) < g^{\frac{1}{2}+\varepsilon}$$ and $$g^{\frac{1}{2}-\varepsilon} < ec(g) < g^{\frac{1}{2}+\varepsilon}.$$
rows.context_text: After establishing via random graphs that $c(g) \geq g^{1/2-o(1)}$, the author conjectures the true growth rate of the cop number (and nonorientable cop number) as a function of genus is exactly $g^{1/2}$. The conjecture has been unchallenged as of the writing of the notes.
rows.source_text: $c(g) = g^{\frac{1}{2}+o(1)}$ and $ec(g) = g^{\frac{1}{2}+o(1)}$. In other words, for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $g_0$ such that for every $g \geq g_0$, $$g^{\frac{1}{2}-\varepsilon} < c(g) < g^{\frac{1}{2}+\varepsilon}$$ and $$g^{\frac{1}{2}-\varepsilon} < ec(g) < g^{\frac{1}{2}+\varepsilon}.$$
rows.class_notes: c(g), ec(g) = max cop number over graphs of (non)orientable genus g; needs genus/embedding layer plus the epsilon-g_0 asymptotic wrapper spelled out in the statement.

rows.title: Question (Riemannian surfaces): cop number bounded by genus
rows.paper_title: Notes on Cops and Robber game on graphs
rows.statement_text: Is the cop number of surfaces bounded in terms of the genus?
rows.context_text: In Section 3 the author introduces a continuous Cops and Robbers game on Riemannian surfaces and poses several basic open questions. The first asks whether the cop number of a Riemannian surface, defined via players moving at unit velocity, is a finite function of the topological genus.
rows.source_text: Is the cop number of surfaces bounded in terms of the genus?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1710.11281 — Question (Riemannian surfaces): cop number bounded by genus
rows.status_semantics: The question asks whether the cop number of a Riemannian surface is a finite function of its topological genus. Mohar himself answered this affirmatively in a 2024 Canadian Journal of Mathematics paper extending the cops-and-robbers game to compact geodesic spaces: for a compact geodesic surface of genus g, the cop number is O(g) (upper bound) and Omega(sqrt(g)) (lower bound), with three cops sufficing for genus 0 or 1.
rows.defer_reason: Continuous pursuit-evasion game on Riemannian surfaces, not graph theory.
rows.class_notes: Recorded as solved.

rows.title: Question (Riemannian surfaces): cop converging to robber
rows.paper_title: Notes on Cops and Robber game on graphs
rows.statement_text: If the cops have a strategy to catch the robber, could it in fact be achieved that one of the cops comes arbitrarily close to the robber (or even onto the same point) in the continuous version of the game?
rows.context_text: In the continuous game on a Riemannian surface a robber is 'caught' when a cop comes within distance $\frac{1}{2}$. The author asks whether a winning strategy can always be strengthened so that a cop actually converges to the robber's exact position.
rows.source_text: If the cops have a strategy to catch the robber, could it in fact be achieved that one of the cops comes arbitrarily close to the robber (or even onto the same point) in the continuous version of the game?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1710.11281 — Question (Riemannian surfaces): cop converging to robber
rows.status_semantics: The question asks whether a winning cop strategy on a Riemannian surface (where capture means coming within distance 1/2) can always be strengthened so that a cop converges to the robber's exact position. For hyperbolic surfaces (constant negative curvature), arXiv:2402.05753 (2024) proves a positive partial answer: two cops have a strategy to come arbitrarily close to the robber, regardless of the genus. The general question for arbitrary Riemannian surfaces appears to remain open.
rows.defer_reason: Continuous Cops and Robbers on Riemannian surfaces (real geometry/analysis), not graph theory.

rows.title: Question (Riemannian surfaces): worst surfaces of fixed genus
rows.paper_title: Notes on Cops and Robber game on graphs
rows.statement_text: Which surfaces of a fixed genus are the worst (with respect to the cop number)?
rows.context_text: Among all Riemannian surfaces sharing the same topological genus, the author asks which geometric realization maximises the cop number, as part of a broader investigation of the continuous Cops and Robbers game on surfaces.
rows.source_text: Which surfaces of a fixed genus are the worst (with respect to the cop number)?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1710.11281 — Question (Riemannian surfaces): worst surfaces of fixed genus
rows.defer_reason: Riemannian-surface game question, and 'which surfaces are worst' is also an open-ended extremal request.

rows.title: Question (Riemannian surfaces): supremum of cop number for geometrically bounded surfaces
rows.paper_title: Notes on Cops and Robber game on graphs
rows.statement_text: What is the supremum of the cop numbers taken over all surfaces whose cut locus is at least $1$ and whose area is at most $\alpha$? Is it bounded?
rows.context_text: The author asks whether restricting to Riemannian surfaces with a positive lower bound on the injectivity radius and an upper bound on total area is enough to guarantee a finite supremum for the cop number.
rows.source_text: What is the supremum of the cop numbers taken over all surfaces whose cut locus is at least $1$ and whose area is at most $\alpha$? Is it bounded?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1710.11281 — Question (Riemannian surfaces): supremum of cop number for geometrically bounded surfaces
rows.status_semantics: No paper appears to directly resolve the question for arbitrary Riemannian surfaces with positive injectivity radius (cut locus ≥1) and bounded area. Two closely related developments have appeared since 2017: arXiv:2205.11633 establishes that the cop number of any compact geodesic surface is bounded as a function of its genus (c=3 for low genus, c=O(g) in general), and arXiv:2402.05753 shows that two cops suffice (in the sense of coming arbitrarily close to the robber) on hyperbolic surfaces of any genus. Because bounded injectivity radius together with bounded area classically forces bounded genus, the result of 2205.11633 indirectly supports an affirmative answer to the question, but this connection does not appear to be made explicit in either paper.
rows.defer_reason: Supremum of cop numbers over Riemannian surfaces with injectivity-radius/area constraints; continuous geometry, not graph theory.

rows.title: Question (Riemannian surfaces): constant curvature surfaces
rows.paper_title: Notes on Cops and Robber game on graphs
rows.statement_text: Are the surfaces of constant curvature any simpler than general Riemannian surfaces with respect to the Cops and Robber game?
rows.context_text: The author asks whether the special geometric structure of constant-curvature surfaces (spherical, Euclidean, or hyperbolic) renders the continuous Cops and Robbers game more tractable than on general Riemannian surfaces.
rows.source_text: Are the surfaces of constant curvature any simpler than general Riemannian surfaces with respect to the Cops and Robber game?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1710.11281 — Question (Riemannian surfaces): constant curvature surfaces
rows.status_semantics: Mohar's question asks whether constant-curvature surfaces (spherical, Euclidean, hyperbolic) admit a simpler Cops and Robber game than general Riemannian surfaces. The 2024 paper arXiv:2402.05753 by Iršič, Mohar, and Wesolek gives a strong affirmative answer for the hyperbolic case: two cops suffice on any hyperbolic surface regardless of genus. By contrast, the cop number of general compact geodesic surfaces grows as O(g) with genus (arXiv:2205.11633). Constant negative curvature is thus confirmed to impose a dramatic simplification; the spherical and Euclidean cases remain less explicitly treated in the literature.
rows.defer_reason: 'Are constant-curvature surfaces any simpler' is a vague meta question about a continuous Riemannian game, not graph theory.

rows.title: Open Problem: Characterization of parameters satisfying (CC)
rows.paper_title: On generalized choice and coloring numbers
rows.statement_text: Characterize the (hereditary, connected) graph parameters $f$ satisfying property (CC): for all integers $p$ and $s$, there exist two integers $p'$ and $s'$ such that $\mathrm{col}_{f,p'}(G) \leq s'$ for every graph $G$ satisfying $\chi^\ell_{f,p}(G) \leq s$.
rows.context_text: The authors show that the fan parameter does not satisfy (CC) (Lemma 2), and provide two sufficient conditions (via property (CD) and via Lemma 4) covering all parameters previously studied in the literature, but explicitly state they were unable to fully describe the class of parameters satisfying (CC). This leaves the characterization problem open.
rows.source_text: Characterize the (hereditary, connected) graph parameters $f$ satisfying property (CC): for all integers $p$ and $s$, there exist two integers $p'$ and $s'$ such that $\mathrm{col}_{f,p'}(G) \leq s'$ for every graph $G$ satisfying $\chi^\ell_{f,p}(G) \leq s$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1801.06824 — Open Problem: Characterization of parameters satisfying (CC)
rows.defer_reason: 'Characterize the graph parameters f satisfying (CC)' is a characterization request over a space of parameters, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.3
rows.paper_title: Separation choosability and dense bipartite induced subgraphs
