rows.statement_text: There is a function $x_1(d)$ that satisfies $x_1(d) \to \infty$ as $d \to \infty$ such that $\mathrm{ch}_{\mathrm{sep}}(G) \geq x_1(d)$ for any graph $G$ with minimum degree $d$.
rows.context_text: The authors prove Theorem 1.2 that separation choosability grows logarithmically in minimum degree for bipartite graphs, and ask whether the bipartiteness assumption can be dropped. The difficulty is that a bad list assignment with maximum separation on a graph does not necessarily retain maximum separation when edges are added, so the standard bipartite-subgraph reduction used for ordinary choosability fails.
rows.source_text: There is a function $x_1(d)$ that satisfies $x_1(d) \to \infty$ as $d \to \infty$ such that $\mathrm{ch}_{\mathrm{sep}}(G) \geq x_1(d)$ for any graph $G$ with minimum degree $d$.
rows.class_notes: Needs separation choosability ch_sep as a primitive (established list-coloring-with-separation notion, definable finitely).

rows.title: Conjecture 1.4
rows.paper_title: Separation choosability and dense bipartite induced subgraphs
rows.statement_text: There are functions $x_2(d)$ and $x_3(d)$ satisfying $x_2(d) \to \infty$ and $x_3(d) \to \infty$ as $d \to \infty$ such that any graph with minimum degree at least $d$ contains a complete subgraph on $x_2(d)$ vertices or a bipartite induced subgraph with minimum degree at least $x_3(d)$.
rows.context_text: This conjecture, if true, would imply Conjecture 1.3: since $\mathrm{ch}_{\mathrm{sep}}(K_{d+1}) \sim \sqrt{d}$ (Kratochvíl–Tuza–Voigt) and Theorem 1.2 handles the dense bipartite induced subgraph case, the two alternatives together would give a separation-choosability lower bound tending to infinity for any graph of large minimum degree.
rows.source_text: There are functions $x_2(d)$ and $x_3(d)$ satisfying $x_2(d) \to \infty$ and $x_3(d) \to \infty$ as $d \to \infty$ such that any graph with minimum degree at least $d$ contains a complete subgraph on $x_2(d)$ vertices or a bipartite induced subgraph with minimum degree at least $x_3(d)$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.4 from arXiv:1802.03727 — that any graph with minimum degree $d$ contains either a clique on $x_2(d)$ vertices or a bipartite induced subgraph with minimum degree $x_3(d)$ (both functions tending to infinity) — appears to remain open as a general statement. Kwan, Sudakov, and Tran (Combinatorica 2020, arXiv:1810.12144) proved that any $K_t$-free graph with minimum degree at least $d$ contains a bipartite induced subgraph of minimum degree at least $c_t \log d/\log\log d$, which confirms Conjecture 1.3 from the same source paper (the consequence that Conjecture 1.4 was designed to imply) but does not settle Conjecture 1.4 in full generality. No paper proving or disproving the general dichotomy was found in the indexed literature.
rows.class_notes: Esperet-Kang-Thomasse-style min-degree statement in fully standard vocabulary; 'functions tending to infinity' needs a for-all-c/exists-d wrapper.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.5
rows.paper_title: Separation choosability and dense bipartite induced subgraphs
rows.statement_text: There is a constant $C > 0$ such that any triangle-free graph with minimum degree at least $d$ contains a bipartite induced subgraph of minimum degree at least $C \log d$.
rows.context_text: This is a quantitative sharpening of Conjecture 1.4 restricted to triangle-free graphs; the logarithmic bound would be sharp up to the choice of $C$ (Theorem 3.7). The second part of the paper offers partial progress through Theorem 1.7 and several subcases proved in Section 3.
rows.source_text: There is a constant $C > 0$ such that any triangle-free graph with minimum degree at least $d$ contains a bipartite induced subgraph of minimum degree at least $C \log d$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.5 from arXiv:1802.03727 was first approached by Kwan, Letzter, Sudakov, and Tran (arXiv:1810.12144), who proved that any K_t-free graph with minimum degree d contains an induced bipartite subgraph of minimum degree at least c_t log d / log log d—nearly but not fully resolving the conjecture. The conjecture was then fully resolved by Martinsson (arXiv:2501.18238), who proved Harris' conjecture that triangle-free d-degenerate graphs have fractional chromatic number at most (4+o(1))d/ln d; the C log d bipartite induced subgraph bound follows as a direct consequence via the standard argument that low fractional chromatic number forces a bipartite induced subgraph of high minimum degree.
rows.class_notes: Solved (Kwan-Letzter-Sudakov-Tran); real constant C with log d needs a bounded wrapper.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.6
rows.paper_title: Separation choosability and dense bipartite induced subgraphs
rows.statement_text: There exist $d_0$ and $g_0$ such that any graph of girth at least $g_0$ with minimum degree at least $d_0$ contains a bipartite induced subgraph of minimum degree at least $3$.
rows.context_text: This is a further variation on Conjecture 1.4 using large girth instead of triangle-freeness. The weaker statement with 3 replaced by 2 (i.e., containing an even hole) is true with $g_0 = 4$ and $d_0 = 3$ by Radovanović–Vušković; detecting bipartite induced subgraphs of minimum degree at least 3 is NP-complete.
rows.source_text: There exist $d_0$ and $g_0$ such that any graph of girth at least $g_0$ with minimum degree at least $d_0$ contains a bipartite induced subgraph of minimum degree at least $3$.
rows.class_notes: Fully self-contained: girth, min degree, bipartite induced subgraph are all standard.

rows.title: Problem 1.3
rows.paper_title: Planar graphs without cycles of length 4 or 5 are (11:3)-colorable
rows.statement_text: What is the infimum of fractional chromatic numbers of planar graphs without cycles of length 4 or 5?
rows.context_text: The authors prove that every planar graph without cycles of length 4 or 5 is $(11:3)$-colorable, giving an upper bound of $\frac{11}{3}$ on the fractional chromatic number. They note the counterexample to Steinberg's conjecture from [4] is $(6:2)$-colorable, so the answer could be as low as $3$.
rows.source_text: What is the infimum of fractional chromatic numbers of planar graphs without cycles of length 4 or 5?
rows.status_semantics: The exact infimum of fractional chromatic numbers of planar graphs without cycles of length 4 or 5 remains open. The original upper bound of 11/3 from the source paper was subsequently improved to 7/2 by Wang (referenced in arXiv:2511.12914). A November 2025 paper (arXiv:2511.12914) further establishes that these graphs are (7m:2m)-DP-colorable for every positive integer m, confirming the 7/2 upper bound via DP-coloring. The lower bound is 3, so the infimum lies in [3, 7/2].
rows.defer_reason: asks for the exact infimum value, not a single proposition

rows.title: Informal conjecture on optimality of 11/3
rows.paper_title: Planar graphs without cycles of length 4 or 5 are (11:3)-colorable
rows.statement_text: It is not clear whether the constant $\frac{11}{3}$ from Theorem 1.1 is the best possible, and we suspect this is not the case.
rows.context_text: Immediately after stating Problem 1.3, the authors express their belief that $\frac{11}{3}$ is not the tight infimum of fractional chromatic numbers for planar graphs without cycles of length 4 or 5, motivating the open problem.
rows.source_text: It is not clear whether the constant $\frac{11}{3}$ from Theorem 1.1 is the best possible, and we suspect this is not the case.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1802.04179 — Informal conjecture on optimality of 11/3
rows.status_semantics: Dvořák and Hu proved every planar graph without cycles of length 4 or 5 has fractional chromatic number at most 11/3, and suspected this bound is not tight. This suspicion was confirmed by Xu and Zhu (arXiv:2511.12914, 2025), who proved such graphs are (7m:2m)-DP-colorable for every positive integer m, establishing an improved upper bound of 7/2 = 3.5 < 11/3 ≈ 3.667. The exact infimum of fractional chromatic numbers for this class remains open, currently bounded between 3 + 1/12 and 7/2.
rows.class_notes: Reads as: some c < 11/3 bounds chi_f for all planar graphs without 4- or 5-cycles; planarity layer needed.

rows.title: Open problem on sublinear round complexity
rows.paper_title: Distributed coloring in sparse graphs with fewer colors
rows.statement_text: It remains interesting to obtain a bound on the round complexity that is sublinear in $n$ regardless of the value of $d$.
rows.context_text: After noting that network decompositions can replace the $O(d^4 \log^3 n)$ round complexity of Theorem 1.3 by $d^3 2^{O(\sqrt{\log n})}$, the authors remark that these alternative bounds are unsatisfying. The problem of achieving a round complexity that is sublinear in $n$ independent of $d$ remains open.
rows.source_text: It remains interesting to obtain a bound on the round complexity that is sublinear in $n$ regardless of the value of $d$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1802.05582 — Open problem on sublinear round complexity
rows.defer_reason: 'obtain a sublinear bound' improve-the-bound phrasing; underlying distributed problem underspecified in record

rows.title: Question on randomized list-coloring round complexity
rows.paper_title: Distributed coloring in sparse graphs with fewer colors
rows.statement_text: Is it possible to avoid the multiplicative factor polynomial in $\Delta$ in a randomized version of the distributed $\Delta$-list-coloring algorithm (Corollary 2.1)?
rows.context_text: Panconesi and Srinivasan gave a randomized $\Delta$-coloring algorithm running in $O(\log^3 n / \log \Delta)$ rounds. The paper's list-coloring extension (Corollary 2.1) achieves $O(\Delta^2 \log^3 n)$ rounds deterministically, but it is noted to be unclear whether a randomized variant can similarly remove the polynomial dependence on $\Delta$.
rows.source_text: Is it possible to avoid the multiplicative factor polynomial in $\Delta$ in a randomized version of the distributed $\Delta$-list-coloring algorithm (Corollary 2.1)?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1802.05582 — Question on randomized list-coloring round complexity
rows.class_notes: References paper-local Corollary 2.1 algorithm; distributed LOCAL model also required.

rows.title: Question 1.5
rows.paper_title: Bounding $χ$ by a fraction of $Δ$ for graphs without large cliques
rows.statement_text: Does there exist a function $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that, for every $c > 1$ and every graph $G$ of maximum degree at most $\Delta$ with no clique of size greater than $\Delta^{1/f(c)}$, we have $\chi_\ell(G) \leq \Delta/c$?
rows.context_text: A linear improvement on the greedy bound for graphs without large cliques was notably missing before this paper. Molloy's Theorem 1.2 gives a bound of $200\omega\,\frac{\Delta\ln\ln\Delta}{\ln\Delta}$, and Spencer's Ramsey-theory result shows the bound cannot be improved when $\omega = \Omega(\Delta^{1/2})$, motivating the question of whether a sublinear clique-number condition suffices for a $\Delta/c$ bound on $\chi_\ell$.
rows.source_text: Does there exist a function $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that, for every $c > 1$ and every graph $G$ of maximum degree at most $\Delta$ with no clique of size greater than $\Delta^{1/f(c)}$, we have $\chi_\ell(G) \leq \Delta/c$?
rows.status_semantics: Question 1.5 is answered affirmatively by the paper’s own main theorem (Theorem 1.1 of arXiv:1803.01051): for sufficiently large $\Delta$, every graph $G$ with maximum degree at most $\Delta$ and $\omega(G) \leq \omega$ satisfies $\chi_c(G) \leq 72\Delta\sqrt{\ln\omega/\ln\Delta}$. Setting $\omega = \Delta^{1/f(c)}$ gives $\chi_c(G) \leq 72\Delta/\sqrt{f(c)}$, which is at most $\Delta/c$ whenever $f(c) \geq (72c)^2$; since $\chi_\ell \leq \chi_c$, the function $f(c) = (72c)^2$ witnesses a positive answer to the question. No subsequent paper is needed to resolve it.
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no question but quantifies over real c and real exponent 1/f(c).

rows.title: List-chromatic analogue of Reed's Conjecture
rows.paper_title: Bounding $χ$ by a fraction of $Δ$ for graphs without large cliques
rows.statement_text: It is possible that Conjecture 1.3 is also true for the list-chromatic number, i.e., $\chi_\ell(G) \leq \left\lceil \frac{1}{2}(\Delta + 1 + \omega) \right\rceil$ for every graph $G$ of maximum degree at most $\Delta$ with no clique of size greater than $\omega$.
rows.context_text: After stating Reed's Conjecture 1.3 for the ordinary chromatic number and noting Reed's partial result as evidence, the authors remark that the same bound may extend to the list-chromatic number. The correspondence chromatic number is always at least as large as the list-chromatic number, so such an extension would be nontrivial.
rows.source_text: It is possible that Conjecture 1.3 is also true for the list-chromatic number, i.e., $\chi_\ell(G) \leq \left\lceil \frac{1}{2}(\Delta + 1 + \omega) \right\rceil$ for every graph $G$ of maximum degree at most $\Delta$ with no clique of size greater than $\omega$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1803.01051 — List-chromatic analogue of Reed's Conjecture
rows.class_notes: List-chromatic version of Reed's conjecture; statement fully explicit in the record.

rows.title: Polynomial dependence of $\delta$ on $d$ in Rödl's theorem
rows.paper_title: Towards Erdos-Hajnal for graphs with no 5-hole
rows.statement_text: A polynomial dependence of $\delta$ on $d$ holds in Theorem 2.2, i.e., the $\delta$ satisfying Rödl's theorem can be taken to be polynomial in $d$; and this would imply the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture itself.
rows.context_text: In the discussion following Rödl's theorem (2.2), the authors note that Rödl's original proof gives a tower-type bound for $1/\delta$ in terms of $1/d$, while Fox and Sudakov [7] improve this to $\delta = 2^{-|V(H)|^{15}(\log(1/d))^2}$. A polynomial dependence is stated as a community conjecture whose truth would imply the full Erdős-Hajnal conjecture.
rows.source_text: A polynomial dependence of $\delta$ on $d$ holds in Theorem 2.2, i.e., the $\delta$ satisfying Rödl's theorem can be taken to be polynomial in $d$; and this would imply the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture itself.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1803.03588 — Polynomial dependence of $\delta$ on $d$ in Rödl's theorem
rows.status_semantics: The polynomial bound on $\delta$ in Rödl's theorem remains unproved for general $H$; the best known bound is the loglog improvement $\delta = 2^{-c(\log(1/d))^2/\log\log(1/d)}$ of Bucić–Nguyen–Scott–Seymour (2023), confirming the gap between the exponential status quo and the conjectured polynomial dependence. Notably, Bucić–Fox–Pham (2024) proved that the polynomial Rödl conjecture is in fact *equivalent* to the full Erdős–Hajnal conjecture (strengthening the mere implication stated in the source paper), and also verified the polynomial Rödl property for the special class of string graphs.
rows.class_notes: Polynomial-Rodl conjecture: strengthening whose truth implies Erdos-Hajnal; Rodl's Theorem 2.2 setup not defined in record.

rows.title: Open Problem: Complexity of 3-colourability of $P_t$-free graphs
rows.paper_title: $H$-colouring $P_t$-free graphs in subexponential time
rows.statement_text: Determine the complexity of $3$-colourability of $P_t$-free graphs for $t \geq 8$.
rows.context_text: Polynomial-time algorithms for 3-colourability of $P_t$-free graphs are known for $t \leq 7$ (combining results for $t \leq 5$, $(k,t)=(3,7)$, and hardness for $P_7$-free graphs). The paper notes this problem remains open for $t \geq 8$, and provides a subexponential-time algorithm as partial progress.
rows.source_text: Determine the complexity of $3$-colourability of $P_t$-free graphs for $t \geq 8$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1803.05396 — Open Problem: Complexity of 3-colourability of $P_t$-free graphs
rows.status_semantics: The open problem asks to determine whether 3-colourability of $P_t$-free graphs (for $t \geq 8$) can be decided in polynomial time or is NP-hard; the question is not settled. The subexponential-time algorithm of the source paper was significantly improved to quasi-polynomial time $n^{\mathcal{O}(\log^2 n)}$ by Pilipczuk, Pilipczuk, and Rz\k{a}\.zewski (2020), using techniques from Independent Set in $P_t$-free graphs. A 2025 paper by Zhou, Zhong, and Huang establishes a polynomial-time algorithm for the restricted class $\mathcal{G}_{10,7}$ ($P_{10}$-free graphs with all induced odd cycles of the same length 7), but the full complexity boundary for $t \geq 8$ remains open.
rows.defer_reason: 'determine the complexity' asks for a classification, not a yes/no proposition

rows.title: Open Problem: Polynomial-time maximum independent set on $P_t$-free graphs
rows.paper_title: $H$-colouring $P_t$-free graphs in subexponential time
rows.statement_text: Determine whether maximum independent set is decidable in polynomial time for $P_t$-free graphs for $t \geq 7$.
rows.context_text: Subexponential-time algorithms for maximum independent set on $P_t$-free graphs were given by Brause and independently by Bascó, Marx and Tuza; the present paper extends the subexponential approach to a broader class of problems including maximum independent set, but polynomial-time decidability for $t \geq 7$ remains open.
rows.source_text: Determine whether maximum independent set is decidable in polynomial time for $P_t$-free graphs for $t \geq 7$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1803.05396 — Open Problem: Polynomial-time maximum independent set on $P_t$-free graphs
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture remains open in its full generality for all $t \geq 7$. The most significant post-2019 advance is the quasi-polynomial-time algorithm of Gartland and Lokshtanov (2020), which shows that Maximum Independent Set on $P_k$-free graphs can be solved in time $n^{O(k^2 \log^3 n)}$ for any fixed $k$, ruling out NP-completeness for any fixed path length. Polynomial-time results have been extended to $P_7$-free graphs with bounded clique number (ISAAC 2025), but polynomial-time solvability for general $P_t$-free graphs with $t \geq 7$ remains open.
rows.class_notes: Yes/no: is MIS polynomial on P_t-free graphs for t >= 7; needs P-time foundation.

rows.title: Θ(√m) excess conjecture for hypergraph cuts
rows.paper_title: Hypergraph cuts above the average
rows.statement_text: For any fixed $2 \leq r \leq k$, the smallest maximum $r$-cut over all $m$-edge $k$-graphs has excess $\Theta(\sqrt{m})$.
rows.context_text: The authors describe this as a 'second plausible conjecture', motivated by: chromatic-number methods giving $\Omega(\sqrt{m})$ lower bounds; the standard deviation of a random cut in a linear $k$-graph being $\Theta(\sqrt{m})$; and the bound being tight for $r=k=2$ and for $r=2$, $k=3$. The paper's main Theorem 1.2 disproves this for all cases with $r \geq 3$ or $k \geq 4$, showing the excess is in fact $\Omega(m^{5/9})$ in those cases.
rows.source_text: For any fixed $2 \leq r \leq k$, the smallest maximum $r$-cut over all $m$-edge $k$-graphs has excess $\Theta(\sqrt{m})$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1803.08462 — Θ(√m) excess conjecture for hypergraph cuts
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that the minimum maximum $r$-cut excess over all $m$-edge $k$-graphs is $\Theta(\sqrt{m})$ for every fixed $2 \le r \le k$ is disproved by Theorem 1.2 of the source paper itself, which shows the excess is $\Omega(m^{5/9})$ for all $r \ge 3$ or $k \ge 4$—strictly larger than $\sqrt{m}$ for large $m$, while the cases $r=k=2$ and $r=2,k=3$ are known to be tight at $\Theta(\sqrt{m})$. Subsequent work has further pushed the lower bound upward: Räty and Tomon (2024) reached $m^{3/5-o(1)}$ for general hypergraphs and $m^{3/4-o(1)}$ (tight) for linear hypergraphs, and Janzer and Portier (2025) proved $m^{2/3-\varepsilon}$ for all sufficiently large $k$, progressively moving the picture far above the $\sqrt{m}$ threshold.
rows.class_notes: Disproved; 'excess' and 'maximum r-cut' of a k-graph are paper-local terms not defined in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 3
rows.paper_title: Single-conflict colouring
rows.statement_text: There exist $C, C' > 0$ such that, for any simple graph $G$ that is embeddable on a surface of Euler genus $g$, if every edge is assigned at most $Ck$ conflicts from $[k]^2$, then $G$ is conflict $k$-colourable, provided $k \geq C'\sqrt{g}$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1 gives an upper bound of the form $\chi_=(G) = O(\sqrt{g}\log g)$ in the $\mu = \Theta(\sqrt{g})$ regime, evocative of Heawood's bound. The authors note that $C < 1/2$ is forced by their second instructive example, and that a previous version of the manuscript incorrectly conjectured $C$ could be taken arbitrarily close to 1.
rows.source_text: There exist $C, C' > 0$ such that, for any simple graph $G$ that is embeddable on a surface of Euler genus $g$, if every edge is assigned at most $Ck$ conflicts from $[k]^2$, then $G$ is conflict $k$-colourable, provided $k \geq C'\sqrt{g}$.
rows.class_notes: 'Conflict k-colourable' is the paper's own notion, not defined in record; also needs Euler-genus embedding layer.

rows.title: Informal conjecture on single-conflict chromatic number of degenerate graphs
rows.paper_title: Single-conflict colouring
rows.statement_text: There might yet be some constant $C' > 0$ such that the single-conflict chromatic number of any $d$-degenerate graph on $n$ vertices is at most $C'\sqrt{d}\log n$.
rows.context_text: The authors discuss whether degeneracy could serve as an alternative approach to proving Theorem 1 in the $\mu = 1$ case, analogous to Heawood's original method. They note that a construction of Kostochka and Zhu shows $d$-degenerate graphs can have single-conflict chromatic number greater than $d$, but the bound $C'\sqrt{d}\log n$ would still imply the $\mu = 1$ case of Theorem 1; Theorem 4 gives the weaker bound $C_2(nd)^{1/4}\log(nd)$.
rows.source_text: There might yet be some constant $C' > 0$ such that the single-conflict chromatic number of any $d$-degenerate graph on $n$ vertices is at most $C'\sqrt{d}\log n$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1803.10962 — Informal conjecture on single-conflict chromatic number of degenerate graphs
rows.status_semantics: Bradshaw and Masařík (arXiv:2112.06333) proved that the single-conflict chromatic number of any simple d-degenerate graph G satisfies χ↮(G) ≤ ⌨12√(d(1+log((d+1)Δ))⌉, which is O(√d·log n), directly answering Question 1.5 posed by Dvořák, Esperet, Kang, and Ozeki. This resolves the conjecture in the simple-graph (μ=1) case, which is precisely the case discussed in the surrounding context concerning the μ=1 instance of Theorem 1. The paper was subsequently published in the Journal of Graph Theory (2025, DOI 10.1002/jgt.23025).
rows.class_notes: Solved; single-conflict chromatic number is a buildable but paper-local primitive not defined here.

rows.title: Conjecture 2.4
rows.paper_title: Pure pairs. II. Excluding all subdivisions of a graph
rows.statement_text: Let $H$ be a graph and let $P$ be a forest of $H$. Then every coherent ideal contains a $P$-filleting of $H$.
rows.context_text: The paper's main theorem (2.3) proves the statement when $P$ is a path of $H$. The authors propose this strengthening, noting it would be best possible since the conclusion fails when $P$ contains a cycle of $H$ (as coherent ideals can have arbitrarily large girth). If true, it would imply the main theorem of [8] (strong Erdős-Hajnal property for all forests) in the same way Theorem 2.3 implies the main theorem of [18].
rows.source_text: Let $H$ be a graph and let $P$ be a forest of $H$. Then every coherent ideal contains a $P$-filleting of $H$.
rows.class_notes: 'Coherent ideal' and 'P-filleting' are paper-local; distinct pure-pairs conjecture, only EH-adjacent (would imply strong EH property for forests, already known).

rows.title: Open Problem: $\Delta + O(1)$ bound for neighbour sum distinguishing colourings
rows.paper_title: Progress on the adjacent vertex distinguishing edge colouring conjecture
rows.statement_text: It is an intriguing open problem whether a $\Delta + O(1)$ bound holds on the number of colours required for a neighbour sum distinguishing edge colouring.
rows.context_text: A neighbour sum distinguishing colouring is a proper edge colouring $c$ with colours in $[\Delta+2]$ such that for every edge $uv$, the sum of colours incident to $u$ and that of $v$ are distinct — a strengthening of AVD-colourings conjectured by Flandrin et al. The best known asymptotic bound is $\Delta + O(\Delta^{1/2})$ due to Przybyło. The authors note that neither Hatami's proof for AVD-colourings nor their own entropy compression argument seems adaptable to this setting.
rows.source_text: It is an intriguing open problem whether a $\Delta + O(1)$ bound holds on the number of colours required for a neighbour sum distinguishing edge colouring.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1804.06104 — Open Problem: $\Delta + O(1)$ bound for neighbour sum distinguishing colourings
rows.class_notes: Delta+O(1) for neighbour-sum-distinguishing edge colouring; NSD is defined in context, needs an exists-constant wrapper.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture: probabilistic methods insufficient for the $\Delta+2$ bound
rows.paper_title: Progress on the adjacent vertex distinguishing edge colouring conjecture
rows.statement_text: One could suspect, as we do, that probabilistic methods such as the Local Lemma or entropy compression will probably not be enough on their own to prove the $\Delta+2$ bound from Conjecture 1, assuming the conjecture is true.
rows.context_text: After reducing Hatami's $\Delta+300$ bound to $\Delta+19$ using entropy compression, the authors reflect on the limitations of probabilistic techniques for fully resolving the Zhang–Liu–Wang conjecture, noting that some extra colours seem inevitably wasted in any random process.
rows.source_text: One could suspect, as we do, that probabilistic methods such as the Local Lemma or entropy compression will probably not be enough on their own to prove the $\Delta+2$ bound from Conjecture 1, assuming the conjecture is true.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1804.06104 — Informal Conjecture: probabilistic methods insufficient for the $\Delta+2$ bound
rows.defer_reason: meta-claim about the limits of probabilistic proof techniques, not a mathematical proposition

rows.title: Open Problem (exact exponent for maximal cliques in c-closed graphs)
rows.paper_title: Finding Cliques in Social Networks: A New Distribution-Free Model
rows.statement_text: Determine the exact exponent of $n$ (between $3/2$ and $2 - 2^{1-c}$) in the expression for the maximum number of maximal cliques in a $c$-closed graph on $n$ vertices.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.4 establishes an upper bound of $O(n^{2-2^{1-c}})$ maximal cliques in any $c$-closed graph on $n$ vertices, while Theorem 1.7 gives a matching lower bound of $\Omega(c^{-3/2} 2^{c/2} n^{3/2})$, leaving the exact exponent of $n$ undetermined for intermediate values of $c$. The two bounds coincide at $3/2$ and diverge as $c$ grows.
rows.source_text: Determine the exact exponent of $n$ (between $3/2$ and $2 - 2^{1-c}$) in the expression for the maximum number of maximal cliques in a $c$-closed graph on $n$ vertices.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1804.07431 — Open Problem (exact exponent for maximal cliques in c-closed graphs)
rows.defer_reason: asks to determine the exact exponent between known bounds

rows.title: Conjecture 1.1 (The Edge-statistics Conjecture)
rows.paper_title: Edge-statistics on large graphs
rows.statement_text: For every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $k_0 = k_0(\varepsilon)$ such that for all integers $k > k_0$ and $0 < \ell < \binom{k}{2}$ we have $\mathrm{ind}(k, \ell) \leq 1/e + \varepsilon$.
rows.context_text: The authors observe that the random graph $G(n,p)$ with $p = \binom{k}{2}^{-1}$ gives $\mathrm{ind}(k,1) \geq 1/e + o_k(1)$, and that the complete bipartite graph with smaller part of size $n/k$ achieves the same asymptotic lower bound for $\ell = k-1$. These constructions, together with additional data, motivate the conjecture that $1/e$ is an asymptotically tight upper bound for every non-trivial $\ell$.
rows.source_text: For every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $k_0 = k_0(\varepsilon)$ such that for all integers $k > k_0$ and $0 < \ell < \binom{k}{2}$ we have $\mathrm{ind}(k, \ell) \leq 1/e + \varepsilon$.
rows.status_semantics: The Edge-statistics Conjecture was fully resolved within months of its arXiv posting. Kwan, Sudakov and Tran (arXiv:1807.05202) proved the conjecture for the range Ω(k) ≤ ℓ ≤ binom(k,2)/2, and Fox and Sauermann (arXiv:1809.01352, Advances in Combinatorics 2020:4) completed the proof by handling the remaining case 1 ≤ ℓ ≤ C·k; Martinsson, Mousset, Noever and Trujić independently covered the sublinear regime ℓ ≪ k^{6/5}. The hypergraph generalisation suggested in the original paper was subsequently proved by Jain, Kwan, Mubayi and Tran (arXiv:2505.03954, IMRN 2025).
rows.class_notes: Solved (Kwan-Sudakov-et al. line); ind(k,l) is defined only in the paper, a limiting density not given in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.2 (The Large Inducibility Conjecture)
rows.paper_title: Edge-statistics on large graphs
rows.statement_text: $\limsup\{\mathrm{ind}(H) : H \notin \{K_{|H|}, \overline{K}_{|H|}\}\} = 1/e$.
rows.context_text: This conjecture for graph inducibilities is presented as an analogue of the Edge-statistics Conjecture (Conjecture 1.1), and would be implied by it. It asserts that no graph other than a complete or empty graph can have inducibility exceeding $1/e$ in the limit over large host graphs.
rows.source_text: $\limsup\{\mathrm{ind}(H) : H \notin \{K_{|H|}, \overline{K}_{|H|}\}\} = 1/e$.
rows.status_semantics: The Large Inducibility Conjecture is settled as a corollary of the Edge-statistics Conjecture (Conjecture 1.1 of arXiv:1805.06848), which the source paper explicitly states implies it. The Edge-statistics Conjecture was fully proved by the combination of Kwan, Sudakov, and Tran (handling the intermediate range) and Fox and Sauermann (arXiv:1809.01352, Advances in Combinatorics 2020, handling the remaining cases 1 ≤ ℓ ≤ C·k). The 2024 paper arXiv:2411.17362 further characterizes which non-trivial graphs achieve inducibility close to 1/e, strengthening the structural picture.
rows.class_notes: Solved; inducibility ind(H) is standard extremal vocabulary but the statement is a limsup over all graphs (real limits).

rows.title: Conjecture 2
rows.paper_title: Extension Complexity of the Correlation Polytope
rows.statement_text: For every $n$-vertex graph $G$, the extension complexity of $\mathrm{COR}(G)$ is $2^{\Omega(\mathrm{tw}(G)+\log n)}$.
rows.context_text: The authors prove the matching upper bound $2^{O(\mathrm{tw}(G)+\log n)}$ in Theorem 1 and conjecture that this is tight in general. If true, the conjecture would improve the bound of Göös, Jain and Watson ($2^{\Omega(n/\log n)}$ for the stable set polytope) to $2^{\Omega(n)}$, and would yield explicit 0/1-polytopes with extension complexity exponential in their dimension, as predicted by the counting argument of Rothvoß.
rows.source_text: For every $n$-vertex graph $G$, the extension complexity of $\mathrm{COR}(G)$ is $2^{\Omega(\mathrm{tw}(G)+\log n)}$.
rows.class_notes: Precise, but correlation-polytope extension complexity needs polyhedral/nonnegative-rank machinery well beyond the graph library.

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: Short rainbow cycles in graphs and matroids
paper_authors: Sebastián González Hermosillo de la Maza
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a simple $n$-vertex graph and $c$ be a colouring of $E(G)$ with $n$ colours, where each colour class has size at least $r$. Then $(G, c)$ contains a cycle $C$ of length at most $\lceil n/r \rceil$ such that no two incident edges of $C$ are the same colour.
rows.context_text: Introduced by the authors as a weakening of Aharoni's Conjecture 3 (the rainbow condition on the whole cycle is replaced by a proper-edge-colouring condition on incident pairs). The authors prove that Conjecture 4 implies the Caccetta-Häggkvist conjecture via a direct colouring construction on the underlying undirected graph of a digraph.
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a simple $n$-vertex graph and $c$ be a colouring of $E(G)$ with $n$ colours, where each colour class has size at least $r$. Then $(G, c)$ contains a cycle $C$ of length at most $\lceil n/r \rceil$ such that no two incident edges of $C$ are the same colour.
rows.class_notes: Strengthening: this properly-coloured short-cycle conjecture implies Caccetta-Haggkvist; statement fully self-contained.

rows.title: Conjecture 12
rows.paper_title: Short rainbow cycles in graphs and matroids
paper_authors: Sebastián González Hermosillo de la Maza
rows.statement_text: Let $M$ be a simple rank-$(n-1)$ matroid and $c$ be a colouring of $E(M)$ with $n$ colours, where each colour class has size at least $2$. Then $M$ contains a rainbow circuit of size at most $\lceil n/2 \rceil$.
rows.context_text: Proposed as the natural matroid analogue of Theorem 5. The authors note it holds for graphic matroids (by Theorem 5) and prove it for cographic matroids (Theorem 13), but observe it fails for general binary matroids because the uniform matroid $U_{n-1,m}$ contains no circuits of size less than $n$.
rows.source_text: Let $M$ be a simple rank-$(n-1)$ matroid and $c$ be a colouring of $E(M)$ with $n$ colours, where each colour class has size at least $2$. Then $M$ contains a rainbow circuit of size at most $\lceil n/2 \rceil$.
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture holds for graphic matroids (by Theorem 5 of the source paper) and cographic matroids (Theorem 13 of the source paper). A 2026 paper by McGuinness (arXiv:2601.17624) extends the result to all regular matroids, proving that every simple regular matroid of rank r with r+1 colour classes of size at least 2 contains a rainbow circuit of size at most ⌈(r+1)/2⌉. The full conjecture for arbitrary simple rank-(n-1) matroids fails: the source paper itself notes that uniform matroids U_{n-1,m} (for large m) provide counterexamples, as their circuits all have size n.
rows.class_notes: Matroid analogue in the CH rainbow-cycle program; needs a finite matroid primitive (circuits, rank).

rows.title: Conjecture 1.6
rows.paper_title: The structure of binary matroids with no induced claw or Fano plane restriction
rows.statement_text: For all $s \geq 1$ there exists an integer $k$ such that if $M$ is a simple binary matroid with no induced $I_s$-restriction or $PG(1, 2)$-restriction, then $\chi(M) \leq k$.
rows.context_text: After showing that the full matroidal Gyárfás-Sumner analogue fails (Theorem 1.3), the authors conjecture that fixing $t = 2$—that is, excluding a triangle $PG(1,2)$ rather than an arbitrary projective geometry—restores a bound on the critical number. The conjecture is verified for $s \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ by Corollary 5.2, which gives $k = 1$; new ideas are needed for larger $s$.
rows.source_text: For all $s \geq 1$ there exists an integer $k$ such that if $M$ is a simple binary matroid with no induced $I_s$-restriction or $PG(1, 2)$-restriction, then $\chi(M) \leq k$.
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture is verified for $s \in \{1,2,3\}$ with $k=1$ in the source paper (Corollary 5.2). Nelson and Nomoto (arXiv:2005.00089, SIAM JDM 2022) subsequently proved the case $s=4$, showing $I_4$-free and triangle-free binary matroids have critical number at most 2. The full conjecture for all $s \geq 5$ remains open.
rows.class_notes: Critical number chi(M), induced restriction and I_s for binary matroids are not defined in the record.

rows.title: Closing Question
rows.paper_title: The Algebraic Connectivity of a Graph and its Complement
rows.statement_text: What is the supremum of all real numbers $c$ such that for any graph $G$ of order at least $2$, $$\max\{\lambda_2(G), \lambda_2(\bar{G})\} \geq c\,?$$
rows.context_text: The paper proves the supremum is at least $\tfrac{2}{5}$. The self-complementary path $P_4$ satisfies $\lambda_2(P_4) = 2 - \sqrt{2} < 0.5858$, giving an upper bound on the supremum. The exact value is left open.
rows.source_text: What is the supremum of all real numbers $c$ such that for any graph $G$ of order at least $2$, $$\max\{\lambda_2(G), \lambda_2(\bar{G})\} \geq c\,?$$
rows.status_semantics: The closing question asks for the exact supremum c such that max{\lambda_2(G), \lambda_2(\bar{G})} \geq c holds for all graphs G of order at least 2; the source paper proved c \geq 2/5. Einollahzadeh and Karkhaneei (arXiv:1901.02047, 2019) subsequently proved the sum conjecture \lambda_2(G) + \lambda_2(\bar{G}) \geq 1, which implies max{\lambda_2(G), \lambda_2(\bar{G})} \geq 1/2, improving the lower bound. The exact supremum remains open, lying in the interval [1/2,\, 2-\sqrt{2}] \approx [0.5, 0.5858], where the upper bound comes from the self-complementary path P_4.
rows.defer_reason: asks for the exact supremum of algebraic-connectivity constant c, not a single proposition

rows.title: Conjecture 7
rows.paper_title: Online Ramsey Numbers and the Subgraph Query Problem
rows.statement_text: (a) The diagonal online random Ramsey numbers satisfy $$\tilde{r}_{rand}(n, n) = 2^{(1+o(1)) \frac{2}{3} n}.$$ (b) The off-diagonal online random Ramsey numbers ($m \geq 3$ fixed and $n \to \infty$) satisfy $$\tilde{r}_{rand}(m, n) = n^{(1+o(1)) \frac{2}{3} m}.$$
rows.context_text: The authors define the online random Ramsey number $\tilde{r}_{rand}(m,n)$ as the maximum over $p \in (0,1)$ of $\tilde{r}(m,n;p)$, where Painter independently colors each edge red with probability $p$. These conjectures on the growth rate are motivated by a connection with the Subgraph Query Problem; Theorem 10 and Conjecture 9 together imply both parts of this conjecture.
rows.source_text: (a) The diagonal online random Ramsey numbers satisfy $$\tilde{r}_{rand}(n, n) = 2^{(1+o(1)) \frac{2}{3} n}.$$ (b) The off-diagonal online random Ramsey numbers ($m \geq 3$ fixed and $n \to \infty$) satisfy $$\tilde{r}_{rand}(m, n) = n^{(1+o(1)) \frac{2}{3} m}.$$
rows.class_notes: Online random Ramsey game r~(m,n;p) is only sketched in context; game semantics plus (1+o(1)) asymptotics required.

rows.title: Conjecture 9
rows.paper_title: Online Ramsey Numbers and the Subgraph Query Problem
rows.statement_text: For any $m \geq 4$, $$f(K_m, p) = 2^{o(m)} p^{-\frac{2}{3}m + c_m},$$ where $c_m$ is a constant depending on $m \bmod 3$ (exact case formula garbled in PDF extraction).
rows.context_text: Here $f(K_m, p)$ is the minimum number of queries needed to find a copy of $K_m$ with probability at least $1/2$ in the Subgraph Query Game on $G(\mathbb{Z}, p)$. The upper bound in this conjecture is proved in Section 5.2, and together with Theorem 12 (the matching lower bound) it is fully verified for $m = 4$ and $m = 5$.
rows.source_text: For any $m \geq 4$, $$f(K_m, p) = 2^{o(m)} p^{-\frac{2}{3}m + c_m},$$ where $c_m$ is a constant depending on $m \bmod 3$ (exact case formula garbled in PDF extraction).
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 9 asserts $f(K_m, p) = 2^{o(m)} p^{-\frac{2}{3}m + c_m}$ for all $m \geq 4$; the source paper itself proves the upper bound (Section 5.2) and verifies the full conjecture (upper and matching lower bounds) for $m = 4$ and $m = 5$. A direct follow-up, arXiv:1911.04413 by Alweiss, Ben Hamida, He, and Moreira (2019, published in Combinatorics, Probability and Computing 2021), further studies $f(H, p)$ and improves upper bounds for all graphs $H$, but resolution of Conjecture 9 for all $m \geq 6$ could not be confirmed from the publicly accessible abstract. No paper fully resolving the conjecture for general $m$ was found.
rows.class_notes: Record explicitly says the case formula for c_m is garbled in PDF extraction; query-game model also paper-local.

rows.title: Informal conjecture on generalization of Theorem 4 to all $\tilde{r}(m,n)$
rows.paper_title: Online Ramsey Numbers and the Subgraph Query Problem
rows.statement_text: We suspect that Theorem 4 can be generalized to $\tilde{r}(m, n)$ in the same way.
rows.context_text: Theorem 4 establishes $\tilde{r}(3,n) = \Omega(n^3/\log^2 n)$ via a Painter strategy that avoids red triangles, in an argument close in spirit to Erdős's alteration method. The authors note that Krivelevich generalized this alteration method to all $r(m,n)$ and conjecture informally that the same can be done for $\tilde{r}(m,n)$.
rows.source_text: We suspect that Theorem 4 can be generalized to $\tilde{r}(m, n)$ in the same way.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1806.09726 — Informal conjecture on generalization of Theorem 4 to all $\tilde{r}(m,n)$
rows.status_semantics: The informal conjecture asks whether Krivelevich's generalization of the alteration method to all $r(m,n)$ can be extended to the online setting for all $\tilde{r}(m,n)$. Guo and Warnke (arXiv:1909.02691, published 2023 in Journal of Graph Theory) apply a refined alteration method specifically to online graph Ramsey games, which is directly in the spirit of the conjecture; however, the abstract does not confirm that bounds for all $m$ are established, so a full resolution cannot be verified from available sources.
rows.defer_reason: 'Theorem 4 can be generalized in the same way' — no explicit proposition stated

rows.title: Question 1.1
rows.paper_title: List-three-coloring graphs with no induced $P_6+rP_3$
rows.statement_text: Is it true that for every (fixed) integer $t > 0$, the 3-coloring problem can be solved in polynomial time when restricted to the class of $P_t$-free graphs?
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 (from the literature) states that if the $k$-coloring problem is polynomial on $H$-free graphs then every connected component of $H$ must be a path. For $k=3$ the converse may hold — it is known to fail for $k>4$ — so the question of whether $P_t$-free graphs always admit a polynomial-time 3-coloring algorithm remains open. The present paper contributes a positive result for the subclass of $(P_6+rP_3)$-free graphs.
rows.source_text: Is it true that for every (fixed) integer $t > 0$, the 3-coloring problem can be solved in polynomial time when restricted to the class of $P_t$-free graphs?
rows.status_semantics: Substantial partial progress has been made: the 3-coloring (and list 3-coloring) problem is known to be polynomial for all $P_t$-free graphs with $t \leq 7$, with the $P_7$ case resolved by Bonomo, Chudnovsky, Maceli, Schaudt, Stein, and Zhong around 2018. For $t \geq 8$ the question remains fully open; $P_8$-free graphs are the smallest unsettled case. Since 2018, incremental progress has appeared for specific subclasses of $P_t$-free graphs (e.g., those with restrictions on odd cycle lengths), but no uniform polynomial algorithm for all fixed $t$ is known.
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no form of the same open problem as axv_1803_05396_00 (3-coloring P_t-free in P for every t).

rows.title: Open Problem (constant c vs. |H|)
rows.paper_title: A tight Erdős-Pósa function for planar minors
rows.statement_text: Find good bounds on $c$ as a function of $|H|$ in the bounding function $f(k) = ck\log(k+1)$; in particular, determine whether $c$ could depend polynomially on $|H|$.
rows.context_text: The constant $c = c(H)$ obtained in the proof of Theorem 1.1 is enormous and not even known to be computable, whereas Chekuri and Chuzhoy's earlier result achieves $c$ depending polynomially on $|H|$ (at the cost of a poly-logarithmic factor $\log^d k$). The authors explicitly leave the question of good bounds on $c$ as a function of $|H|$ as an open problem.
rows.source_text: Find good bounds on $c$ as a function of $|H|$ in the bounding function $f(k) = ck\log(k+1)$; in particular, determine whether $c$ could depend polynomially on $|H|$.
rows.defer_reason: 'find good bounds on c' meta-request about a proof-dependent constant in the Erdos-Posa function
rows.class_notes: The sub-question 'is c polynomial in |H|' is close to a proposition but tied to the paper's Theorem 1.1 setup.

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: Triangle-free subgraphs with large fractional chromatic number
rows.statement_text: For every real number $x \geq 1$ and any integer $g \geq 3$, there exists a positive number $k(x,g)$ such that every graph $G$ with $\chi_f(G) \geq k(x,g)$ contains a subgraph $H$ of girth at least $g$ and with $\chi_f(H) \geq x$.
rows.context_text: Proposed immediately after Theorem 3 as the natural full generalisation to arbitrary girth. Theorem 3 of this paper establishes the case $g = 4$ (triangle-free subgraphs), and Conjecture 4 asks whether the same holds for every girth.
rows.source_text: For every real number $x \geq 1$ and any integer $g \geq 3$, there exists a positive number $k(x,g)$ such that every graph $G$ with $\chi_f(G) \geq k(x,g)$ contains a subgraph $H$ of girth at least $g$ and with $\chi_f(H) \geq x$.
rows.class_notes: Standard vocabulary (chi_f, girth); real threshold x can be quantified over rationals, needs an exists-k(x,g) wrapper.

rows.title: Conjecture 6
rows.paper_title: Triangle-free subgraphs with large fractional chromatic number
rows.statement_text: For every positive integers $k$ and $g \geq 3$, there exists a positive number $c(k,g)$ such that every graph with chromatic number at least $c(k,g)$ contains a subgraph of girth at least $g$ and with average degree at least $k$.
rows.context_text: Proposed by the authors as a weakening of both the Erdős–Hajnal conjecture and Thomassen's conjecture: the hypothesis uses chromatic number (rather than average degree) and the conclusion asks only for large average degree (rather than large chromatic number) in the subgraph.
rows.source_text: For every positive integers $k$ and $g \geq 3$, there exists a positive number $c(k,g)$ such that every graph with chromatic number at least $c(k,g)$ contains a subgraph of girth at least $g$ and with average degree at least $k$.
rows.class_notes: Common weakening of Erdos-Hajnal (girth/chromatic version) and Thomassen's conjecture; fully finite and standard.

rows.title: 2-Approximation Conjecture for Cluster-VD
rows.paper_title: Improved approximation algorithms for hitting 3-vertex paths
rows.statement_text: Cluster-VD can be 2-approximated in polynomial time.
rows.context_text: The authors establish a 9/4-approximation algorithm for the weighted Cluster Vertex Deletion problem (finding a minimum cost vertex set whose deletion leaves no induced $P_3$). Since Cluster-VD is UGC-hard to approximate to within any ratio better than 2 (via a reduction from Vertex Cover), a 2-approximation is the natural target. The authors note their 9/4-approximation already achieves ratio 2 when the largest clique has size at most 4, and can be modified to a 2-approximation on diamond-free graphs, providing partial support for the conjecture.
rows.source_text: Cluster-VD can be 2-approximated in polynomial time.
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that Cluster-VD admits a 2-approximation in polynomial time was resolved affirmatively by Aprile, Drescher, Fiorini, and Huynh in 2020. Their paper arXiv:2007.08057 presents the first 2-approximation algorithm for the (weighted) cluster vertex deletion problem, matching the UGC-hardness lower bound of 2 established via a reduction from Vertex Cover. The result was published in Mathematical Programming in 2021.
rows.class_notes: Approximation-algorithm existence claim for Cluster Vertex Deletion; solved (2-approx now known).

rows.title: Informal conjecture on improved color ratio in Theorem 5
rows.paper_title: Fractional coloring of planar graphs of girth five
rows.statement_text: It might be possible to improve the ratio of extra colors assigned to the vertex $x$ in Theorem 5 a bit; e.g., it could be true that there exists a coloring by subsets of $\{1, \ldots, 9\}$ such that all vertices get at least three colors and $x$ gets five.
rows.context_text: Following Theorem 5, the authors observe that the ratio cannot be improved arbitrarily: when $G$ is the graph obtained from the wheel with five spokes by subdividing each spoke once, $x$ is the hub, and $\varphi$ is a coloring by subsets of $\{1,\ldots,k\}$ with every vertex receiving at least 3 colors, then $|\varphi(x)| \leq \frac{k}{3} + \frac{2k}{9}$. They nonetheless suggest a modest improvement may be achievable.
rows.source_text: It might be possible to improve the ratio of extra colors assigned to the vertex $x$ in Theorem 5 a bit; e.g., it could be true that there exists a coloring by subsets of $\{1, \ldots, 9\}$ such that all vertices get at least three colors and $x$ gets five.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1809.05439 — Informal conjecture on improved color ratio in Theorem 5
rows.class_notes: Concrete example instance given, but hinges on Theorem 5's planar-girth-5 setup with distinguished vertex x, not restated here.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.2
rows.paper_title: Short directed cycles in bipartite digraphs
rows.statement_text: For every integer $k \geq 1$, if $G$ is a bipartite digraph, with $n > 0$ vertices in each part, and every vertex has out-degree more than $n/(k+1)$, then $G$ has girth at most $2k$.
rows.context_text: The central bipartite analogue of the Caccetta-Häggkvist conjecture, motivated by an extremal construction with bipartition into parts of cardinality $(k+1)t$ in which every vertex has out-degree exactly $n/(k+1)$ and no directed cycle of length at most $2k$ exists. The conjecture is implied by the Caccetta-Häggkvist conjecture.
rows.source_text: For every integer $k \geq 1$, if $G$ is a bipartite digraph, with $n > 0$ vertices in each part, and every vertex has out-degree more than $n/(k+1)$, then $G$ has girth at most $2k$.
rows.status_semantics: The source paper (published in Combinatorica 2020) itself proves Conjecture 1.2 for k = 1, 2, 3, 4, 6 and all k ≥ 224,539, establishing substantial partial progress. The full conjecture remains open for the intermediate values 5 and 7 ≤ k ≤ 224,538. No post-2019 follow-up paper extending these results to additional cases was found across multiple targeted searches.
rows.class_notes: Bipartite analogue implied by Caccetta-Haggkvist; weakening/variant, not an alias.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.5
rows.paper_title: Short directed cycles in bipartite digraphs
rows.statement_text: For every integer $k \geq 1$, and every pair of reals $\alpha, \beta > 0$ with $k\alpha + \beta > 1$, if $G$ is a non-null bipartite digraph with bipartition $(A, B)$, where every vertex in $A$ has out-degree at least $\beta|B|$, and every vertex in $B$ has out-degree at least $\alpha|A|$, then $G$ has girth at most $2k$.
rows.context_text: A conjecture stronger than Conjecture 1.2 (the case $\alpha = \beta$) that also implies the Caccetta-Häggkvist conjecture (via $\beta \to 0$). It is best possible for infinitely many pairs $(\alpha, \beta)$, as witnessed by an explicit circulant-type extremal construction; the maximal bad pairs all lie on the lines $\alpha + k\beta = 1$ or $k\alpha + \beta = 1$.
rows.source_text: For every integer $k \geq 1$, and every pair of reals $\alpha, \beta > 0$ with $k\alpha + \beta > 1$, if $G$ is a non-null bipartite digraph with bipartition $(A, B)$, where every vertex in $A$ has out-degree at least $\beta|B|$, and every vertex in $B$ has out-degree at least $\alpha|A|$, then $G$ has girth at most $2k$.
rows.class_notes: Asymmetric strengthening of Conjecture 1.2 that implies Caccetta-Haggkvist; real parameters alpha,beta.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.4
rows.paper_title: Sparse graphs with no polynomial-sized anticomplete pairs
rows.statement_text: For every graph $H$ there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that in every $H$-free $\varepsilon$-bounded graph $G$ with $|G| > 1$ vertices, there is an anticomplete $(\varepsilon|G|, \varepsilon|G|)$-pair.
rows.context_text: A graph $G$ is $\varepsilon$-bounded if its maximum degree is less than $\varepsilon|G|$. By a theorem of Rödl, $H$ satisfies Conjecture 1.3 if and only if both $H$ and $\bar{H}$ satisfy Conjecture 1.4, so the two conjectures are equivalent over all $H$. For certain graphs $H$, however, 1.4 is more tractable than 1.3.
rows.source_text: For every graph $H$ there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that in every $H$-free $\varepsilon$-bounded graph $G$ with $|G| > 1$ vertices, there is an anticomplete $(\varepsilon|G|, \varepsilon|G|)$-pair.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.4 from arXiv:1810.00058 remains open in full generality. Partial progress has been made: Fox, Nguyen, Scott, and Seymour (arXiv:2307.00801) proved the conjecture for H=P4 with the optimal bound δ=ε, and more generally for all graphs H obtainable by vertex-substitution from copies of P4 and its subgraphs, via the stronger 'viral' property. Separately, Bucić, Fox, and Pham (arXiv:2403.08303) proved that the polynomial Rödl conjecture (Conjecture 1.3 in the source paper, which is equivalent to Conjecture 1.4 over all H by Rödl's theorem) is equivalent to the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture, establishing that the full resolution of Conjecture 1.4 is as hard as settling Erdős-Hajnal.
rows.class_notes: epsilon-bounded defined in context; anticomplete pair is standard vocabulary.

rows.title: Open case H = K3 for Conjecture 1.4
rows.paper_title: Sparse graphs with no polynomial-sized anticomplete pairs
rows.statement_text: It remains open whether $H = K_3$ (the triangle) satisfies Conjecture 1.4.
rows.context_text: The paper's main result establishes Conjecture 1.4 for all almost-bipartite graphs $H$; almost-bipartite graphs are triangle-free, so $K_3$ is the simplest graph not covered. This gap is noted both in the abstract and in the body of the paper.
rows.source_text: It remains open whether $H = K_3$ (the triangle) satisfies Conjecture 1.4.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1810.00058 — Open case H = K3 for Conjecture 1.4
rows.class_notes: References Conjecture 1.4 without restating it; special case H=K3 of axv_1810_00058_00.

rows.title: Conjecture 3.4
rows.paper_title: Sparse graphs with no polynomial-sized anticomplete pairs
rows.statement_text: For every graph $H$ there exist $\varepsilon, s > 0$ such that for every $H$-free $\varepsilon$-bounded graph $G$ with $|G| > 1$, and every $c$ with $0 \leq c \leq 1$, there is a $c$-sparse $(\varepsilon c^s |G|,\, \varepsilon|G|)$-pair in $G$.
rows.context_text: This is the $\varepsilon$-bounded specialization of Conjecture 3.3; for $\varepsilon$-bounded graphs the dense outcome is excluded. The implications $3.3 \Rightarrow 3.4 \Rightarrow 1.4$ hold. By analogy with 1.3 and 1.4, a theorem of Rödl implies that $H$ satisfies 3.3 if and only if both $H$ and $\bar{H}$ satisfy 3.4.
rows.source_text: For every graph $H$ there exist $\varepsilon, s > 0$ such that for every $H$-free $\varepsilon$-bounded graph $G$ with $|G| > 1$, and every $c$ with $0 \leq c \leq 1$, there is a $c$-sparse $(\varepsilon c^s |G|,\, \varepsilon|G|)$-pair in $G$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 3.4 from arXiv:1810.00058, asserting the existence of c-sparse pairs in H-free ε-bounded graphs with quantitative polynomial bounds, remains open in general. Partial progress was made in arXiv:2307.00801 (Fox, Nguyen, Scott, Seymour), which proves the conjecture for H = P_4 with the optimal parameter and more generally for all graphs obtainable by vertex-substitution from P_4 and its subgraphs. The parent Conjecture 3.3 (which implies 3.4) was shown by Bucić, Fox, and Pham (arXiv:2403.08303) to be equivalent to the Erdős–Hajnal conjecture, placing Conjecture 3.4 in the broader landscape of open Ramsey-type problems.
rows.class_notes: 'c-sparse pair' and Conjecture 3.3 are paper-local and not defined in the record.

rows.title: Open Problem: 3-colourability of ternary graphs
rows.paper_title: Proof of the Kalai-Meshulam conjecture
rows.statement_text: It may be that all ternary graphs are 3-colourable.
rows.context_text: Bonamy, Charbit and Thomassé proved that every ternary graph is $c$-colourable for some absolute constant $c$, and a stronger result by two of the present authors shows bounded chromatic number whenever clique number is bounded and no induced cycle has length $p\bmod q$. The paper notes that whether the bound can be taken to be 3 for ternary graphs specifically remains open.
rows.source_text: It may be that all ternary graphs are 3-colourable.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1810.00065 — Open Problem: 3-colourability of ternary graphs
rows.class_notes: 'Ternary graph' (no induced cycle of length divisible by 3) is paper-local and not defined in the record; clean once recovered.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.5 (sparse strong EH-property)
rows.paper_title: Caterpillars in Erdős-Hajnal
rows.statement_text: A graph $H$ has the sparse strong EH-property if and only if $H$ is a forest.
rows.context_text: The sparse strong EH-property of $H$ requires that there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every $H$-free graph $G$ with $|V(G)| \geq 2$ either has a vertex with at least $\varepsilon|V(G)|$ neighbours or contains two anticomplete sets of vertices each of cardinality at least $\varepsilon|V(G)|$. Erdős's construction shows the property forces $H$ to be a forest; the conjecture asserts the converse.
rows.source_text: A graph $H$ has the sparse strong EH-property if and only if $H$ is a forest.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.5 is fully resolved. The 'only if' direction — that the sparse strong EH-property forces H to be a forest — was already known from Erdős's construction. The converse — that every forest H has the sparse strong EH-property — was proved by Chudnovsky, Scott, Seymour, and Spirkl in 'Pure Pairs. I. Trees and Linear Anticomplete Pairs' (arXiv:1809.00919), which appeared as an arXiv preprint in September 2018 (one month before the caterpillar paper) and was published in Advances in Mathematics in 2020; Seymour and Spirkl are co-authors on both papers.
rows.class_notes: Sparse strong EH-property fully defined in context; a sparse variant of Erdos-Hajnal, solved.

rows.title: Question 1.3
rows.paper_title: Colouring Graphs with Sparse Neighbourhoods: Bounds and Applications
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a $\lfloor (1-\varepsilon)(\Delta(G)+1) \rfloor + 1$-critical graph with $\omega(G) \leq (1-\alpha)(\Delta(G)+1)$. What is the largest $\delta = \delta(\varepsilon,\alpha)$, such that $G$ is $\delta$-sparse?
rows.context_text: This question isolates the first of two steps in the King–Reed proof strategy for Reed's conjecture: showing that a graph which is critical with respect to a list assignment and has bounded clique number must have sparse neighbourhoods. Improving $\delta$ directly strengthens the $\varepsilon$ achievable in Theorem 1.2.
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a $\lfloor (1-\varepsilon)(\Delta(G)+1) \rfloor + 1$-critical graph with $\omega(G) \leq (1-\alpha)(\Delta(G)+1)$. What is the largest $\delta = \delta(\varepsilon,\alpha)$, such that $G$ is $\delta$-sparse?
rows.status_semantics: Question 1.3 asks for the optimal δ(ε,α) quantifying how sparse a critical graph with bounded clique number must be — Step 1 of the King–Reed strategy for Reed's conjecture. A 2020 master's thesis by Kroeker (supervised by Postle at UWaterloo) directly addresses sparsity of list-critical graphs with small clique number, deriving new density lemmas by embedding the graph in a complete multipartite graph with many parts of size three. Kelly and Postle (arXiv:1911.02672) separately prove an improved lower bound on the density of k-critical graphs with clique number less than k/2. Neither work fully determines the optimal δ(ε,α), so the question remains open.
rows.defer_reason: Asks for the largest function delta(eps,alpha), a determine-the-value question, and 'delta-sparse' is not defined in the record.

rows.title: Question 1.4
rows.paper_title: Colouring Graphs with Sparse Neighbourhoods: Bounds and Applications
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a $\delta$-sparse graph. What is the largest $\varepsilon = \varepsilon(\delta)$ such that $\chi(G) \leq (1-\varepsilon)(\Delta+1)$?
rows.context_text: This isolates the second step in the King–Reed proof: given neighbourhood sparsity, bound the chromatic number below $\Delta+1$. Molloy–Reed obtained $\varepsilon(\delta)\approx 0.0238\delta$ and Bruhn–Joos improved to $\approx 0.1827\delta - 0.0778\delta^{3/2}$; the paper further improves this using an iterative naive colouring procedure.
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a $\delta$-sparse graph. What is the largest $\varepsilon = \varepsilon(\delta)$ such that $\chi(G) \leq (1-\varepsilon)(\Delta+1)$?
rows.status_semantics: The exact optimal function ε(δ) characterising the best chromatic bound for δ-sparse graphs remains unknown. Hurley, de Joannis de Verclos, and Kang (arXiv:2007.07874, Advances in Combinatorics 2022) directly improved the lower bound on ε(δ) via an iterated colouring procedure for graphs of bounded local density (the same σ-sparse setting), claiming their leading term is asymptotically optimal as σ→0 and surpassing the Bonamy–Perrett–Postle bound. No paper has determined the exact value of ε(δ) for any specific δ > 0.
rows.defer_reason: Asks for the largest function eps(delta); determine-the-optimal-constant question, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Open problem on queue-number of bounded layered treewidth graphs
rows.paper_title: Minor-closed graph classes with bounded layered pathwidth
rows.statement_text: It is open whether graphs of bounded layered treewidth have bounded queue-number.
rows.context_text: Dujmović et al. proved that every $n$-vertex graph with layered treewidth $k$ has queue-number $O(k \log n)$. Subsequent to the submission of this paper, improved bounds on queue-number were obtained, but the question of whether a constant bound holds for graphs of bounded layered treewidth remains open.
rows.source_text: It is open whether graphs of bounded layered treewidth have bounded queue-number.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1810.08314 — Open problem on queue-number of bounded layered treewidth graphs
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no boundedness question; needs queue-number and layered-treewidth primitives.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.8
rows.paper_title: Additive Approximation of Generalized Turán Questions
rows.statement_text: For every graph $T$, family of graphs $\mathcal{F}$ such that no $F \in \mathcal{F}$ is a subgraph of a blow-up of $T$, and $\epsilon > 0$, it is NP-hard to approximate $\mathrm{ex}(G, T, \mathcal{F})$ up to additive error of $n^{v(T)-\epsilon}$ for a given input graph $G$ on $n$ vertices.
rows.context_text: The authors prove Theorem 1.7 as a special case (for $T = K_m$, $\mathcal{F} = \{K_k\}$ with $k \geq m+2$) and note that Proposition 1.5 covers the complementary easy regime (when some $F \in \mathcal{F}$ is a subgraph of a blow-up of $T$). They believe that excluding those easy cases no significantly better approximation than $\epsilon n^{v(T)}$ is achievable in polynomial time, leading to this conjecture. Section 6 of the paper contains further remarks on this conjecture and related open problems.
rows.source_text: For every graph $T$, family of graphs $\mathcal{F}$ such that no $F \in \mathcal{F}$ is a subgraph of a blow-up of $T$, and $\epsilon > 0$, it is NP-hard to approximate $\mathrm{ex}(G, T, \mathcal{F})$ up to additive error of $n^{v(T)-\epsilon}$ for a given input graph $G$ on $n$ vertices.
rows.class_notes: NP-hardness of additive approximation of generalized Turan numbers; precise once a hardness framework exists.

rows.title: Research Question (Introduction)
rows.paper_title: Graph Isomorphism for $(H_1,H_2)$-free Graphs: An Almost Complete Dichotomy
rows.statement_text: Is it possible to determine the computational complexity of Graph Isomorphism for $(H_1, H_2)$-free graphs for all pairs $H_1, H_2$?
rows.context_text: Kratsch and Schweitzer [22] initiated the classification of Graph Isomorphism for $(H_1,H_2)$-free graphs, and Schweitzer [30] later proved that only finitely many cases remain open without specifying them. This question frames the paper's programme to complete the dichotomy.
rows.source_text: Is it possible to determine the computational complexity of Graph Isomorphism for $(H_1, H_2)$-free graphs for all pairs $H_1, H_2$?
rows.defer_reason: Meta programme question ('determine the complexity for all pairs'), a classification agenda rather than one proposition.

rows.title: Open Question: FPT Algorithm for Graph Isomorphism Parameterized by Clique-Width
rows.paper_title: Graph Isomorphism for $(H_1,H_2)$-free Graphs: An Almost Complete Dichotomy
rows.statement_text: Whether an FPT algorithm exists for Graph Isomorphism when parameterized by clique-width is still open.
rows.context_text: Lokshtanov et al. [23] gave an FPT algorithm parameterized by treewidth, and Grohe and Schweitzer [20] proved XP membership parameterized by clique-width (i.e., polynomial-time solvability for each fixed clique-width). The question of whether the parameter clique-width admits an FPT algorithm remains unresolved.
rows.source_text: Whether an FPT algorithm exists for Graph Isomorphism when parameterized by clique-width is still open.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1811.12252 — Open Question: FPT Algorithm for Graph Isomorphism Parameterized by Clique-Width
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no: existence of an FPT algorithm for Graph Isomorphism parameterized by clique-width.

rows.title: Open Cases: Clique-Width Classification for $(H_1,H_2)$-Free Graphs
rows.paper_title: Graph Isomorphism for $(H_1,H_2)$-free Graphs: An Almost Complete Dichotomy
rows.statement_text: Determine, for each of the five remaining pairs $(H_1, H_2)$, whether the class of $(H_1, H_2)$-free graphs has bounded or unbounded clique-width.
rows.context_text: The paper continues a project [4,8,11,12,14,15] classifying the boundedness of clique-width of $(H_1,H_2)$-free graph classes. By showing that (gem, $P_1+2P_2$)-free graphs have unbounded clique-width, the authors reduce the number of open pairs from six to five.
rows.source_text: Determine, for each of the five remaining pairs $(H_1, H_2)$, whether the class of $(H_1, H_2)$-free graphs has bounded or unbounded clique-width.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1811.12252 — Open Cases: Clique-Width Classification for $(H_1,H_2)$-Free Graphs
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine for each of the five remaining pairs' — the pairs are not listed in the record and it is a classification task, not one proposition.

rows.title: Informal: Δ = o(n) condition not necessary for Corollary 2
rows.paper_title: Frozen $(Δ+1)$-colourings of bounded degree graphs
rows.statement_text: The condition $\Delta = o(n)$ in Corollary 2 is probably not necessary.
rows.context_text: Corollary 2 states that for a graph on $n$ vertices with $\Delta = o(n)$, Glauber dynamics for $(\Delta+1)$-colourings starting at a uniformly random colouring gives an almost uniform sampler. The authors remark immediately after the corollary that the constraint $\Delta = o(n)$ is likely an artifact of the proof technique.
rows.source_text: The condition $\Delta = o(n)$ in Corollary 2 is probably not necessary.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1811.12650 — Informal: Δ = o(n) condition not necessary for Corollary 2
rows.class_notes: 'Condition probably not necessary' remark; Glauber dynamics / almost-uniform-sampler setup of Corollary 2 not fully specified in the record.

rows.title: Informal: Exponent in Theorem 1 not optimal
rows.paper_title: Frozen $(Δ+1)$-colourings of bounded degree graphs
rows.statement_text: The dependence on $\Delta$ in the exponent of the bound $\mathbb{P}(\alpha \text{ is frozen}) \leq (6/7)^{n/(\Delta+1)}$ in Theorem 1 is not best possible.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1 gives the upper bound $(6/7)^{n/(\Delta+1)}$ on the probability a uniform random $(\Delta+1)$-colouring is frozen. Proposition 4 provides a matching lower bound with exponent $e^{-3\log(\Delta)/\Delta \cdot n}$, leaving a gap of a $\log\Delta$ factor. The authors believe the upper bound can be tightened to close this gap.
rows.source_text: The dependence on $\Delta$ in the exponent of the bound $\mathbb{P}(\alpha \text{ is frozen}) \leq (6/7)^{n/(\Delta+1)}$ in Theorem 1 is not best possible.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1811.12650 — Informal: Exponent in Theorem 1 not optimal
rows.defer_reason: 'Not best possible' improve-the-exponent remark with no target bound stated.

rows.title: Problem 2.15
rows.paper_title: On the Complexity of Digraph Colourings and Vertex Arboricity
rows.statement_text: Let $F$ be a fixed (multi-)digraph. Instance: A (multi-)digraph $D$. Decide whether $D$ is circularly $F$-colourable.
rows.context_text: A digraph $D$ is circularly $F$-colourable if there exists a circular homomorphism from $D$ to $F$. This problem generalises Problem 1 and serves as a directed analogue of the $H$-colouring problem for graphs. Conjecture 2.16 asserts that NP-completeness holds whenever $F$ contains a directed cycle, and Theorem 2.17 establishes this for almost all such cases.
rows.source_text: Let $F$ be a fixed (multi-)digraph. Instance: A (multi-)digraph $D$. Decide whether $D$ is circularly $F$-colourable.
rows.status_semantics: Problem 2.15 asks for the complexity of deciding circular F-colourability for a fixed digraph F. Conjecture 2.16 (stated in the same paper) asserts this problem is NP-complete whenever F contains a directed cycle. Theorem 2.17 of the source paper already establishes NP-completeness for almost all such F: specifically for digon-free digraphs with directed cycles, digraphs whose symmetric part contains an odd cycle, and 2-colourable digraphs. The remaining open case is digraphs F that contain a directed cycle whose symmetric part is non-empty and bipartite. No external follow-up paper resolving the full conjecture was found in the literature.
rows.defer_reason: Record is a decision-problem definition (instance/question), not a proposition; the associated conjecture is the separate row axv_1812_02420_01.

rows.title: Conjecture 2.16
rows.paper_title: On the Complexity of Digraph Colourings and Vertex Arboricity
rows.statement_text: Let $F$ be a digraph which contains a directed cycle. Then the circular $F$-colouring problem is NP-complete.
rows.context_text: The authors conjecture that, under the assumption $\mathsf{P}\neq\mathsf{NP}$, the only polynomially solvable instances of Problem 2.15 are those where $F$ is acyclic (for which only acyclic digraphs map circularly to $F$). Theorem 2.17 confirms this conjecture for almost all cases.
rows.source_text: Let $F$ be a digraph which contains a directed cycle. Then the circular $F$-colouring problem is NP-complete.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 2.16 asserts NP-completeness of circular F-colouring for every digraph F containing a directed cycle. Theorem 2.17 in the same paper confirms the conjecture for all such F that are digon-free, have an odd cycle in their symmetric part, or are 2-colourable. The sole remaining open case is F containing a directed cycle whose symmetric part is non-empty, bipartite, and such that F is not 2-colourable; no subsequent work resolving this case was found in the literature.
rows.class_notes: NP-completeness dichotomy claim, but 'circular homomorphism' is paper-local and not defined in the record.

rows.title: Problem 3.21
rows.paper_title: On the Complexity of Digraph Colourings and Vertex Arboricity
rows.statement_text: Let $p\geq 1$ be a fixed real number. Instance: A (multi-)digraph $D$. Decide whether $\vec{\chi}_{f}(D)\leq p$.
rows.context_text: This problem asks for the complexity of deciding whether the fractional dichromatic number of a digraph is at most $p$. The paper proves NP-completeness for all real $p>1$ with $p\neq 2$, mirroring results of Feder et al. for the circular dichromatic number; the case $p=2$ is explicitly left open.
rows.source_text: Let $p\geq 1$ be a fixed real number. Instance: A (multi-)digraph $D$. Decide whether $\vec{\chi}_{f}(D)\leq p$.
rows.defer_reason: Decision-problem definition asking to determine complexity for each p; only the p=2 case is open and no proposition is asserted.

rows.title: Problem 5.40
rows.paper_title: On the Complexity of Digraph Colourings and Vertex Arboricity
rows.statement_text: For given $k,b\in\mathbb{N}$ with $k\geq b$, is there a directed graph $\vec{K}(k,b)$ with vertex set $\binom{[k]}{b}$ such that the following holds? The subdigraph of $\vec{K}(k,b)$ induced by any $\{B_{1},\ldots,B_{l}\}\subseteq\binom{[k]}{b}$ is acylic if and only if $\bigcap_{i=1}^{l}{B_{i}}\neq\emptyset$.
rows.context_text: Such 'directed Kneser graphs' $\vec{K}(k,b)$ would allow $b$-tuple $k$-colourings of digraphs to be equivalent to the existence of circular homomorphisms to $\vec{K}(k,b)$, tying the fractional dichromatic number to circular homomorphism theory. Existence is confirmed for $k=b+1$ via the directed cycle of length $b+1$, but the general case is open.
rows.source_text: For given $k,b\in\mathbb{N}$ with $k\geq b$, is there a directed graph $\vec{K}(k,b)$ with vertex set $\binom{[k]}{b}$ such that the following holds? The subdigraph of $\vec{K}(k,b)$ induced by any $\{B_{1},\ldots,B_{l}\}\subseteq\binom{[k]}{b}$ is acylic if and only if $\bigcap_{i=1}^{l}{B_{i}}\neq\emptyset$.
rows.class_notes: Self-contained finite existence question: directed Kneser digraph with induced-acyclicity iff nonempty intersection.

rows.title: Problem 4
rows.paper_title: 1-subdivisions, fractional chromatic number and Hall ratio
rows.statement_text: Determine the smallest function $g : \mathbb{Z}^+ \to \mathbb{R}^+$ such that for every graph $G$, $\chi_f(G) \leq \rho(G)\,g(|V(G)|)$.
rows.context_text: Since $\chi_f(G) = O(\rho(G) \log n)$ follows from a greedy independent-set argument, and the graphs produced by Corollary 3 have doubly exponential size in their fractional chromatic number (giving $g(n) = \Omega(\log\log n)$), the true growth rate of the smallest such $g$ is unknown.
rows.source_text: Determine the smallest function $g : \mathbb{Z}^+ \to \mathbb{R}^+$ such that for every graph $G$, $\chi_f(G) \leq \rho(G)\,g(|V(G)|)$.
rows.status_semantics: Problem 4 asks for the exact growth rate of the smallest function $g$ such that $\chi_f(G) \leq \rho(G)\,g(|V(G)|)$ for all graphs $G$; at the time of posting the bounds were $\Omega(\log\log n) \leq g(n) \leq O(\log n)$. Steiner (2024, arXiv:2411.16465, published in Combinatorica) substantially narrows this gap by proving a lower bound of $\Omega(\log n/(\log\log n)^3)$, establishing $g(n) = (\log n)^{1-o(1)}$ and showing the growth rate is essentially logarithmic. The exact smallest $g$ (precise constant factors) remains undetermined, so the problem is partially resolved.
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the smallest function g' — determine-the-growth-rate question, not a proposition.

rows.title: Problem 5
rows.paper_title: 1-subdivisions, fractional chromatic number and Hall ratio
rows.statement_text: Do there exist, for some constant $c > 0$, graphs $G$ of arbitrarily large fractional chromatic number such that $\rho(G) \leq c$ and $\alpha_{\deg_H}(H) \geq |E(H)|/c$ for every $H \subseteq G$?
rows.context_text: To lower-bound $\chi_f$ of the probabilistically constructed graphs in Theorem 1, the authors assign vertex weights proportional to degrees. Problem 5 asks whether such degree-weighted independence certificates can simultaneously be large while the ordinary Hall ratio remains bounded, motivating a search for intermediate weight families $\mathcal{F}$ between $\{0,1\}$-valued weights (Hall ratio) and all non-negative weights (fractional chromatic number).
rows.source_text: Do there exist, for some constant $c > 0$, graphs $G$ of arbitrarily large fractional chromatic number such that $\rho(G) \leq c$ and $\alpha_{\deg_H}(H) \geq |E(H)|/c$ for every $H \subseteq G$?
rows.status_semantics: Problem 5 was affirmatively solved by Raphael Steiner (arXiv:2411.16465, published in Combinatorica 2025). Theorem 1.5 of that paper constructs, for every fixed δ > 0 and sufficiently large n, an n-vertex graph G with χ_f(G) ≥ log log n / (50 log log log n), ρ(G) ≤ 4 + δ, and α_{deg_H}(H) ≥ |E(H)| / (4 + δ) for every subgraph H ⊆ G, directly answering the problem in the affirmative. The companion arXiv:2007.11853 (Dvořák, on weighted sublinear separators) does not bear on Problem 5.
rows.class_notes: Yes/no existence question (solved), but the degree-weighted independence notation alpha_degH is not defined in the record.

rows.title: Problem 8
rows.paper_title: 1-subdivisions, fractional chromatic number and Hall ratio
rows.statement_text: Is it true that a class $\mathcal{C}$ has bounded expansion if and only if there exists a function $f_6 : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for every $r \in \mathbb{N}$ and every $G \in \mathcal{C} \mathbin{\triangledown} r$, $\rho(G) \leq f_6(r)$?
rows.context_text: Corollary 7 gives an analogous characterisation of bounded expansion via $\chi_f$ in shallow minors. The forward direction (bounded expansion implies bounded Hall ratio in shallow minors) is clear; the converse argument fails by Theorem 2. Proposition 1 shows a nowhere-dense class with unbounded Hall ratio, but no class with unbounded expansion and bounded Hall ratio in all shallow minors is known.
rows.source_text: Is it true that a class $\mathcal{C}$ has bounded expansion if and only if there exists a function $f_6 : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for every $r \in \mathbb{N}$ and every $G \in \mathcal{C} \mathbin{\triangledown} r$, $\rho(G) \leq f_6(r)$?
rows.class_notes: Precise iff characterization question; needs shallow-minor (C nabla r), bounded-expansion and Hall-ratio primitives.

rows.title: Informal Question (Section 2)
rows.paper_title: Lipschitz bijections between boolean functions
rows.statement_text: Does there exist a map $\phi$ from Dictator to XOR which is $C$-Lipschitz, where each output bit depends on at most $k$ input bits, and such that $\phi^{-1}$ is $o(\log n / \log k)$-Lipschitz as $n, k \to \infty$?
rows.context_text: Theorem 1 shows that for general maps the inverse must be at least $\Omega(1/k)\cdot\log n$-Lipschitz, while for linear maps the bound is $\Omega(1/\log k)\cdot\log n$-Lipschitz. Rao and Shinkar's construction achieves $\delta(2,k)=O(1/\log k)$ for linear maps, matching the linear-map lower bound, but a gap remains for general maps between $\Omega(1/k)$ and $O(1/\log k)$.
rows.source_text: Does there exist a map $\phi$ from Dictator to XOR which is $C$-Lipschitz, where each output bit depends on at most $k$ input bits, and such that $\phi^{-1}$ is $o(\log n / \log k)$-Lipschitz as $n, k \to \infty$?
rows.defer_reason: Lipschitz maps between boolean functions on the hypercube — boolean function analysis, not graph/digraph/hypergraph theory.

rows.title: Problem 1.4
rows.paper_title: The hat guessing number of graphs
rows.statement_text: Do there exist functions $f_i : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$, $1 \leq i \leq 3$ such that (i) if the maximum degree of $G$ is $\Delta$, then $HG(G) \leq f_1(\Delta)$; (ii) if $G$ is $d$-degenerate, then $HG(G) \leq f_2(d)$; (iii) if the minimum degree of $G$ is $\delta$, then $HG(G) \geq f_3(\delta)$, and $f_3(\delta)$ tends to infinity when $\delta$ tends to infinity.
rows.context_text: The authors seek to understand how basic graph parameters control the hat guessing number. The existence of $f_1$ is already known via the Lovász Local Lemma ($HG(G) < e^\Delta$), but whether $f_2$ (degeneracy bound) or $f_3$ (minimum-degree lower bound) exist is unclear. Theorems 1.6 and 1.8 provide partial progress toward part (ii).
