rows.source_text: Do there exist functions $f_i : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$, $1 \leq i \leq 3$ such that (i) if the maximum degree of $G$ is $\Delta$, then $HG(G) \leq f_1(\Delta)$; (ii) if $G$ is $d$-degenerate, then $HG(G) \leq f_2(d)$; (iii) if the minimum degree of $G$ is $\delta$, then $HG(G) \geq f_3(\delta)$, and $f_3(\delta)$ tends to infinity when $\delta$ tends to infinity.
rows.status_semantics: Problem 1.4 remains largely open. Part (i) (HG(G) bounded by a function of maximum degree) was already settled before the problem was stated via the Lovász Local Lemma. Parts (ii) and (iii) are unresolved: for part (ii), several papers have studied hat guessing numbers of degenerate and strongly degenerate graphs and shown that HG can grow at least as fast as $2^{2^{d-1}}$ for $d$-degenerate graphs, but whether an upper bound $f_2(d)$ exists is still open; for part (iii), no evidence of a minimum-degree lower bound was found.
rows.class_notes: Three-part existence-of-bounding-functions conjunction; needs a hat-guessing-number primitive (standard game definition).

rows.title: Open question: exact value of $HG(K_{n,n})$
rows.paper_title: The hat guessing number of graphs
rows.statement_text: What is the exact value of $HG(K_{n,n})$?
rows.context_text: After establishing $\Omega\!\left(n^{\frac{1}{2}-o(1)}\right) = HG(K_{n,n}) \leq n+1$ by combining results of Gadouleau–Georgiou with Theorem 1.2, the authors note that the determination of the exact value of $HG(K_{n,n})$ is left as an interesting open question.
rows.source_text: What is the exact value of $HG(K_{n,n})$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1812.09752 — Open question: exact value of $HG(K_{n,n})$
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the exact value of HG(K_{n,n})' — determine-the-value question.

rows.title: Open Problem (Section 1.1)
rows.paper_title: Baker game and polynomial-time approximation schemes
rows.statement_text: It is an open problem whether some variation on the local search approach can give PTAS for all monotone FO optimization problems.
rows.context_text: Cabello–Gajser and Har-Peled–Quanrud showed that the trivial local search algorithm (performing bounded-size changes on an initial solution as long as it can be improved) gives PTASes for maximum independent set and minimum dominating set, as well as many related problems, on classes with strongly sublinear separators. The question is whether this approach, or a variation thereof, can be extended to cover all monotone first-order optimization problems on such classes.
rows.source_text: It is an open problem whether some variation on the local search approach can give PTAS for all monotone FO optimization problems.
rows.status_semantics: The open problem asks whether a local search variation yields PTAS for all monotone first-order optimization problems on classes with strongly sublinear separators. Dvořák's follow-up work (arXiv:2103.08698, SWAT 2022) obtains PTAS for fractionally treewidth-fragile classes (which include all common strongly sublinear separator classes) and a QPTAS for all strongly sublinear separator classes, via Baker-style decomposition techniques rather than local search. The specific question about the local search approach remains unresolved, and PTAS for the full generality of strongly sublinear separator classes (beyond fractionally treewidth-fragile ones) is still open.
rows.defer_reason: 'Whether some variation on the local search approach can give PTAS' — vague meta question about a proof technique.

rows.title: Conjecture on LB and QLB equivalence
rows.paper_title: Information-theoretic lower bounds for quantum sorting
rows.statement_text: The two lower bounds $\mathrm{LB}(P)$ and $\mathrm{QLB}(P)$ are within a constant factor of each other for all posets $P$.
rows.context_text: In the Conclusion, the authors present a table comparing the classical quantity $\mathrm{LB}(P) = n(\ln n - H(P))$ and the quantum quantity $\mathrm{QLB}(P) = n(H_n - QH(P))$, where $H(P) = \min_{z \in C(P)} h(z)$ is the entropy and $QH(P) = \mathbb{E}_{z \in C(P)}[h(z)]$ is its averaged variant. The authors state their findings support a conjecture relating these two bounds, but the remainder of the statement is corrupted in the PDF source.
rows.source_text: The two lower bounds $\mathrm{LB}(P)$ and $\mathrm{QLB}(P)$ are within a constant factor of each other for all posets $P$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1902.06473 — Conjecture on LB and QLB equivalence
rows.defer_reason: Information-theoretic lower bounds for quantum sorting of posets — not graph/digraph/hypergraph theory; statement also noted as corrupted in source.

rows.title: Remark 1.2 (Open Problem on Sparse Densities)
rows.paper_title: Moderate deviations of subgraph counts in the Erdős-Rényi random graphs $G(n,m)$ and $G(n,p)$
rows.statement_text: The problem of finding the asymptotic rate across the whole range of sparse densities remains open.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 establishes the asymptotic rate for moderate deviations of subgraph counts in $G(n,m)$ when $t(n)$ is bounded away from 1, with a partial extension to sparser regimes satisfying $\max\{t^{1/2}n^{-1/2}\log n,\, t^{e-3/2}\} \ll \alpha_n \ll \min\{t^{2e-5/2}n^{1/2},\, t^{e+2}n^{1/2}\}$. The authors note they initially proved results only in the dense case $t\in(0,1)$ constant and have only partially extended to sparser regimes.
rows.source_text: The problem of finding the asymptotic rate across the whole range of sparse densities remains open.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1902.06830 — Remark 1.2 (Open Problem on Sparse Densities)
rows.status_semantics: The open problem of finding the asymptotic rate for moderate deviations of subgraph counts across the entire range of sparse densities in $G(n,m)$ and $G(n,p)$ remains open for general subgraphs. For triangle counts specifically, Alvarado, Gonçalves de Oliveira, and Griffiths (2023, published 2025) determined $\log\mathbb{P}(N_{\triangle}(G) > (1+\delta)p^3 n^3)$ up to a constant factor for densities above $n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2}$, and also handled the lower tail for cherries. Alvarado, Dias, and Griffiths (2024, published 2025) further resolved the lower tail for triangle counts in $G(n,m)$ in the regime $n^{-1} \ll \delta \ll n^{-3/4}$. The full conjecture—covering all subgraphs and all sparse density regimes—remains open.
rows.defer_reason: 'Finding the asymptotic rate across the whole range of sparse densities remains open' — determine-the-rate meta problem.

rows.title: Open question on $R_\ell(K_{1,2}, k)$ for odd $k$
rows.paper_title: List Ramsey numbers
rows.statement_text: We do not know whether $R_\ell(K_{1,2}, k)$ is $k + 1$ or $k + 2$ when $k$ is odd.
rows.context_text: For even $k$, the paper establishes $R_\ell(K_{1,2}, k) = k+1 = R(K_{1,2}, k)$ using the result of Häggkvist and Janssen that $\chi'_\ell(K_n) = \chi'(K_n)$ for odd $n$. The case of odd $k$ is equivalent to the List Colouring Conjecture for cliques of even order, which asks whether $\chi'_\ell(K_n)$ equals $n$ or $n-1$ for even $n$.
rows.source_text: We do not know whether $R_\ell(K_{1,2}, k)$ is $k + 1$ or $k + 2$ when $k$ is odd.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1902.07018 — Open question on $R_\ell(K_{1,2}, k)$ for odd $k$
rows.class_notes: Two-valued dichotomy question; list Ramsey number R_ell not defined in the record; equivalent to the List Colouring Conjecture for even cliques.

rows.title: Open problem on equality $R_\ell(K_r, 2) = R(K_r, 2)$ for $r > 3$
rows.paper_title: List Ramsey numbers
rows.statement_text: For $r > 3$ we cannot even decide the equality of the two Ramsey numbers of $K_r$ when $k = 2$.
rows.context_text: The classic probabilistic lower bound gives $2^{r/2} < R_\ell(K_r, 2) \leq R(K_r, 2) < 2^{2r}$. The equality $R_\ell(K_3, 2) = 6 = R(K_3, 2)$ is easily verified, but for larger complete graphs the question of whether the list and ordinary 2-colour Ramsey numbers coincide is entirely open.
rows.source_text: For $r > 3$ we cannot even decide the equality of the two Ramsey numbers of $K_r$ when $k = 2$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1902.07018 — Open problem on equality $R_\ell(K_r, 2) = R(K_r, 2)$ for $r > 3$
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no family (does R_list(K_r,2)=R(K_r,2) for r>3?) but list Ramsey number is a new finite primitive to build.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (Introduction) — analogue of Kneser's theorem
rows.paper_title: Concatenating bipartite graphs
rows.statement_text: Maybe something like [the bound in 1.3 being always best possible] is true: for all $x, y \in (0, 1]$, there is an integer $k > 0$ with $\phi(x, y) = \frac{\lceil kx \rceil + \lceil ky \rceil - 1}{k}$.
rows.context_text: The authors were motivated by Kneser's theorem from additive group theory and originally entertained a 'wild conjecture' that the upper bound in 1.3 is always tight. This conjecture is stated to be false, but the authors suggest that something like it may still be true, leaving the correct characterisation of $\phi(x,y)$ open.
rows.source_text: Maybe something like [the bound in 1.3 being always best possible] is true: for all $x, y \in (0, 1]$, there is an integer $k > 0$ with $\phi(x, y) = \frac{\lceil kx \rceil + \lceil ky \rceil - 1}{k}$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1902.10878 — Informal Conjecture (Introduction) — analogue of Kneser's theorem
rows.class_notes: Formula candidate is precise but phi(x,y) (concatenation density function) is paper-local and undefined in context; also hedged 'maybe something like'.

rows.title: Open Question — symmetry of $\psi$ (biconstrained case)
rows.paper_title: Concatenating bipartite graphs
rows.statement_text: Does $\psi(x, y) = \psi(y, x)$ hold for all $x, y \in (0, 1]$?
rows.context_text: The authors prove Theorem 2.3: $\phi(x,y) = \phi(y,x)$ for all $x,y \in (0,1]$, via a linear-programming argument. They remark immediately after that they have been unable to establish the analogous symmetry for the biconstrained function $\psi$, even though they have no counterexample.
rows.source_text: Does $\psi(x, y) = \psi(y, x)$ hold for all $x, y \in (0, 1]$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1902.10878 — Open Question — symmetry of $\psi$ (biconstrained case)
rows.class_notes: Clean symmetry question but the biconstrained function psi is paper-local and not defined in context_text.

rows.title: Combinatorial Polynomial-Time Algorithm for MIS/MWIS in Perfect Graphs
rows.paper_title: On the Maximum Weight Independent Set Problem in graphs without induced cycles of length at least five
rows.statement_text: No combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm is known for any of MIS, MWIS, MC, and MWC in perfect graphs; finding one is a major open problem in the field. At the moment we do not even have a polynomial-time combinatorial algorithm to solve MIS in perfect graphs with no hole of length four.
rows.context_text: The Grötschel–Lovász–Schrijver algorithm solves MWIS on perfect graphs via the ellipsoid method, which the authors distinguish from combinatorial algorithms (roughly, algorithms not using division and operating directly on vertices and edges). Even the restricted case of MIS in perfect graphs with no $C_4$ (hole of length four) lacks a combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm.
rows.source_text: No combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm is known for any of MIS, MWIS, MC, and MWC in perfect graphs; finding one is a major open problem in the field. At the moment we do not even have a polynomial-time combinatorial algorithm to solve MIS in perfect graphs with no hole of length four.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1903.04761 — Combinatorial Polynomial-Time Algorithm for MIS/MWIS in Perfect Graphs
rows.status_semantics: The open problem of finding a combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm for MIS/MWIS in perfect graphs remains largely unresolved. Abrishami and Chudnovsky (arXiv:2110.00108, 2021) made partial progress by giving a combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm for MWIS in perfect graphs of bounded degree that contain no prism and no hole of length four as induced subgraphs—directly addressing the C4-free subcase highlighted in the source paper, but under additional structural restrictions. No combinatorial poly-time algorithm is known for all perfect graphs or even for all C4-free perfect graphs without the bounded-degree and prism-free conditions.
rows.defer_reason: 'Combinatorial algorithm' is only informally delimited (context says 'roughly'); not a single precise proposition.

rows.title: FPT Algorithm for MWIS in (Long-Hole, k-Prism)-Free Graphs
rows.paper_title: On the Maximum Weight Independent Set Problem in graphs without induced cycles of length at least five
rows.statement_text: We do not see how to turn Theorem 1.1 into an FPT-algorithm (without using the result of [1]).
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 gives a polynomial-time algorithm running in time $n^{O(k)}$ for MWIS in (long-hole, $k$-prism)-free $n$-vertex graphs for any fixed integer $k > 0$. Immediately after stating the theorem the authors remark that extending this to a fixed-parameter tractable algorithm parameterized by $k$ appears to be out of reach with their techniques, unless the result of Abrishami et al. [1] is invoked.
rows.source_text: We do not see how to turn Theorem 1.1 into an FPT-algorithm (without using the result of [1]).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1903.04761 — FPT Algorithm for MWIS in (Long-Hole, k-Prism)-Free Graphs
rows.defer_reason: Meta remark about the authors' technique ('we do not see how'), relative to Theorem 1.1 and avoidance of [1]; not a posed proposition.

rows.title: Informal question on Behrend-style sets avoiding affine patterns with mixed-sign coefficients
rows.paper_title: Triforce and Corners
rows.statement_text: We do not know if there are subsets of $[N]$, in the style of Behrend's construction, with $N^{1-o(1)}$ elements avoiding such patterns (e.g., $2x + 2y = 3w + z$).
rows.context_text: In Section 3.1, the authors discuss why the Ruzsa–Bergelson–Host–Kra approach for constructing popular-difference counterexamples fails for certain linear identities $a_1P(b_1)+\cdots+a_4P(b_4)=0$ in which there are two positive and two negative $a_i$'s (rather than the symmetric case). They note that it is unknown whether large Behrend-style sets avoiding such patterns exist.
rows.source_text: We do not know if there are subsets of $[N]$, in the style of Behrend's construction, with $N^{1-o(1)}$ elements avoiding such patterns (e.g., $2x + 2y = 3w + z$).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1903.04863 — Informal question on Behrend-style sets avoiding affine patterns with mixed-sign coefficients
rows.defer_reason: Additive combinatorics (Behrend-style sets in [N] avoiding linear patterns), no graph content.

rows.title: Informal conjecture on extending Mandache's Theorem 1.1 to all abelian groups
rows.paper_title: Triforce and Corners
rows.statement_text: It seems likely that the result [the lower bound $M(\delta) \geq m_{\mathrm{convex}}(\delta)$] can be extended to all abelian groups via Bohr sets, though as far as we know this has not yet been worked out.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 (Mandache) establishes the lower bound $M(\delta) \geq m_{\mathrm{convex}}(\delta)$ only for $G_n = \mathbb{F}_p^n$ with fixed prime $p$, via an arithmetic regularity argument combined with Fourier-analytic ideas. The authors believe a Bohr-set analogue would extend this to arbitrary abelian groups.
rows.source_text: It seems likely that the result [the lower bound $M(\delta) \geq m_{\mathrm{convex}}(\delta)$] can be extended to all abelian groups via Bohr sets, though as far as we know this has not yet been worked out.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1903.04863 — Informal conjecture on extending Mandache's Theorem 1.1 to all abelian groups
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that Mandache's lower bound $M(\delta) \geq m_{\mathrm{convex}}(\delta)$ could be extended to all abelian groups via Bohr sets was resolved by Aaron Berger in a paper submitted in September 2019, just months after the Fox–Sah–Sawhney–Stoner–Zhao preprint. Berger generalizes Mandache's popular-differences result for corners from finite fields $\mathbb{F}_p^n$ to all compact abelian groups (which includes all finite abelian groups) and to corners in $\mathbb{Z}^2$. The paper was published in the Mathematical Proceedings of the Cambridge Philosophical Society in 2021.
rows.defer_reason: Arithmetic combinatorics over abelian groups (popular differences, Bohr sets), not graph theory.
rows.class_notes: Marked solved; still out of scope.

rows.title: Optimal degree bounds $D_c$ for small $c$
rows.paper_title: Bounded maximum degree conjecture holds precisely for $c$-crossing-critical graphs with $c \leq 12$
rows.statement_text: Determine the optimal values of $D_c$ for each $c \in \{2, 3, \ldots, 12\}$, where $D_c$ is the smallest integer such that every $c$-crossing-critical graph has maximum degree at most $D_c$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 guarantees the existence of finite bounds $D_c$ for each $c \leq 12$, but the proof is asymptotic and does not yield explicit values. The paper notes $D_1 = 4$, $D_2 \geq 8$, and that the optimal $D_2$ should be within reach using existing characterisation results, while the remaining bounds are left to future investigations.
rows.source_text: Determine the optimal values of $D_c$ for each $c \in \{2, 3, \ldots, 12\}$, where $D_c$ is the smallest integer such that every $c$-crossing-critical graph has maximum degree at most $D_c$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1903.05363 — Optimal degree bounds $D_c$ for small $c$
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the optimal values of D_c' is a determine-the-value task, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Open Question on Convex Unit Distance Realization of Graphs $G_k$
rows.paper_title: Convexly independent subsets of Minkowski sums of convex polygons
rows.statement_text: Can the graphs $G_k$ arising from the construction be realized as convex unit distance graphs?
rows.context_text: The construction yields a family of bipartite graphs $(G_k)_{k\geq 1}$ with $2^{k+1}$ vertices and $(k+2)2^{k-1}$ edges, connected to the Erdős–Moser unit distance problem for convex $n$-gons. The authors note that if $G_k$ cannot be so realized, finding a forbidden pattern in $G_k$ would extend the list of forbidden matrices of Aggarwal [1], since none of those matrices appears in $G_k$.
rows.source_text: Can the graphs $G_k$ arising from the construction be realized as convex unit distance graphs?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1903.11287 — Open Question on Convex Unit Distance Realization of Graphs $G_k$
rows.defer_reason: Discrete geometry (convex unit-distance realizability in the plane), plus paper-local family G_k.

rows.title: Optimal n for $L^d_n$-list-colorability
rows.paper_title: Mixing properties of colorings of the $\mathbb{Z}^d$ lattice
rows.statement_text: Fixing $d$, what is the optimal value of $n$ such that $[n]^d$ is $L^d_n$-list-colorable?
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.2 establishes that $[n]^d$ is $L^d_n$-list-colorable whenever $n \geq d+2$. The paper shows this threshold is not achieved for $n < d+2$ in specific cases (e.g., $[2]^3$ is not $L^3_2$-list-colorable), making the optimal threshold an open question whose notion of optimality is justified by Proposition 3.3.
rows.source_text: Fixing $d$, what is the optimal value of $n$ such that $[n]^d$ is $L^d_n$-list-colorable?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the optimal value of n' is a determine-the-threshold question; L^d_n lists are also paper-local.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.2
rows.paper_title: Isoperimetry, Stability, and Irredundance in Direct Products
rows.statement_text: If $H_1, \ldots, H_n$ are complete multipartite graphs and $G = \prod_{i=1}^{n} H_i$, then $\alpha(G) = IR(G)$.
rows.context_text: The authors propose this as a further strengthening of Burcroff's Conjecture 1.1, removing the assumption that the complete multipartite graphs in the direct product are balanced. It is stated at the end of the introduction after the main results are summarised.
rows.source_text: If $H_1, \ldots, H_n$ are complete multipartite graphs and $G = \prod_{i=1}^{n} H_i$, then $\alpha(G) = IR(G)$.
rows.class_notes: Direct product, independence number and upper irredundance IR are all standard finite vocabulary; strengthens Burcroff's conjecture.

rows.title: Open Question: Recognition of Switchable Graphs
rows.paper_title: The Perfect Matching Reconfiguration Problem
rows.statement_text: It is not clear whether graphs in the Switchable class (the hereditary class of graphs for which the reconfiguration graph of perfect matchings under flips is connected) can be recognized in polynomial time.
rows.context_text: The authors of [14] characterize the hereditary class of general (non-bipartite) graphs for which the reconfiguration graph of perfect matchings under flips is connected, calling it Switchable. The paper notes that the recognition complexity of this class remains open.
rows.source_text: It is not clear whether graphs in the Switchable class (the hereditary class of graphs for which the reconfiguration graph of perfect matchings under flips is connected) can be recognized in polynomial time.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1904.06184 — Open Question: Recognition of Switchable Graphs
rows.class_notes: Precise poly-time recognition question; Switchable class is defined in context via connectivity of the perfect-matching flip reconfiguration graph.

rows.title: Conjecture 5
rows.paper_title: Every planar graph with $Δ\geqslant 8$ is totally $(Δ+2)$-choosable
rows.statement_text: Every simple graph $G$ satisfies $\chi''_\ell(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 2$.
rows.context_text: This is the list coloring analogue of the Total Coloring Conjecture (Conjecture 4), asserting that the list total chromatic number never exceeds $\Delta + 2$. It is stated without a bibliographic citation in the paper, appearing as a natural companion to Conjecture 4. The paper's main result (Corollary 1) proves Conjecture 5 for all planar graphs with $\Delta \geq 8$.
rows.source_text: Every simple graph $G$ satisfies $\chi''_\ell(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 2$.
rows.class_notes: List Total Coloring Conjecture (chi''_list <= Delta+2); classical, fully finite.

rows.title: Open Problem (induced st-path of excess length three)
rows.paper_title: Detecting a long odd hole
rows.statement_text: Is there a polynomial-time algorithm to test whether a graph contains an induced path between specified vertices $s, t$ of length at least three more than the shortest $st$-path?
rows.context_text: Berger, Spirkl, and Seymour (including a co-author of the present paper) have a poly-time algorithm to detect an induced $st$-path longer than the shortest $st$-path. Whether a poly-time algorithm exists to detect one that is at least three longer remains open, as noted in the introduction.
rows.source_text: Is there a polynomial-time algorithm to test whether a graph contains an induced path between specified vertices $s, t$ of length at least three more than the shortest $st$-path?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1904.12273 — Open Problem (induced st-path of excess length three)
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no poly-time detection question for induced st-path of excess length three.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (heavy path extension)
rows.paper_title: Detecting a long odd hole
rows.statement_text: If $C$ is a shortest long odd hole in a graph $G$ and $M$ is a set of $C$-major vertices such that one vertex of $M$ is nonadjacent to all the others, then there exists a path of $C$ of bounded length such that every vertex of $M$ has a neighbour in that path.
rows.context_text: The authors attempted to extend the 'heavy edge' lemma from the odd hole algorithm of [6] to the long odd hole setting: a 'heavy path' of bounded length containing a neighbour of every vertex in $M$ would suffice for constructing the cleaning list. The authors believe the extension is plausible but were unable to prove it, and had to use a more complicated replacement argument instead.
rows.source_text: If $C$ is a shortest long odd hole in a graph $G$ and $M$ is a set of $C$-major vertices such that one vertex of $M$ is nonadjacent to all the others, then there exists a path of $C$ of bounded length such that every vertex of $M$ has a neighbour in that path.
rows.class_notes: Relies on paper-local notions (C-major vertices, cleaning list) and an unquantified 'bounded length'.

rows.title: Open Question (running time improvement for long hole detection)
rows.paper_title: Detecting a long odd hole
rows.statement_text: Can the running times of $O(|G|^{\ell+1})$ for detecting a long hole and $O(|G|^{20\ell+40})$ for detecting a long odd hole — both $|G|^{O(\ell)}$ — be substantially improved for fixed $\ell$?
rows.context_text: The paper gives algorithms with running time $|G|^{O(\ell)}$ for both long hole and long odd hole detection. Both problems are NP-hard when $\ell$ is part of the input, but whether the dependence on $\ell$ in the exponent can be reduced for fixed $\ell$ is left as an open question.
rows.source_text: Can the running times of $O(|G|^{\ell+1})$ for detecting a long hole and $O(|G|^{20\ell+40})$ for detecting a long odd hole — both $|G|^{O(\ell)}$ — be substantially improved for fixed $\ell$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1904.12273 — Open Question (running time improvement for long hole detection)
rows.defer_reason: 'Can the running times be substantially improved' is an improve-the-bound question, not a proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.1
rows.paper_title: Books versus triangles at the extremal density
rows.statement_text: If $n/6 \leq b < n/4$, then every graph on $n$ vertices with at least $\lfloor n^2/4 \rfloor$ edges and book number at most $b$ which is not the balanced complete bipartite graph has at least $b^2(n - 4b)$ triangles, with equality if and only if the graph is $S_{b,n}$.
rows.context_text: The authors introduce the 3-prism blow-up $S_{b,n}$ — formed by blowing up the 3-prism graph with four parts of size $b$ and two parts of size $\lfloor(n-4b)/2\rfloor$ and $\lceil(n-4b)/2\rceil$ — as the conjectured extremal construction. The conjecture implies an asymptotically tight bound on $t(n,b)$ for $n/6 \leq b \leq n/4 - \omega(1)$, fully addressing Mubayi's question about the $\gamma(\beta)$ dependency. A footnote notes the construction is essentially identical to one in an earlier arXiv version of Mubayi's paper.
rows.source_text: If $n/6 \leq b < n/4$, then every graph on $n$ vertices with at least $\lfloor n^2/4 \rfloor$ edges and book number at most $b$ which is not the balanced complete bipartite graph has at least $b^2(n - 4b)$ triangles, with equality if and only if the graph is $S_{b,n}$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.1 remains open in full generality. The authors themselves proved it for the boundary case b = n/6 and for the near-upper-boundary range 0.2495n ≤ b < n/4, leaving the bulk of the interval n/6 < b < 0.2495n unresolved. No subsequent paper resolving the remaining cases was found in the indexed literature as of May 2026.
rows.class_notes: Fully quantified finite extremal statement; book number is standard and S_{b,n} is defined in context.

rows.title: Open Problem: asymptotic behavior of $Q(s,r)$ when $s$ and $r$ grow together
rows.paper_title: On the product dimension of clique factors
rows.statement_text: Determine the asymptotic behavior of $Q(s, r)$ when $s$ and $r$ grow together (i.e., when $s$ is not fixed as $r \to \infty$).
rows.context_text: The paper resolves $Q(s,r) = (1+o(1))\log_2 r$ for every fixed $s$ as $r \to \infty$, but explicitly states in the abstract that the problem of determining the asymptotic behavior of $Q(s,r)$ when both $s$ and $r$ grow simultaneously remains open.
rows.source_text: Determine the asymptotic behavior of $Q(s, r)$ when $s$ and $r$ grow together (i.e., when $s$ is not fixed as $r \to \infty$).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1905.10483 — Open Problem: asymptotic behavior of $Q(s,r)$ when $s$ and $r$ grow together
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the asymptotic behavior of Q(s,r)' with s,r growing together is a determine-asymptotics task, not a proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.12
rows.paper_title: Combinatorial anti-concentration inequalities, with applications
rows.statement_text: Fix $p \in (0,1)$ and fix a graph $H$ with $h$ non-isolated vertices. Let $G \in G(n,p)$. Then for any $x \in \mathbb{N}$, $$\Pr(X_H = x) = O\!\left(1/\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(X_H)}\right) = O\!\left(1/n^{h-1}\right).$$
rows.context_text: The authors establish via Corollary 1.11 that $\Pr(X_H = x) = O(1/\sqrt{r})$ where $r$ is the number of edge-disjoint copies of $H$ in $G$, giving $O(1/n)$ in $G(n,p)$, but believe this is far from optimal. The conjectured bound matches the Gaussian scale and is best-possible given the known CLT for $X_H$. Theorem 1.14 confirms the conjecture for cliques $H = K_h$.
rows.source_text: Fix $p \in (0,1)$ and fix a graph $H$ with $h$ non-isolated vertices. Let $G \in G(n,p)$. Then for any $x \in \mathbb{N}$, $$\Pr(X_H = x) = O\!\left(1/\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(X_H)}\right) = O\!\left(1/n^{h-1}\right).$$
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.12 (that Pr(X_H = x) = O(1/sqrt(Var(X_H))) = O(1/n^{h-1}) for any fixed graph H with h non-isolated vertices in G(n,p)) has been resolved affirmatively for connected graphs H by Sah and Sawhney (arXiv:2006.11369, JLMS 2022), who established a local CLT for connected subgraph counts achieving the optimal scale. However, the same paper provides a counterexample showing the bound fails for certain disconnected graphs H, so the conjecture as stated for all graphs with non-isolated vertices is disproved. A companion paper (arXiv:1905.12749, Ann. Prob. 2021) by the same authors proved the near-optimal bound O(n^{1-h+o(1)}) for connected H, confirming the conjecture up to a subpolynomial factor.
rows.class_notes: Precise anti-concentration bound for subgraph counts in G(n,p); needs probability-on-graphs plus O() wrapper.

rows.title: Question 1
rows.paper_title: A Note on Graphs of Dichromatic Number 2
rows.statement_text: What is the largest minor-closed class $\mathcal{G}_2$ of undirected graphs with dichromatic number at most 2?
rows.context_text: The authors note that while $\vec{\chi}(K_6) = 2$ and $\vec{\chi}(K_7) = 3$, so $\mathcal{G}_2$ is a subclass of the $K_7$-minor-free graphs, it seems to be much smaller: there exist $K_{3,3}$-minor-free graphs with dichromatic number greater than 2.
rows.source_text: What is the largest minor-closed class $\mathcal{G}_2$ of undirected graphs with dichromatic number at most 2?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the largest minor-closed class' is a characterization question, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Variant of partitioning_planar_digraphs: that OPG problem asserts planar (di)graphs have dichromatic number <= 2, i.e. planar graphs lie in the class G_2 asked about here.

rows.title: Open problem: Combinatorial algorithm for MIS on perfect graphs
rows.paper_title: The independent set problem is FPT for even-hole-free graphs
rows.statement_text: It remains an open question to find a combinatorial algorithm for the maximum independent set problem on perfect graphs. In fact, we do not even have a combinatorial FPT algorithm for the maximum independent set problem on perfect graphs.
rows.context_text: The maximum independent set problem on perfect graphs is solvable in polynomial time via the ellipsoid method, but a combinatorial algorithm (i.e., one not relying on the ellipsoid method) is unknown. This is mentioned as a side remark motivating the study of related graph classes.
rows.source_text: It remains an open question to find a combinatorial algorithm for the maximum independent set problem on perfect graphs. In fact, we do not even have a combinatorial FPT algorithm for the maximum independent set problem on perfect graphs.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1907.01083 — Open problem: Combinatorial algorithm for MIS on perfect graphs
rows.defer_reason: 'Combinatorial algorithm' (not ellipsoid-based) is an informal notion; not a precise proposition.
rows.class_notes: Restates the combinatorial-MIS-on-perfect-graphs open problem also recorded at axv_1903_04761_00.

rows.title: Problem 1
rows.paper_title: Coloring count cones of planar graphs
rows.statement_text: Does there exist a polynomial-time algorithm which, given a near-triangulation $G$ with the outer face bounded by a 4-cycle $C$ and a 4-coloring $\psi$ of $C$, correctly decides whether $\psi$ extends to a 4-coloring of $G$?
rows.context_text: The authors note that even very basic precoloring extension questions for planar graphs are wide open. There exist infinitely many near-triangulations $G$ whose outer 4-cycle has a precoloring that does not extend to a 4-coloring of $G$, yet no good characterization of such graphs is known.
rows.source_text: Does there exist a polynomial-time algorithm which, given a near-triangulation $G$ with the outer face bounded by a 4-cycle $C$ and a 4-coloring $\psi$ of $C$, correctly decides whether $\psi$ extends to a 4-coloring of $G$?
rows.status_semantics: Problem 1 asks whether a polynomial-time algorithm exists to decide if a 4-coloring of the outer 4-cycle of a near-triangulation extends to the whole graph; the general question remains open. Dvořák, Moore, Seifrtová, and Šámal (arXiv:2312.13061) provide partial progress by giving linear-time algorithms for the special subclass of planar near-Eulerian-triangulations (all interior vertices have even degree) when the outer face has length at most 5, which covers the 4-cycle case within that restricted class. No resolution of the full statement for arbitrary near-triangulations was found in the literature.
rows.class_notes: Precise poly-time decision question for 4-precoloring extension on near-triangulations; also needs planarity layer.

rows.title: Conjecture 8
rows.paper_title: Coloring count cones of planar graphs
rows.statement_text: Every plane near-cubic graph $\tilde{G}$ with $d(\tilde{G}) = 5$ satisfies $n_{\tilde{G}} \in B'_5$.
rows.context_text: The cone $B'_5$ is defined as the subcone of $B_5$ spanned by the rays $\operatorname{ray}(\tilde{R}_{5,1}), \ldots, \operatorname{ray}(\tilde{R}_{5,11})$, excluding the ray corresponding to the non-planar graph $\tilde{R}_{5,12}$. By Lemma 7, membership of $n_{\tilde{G}}$ in the full cone $B_5$ is equivalent to the Four Color Theorem, so this conjecture is a strict strengthening of the Four Color Theorem. The authors verify it for all plane near-cubic graphs with fewer than 30 vertices.
rows.source_text: Every plane near-cubic graph $\tilde{G}$ with $d(\tilde{G}) = 5$ satisfies $n_{\tilde{G}} \in B'_5$.
rows.class_notes: Depends on paper-local objects (vector n_G, cones B_5 and B'_5, rays of graphs R_{5,i}) not defined in context; a strengthening of the Four Color Theorem.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.3
rows.paper_title: Quasi-polynomial time approximation schemes for the Maximum Weight Independent Set Problem in H-free graphs
rows.statement_text: For every forest $H$ of maximum degree at most three, MWIS admits a QPTAS and a subexponential-time algorithm in the class of graphs that do not contain any subdivision of $H$ as an induced subgraph.
rows.context_text: Following Theorems 1.1 and 1.2, which establish QPTASes and subexponential-time algorithms for MWIS in $H$-free graphs where every component of $H$ is a path or a subdivided claw, the authors conjecture a broader generalization to all forests of maximum degree at most three and graphs excluding any subdivision of $H$ as an induced subgraph. The paper itself proves Conjecture 1.3 for $H$ being a forest with at most three vertices of degree three (with a $2^{O(|V(G)|^{40/41}\log|V(G)|)}$ subexponential bound), but the full generality remains open.
rows.source_text: For every forest $H$ of maximum degree at most three, MWIS admits a QPTAS and a subexponential-time algorithm in the class of graphs that do not contain any subdivision of $H$ as an induced subgraph.
rows.class_notes: Precise algorithmic conjecture (QPTAS + subexponential MWIS in H-subdivision-free graphs); QPTAS notion needs the complexity foundation.

rows.title: Question on extremal self-annihilating subspaces (Theorem 2.3)
rows.paper_title: Combinatorics in the exterior algebra and the Bollobás Two Families Theorem
rows.statement_text: For $r < n/2$, are all extremal self-annihilating subspaces $W \subseteq \bigwedge^r V$ with $\dim W = \binom{n-1}{r-1}$ of the form $\{v \wedge z : z \in \bigwedge^{r-1} V\}$ for some fixed $v \in V$?
rows.context_text: Theorem 2.3 shows that a self-annihilating subspace $W \subseteq \bigwedge^r V$ satisfies $\dim W \leq \binom{n-1}{r-1}$ for $r \leq n/2$, with equality achieved by spaces of the form $\{v \wedge z : z \in \bigwedge^{r-1} V\}$. The authors verify this characterization for $r = 1$ and $r = 2$ and ask whether it holds in general.
rows.source_text: For $r < n/2$, are all extremal self-annihilating subspaces $W \subseteq \bigwedge^r V$ with $\dim W = \binom{n-1}{r-1}$ of the form $\{v \wedge z : z \in \bigwedge^{r-1} V\}$ for some fixed $v \in V$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1907.06019 — Question on extremal self-annihilating subspaces (Theorem 2.3)
rows.defer_reason: Exterior algebra / linear algebra (self-annihilating subspaces), not graph or hypergraph theory.

rows.title: Question on extremal mutually annihilating pairs (Theorem 2.4)
rows.paper_title: Combinatorics in the exterior algebra and the Bollobás Two Families Theorem
rows.statement_text: For $r, s < n/2$, characterize all pairs of subspaces $U \subseteq \bigwedge^r V$ and $W \subseteq \bigwedge^s V$ that are mutually annihilating and attain $\dim U \cdot \dim W = \binom{n-1}{r-1}\binom{n-1}{s-1}$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 2.4 bounds $\dim U \cdot \dim W$ for mutually annihilating pairs $U \subseteq \bigwedge^r V$, $W \subseteq \bigwedge^s V$ with $r, s \leq n/2$. Equality is achieved by fixing $v \in V$ and setting $U = \{v \wedge z : z \in \bigwedge^{r-1} V\}$ and $W = \{v \wedge z : z \in \bigwedge^{s-1} V\}$, and the authors ask for a full characterization of all extremal examples.
rows.source_text: For $r, s < n/2$, characterize all pairs of subspaces $U \subseteq \bigwedge^r V$ and $W \subseteq \bigwedge^s V$ that are mutually annihilating and attain $\dim U \cdot \dim W = \binom{n-1}{r-1}\binom{n-1}{s-1}$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1907.06019 — Question on extremal mutually annihilating pairs (Theorem 2.4)
rows.defer_reason: Exterior algebra characterization problem, not graph theory (and 'characterize' is also meta).

rows.title: Gap problem for 1-ball reconstruction threshold
rows.paper_title: Shotgun reconstruction in the hypercube
rows.statement_text: Determine the correct threshold $q^*(n)$ such that almost every $q$-colouring of the hypercube $Q_n$ is reconstructible from the multiset of its coloured 1-balls, narrowing the gap between the lower bound $q = \Omega(n)$ and the upper bound $q \geq n^{2+\varepsilon}$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.2 shows that for $q \geq n^{2+\varepsilon}$ almost every $q$-colouring of $Q_n$ is 1-distinguishable, while a counting argument shows that $\Omega(n)$ colours are necessary for reconstruction from 1-balls. The authors note explicitly that 'it would be interesting to narrow the gap', with further discussion deferred to Section 6 (open questions).
rows.source_text: Determine the correct threshold $q^*(n)$ such that almost every $q$-colouring of the hypercube $Q_n$ is reconstructible from the multiset of its coloured 1-balls, narrowing the gap between the lower bound $q = \Omega(n)$ and the upper bound $q \geq n^{2+\varepsilon}$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1907.07250 — Gap problem for 1-ball reconstruction threshold
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the correct threshold / narrow the gap' is an improve-the-bounds task, not a proposition.
rows.class_notes: Shotgun reconstruction of hypercube colourings; graph-flavored but deferred as meta.

rows.title: Open problem on minimum independence ratio of Mycielski graphs
rows.paper_title: Revisiting a theorem by Folkman on graph colouring
rows.statement_text: The exact value of the minimum independence ratio of Mycielski graphs seems to be unknown.
rows.context_text: While Cropper, Gyárfás and Lehel [CGL06] established asymptotic bounds $c_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\log m}}{m} \leq \mathrm{mir}(M_s) \leq c_2 \cdot \frac{\log m}{m}$ for every integer $m > 3$ (where $s$ is one less than the Ramsey number $R(3,m)$), no exact formula for $\mathrm{mir}(M_k)$ is known. The authors flag this gap while discussing why replacing the constant $2$ by a larger integer $k$ fails to generalise Observation 1.1.
rows.source_text: The exact value of the minimum independence ratio of Mycielski graphs seems to be unknown.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1907.11429 — Open problem on minimum independence ratio of Mycielski graphs
rows.defer_reason: 'Exact value seems to be unknown' is a determine-the-value remark, not a posed proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 5
rows.paper_title: Edge-partitioning 3-edge-connected graphs into paths
rows.statement_text: There is a function $f$ such that, for any fixed tree $T$ with $m$ leaves, every $f(m)$-edge-connected graph with minimum degree at least $f(|E(T)|)$ and number of edges divisible by $|E(T)|$ has a $T$-decomposition.
rows.context_text: This conjecture is posed by the paper's authors as an alternative to Conjecture 2 (which fails for trees of maximum degree three), bounding the required edge-connectivity in terms of the number of leaves of $T$ rather than its maximum degree. The paper's main result implies $f(2) = 3$ is achievable, and the authors note that establishing the existence of $f(3)$ would already contain the claw case.
rows.source_text: There is a function $f$ such that, for any fixed tree $T$ with $m$ leaves, every $f(m)$-edge-connected graph with minimum degree at least $f(|E(T)|)$ and number of edges divisible by $|E(T)|$ has a $T$-decomposition.
rows.class_notes: Existence-of-a-function conjecture for T-decompositions of highly edge-connected graphs; needs the exists-f bounded wrapper.

rows.title: Conjecture 2
rows.paper_title: Bounding the number of cycles in a graph in terms of its degree sequence
rows.statement_text: Every simple graph with $m$ edges has at most $O(\kappa_1^m)$ cycles, where $\kappa_1 = (2+2\sqrt{2})^{1/5} \approx 1.3701$.
rows.context_text: The Arman–Tsaturian construction [2] gives simple graphs with $\Omega(\kappa_1^m)$ cycles, which is the best known lower bound. The authors verified by computer several similar constructions and could not find a better one, leading them to conjecture that this construction is optimal. Remark 9 notes that any counterexample would necessarily have average degree in the range $(4.24, 7.18)$.
rows.source_text: Every simple graph with $m$ edges has at most $O(\kappa_1^m)$ cycles, where $\kappa_1 = (2+2\sqrt{2})^{1/5} \approx 1.3701$.
rows.class_notes: O(kappa_1^m) bound with irrational constant (2+2*sqrt(2))^(1/5) needs reals/asymptotic wrapper.

rows.title: Conjecture 1
rows.paper_title: Independence number in triangle-free graphs avoiding a minor
rows.statement_text: For every positive integer $t$, if an $n$-vertex graph $G$ does not contain $K_{t+1}$ as a minor then $\alpha(G) \geq n/t$.
rows.context_text: This conjecture is a natural weakening of Hadwiger's conjecture: since Hadwiger's conjecture would imply every $n$-vertex $K_{t+1}$-minor-free graph has chromatic number at most $t$ and thus an independent set of size at least $n/t$, this bound has attracted considerable attention (see Seymour's survey [31]). Duchet and Meyniel proved the bound holds within a factor of 2 in 1982, and subsequent improvements have been made by Fox, Balogh–Kostochka, and others. The present paper studies this conjecture restricted to triangle-free graphs.
rows.source_text: For every positive integer $t$, if an $n$-vertex graph $G$ does not contain $K_{t+1}$ as a minor then $\alpha(G) \geq n/t$.
rows.class_notes: Classical weakening of Hadwiger: K_{t+1}-minor-free implies alpha >= n/t; fully finite standard vocabulary.

rows.title: Conjecture on $MR_t(s,k)$ for $s \geq 4$
rows.paper_title: Independence number in triangle-free graphs avoiding a minor
rows.statement_text: For all $s \geq 4$, a similar bound must hold: $MR_t(s, k) \leq t^{1-\varepsilon}k$ for sufficiently large $t$, and here $\varepsilon$ cannot be larger than $1/s$.
rows.context_text: The paper's main result (Theorem 1) establishes $MR_t(3,k) \leq t^{1-\varepsilon}k$ for any $0 < \varepsilon < 1/26$ and all sufficiently large $t$; Erdős's construction (Theorem 2) shows $\varepsilon \leq 1/3$ is essentially tight for $s=3$. The authors conjecture in Section 4 that an analogous upper bound holds for $K_s$-free graphs with $s \geq 4$, with tightness at $\varepsilon = 1/s$.
rows.source_text: For all $s \geq 4$, a similar bound must hold: $MR_t(s, k) \leq t^{1-\varepsilon}k$ for sufficiently large $t$, and here $\varepsilon$ cannot be larger than $1/s$.
rows.class_notes: MR_t(s,k) is paper-local and not defined in context; statement also hedged ('a similar bound must hold', 'sufficiently large t').

rows.title: Open problem on quantum ergodicity over small spectral windows (Remark 1.2)
rows.paper_title: High-girth near-Ramanujan graphs with localized eigenvectors
rows.statement_text: The problem of controlling the other eigenvectors well enough to say that the quantum-ergodicity average in a small spectral window $I \subset (-2\sqrt{d}, 2\sqrt{d})$ of width roughly $1/\log(m)$ is not equidistributed is not pursued here and remains open. It would also be interesting to determine whether the constructions of Theorem 1.3 can prove a lower bound on the length of the smallest such window that is possible.
rows.context_text: The works [ALM15, BLML15] prove quantum ergodicity on sequences of expander graphs when the spectral average (3) is taken over a window $I$ of width roughly $1/\log(m)$. Theorem 1.3 produces many localized eigenvectors concentrated in a very small spectral window due to high eigenvalue multiplicity, but whether this implies that the QE average itself fails to be equidistributed in that window requires controlling all other eigenvectors in the window, which the paper does not achieve.
rows.source_text: The problem of controlling the other eigenvectors well enough to say that the quantum-ergodicity average in a small spectral window $I \subset (-2\sqrt{d}, 2\sqrt{d})$ of width roughly $1/\log(m)$ is not equidistributed is not pursued here and remains open. It would also be interesting to determine whether the constructions of Theorem 1.3 can prove a lower bound on the length of the smallest such window that is possible.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1908.03694 — Open problem on quantum ergodicity over small spectral windows (Remark 1.2)
rows.defer_reason: Vague 'remains open / would be interesting' about quantum-ergodicity equidistribution in small spectral windows; no single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Spectral graph theory flavored but analytic (QE averages on expander sequences).

rows.title: Question 1.12
rows.paper_title: Avoidable paths in graphs
rows.statement_text: For every positive integer $k$, does every graph $G$ either not contain two disjoint $P_k$, or contain two disjoint avoidable $P_k$?
rows.context_text: The analogous statement for non-adjacent avoidable $P_k$ is established as Corollary 1.11. The authors note the answer is positive for $k \in \{1,2\}$ by results of Beisegel et al., but then provide an explicit counterexample for every $k \geq 3$ (a cycle on $2k-1$ vertices with one extra vertex adjacent to two consecutive cycle vertices), resolving the question negatively within the same paper.
rows.source_text: For every positive integer $k$, does every graph $G$ either not contain two disjoint $P_k$, or contain two disjoint avoidable $P_k$?
rows.status_semantics: Question 1.12 is resolved negatively within the same paper: the authors provide an explicit counterexample for every k >= 3 (a cycle on 2k-1 vertices with one additional vertex adjacent to two consecutive cycle vertices), showing that two disjoint P_k need not contain two disjoint avoidable P_k. The cases k in {1,2} have positive answers by results of Beisegel et al. A web search found no follow-up papers further studying this specific question.
rows.class_notes: 'Avoidable P_k' is the paper's own notion, not defined in context_text; disproved with explicit counterexample for k>=3, so right-polarity target once avoidability is defined.

rows.title: Question 4.1
rows.paper_title: Avoidable paths in graphs
rows.statement_text: Does there exist a family $\mathcal{H}$ of connected graphs, not containing any path, such that any graph is either $\mathcal{H}$-free or contains an avoidable element of $\mathcal{H}$?
rows.context_text: Having established avoidable paths of every length, the authors ask whether avoidability extends to other graph structures. They note that even allowing a family rather than a single pattern, no candidate beyond paths seems to survive tests on chordal graphs or simple ad hoc constructions, and that the notion of avoidability for non-path structures is deliberately left open to interpretation.
rows.source_text: Does there exist a family $\mathcal{H}$ of connected graphs, not containing any path, such that any graph is either $\mathcal{H}$-free or contains an avoidable element of $\mathcal{H}$?
rows.status_semantics: Question 4.1 is directly addressed by Gurvich, Krnc, Milanič, and Vyalyi in 'Avoidability beyond paths' (arXiv:2208.12803, published in Electronic Journal of Combinatorics 2025). That paper introduces a framework of two-rooted graphs to generalise avoidability beyond paths, and provides both positive and negative results about which connected non-path structures admit an avoidability property. The question is not fully resolved: no family H satisfying the exact condition of Question 4.1 is identified, but the framework clarifies structural obstructions and establishes partial answers for restricted graph classes such as C5-free graphs.
rows.defer_reason: Avoidability for non-path structures is 'deliberately left open to interpretation' per the authors; not a single well-defined proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture on polynomial-time solvability of bounded sub-determinant integer programs
rows.paper_title: The stable set problem in graphs with bounded genus and bounded odd cycle packing number
rows.statement_text: Integer programs with bounded sub-determinants can be solved in polynomial time. In particular, the stable set problem on graphs with $\mathrm{ocp}(G) \leq k$ is polynomial for every fixed $k$.
rows.context_text: The authors note that recent work links the complexity of integer programs to the magnitude of their sub-determinants, and that it is tempting to believe bounded sub-determinant integer programs are polynomial-time solvable. This would imply the stable set problem is polynomial for every fixed bound $k$ on the odd cycle packing number, which remains open for $k \geq 2$.
rows.source_text: Integer programs with bounded sub-determinants can be solved in polynomial time. In particular, the stable set problem on graphs with $\mathrm{ocp}(G) \leq k$ is polynomial for every fixed $k$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1908.06300 — Conjecture on polynomial-time solvability of bounded sub-determinant integer programs
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture has two components: the general claim that all bounded sub-determinant integer programs are polynomial-time solvable, and the specific claim that the stable set problem is polynomial for every fixed odd cycle packing bound $k$. The latter was settled by arXiv:2106.05947 (FOCS 2021), which gives the first polynomial-time algorithm for the weighted stable set problem on graphs with at most $k$ vertex-disjoint odd cycles for any constant $k$, extending the previously known cases $k=0$ (bipartite) and $k=1$. The broader claim about all bounded sub-determinant IPs remains open in full generality; known positive results cover bimodular matrices and matrices with at most two nonzero entries per row.
rows.class_notes: The IP half is informal; the formalizable core is: stable set is polynomial on graphs with odd cycle packing number <= k for each fixed k.

rows.title: Open question on stable set for bounded ocp and arbitrary Euler genus
rows.paper_title: The stable set problem in graphs with bounded genus and bounded odd cycle packing number
rows.statement_text: Is the stable set problem polynomial-time solvable for graphs $G$ with $\mathrm{ocp}(G) \leq k$ (for fixed $k$) embedded in a surface of arbitrary Euler genus?
rows.context_text: The paper's main result (Theorem 2) gives a polynomial-time algorithm when both the odd cycle packing number and the Euler genus are fixed constants. Immediately after stating this result the authors note they do not know whether the genus bound can be dropped while retaining polynomial-time solvability.
rows.source_text: Is the stable set problem polynomial-time solvable for graphs $G$ with $\mathrm{ocp}(G) \leq k$ (for fixed $k$) embedded in a surface of arbitrary Euler genus?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1908.06300 — Open question on stable set for bounded ocp and arbitrary Euler genus
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no: drop the genus bound from Theorem 2 while keeping ocp fixed.

rows.title: Exact Ramsey growth rate dependence on G for link hypergraphs
rows.paper_title: Independent sets in hypergraphs with a forbidden link
rows.statement_text: It would be interesting to determine the exact dependence on $G$ in the bounds $\Omega(1/g) \le \frac{\log r(LG,\, K_n^{(3)})}{n \log n} \le O(s)$, where $G$ is a non-bipartite graph on $s$ vertices with odd girth $g \ge 3$.
rows.context_text: Theorems 1.3 and 1.4 together show that for a non-bipartite graph $G$ on $s$ vertices with odd girth $g \ge 3$ and $n$ sufficiently large, $\Omega(1/g) \le \log r(LG, K_n^{(3)})/(n\log n) \le O(s)$. The exact asymptotic constant as a function of $G$ (or of $s$ and $g$) remains open.
rows.source_text: It would be interesting to determine the exact dependence on $G$ in the bounds $\Omega(1/g) \le \frac{\log r(LG,\, K_n^{(3)})}{n \log n} \le O(s)$, where $G$ is a non-bipartite graph on $s$ vertices with odd girth $g \ge 3$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1909.05988 — Exact Ramsey growth rate dependence on G for link hypergraphs
rows.defer_reason: 'It would be interesting to determine the exact dependence' — determine-the-constant request, not a proposition.

rows.title: Determine the order of f_k(N; s, t) in general
rows.paper_title: Independent sets in hypergraphs with a forbidden link
rows.statement_text: Determine the order of $f_k(N; s, t)$ for all valid parameters $k \ge 3$, $s \ge k+1$, and $1 \le t \le \binom{s}{k}$.
rows.context_text: Despite substantial progress on the qualitative phase-transition structure of $f_k(N;s,t)$ as $t$ varies (the Erdős–Hajnal threshold conjectures, now resolved), the exact order of $f_k(N;s,t)$ has remained wide open. Theorem 1.6 determines the order $f_3(N;s,t) = \Theta(\log N/\log\log N)$ for a constant fraction of $t$-values in the range $(e(s), E(s)]$ for each fixed $s \ge 4$.
rows.source_text: Determine the order of $f_k(N; s, t)$ for all valid parameters $k \ge 3$, $s \ge k+1$, and $1 \le t \le \binom{s}{k}$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1909.05988 — Determine the order of f_k(N; s, t) in general
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the order of f_k(N;s,t)' — open-ended determination; f_k also not defined in the record.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (f(n) = 2n−2)
rows.paper_title: Disproportionate division
rows.statement_text: We suspect that the above construction reflects the truth and that $f(n) = 2n - 2$ for all $n \geq 2$.
rows.context_text: The authors give a construction (attributed to Segal-Halevi [6]) showing $f(n) \geq 2n-2$ for all $n \geq 2$, where $f(n)$ is the maximum number of cuts needed for disproportionate division with $n$ agents. Theorem 1.1 establishes $f(n) \leq 3n-4$, leaving a gap. The authors believe the lower bound is tight and that the truth is $f(n) = 2n-2$.
rows.source_text: We suspect that the above construction reflects the truth and that $f(n) = 2n - 2$ for all $n \geq 2$.
rows.defer_reason: Cake-cutting / disproportionate division of the interval; measure partitioning, not graph theory.

rows.title: Conjecture 3.1
rows.paper_title: Disproportionate division
rows.statement_text: For any $n \geq 2$ non-atomic probability measures $\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_n$ on the unit circle $S^1$ and non-negative reals $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_n$ summing to $1$, there exists a partition of the set $[n] = P \cup Q$ into two nonempty sets and a partition of the circle $S^1 = X \cup X^c$ into two intervals such that $$\min_{i \in P} \mu_i(X) = \sum_{j \in P} \alpha_j \quad \text{and} \quad \min_{i \in Q} \mu_i(X^c) = \sum_{j \in Q} \alpha_j.$$
rows.context_text: The authors introduce this conjecture as a topological measure-partitioning statement whose truth would imply, by a simple inductive argument, that $f(n) = 2n-2$ for all $n \geq 2$, which would be optimal for disproportionate division. The $n=2$ case is known and is essentially the Stromquist–Woodall theorem, but the conjecture is open already for $n=3$.
rows.source_text: For any $n \geq 2$ non-atomic probability measures $\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_n$ on the unit circle $S^1$ and non-negative reals $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_n$ summing to $1$, there exists a partition of the set $[n] = P \cup Q$ into two nonempty sets and a partition of the circle $S^1 = X \cup X^c$ into two intervals such that $$\min_{i \in P} \mu_i(X) = \sum_{j \in P} \alpha_j \quad \text{and} \quad \min_{i \in Q} \mu_i(X^c) = \sum_{j \in Q} \alpha_j.$$
rows.defer_reason: Topological measure-partitioning statement on non-atomic measures on S^1; not graph/hypergraph theory.

rows.title: Classical MIS Dichotomy Conjecture
rows.paper_title: When Maximum Stable Set can be solved in FPT time
rows.statement_text: For every connected graph $H$, Maximum Independent Set in $H$-free graphs is in $\mathsf{P}$ if and only if $H \in \{P_\ell\}_{\ell} \cup \{S_{i,j,k}\}_{i \leq j \leq k}$.
rows.context_text: The polynomial-time complexity of MIS in $H$-free graphs has been studied since the early 1980s. Alekseev showed that if $H$ is neither a path nor a subdivided claw then MIS is NP-complete on $H$-free graphs, motivating this conjecture about the full dichotomy. The authors note that an even stronger conjecture is postulated by Lozin [28].
rows.source_text: For every connected graph $H$, Maximum Independent Set in $H$-free graphs is in $\mathsf{P}$ if and only if $H \in \{P_\ell\}_{\ell} \cup \{S_{i,j,k}\}_{i \leq j \leq k}$.
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that MIS in H-free graphs is in P if and only if H is a path or subdivided claw remains open at the polynomial-time level. The landmark post-2019 development is arXiv:2305.15738 (Gartland, Lokshtanov, Masařík, M. Pilipczuk, M. Pilipczuk, Rzążewski; STOC 2024), which proves MWIS is solvable in quasi-polynomial time for every H-free graph class where H is a path or subdivided claw, closing the gap between predicted-tractable cases and NP-hardness. Polynomial-time algorithms are established for specific subclasses (P_t-free for t ≤ 6, fork-free/S_{1,1,2}-free), but polynomial-time solvability for all S_{i,j,k}-free graphs remains open.
rows.class_notes: Full P-vs-NP-complete dichotomy for MIS in H-free graphs; 'only if' side implicitly conditioned on P != NP.

rows.title: Parameterized MIS Dichotomy
rows.paper_title: When Maximum Stable Set can be solved in FPT time
rows.statement_text: Is MIS (randomized) FPT or $W[1]$-hard in $H$-free graphs?
rows.context_text: This is the parameterized counterpart of the classical MIS dichotomy question. Alekseev's NP-hardness reduction does not transfer to $W[1]$-hardness, leaving a priori more candidate graphs $H$ for which the parameterized status is open. Dabrowski et al. initiated a systematic study of this question.
rows.source_text: Is MIS (randomized) FPT or $W[1]$-hard in $H$-free graphs?
rows.status_semantics: The full FPT vs W[1]-hard dichotomy for MIS in H-free graphs remains open as of 2026. Significant partial progress has been made: the dichotomy is completely resolved for all 4-vertex forbidden subgraphs, and many 5-vertex cases are settled, but cases such as P7-free, S_{1,1,3}-free, and S_{1,2,2}-free graphs remain open. A 2020 follow-up by Dvořák et al. strengthened hardness results by proving parameterized inapproximability lower bounds under ETH/Gap-ETH for graph classes where W[1]-hardness was already known, without resolving new FPT cases.
rows.defer_reason: Classification request: determine for which H the problem is FPT vs W[1]-hard; no concrete dichotomy statement is fixed in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 1
rows.paper_title: When Maximum Stable Set can be solved in FPT time
rows.statement_text: For any integer $t$, MIS can be solved in FPT time in $P(t,t,t,t)$-free graphs.
rows.context_text: MIS was already shown FPT on $P(t,t,t)$-free graphs; this conjecture proposes the natural next step for four-vertex paths with clique substitutions. The present paper proves the special case $P(1,t,t,t)$-free graphs as partial progress toward Conjecture 1.
rows.source_text: For any integer $t$, MIS can be solved in FPT time in $P(t,t,t,t)$-free graphs.
rows.class_notes: P(t,t,t,t) = 4-vertex path with clique substitutions; gloss in context suffices to recover the definition.

rows.title: Conjecture 2
rows.paper_title: When Maximum Stable Set can be solved in FPT time
rows.statement_text: For any integers $t$ and $\ell$, MIS is FPT in $P_\ell(t)$-free graphs, where $P_\ell(t) = P(t, t, \ldots, t)$ with the sequence $t, t, \ldots, t$ of length $\ell$.
rows.context_text: This is described as a far more distant milestone than Conjecture 1, extending the FPT claim to clique-substituted paths of arbitrary length. The authors remark that even the parameterized complexity of MIS in $P_7$-free graphs (the $t=1$, $\ell=7$ case) is currently open.
rows.source_text: For any integers $t$ and $\ell$, MIS is FPT in $P_\ell(t)$-free graphs, where $P_\ell(t) = P(t, t, \ldots, t)$ with the sequence $t, t, \ldots, t$ of length $\ell$.
rows.class_notes: Generalization of Conjecture 1 (axv_1909_08426_02) to clique-substituted paths of arbitrary length.

rows.title: Informal belief on FPT candidates
rows.paper_title: When Maximum Stable Set can be solved in FPT time
rows.statement_text: There will be very few connected candidates $H$ (as described by the structural characterisation of FPT candidate graphs) which will not end up in (randomized) FPT.
rows.context_text: After settling all four remaining five-vertex graph cases as (randomized) FPT, the authors express a broader belief that almost all graphs $H$ satisfying the structural conditions identified in Figure 1 will yield FPT algorithms for MIS in $H$-free graphs.
rows.source_text: There will be very few connected candidates $H$ (as described by the structural characterisation of FPT candidate graphs) which will not end up in (randomized) FPT.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1909.08426 — Informal belief on FPT candidates
rows.defer_reason: Informal belief ('very few candidates will not end up in FPT'), not a proposition.

rows.title: Size of symmetric intersecting families in $[k]^n$
rows.paper_title: On symmetric intersecting families of vectors
rows.statement_text: How large can a symmetric, intersecting subfamily of $[k]^n$ really be (for fixed $k \geq 3$)?
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 establishes that any symmetric intersecting $A \subset [k]^n$ has size $o(k^n)$, but the precise asymptotics remain open. The best constructions known to the authors are set-intersecting families, e.g., those based on lines of a projective plane $PG(2,q)$, yielding size roughly $k^{n-\sqrt{n}}$.
rows.source_text: How large can a symmetric, intersecting subfamily of $[k]^n$ really be (for fixed $k \geq 3$)?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1909.11578 — Size of symmetric intersecting families in $[k]^n$
rows.defer_reason: Intersecting families of vectors in [k]^n — set-system extremal combinatorics, not graph theory; also a 'how large' determination.

rows.title: Largest symmetric intersecting families are set-intersecting
rows.paper_title: On symmetric intersecting families of vectors
rows.statement_text: It seems possible (but maybe impossible to prove) that the largest symmetric intersecting families in $[k]^n$ are set-intersecting.
rows.context_text: A family $A \subset [k]^n$ is set-intersecting if there exists a symmetric intersecting family $\mathcal{B}$ of subsets of $[n]$ and $\ell \in [k]$ such that $x \in A$ if and only if there is some $B \in \mathcal{B}$ with $x_i = \ell$ for all $i \in B$. The authors note that all their best constructions have this form, motivating the conjecture.
rows.source_text: It seems possible (but maybe impossible to prove) that the largest symmetric intersecting families in $[k]^n$ are set-intersecting.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1909.11578 — Largest symmetric intersecting families are set-intersecting
rows.defer_reason: Symmetric intersecting families of vectors — set systems, out of graph scope; also hedged ('seems possible').

rows.title: Polynomial improvement on symmetric intersecting family size bound
rows.paper_title: On symmetric intersecting families of vectors
rows.statement_text: Show that there exist constants $c, \delta > 0$ (possibly depending on $k$) such that for any symmetric intersecting $A \subset [k]^n$, we have $\log_k |A| \leq n - cn^{\delta}$.
rows.context_text: This is posed in Section 4 as a concrete and more modest goal, falling short of identifying the exact extremal size: even a polynomial gain over the $o(k^n)$ bound of Theorem 1.1, expressed as $\log_k |A| \leq n - cn^\delta$, would be a significant advance.
rows.source_text: Show that there exist constants $c, \delta > 0$ (possibly depending on $k$) such that for any symmetric intersecting $A \subset [k]^n$, we have $\log_k |A| \leq n - cn^{\delta}$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1909.11578 — Polynomial improvement on symmetric intersecting family size bound
rows.defer_reason: Precise bound but about symmetric intersecting families in [k]^n — set systems, not graph/hypergraph theory.

rows.title: Open problem: p-entropic matroids linear for prime p
rows.paper_title: Entropic matroids and their representation
rows.statement_text: Our results leave open the possibility for $p$-entropic matroids to be linear whenever $p$ is prime.
rows.context_text: The paper proves that $p$-entropic matroids are $\mathbb{F}_p$-representable for $p=2$ and $p=3$, and shows they are not linear for $p=9$ (a composite). The central remaining open problem is whether $p$-entropic matroids are always $\mathbb{F}_p$-representable when $p$ is prime, with only partial cases resolved in this work.
rows.source_text: Our results leave open the possibility for $p$-entropic matroids to be linear whenever $p$ is prime.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1909.12175 — Open problem: p-entropic matroids linear for prime p
rows.defer_reason: Representability of entropic matroids over F_p — matroid/information theory, outside the graph-theory repo areas.

rows.title: Open problem: χ-bounded vs. polynomially χ-bounded
rows.paper_title: Graphs of bounded cliquewidth are polynomially $χ$-bounded
rows.statement_text: Is there a hereditary graph class that is $\chi$-bounded but (provably) not polynomially $\chi$-bounded?
rows.context_text: The authors remark that no hereditary graph class is currently known to be $\chi$-bounded while provably failing to be polynomially $\chi$-bounded, citing [Esp17]. This observation motivates the study of polynomial $\chi$-boundedness and frames the paper's main contribution as evidence toward a positive answer for the class of graphs of bounded cliquewidth.
rows.source_text: Is there a hereditary graph class that is $\chi$-bounded but (provably) not polynomially $\chi$-bounded?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1910.00697 — Open problem: χ-bounded vs. polynomially χ-bounded
rows.status_semantics: Briański, Davies, and Walczak (2022) answered the open problem affirmatively: they construct hereditary graph classes that are χ-bounded but not polynomially χ-bounded, published in Combinatorica. This resolves the question posed by Bonamy and Pilipczuk and refutes the hope (attributed to Esperet) that every χ-bounded hereditary class admits a polynomial bounding function. Follow-up work by Chudnovsky, Cook, Davies, and Oum (arXiv:2310.11167) introduced the ‘Pollyanna’ framework to study which graph classes preserve polynomial χ-boundedness when intersected with an arbitrary χ-bounded class.
rows.class_notes: Solved (positively) after this paper; quantifies over hereditary classes, encodable as induced-subgraph-closed predicates.

rows.title: Open Problem (Extending Induced Arithmetic Removal to Higher Complexity and General Groups)
rows.paper_title: Induced arithmetic removal: complexity 1 patterns over finite fields
rows.statement_text: Extend the induced arithmetic removal lemma beyond complexity 1 patterns over finite fields to higher complexity patterns and to general abelian groups (even the case of $\mathbb{Z}$ is open).
rows.context_text: After establishing Theorem 1.14 for complexity 1 patterns over $\mathbb{F}_q$, the authors note that extending to higher complexity and to general groups remains open. They observe that for affine-invariant patterns (which avoid a regularisation difficulty near the origin), the problem for bounded complexity patterns over finite fields has been solved, but the general induced setting is still unresolved.
rows.source_text: Extend the induced arithmetic removal lemma beyond complexity 1 patterns over finite fields to higher complexity patterns and to general abelian groups (even the case of $\mathbb{Z}$ is open).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1911.03427 — Open Problem (Extending Induced Arithmetic Removal to Higher Complexity and General Groups)
rows.defer_reason: Arithmetic removal lemmas over finite fields / abelian groups — additive combinatorics; also an 'extend the result' meta request.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (Induced Arithmetic Removal for Arbitrary Abelian Groups)
rows.paper_title: Induced arithmetic removal: complexity 1 patterns over finite fields
rows.statement_text: A statement analogous to Theorem 1.14 holds for arbitrary patterns with coefficients in $\mathbb{Z}$ over arbitrary abelian groups, subject to appropriate caveats concerning infinite complexity patterns and trivial solutions.
rows.context_text: Immediately following the statement of the main theorem (Theorem 1.14) and its remark on tower-type bounds, the authors write: 'We conjecture that a similar statement holds for arbitrary patterns (with coefficients in $\mathbb{Z}$) in arbitrary abelian groups.' They decline to give a precise formulation due to unresolved issues with infinite complexity and trivial solutions.
rows.source_text: A statement analogous to Theorem 1.14 holds for arbitrary patterns with coefficients in $\mathbb{Z}$ over arbitrary abelian groups, subject to appropriate caveats concerning infinite complexity patterns and trivial solutions.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1911.03427 — Informal Conjecture (Induced Arithmetic Removal for Arbitrary Abelian Groups)
rows.defer_reason: Additive combinatorics (removal for arbitrary abelian groups); authors themselves decline a precise formulation.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (Polynomial Quantitative Bound for Complexity 1 Systems)
rows.paper_title: Induced arithmetic removal: complexity 1 patterns over finite fields
rows.statement_text: For complexity 1 systems, the dependence of $\delta$ on $\epsilon$ in Definition 1.10 satisfies $\delta = \epsilon^{O_A(1)}$, i.e., polynomial dependence.
rows.context_text: In Section 1.2, after defining complexity 1 (Definition 1.10), the authors remark that the general dependence of $\delta$ on $\epsilon$ is a wide open problem and state conjecturally that polynomial dependence holds. Reference [20] is cited for further discussion of this quantitative question.
rows.source_text: For complexity 1 systems, the dependence of $\delta$ on $\epsilon$ in Definition 1.10 satisfies $\delta = \epsilon^{O_A(1)}$, i.e., polynomial dependence.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1911.03427 — Informal Conjecture (Polynomial Quantitative Bound for Complexity 1 Systems)
rows.defer_reason: Quantitative dependence in an arithmetic removal lemma — additive combinatorics, not graph theory.

rows.title: Conjecture 2
rows.paper_title: Jones' Conjecture in subcubic graphs
rows.statement_text: For every planar graph $G$, we have $\mathrm{fvs}(G) \leq 2 \cdot \mathrm{fp}(G)$, where $\mathrm{fp}(G)$ is the maximum size of a face-packing of $G$, i.e., a cycle-packing where, for some embedding of $G$, every cycle bounds a face.
rows.context_text: The authors pose this stronger conjecture to highlight how little is understood around Jones' Conjecture. They note that the example of many nested disjoint cycles shows the embedding cannot be fixed, and that the simple discharging argument giving $\mathrm{fvs}(G) \leq 3\,\mathrm{cp}(G)$ does not imply this conjecture even with factor 3 instead of 2.
rows.source_text: For every planar graph $G$, we have $\mathrm{fvs}(G) \leq 2 \cdot \mathrm{fp}(G)$, where $\mathrm{fp}(G)$ is the maximum size of a face-packing of $G$, i.e., a cycle-packing where, for some embedding of $G$, every cycle bounds a face.
rows.class_notes: Strengthening of Jones' conjecture: replaces cycle-packing cp(G) by face-packing fp(G) <= cp(G); needs an embedding layer for 'bounds a face'.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.1
rows.paper_title: Bounded VC-dimension implies the Schur-Erdos conjecture
rows.statement_text: For $d \geq 2$, there exists a constant $\varepsilon(d)$ such that every graph on $n$ vertices with VC-dimension at most $d$ contains a clique or an independent set of size $n^{\varepsilon(d)}$.
rows.context_text: This is the VC-dimension specialisation of the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture proposed by the paper's authors. The best known bound for graphs of bounded VC-dimension is $e^{(\log n)^{1-o(1)}}$, established in a companion paper by the same authors; Conjecture 4.1 asks for the much stronger polynomial bound.
rows.source_text: For $d \geq 2$, there exists a constant $\varepsilon(d)$ such that every graph on $n$ vertices with VC-dimension at most $d$ contains a clique or an independent set of size $n^{\varepsilon(d)}$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 4.1 from arXiv:1912.02342 has been fully resolved. Nguyen, Scott, and Seymour (arXiv:2312.15572, 2023) proved that for every d > 0, every n-vertex graph of VC-dimension at most d contains a clique or stable set of size at least n^c for an explicit constant c > 0 depending only on d, with the bound c_d ≥ 2^{-2^{O(d)}}. The proof also settles a related conjecture of Chernikov, Starchenko, and Thomas on graphs definable in NIP structures.
rows.class_notes: Erdos-Hajnal for graphs of bounded VC-dimension; VC-dimension of the neighborhood set system is a buildable finite primitive; marked solved.

rows.title: Conjecture 8
rows.paper_title: The Last Temptation of William T. Tutte
rows.statement_text: For every planar triangulation whose 4-coloring complex is disconnected, there is a component of even parity and a component of odd parity.
rows.context_text: After resolving Tutte's original question by constructing triangulations with arbitrarily many same-parity components, the authors identify this closely related conjecture based on extensive computation. It refines the picture by asserting that disconnected coloring complexes always contain both parities.
rows.source_text: For every planar triangulation whose 4-coloring complex is disconnected, there is a component of even parity and a component of odd parity.
rows.class_notes: '4-coloring complex' and component 'parity' are paper-specific notions not defined in the record; also needs planar triangulation layer.

rows.title: Problem 1.1
rows.paper_title: Acyclic subgraphs of tournaments with high chromatic number
rows.statement_text: What is the minimum integer $f\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(k}\right)$ such that every $f\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(k}\right)$-chromatic oriented graph has an acyclic $k$-chromatic subgraph?
rows.context_text: Addario-Berry, Havet, Sales, Reed, and Thomassé observed that if a $k$-chromatic oriented graph has no directed cycle it contains every oriented tree of order $k$, and suggested that proving acyclic subgraphs of high chromatic number exist in $k$-chromatic oriented graphs could yield improved bounds towards Burr's conjecture. The trivial two-part partition gives $f(k)\leq k^{2}$, subsequently refined to $f(k)\leq k^{2}-2k+2$, but substantially improving this bound seems to require new ideas.
rows.source_text: What is the minimum integer $f\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(k}\right)$ such that every $f\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(k}\right)$-chromatic oriented graph has an acyclic $k$-chromatic subgraph?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the minimum integer f(k)' — determine-the-value question, not a proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.1
rows.paper_title: Acyclic subgraphs of tournaments with high chromatic number
rows.statement_text: Every $n$-vertex tournament has an acyclic subgraph with chromatic number at least $n^{3/4-o\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(1}\right)}$. That is, $g\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(k}\right)\leq k^{4/3+o\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(1}\right)}$.
rows.context_text: Having established $k^{4/3-o(1)}\leq g(k)\leq k^{9/5+o(1)}$, the authors suspect the lower bound is closer to the truth and propose this conjecture. The lower bound is achieved via a randomized projective-plane construction, and the authors also wonder whether a simpler explicit construction on $n=q^{2}$ vertices attains the same bound.
rows.source_text: Every $n$-vertex tournament has an acyclic subgraph with chromatic number at least $n^{3/4-o\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(1}\right)}$. That is, $g\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(k}\right)\leq k^{4/3+o\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(1}\right)}$.
rows.class_notes: n^{3/4-o(1)} lower bound on chromatic number of an acyclic subgraph of every tournament; the o(1) needs the asymptotics idiom.

rows.title: Open Problem (edge-EP property characterization for planar graph expansions)
rows.paper_title: Erdős-Pósa from ball packing
rows.statement_text: Finding a characterization for the planar graphs $J$ such that $J$-expansions have the edge-Erd\H{o}s-P\'osa property remains an intriguing open problem.
rows.context_text: Robertson and Seymour showed that $J$-expansions have the vertex-EP property if and only if $J$ is planar. By contrast, it was shown in [4] that there exist planar graphs $J$ — in particular sufficiently large ladders and subcubic trees with sufficiently large pathwidth — for which $J$-expansions do not have the edge-EP property. The precise characterization of which planar $J$ yield the edge-EP property is unknown.
rows.source_text: Finding a characterization for the planar graphs $J$ such that $J$-expansions have the edge-Erd\H{o}s-P\'osa property remains an intriguing open problem.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1912.07965 — Open Problem (edge-EP property characterization for planar graph expansions)
rows.defer_reason: 'Finding a characterization' of planar J whose expansions have the edge-Erdos-Posa property — grand characterization request, no candidate stated.

rows.title: Conjecture on unavoidable G-dependence in Theorem 2
rows.paper_title: Extremal and Ramsey results on graph blowups
