rows.statement_text: For some graph $H$, the dependence on $G$ in the multiplicative factor $a = a(G, H, r)$ of the bound $B(G \xrightarrow{r} H; t) \leq a \cdot b^t$ (Theorem 2) is unavoidable.
rows.context_text: Theorem 2 shows that the exponential constant $b$ can be taken independent of $G$, but the multiplicative prefactor $a$ still depends on $G$. The authors believe this residual dependence is necessary for some $H$, and refer to the concluding remarks for further details.
rows.source_text: For some graph $H$, the dependence on $G$ in the multiplicative factor $a = a(G, H, r)$ of the bound $B(G \xrightarrow{r} H; t) \leq a \cdot b^t$ (Theorem 2) is unavoidable.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1912.08328 — Conjecture on unavoidable G-dependence in Theorem 2
rows.status_semantics: Girão and Hancock (arXiv:2205.12826, European J. Combin. 120 (2024) 103984) proved the conjecture affirmatively: for every 3-chromatically connected graph H (including triangles), the dependence on G in the multiplicative factor a(G,H,r) of the bound B(G→rH;t) ≤ a·b^t is unavoidable. They also showed the complementary result that for forests F the factor a can be chosen independently of G.
rows.class_notes: Relies on Theorem 2's notation B(G ->_r H; t) and prefactor a(G,H,r), not defined in the record; marked solved.

rows.title: Conjecture 2.2
rows.paper_title: Maximum independent sets in (pyramid, even hole)-free graphs
rows.statement_text: There is a polynomial $P$ such that every graph $G$ that contains no prism, pyramid, theta or turtle has at most $P(|V(G)|)$ minimal separators.
rows.context_text: The authors survey families of graphs with exponentially many minimal separators (k-prisms, k-pyramids, k-thetas, k-turtles, k-ladders) and observe that every known such example contains a prism, pyramid, theta, or turtle. They propose Conjecture 2.2 as 'in some sense the best possible statement regarding bounding the number of minimal separators.' The paper proves a weaker result (Theorem 2.3) for the class $\mathcal{C}$ of (square, prism, pyramid, theta, even wheel)-free graphs.
rows.source_text: There is a polynomial $P$ such that every graph $G$ that contains no prism, pyramid, theta or turtle has at most $P(|V(G)|)$ minimal separators.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 2.2 from arXiv:1912.11246 was proved in full by Abrishami, Chudnovsky, Dibek, Thomassé, Trotignon, and Vušković in arXiv:2005.05042. Their Theorem 1.2 establishes that every (theta, pyramid, prism, turtle)-free graph G has at most |V(G)|^18 minimal separators, constructible in polynomial time. The result is optimal in the sense that excluding only three of the four forbidden subgraphs still permits exponentially many minimal separators.
rows.class_notes: 'Turtle' is paper-local and undefined in the record (prism/pyramid/theta are standard); marked solved.

rows.title: Open problem: MIS complexity in even-hole-free graphs
rows.paper_title: Maximum independent sets in (pyramid, even hole)-free graphs
rows.statement_text: The complexity of computing a maximum independent set in an even-hole-free graph is not known.
rows.context_text: Stated in the introduction as motivation for the paper's approach. The paper proves a polynomial-time algorithm for the strictly smaller class of (even hole, pyramid)-free graphs, with the hope that understanding the pyramid-free case may shed light on the full even-hole-free class.
rows.source_text: The complexity of computing a maximum independent set in an even-hole-free graph is not known.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1912.11246 — Open problem: MIS complexity in even-hole-free graphs
rows.status_semantics: The complexity of maximum independent set in even-hole-free graphs (polynomial-time vs. NP-hard) remains open. Significant partial progress has been made: Abrishami et al. (arXiv:2005.05042, 2020) give a polynomial-time MWIS algorithm for (theta, pyramid, prism, turtle)-free graphs, subsuming the (pyramid, even hole)-free result. More decisively, Chudnovsky, Gartland, Hajebi, Lokshtanov, and Spirkl (arXiv:2407.08927, 2024) prove that every n-vertex even-hole-free graph has tree independence number at most c·log^{10}(n), yielding a quasi-polynomial time algorithm for MWIS across the full even-hole-free class—a major advance that nonetheless leaves the polynomial vs. NP-hard question unresolved.
rows.defer_reason: 'The complexity ... is not known' — determine-the-complexity meta statement rather than a fixed yes/no proposition.
rows.class_notes: Could be recast as 'MIS is in P on even-hole-free graphs?' but the record does not commit to a polarity.

rows.title: Open problem: polynomial separator property for (prism, pyramid, theta, even wheel)-free graphs
rows.paper_title: Maximum independent sets in (pyramid, even hole)-free graphs
rows.statement_text: It is unknown whether (prism, pyramid, theta, even wheel)-free graphs have polynomially many minimal separators.
rows.context_text: The authors note that a weakening of Conjecture 2.2 is obtained by restricting to (prism, pyramid, theta, even wheel)-free graphs (since prisms, thetas, and turtles all contain even holes). They explicitly state they 'were not able to prove that (prism, pyramid, theta, even wheel)-free graphs have polynomially many minimal separators,' which is why the main result (Theorem 2.3) additionally excludes squares.
rows.source_text: It is unknown whether (prism, pyramid, theta, even wheel)-free graphs have polynomially many minimal separators.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:1912.11246 — Open problem: polynomial separator property for (prism, pyramid, theta, even wheel)-free graphs
rows.status_semantics: The open problem of whether (prism, pyramid, theta, even wheel)-free graphs have polynomially many minimal separators remains unresolved as stated. Abrishami, Chudnovsky, Dibek, Thomassé, Trotignon, and Vušković (arXiv:2005.05042, 2020) proved the closely related result that (theta, pyramid, prism, turtle)-free graphs have at most $|V(G)|^{18}$ minimal separators constructible in polynomial time; this shares three of the four forbidden subgraphs with the open problem but uses turtle in place of even wheel. Whether this result implies the even-wheel-free case (via class inclusion) could not be determined from the abstract alone, leaving the exact conjecture from arXiv:1912.11246 open or at most partially addressed.
rows.class_notes: Weakening of Conjecture 2.2 (axv_1912_11246_00) with standard forbidden induced subgraphs; needs 'exists polynomial bound over the class' wrapper.

rows.title: Question 6
rows.paper_title: Sublinear separators in intersection graphs of convex shapes
rows.statement_text: Given a polynomial $p$, do there necessarily exist real numbers $c, s \geq 1$ and a positive integer $d$, such that the following holds: every graph $G$ satisfying $\mathrm{col}_r(G) \leq p(r)$ for every non-negative integer $r$ admits a $(c, \sqsubseteq_s)$-tame representation in $\mathbb{R}^d$?
rows.context_text: Theorem 4 shows that graphs with $(c,\sqsubseteq_s)$-tame representations in $\mathbb{R}^d$ have strong coloring numbers bounded by $\delta r^d$, paralleling Krauthgamer–Lee's characterization (Theorem 5) of polynomial-growth graph classes via grid embeddings. Answering Question 6 would determine whether Theorem 4 yields an analogous geometric characterization of graphs with polynomially bounded strong coloring numbers.
rows.source_text: Given a polynomial $p$, do there necessarily exist real numbers $c, s \geq 1$ and a positive integer $d$, such that the following holds: every graph $G$ satisfying $\mathrm{col}_r(G) \leq p(r)$ for every non-negative integer $r$ admits a $(c, \sqsubseteq_s)$-tame representation in $\mathbb{R}^d$?
rows.class_notes: Precise but '(c,⊑_s)-tame representation in R^d' is a paper-local geometric notion not defined in the record; also needs real-geometry layer.

rows.title: Informal conjecture: negative answer to Question 6
rows.paper_title: Sublinear separators in intersection graphs of convex shapes
rows.statement_text: The answer to Question 6 is negative: there exist graphs $G$ with $\mathrm{col}_r(G)$ polynomially bounded in $r$ that do not admit any $(c, \sqsubseteq_s)$-tame representation in any $\mathbb{R}^d$.
rows.context_text: The authors write 'We suspect that the answer to Question 6 is negative based on the following.' They then demonstrate (Theorem 7) that a box-shaped version of such a representation fails, which motivates the suspicion that the full statement also fails.
rows.source_text: The answer to Question 6 is negative: there exist graphs $G$ with $\mathrm{col}_r(G)$ polynomially bounded in $r$ that do not admit any $(c, \sqsubseteq_s)$-tame representation in any $\mathbb{R}^d$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2001.01552 — Informal conjecture: negative answer to Question 6
rows.class_notes: Negation of Question 6 (axv_2001_01552_00); inherits the undefined tame-representation notion.

rows.title: Open Question: (even hole, K4, diamond)-free treewidth/cliquewidth
rows.paper_title: (Theta, triangle)-free and (even hole, $K_4$)-free graphs. Part 2 : bounds on treewidth
rows.statement_text: Is it true that (even hole, $K_4$, diamond)-free graphs have bounded treewidth (or cliquewidth)?
rows.context_text: The paper proves bounded treewidth for (theta, triangle, $S_{i,j,k}$)-free and (even hole, pyramid, $K_t$, $S_{i,j,k}$)-free graphs. The analogous question for graphs excluding the diamond ($K_4$ minus one edge) instead of a subdivided claw is explicitly listed as open.
rows.source_text: Is it true that (even hole, $K_4$, diamond)-free graphs have bounded treewidth (or cliquewidth)?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2001.01607 — Open Question: (even hole, K4, diamond)-free treewidth/cliquewidth
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture asks whether (even hole, $K_4$, diamond)-free graphs have bounded treewidth or cliquewidth. Abrishami, Chudnovsky, Hajebi, and Spirkl (arXiv:2203.06775, 2022) proved a partial result: for every $t > 0$, every (even hole, diamond, pyramid, $K_t$)-free graph has bounded treewidth — a special case of the conjecture that additionally requires pyramid-free graphs. Search results indicate that a structure theorem (every even-hole-free graph of sufficiently large treewidth contains $K_4$ or a diamond as an induced subgraph) was subsequently proved, which would directly resolve the conjecture, but the specific paper establishing this result could not be verified within the search budget.
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no with standard vocabulary (even hole, diamond, treewidth); cliquewidth half would need an extra primitive.

rows.title: Open Question: (theta, triangle)-free graphs of bounded degree
rows.paper_title: (Theta, triangle)-free and (even hole, $K_4$)-free graphs. Part 2 : bounds on treewidth
rows.statement_text: For every fixed integer $t \geq 4$, do (theta, triangle)-free graphs of maximum degree $t$ have bounded treewidth?
rows.context_text: Treewidth of (theta, triangle)-free graphs is unbounded in general. For $t = 1, 2$ bounded treewidth is trivial; for $t = 3$ it follows from Corollary 4.3 in [2] (even-hole-free graphs with maximum degree $t$). The question for $t \geq 4$ is explicitly stated as open.
rows.source_text: For every fixed integer $t \geq 4$, do (theta, triangle)-free graphs of maximum degree $t$ have bounded treewidth?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2001.01607 — Open Question: (theta, triangle)-free graphs of bounded degree
rows.status_semantics: The question of whether (theta, triangle)-free graphs of maximum degree $t\geq 4$ have bounded treewidth was affirmatively resolved by Abrishami, Chudnovsky, Dibek, Hajebi, Rz\u0105\u017cewski, Spirkl, and Vu\u0161kovi\u0107 (arXiv:2108.01162, 2021). That paper proves that every graph of bounded maximum degree with sufficiently large treewidth must contain either a theta or a triangle as an induced subgraph; by contrapositive this immediately gives bounded treewidth for (theta, triangle)-free graphs of bounded maximum degree, settling the open question for all $t$. The internal reference arXiv:2203.06775 addresses a related but distinct conjecture (Conjecture 1.5 of the source paper) about (even hole, diamond, pyramid, $K_t$)-free graphs.
rows.class_notes: Solved; theta and treewidth are standard notions, bounded-treewidth wrapper needed.

rows.title: Open Question: (triangle, S1,2,3)-free graphs cliquewidth
rows.paper_title: (Theta, triangle)-free and (even hole, $K_4$)-free graphs. Part 2 : bounds on treewidth
rows.statement_text: Do (triangle, $S_{1,2,3}$)-free graphs have bounded cliquewidth?
rows.context_text: Bounded cliquewidth is known for (triangle, $S_{1,2,2}$)-free graphs and for (triangle, $H$)-free graphs when $H$ is contained in a graph from $H_B = \{P_6, S_{1,1,3}\}$. The status of $S_{1,2,3}$ is explicitly open; the authors refer to [16] for further open problems of the same flavour.
rows.source_text: Do (triangle, $S_{1,2,3}$)-free graphs have bounded cliquewidth?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2001.01607 — Open Question: (triangle, S1,2,3)-free graphs cliquewidth
rows.class_notes: Cliquewidth is a buildable but nonstandard primitive; S_{1,2,3} is standard subdivided-claw vocabulary.

rows.title: Maximum Independent Set complexity for (even hole, K4)-free and (theta, triangle)-free graphs
rows.paper_title: (Theta, triangle)-free and (even hole, $K_4$)-free graphs. Part 2 : bounds on treewidth
rows.statement_text: Determine the computational complexity of the Maximum Independent Set problem for (even hole, $K_4$)-free graphs and for (theta, triangle)-free graphs.
rows.context_text: Maximum Independent Set is polynomial for (even hole, triangle)-free and (even hole, pyramid)-free graphs. The paper gives polynomial algorithms for (theta, triangle, $S_{i,j,k}$)-free and (even hole, pyramid, $K_t$, $S_{i,j,k}$)-free graphs via bounded treewidth, but the complexity for the larger classes (even hole, $K_4$)-free and (theta, triangle)-free is stated as unknown.
rows.source_text: Determine the computational complexity of the Maximum Independent Set problem for (even hole, $K_4$)-free graphs and for (theta, triangle)-free graphs.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2001.01607 — Maximum Independent Set complexity for (even hole, K4)-free and (theta, triangle)-free graphs
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the computational complexity' is an open-ended task, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Maximum Independent Set complexity for H-free graphs when H contains P7, S1,1,3, or S1,2,2
rows.paper_title: (Theta, triangle)-free and (even hole, $K_4$)-free graphs. Part 2 : bounds on treewidth
rows.statement_text: Determine the computational complexity of the Maximum Independent Set problem for $H$-free graphs whenever $H$ is some $S_{i,j,k}$ that contains either $P_7$, $S_{1,1,3}$, or $S_{1,2,2}$.
rows.context_text: The problem is polynomial for $H \subseteq P_k$ with $k \leq 6$ and for $H \subseteq S_{i,j,k}$ with $(i,j,k) \leq (1,1,2)$, and is NP-hard when $H$ is not an induced subgraph of any $S_{i,j,k}$. The boundary region where $H$ contains $P_7$, $S_{1,1,3}$, or $S_{1,2,2}$ is listed as completely open.
rows.source_text: Determine the computational complexity of the Maximum Independent Set problem for $H$-free graphs whenever $H$ is some $S_{i,j,k}$ that contains either $P_7$, $S_{1,1,3}$, or $S_{1,2,2}$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2001.01607 — Maximum Independent Set complexity for H-free graphs when H contains P7, S1,1,3, or S1,2,2
rows.status_semantics: The computational complexity of Maximum Independent Set for $H$-free graphs when $H$ contains $P_7$, $S_{1,1,3}$, or $S_{1,2,2}$ remains unresolved as a full polynomial/NP-hard dichotomy. Partial progress exists: quasi-polynomial-time algorithms apply to all $P_k$-free graphs (hence $P_7$-free), and polynomial-time algorithms have been obtained for bounded-degree graphs excluding any fixed subdivided claw as an induced subgraph, covering $S_{1,1,3}$- and $S_{1,2,2}$-free cases under bounded degree. No polynomial-time algorithm or NP-hardness result for the general unbounded-degree cases has been found.
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the computational complexity' over a family of H is not a single proposition.

rows.title: FPT on $H_{t,t}$-free graphs for Grundy Coloring and b-Chromatic Core
rows.paper_title: Grundy Coloring & friends, Half-Graphs, Bicliques
rows.statement_text: Are \textsc{Grundy Coloring} and \textsc{b-Chromatic Core} fixed-parameter tractable on $H_{t,t}$-free graphs when parameterized by $k$?
rows.context_text: The W[1]-hardness constructions for both problems rely heavily on large half-graphs as color-propagation units; excluding large half-graphs may restore tractability. However, the key combinatorial lemma that gives FPT on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs collapses on $H_{t,t}$-free graphs, so the question is explicitly left open.
rows.source_text: Are \textsc{Grundy Coloring} and \textsc{b-Chromatic Core} fixed-parameter tractable on $H_{t,t}$-free graphs when parameterized by $k$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2001.03794 — FPT on $H_{t,t}$-free graphs for Grundy Coloring and b-Chromatic Core
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no FPT question (conjunction of two problems); half-graph H_{t,t} is standard vocabulary.

rows.title: FPT on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs for Grundy Coloring
rows.paper_title: Grundy Coloring & friends, Half-Graphs, Bicliques
rows.statement_text: Does \textsc{Grundy Coloring} admit a fixed-parameter tractable algorithm on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs when parameterized by $k$?
rows.context_text: The paper establishes FPT algorithms for b-Chromatic Core and Partial Grundy Coloring on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs (parameterized by $k+t$), but the analogous question for Grundy Coloring on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs is explicitly left unsettled.
rows.source_text: Does \textsc{Grundy Coloring} admit a fixed-parameter tractable algorithm on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs when parameterized by $k$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2001.03794 — FPT on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs for Grundy Coloring
rows.status_semantics: The open question of whether \textsc{Grundy Coloring} is FPT on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs parameterized by $k$ was resolved affirmatively by Agrawal, Lokshtanov, Panolan, Saurabh, and Verma (arXiv:2410.20629, STACS 2025), which provides an FPT algorithm for Grundy Coloring on $K_{i,j}$-free graphs (subsuming $K_{t,t}$-free) parameterized by $k$, alongside an FPT algorithm for Partial Grundy Coloring on general graphs.
rows.class_notes: Solved yes/no FPT question on K_{t,t}-free graphs.

rows.title: Parameterized complexity of Partial Grundy Coloring on general graphs
rows.paper_title: Grundy Coloring & friends, Half-Graphs, Bicliques
rows.statement_text: What is the parameterized complexity of \textsc{Partial Grundy Coloring} on general graphs, parameterized by $k$?
rows.context_text: The authors were unable to prove W[1]-hardness for Partial Grundy Coloring because half-graphs cannot serve as color-propagation units (the partial Grundy number is already large on a large half-graph $H_{t,t}$). The class of $H_{t,t}$-free graphs is suggested as a promising intermediate case for understanding the general-graph complexity.
rows.source_text: What is the parameterized complexity of \textsc{Partial Grundy Coloring} on general graphs, parameterized by $k$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2001.03794 — Parameterized complexity of Partial Grundy Coloring on general graphs
rows.status_semantics: The open problem from arXiv:2001.03794 — whether Partial Grundy Coloring on general graphs is FPT when parameterized by k — was resolved affirmatively. Agrawal, Lokshtanov, Panolan, Saurabh, and Verma (STACS 2025, arXiv:2410.20629) give an FPT algorithm for Partial Grundy Coloring on general graphs, along with an FPT algorithm for Grundy Coloring on K_{i,j}-free graphs, directly answering the question posed in the source paper.
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the parameterized complexity' asks for a classification, not a single proposition (even though later resolved).

rows.title: Informal open question on the exact value of $b_\varepsilon$
rows.paper_title: A note on sublinear separators and expansion
rows.statement_text: It is unclear whether the upper or the lower bound on $b_\varepsilon$ can be improved for $0<\varepsilon<\frac{1}{2}$. While the fact that $b_{1/2}=0$ matches the lower bound suggests that a better construction improving the upper bound in general could exist, it is also plausible that this is just a "dimension 2" artifact and in fact the lower bound might be possible to improve for $\varepsilon<1/2$ (possibly leading to discontinuity of $b_\varepsilon$ at $\varepsilon=1/2$).
rows.context_text: After establishing $\frac{1}{2\varepsilon}-1\leq b_\varepsilon\leq\frac{1}{2\varepsilon}-\frac{1}{2}$ for $0<\varepsilon<\frac{1}{2}$ and $b_\varepsilon=0$ for $\frac{1}{2}\leq\varepsilon\leq 1$, the paper notes that the remaining gap of $\frac{1}{2}$ in the bounds on $b_\varepsilon$ is unresolved, and that the behaviour at $\varepsilon=1/2$ makes the problem structurally interesting.
rows.source_text: It is unclear whether the upper or the lower bound on $b_\varepsilon$ can be improved for $0<\varepsilon<\frac{1}{2}$. While the fact that $b_{1/2}=0$ matches the lower bound suggests that a better construction improving the upper bound in general could exist, it is also plausible that this is just a "dimension 2" artifact and in fact the lower bound might be possible to improve for $\varepsilon<1/2$ (possibly leading to discontinuity of $b_\varepsilon$ at $\varepsilon=1/2$).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2001.09679 — Informal open question on the exact value of $b_\varepsilon$
rows.defer_reason: 'Unclear whether the upper or lower bound can be improved' — improve-the-bounds meta, no single proposition.

rows.title: Informal conjecture $b'_\varepsilon = b_\varepsilon$ for $0<\varepsilon<\tfrac{1}{2}$
rows.paper_title: A note on sublinear separators and expansion
rows.statement_text: It seems likely that $b'_\varepsilon = b_\varepsilon$ when $0 < \varepsilon < \frac{1}{2}$ as well.
rows.context_text: The parameter $b'_\varepsilon$ is defined analogously to $b_\varepsilon$ but with the one-sided condition $s_\mathcal{G}(n)=\Omega(n^{1-\varepsilon})$ rather than $\Theta(n^{1-\varepsilon})$; equality $b'_\varepsilon=b_\varepsilon=0$ already holds for $\frac{1}{2}\leq\varepsilon\leq 1$, but extending this to $0<\varepsilon<\frac{1}{2}$ requires showing that a one-sided lower bound on separators alone forces the same expansion lower bound, which is not obvious.
rows.source_text: It seems likely that $b'_\varepsilon = b_\varepsilon$ when $0 < \varepsilon < \frac{1}{2}$ as well.
rows.class_notes: b_eps and b'_eps are paper-local parameters (separator-vs-expansion exponents) only partially defined in context.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture on Existence of Hoppers for Large Euler Genus
rows.paper_title: Excluded minors for the Klein Bottle I. Low connectivity case
rows.statement_text: Hoppers may show up when the Euler genus is large enough.
rows.context_text: Hoppers are a mysterious special class of graphs that arise as building blocks for obstructions of connectivity two for arbitrary Euler genus. The paper proves that hoppers do not exist when the genus is small and the authors are unable to construct any example for larger genus, yet they believe hoppers will eventually appear for sufficiently large genus.
rows.source_text: Hoppers may show up when the Euler genus is large enough.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2002.00258 — Informal Conjecture on Existence of Hoppers for Large Euler Genus
rows.class_notes: 'Hopper' is a paper-local class never defined in the record; statement also hedged ('may show up').

rows.title: Informal Conjecture on Universal N0 for Bumping a Ladder
rows.paper_title: Excluding a ladder
rows.statement_text: There exists a constant $N_0$ such that for every $k$, Theorem 2 holds with $N = N_0$. For all we know, this might even be true with $N_0 = 4$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 2 (Bumping a ladder) asserts that every 3-connected graph containing a union of $N$ vertex-disjoint copies of $L_k$ as a minor contains $L_{k+1}$ as a minor, but the value of $N$ a priori depends on $k$. The authors remark that while 3-connectivity is necessary (see Figure 1), they believe the dependence of $N$ on $k$ is not.
rows.source_text: There exists a constant $N_0$ such that for every $k$, Theorem 2 holds with $N = N_0$. For all we know, this might even be true with $N_0 = 4$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2002.00496 — Informal Conjecture on Universal N0 for Bumping a Ladder
rows.class_notes: Context fully restates Theorem 2 (bumping a ladder), so the exists-N0 uniformization is self-contained; ladders L_k are standard.

rows.title: Open Problem: Characterization of Unavoidable Graphs
rows.paper_title: Excluding a ladder
rows.statement_text: Obtain a full characterization of unavoidable graphs, where a graph $H$ is unavoidable if the cover graph of every poset with large enough dimension contains $H$ as a minor.
rows.context_text: The known positive results are that every ladder and $K_4$ are unavoidable. Kelly's construction shows that every unavoidable graph must be a minor of some graph from that construction, providing a necessary condition. The paper's Conjecture 6 proposes that this necessary condition is also sufficient.
rows.source_text: Obtain a full characterization of unavoidable graphs, where a graph $H$ is unavoidable if the cover graph of every poset with large enough dimension contains $H$ as a minor.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2002.00496 — Open Problem: Characterization of Unavoidable Graphs
rows.defer_reason: 'Obtain a full characterization' is a characterization goal, not a proposition; the concrete version is Conjecture 6 (next row).

rows.title: Conjecture 6
rows.paper_title: Excluding a ladder
rows.statement_text: A graph $H$ is unavoidable if and only if $H$ is a minor of some graph from Kelly's construction.
rows.context_text: Kelly's construction gives posets with unbounded dimension whose cover graphs are planar and have pathwidth 3, implying every unavoidable graph must be a minor of some graph in this family. The authors conjecture this necessary condition is also sufficient, and note an equivalent reformulation: $H$ is unavoidable if and only if $H$ is a minor of some graph obtained by gluing copies of $K_4$ along edges in a path-like way and subdividing all horizontal edges once.
rows.source_text: A graph $H$ is unavoidable if and only if $H$ is a minor of some graph from Kelly's construction.
rows.class_notes: Precise iff, but Kelly's construction and poset-dimension cover-graph machinery are not defined in the record.

rows.title: Problem 3
rows.paper_title: An update on reconfiguring $10$-colorings of planar graphs
rows.statement_text: What is the minimum integer $\kappa$ such that for every planar graph $G$ with $n$ vertices, $R_{\kappa}(G)$ has diameter $O(n)$?
rows.context_text: Planar graphs are 5-degenerate and have maximum average degree less than 6; prior results give $R_8(G)$ diameter $O(n(\log n)^7)$ and $R_{12}(G)$ diameter at most $6n$ for planar $G$. The paper improves the upper bound to $\kappa \leq 10$ via Theorem 4, and an isolated 6-coloring of the icosahedron shows $\kappa \geq 7$; the authors note that $\kappa = 7$ cannot be excluded.
rows.source_text: What is the minimum integer $\kappa$ such that for every planar graph $G$ with $n$ vertices, $R_{\kappa}(G)$ has diameter $O(n)$?
rows.status_semantics: Problem 3 asks for the minimum $\kappa$ such that $R_\kappa(G)$ has diameter $O(n)$ for every $n$-vertex planar graph $G$. The source paper establishes $7 \leq \kappa \leq 10$. A 2024 paper (arXiv:2411.00679, EJC 2025) proves a conjecture of Dvo\v{r}\'ak and Feghali in the list-coloring setting, showing that any two 10-list-colorings of a planar graph can be connected by a recoloring sequence of length linear in the number of vertices. The exact minimum $\kappa$ for uniform $k$-colorings of general planar graphs remains open.
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the minimum integer kappa' asks for an exact value with only 7<=kappa<=10 known.
rows.class_notes: Concrete instances (e.g. kappa=10 suffices) would be B2-able but the record asks for the optimum.

rows.title: Informal conjecture on the generality of the probabilistic minor strategy
rows.paper_title: Clique minors in graphs with a forbidden subgraph
rows.statement_text: The strategy introduced in this paper, or minor modifications of it, could provide a useful tool for finding clique minors in graphs under other structural restrictions as well.
rows.context_text: After showing that the same randomised star-contraction strategy yields both Theorem 1 (for $K_s$-free graphs with bounded independence number) and a short proof of the Kühn–Osthus result for $K_{s,t}$-free graphs, the authors note that the approach performs well in two quite different structural settings.
rows.source_text: The strategy introduced in this paper, or minor modifications of it, could provide a useful tool for finding clique minors in graphs under other structural restrictions as well.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2002.11100 — Informal conjecture on the generality of the probabilistic minor strategy
rows.defer_reason: Pure meta remark that a proof strategy 'could provide a useful tool'; no mathematical proposition.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (outerplanar strongly perfect graphs)
rows.paper_title: New Examples of Minimal Non-Strongly-Perfect Graphs
rows.statement_text: While obtaining a complete list of minimal non-strongly-perfect graphs appears to be out of reach, one might conjecture that the characterization of outerplanar strongly perfect graphs can be obtained [statement truncated in PDF source].
rows.context_text: At the end of the paper the authors remark that a full characterisation of minimal non-strongly-perfect graphs is currently intractable, but suggest that restricting to the outerplanar setting may be a more approachable subproblem.
rows.source_text: While obtaining a complete list of minimal non-strongly-perfect graphs appears to be out of reach, one might conjecture that the characterization of outerplanar strongly perfect graphs can be obtained [statement truncated in PDF source].
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2003.01846 — Informal Conjecture (outerplanar strongly perfect graphs)
rows.defer_reason: Characterization goal ('characterization of outerplanar strongly perfect graphs can be obtained') and the statement is truncated in the source.

rows.title: Combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm for MWIS in perfect graphs
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs of bounded treewidth and the container method
rows.statement_text: Design a combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm for the Maximum Weight Independent Set problem in perfect graphs.
rows.context_text: The polynomial-time algorithm of Grötschel, Lovász, and Schrijver for MWIS in perfect graphs relies on the ellipsoid method. The authors explicitly note that designing a combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm for this problem remains an important open problem.
rows.source_text: Design a combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm for the Maximum Weight Independent Set problem in perfect graphs.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2003.05185 — Combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm for MWIS in perfect graphs
rows.status_semantics: The problem of designing a combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm for MWIS in perfect graphs (bypassing the ellipsoid-method-based algorithm of Grötschel, Lovász, and Schrijver) remains open in full generality. A verified partial result was published in 2024: Abrishami, Chudnovsky, Dibek, and Vušković give a combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm for perfect graphs of bounded degree that exclude a prism or a hole of length four as an induced subgraph, using even-set balanced separators and submodular function minimization. No paper resolving the full problem for all perfect graphs was found.
rows.defer_reason: 'Design a combinatorial algorithm' is a task; 'combinatorial' has no formal meaning.

rows.title: MWIS in P7-free graphs
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs of bounded treewidth and the container method
rows.statement_text: Determine the computational complexity of the Maximum Weight Independent Set problem in $P_7$-free graphs.
rows.context_text: Polynomial-time algorithms for MWIS in $P_5$-free graphs (2014) and $P_6$-free graphs (2019) were previously known. This paper extends tractability to the class $\mathcal{C}$ (which contains all $P_5$-free graphs), but the authors note that the case of $P_7$-free graphs remains open.
rows.source_text: Determine the computational complexity of the Maximum Weight Independent Set problem in $P_7$-free graphs.
rows.status_semantics: The full complexity of Maximum Weight Independent Set in P7-free graphs remains open. Gartland and Lokshtanov (FOCS 2020) established a quasi-polynomial time algorithm running in n^{O(k^2 log^3 n)} for all Pk-free graphs (including P7-free), the first super-polynomial upper bound beyond NP-hardness evidence. More recently, Chudnovsky, Czyzewska, Kluk, Pilipczuk, and Rzazewski (ISAAC 2025) extended polynomial-time tractability to P7-free graphs of bounded clique number. The full polynomial-time resolution for all P7-free graphs remains open as of 2026.
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the computational complexity' of MWIS in P7-free graphs is not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: The P-vs-NP-hard dichotomy instance ('MWIS in P7-free is in P') would be a B2-able sharpening.

rows.title: The $\varepsilon$-$t$-Net Problem
rows.paper_title: The $ε$-$t$-Net Problem
rows.statement_text: How small are the smallest $\varepsilon$-$t$-nets for $H$? Can we compute them efficiently?
rows.context_text: Given a finite hypergraph $H=(V,E)$, a positive integer $t$, and $\varepsilon\in(t/|V|,1)$, an $\varepsilon$-$t$-net is a family $S\subseteq\binom{V}{t}$ of $t$-element subsets of $V$ such that every hyperedge $e\in E$ with $|e|\geq\varepsilon|V|$ contains some $s\in S$. The problem generalises the classical $\varepsilon$-net problem ($t=1$) and the Mnet notion ($t=\Theta(\varepsilon|V|)$), and arises naturally in secret sharing and other combinatorial contexts.
rows.source_text: How small are the smallest $\varepsilon$-$t$-nets for $H$? Can we compute them efficiently?
rows.defer_reason: 'How small are the smallest eps-t-nets? Can we compute them efficiently?' — open-ended size + algorithmic questions.
rows.class_notes: Hypergraph setting, so in scope area-wise, but no single proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.2
rows.paper_title: A universal exponent for homeomorphs
rows.statement_text: For each 3-graph $H$, there exists $C_H > 0$ such that any 3-graph $G$ on $n$ vertices with at least $C_H n^{5/2}$ edges contains a homeomorph of $H$.
rows.context_text: The paper proves the exponent $3-1/5$ is universal but does not believe it to be tight. The conjectured optimal exponent $5/2 = 3 - 1/2$ is motivated by the Brown–Erdős–Sós result that $5/2$ is the correct exponent for the sphere $S^2$ (i.e., $K^3_4$), and by the analogy that homeomorphs in 3-graphs should appear roughly once spheres (homeomorphs of $S^2$) can be found, just as homeomorphs in graphs appear roughly once cycles (homeomorphs of $S^1$) can be found.
rows.source_text: For each 3-graph $H$, there exists $C_H > 0$ such that any 3-graph $G$ on $n$ vertices with at least $C_H n^{5/2}$ edges contains a homeomorph of $H$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.2 posits that the optimal exponent for homeomorphs of any fixed 3-graph $H$ is $5/2$; the source paper itself establishes the weaker universal bound $3-1/5 \approx 2.8$. Maya Sankar (2024) subsequently improved this upper bound to $8/3 \approx 2.667$ for all 2-dimensional simplicial complexes, bringing it closer to the conjectured optimum. The full conjecture remains open.
rows.class_notes: Precise extremal statement, but 'homeomorph of a 3-graph' (topological copy of the 2-complex) is not defined in the record and needs a topological primitive.

rows.title: Algorithm Termination Conjecture
rows.paper_title: Pentagon contact representations
rows.statement_text: We conjecture that the algorithm always terminates.
rows.context_text: The paper proposes an algorithm for computing homothetic pentagon contact representations based on systems of linear equations derived from five color forests. When the solution is non-negative it encodes distances between pentagon corners and the representation is constructed; otherwise negative variables guide a local change of the five color forest and the procedure restarts. The authors note that a proof of this conjecture would yield an existence proof for pentagon contact representations independent of Schramm's Monster Packing Theorem. Analogous algorithms for homothetic triangle and square contact representations exist but also lack termination proofs.
rows.source_text: We conjecture that the algorithm always terminates.
rows.class_notes: Termination claim about a specific paper-local algorithm (five-color-forest linear systems) not fully specified in the record.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture on reflexive graphs and coloring abundance
rows.paper_title: Reflexive coloring complexes for 3-edge-colorings of cubic graphs
rows.statement_text: Graphs with a large number of colorings tend to be reflexive.
rows.context_text: The authors note that their results and extensive computational evidence support this observation, while acknowledging it appears counterintuitive: a large coloring complex might have too many colorings for the original graph to be reflexive. The underlying open question of why so many reflexive graphs exist (and why any exist at all) is stated as the central remaining problem.
rows.source_text: Graphs with a large number of colorings tend to be reflexive.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2004.06788 — Informal Conjecture on reflexive graphs and coloring abundance
rows.defer_reason: 'Graphs with many colorings tend to be reflexive' is a vague tendency claim, not a proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 7.1
rows.paper_title: Enumerating minimal dominating sets in the (in)comparability graphs of bounded dimension posets
rows.statement_text: For every $t$, there is an output-polynomial time algorithm for Dom-Enum in incomparability graphs of $S_{t}$-free posets.
rows.context_text: The algorithm of Corollary 5.3 already covers all comparability graphs of $S_{t}$-free posets. The paper asks what can be said about Dom-Enum in $H$-free incomparability graphs; since co-bipartite graphs are incomparability graphs, the question is only interesting for co-bipartite $H$. This conjecture is proposed as the natural counterpart of Theorem 1.2 for the $S_t$-free setting.
rows.source_text: For every $t$, there is an output-polynomial time algorithm for Dom-Enum in incomparability graphs of $S_{t}$-free posets.
rows.class_notes: Existence of an output-polynomial Dom-Enum algorithm; S_t standard-example posets and Dom-Enum are standard in this literature.

rows.title: Conjecture 7.3
rows.paper_title: Enumerating minimal dominating sets in the (in)comparability graphs of bounded dimension posets
rows.statement_text: For any co-bipartite $H$, there is an output-polynomial time algorithm for Dom-Enum in $H$-free incomparability graphs.
rows.context_text: This is the broadest of the three conjectures in the concluding section, subsuming Conjecture 7.2. The authors note there is no blatant reason why the incomparability condition should be necessary, leaving open whether the result might hold for all $H$-free graphs with co-bipartite $H$.
rows.source_text: For any co-bipartite $H$, there is an output-polynomial time algorithm for Dom-Enum in $H$-free incomparability graphs.
rows.class_notes: Broadest of the paper's Dom-Enum conjectures (subsumes Conjecture 7.2); output-polynomial enumeration needs complexity foundation.

rows.title: Problem 3
rows.paper_title: Asymmetric list sizes in bipartite graphs
rows.statement_text: Given $\Delta_A$ and $\Delta_B$, what are optimal choices of $k_A \leq \Delta_A$ and $k_B \leq \Delta_B$ for which any bipartite graph $G = (V = A \cup B, E)$ with parts $A$ and $B$ having maximum degrees at most $\Delta_A$ and $\Delta_B$, respectively, is $(k_A, k_B)$-choosable?
rows.context_text: The authors introduce $(k_A, k_B)$-choosability as an asymmetric generalisation of ordinary list colouring of bipartite graphs, where vertices in part $A$ receive lists of size $k_A$ and vertices in part $B$ receive lists of size $k_B$. Problem 3 asks for the optimal list-size pairs in terms of the one-sided maximum degrees $\Delta_A$ and $\Delta_B$, and subsumes Conjecture 2 as its symmetric special case.
rows.source_text: Given $\Delta_A$ and $\Delta_B$, what are optimal choices of $k_A \leq \Delta_A$ and $k_B \leq \Delta_B$ for which any bipartite graph $G = (V = A \cup B, E)$ with parts $A$ and $B$ having maximum degrees at most $\Delta_A$ and $\Delta_B$, respectively, is $(k_A, k_B)$-choosable?
rows.defer_reason: 'What are optimal choices of k_A, k_B' asks for optimal parameter pairs, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Asymmetric generalization of the matched OPG bipartite list-chromatic problem (subsumes its symmetric Conjecture 2).

rows.title: Conjecture 7
rows.paper_title: Asymmetric list sizes in bipartite graphs
rows.statement_text:
Let the positive integers $\Delta_A, \Delta_B, k_A, k_B$ satisfy one of the following.
(i) Given $\varepsilon > 0$, we have $\Delta_A, \Delta_B \geq \Delta_0$ for some $\Delta_0 = \Delta_0(\varepsilon)$, and $k_A \geq \Delta_A^\varepsilon$ and $k_B \geq \Delta_B^\varepsilon$.
(ii) For some absolute constant $C > 1$, $k_A \geq C \log \Delta_B$ and $k_B \geq C \log \Delta_A$.
(iii) $\Delta_A = \Delta_B = \Delta$, and, for some absolute constant $C > 0$, $k_B \geq C(\Delta/\log\Delta)^{1/k_A} \log\Delta$ or $k_A \geq C(\Delta/\log\Delta)^{1/k_B} \log\Delta$.
Then any bipartite graph $G = (V = A \cup B, E)$ with parts $A$ and $B$ having maximum degrees at most $\Delta_A$ and $\Delta_B$, respectively, is $(k_A, k_B)$-choosable.
rows.context_text: Motivated by the asymptotic behaviour of $(k_A, k_B)$-choosability for complete bipartite graphs (Theorem 6 and Theorem 16), the authors conjecture three concrete asymmetric analogues of the Krivelevich–Alon conjecture. Condition (i) is weaker than Conjecture 2; conditions (ii) and (iii) are stronger. The paper shows Conjecture 7 holds for complete bipartite graphs (Theorem 15) and provides partial progress in the general case via Theorem 4 and its corollaries.
rows.source_text:
Let the positive integers $\Delta_A, \Delta_B, k_A, k_B$ satisfy one of the following.
(i) Given $\varepsilon > 0$, we have $\Delta_A, \Delta_B \geq \Delta_0$ for some $\Delta_0 = \Delta_0(\varepsilon)$, and $k_A \geq \Delta_A^\varepsilon$ and $k_B \geq \Delta_B^\varepsilon$.
(ii) For some absolute constant $C > 1$, $k_A \geq C \log \Delta_B$ and $k_B \geq C \log \Delta_A$.
(iii) $\Delta_A = \Delta_B = \Delta$, and, for some absolute constant $C > 0$, $k_B \geq C(\Delta/\log\Delta)^{1/k_A} \log\Delta$ or $k_A \geq C(\Delta/\log\Delta)^{1/k_B} \log\Delta$.
Then any bipartite graph $G = (V = A \cup B, E)$ with parts $A$ and $B$ having maximum degrees at most $\Delta_A$ and $\Delta_B$, respectively, is $(k_A, k_B)$-choosable.
rows.class_notes: Asymmetric strengthening/analogue of the Alon–Krivelevich OPG conjecture; (k_A,k_B)-choosability is defined in the sibling row's context and is a routine list-coloring wrapper.

rows.title: Optimality of the $o(n^{3/2})$ bound in Corollary 1.4
rows.paper_title: The regularity method for graphs with few 4-cycles
rows.statement_text: Is the exponent $3/2$ in the conclusion of Corollary 1.4 best possible? (Corollary 1.4 states: every $n$-vertex $C_5$-free graph can be made triangle-free by deleting $o(n^{3/2})$ edges.)
rows.context_text: Immediately after stating Corollary 1.4, the authors note that they do not know whether the exponent $3/2$ is best possible. Proposition 1.5 shows that the hypothesis in the related Corollary 1.3 cannot be relaxed, but does not directly address the tightness of the exponent in Corollary 1.4. Proposition 1.7 shows the exponent $3/2$ in Theorem 1.6 is tight.
rows.source_text: Is the exponent $3/2$ in the conclusion of Corollary 1.4 best possible? (Corollary 1.4 states: every $n$-vertex $C_5$-free graph can be made triangle-free by deleting $o(n^{3/2})$ edges.)
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2004.10180 — Optimality of the $o(n^{3/2})$ bound in Corollary 1.4
rows.class_notes: Yes/no tightness question; Corollary 1.4 is restated in full, but 'best possible' needs an o()/lower-bound-family asymptotic reading.

rows.title: Improvement of the bound in Theorem 1.12
rows.paper_title: The regularity method for graphs with few 4-cycles
rows.statement_text: Can the bound $o(\sqrt{n})$ for subsets of $[n]$ without a nontrivial solution to $x_1+x_2+2x_3=x_4+3x_5$ be improved to $n^{1/2-\varepsilon}$ for some $\varepsilon>0$?
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.12 shows that every subset of $[n]$ without a nontrivial solution to $x_1+x_2+2x_3=x_4+3x_5$ has size $o(\sqrt{n})$, improving on the trivial Sidon-set upper bound of $(1+o(1))\sqrt{n}$. The authors explicitly identify as an open problem whether the bound can be further strengthened to $n^{1/2-\varepsilon}$.
rows.source_text: Can the bound $o(\sqrt{n})$ for subsets of $[n]$ without a nontrivial solution to $x_1+x_2+2x_3=x_4+3x_5$ be improved to $n^{1/2-\varepsilon}$ for some $\varepsilon>0$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2004.10180 — Improvement of the bound in Theorem 1.12
rows.defer_reason: Additive combinatorics: sizes of subsets of [n] avoiding a linear equation, no graph content.

rows.title: Conjecture 5 (Improved Approximation Conjecture)
rows.paper_title: An algorithmic weakening of the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture
rows.statement_text: Every graph $H$ satisfies the improved approximation property, i.e., there exists a constant $\varepsilon > 0$ such that MIS admits a (randomized) $n^{1-\varepsilon}$-approximation polynomial algorithm on every $H$-free $n$-vertex graph $G$.
rows.context_text: This conjecture is introduced as an algorithmic weakening of the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture. It states informally that the inapproximability of MIS in general graphs can always be beaten in any proper hereditary class. It is strictly weaker than the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture, since MIS is polynomial in $P_6$-free graphs while it is open whether $P_5$ satisfies the Erdős-Hajnal property.
rows.source_text: Every graph $H$ satisfies the improved approximation property, i.e., there exists a constant $\varepsilon > 0$ such that MIS admits a (randomized) $n^{1-\varepsilon}$-approximation polynomial algorithm on every $H$-free $n$-vertex graph $G$.
rows.class_notes: Single proposition (every H has the n^{1-eps}-approximation property for MIS on H-free graphs); algorithmic weakening of Erdos–Hajnal.

rows.title: Question: optimal approximation exponent for MIS in H-free graphs
rows.paper_title: An algorithmic weakening of the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture
rows.statement_text: For a given $H$, what is the largest $\varepsilon > 0$ such that MIS admits an $O(n^{1-\varepsilon})$-approximation algorithm in $H$-free graphs?
rows.context_text: After establishing that many graphs $H$ satisfy the improved approximation property, the authors ask how to optimise the approximation ratio. They investigate this in Sections 3 and 4, proving e.g. a $\sqrt{OPT}$-approximation in $C_4$-free graphs and a tight inapproximability bound in triangle-free and high-girth graphs.
rows.source_text: For a given $H$, what is the largest $\varepsilon > 0$ such that MIS admits an $O(n^{1-\varepsilon})$-approximation algorithm in $H$-free graphs?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2004.12166 — Question: optimal approximation exponent for MIS in H-free graphs
rows.defer_reason: Asks 'what is the largest epsilon' — an optimal-value question, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (polynomial expansion and bounded twin-width)
rows.paper_title: Twin-width I: tractable FO model checking
rows.statement_text: All classes of polynomial expansion may also have bounded twin-width.
rows.context_text: The authors note that proper minor-closed classes have bounded twin-width, and observe that bounded twin-width is incomparable with bounded degree, bounded expansion, and nowhere denseness. They remark 'As far as we know, all classes of polynomial expansion may also have bounded twin-width,' suggesting this as an open direction. Figure 3 depicts this with a dash-dotted edge indicating possible inclusion.
rows.source_text: All classes of polynomial expansion may also have bounded twin-width.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2004.14789 — Informal Conjecture (polynomial expansion and bounded twin-width)
rows.class_notes: Same question as Twin-width II row: do polynomial-expansion classes have bounded twin-width; needs twin-width and expansion primitives plus class-level quantification.

rows.title: Question (common superclass for FPT FO model checking)
rows.paper_title: Twin-width I: tractable FO model checking
rows.statement_text: Do bounded twin-width and nowhere dense classes admit a natural common superclass still admitting an FPT algorithm for FO model checking?
rows.context_text: Figure 3 shows that bounded twin-width and nowhere dense classes together roughly subsume all current knowledge on fixed-parameter tractability of FO model checking. The authors explicitly pose whether a natural common superclass of both still admits an FPT algorithm for FO model checking.
rows.source_text: Do bounded twin-width and nowhere dense classes admit a natural common superclass still admitting an FPT algorithm for FO model checking?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2004.14789 — Question (common superclass for FPT FO model checking)
rows.status_semantics: The natural candidate common superclass — monadically NIP (monadically dependent) classes — has been identified in the literature and is known to contain both bounded twin-width and nowhere dense classes. Significant partial progress has been made: FPT FO model checking was proved for all monadically stable graph classes (which strictly contain all nowhere dense classes) in 2023, and for all classes of bounded merge-width (which unify bounded twin-width and bounded expansion) in 2025. The full question of whether all monadically NIP/dependent classes admit FPT FO model checking remains an open conjecture, widely regarded as the central problem in the area.
rows.defer_reason: Asks for a 'natural common superclass' — open-ended search for a class, not a proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.4
rows.paper_title: Graphs with polynomially many minimal separators
rows.statement_text: There exists $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that if no induced subgraph of $G$ is a $k$-creature, then $G$ has at most $|V(G)|^{f(k)}$ minimal separators.
rows.context_text: A $k$-creature is a graph whose vertex set partitions into sets $A, B, \{x_1,\ldots,x_k\}, \{y_1,\ldots,y_k\}$ satisfying connectivity, anticomplete, and matching conditions; every $k$-creature contains a theta, pyramid, prism, or turtle. The authors propose this as a stronger generalisation of Theorem 1.2, noting that even if true it does not fully characterise classes with the polynomial separator property (the class $\mathcal{D}$ of induced subgraphs of $k$-turtles with very long paths has the polynomial separator property yet contains $k$-creatures of arbitrarily large $k$).
rows.source_text: There exists $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that if no induced subgraph of $G$ is a $k$-creature, then $G$ has at most $|V(G)|^{f(k)}$ minimal separators.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.4 was disproved by Gartland and Lokshtanov (arXiv:2007.08761, posted July 2020), who exhibited a counterexample showing that forbidding k-creatures for a fixed k does not suffice to guarantee polynomially many minimal separators. Their paper proves instead the weaker result that hereditary classes excluding k-creatures are 'strongly quasi-tame' (quasi-polynomially many minimal separators) under an additional condition that minimal separators can be dominated by a bounded number of vertices. Related SODA 2023 work showed that forbidding both k-creatures and k-critters yields a quasi-polynomial separator bound.
rows.class_notes: k-creature only sketched in context ('connectivity, anticomplete, and matching conditions' unspecified); disproved, so right-polarity target once recovered.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.10
rows.paper_title: Many flows in the group connectivity setting
rows.statement_text: There exists a fixed constant $c > 1$ so that the following holds. For every 3-edge-connected oriented graph $G = (V, E)$ of order $n$, every abelian group $\Gamma$ with $|\Gamma| \geq 6$, and every $f : E \to \Gamma$, there exist at least $c^n$ flows $\phi : E \to \Gamma$ with $\phi(e) \neq f(e)$ for every $e \in E$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.9 establishes an explicit exponential lower bound on the number of $\Gamma$-connected flows for groups of size $k \geq 8$, but yields only the existence of a single flow for $k = 6, 7$. The authors attribute this gap to a shortcoming of their techniques and conjecture the exponential bound holds for all abelian groups of size at least 6. Theorem 1.9 confirms the conjecture for all groups except $\mathbb{Z}_6$ and $\mathbb{Z}_7$; Theorem 1.11 gives a superpolynomial (but not exponential) lower bound for $\mathbb{Z}_6$.
rows.source_text: There exists a fixed constant $c > 1$ so that the following holds. For every 3-edge-connected oriented graph $G = (V, E)$ of order $n$, every abelian group $\Gamma$ with $|\Gamma| \geq 6$, and every $f : E \to \Gamma$, there exist at least $c^n$ flows $\phi : E \to \Gamma$ with $\phi(e) \neq f(e)$ for every $e \in E$.
rows.class_notes: Group-connectivity flow counting; finite statement (exponentially many Gamma-flows avoiding forbidden values) over finite abelian groups.

rows.title: Optimality of the exponent 1/4
rows.paper_title: On the cop number of graphs of high girth
rows.statement_text: Is the coefficient $\frac{1}{4}$ of $g$ in the lower bound $\Omega\!\left((\delta-1)^{g/4}\right)$ for the cop number of graphs of girth $g$ and minimum degree $\delta$ best possible, i.e., can the exponent be improved to $\left(\frac{1}{4}+\varepsilon\right)g$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$?
rows.context_text: Immediately after stating Theorem 1.1, the authors note: 'One may naturally ask if the coefficient $\frac{1}{4}$ of $g$ is best possible. While we do not have an answer to this question …'. They exhibit conditional evidence (via Meyniel's conjecture and Conjecture 1.3) that $\frac{1}{4}$ is optimal, and also prove via Ramanujan graphs that the true exponent cannot exceed $\frac{3}{8}$.
rows.source_text: Is the coefficient $\frac{1}{4}$ of $g$ in the lower bound $\Omega\!\left((\delta-1)^{g/4}\right)$ for the cop number of graphs of girth $g$ and minimum degree $\delta$ best possible, i.e., can the exponent be improved to $\left(\frac{1}{4}+\varepsilon\right)g$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$?
rows.defer_reason: 'Is the coefficient 1/4 best possible / can the exponent be improved' — a bound-improvement question with unpinned asymptotic quantification.

rows.title: Open problem: convex drawings vs. pseudocircular drawings
rows.paper_title: Topological Drawings meet Classical Theorems from Convex Geometry
rows.statement_text: The relation between convex drawings and pseudocircular drawings remains open.
rows.context_text: Arroyo, Richter, and Sunohara proved that hereditary-convex drawings are precisely the pseudospherical drawings (i.e., pseudocircular drawings satisfying two additional properties). The analogous characterisation for (non-hereditary) convex drawings in terms of pseudocircular drawings has not been established.
rows.source_text: The relation between convex drawings and pseudocircular drawings remains open.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2005.12568 — Open problem: convex drawings vs. pseudocircular drawings
rows.defer_reason: 'The relation between convex and pseudocircular drawings remains open' — no proposition stated.

rows.title: Question 1.5
rows.paper_title: Finding an induced path that is not a shortest path
rows.statement_text: For fixed $k > 1$, is there a polynomial-time algorithm that, given a graph $G$ and $u, v \in V(G)$, decides whether there is an induced $uv$-path $P$ in $G$ of length at least $d(u, v) + k$?
rows.context_text: The paper's main result (Theorem 1.1) gives a polynomial-time algorithm for the case $k = 1$ (detecting any induced non-shortest path), and the authors note the algorithm can be adjusted for $k = 2$. The question remains open even for $k = 3$. Fixing $k$ is necessary, since Theorem 1.6 shows the problem is NP-hard when $k$ is part of the input (e.g., deciding whether there exists a $uv$-NSP of length at least $2d_G(u,v)$).
rows.source_text: For fixed $k > 1$, is there a polynomial-time algorithm that, given a graph $G$ and $u, v \in V(G)$, decides whether there is an induced $uv$-path $P$ in $G$ of length at least $d(u, v) + k$?
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no: poly-time detection of an induced uv-path of length >= d(u,v)+k for fixed k.

rows.title: Conjecture 7
rows.paper_title: Inverse problems for minimal complements and maximal supplements
rows.statement_text: We have that $T(n) = \widetilde{\Theta}(\sqrt{n})$. More generally, we have that $T(G) = \widetilde{\Theta}(\sqrt{|G|})$.
rows.context_text: The paper defines $\widetilde{\Theta}(g(n))$ to mean $f = \Omega(g(n)(\log n)^a)$ and $f(n) = O(g(n)(\log n)^b)$ for some integers $a,b$ (i.e., equality up to polylogarithmic factors). Theorem 1 gives a lower bound $T(n) \geq n^{1/3}/(2(\log n/\log 2)^{2/3})$ and Theorem 3 gives $T(n) = O(n^{3/4+\varepsilon})$; the conjecture proposes the true growth rate is $\widetilde{\Theta}(\sqrt{n})$.
rows.source_text: We have that $T(n) = \widetilde{\Theta}(\sqrt{n})$. More generally, we have that $T(G) = \widetilde{\Theta}(\sqrt{|G|})$.
rows.defer_reason: Minimal complements / maximal supplements in abelian groups — additive combinatorics, not graph theory.

rows.title: Question 8
rows.paper_title: Inverse problems for minimal complements and maximal supplements
rows.statement_text: Is the behavior of $T(n)$ different when $n$ is prime? How much does $T(G)$ depend on the structure of $G$ in addition to the order of $G$?
rows.context_text: The best upper bound constructions for $T(n)$ rely on the existence of subgroups of certain orders, so the best known upper bound for $T(p)$ when $p$ is prime is only $O(p^{3/4+\varepsilon})$, which may not be tight.
rows.source_text: Is the behavior of $T(n)$ different when $n$ is prime? How much does $T(G)$ depend on the structure of $G$ in addition to the order of $G$?
rows.defer_reason: Additive combinatorics in finite groups, not graph theory; also a vague 'how much does it depend' question.

rows.title: Question 9
rows.paper_title: Inverse problems for minimal complements and maximal supplements
rows.statement_text: What is the optimal function in Theorem 5?
rows.context_text: Theorem 5 shows that every non-empty solid subset of a finite abelian group $G$ of size $O((|G|/\log|G|)^{1/4})$ is a maximal supplement, but it is unclear whether this bound is anywhere near optimal.
rows.source_text: What is the optimal function in Theorem 5?
rows.defer_reason: Additive combinatorics (optimal function in a theorem about maximal supplements), not graph theory.

rows.title: Question 10
rows.paper_title: Inverse problems for minimal complements and maximal supplements
rows.statement_text: How does the probability that a random subset of a finite group $G$ with parameter $p = p(|G|)$ is a minimal complement (or maximal supplement) vary with $p$? Is there a (sharp) threshold?
rows.context_text: Although being a minimal complement is not a monotone property, Theorems 1 and 3 together show that for small $p$ a random set is with high probability a minimal complement, and for large $p$ it is with high probability not a minimal complement, suggesting threshold behavior.
rows.source_text: How does the probability that a random subset of a finite group $G$ with parameter $p = p(|G|)$ is a minimal complement (or maximal supplement) vary with $p$? Is there a (sharp) threshold?
rows.defer_reason: Random subsets of finite groups as minimal complements — probabilistic additive combinatorics, not graph theory.

rows.title: Conjecture 5
rows.paper_title: A Thomassen-type method for planar graph recoloring
rows.statement_text: If $G$ is a planar graph of girth at least five on $n$ vertices, then $R_6(G)$ has diameter $O(n)$.
rows.context_text: Planar graphs of girth at least five are 2-degenerate and can be colored from lists of size three; combining Lemma 2 with Theorem 1 already shows 7 colors suffice for linear diameter. The authors believe this bound can be improved to 6, and suspect that a variation of their Thomassen-type method can be used to prove the conjecture, though several complications arise.
rows.source_text: If $G$ is a planar graph of girth at least five on $n$ vertices, then $R_6(G)$ has diameter $O(n)$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 5 (that $R_6(G)$ has diameter $O(n)$ for planar graphs of girth at least five on $n$ vertices) appears to remain open in full generality. A directly relevant follow-up, arXiv:2112.00631 (SIAM J. Discrete Math. 37(1), 2023), addresses exactly the same setting and proves that the 4-recolouring graph of every planar graph of girth five is connected via a discharging argument, as well as diameter theorems for the recolouring graph; whether the $R_6$ linear-diameter statement is among those results could not be confirmed from the abstract alone, so the conjecture is classified as partially addressed.
rows.class_notes: R_6(G) is the standard 6-recoloring reconfiguration graph (buildable primitive); diameter O(n) needs a constant wrapper plus planarity/girth hypotheses.

rows.title: The Small Conjecture
rows.paper_title: Twin-width II: small classes
rows.statement_text: Every small hereditary class has bounded twin-width.
rows.context_text: The paper proves that every bounded twin-width class is small (has at most $n! c^n$ labeled $n$-vertex graphs for some constant $c$), unifying results for bounded treewidth, proper subclasses of permutation graphs, and proper minor-closed classes. The small conjecture posits the converse. The authors explore it by verifying bounded twin-width for many natural small hereditary classes that could serve as counterexamples, including subdivisions of $K_n$, graphs of bounded stack or queue number, and cubic expanders from iterated random 2-lifts.
rows.source_text: Every small hereditary class has bounded twin-width.
rows.status_semantics: The Small Conjecture ('every small hereditary class has bounded twin-width') was disproved by Bonnet, Geniet, Tessera, and Thomassé in Twin-width VII (arXiv:2204.12330, 2022), who constructed a family of induced subgraphs of an infinite Cayley graph that is small (as labeled graphs) yet has unbounded twin-width. Prior to the disproof, Twin-width IV (arXiv:2102.03117, 2021) established a positive resolution in the restricted setting of hereditary classes of ordered graphs, where bounded twin-width and smallness (growth at most 2^{O(n)}) are equivalent.
rows.class_notes: Small conjecture (disproved): 'small' defined in context (at most n! c^n labeled graphs); quantifies over hereditary classes, twin-width primitive required.

rows.title: Question: polynomial expansion and twin-width
rows.paper_title: Twin-width II: small classes
rows.statement_text: Do classes with polynomial expansion have bounded twin-width?
rows.context_text: Towards the small conjecture, the authors identify showing that small sparse classes have bounded (sparse) twin-width as a first, challenging step. Since classes with polynomial expansion are small, confirming bounded twin-width for them would constitute meaningful progress.
rows.source_text: Do classes with polynomial expansion have bounded twin-width?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2006.09877 — Question: polynomial expansion and twin-width
rows.class_notes: Same proposition as the Twin-width I remark on polynomial expansion vs bounded twin-width.

rows.title: Informal conjecture on MIS approximability vs. Min Dominating Set
rows.paper_title: Twin-width III: Max Independent Set, Min Dominating Set, and Coloring
rows.statement_text: Max Independent Set may have a very different approximability status than Min Dominating Set on bounded twin-width graphs.
rows.context_text: Theorem 6 establishes $O(1)$-approximation algorithms for Min Dominating Set and Min $r$-Dominating Set on bounded twin-width graphs, but Max Independent Set (Distance-1 MIS) is explicitly excluded from that theorem. The authors note further that a constant approximation for Max Independent Set on bounded twin-width graphs with arbitrarily large clique number would imply a PTAS, and they give some evidence that MIS sits in a fundamentally different approximability regime.
rows.source_text: Max Independent Set may have a very different approximability status than Min Dominating Set on bounded twin-width graphs.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2007.14161 — Informal conjecture on MIS approximability vs. Min Dominating Set
rows.status_semantics: The 2022/2023 paper by Bergé, Bonnet, Déprés, and Watrigant (arXiv:2207.07708, STACS 2023) directly addresses MIS approximability on bounded twin-width graphs, establishing a polynomial-time n^ε-approximation algorithm and a time–approximation tradeoff (O(1)^{2^q-1}-approximation in time exp(O_q(n^{2^{-q}}))). This confirms that MIS sits in a strictly worse approximability regime than Min Dominating Set (which admits an O(1)-approximation) on bounded twin-width graphs, supporting the conjecture. However, no hardness result ruling out a constant-factor approximation for MIS on bounded twin-width has been established, so the precise approximability boundary remains open.
rows.defer_reason: 'May have a very different approximability status' — informal comparison, not a proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.2
rows.paper_title: Automorphism groups of maps in linear time
rows.statement_text: There is no $\varepsilon > 0$ for which there is an algorithm for testing isomorphism of maps of unrestricted genus in time $O(n^{2-\varepsilon})$.
rows.context_text: The best known algorithm for the simultaneous conjugation problem runs in time $O(n^2 \log d / \log n + dn \log n)$, yielding an $O(n^2/\log n)$ algorithm for map isomorphism and automorphism problems of unrestricted genus. In complexity theory this is not considered 'truly subquadratic', which motivates the conjecture.
rows.source_text: There is no $\varepsilon > 0$ for which there is an algorithm for testing isomorphism of maps of unrestricted genus in time $O(n^{2-\varepsilon})$.
rows.class_notes: Fine-grained lower-bound conjecture (no truly subquadratic map-isomorphism algorithm); objects are combinatorial maps.

rows.title: Open subproblem: conditional superlinear lower bound
rows.paper_title: Automorphism groups of maps in linear time
rows.statement_text: An interesting open subproblem is to prove a conditional ``truly superlinear'' lower bound for any of the mentioned problems (map isomorphism, simultaneous conjugation).
rows.context_text: Some progress has been made: the communication complexity of the simultaneous conjugation problem is $\Omega(dn\log n)$ for $d>1$, and under the decision tree model the search version has lower bound $\Omega(n\log n)$. A conditional lower bound ruling out, e.g., $O(n\,\mathrm{polylog}\,n)$ algorithms remains open.
rows.source_text: An interesting open subproblem is to prove a conditional ``truly superlinear'' lower bound for any of the mentioned problems (map isomorphism, simultaneous conjugation).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2008.01616 — Open subproblem: conditional superlinear lower bound
rows.defer_reason: Task to 'prove a conditional truly superlinear lower bound for any of the mentioned problems' — meta, hypothesis unspecified.

rows.title: Question 4.1
rows.paper_title: A note on deterministic zombies
rows.statement_text: For every graph $G$, and for a graph $G'$ obtained from $G$ by successively adding vertices of degree 1, does it always hold that $z(G') = z(G)$?
rows.context_text: This question asks whether attaching pendant vertices (leaves) to a graph leaves its zombie number unchanged. It can be interpreted as asking whether zombies can benefit from waiting at a designated position before activating at the start of the game.
rows.source_text: For every graph $G$, and for a graph $G'$ obtained from $G$ by successively adding vertices of degree 1, does it always hold that $z(G') = z(G)$?
rows.class_notes: Zombie number z(G) is the deterministic zombies-and-survivors game parameter — not defined in context but standard in that literature; needs game primitive.

rows.title: Question 4.2
rows.paper_title: A note on deterministic zombies
rows.statement_text: For any graph $G$, is there an integer $k$ such that, for $G'_k$ the graph obtained from $G$ by subdividing all edges $k$ times then adding the original edges, $z(G'_k) > z(G) + 1$?
rows.context_text: This question probes how iterated edge subdivision combined with re-adding the original edges affects the zombie number. The authors remark that $k = 5$ may be a valid universal answer for any graph $G$.
rows.source_text: For any graph $G$, is there an integer $k$ such that, for $G'_k$ the graph obtained from $G$ by subdividing all edges $k$ times then adding the original edges, $z(G'_k) > z(G) + 1$?
rows.class_notes: Precise construction (k-fold subdivision plus original edges); same zombie-number primitive as Question 4.1.

rows.title: Conjecture 2
rows.paper_title: On the tree-width of even-hole-free graphs
rows.statement_text: There is a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that every even-hole-free graph of degree at most $d$ has tree-width at most $f(d)$.
rows.context_text: The class of even-hole-free graphs has unbounded tree-width in general (e.g., complete graphs and chordal graphs). Bounded degree is conjectured to be a sufficient structural restriction. If true, it would imply that even-hole-freeness is testable with constant query complexity and polylogarithmic running time in the bounded-degree graph model, via CMSO testability on bounded tree-width graphs. The paper proves the conjecture for $d \leq 3$ (subcubic graphs) and proves a weakening for $d = 4$.
rows.source_text: There is a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that every even-hole-free graph of degree at most $d$ has tree-width at most $f(d)$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 2 from arXiv:2008.05504 — that every even-hole-free graph of degree at most $d$ has tree-width at most $f(d)$ for some function $f$ — was resolved affirmatively within weeks of being posted. Abrishami, Chudnovsky, and Vušković (arXiv:2009.01297, submitted September 2020) proved the full statement by showing that decomposition cutsets in even-hole-free bounded-degree graphs can be organized into a bounded number of well-behaved collections, yielding bounded treewidth. The internal reference arXiv:2108.01162 addresses a related but distinct conjecture about unavoidable induced subgraphs in bounded-degree graphs; arXiv:2507.06169 provides a counterexample to a different conjecture of Trotignon about outerstring induced subgraphs, not to Conjecture 2.
rows.class_notes: Even-hole-free + max degree d implies treewidth at most f(d); solved (Abrishami-Chudnovsky-Vuskovic line).

rows.title: Conjecture 3
rows.paper_title: On the tree-width of even-hole-free graphs
rows.statement_text: For every $d \in \mathbb{N}$ there is a function $f_d : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that every graph with degree at most $d$ and tree-width at least $f_d(k)$ contains a $(k \times k)$-wall or the line graph of a $(k \times k)$-wall as an induced subgraph.
rows.context_text: This is an induced grid theorem analogue for bounded-degree graphs, motivated by the construction of (even hole, $K_4$)-free graphs of unbounded tree-width that requires vertices of large degree and large clique minors. The conjecture is described as 'wide open' and formally implies Conjecture 2. The paper's Theorem 1.1 (the induced grid theorem for minor-free graphs) can be seen as a step in the direction of its proof.
rows.source_text: For every $d \in \mathbb{N}$ there is a function $f_d : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that every graph with degree at most $d$ and tree-width at least $f_d(k)$ contains a $(k \times k)$-wall or the line graph of a $(k \times k)$-wall as an induced subgraph.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 3 from arXiv:2008.05504 was proved by Korhonen (arXiv:2203.13233, JCTB 2023), who showed that every graph of bounded maximum degree and sufficiently large treewidth contains a large wall or the line graph of a large wall as an induced subgraph. Prior partial progress by Abrishami, Chudnovsky, Dibek, Hajebi, and Rzążewski (arXiv:2108.01162) established the conclusion for $S_{t,t,t}$-free graphs and $(t$-theta, $t$-pyramid)-free graphs of bounded degree and large treewidth.
rows.class_notes: Induced grid theorem for bounded degree: needs wall and line-graph-of-wall primitives; solved (Korhonen).

rows.title: Conjecture 11
rows.paper_title: Coloring Drawings of Graphs
rows.statement_text: If $G$ is a universally cell 3-colorable graph which does not have a subcontraction isomorphic to $K^+_{3,n-3}$ for some $n \geq 7$, then $G$ is 3-flowable.
rows.context_text: The paper exhibits an infinite family of universally cell 3-colorable graphs ($K^+_{3,n-3}$ for $n \geq 7$) that do not admit a nowhere-zero 3-flow, showing the two properties are not equivalent in general. The authors believe excluding these graphs as subcontractions is sufficient to recover equivalence between universal cell 3-colorability and 3-flowability. Remarkably, a positive resolution of this conjecture would also imply Tutte's 3-Flow Conjecture.
rows.source_text: If $G$ is a universally cell 3-colorable graph which does not have a subcontraction isomorphic to $K^+_{3,n-3}$ for some $n \geq 7$, then $G$ is 3-flowable.
rows.class_notes: 'Universally cell 3-colorable' and K^+_{3,n-3} are paper-local and undefined in context; a positive resolution would imply Tutte's 3-flow conjecture.

rows.title: Conjecture 5
rows.paper_title: On a conjecture by Anthony Hill
rows.statement_text: The crossing number of $M_{n,t}$ is equal to $$H(n) - \frac{1}{2}\,t(k-1)(k-2),$$ where $k = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$.
rows.context_text: Here $M_{n,t}$ denotes the graph obtained from $K_n$ by removing a matching of size $t$, and $k = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$. Corollary 4 shows that geodesic drawings of $M_{n,t}$ built from sets of strength $0$ achieve this crossing count; the existence of many such sets of strength $0$ motivates the conjecture that this value is optimal.
rows.source_text: The crossing number of $M_{n,t}$ is equal to $$H(n) - \frac{1}{2}\,t(k-1)(k-2),$$ where $k = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$.
rows.class_notes: H(n) is the standard Harary-Hill value for cr(K_n); M_{n,t} = K_n minus a size-t matching is defined in context.

rows.title: Open Problem: Finding a Shortest Even Hole
rows.paper_title: Detecting a long even hole
rows.statement_text: Finding a shortest even hole in a graph (if one exists).
rows.context_text: After surveying polynomial-time algorithms for detecting even holes, the authors note that finding a shortest even hole remains open: "Another is finding a shortest even hole if there is one; this we have not yet been able to solve." This is posed as an analogue of the result of Chudnovsky, Scott and Seymour [8] that finds a shortest odd hole in $O(|G|^{14})$ time.
rows.source_text: Finding a shortest even hole in a graph (if one exists).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2009.05691 — Open Problem: Finding a Shortest Even Hole
rows.status_semantics: The problem of finding a shortest even hole in polynomial time was resolved by Cheong and Lu in a paper posted to arXiv on August 15, 2020 — roughly one month before Cook and Seymour posed it as an open problem — giving an O(n^31) algorithm; Chiu, Lai, and Lu subsequently improved this to O(n^23) in July 2022.
rows.class_notes: Statement is a task phrase, not a proposition; intended claim (poly-time algorithm for shortest even hole) is clear from context but needs restatement; now solved.

rows.title: Open Question: Detecting Holes of Length Divisible by Three
rows.paper_title: Detecting a long even hole
rows.statement_text: Can holes whose length is a multiple of three be detected in polynomial time?
rows.context_text: Having focused on even and odd holes, the authors remark: "what about holes of length a multiple of three, can we detect them in polynomial time? Triangle-free graphs with no holes of length a multiple of three have some very interesting properties [9], but we currently have no idea how to recognize such graphs." This suggests the problem of recognising graphs with no hole of length $\equiv 0 \pmod{3}$ as a natural next direction.
rows.source_text: Can holes whose length is a multiple of three be detected in polynomial time?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2009.05691 — Open Question: Detecting Holes of Length Divisible by Three
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no: poly-time detection of holes of length divisible by three.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (§1.2, Theorem 1.1 remarks)
rows.paper_title: Typical and Extremal Aspects of Friends-and-Strangers Graphs
rows.statement_text: The threshold for $\mathrm{FS}(X, Y)$ to be connected when $X, Y \sim G(n,p)$ is determined by the isolated-vertex threshold: ``it seems that (as in the usual case of a binomial random graph) this local obstruction to connectedness tells essentially the whole story.''
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 establishes a lower bound $p \leq (2^{-1/2}-\varepsilon)/n^{1/2}$ arising from isolated vertices and an upper bound $p \geq \exp(2(\log n)^{2/3})/n^{1/2}$ for the high-probability connectivity threshold of $\mathrm{FS}(X,Y)$. The authors suggest the gap between these bounds is an artifact of the proof method and that the two thresholds should coincide up to lower-order factors.
rows.source_text: The threshold for $\mathrm{FS}(X, Y)$ to be connected when $X, Y \sim G(n,p)$ is determined by the isolated-vertex threshold: ``it seems that (as in the usual case of a binomial random graph) this local obstruction to connectedness tells essentially the whole story.''
rows.status_semantics: Three follow-up papers have narrowed the gap in Theorem 1.1 of the source paper. Milojevic (arXiv:2210.03864, 2022) improved the isolated-vertex lower bound and disproved two formal conjectures from the source paper about threshold probabilities. Krishnan and Li (arXiv:2410.21334, 2024) established k-connectivity for p ≥ n^{-1/2+o(1)}, bringing the connectivity upper bound and isolated-vertex lower bound to the same asymptotic order n^{-1/2+o(1)}, broadly consistent with the informal conjecture. A sharp threshold matching an exact constant has not been proved.
rows.defer_reason: 'Tells essentially the whole story' / thresholds coincide 'up to lower-order factors' — informal, no precise proposition.

rows.title: Open Question (§1.2, Theorem 1.4 remarks)
rows.paper_title: Typical and Extremal Aspects of Friends-and-Strangers Graphs
rows.statement_text: Do the cutoff for $\mathrm{FS}(X, Y)$ to avoid isolated vertices and the cutoff for $\mathrm{FS}(X, Y)$ to have exactly 2 connected components coincide when $r \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$? That is, is $d_{r,r} = \lceil (3r+1)/4 \rceil$ for all $r \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$?
