rows.context_text: Theorem 1.4 proves $\lceil (3r+1)/4 \rceil \leq d_{r,r} \leq \lceil (3r+2)/4 \rceil$; the two bounds differ by 1 exactly when $r \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$ and are equal otherwise. The lower bound is the isolated-vertex threshold, and the authors note: ``we do not know how to prove that the cutoffs are exactly the same when $r \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$.''
rows.source_text: Do the cutoff for $\mathrm{FS}(X, Y)$ to avoid isolated vertices and the cutoff for $\mathrm{FS}(X, Y)$ to have exactly 2 connected components coincide when $r \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$? That is, is $d_{r,r} = \lceil (3r+1)/4 \rceil$ for all $r \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$?
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture asks whether $d_{r,r} = \lceil(3r+1)/4\rceil$ for all $r \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, i.e., whether the cutoffs for $\mathrm{FS}(X,Y)$ to avoid isolated vertices and to have exactly two connected components coincide in this residue class. This was settled affirmatively by the paper arXiv:2309.03848 on bipartite friends-and-strangers graphs, whose Corollary 1.3 establishes $d_{r,r} = \lceil(3r+1)/4\rceil$ for all $r$ (including $r \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$), and explicitly states that this settles ADK Conjecture 7.4 (the journal-version numbering of the open question from arXiv:2009.07840). The same paper also proves, as a main theorem, that the two cutoffs are equal for all $r$.
rows.class_notes: Precise equality d_{r,r} = ceil((3r+1)/4), but d_{r,r} and the FS(X,Y) framework are not defined in this record's context; solved.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.1
rows.paper_title: Fractional vertex-arboricity of planar graphs
rows.statement_text: Every planar graph has fractional vertex-arboricity at most two.
rows.context_text: This is the fractional generalization of the Albertson-Berman Conjecture: since $|V(G)|/a(G) \leq \mathrm{va}_f(G)$, the conjecture implies $a(G) \geq |V(G)|/2$. If true, it would also imply that every planar graph has fractional chromatic number at most four.
rows.source_text: Every planar graph has fractional vertex-arboricity at most two.
rows.class_notes: Fractional vertex-arboricity of planar graphs at most 2; fractional relaxation of Albertson-Berman, rational-valued parameter is fine.

rows.title: Problem 1.2
rows.paper_title: Extension of Gyarfas-Sumner conjecture to digraphs
rows.statement_text: What are the finite sets $\mathcal{F}$ of digraphs for which the class $\mathrm{Forb}_{\mathrm{ind}}(\mathcal{F})$ has bounded dichromatic number?
rows.context_text: This is the central problem of the paper, extending the Gyárfás-Sumner interrogation to directed graphs via the dichromatic number $\vec{\chi}$. A finite set $\mathcal{F}$ of digraphs is called heroic if $\mathrm{Forb}_{\mathrm{ind}}(\mathcal{F})$ has bounded dichromatic number. The paper focuses primarily on characterizing heroic sets of size three.
rows.source_text: What are the finite sets $\mathcal{F}$ of digraphs for which the class $\mathrm{Forb}_{\mathrm{ind}}(\mathcal{F})$ has bounded dichromatic number?
rows.status_semantics: Problem 1.2 asks for a full characterization of all finite sets of digraphs that are heroic (i.e., whose avoidance guarantees bounded dichromatic number); this characterization remains open. Partial progress includes: locally out-transitive oriented graphs shown to have dichromatic number at most 2 (arXiv:2103.07886); (P6, triangle)-free oriented graphs shown to have dichromatic number at most 382 (arXiv:2212.02272); heroes in oriented complete multipartite graphs nearly fully characterized (arXiv:2202.13306). A 2026 preprint (arXiv:2602.08736) disproves the related conjecture that {oriented star forest, hero} always forms a heroic pair, by constructing (Claw, C3)-free orientations of rook graphs with unbounded dichromatic number.
rows.defer_reason: 'What are the finite sets F ...' — a characterization program (heroic sets), not a single proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.2
rows.paper_title: Extension of Gyarfas-Sumner conjecture to digraphs
rows.statement_text: Let $H$ be a hero and let $F$ be an oriented forest. The set $\{\overleftrightarrow{K_2}, H, F\}$ is heroic if and only if: either $F$ is the disjoint union of oriented stars, or $H$ is a transitive tournament.
rows.context_text: This is the main conjecture of the paper, a digraph analog of the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture for oriented graphs. The 'only if' direction is proved in Section 6.1. The case where both conditions hold simultaneously (H a transitive tournament and F a disjoint union of oriented stars) is already settled by Chudnovsky, Scott and Seymour [6].
rows.source_text: Let $H$ be a hero and let $F$ be an oriented forest. The set $\{\overleftrightarrow{K_2}, H, F\}$ is heroic if and only if: either $F$ is the disjoint union of oriented stars, or $H$ is a transitive tournament.
rows.status_semantics: The 'only if' direction of Conjecture 4.2 is established in the source paper, and the case where both conditions hold simultaneously was resolved prior to posting by Chudnovsky–Scott–Seymour. Several special cases of the 'if' direction have since been proved: Aboulker, Aubian, and Charbit (2021) prove the case F = S₂⁺ with H = C₃; Aboulker, Aubian, Charbit, and Thomassé (2022) establish bounded dichromatic number for ⃗P₆-free triangle-free oriented graphs. A 2026 preprint by Aubian and Kuffner claims to disprove a conjecture of Aboulker, Charbit, and Naserasr via (claw, C₃)-free digraphs with unbounded dichromatic number, but the precise connection to Conjecture 4.2 was not confirmed from the abstract alone.
rows.class_notes: Relies on 'hero' and 'heroic' (defined only in sibling row axv_2009_13319_00's context / Berger et al.); easily recoverable, then a clean iff over dichromatic number.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.4
rows.paper_title: Extension of Gyarfas-Sumner conjecture to digraphs
rows.statement_text: Given an oriented forest $F$ and for every integer $k$, $\{\overleftrightarrow{K_2}, K_k, F\}$ is heroic.
rows.context_text: This conjecture is equivalent to the special case of Conjecture 4.2 where $H = TT_k$ is a transitive tournament, since every tournament on at least $2k$ vertices contains $TT_k$ as an induced subdigraph. It generalises the heroic-set result for $\{\overleftrightarrow{K_2}, K_k, P^+(3)\}$ proved in Theorem 4.3, where $K_k$ denotes the digraph on $k$ vertices with no arc.
rows.source_text: Given an oriented forest $F$ and for every integer $k$, $\{\overleftrightarrow{K_2}, K_k, F\}$ is heroic.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 4.4 — that $\{\overleftrightarrow{K_2}, K_k, F\}$ is heroic for every oriented forest $F$ and every integer $k$ — remains open in full generality. Two follow-up papers from the curated corpus establish special cases: arXiv:2103.07886 handles $F = S_2^+$ (oriented star with two out-edges) and arXiv:2212.02272 handles $F = \overrightarrow{P}_6$ with the clique constraint $\omega \le 2$ (triangle-free). A separate paper (arXiv:2202.13306) is noted to disprove a conjecture of Aboulker, Charbit, and Naserasr in the context of heroes in oriented complete multipartite graphs, but its abstract does not specify which conjecture from the source paper, so it is not attributed to Conjecture 4.4 here.
rows.class_notes: 'Heroic' paper-local (defined in axv_2009_13319_00's context); special case of Conjecture 4.2 (axv_2009_13319_01) with H a transitive tournament.

rows.title: Research direction: minimal heroic tournament families
rows.paper_title: Extension of Gyarfas-Sumner conjecture to digraphs
rows.statement_text: Characterize all finite and minimal families $\mathcal{H} = \{H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_k\}$ of tournaments such that $\{\overrightarrow{K_2}, \overleftrightarrow{K_2}, H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_k\}$ is a heroic set.
rows.context_text: Theorem 2.2 of Berger et al. [4] characterizes minimal heroic sets of this form with $|\mathcal{H}|=1$ (i.e., single-hero extensions). The authors note that a minimal heroic subset of order 2 or more cannot contain a hero tournament, making the general classification a natural open direction.
rows.source_text: Characterize all finite and minimal families $\mathcal{H} = \{H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_k\}$ of tournaments such that $\{\overrightarrow{K_2}, \overleftrightarrow{K_2}, H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_k\}$ is a heroic set.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2009.13319 — Research direction: minimal heroic tournament families
rows.defer_reason: Open-ended characterization request ('characterize all minimal heroic families'), not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Heroic-set vocabulary is precise but the target is a grand classification.

rows.title: Open Problem: exact constant in the exponent
rows.paper_title: Powers of paths in tournaments
rows.statement_text: It would be interesting to find the exact value of the constant factor in the exponent. Optimizing our proof can yield a lower bound of $n/2^{ck+o(k)}$ with $c \approx 3.9$, but is unlikely to give the correct bound.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1 and Yuster's construction together show that the optimal guaranteed length of the $k$-th power of a directed path in any $n$-vertex tournament has the form $n/2^{\Theta(k)}$. The paper provides a lower bound $n/2^{4k+6k}$ and an upper bound $k(k+1)n/2^k$, leaving a gap in the constant in the exponent.
rows.source_text: It would be interesting to find the exact value of the constant factor in the exponent. Optimizing our proof can yield a lower bound of $n/2^{ck+o(k)}$ with $c \approx 3.9$, but is unlikely to give the correct bound.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2010.05735 — Open Problem: exact constant in the exponent
rows.defer_reason: 'It would be interesting to find the exact constant in the exponent' — value-determination, not a proposition.

rows.title: Near-sunflower analogue of the Erdős-Rado Sunflower Conjecture
rows.paper_title: Near-sunflowers and focal families
rows.statement_text: If $\mathcal{F}$ is a $k$-uniform family of sets containing no near-sunflower of size $r$, then $|\mathcal{F}| \leq C^k$, where $C$ is a constant depending only on $r$.
rows.context_text: The paper introduces near-sunflowers as a weakening of sunflowers (allowing elements to belong to $0, 1, r-1,$ or $r$ sets in the family) and proposes this bound as a weaker version of the notorious Erdős-Rado sunflower conjecture. While the paper resolves the Erdős-Szemerédi-type question (exponential bound in $n$) for near-sunflowers via Theorem 1.3, the Erdős-Rado-type question (exponential bound in $k$) is explicitly left open; Section 6 proves the bound only under a stronger condition.
rows.source_text: If $\mathcal{F}$ is a $k$-uniform family of sets containing no near-sunflower of size $r$, then $|\mathcal{F}| \leq C^k$, where $C$ is a constant depending only on $r$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2010.05992 — Near-sunflower analogue of the Erdős-Rado Sunflower Conjecture
rows.defer_reason: Near-sunflower analogue of the Erdos-Rado sunflower conjecture — extremal set systems, excluded by the plan's set-systems scope rule.
rows.class_notes: Genuinely borderline: k-uniform families could be read as hypergraphs, but sunflower-type intersection problems are pure extremal set theory with no graph structure; ruled B5 consistently with the other sunflower rows; would be B2/needs-primitive if rescoped.

rows.title: Informal conjecture on non-tightness of upper bound for binary focal families
rows.paper_title: Near-sunflowers and focal families
rows.statement_text: For $q = 2$ and large $n$, the upper bound $g^{q\text{-ff}}_r(n) \leq (r-1)\, q^{\lceil (r-2)n/(r-1) \rceil}$ of Theorem 1.3 is not tight.
rows.context_text: Proposition 3.2 shows via Reed-Solomon codes that the upper bound is essentially tight when $q \geq n$ and $q$ is a prime power. The authors believe the binary case ($q = 2$) behaves differently, and support this belief by proving in Section 4 that the bound can be significantly improved when the family of binary vectors forms a linear code (closed under addition modulo 2).
rows.source_text: For $q = 2$ and large $n$, the upper bound $g^{q\text{-ff}}_r(n) \leq (r-1)\, q^{\lceil (r-2)n/(r-1) \rceil}$ of Theorem 1.3 is not tight.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2010.05992 — Informal conjecture on non-tightness of upper bound for binary focal families
rows.status_semantics: A 2024 follow-up by Huang, Shangguan, Zhang, and Zhao (arXiv:2410.23611) establishes asymptotically optimal bounds on the maximum size of focal-free uniform hypergraphs and codes, proving that the Alon–Holzman multiplicative constant (r-1) in the upper bound is not tight in general (specifically showing the true constant is strictly smaller as q→∞). This supports the spirit of the conjecture that the binary (q=2) bound is not tight, but the paper's sharpest results are in the large-q regime; whether the full q=2 conjecture is resolved for all large n remains unclear from available abstracts.
rows.defer_reason: Focal families of binary vectors (coding-theoretic set systems), out of graph/digraph/hypergraph scope.
rows.class_notes: Clearest B5 of the cluster: even if rescoped it would only be B3 — g^{q-ff}_r(n), 'focal family' and the exact Theorem 1.3 bound are paper-local and absent from the record, and 'not tight' is a meta-claim.

rows.title: Problem 1.4
rows.paper_title: Even Circuits in Oriented Matroids
rows.statement_text: Given an oriented matroid $\vec{M}$, decide whether there exists a directed circuit of even size in $\vec{M}$.
rows.context_text: This is the paper's straight-forward generalisation of the even dicycle problem from digraphs to oriented matroids, and constitutes the central algorithmic motivation of the work. The authors show in Theorem 1.6 that this problem is polynomially equivalent to recognising non-even oriented regular matroids.
rows.source_text: Given an oriented matroid $\vec{M}$, decide whether there exists a directed circuit of even size in $\vec{M}$.
rows.defer_reason: Oriented-matroid algorithmic problem (even directed circuit recognition), matroid theory rather than graph theory, and an algorithmic task rather than a proposition.

rows.title: Open Problem (Spherical Density Regime)
rows.paper_title: Efficient polynomial-time approximation scheme for the genus of dense graphs
rows.statement_text: Determine whether efficient constant-factor approximation algorithms exist for the genus of graphs with average degree at most 6 (spherical density regime), or prove that approximating the genus in this regime is hard.
rows.context_text: The authors partition the genus approximation problem into three density regimes. The spherical density regime (average degree $\leq 6$) is described as 'probably most interesting'; no efficient constant-factor approximation algorithm is known for it, and the authors identify resolving this as 'the major open problem'. Conjecture 1.1 (APX-hardness for cubic graphs) is the leading candidate resolution.
rows.source_text: Determine whether efficient constant-factor approximation algorithms exist for the genus of graphs with average degree at most 6 (spherical density regime), or prove that approximating the genus in this regime is hard.
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine whether approximation algorithms exist or prove hardness' — resolve-either-way research direction, not one proposition.
rows.class_notes: Concerns genus approximation in the average-degree-at-most-6 regime.

rows.title: Open Problem (asymptotic behavior of f(H,q))
rows.paper_title: Divisible subdivisions
rows.statement_text: Determine or estimate the asymptotic behavior of the best possible bound for $f(H, q)$ such that every $K_f$-minor contains a subdivision of $H$ in which every edge is replaced by a path whose length is divisible by $q$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1 guarantees a finite $f = f(H,q)$ for every graph $H$ of maximum degree at most 3 and every positive integer $q$, but the proof makes essentially no attempt to optimize $f$. The authors note in the introduction and again in the concluding remarks that the problem of determining the asymptotic behavior of $f(H,q)$ remains open, and that Ramsey constructions from the literature give some improvement but still yield bounds that are surely far from optimal.
rows.source_text: Determine or estimate the asymptotic behavior of the best possible bound for $f(H, q)$ such that every $K_f$-minor contains a subdivision of $H$ in which every edge is replaced by a path whose length is divisible by $q$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2012.05112 — Open Problem (asymptotic behavior of f(H,q))
rows.status_semantics: Das, Draganić, and Steiner (arXiv:2111.05723, JCTB 2023) resolved the asymptotic behavior of f(H,q) by proving f(H,q) ≤ (21/2)qn + 8n + 14q for subcubic H on n vertices, and showing this bound is optimal up to a constant multiplicative factor. This settles the open problem: the asymptotic order of f(H,q) is linear in qn. A 2025 follow-up by Hou and Wang (arXiv:2510.05697) studies the related function s_q(H) in complete-graph subdivisions and achieves further improvements for special cases.
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine or estimate the asymptotic behavior of f(H,q)' — value-determination request.
rows.class_notes: Status solved, but the record is still a determine-the-growth ask.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (linearity of g(q))
rows.paper_title: Divisible subdivisions
rows.statement_text: It seems plausible to believe that the first lemma is not tight and that the function $g(q)$ in Theorem 2 is linear in $q$ for any integer $q$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 2 establishes $g(q) = O(q \log q)$, and for prime $q$ the bound improves to $g(q) < 4q$. The authors note in the concluding remarks that Lemma 3.1 (which gives $\lceil 2q \ln q \rceil$ vertices) is likely not tight, and conjecture informally that the logarithmic factor is an artifact of the probabilistic method used.
rows.source_text: It seems plausible to believe that the first lemma is not tight and that the function $g(q)$ in Theorem 2 is linear in $q$ for any integer $q$.
rows.status_semantics: Das, Draganić, and Steiner (arXiv:2111.05723, 2021) proved that for any subcubic $n$-vertex graph $H$ and integer $q \geq 2$, the Alon–Krivelevich function satisfies $f(H,q) \leq \frac{21}{2}qn + 8n + 14q$, which is linear in $q$ and optimal up to a constant factor. Since cycles are subcubic, this directly establishes $g(q) = O(q)$, confirming Conjecture (linearity of $g(q)$) from arXiv:2012.05112 and removing the $\log q$ factor that Alon and Krivelevich attributed to the probabilistic method. The paper was subsequently published in the Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series B (2024).
rows.class_notes: g(q) of the paper's Theorem 2 is never defined in the record; linear-in-q claim needs recovery from paper.

rows.title: Informal belief on Conjecture 1.7 and polylog bound
rows.paper_title: Pure pairs. VII. Homogeneous submatrices in 0/1-matrices with a forbidden submatrix
rows.statement_text: Our guess is that Conjecture 1.7 is false. Perhaps $n/\operatorname{polylog}(n)$ might be true?
rows.context_text: After stating Conjecture 1.7, the authors remark that while it is a natural extension of the unordered theory, they do not believe it holds, and informally suggest that a bound of $n/\operatorname{polylog}(n)$ for the sizes of the pure pair might be the correct weaker conclusion.
rows.source_text: Our guess is that Conjecture 1.7 is false. Perhaps $n/\operatorname{polylog}(n)$ might be true?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2101.03537 — Informal belief on Conjecture 1.7 and polylog bound
rows.class_notes: References unstated Conjecture 1.7; the suggested n/polylog(n) pure-pair bound cannot be stated from this record alone.

rows.title: Problem 4.4
rows.paper_title: On the dichromatic number of surfaces
rows.statement_text: What is the complexity of $\Sigma$-$k$-\textsc{Dicolourability} for $k \in \{4, 5\}$ and $\Sigma$ different from the sphere?
rows.context_text: The paper establishes that Σ-2-DICOLOURABILITY is NP-complete for any surface, and that Σ-k-DICOLOURABILITY is polynomial-time solvable for any surface Σ and any integer k ≥ 6. The complexity for k ∈ {4, 5} on surfaces other than the sphere is explicitly left open.
rows.source_text: What is the complexity of $\Sigma$-$k$-\textsc{Dicolourability} for $k \in \{4, 5\}$ and $\Sigma$ different from the sphere?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the complexity of Sigma-k-Dicolourability for k in {4,5}?' — open-ended complexity determination.

rows.title: Erdős-Hajnal property for $\{C_8, \overline{C_8}\}$
rows.paper_title: Erdos-Hajnal for graphs with no 5-hole
rows.statement_text: Does $\{C_8, \overline{C_8}\}$ have the Erdős-Hajnal property?
rows.context_text: The paper proves that $\{C_6, \overline{C_6}\}$ (result 1.7) and $\{C_7, \overline{C_7}\}$ (result 1.8) each have the Erdős-Hajnal property. The authors remark that it would be nice to know whether the same holds for $\{C_8, \overline{C_8}\}$, but that this remains open.
rows.source_text: Does $\{C_8, \overline{C_8}\}$ have the Erdős-Hajnal property?
rows.class_notes: Special-case instance of the Erdos-Hajnal conjecture for the pair {C8, complement of C8}; clean yes/no modulo the n^epsilon quantifier.

rows.title: Open Complexity Problem (2-F-PFC for oriented trees of maximum degree ≥ 3)
rows.paper_title: Colorings of oriented planar graphs avoiding a monochromatic subgraph
rows.statement_text: The complexity of the $2$-$F$-PFC where the underlying undirected tree of $F$ has maximum degree at least $3$ remains partially open. In particular, the complexity for those graphs $F$ where successively deleting all leaves yields a star $K_{1,n}$ is not determined.
rows.context_text: The paper proves NP-hardness of 2-$\vec{T}$-PFC when the oriented tree $\vec{T}$ reduces to an oriented path of length 2 or 3 after repeatedly removing all leaves. The analogous question for oriented trees whose leaf-pruned form is a star $K_{1,n}$ is explicitly left open in the introduction.
rows.source_text: The complexity of the $2$-$F$-PFC where the underlying undirected tree of $F$ has maximum degree at least $3$ remains partially open. In particular, the complexity for those graphs $F$ where successively deleting all leaves yields a star $K_{1,n}$ is not determined.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2102.07705 — Open Complexity Problem (2-F-PFC for oriented trees of maximum degree ≥ 3)
rows.defer_reason: 'Complexity remains partially open / is not determined' — a description of a complexity gap, relying on paper-local 2-F-PFC notation.

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: Improved bounds for weak coloring numbers
rows.statement_text: Planar graphs have $r$-th weak coloring numbers in $O(r^2 \log r)$.
rows.context_text: The best known lower bound for the maximum $r$-th weak coloring number of planar graphs is $\Omega(r^2 \log r)$ (proved in this paper via Corollary 3) and the best upper bound is $O(r^3)$ due to Van den Heuvel, Ossona de Mendez, Quiroz, Rabinovich, and Siebertz. The authors find it particularly intriguing to close this gap and believe the lower bound gives the correct order of magnitude.
rows.source_text: Planar graphs have $r$-th weak coloring numbers in $O(r^2 \log r)$.
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that all planar graphs have r-th weak coloring numbers in O(r^2 \log r) remains open as of 2025. Hodor, La, Micek, and Rambaud (arXiv:2407.04588, SODA 2025) proved this bound for planar graphs of bounded treewidth and more generally for X-minor-free graphs via the 2-treedepth of X, but explicitly state that the exact growth rate for all planar graphs is unknown. The lower bound \Omega(r^2 \log r) from Joret--Micek and the upper bound O(r^3) for general planar graphs still leave a gap.
rows.class_notes: Weak coloring number is a standard sparsity primitive; also needs a bounded O(r^2 log r) wrapper on top of the planarity layer.

rows.title: Conjecture on sharpness of Theorem 1.1
rows.paper_title: Making an $H$-Free Graph $k$-Colorable
rows.statement_text: For each integer $r \geq 3$ there is $c_r$ such that for all positive integers $n, k$ with $n$ sufficiently large in terms of $k$, $$h(n, k, K_r) = \Theta_r\!\left(\frac{n^2}{k^{(r-1)/(r-2)}}\right),$$ i.e., the upper bound $h(n, k, K_r) \leq c_r \dfrac{n^2}{k^{(r-1)/(r-2)}}$ is tight up to the constant factor $c_r$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 establishes the upper bound $h(n,k,K_r) \leq c_r n^2/k^{(r-1)/(r-2)}$ for all $r \geq 3$. The authors prove the matching lower bound for $r=3$ and conjecture the bound is sharp for all $r \geq 3$.
rows.source_text: For each integer $r \geq 3$ there is $c_r$ such that for all positive integers $n, k$ with $n$ sufficiently large in terms of $k$, $$h(n, k, K_r) = \Theta_r\!\left(\frac{n^2}{k^{(r-1)/(r-2)}}\right),$$ i.e., the upper bound $h(n, k, K_r) \leq c_r \dfrac{n^2}{k^{(r-1)/(r-2)}}$ is tight up to the constant factor $c_r$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2102.10220 — Conjecture on sharpness of Theorem 1.1
rows.class_notes: The extremal function h(n,k,H) (edits to make an H-free graph k-colorable) is never defined in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture on sharpness of Theorem 1.5
rows.paper_title: Making an $H$-Free Graph $k$-Colorable
rows.statement_text: For each positive integer $r$, the bound $$h(n, k, W_{2r+1}) \leq c_r \frac{n^2}{k^{2 - 1/(r+1)}}$$ is tight up to the constant factor $c_r$ depending on $r$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.5 gives the upper bound $h(n,k,W_{2r+1}) \leq c_r n^2/k^{2-1/(r+1)}$ for odd wheels when $n \geq k \geq 2$. The authors conjecture this is tight, by analogy with their matching lower bounds in the odd-cycle case.
rows.source_text: For each positive integer $r$, the bound $$h(n, k, W_{2r+1}) \leq c_r \frac{n^2}{k^{2 - 1/(r+1)}}$$ is tight up to the constant factor $c_r$ depending on $r$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2102.10220 — Conjecture on sharpness of Theorem 1.5
rows.class_notes: Same undefined h(n,k,H) function as the sibling row; tightness claim for odd wheels needs paper recovery.

rows.title: Question 2.1
rows.paper_title: Making an $H$-Free Graph $k$-Colorable
rows.statement_text: Given a graph $G$, how many edges of $G$ can we cover by the union of $k$ neighborhoods of vertices of $G$?
rows.context_text: The authors define $u(G,k)$ as the maximum number of edges covered by the union of $k$ vertex neighborhoods and study $m(n,k) = \min_G(e(G) - u(G,k))$. Bounds on this quantity are the main tool for proving upper bounds on $h(n,k,H)$ when $H$ is a clique or an odd wheel.
rows.source_text: Given a graph $G$, how many edges of $G$ can we cover by the union of $k$ neighborhoods of vertices of $G$?
rows.defer_reason: 'How many edges can we cover by k neighborhoods?' — open-ended quantity question, not a proposition.

rows.title: Open problem on $m(n,k)$ for fixed $k \geq 2$
rows.paper_title: Making an $H$-Free Graph $k$-Colorable
rows.statement_text: What is the exact or asymptotic value of $m(n,k)$ for any fixed $k \geq 2$?
rows.context_text: The authors prove $m(n,k) \leq n^2/(ek)$ (Corollary 2.6) and derive a lower bound from an Erdős–Rényi random graph heuristic that is within 20% of this upper bound for large $k$. They state explicitly: 'We have no idea what the exact or asymptotic value of $m(n,k)$ is for any fixed $k \geq 2$.'
rows.source_text: What is the exact or asymptotic value of $m(n,k)$ for any fixed $k \geq 2$?

rows.defer_reason: 'What is the exact or asymptotic value of m(n,k)?' — value-determination request.

rows.title: Conjecture 1
rows.paper_title: Random 2-cell embeddings of multistars
rows.statement_text: For every $n$-vertex simple graph $G$, the expected number of faces when selecting an orientable embedding of $G$ uniformly at random is $O(n)$.
rows.context_text: The authors exhibit a general construction of graph families achieving a linear number of expected faces but were unable to find any family with a superlinear number of expected faces. Results for $d$-regular and $d$-degenerate graphs (Theorem 11 and Corollary 12) are presented as supporting evidence.
rows.source_text: For every $n$-vertex simple graph $G$, the expected number of faces when selecting an orientable embedding of $G$ uniformly at random is $O(n)$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1 from arXiv:2103.05036 is fully resolved by Campion Loth and Mohar (2023), who proved that for any $n$-vertex multigraph with maximum edge-multiplicity $\mu$, the expected number of faces satisfies $E[F] \leq n(H_{2\mu}+1)$; for simple graphs this specialises to the conjectured $O(n)$ bound, further sharpened to $E[F] \leq \frac{\pi^2}{6}n$ by the same paper. Subsequent work (arXiv:2211.01032, SODA 2024) shows that for complete graphs and most natural graph families the expectation is in fact $\Theta(\ln n)$, far below the linear upper bound.
rows.class_notes: Needs the 2-cell embedding/rotation-system layer plus expectation over uniform random embeddings and an O(n) wrapper; solved (right-polarity target).

rows.title: Informal Conjecture on Chromatic Number of $G_{m,t}$
rows.paper_title: Hitting all maximum independent sets
rows.statement_text: The chromatic number of the graph $G_{m,t}$ described in the proof of Theorem 1.4, where $4t^2 \leq m$, is $\Theta(t^2)$.
rows.context_text: Alon states this as a conjecture he raised more than ten years ago, motivated by results in [2] (Alon, Hassidim, Lubetzky, Stav and Weinstein, FOCS 2008) and mentioned in several lectures. The paper establishes that the chromatic number is at least $2t$ and at most $O(t^2)$, leaving the exact order unresolved.
rows.source_text: The chromatic number of the graph $G_{m,t}$ described in the proof of Theorem 1.4, where $4t^2 \leq m$, is $\Theta(t^2)$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2103.05998 — Informal Conjecture on Chromatic Number of $G_{m,t}$
rows.class_notes: G_{m,t} is a construction 'described in the proof of Theorem 1.4' and not defined in the record.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (fractional treewidth-fragility of sublinear-separator classes)
rows.paper_title: Approximation metatheorems for classes with bounded expansion
rows.statement_text: All classes with sublinear separators are fractionally treewidth-fragile.
rows.context_text: The author notes that efficiently fractionally treewidth-fragile classes include all hereditary classes with sublinear separators and bounded maximum degree, all proper minor-closed classes, and many geometric graph classes. The author then remarks that it is possible, and has been conjectured by them, that the property extends to all classes with sublinear separators.
rows.source_text: All classes with sublinear separators are fractionally treewidth-fragile.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2103.08698 — Informal Conjecture (fractional treewidth-fragility of sublinear-separator classes)
rows.class_notes: Needs fractional treewidth-fragility as a new primitive plus class-level sublinear-separator asymptotics.

rows.title: Problem 6
rows.paper_title: Approximation metatheorems for classes with bounded expansion
rows.statement_text: Do monotone minimization problems expressible in the first order logic admit constant factor approximation in all classes with bounded expansion? And PTASes in all efficiently fractionally treewidth-fragile graph classes?
rows.context_text: The paper's techniques apply to minimization problems only with error bounded by a fraction of the total vertex weight rather than the optimal, so constant-factor approximation in bounded-expansion classes and PTASes in fractionally treewidth-fragile classes for minimization remain open. As a concrete simplest case, it is unknown whether a PTAS exists for weighted vertex cover in fractionally treewidth-fragile graph classes.
rows.source_text: Do monotone minimization problems expressible in the first order logic admit constant factor approximation in all classes with bounded expansion? And PTASes in all efficiently fractionally treewidth-fragile graph classes?
rows.defer_reason: Compound meta-question (two questions: constant-factor approx in bounded expansion, and PTASes in treewidth-fragile classes) over FO-expressible problem families.
rows.class_notes: Its concrete test case (weighted VC PTAS) reappears in axv_2105_01780_00.

rows.title: Problem 7
rows.paper_title: Approximation metatheorems for classes with bounded expansion
rows.statement_text: Do monotone maximization problems expressible in the first order logic admit an $O(n^{o(1)})$-factor approximation for $n$-vertex graphs from nowhere-dense classes?
rows.context_text: Many results for classes with bounded expansion extend to nowhere-dense graph classes with constants replaced by $n^{o(1)}$ terms. The paper's approach does not apply to nowhere-dense classes because it relies on a quantifier elimination result specific to bounded-expansion classes, leaving the approximability question in nowhere-dense classes open.
rows.source_text: Do monotone maximization problems expressible in the first order logic admit an $O(n^{o(1)})$-factor approximation for $n$-vertex graphs from nowhere-dense classes?
rows.class_notes: Single yes/no question, but requires FO-logic-on-graphs, nowhere-dense classes, and approximation-algorithm foundations.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.1
rows.paper_title: Sunflowers in set systems of bounded dimension
rows.statement_text: For $d \geq 1$ and $r \geq 3$, there is a constant $C = C(d, r)$ such that $f^d_r(k) \leq C^k$.
rows.context_text: The authors note that the Erdős-Rado sunflower conjecture implies this weaker statement about families of bounded VC-dimension $d$, where $f^d_r(k)$ is the minimum family size guaranteeing an $r$-sunflower among $k$-sets with VC-dimension at most $d$. The paper proves the conjecture for $d = 1$ (Theorem 1.2) and establishes the slightly weaker bound $f^d_r(k) \leq 2^{10k(dr)^2 \log^* k}$ for $d \geq 2$ (Theorem 1.3), leaving the full conjecture open for $d \geq 2$.
rows.source_text: For $d \geq 1$ and $r \geq 3$, there is a constant $C = C(d, r)$ such that $f^d_r(k) \leq C^k$.
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that $f^d_r(k) \leq C^k$ for all $d \geq 1$ and $r \geq 3$ is proven for $d=1$ by the original paper (Theorem 1.2). For $d \geq 2$, Balogh, Bernshteyn, Delcourt, Ferber, and Pham (arXiv:2408.04165, 2024) substantially improved the bound from $2^{10k(dr)^2 \log^* k}$ to $(Cr(\log d + \log^* k))^k$, but the pure exponential $C^k$ with $C = C(d,r)$ independent of $k$ remains open for $d \geq 2$. The $d=1$ case is also resolved sharply in 2408.04165 with the tight bound $(r-1)^k$.
rows.defer_reason: Sunflower conjecture for set systems of bounded VC-dimension — extremal set systems, excluded by plan scope.
rows.class_notes: Borderline like axv_2010_05992_00 and ruled the same way for consistency: f^d_r(k) is fully defined in context and finite, but sunflowers in bounded-VC-dimension set systems are extremal set theory, not hypergraph theory in the plan's sense.

rows.title: Question 1.6
rows.paper_title: On a recolouring version of Hadwiger's conjecture
rows.statement_text: What is the infimum $c$ such that for any large enough $t$, there is a graph that admits a quasi-$K_{t}$-minor but no $K_{ct}$-minor?
rows.context_text: Follows from the strong disproof of Conjecture 1.4. The paper establishes the bounds $\frac{1}{2}\leqslant c\leqslant\frac{2}{3}$; the trivial upper bound $c\leqslant 1$ comes from the fact that every quasi-$K_{2t}$-minor-free graph admits a $K_t$-minor.
rows.source_text: What is the infimum $c$ such that for any large enough $t$, there is a graph that admits a quasi-$K_{t}$-minor but no $K_{ct}$-minor?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the infimum c such that...' — value-determination; also depends on the paper-local quasi-K_t-minor notion.

rows.title: Question 1.7
rows.paper_title: On a recolouring version of Hadwiger's conjecture
rows.statement_text: Is there a constant $c^{\prime}$ such that for every $t$, all the $c^{\prime}\cdot t$-colourings of a graph with no $K_{t}$-minor form a single equivalence class?
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.3 shows $c'\geqslant\frac{3}{2}$ if such a constant exists. The best known upper bound comes from results of Kostochka and Thomason showing degeneracy $O(t\sqrt{\log t})$ for $K_t$-minor-free graphs, combined with the fact that all $k$-colourings of $d$-degenerate graphs are Kempe equivalent for $k>d$.
rows.source_text: Is there a constant $c^{\prime}$ such that for every $t$, all the $c^{\prime}\cdot t$-colourings of a graph with no $K_{t}$-minor form a single equivalence class?
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no once Kempe equivalence of colorings is built; context confirms the equivalence relation is Kempe equivalence.

rows.title: Conjecture on $R_\ell(\mathcal{H}_s, k)$
rows.paper_title: Multicolor list Ramsey numbers grow exponentially
rows.statement_text: For $s \geq 2$, if $\mathcal{H}_s$ is the family of graphs with chromatic number greater than $s$, then $R_\ell(\mathcal{H}_s, k) = s^k + 1$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 8 of the paper establishes $\frac{1}{e} \cdot s^k \leq R_\ell(\mathcal{H}_s, k) \leq s^k + 1$, where the upper bound follows from $R_\ell(\mathcal{H}_s, k) \leq R(\mathcal{H}_s, k) = s^k + 1$. The authors conjecture that the upper bound is tight.
rows.source_text: For $s \geq 2$, if $\mathcal{H}_s$ is the family of graphs with chromatic number greater than $s$, then $R_\ell(\mathcal{H}_s, k) = s^k + 1$.
rows.class_notes: Needs the list Ramsey number R_ell as a (buildable) primitive; H_s is defined in the statement itself.

rows.title: Determine the exponential constant in $R_\ell(H,k)$
rows.paper_title: Multicolor list Ramsey numbers grow exponentially
rows.statement_text: It would be interesting to determine $\lim_{k\to\infty} R_\ell(H,k)^{1/k}$, even in just the case $H = K_3$.
rows.context_text: Theorems 1 and 2 together give $\frac{1}{e}\cdot 2^k \leq R_\ell(K_3,k) \leq (4+o(1))^k$, but the exact exponential constant is unknown. The authors note that their results do not even establish that the limit $\lim_{k\to\infty} R_\ell(H,k)^{1/k}$ exists.
rows.source_text: It would be interesting to determine $\lim_{k\to\infty} R_\ell(H,k)^{1/k}$, even in just the case $H = K_3$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2103.15175 — Determine the exponential constant in $R_\ell(H,k)$
rows.defer_reason: 'It would be interesting to determine the limit' — value-determination (existence of the limit itself is unknown).

rows.title: Question (polynomial weak coloring numbers for intersection graph classes)
rows.paper_title: Weak Coloring Numbers of Intersection Graphs
rows.statement_text: Are the $k$-th weak coloring numbers polynomial in $k$ for the graph classes described in Lemma 1: intersection graphs of $t$-thin sets of (a) scaled and translated copies of the same centrally symmetric compact convex object, or comparable axis-aligned boxes; or (b) $b$-ball-like objects for some $b\geq 1$?
rows.context_text: Joret and Wood conjectured that every graph class with polynomial strong coloring numbers also has polynomial weak coloring numbers; Grohe et al. disproved this via edge-subdivision constructions. The authors argue the conjecture might still hold for natural geometric classes such as those covered by Lemma 1 (thin intersection graphs of convex objects in $\mathbb{R}^d$), and explicitly ask whether this is the case.
rows.source_text: Are the $k$-th weak coloring numbers polynomial in $k$ for the graph classes described in Lemma 1: intersection graphs of $t$-thin sets of (a) scaled and translated copies of the same centrally symmetric compact convex object, or comparable axis-aligned boxes; or (b) $b$-ball-like objects for some $b\geq 1$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2103.17094 — Question (polynomial weak coloring numbers for intersection graph classes)
rows.class_notes: Depends on the paper's Lemma 1 classes (t-thin sets, b-ball-like objects in R^d); geometric definitions not fully in the record.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture on similar growth of $N_{DFL}(\varepsilon)$ and $1/\delta_{TRL}(\varepsilon)$
rows.paper_title: Removal lemmas and approximate homomorphisms
rows.statement_text: We suspect that $N_{DFL}(\varepsilon)$ and $1/\delta_{TRL}(\varepsilon)$ have similar growth.
rows.context_text: The authors note that the best known lower bound on $N_{DFL}(\varepsilon)$ has the form $(1/\varepsilon)^{c\log(1/\varepsilon)}$ arising from the Behrend construction, while $1/\delta_{TRL}(\varepsilon)$ satisfies $1/\delta_{TRL}(\varepsilon) \leq \text{tower}(O(\log(1/\varepsilon)))$ (Fox [8]). Theorem 1.12 provides supporting evidence: if $N_{DFL}(\varepsilon)$ grows subexponentially in $\varepsilon^{-1}$, then so does $1/\delta_{TRL}(\varepsilon)$. If the suspected similarity holds, Theorem 1.10 and inequality (1.1) would together give comparable lower and upper bounds on $M_{TFL}(\varepsilon)$.
rows.source_text: We suspect that $N_{DFL}(\varepsilon)$ and $1/\delta_{TRL}(\varepsilon)$ have similar growth.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2104.11626 — Informal Conjecture on similar growth of $N_{DFL}(\varepsilon)$ and $1/\delta_{TRL}(\varepsilon)$
rows.defer_reason: 'We suspect X and Y have similar growth' — no precise comparison stated, and N_DFL/delta_TRL are paper-local quantities.

rows.title: Open Problem: PTAS for weighted minimization problems in fractionally treewidth-fragile classes
rows.paper_title: Approximation schemes for bounded distance problems on fractionally treewidth-fragile graphs
rows.statement_text: For the minimization problems, we do not know whether fractional treewidth-fragility is sufficient even for the distance-1 problems. In particular, consider the Minimum Vertex Cover problem in fractionally treewidth-fragile graphs, or more generally in hereditary classes with sublinear separators: while the unweighted version can be dealt with by the local search method, we do not know whether there exists a PTAS for the weighted version of this problem.
rows.context_text: The paper's main results (Theorem 2 and its corollaries) apply exclusively to maximization problems; the authors extend fractional treewidth-fragility techniques to handle bounded-distance dependencies for near-monotone maximization. For minimization problems the analogous question is left open, with weighted Minimum Vertex Cover in fractionally treewidth-fragile (or sublinear-separator) classes offered as a concrete test case.
rows.source_text: For the minimization problems, we do not know whether fractional treewidth-fragility is sufficient even for the distance-1 problems. In particular, consider the Minimum Vertex Cover problem in fractionally treewidth-fragile graphs, or more generally in hereditary classes with sublinear separators: while the unweighted version can be dealt with by the local search method, we do not know whether there exists a PTAS for the weighted version of this problem.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2105.01780 — Open Problem: PTAS for weighted minimization problems in fractionally treewidth-fragile classes
rows.class_notes: Concrete yes/no core: does weighted Minimum Vertex Cover admit a PTAS on fractionally treewidth-fragile classes; same test case is cited in context of axv_2103_08698_01.

rows.title: Open Problem: 5-Coloring complexity for H-free graphs with a P4 component
rows.paper_title: Complexity dichotomy for List-5-Coloring with a forbidden induced subgraph
rows.statement_text: The complexity of the $5$-Coloring Problem restricted to $H$-free graphs remains open when $H$ has exactly one connected component $C$ isomorphic to $P_4$, and $H - V(C)$ is an induced subgraph of $rP_3$ containing at least one edge for some $r \geq 1$.
rows.context_text: After proving that the $k$-Coloring Problem on $2P_4$-free graphs is NP-complete for all $k \geq 5$ (Theorem 7), the authors observe that combined with Theorem 4 this leaves the 5-Coloring complexity unresolved only for the described family of forbidden subgraphs $H$. This is the last remaining gap toward a full dichotomy for the $5$-Coloring Problem on $H$-free graphs.
rows.source_text: The complexity of the $5$-Coloring Problem restricted to $H$-free graphs remains open when $H$ has exactly one connected component $C$ isomorphic to $P_4$, and $H - V(C)$ is an induced subgraph of $rP_3$ containing at least one edge for some $r \geq 1$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2105.01787 — Open Problem: 5-Coloring complexity for H-free graphs with a P4 component
rows.status_semantics: The open problem about the complexity of the List-5-Coloring Problem restricted to H-free graphs, when H has a P4 component and H minus V(C) is an induced subgraph of rP3 containing at least one edge, was fully resolved by Chudnovsky, Hajebi, and Spirkl (arXiv:2311.05713, Combinatorica 2024). Their main theorem establishes a complete complexity dichotomy for List-k-Coloring on H-free graphs for all k > 4: the problem is polynomial-time solvable if and only if every component of H is a path on at most three vertices, or removing the isolated vertices of H leaves an induced subgraph of the five-vertex path P5. For every H in the conjecture's family, the non-isolated part of H contains a P4 plus at least one further edge (contributing at least 2 more vertices), giving at least 6 vertices total, which cannot embed in P5; since neither polynomial condition holds, List-5-Coloring is NP-complete for all such H-free graphs.
rows.defer_reason: Describes the remaining open family of cases in a complexity dichotomy ('remains open when...'), not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Status solved — the dichotomy gap was later closed.

rows.title: Open Question: NP-hardness of List-k-Coloring on rP3-free graphs
rows.paper_title: Complexity dichotomy for List-5-Coloring with a forbidden induced subgraph
rows.statement_text: Does there exist $k \in \mathbb{N}$ for which the List-$k$-Coloring Problem restricted to $rP_3$-free graphs is NP-hard?
rows.context_text: The authors note that while Theorems 13 and 17 (the main results of Sections 2 and 3) are proved for the List-$k$-Coloring Problem on $rP_3$-free graphs for arbitrary $k$, the tools of Section 4 fail to extend beyond $k = 5$. The authors were unable to settle whether the general List-$k$-Coloring Problem on $rP_3$-free graphs can be NP-hard for some $k$.
rows.source_text: Does there exist $k \in \mathbb{N}$ for which the List-$k$-Coloring Problem restricted to $rP_3$-free graphs is NP-hard?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2105.01787 — Open Question: NP-hardness of List-k-Coloring on rP3-free graphs
rows.status_semantics: The open question asks whether List-$k$-Coloring on $rP_3$-free graphs is NP-hard for some $k\in\mathbb{N}$. arXiv:2311.05713 (Chudnovsky, Hajebi, Spirkl; Combinatorica 2024) proves a full complexity dichotomy for all $k>4$: List-$k$-Coloring on $H$-free graphs is polynomial-time solvable whenever every component of $H$ is a path on at most three vertices, and $rP_3$ satisfies this condition. Combined with the source paper's polynomial result for $k=5$ and classical results for $k\leq 4$, List-$k$-Coloring on $rP_3$-free graphs is polynomial-time solvable for all $k\in\mathbb{N}$, answering the open question in the negative.
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no NP-hardness question (List-k-Coloring on rP3-free graphs); status solved, right-polarity target.

rows.title: Open problem on parameters governing $\overrightarrow{r}_1(H)$
rows.paper_title: Ramsey numbers of sparse digraphs
rows.statement_text: What natural parameters (analogous to degeneracy and vertex count for undirected graphs) determine the growth order of $\overrightarrow{r}_1(H)$ for acyclic digraphs? In particular, is $\overrightarrow{r}_1(H)$ polynomial or super-polynomial in $n$ when $H$ has bounded degree?
rows.context_text: For undirected graphs, the Ramsey number is governed by degeneracy and vertex count (Conlon–Fox–Sudakov conjecture, verified up to a $\log^2 d$ factor). The authors introduce the notion of 'multiscale complexity' as a candidate parameter for the directed setting, but stress that the full picture remains unresolved and deserves further research.
rows.source_text: What natural parameters (analogous to degeneracy and vertex count for undirected graphs) determine the growth order of $\overrightarrow{r}_1(H)$ for acyclic digraphs? In particular, is $\overrightarrow{r}_1(H)$ polynomial or super-polynomial in $n$ when $H$ has bounded degree?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2105.02383 — Open problem on parameters governing $\overrightarrow{r}_1(H)$
rows.status_semantics: The source paper itself already demonstrates that bounded degree alone does not suffice for polynomial (let alone linear) oriented Ramsey numbers, constructing bounded-degree acyclic digraphs with $\overrightarrow{r}_1(H) \ge n^{\Omega(\Delta^{2/3}/\log^{5/3}\Delta)}$, while the complete characterization via natural parameters remains open. The 2025 follow-up by Bradač, Morawski, Sudakov, and Wigderson extends the linear upper bound regime to acyclic digraphs with 'graded bandwidth' (local edge structure), unifying previous results on bounded height, bounded bandwidth, and tree blowups. However, the full picture is explicitly acknowledged as far from resolved: a complete analogue of the degeneracy-plus-vertex-count framework for undirected graphs has not been found for the directed setting.
rows.defer_reason: Asks which parameters govern directed Ramsey growth; not a single proposition
rows.class_notes: The 'polynomial vs super-polynomial' sub-question is still a what-question over an undefined parameter regime

rows.title: Union cover variant (open problem)
rows.paper_title: Strengthening Rodl's theorem
rows.statement_text: For every graph $H$, and all $\varepsilon > 0$, there is an integer $N$ such that for every $H$-free graph $G$, $V(G)$ is the union of at most $N$ $\varepsilon$-restricted subsets (not necessarily pairwise disjoint).
rows.context_text: The authors note this as a statement midway between Theorem 1.3 (partition into weakly $\varepsilon$-restricted sets) and their main Theorem 1.4 (partition into $\varepsilon$-restricted sets). They remark that 'this variation does not seem to be easy, although it does not imply 1.4 as far as we know.'
rows.source_text: For every graph $H$, and all $\varepsilon > 0$, there is an integer $N$ such that for every $H$-free graph $G$, $V(G)$ is the union of at most $N$ $\varepsilon$-restricted subsets (not necessarily pairwise disjoint).
rows.class_notes: Paper-local notion 'epsilon-restricted subset' (Rodl-type density restriction) is not defined in the record

rows.title: Conjecture on tightness of the upper bound for all r ≥ 3
rows.paper_title: The upper logarithmic density of monochromatic subset sums
rows.statement_text: We suspect that our upper bound $c_r \leq \left(1-\frac{1}{2b_0}\right)\left(1+\frac{1}{2^r b_0 - r}\right)$ is also tight for all $r \geq 3$, where $b_0 > 1$ is the unique root of $b^r - 2rb + r - 1 = 0$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1 gives a general upper bound for $c_r$ for all $r\geq 2$ and proves it is achieved (i.e., tight) for $r=2$, yielding $c_2=(2+\sqrt{3})/4$. The authors believe the same formula gives the exact value for all larger $r$, but remark that their methods are insufficient to prove this, directing the reader to brief concluding remarks for further discussion.
rows.source_text: We suspect that our upper bound $c_r \leq \left(1-\frac{1}{2b_0}\right)\left(1+\frac{1}{2^r b_0 - r}\right)$ is also tight for all $r \geq 3$, where $b_0 > 1$ is the unique root of $b^r - 2rb + r - 1 = 0$.

rows.title: Question 1.1
rows.paper_title: Which graphs can be counted in $C_4$-free graphs?
rows.statement_text: For which graphs $F$ is there an $F$-counting lemma in $C_4$-free graphs?
rows.context_text: This is the central open problem of the paper. The authors formally define what it means for a graph $F$ to be 'countable' (Definition 1.3) and construct infinite families of countable graphs, but a complete characterization remains open. They note that a necessary condition is that $F$ has girth at least 5 (Remark 1.6).
rows.source_text: For which graphs $F$ is there an $F$-counting lemma in $C_4$-free graphs?
rows.defer_reason: 'For which graphs F' characterization question, and 'countable' is a paper-local Definition 1.3

rows.title: Open Problem 2.17
rows.paper_title: Which graphs can be counted in $C_4$-free graphs?
rows.statement_text: Are the dodecahedral and Petersen graphs countable?
rows.context_text: After constructing many countable graphs of girth at least 5 via a recursive 'islands and bridges' method, the authors observe that their techniques appear insufficient to handle 3-regular graphs of girth at least 5. The dodecahedral graph (girth 5, 3-regular) and the Petersen graph (girth 5, 3-regular) are highlighted as the simplest open cases.
rows.source_text: Are the dodecahedral and Petersen graphs countable?
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no once 'countable' (F-counting lemma in C4-free graphs, paper Definition 1.3) is recovered

rows.title: Question 1
rows.paper_title: Geometric and o-minimal Littlewood-Offord problems
rows.statement_text: Assuming only that each $a_i \neq 0$, what geometric constraints can be imposed on a set $S \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ which imply non-trivial uniform upper bounds on $\Pr(X \in S)$?
rows.context_text: The paper introduces a new generalisation of the Littlewood–Offord problem to $\mathbb{R}^d$ in which no assumption is made on the sizes of the nonzero vectors $a_1,\ldots,a_n$, only that they are nonzero. The authors observe that this makes the question much more about the geometry of $S$ than about spatial anti-concentration of $X = a_1\xi_1 + \cdots + a_n\xi_n$, giving it a very different flavour from previous Littlewood–Offord variants.
rows.source_text: Assuming only that each $a_i \neq 0$, what geometric constraints can be imposed on a set $S \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ which imply non-trivial uniform upper bounds on $\Pr(X \in S)$?
rows.status_semantics: Question 1 from arXiv:2106.04894 asks, for arbitrary nonzero vectors $a_i$, which geometric constraints on $S \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ yield non-trivial uniform upper bounds on $\Pr(X \in S)$. The source paper itself answers this for o-minimal sets (those definable in an o-minimal structure) not containing a line segment, giving a bound of $n^{-1/2+o(1)}$. A 2025 paper by Grebennikov and Kwan (arXiv:2505.24699) extends this programme substantially, proving that sets in convex position and algebraic varieties of prescribed dimension and degree also yield such bounds, thereby resolving Conjecture 10.1 of Fox-Kwan-Spink as well as a conjecture of Kwan-Sauermann; the general question of characterising all admissible geometric constraints remains an open research direction.
rows.defer_reason: Littlewood-Offord anti-concentration in R^d: probability/geometry, not graph theory; also a vague what-question

rows.title: Conjecture 1.2
rows.paper_title: Geometric and o-minimal Littlewood-Offord problems
rows.statement_text: In Theorem 1.1 we have $\Pr(X \in S) \leq C_S/\sqrt{n}$, for some $C_S$ depending only on $S$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 establishes $\Pr(X \in S) \leq n^{-1/2+\alpha}$ for any $\alpha > 0$ when $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ is definable with respect to an o-minimal structure and contains no line segment; the conjecture posits that the optimal $O(1/\sqrt{n})$ bound from the classical Erd\H{o}s–Littlewood–Offord theorem also holds in this geometric setting. The authors prove it when $S$ satisfies the generic intersection property, which covers convex or irreducible algebraic plane curves in $\mathbb{R}^2$, irreducible complex algebraic curves, and the boundary of a 3-dimensional ball. They also remark that a full resolution of the Gotsman–Linial conjecture would imply Conjecture 1.2 for all semi-algebraic sets not containing a line segment.
rows.source_text: In Theorem 1.1 we have $\Pr(X \in S) \leq C_S/\sqrt{n}$, for some $C_S$ depending only on $S$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.2 — the optimal $O(1/\sqrt{n})$ anti-concentration bound for random signed sums landing in an o-minimal set containing no line segment — has been established for the semialgebraic case by Grebennikov and Kwan (2025, arXiv:2505.24699), who prove $\Pr[\xi_1 a_1+\cdots+\xi_n a_n \in S] \le O_S(n^{-1/2})$ via a lattice-point-counting reduction; the paper explicitly states it resolves conjectures of Fox–Kwan–Spink and Kwan–Sauermann. Whether this covers the full conjecture for all o-minimal (not just semialgebraic) structures is not confirmed from the available abstract and HTML.
rows.defer_reason: o-minimal Littlewood-Offord bound: real geometry/probability, not graph theory

rows.title: Direct Proof of Horizontal Uniformity
rows.paper_title: The runsort permuton
rows.statement_text: The pointwise density of $R$ in $C^+ = \{(x,y)\in[0,1]^2 : x < ye^{1-y}\}$ depends only on the vertical coordinate $y$ (``horizontal uniformity''). The authors state they do not know how to establish this directly, that is, without recourse to the explicit characterization of $R$.
rows.context_text: After defining the runsort permuton $R$ and noting that its absolutely continuous part has support $C^+$, the authors observe that the density in $C^+$ is independent of $x$. They remark that this horizontal uniformity is not combinatorially obvious and that a direct combinatorial proof is unknown.
rows.source_text: The pointwise density of $R$ in $C^+ = \{(x,y)\in[0,1]^2 : x < ye^{1-y}\}$ depends only on the vertical coordinate $y$ (``horizontal uniformity''). The authors state they do not know how to establish this directly, that is, without recourse to the explicit characterization of $R$.

rows.title: Open problem: no-kernel lower bound at twin-width 4 for Connected/Total k-Dominating Set
rows.paper_title: Twin-width and polynomial kernels
rows.statement_text: Can one show that Connected $k$-Dominating Set and Total $k$-Dominating Set on graphs of twin-width at most $4$ do not admit a polynomial kernel (unless $\mathrm{coNP} \subseteq \mathrm{NP/poly}$), even when a $4$-sequence of the graph is given?
rows.context_text: The authors establish via local gadget modifications of their main reduction (Theorem 1) that Connected $k$-Dominating Set and Total $k$-Dominating Set have no polynomial kernel on graphs of bounded twin-width, but with a worse upper bound on the twin-width than 4. Bringing the twin-width bound down to 4—as achieved for plain $k$-Dominating Set in Theorem 1—would require additional work.
rows.source_text: Can one show that Connected $k$-Dominating Set and Total $k$-Dominating Set on graphs of twin-width at most $4$ do not admit a polynomial kernel (unless $\mathrm{coNP} \subseteq \mathrm{NP/poly}$), even when a $4$-sequence of the graph is given?

rows.title: Informal Belief on Degeneracy Bound for Hat Guessing Number

rows.statement_text: It seems plausible that there exists a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for every $d$-degenerate graph $G$, $\mathrm{HG}(G) \leq f(d)$.
rows.context_text: The authors remark in the open problems section that such a bounding function seems plausible, noting that it would imply absolute constant bounds on the hat guessing number of planar graphs (which are $5$-degenerate) and a Hadwiger-number bound via degeneracy results.
rows.source_text: It seems plausible that there exists a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for every $d$-degenerate graph $G$, $\mathrm{HG}(G) \leq f(d)$.

rows.title: Question on Typical Hat Guessing Number of G(n,1/2)
rows.paper_title: On the Hat Guessing Number of Graphs
rows.statement_text: What is the typical asymptotic behavior of the hat guessing number of the random graph $G(n,1/2)$? In particular, is $\mathrm{HG}(G(n,1/2))$ equal to $o(n)$ with high probability?
rows.context_text: The paper improves the known lower bound from $(2-o(1))\log_2 n$ to $n^{1-o(1)}$ with high probability, but the true order of growth remains unknown. The upper bound is $n$ trivially.
rows.source_text: What is the typical asymptotic behavior of the hat guessing number of the random graph $G(n,1/2)$? In particular, is $\mathrm{HG}(G(n,1/2))$ equal to $o(n)$ with high probability?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2107.05995 — Question on Typical Hat Guessing Number of G(n,1/2)
rows.status_semantics: A direct follow-up, arXiv:2302.04122 (2023), extends the n^{1-o(1)} lower bound from G(n,1/2) to all random graphs G(n,p) with constant edge probability p \in (0,1), and also establishes an upper bound of (1-o(1))n with high probability. These bounds narrow the gap but do not definitively resolve whether HG(G(n,1/2)) = o(n) holds with high probability, so the precise asymptotic order remains open.
rows.class_notes: Target proposition: HG(G(n,1/2)) = o(n) whp; needs hat-guessing primitive plus random-graph asymptotics

rows.title: Problem on Hat Guessing Number After Adding a Universal Vertex
rows.paper_title: On the Hat Guessing Number of Graphs
rows.statement_text: Establish an upper bound for $\mathrm{HG}(G')$ as a function of $\mathrm{HG}(G)$, where $G'$ is obtained from $G$ by adding a single vertex connected to all vertices of $G$, or show that no such function exists.
rows.context_text: Using the graphs $G_d(N)$ from [10], the authors show that for arbitrarily large $q = \mathrm{HG}(G)$ the hat guessing number of $G'$ can exceed $q^2$ (specifically reaching $q^2+q$), so adding a universal vertex can dramatically increase the hat guessing number.
rows.source_text: Establish an upper bound for $\mathrm{HG}(G')$ as a function of $\mathrm{HG}(G)$, where $G'$ is obtained from $G$ by adding a single vertex connected to all vertices of $G$, or show that no such function exists.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2107.05995 — Problem on Hat Guessing Number After Adding a Universal Vertex
rows.class_notes: Formalizes as: does there exist f with HG(G+universal vertex) <= f(HG(G))

rows.title: Informal remark on completeness of known forests satisfying Esperet's conjecture
rows.paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number. II. Excluding a star-forest
rows.statement_text: As far as we know (although it seems unlikely), [the forests described in results 1.4–1.6 and their induced subgraphs] might be all the forests that satisfy [Esperet's conjecture 1.2].
rows.context_text: After establishing their main results on star-forests and double stars, the authors pause to note the state of knowledge: the only forests known to satisfy Esperet's polynomial conjecture are those built from disjoint unions of stars, double stars, and $K_2$. They remark that these may be all such forests, though they consider this unlikely.
rows.source_text: As far as we know (although it seems unlikely), [the forests described in results 1.4–1.6 and their induced subgraphs] might be all the forests that satisfy [Esperet's conjecture 1.2].
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2107.11780 — Informal remark on completeness of known forests satisfying Esperet's conjecture
rows.status_semantics: The remark in arXiv:2107.11780 that star-forests, double stars, K₂, and their induced subgraphs might constitute the complete list of forests H for which H-free graphs are polynomially χ-bounded is refuted: Nguyen (December 2025, arXiv:2512.24907) proved that P₅-free graphs are polynomially χ-bounded, and P₅ is a forest that is not an induced subgraph of any star, double star, or K₂. Separately, Briański–Davies–Walczak (2022, arXiv:2201.08814) disproved Esperet’s general conjecture by constructing hereditary χ-bounded classes that are not polynomially χ-bounded, confirming that polynomial χ-bounds are not automatic even within χ-bounded classes.
rows.class_notes: Disproved; refers to internal results 1.4-1.6 and Esperet's Conjecture 1.2 not restated in the record

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: Threshold Ramsey multiplicity for odd cycles
rows.statement_text: For any sufficiently large odd integer $k$, $m(C_k) = (k-1)!/2$.
rows.context_text: The extremal coloring $\chi(k, k-1)$ of $K_{2k-1}$ contains exactly $(k-1)!/2$ monochromatic copies of $C_k$ (all in the blue clique of order $k$), giving an upper bound on $m(C_k)$. The authors suggest this upper bound is in fact tight for all sufficiently large odd $k$, strengthening the $(ck)^k$ lower bound proved as Theorem 3.
rows.source_text: For any sufficiently large odd integer $k$, $m(C_k) = (k-1)!/2$.
rows.class_notes: Threshold Ramsey multiplicity m(C_k) is definable from standard vocabulary (min mono copies over 2-colorings of K_{r(C_k)})

rows.title: Conjecture 3
rows.paper_title: Threshold Ramsey multiplicity for paths and even cycles
rows.statement_text: For sufficiently large $k$, if $k$ is even, then $m(P_k) = k!/2$ and if $k$ is odd, then $m(P_k) = \frac{k-1}{4}(k-1)!$.
rows.context_text: The authors identify specific extremal colorings $\chi(a_0, b_0)$ of $K_{r(P_k)}$ that achieve exact counts of monochromatic $P_k$, demonstrating tightness of Theorem 2 up to the constant $c$. These colorings are believed to realize the threshold Ramsey multiplicity for all sufficiently large $k$.
rows.source_text: For sufficiently large $k$, if $k$ is even, then $m(P_k) = k!/2$ and if $k$ is odd, then $m(P_k) = \frac{k-1}{4}(k-1)!$.
rows.class_notes: Same threshold Ramsey multiplicity setup as axv_2108_00987_00, for paths

rows.title: Conjecture 1.1
rows.paper_title: Irregular Subgraphs
rows.statement_text: Every $d$-regular graph $G$ on $n$ vertices contains a spanning subgraph $H$ so that for every $k$, $0 \leq k \leq d$, $\left|m(H, k) - \frac{n}{d+1}\right| \leq 2$.
rows.context_text: The paper introduces this conjecture as one of two central open problems on irregular spanning subgraphs. A small value of $m(H)$ measures the irregularity of $H$, and the conjecture asserts that every $d$-regular graph has a spanning subgraph nearly as irregular as its degree permits. The conjecture is tight: the vertex-disjoint union of two 4-cycles shows the additive 2 cannot be reduced to 1 in general.
rows.source_text: Every $d$-regular graph $G$ on $n$ vertices contains a spanning subgraph $H$ so that for every $k$, $0 \leq k \leq d$, $\left|m(H, k) - \frac{n}{d+1}\right| \leq 2$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.1 remains open for general d, but significant partial progress has been made. Fox, Luo, and Pham (2022) confirmed the asymptotic weaker version (Conjecture 1.2) for d = o(n/(log n)^12) via a probabilistic argument. Ma and Xie (2024) proved, via deterministic local adjustment techniques, that the conjecture holds exactly for d=3 (cubic graphs, in a strong form) and gave a general bound |m(H,k) - n/(d+1)| ≤ 2d² independent of n for all d-regular graphs — the first such n-independent bound.
rows.class_notes: m(H,k) (presumably number of degree-k vertices of H) is never defined in the record

rows.title: Conjecture 1.2
rows.paper_title: Irregular Subgraphs
rows.statement_text: Every graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and minimum degree $\delta$ contains a spanning subgraph $H$ satisfying $m(H) \leq \frac{n}{\delta+1} + 2$.
rows.context_text: This is the second of the two central conjectures introduced by the authors, generalising Conjecture 1.1 from regular graphs to arbitrary graphs with minimum degree $\delta$. Both conjectures remain open; the paper proves asymptotic relaxations showing the bound $(1+o(1))\frac{n}{\delta+1}+2$ is achievable when $n$ is large.
rows.source_text: Every graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and minimum degree $\delta$ contains a spanning subgraph $H$ satisfying $m(H) \leq \frac{n}{\delta+1} + 2$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.2 from arXiv:2108.02685 remains open in full generality. Fox, Luo, and Pham (2022) proved the asymptotic version for d-regular graphs when d = o(n/(log n)^{12}) via probabilistic methods. Ma and Xie (2024) obtained the first n-independent bound for d-regular graphs (within 2d^2 of the conjectured value) and proved the conjecture in strong form for d=3 regular graphs using deterministic local-adjustment techniques. Luzar, Przybylo, and Sotak (2024) independently resolved the cubic case exactly, showing deviation from n/4 is at most 1/2 (up to three exceptions). The exact bound for general graphs with minimum degree delta is still open.
rows.class_notes: Uses undefined paper-local m(H); companion to axv_2108_02685_00

rows.title: Conjecture 1
rows.paper_title: Triangle-free planar graphs with at most $64^{n^{0.731}}$ 3-colorings
rows.statement_text: There exists a constant $c > 1$ such that every $n$-vertex triangle-free planar graph has at least $c^{n^{\log_{9/2} 3}}$ distinct proper 3-colorings.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1 of the paper provides, for infinitely many $n$, an $n$-vertex triangle-free planar graph with at most $64^{n^{\log_{9/2} 3}} < 64^{n^{0.731}}$ 3-colorings, improving Thomassen's construction. The authors conjecture that the exponent $\log_{9/2} 3 \approx 0.731$ in the upper bound is optimal, making Conjecture 1 the matching lower-bound statement.
rows.source_text: There exists a constant $c > 1$ such that every $n$-vertex triangle-free planar graph has at least $c^{n^{\log_{9/2} 3}}$ distinct proper 3-colorings.
rows.class_notes: Needs planarity layer plus real exponent c^{n^{log_{9/2}3}} for counting 3-colorings

rows.title: Open Problem (Section 1): Balanced Separator for $U_t$
rows.paper_title: Universality in minor-closed graph classes
rows.statement_text: It is open whether $U_t$ satisfies (K2), that is, whether every finite $n$-vertex subgraph of $U_t$ has a balanced separator of order $O(\sqrt{n})$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.3 constructs, for each $t \in \mathbb{N}$, a countable graph $U_t$ that contains every $K_t$-minor-free graph as an induced subgraph and satisfies properties (K1), (K3), and (K4). Whether $U_t$ also satisfies (K2) (the $O(\sqrt{n})$ balanced separator property shared by planar graphs via the Lipton–Tarjan theorem) remains open. A weaker bound of $O(n^{3/4} \log n)$ on balanced separator size follows from a result of Plotkin, Rao, and Smith.
rows.source_text: It is open whether $U_t$ satisfies (K2), that is, whether every finite $n$-vertex subgraph of $U_t$ has a balanced separator of order $O(\sqrt{n})$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2109.00327 — Open Problem (Section 1): Balanced Separator for $U_t$
rows.class_notes: Refers to the paper-constructed countable universal graph U_t and internal property labels (K1)-(K4)

rows.title: Open problem: improved quantitative rank bounds
rows.paper_title: Rank of matrices with entries from a multiplicative group
rows.statement_text: It would be very interesting to get better bounds even for cases where the rank of the multiplicative group is very small, for example when all non-diagonal elements are powers of two.
rows.context_text: After the first proof of Theorem 1, the authors note that the quantitative bound obtained (essentially $\log\log n \leq r d^7$, improved to $\log n \leq r d^8$ in Section 4) is quite weak. They pose the challenge of obtaining sharper rank lower bounds, highlighting the special case where all off-diagonal entries are integer powers of $2$ as a concrete open instance.
rows.source_text: It would be very interesting to get better bounds even for cases where the rank of the multiplicative group is very small, for example when all non-diagonal elements are powers of two.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2109.00618 — Open problem: improved quantitative rank bounds
rows.defer_reason: Matrix rank over multiplicative groups: linear algebra/number theory; also a vague 'better bounds' request

rows.title: Conjecture 1.10
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions III. Three-path-configurations and logarithmic treewidth
rows.statement_text: For all $t \geq 0$ there exists $c = c(t)$ such that if $G$ is a graph with no $K_t$, no $K_{t,t}$, no subdivision of $W_{t \times t}$ and no line graph of a subdivision of $W_{t \times t}$ as induced subgraphs, then $\mathrm{tw}(G) \leq c \log(|V(G)|)$.
rows.context_text: The authors propose this as a natural extension of the main results and describe it as a variant of a conjecture of Sintiari and Trotignon [17]. It posits that excluding the four known families responsible for large treewidth (complete graphs, complete bipartite graphs, subdivided walls, line graphs of subdivided walls) as induced subgraphs forces treewidth to be at most logarithmic in the number of vertices.
rows.source_text: For all $t \geq 0$ there exists $c = c(t)$ such that if $G$ is a graph with no $K_t$, no $K_{t,t}$, no subdivision of $W_{t \times t}$ and no line graph of a subdivision of $W_{t \times t}$ as induced subgraphs, then $\mathrm{tw}(G) \leq c \log(|V(G)|)$.
rows.class_notes: Needs walls W_{txt}, subdivisions, line graphs and treewidth; all standard structural vocabulary

rows.title: Informal Belief on Non-Optimality of p-Goodness Bound
rows.paper_title: Ramsey goodness of books revisited
rows.statement_text: The bound $n \geq 2^{k^{10p}}$ in Theorem 1.1 is not best possible.
rows.context_text: Immediately after stating Theorem 1.1, the authors remark that they do not expect the bound $n \geq 2^{k^{10p}}$ to be best possible, and indicate that they discuss this further in the concluding remarks (Section 4).
rows.source_text: The bound $n \geq 2^{k^{10p}}$ in Theorem 1.1 is not best possible.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2109.09205 — Informal Belief on Non-Optimality of p-Goodness Bound
