rows.title: Question on Eliminating the Regularity Lemma from Nikiforov–Rousseau's General Theorem
rows.paper_title: Ramsey goodness of books revisited
rows.statement_text: Is it possible to completely eliminate the use of the regularity lemma from the proof of the general Ramsey goodness theorem of Nikiforov and Rousseau (Theorem 2.1 of \cite{28})?
rows.context_text: The authors show that their techniques can avoid the regularity lemma for Theorem 1.3, a special case of Nikiforov–Rousseau's most general goodness result. They ask whether this elimination can be extended to the full generality of that theorem, noting it would be very interesting to see how far these ideas can be pushed.
rows.source_text: Is it possible to completely eliminate the use of the regularity lemma from the proof of the general Ramsey goodness theorem of Nikiforov and Rousseau (Theorem 2.1 of \cite{28})?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2109.09205 — Question on Eliminating the Regularity Lemma from Nikiforov–Rousseau's General Theorem
rows.defer_reason: Asks whether a proof technique (regularity lemma) can be eliminated; proof-method meta-question

rows.title: Conjecture 1.3
rows.paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number. IV. A near-polynomial bound for excluding the five-vertex path
rows.statement_text: For every forest $H$, there exists $c > 0$ such that $\chi(G) \leq \omega(G)^c$ for every $H$-free graph $G$.
rows.context_text: This polynomial $\chi$-boundedness conjecture for forest-free graphs is described as beautiful and still open; it has been proved only for a smaller collection of forests and has not been settled for any tree containing $P_5$. Showing it for $H = P_5$ would imply the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture for $P_5$.
rows.source_text: For every forest $H$, there exists $c > 0$ such that $\chi(G) \leq \omega(G)^c$ for every $H$-free graph $G$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.3 — that for every forest H there exists c > 0 such that every H-free graph G satisfies chi(G) <= omega(G)^c — remains open in full generality. It has been established for star-forests and double stars (Scott–Seymour–Spirkl), and a fractional-colouring version has been proved for all multibrooms by Nguyen–Scott–Seymour (2024). For trees of radius two a polynomial bound in tau_d(G) is known (Scott–Seymour 2023), which yields a doubly-exponential but not polynomial bound in omega(G). For P_5-free graphs the best known bound is chi(G) <= omega(G)^{log_2(omega(G))}, a near-polynomial result proved in the source paper itself.
rows.class_notes: Polynomial chi-boundedness for forest-free graphs; strengthens Erdos-Hajnal for forests (implies EH for each forest H)

rows.title: Problem 1
rows.paper_title: Improved lower bound for the list chromatic number of graphs with no $K_t$ minor
rows.statement_text: Does every $K_t$-minor-free graph $G$ satisfy $\chi_\ell(G) \leq 2t$?
rows.context_text: Having established the lower bound $(2-o(1))t$ for the maximum list chromatic number of $K_t$-minor free graphs, the author asks whether the probabilistic lower-bound construction is essentially optimal up to lower-order terms, or whether the lower bound can be further improved beyond $2t$.
rows.source_text: Does every $K_t$-minor-free graph $G$ satisfy $\chi_\ell(G) \leq 2t$?
rows.class_notes: Quantitative 2t variant of the List Hadwiger conjecture; needs minor relation plus list coloring, both standard

rows.title: Open Problem: characterisation of ℓ-holed graphs for ℓ ∈ {4, 5, 6}
rows.paper_title: Graphs with all holes the same length
rows.statement_text: Give a complete structural description of $\ell$-holed graphs (graphs in which every hole has length exactly $\ell$) for $\ell \in \{4, 5, 6\}$, analogous to Theorem 1.3 for $\ell \geq 7$.
rows.context_text: The paper establishes a complete structural description of $\ell$-holed graphs for all $\ell \geq 7$ (Theorem 1.3), but explicitly states: "We were not able to do this for $\ell = 4, 5, 6$." The case $\ell = 5$ is highlighted separately: "when all holes have length five, we do not know a complete description, but such graphs were studied [12] in the context of algebraic combinatorics and commutative algebra."
rows.source_text: Give a complete structural description of $\ell$-holed graphs (graphs in which every hole has length exactly $\ell$) for $\ell \in \{4, 5, 6\}$, analogous to Theorem 1.3 for $\ell \geq 7$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2110.09970 — Open Problem: characterisation of ℓ-holed graphs for ℓ ∈ {4, 5, 6}
rows.defer_reason: Asks for a complete structural description of l-holed graphs for l in {4,5,6}; grand characterization, not a proposition

rows.title: Conjecture 7
rows.paper_title: Improved pyrotechnics : Closer to the burning graph conjecture
rows.statement_text: Let $T$ be a tree of growth $k$ and of order $n$. If $R$ is a finite set of integers such that $\{0, 1, \ldots, k\} \subseteq R$ and $\sum_{r \in R}(2r+1) \geq n$, then $T$ is $R$-burnable.
rows.context_text: The authors propose this as a stronger version of the Burning Number Conjecture via a reformulation using $R$-burnability: the BNC states that every connected graph on $n$ vertices is $\{0, 1, \ldots, \lceil\sqrt{n}\rceil - 1\}$-burnable, since $\sum_{r=0}^{\lceil\sqrt{n}\rceil - 1}(2r+1) = \lceil\sqrt{n}\rceil^2 \geq n$. Conjecture 7 generalises this to trees of bounded growth by requiring the set $R$ to contain all radii up to $k$ and have sufficient total volume.
rows.source_text: Let $T$ be a tree of growth $k$ and of order $n$. If $R$ is a finite set of integers such that $\{0, 1, \ldots, k\} \subseteq R$ and $\sum_{r \in R}(2r+1) \geq n$, then $T$ is $R$-burnable.
rows.class_notes: 'Tree of growth k' and full definition of R-burnability are paper-local; context only sketches the BNC reformulation

rows.title: Conjecture 5
rows.paper_title: Active clustering for labeling training data
rows.statement_text: For an $n$-set with a random partition with probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k$, the clique algorithm has minimal average complexity among all AC algorithms.
rows.context_text: In the model where each of $n$ items independently belongs to class $C_i$ with probability $p_i$ (with $p_1 \geq p_2 \geq \cdots \geq p_k$), the authors argue that an optimal algorithm should compare each new element first to the largest identified class. The clique algorithm does exactly this, comparing each new item to blocks in decreasing order of size. The conjecture is stated directly before Theorem 6, which characterises the asymptotic expected query count of the clique algorithm as $\sum_{i=1}^k i p_i n$, offered as supporting evidence.
rows.source_text: For an $n$-set with a random partition with probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k$, the clique algorithm has minimal average complexity among all AC algorithms.
rows.defer_reason: Average-case optimality of a query-based clustering algorithm on set partitions; not graph/hypergraph theory

rows.title: Conjecture (Section 7)
rows.paper_title: Discrepancy of arithmetic progressions in grids
rows.statement_text: The lower bound for the discrepancy of arithmetic progressions in grids given in Theorem 1.3 is tight up to the constant factor, i.e., for any positive integer $d$ and $N = (N_1, \ldots, N_d)$ with $N_1 \cdots N_d \geq 3$, $$\mathrm{disc}(\mathcal{A}_N) = \Theta_d\!\left(\max_{I \subseteq [d]} \left(\prod_{i \in I} N_i\right)^{\frac{1}{2|I|+2}}\right).$$
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.3 establishes matching lower and upper bounds for the discrepancy of arithmetic progressions in general grids of differing side lengths, but the upper bound carries an extra sub-logarithmic factor $\frac{\log(N_1 \cdots N_d)}{\log\log(N_1 \cdots N_d)}$ relative to the lower bound. The authors conjecture in their concluding remarks (Section 7) that this gap is an artifact of the proof and the lower bound is in fact tight up to a constant depending only on $d$.
rows.source_text: The lower bound for the discrepancy of arithmetic progressions in grids given in Theorem 1.3 is tight up to the constant factor, i.e., for any positive integer $d$ and $N = (N_1, \ldots, N_d)$ with $N_1 \cdots N_d \geq 3$, $$\mathrm{disc}(\mathcal{A}_N) = \Theta_d\!\left(\max_{I \subseteq [d]} \left(\prod_{i \in I} N_i\right)^{\frac{1}{2|I|+2}}\right).$$
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that the lower bound in Theorem 1.3 is tight up to a constant factor (i.e., no logarithmic factor) remains open. Li and Nikolov (2025, arXiv:2504.12598) improved the upper bound gap from a factor of log|Omega_N|/(log log|Omega_N|) to sqrt(log|Omega_N|), using factorization norms, but explicitly state that removing this final sqrt(log) factor is beyond their methods. The conjecture itself is restated as open in that paper.
rows.defer_reason: Discrepancy of arithmetic progressions in grids: discrepancy theory/additive combinatorics, not graph theory

rows.title: Open Challenge: Efficient Approximation of Twin-Width for Unordered Graphs
rows.paper_title: Twin-width VI: the lens of contraction sequences
rows.statement_text: Efficiently approximating twin-width (that is, returning an $f(d)$-sequence when the twin-width of the input is at most $d$) remains an open challenge for unordered graphs.
rows.context_text: The authors note that efficient approximation of twin-width has been achieved for classes of totally ordered binary structures and for all mentioned classes of bounded twin-width (bounded tree-width, proper minor-closed, hereditary subclasses of permutation graphs, etc.), but the problem is unresolved for general unordered graphs. This open challenge is stated in the introduction as a key missing piece in the twin-width landscape.
rows.source_text: Efficiently approximating twin-width (that is, returning an $f(d)$-sequence when the twin-width of the input is at most $d$) remains an open challenge for unordered graphs.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2111.00282 — Open Challenge: Efficient Approximation of Twin-Width for Unordered Graphs
rows.status_semantics: The open challenge of efficiently approximating twin-width for unordered graphs remains unresolved for general graphs, as confirmed by Bonnet's own open-questions page (updated 2025). Partial progress exists: an FPT approximation algorithm has been found for graphs with small feedback edge number (Balabán, Ganian, Rocton 2023), but no XP or FPT approximation is known for the general unordered case. Exact computation of twin-width is NP-hard—deciding whether twin-width is at most 4 is NP-complete.
rows.defer_reason: 'Efficiently approximating twin-width remains an open challenge' leaves the efficiency notion and approximation function unquantified

rows.title: Question 1.3
rows.paper_title: Pure pairs. IX. Transversal trees
rows.statement_text: Do there exist $c, \varepsilon > 0$ with the following property? Let $\mathcal{B} = (B_1, B_2, B_3)$ be a blockade in a graph $G$, and let $W$ be its width. If there is no $\mathcal{B}$-transversal triangle, then there exist distinct $i, j \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $X \subseteq B_i$ and $Y \subseteq B_j$, such that $X, Y$ is a pure pair, and $|X|, |Y| \geq \varepsilon W^c$.
rows.context_text: This is a variant of a conjecture of Conlon, Fox and Sudakov. Settling Question 1.3 was the authors' initial goal, though they were unable to do so. It is also equivalent to the $k=3$ case of Theorem 1.4 (which handles cycles of length $k \geq 4$), and can be reduced to the sparse case where every vertex in $B_i$ has at most $|B_j|/100$ neighbours in $B_j$ for all distinct $i, j \in \{1,2,3\}$.
rows.source_text: Do there exist $c, \varepsilon > 0$ with the following property? Let $\mathcal{B} = (B_1, B_2, B_3)$ be a blockade in a graph $G$, and let $W$ be its width. If there is no $\mathcal{B}$-transversal triangle, then there exist distinct $i, j \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $X \subseteq B_i$ and $Y \subseteq B_j$, such that $X, Y$ is a pure pair, and $|X|, |Y| \geq \varepsilon W^c$.
rows.class_notes: Blockade, width, transversal triangle and pure pair are series-local vocabulary not defined in the record

rows.title: Informal: which graphs H admit polynomial-size pure pairs
rows.paper_title: Pure pairs. IX. Transversal trees
rows.statement_text: For which ordered graphs $H$ can we get $|X|, |Y| \geq \varepsilon W^c$ (for some fixed $c > 0$ depending only on $H$) as the pure pair conclusion when there is no ordered $\mathcal{B}$-transversal copy of $H$ in a blockade of width $W$?
rows.context_text: Immediately after stating Theorem 1.2 (Erdős–Hajnal–Pach), the authors ask for which $H$ the pure pair conclusion can be made polynomial in $W$. They note that $c = 1$ (linear pure pairs) is achievable only for $|H| \leq 2$, and flag the polynomial question as 'more promising'. The bulk of the paper provides partial answers for trees and caterpillars.
rows.source_text: For which ordered graphs $H$ can we get $|X|, |Y| \geq \varepsilon W^c$ (for some fixed $c > 0$ depending only on $H$) as the pure pair conclusion when there is no ordered $\mathcal{B}$-transversal copy of $H$ in a blockade of width $W$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2111.00532 — Informal: which graphs H admit polynomial-size pure pairs
rows.defer_reason: 'For which ordered graphs H' characterization question, also relying on series-local blockade vocabulary

rows.title: Informal: characterization of graphs with the strong transversal property
rows.paper_title: Pure pairs. IX. Transversal trees
rows.statement_text: Which graphs $H$ have the strong transversal property, i.e., for which $H$ does there exist $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every graph $G$ with an $(\varepsilon W, \varepsilon W)$-cohesive blockade $\mathcal{B} = (B_1, \ldots, B_{|H|})$ of local degree less than $\varepsilon W$ contain a $\mathcal{B}$-transversal copy of $H$?
rows.context_text: The question opens Section 2. The authors note that $H$ must be a forest, and give partial results in Theorem 2.1: paths have the property; certain caterpillar-like trees do; trees with a vertex of degree $d$ satisfying $2^{d-1} \geq |H|$ do not. No complete characterization is given, and the converse of the component-wise criterion fails.
rows.source_text: Which graphs $H$ have the strong transversal property, i.e., for which $H$ does there exist $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every graph $G$ with an $(\varepsilon W, \varepsilon W)$-cohesive blockade $\mathcal{B} = (B_1, \ldots, B_{|H|})$ of local degree less than $\varepsilon W$ contain a $\mathcal{B}$-transversal copy of $H$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2111.00532 — Informal: characterization of graphs with the strong transversal property
rows.defer_reason: 'Which graphs H...' characterization question, not a single proposition; also relies on paper-local blockade/transversal definitions
rows.class_notes: Even sharpened it would need the paper's cohesive-blockade vocabulary.

rows.title: Question 6
rows.paper_title: Weak diameter coloring of graphs on surfaces
rows.statement_text: For a non-negative integer $r$, let $\mathcal{G}_r$ be the class of plane graphs with no separating triangles such that each vertex is at distance at most $r$ from a face of length at least four. Does $\mathcal{G}_r$ have weak diameter chromatic number at most two?
rows.context_text: The authors suspect that a substantial relaxation of the triangle-free assumption is sufficient to allow coloring by two colors. They note that the HEX-lemma-based example with triangulated grids shows that non-triangular faces are essential, and remark that the question has a positive answer in the special case where the graph has exactly one non-triangular face (since proximity to a fixed face forces bounded treewidth and hence asymptotic dimension one).
rows.source_text: For a non-negative integer $r$, let $\mathcal{G}_r$ be the class of plane graphs with no separating triangles such that each vertex is at distance at most $r$ from a face of length at least four. Does $\mathcal{G}_r$ have weak diameter chromatic number at most two?
rows.class_notes: 'Weak diameter chromatic number' is not defined in the record; plane-graph class G_r otherwise precise.

rows.title: Polynomial Kempe sequences for degenerate graphs
rows.paper_title: Kempe changes in degenerate graphs
rows.statement_text: Can any two $k$-colorings of a $(k-1)$-degenerate graph on $n$ vertices always be connected by a sequence of Kempe changes whose length is polynomial in $n$?
rows.context_text: Las Vergnas and Meyniel proved in 1981 that all $k$-colorings of a $d$-degenerate graph are Kempe equivalent when $k > d+1$, but their proof yields a sequence that may be exponential in the number of vertices. Whether a polynomial bound can be achieved is posed as an intriguing open question and is the central motivation for all three main results of the paper.
rows.source_text: Can any two $k$-colorings of a $(k-1)$-degenerate graph on $n$ vertices always be connected by a sequence of Kempe changes whose length is polynomial in $n$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2112.02313 — Polynomial Kempe sequences for degenerate graphs
rows.class_notes: Needs a Kempe-change step relation plus a polynomial-length wrapper; all finite and standard.

rows.title: Problem 1.2
rows.paper_title: Quasiplanar Graphs, String Graphs, and the Erdos-Gallai Problem
rows.statement_text: Fix an integer $r \geq 4$. Is it true that every $K_r$-free segment graph on $n$ vertices has an induced subgraph on $\Omega_r(n)$ vertices which is $K_{r-1}$-free?
rows.context_text: Motivated by the approach of coloring $K_r$-free string graphs to prove Conjecture 1.1: if each color class could be made $K_4$-free, Ackerman's result would bound the edges per class by $O(n)$. Although Krawczyk–Walczak showed $K_r$-free string graphs can require $\Omega_r(\log\log n)$ colors with $K_{r-1}$-free classes, the question of finding a linearly large $K_{r-1}$-free induced subgraph in a $K_r$-free segment graph remains open. The paper's Theorems 1.5 and 1.6 address analogous questions for string graphs (with a $\log^2 n$ loss), but the segment-graph case with a linear guarantee is unresolved.
rows.source_text: Fix an integer $r \geq 4$. Is it true that every $K_r$-free segment graph on $n$ vertices has an induced subgraph on $\Omega_r(n)$ vertices which is $K_{r-1}$-free?
rows.class_notes: Segment intersection graphs need a real-geometry representation primitive; Omega_r(n) also needs an asymptotic wrapper.

rows.title: Question 9
rows.paper_title: Edge Partitions of Complete Geometric Graphs (Part 2)
rows.statement_text: Is the upper bound in Theorem 2 tight up to lower-order terms?
rows.context_text: Theorem 2 shows that for a convex $n$-point set $P$ and every $k \in \mathbb{N}$, $K(P)$ admits a partition into at most $\frac{n}{\sqrt{2k}}$ $k$-planar subgraphs (for appropriate $s$), while at least $\frac{n-1}{4.93\sqrt{k}}$ subgraphs are required. Question 9 asks whether these bounds match up to lower-order terms. The authors note that for $k=2$ they can show a lower bound of $\frac{3n}{10}$ almost matching the upper bound $\frac{n}{3}$, but omit this from the current version.
rows.source_text: Is the upper bound in Theorem 2 tight up to lower-order terms?
rows.defer_reason: 'Is the bound tight up to lower-order terms?' is a tightness/meta question referencing internal Theorem 2

rows.title: Question (Section 3, implicit representation for speed $2^{O(n\log n)}$)
rows.paper_title: Implicit representation of sparse hereditary families
rows.statement_text: Is it possible that hereditary families with speed $f(n) \leq 2^{O(n \log n)}$ always admit an implicit representation of size $O(n^{1/2} \log n)$?
rows.context_text: Hatami and Hatami [13] showed that for every $\delta > 0$ there are hereditary families with speed $f(n) \leq 2^{O(n \log n)}$ for which any implicit representation requires labels of size at least $\Omega(n^{1/2-\delta})$, and they raise the question of whether the exponent $1/2$ can be improved. Alon here asks the complementary upper-bound question: whether $O(n^{1/2} \log n)$ always suffices in this speed range.
rows.source_text: Is it possible that hereditary families with speed $f(n) \leq 2^{O(n \log n)}$ always admit an implicit representation of size $O(n^{1/2} \log n)$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2201.00328 — Question (Section 3, implicit representation for speed $2^{O(n\log n)}$)
rows.class_notes: Hereditary-family speed and adjacency labeling schemes are standard; O-notation quantifiers need asymptotic idiom.

rows.title: Question (Section 3, implicit representation for speed $2^{n^{1+\varepsilon}}$)
rows.paper_title: Implicit representation of sparse hereditary families
rows.statement_text: If the speed of a hereditary family satisfies $f(n) < 2^{n^{1+\varepsilon}}$ for a sufficiently small fixed $\varepsilon > 0$, is there always an implicit representation of size at most $O(n^{2/3} \log n)$?
rows.context_text: This is posed as a follow-up to the $O(n^{1/2}\log n)$ question for the slower-speed regime. Theorem 1.1 guarantees only $O(n^{1-1/d}\log n)$ under the weaker bound $f(n) \leq 2^{(1/4-\varepsilon)n^2}$, and the question asks whether the intermediate speed range $f(n) < 2^{n^{1+\varepsilon}}$ forces a sub-polynomial improvement to $n^{2/3}$.
rows.source_text: If the speed of a hereditary family satisfies $f(n) < 2^{n^{1+\varepsilon}}$ for a sufficiently small fixed $\varepsilon > 0$, is there always an implicit representation of size at most $O(n^{2/3} \log n)$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2201.00328 — Question (Section 3, implicit representation for speed $2^{n^{1+\varepsilon}}$)
rows.class_notes: Companion to axv_2201_00328_00 in the intermediate speed regime.

rows.title: Possibility 1.6
rows.paper_title: Pure pairs. VIII. Excluding a sparse graph
rows.statement_text: For all $c > 0$, there exists $\xi > 0$ with the following property. For every graph $H$ with congestion at most $\xi$, there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that for every graph $G$ with $|G| > 1$ that is $H$-free and $\bar{H}$-free, there is a pure pair $A, B$ in $G$ with $|A| \geq \varepsilon|G|$ and $|B| \geq \varepsilon|G|^{1-c}$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.3 (from an earlier paper) gives a pure pair where one set has linear size, and the authors ask whether an analogous strengthening of their main result 1.4 holds: can one of the two sets always be taken to have linear size rather than polynomial size $\varepsilon|G|^{1-c}$? The authors explicitly state they were unable to decide this. The proof technique for 1.4 cannot be directly extended to resolve 1.6 because a key inductive argument requires strictly increasing shrinkage parameters, leaving no room for the stronger conclusion.
rows.source_text: For all $c > 0$, there exists $\xi > 0$ with the following property. For every graph $H$ with congestion at most $\xi$, there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that for every graph $G$ with $|G| > 1$ that is $H$-free and $\bar{H}$-free, there is a pure pair $A, B$ in $G$ with $|A| \geq \varepsilon|G|$ and $|B| \geq \varepsilon|G|^{1-c}$.
rows.class_notes: 'Congestion' of H is paper-local and not defined in the record; pure-pair part is standard.

rows.title: Open question on Theorem 3.3 with stronger coherence
rows.paper_title: Pure pairs. VIII. Excluding a sparse graph
rows.statement_text: Is Theorem 3.3 true with the hypothesis that $G$ is $(\varepsilon|G|^{1-c},\, \varepsilon|G|^{1-c})$-coherent replaced by the stronger hypothesis that $G$ is $(\varepsilon|G|^{1-c},\, \varepsilon|G|)$-coherent?
rows.context_text: In Section 4, the authors note that the stronger coherence condition $(\varepsilon|G|^{1-c}, \varepsilon|G|)$-coherent would suffice for almost all of the proof of Theorem 3.3, and they write the proof using only this weaker form wherever possible. Resolving this question in the affirmative would be a step toward proving Possibility 1.6.
rows.source_text: Is Theorem 3.3 true with the hypothesis that $G$ is $(\varepsilon|G|^{1-c},\, \varepsilon|G|^{1-c})$-coherent replaced by the stronger hypothesis that $G$ is $(\varepsilon|G|^{1-c},\, \varepsilon|G|)$-coherent?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2201.04062 — Open question on Theorem 3.3 with stronger coherence
rows.class_notes: References internal Theorem 3.3 and undefined coherence notion; not recoverable from this record.

rows.title: Question 3.1
rows.paper_title: A counterexample to a conjecture about triangle-free induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number
rows.statement_text: Are there graphs with clique number $3$ and arbitrarily large chromatic number whose all triangle-free induced subgraphs have chromatic number at most $3$?
rows.context_text: After Theorem 1.3 disposes of all cases $r \geq 5$ in Conjecture 1.1, the case $r = 4$ is the only one remaining open. This question, which captures exactly that case, was first asked by James Davies (private communication).
rows.source_text: Are there graphs with clique number $3$ and arbitrarily large chromatic number whose all triangle-free induced subgraphs have chromatic number at most $3$?
rows.class_notes: Existence of a family with omega=3, unbounded chi, and all triangle-free induced subgraphs 3-chromatic; fully standard vocabulary.

rows.title: Question 3.2
rows.paper_title: A counterexample to a conjecture about triangle-free induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number
rows.statement_text: Is there a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for every digraph $D$ with no induced directed cycle of odd length, we have $\vec{\chi}(D) \leq f(\omega(D))$?
rows.context_text: Motivated by Theorem 1.5, which shows that the digraph analogue of the Scott–Seymour theorem on odd holes fails, the authors ask whether banning all odd-length induced directed cycles forces the dichromatic number to be bounded by a function of the clique number.
rows.source_text: Is there a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for every digraph $D$ with no induced directed cycle of odd length, we have $\vec{\chi}(D) \leq f(\omega(D))$?
rows.class_notes: Chi-bounding-function wrapper over dichromatic number vs clique number; no induced odd directed cycle is standard.

rows.title: Question 1
rows.paper_title: Disproof of a Conjecture by Woodall
rows.statement_text: Is every graph without a $K_{4,4}$-minor $7$-choosable? Is every graph without a $K_{3,5}$-minor $7$-choosable?
rows.context_text: After disproving Woodall's Conjecture for large comparable $s$ and $t$, the authors identify $K_{4,4}$ and $K_{3,5}$ as the smallest parameter pairs for which the conjecture is not yet settled by prior results, since Jørgensen's result gives only $8$-choosability for $K_{4,4}$-minor-free graphs and no sharp result is known for $K_{3,5}$.
rows.source_text: Is every graph without a $K_{4,4}$-minor $7$-choosable? Is every graph without a $K_{3,5}$-minor $7$-choosable?
rows.class_notes: Smallest open special cases (K_{4,4}, K_{3,5}) of Woodall's K_{s,t}-minor choosability conjecture.

rows.title: Question 2
rows.paper_title: Disproof of a Conjecture by Woodall
rows.statement_text: Is it true that for all integers $1 \leq s \leq t$, every graph $G$ with $G \not\succeq K_{s,t}$ satisfies $\chi_{\ell}(G) \leq 2s + t$?
rows.context_text: Theorem 1 shows that the bound $s+t-1$ in Woodall's conjecture is false for large comparable $s$ and $t$, and that the list chromatic number can exceed $(1-\varepsilon)(2s+t)$; the authors ask whether the matching upper bound $2s+t$ holds universally as the correct asymptotic threshold.
rows.source_text: Is it true that for all integers $1 \leq s \leq t$, every graph $G$ with $G \not\succeq K_{s,t}$ satisfies $\chi_{\ell}(G) \leq 2s + t$?
rows.class_notes: Weakened bound 2s+t replacing s+t-1 after disproof of Woodall's conjecture.

rows.title: Open problem: Woodall's conjecture for fixed s
rows.paper_title: Disproof of a Conjecture by Woodall
rows.statement_text: It remains an interesting open problem whether Conjecture 3 (that every graph without a $K_{s,t}$-minor is $(s+t-1)$-choosable) remains true if $s$ is fixed and $t$ is sufficiently large in terms of $s$.
rows.context_text: The disproof via Theorem 1 requires $s$ and $t$ to be of comparable size ($s \leq t \leq Cs$); the regime of fixed $s$ with $t \to \infty$ is not covered and, unlike the chromatic number case (settled by Kostochka), no analogous result is known for the list chromatic number.
rows.source_text: It remains an interesting open problem whether Conjecture 3 (that every graph without a $K_{s,t}$-minor is $(s+t-1)$-choosable) remains true if $s$ is fixed and $t$ is sufficiently large in terms of $s$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2201.09115 — Open problem: Woodall's conjecture for fixed s
rows.class_notes: Woodall restricted to fixed s with t sufficiently large; needs an eventually-quantifier wrapper.

rows.title: Open problem: smallest counterexample to Woodall's conjecture
rows.paper_title: Disproof of a Conjecture by Woodall
rows.statement_text: It would also be interesting to determine the smallest values of $s$ and $t$ (or of $s + t$) for which Conjecture 3 (that every graph without a $K_{s,t}$-minor is $(s+t-1)$-choosable) fails.
rows.context_text: The probabilistic construction in the proof of Theorem 1 yields counterexamples only for astronomically large $s$ and $t$; the smallest explicit failing parameter pair is left as an open problem, motivating the study of small cases such as Question 1.
rows.source_text: It would also be interesting to determine the smallest values of $s$ and $t$ (or of $s + t$) for which Conjecture 3 (that every graph without a $K_{s,t}$-minor is $(s+t-1)$-choosable) fails.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2201.09115 — Open problem: smallest counterexample to Woodall's conjecture
rows.status_semantics: Steiner's 2022 disproof of Woodall's conjecture used a probabilistic argument that only yields counterexamples for astronomically large parameters (estimated around 10^29). A 2025 follow-up by Vanderbush in Graphs and Combinatorics substantially reduced this, constructing explicit counterexamples for (s,t)=(t,t) with t ≥ 48 (t ≠ 49) and (s,t)=(t,t+1) with t ≥ 54 (t ≠ 55). The question of determining the absolute smallest s and t for which the conjecture fails remains open.
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the smallest s,t for which the conjecture fails' is a determine-the-value ask, not a single proposition
rows.class_notes: Variant/refinement request around Woodall's conjecture counterexamples.

rows.title: Open problem on construction size
rows.paper_title: Chordal directed graphs are not $χ$-bounded
rows.statement_text: It would be interesting to know if the size of the $(k+1)$-dichromatic example in $\mathcal{C}_3$ can be reduced below the current bound of $n^{2^{\mathrm{poly}(n)}}$, which is larger than the $2^{\mathrm{poly}(|G_k|)}$ bound achieved by Zykov's construction for triangle-free graphs.
rows.context_text: The authors observe in the Further Works section that their Zykov-style digraph construction yields graphs whose size grows much faster than the analogous triangle-free undirected graphs, and they flag reducing this size as an open problem.
rows.source_text: It would be interesting to know if the size of the $(k+1)$-dichromatic example in $\mathcal{C}_3$ can be reduced below the current bound of $n^{2^{\mathrm{poly}(n)}}$, which is larger than the $2^{\mathrm{poly}(|G_k|)}$ bound achieved by Zykov's construction for triangle-free graphs.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2202.01006 — Open problem on construction size
rows.defer_reason: 'Would be interesting to reduce the size of the construction' — improve-the-bound meta ask tied to a paper-local construction

rows.title: Conjecture 1.3
rows.paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number. V. Excluding a tree of radius two and a complete multipartite graph
rows.statement_text: For every forest $H$, there is a polynomial $f$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f(\omega(G))$ for every $H$-free graph $G$.
rows.context_text: This conjecture is presented as what the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture (1.1) and Esperet's conjecture (that every $\chi$-bounded hereditary class has a polynomial bounding function) would jointly imply for forest-free graph classes. Esperet's conjecture has been disproved in full generality but remains open for classes excluding a forest, and Conjecture 1.3 is known only for a few special families of trees.
rows.source_text: For every forest $H$, there is a polynomial $f$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f(\omega(G))$ for every $H$-free graph $G$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.3 — polynomial chi-boundedness for every forest-free graph class — remains open in full generality as of May 2026. Significant partial progress has been made: Nguyen (arXiv:2512.24907, 2025) proved the conjecture for H = P_5, the five-vertex path, resolving a case open since 1985; Davies and Yuditsky (arXiv:2407.16882, 2024) proved it for all forests H when the host graphs have bounded boxicity. The ongoing Scott–Seymour series establishes polynomial bounds for several additional tree families, but no uniform proof for all forests exists.
rows.class_notes: Polynomial Gyarfas-Sumner: for every forest H a polynomial chi-binding function; standard vocabulary plus polynomial wrapper.

rows.title: Question: Extending Theorem 1.6 to paths
rows.paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number. V. Excluding a tree of radius two and a complete multipartite graph
rows.statement_text: Is it true that if $H$ is a path, then for every integer $d \geq 1$, there is a polynomial $f$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f(\tau_d(G))$ for every $H$-free graph $G$?
rows.context_text: This question, suggested by a referee and included by the authors, asks whether the main result (Theorem 1.6 for trees of radius two) can be extended to paths—another prominent family of forests for which polynomial bounds in terms of $\tau_2(G)$ are already known (via results of Bonamy et al. and Scott-Seymour-Spirkl).
rows.source_text: Is it true that if $H$ is a path, then for every integer $d \geq 1$, there is a polynomial $f$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f(\tau_d(G))$ for every $H$-free graph $G$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2202.05557 — Question: Extending Theorem 1.6 to paths
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture asks whether polynomial bounds chi(G) <= f(tau_d(G)) hold for every path-free graph G, extending Theorem 1.6 (proved for trees of radius two) to paths. A direct successor in the series, 'Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VIII. Excluding a path and a complete multipartite graph' by Nguyen (Journal of Graph Theory, 2024, DOI 10.1002/jgt.23129), bears a title that mirrors the structure of the source paper and appears to address exactly this question; however, the full theorem statement could not be verified due to paywall access. The internal corpus references (arXiv:2409.09397, arXiv:2409.09400) concern Gyarfas-Sumner stable-set bounds for forest-free graphs and do not address this specific conjecture about tau_d and chromatic number.
rows.class_notes: tau_d(G) is paper-local and not defined in the record.

rows.title: Question: Polynomial bound analogue of Esperet's conjecture for $\tau_d$
rows.paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number. V. Excluding a tree of radius two and a complete multipartite graph
rows.statement_text: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a hereditary class of graphs, and $d \geq 1$. Suppose that there is a function $f$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f(\tau_d(G))$ for each $G \in \mathcal{C}$. Can we always choose $f$ to be a polynomial? What if $d = 2$?
rows.context_text: This question, suggested by a referee and included by the authors, asks for an analogue of Esperet's conjecture in the setting of $\tau_d(G)$ in place of $\omega(G)$. It is motivated by the polynomial bounds proved in Theorem 1.6 and asks whether being $\tau_d$-bounded always implies being polynomially $\tau_d$-bounded.
rows.source_text: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a hereditary class of graphs, and $d \geq 1$. Suppose that there is a function $f$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f(\tau_d(G))$ for each $G \in \mathcal{C}$. Can we always choose $f$ to be a polynomial? What if $d = 2$?

rows.class_notes: Precise Esperet-style question but depends on the undefined tau_d parameter.

rows.title: Open case of Theorem 3 for odd $n$
rows.paper_title: Structured Codes of Graphs
rows.statement_text: Determine $M_{F_{2c}}(n)$ for odd $n \geq 5$. The upper bound $M_{F_{2c}}(n) \leq 2^{n-2}$ holds for all $n$, but the best construction for odd $n$ yields only $2^{n-2} - \binom{n-2}{(n-3)/2}$ graphs.
rows.context_text: Theorem 3 establishes $M_{F_{2c}}(n) = 2^{n-2}$ for all even $n$ via matching upper and lower bounds. For odd $n$ the upper bound still holds, but the construction from the even case fails; the best the authors could achieve gives $2^{n-2} - \binom{n-2}{(n-3)/2}$, leaving a gap. For $n = 3$ the upper bound is attained (a triangle and the empty graph), but no general matching construction is known.
rows.source_text: Determine $M_{F_{2c}}(n)$ for odd $n \geq 5$. The upper bound $M_{F_{2c}}(n) \leq 2^{n-2}$ holds for all $n$, but the best construction for odd $n$ yields only $2^{n-2} - \binom{n-2}{(n-3)/2}$ graphs.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2202.06810 — Open case of Theorem 3 for odd $n$
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine M_{F_{2c}}(n) for odd n' — determine-the-value ask, and M_{F_{2c}} is paper-local notation
rows.class_notes: Could be sharpened to 'M = 2^{n-2} for odd n?' but the record poses it as determination.

rows.title: Optimal bound question
rows.paper_title: Asymptotic dimension of intersection graphs
rows.statement_text: Is it possible to decrease the bound to $n$ in Theorem 2 (or at least in Theorem 1)?
rows.context_text: The paper proves that intersection graphs of $f$-space-filling systems of subsets of a metric space of Assouad-Nagata dimension $n$ have asymptotic dimension at most $2n+1$. The authors remark that this bound is likely not optimal, noting that interval graphs (intersection graphs of balls in $\mathbb{R}^1$) have asymptotic dimension $1$ rather than $3$. They also observe that $n$-dimensional grids have asymptotic dimension $n$ and can be represented as touching graphs of balls in $\mathbb{R}^n$, so the bound cannot be decreased below $n$.
rows.source_text: Is it possible to decrease the bound to $n$ in Theorem 2 (or at least in Theorem 1)?
rows.class_notes: References internal Theorems 1/2 and undefined f-space-filling systems; asymptotic dimension is an infinite-metric notion.

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: Expected number of faces in a random embedding of any graph is at most linear
rows.statement_text: For any simple graph $G$ of order $n$, $E[F] \leq \frac{1}{3}n + 1$.
rows.context_text: The paper proves $E[F] \leq \frac{\pi^2}{6}n$ for simple graphs (Theorem 8), but no examples approaching this constant are known. A chain of triangles connected by cut edges achieves $E[F] = \frac{1}{3}n+1$, which the authors conjecture is the true optimal bound.
rows.source_text: For any simple graph $G$ of order $n$, $E[F] \leq \frac{1}{3}n + 1$.
rows.class_notes: Expected face count over uniformly random rotation systems: finite probability plus embedding layer.

rows.title: Question (which hereditary classes are poly-χ-bounded?)
rows.paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes
rows.statement_text: Which hereditary classes are poly-$\chi$-bounded?
rows.context_text: Esperet's conjecture that every $\chi$-bounded hereditary class is poly-$\chi$-bounded was recently disproved by Briański, Davies and Walczak. This motivates the overarching question of characterising which hereditary classes do have a polynomial binding function.
rows.source_text: Which hereditary classes are poly-$\chi$-bounded?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2202.09118 — Question (which hereditary classes are poly-χ-bounded?)

rows.title: Open problem (poly-χ-bounded for k-multihole with only odd cycles)
rows.paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes
rows.statement_text: For any integer $k \geq 1$, is the class of graphs with no $k$-multihole in which all the cycles have odd length polynomially $\chi$-bounded?
rows.context_text: This class is $\chi$-bounded [9], but it contains the class of $\{P_5, C_5\}$-free graphs. The authors state they cannot yet prove it is poly-$\chi$-bounded, referring to [15] for the best current bounds.
rows.source_text: For any integer $k \geq 1$, is the class of graphs with no $k$-multihole in which all the cycles have odd length polynomially $\chi$-bounded?
