rows.title: Informal conjecture (polynomial Gyárfás–Sumner)
rows.paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes
rows.statement_text: It might be true that every forest $H$ is good, i.e., the class of $H$-free graphs is polynomially $\chi$-bounded (strengthening the Gyárfás–Sumner conjecture from $\chi$-boundedness to polynomial $\chi$-boundedness).
rows.context_text: Say a forest $H$ is good if the class of $H$-free graphs is polynomially $\chi$-bounded. This has been proved only for a few simple kinds of tree and some of their disjoint unions; the polynomial strengthening of Gyárfás–Sumner is fully open.
rows.source_text: It might be true that every forest $H$ is good, i.e., the class of $H$-free graphs is polynomially $\chi$-bounded (strengthening the Gyárfás–Sumner conjecture from $\chi$-boundedness to polynomial $\chi$-boundedness).
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that every forest H is good (i.e., H-free graphs are polynomially χ-bounded) remains fully open for general H-free graph classes. Partial progress exists: companion paper arXiv:2202.10412 (same 2022 series) proves compositional closure results (H good implies H∪P₄ good), and arXiv:2407.16882 (2024) proves the polynomial Gyárfás–Sumner conjecture holds for the restricted class of intersection graphs of axis-aligned boxes in ℝ^d with no induced forest subgraph. The full statement for arbitrary H-free graphs is unresolved.
rows.class_notes: Same polynomial Gyarfas-Sumner statement as Conjecture 1.3 of arXiv 2202.05557.

rows.title: Open problem (disjoint union of good trees)
rows.paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes
rows.statement_text: It is not known that if trees $H_1$, $H_2$ are good (i.e., the class of $H_i$-free graphs is polynomially $\chi$-bounded for $i=1,2$), then the disjoint union of $H_1$ and $H_2$ is also good.
rows.context_text: Trees of diameter three are good [14], but whether disjoint unions of diameter-three trees are good is unknown. Self-isolation provides a sufficient condition: if $H_1$ and $H_2$ are good forests and one of them is self-isolating, their disjoint union is good.
rows.source_text: It is not known that if trees $H_1$, $H_2$ are good (i.e., the class of $H_i$-free graphs is polynomially $\chi$-bounded for $i=1,2$), then the disjoint union of $H_1$ and $H_2$ is also good.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2202.09118 — Open problem (disjoint union of good trees)
rows.status_semantics: The general question of whether the disjoint union of two good trees is always good remains open as of 2026. Partial progress exists: arXiv:2202.10412 (submitted three days after the source paper) proves that if $H$ is a good forest then $H \cup P_4$ is also good, and the source paper itself notes that the disjoint union of two good forests is good whenever one is self-isolating. No paper resolving the full conjecture was found in the indexed literature.
rows.class_notes: 'Good' is defined inline (poly chi-bounded H-free class), so the closure-under-disjoint-union statement is self-contained.

rows.title: Question (are all graphs self-isolating?)
rows.paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes
rows.statement_text: Could it be that all graphs are self-isolating?
rows.context_text: The authors note that they know very few graphs that have the self-isolating property and none that are known not to have it. The only connected graphs known to be self-isolating are complete graphs, paths of arbitrary length, and complete bipartite graphs (proved in the paper and its predecessors).
rows.source_text: Could it be that all graphs are self-isolating?
rows.class_notes: 'Self-isolating' is paper-local and not defined in the record.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (every forest is good)
rows.paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VI. Adding a four-vertex path
rows.statement_text: Perhaps every forest is good (i.e., for every forest $H$, the class of $H$-free graphs is polynomially $\chi$-bounded).
rows.context_text: After recalling that Esperet's conjecture that every $\chi$-bounded hereditary class is polynomially $\chi$-bounded was disproved, the authors ask which hereditary classes are polynomially $\chi$-bounded and raise in particular whether the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture can be strengthened to polynomial $\chi$-boundedness. They note that among trees, only those not containing $P_5$ are currently known to be good, and that it is not even known whether $P_5$ is good.
rows.source_text: Perhaps every forest is good (i.e., for every forest $H$, the class of $H$-free graphs is polynomially $\chi$-bounded).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2202.10412 — Informal Conjecture (every forest is good)
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that every forest H is good (H-free graphs are polynomially χ-bounded) remains open in general, but has seen major progress. At the time of the source paper, P₅ was the smallest forest not known to be good; in December 2025 Tung H. Nguyen (arXiv:2512.24907) proved that P₅-free graphs are polynomially χ-bounded, resolving that open case. The polynomial Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture has also been established for bounded-boxicity graphs (arXiv:2407.16882). Forests requiring longer induced paths (P₆ and beyond) remain open.
rows.class_notes: Polynomial strengthening of Gyarfas-Sumner (the matched OPG problem); restates axv_2202_05557_00.

rows.title: Conjecture 2.3
rows.paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VI. Adding a four-vertex path
rows.statement_text: Let $\psi, \sigma : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ be non-decreasing polynomials, let $q \geq 0$ an integer, and let $H$ be a broom. Then there is a non-decreasing polynomial $\phi : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that if $G$ is a graph, and $\chi(G) > \phi(\omega(G))$, and $G$ contains no $(\psi, q)$-scattering, then $G$ [contains] a $\sigma$-nondominating copy of $H$.
rows.context_text: The authors remark that Conjecture 2.3 is 'an appealing possible strengthening of 2.2 that we could not prove.' Theorem 2.2 (which is proved) requires $H$ to be a broom and $J$ a path, finding both; Conjecture 2.3 would give a $\sigma$-nondominating copy of a broom alone without needing a path. This would extend the self-isolating property (proved for paths as Corollary 2.4) to brooms.
rows.source_text: Let $\psi, \sigma : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ be non-decreasing polynomials, let $q \geq 0$ an integer, and let $H$ be a broom. Then there is a non-decreasing polynomial $\phi : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that if $G$ is a graph, and $\chi(G) > \phi(\omega(G))$, and $G$ contains no $(\psi, q)$-scattering, then $G$ [contains] a $\sigma$-nondominating copy of $H$.
rows.class_notes: Technical strengthening toward polynomial Gyarfas-Sumner; (psi,q)-scattering and sigma-nondominating are paper-local.

rows.title: Conjecture 3
rows.paper_title: Exact Matching in Graphs of Bounded Independence Number
rows.statement_text: The problem of counting perfect matchings in input graphs of independence number $2$ is $\#P$-complete.
rows.context_text: The authors note that for all previously known graph classes on which EM is tractable (planar graphs, $K_{3,3}$-minor-free graphs, complete and complete bipartite graphs), counting perfect matchings is also polynomial. Their new results for graphs of independence number 2 break this alignment, since no polynomial counting scheme is known for this class and the authors believe counting is hard.
rows.source_text: The problem of counting perfect matchings in input graphs of independence number $2$ is $\#P$-complete.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 3 from arXiv:2202.11988, asserting that counting perfect matchings in graphs of independence number at most 2 is #P-complete, was resolved by El Maalouly and Wang in arXiv:2210.15014 (October 2022). Their paper proves #P-completeness for both general graphs with independence number ≤ 2 and bipartite graphs with bipartite independence number ≤ 2, via reductions from counting perfect matchings in bipartite graphs using graph-theoretic constructions.
rows.class_notes: #P-completeness of counting perfect matchings at independence number 2; solved, needs complexity foundation.

rows.title: Question 1.4
rows.paper_title: Heroes in oriented complete multipartite graphs
rows.statement_text: Is $\Delta(1,2,2)$ a hero in oriented complete multipartite graphs?
rows.context_text: The paper characterizes heroes in oriented complete multipartite graphs up to the status of $\Delta(1,2,2)$. If the answer is no, heroes in oriented complete multipartite graphs are precisely those described in Theorem 1.3; if the answer is yes, a different complete characterization involving $\Delta(1,2,2)$ holds.
rows.source_text: Is $\Delta(1,2,2)$ a hero in oriented complete multipartite graphs?
rows.status_semantics: Question 1.4 is answered negatively: Δ(1,2,2) is not a hero in oriented complete multipartite graphs. Remark 1.5 of the revised paper (v2, December 2023; published Journal of Graph Theory 105(4):652–669, 2024) records that Bartosz Walczak proved non-interlaced ordered graphs have unbounded chromatic number, which together with Theorem 4.2 of the paper implies Δ(1,2,2) is not a hero. Consequently, heroes in oriented complete multipartite graphs are precisely those characterised in Theorem 1.3.
rows.class_notes: 'Hero' (bounded dichromatic number in H-free members) and Delta(1,2,2) are standard in the hero literature; disproved, so right-polarity target.

rows.title: Question 5.4
rows.paper_title: Heroes in oriented complete multipartite graphs
rows.statement_text: Let $H$ and $F$ be digraphs such that $\Delta(1,1,H)$ is a hero in $Forb_{ind}(F)$ and $H$ is a hero in $Forb_{ind}(K_{1}+F)$. Then $\Delta(1,1,H)$ is a hero in $Forb_{ind}(K_{1}+F)$.
rows.context_text: This question asks whether a compositional hero property transfers across the $K_1 + F$ operation. It is motivated by the observation that knowing heroes in $Forb_{ind}(\bar{K}_t)$ does not straightforwardly help in understanding heroes in $Forb_{ind}(\bar{K}_{t+1})$, even though such a transfer would be natural.
rows.source_text: Let $H$ and $F$ be digraphs such that $\Delta(1,1,H)$ is a hero in $Forb_{ind}(F)$ and $H$ is a hero in $Forb_{ind}(K_{1}+F)$. Then $\Delta(1,1,H)$ is a hero in $Forb_{ind}(K_{1}+F)$.
rows.class_notes: Compositional hero transfer across K_1+F; hero/Forb_ind/join vocabulary standard in this literature though the join convention for digraphs needs fixing carefully.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.5
rows.paper_title: Pure pairs. X. Tournaments and the strong Erdos-Hajnal property
rows.statement_text: A tournament has the strong EH-property if and only if it admits a numbering for which the backedge graph is a forest.
rows.context_text: The paper proves (Theorem 1.4) that the forward direction holds: every tournament with the strong EH-property admits a numbering for which the backedge graph is a forest. The authors initially thought the converse unlikely but failed to find a counterexample. This would be a tournament analogue of Theorem 1.3, which characterises graphs with the strong EH-property as those for which one of $H$ or $\bar{H}$ is a forest.
rows.source_text: A tournament has the strong EH-property if and only if it admits a numbering for which the backedge graph is a forest.
rows.class_notes: Needs strong-EH-property primitive (linear pure pairs in tournaments) plus backedge-graph-of-numbering definition; both buildable.

rows.title: Informal question: can the D5-freeness hypothesis in Theorem 1.6 be dropped?
rows.paper_title: Pure pairs. X. Tournaments and the strong Erdos-Hajnal property
rows.statement_text: Perhaps the second sentence in 1.6 (the condition that $H$ is $D_5$-free) can be omitted, but that remains open.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.6 states that if a tournament $H$ admits a numbering $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ whose backedge graph $B$ has at most three edges, and $H$ is $D_5$-free, then $H$ has the strong EH-property. The $D_5$-freeness condition excludes exactly the case where the three backedges form the unique three-edge backedge graph of $D_5$; the authors leave open whether this exclusion is truly necessary.
rows.source_text: Perhaps the second sentence in 1.6 (the condition that $H$ is $D_5$-free) can be omitted, but that remains open.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2202.13977 — Informal question: can the D5-freeness hypothesis in Theorem 1.6 be dropped?
rows.class_notes: Context fully reconstructs the proposition: every tournament with a numbering whose backedge graph has at most 3 edges has the strong EH-property (D5-freeness dropped).

rows.title: Girth Conjecture (strengthening of Theorem 3)
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs of induced subgraphs of large chromatic number
rows.statement_text: For every triangle-free graph $F$, there is a constant $c_F$ and graphs $G$ of arbitrarily large chromatic number and the same girth as $F$ such that every $F$-free induced subgraph of $G$ is $c_F$-colourable.
rows.context_text: Theorem 3 guarantees, for triangle-free $F$, a triangle-free $G$, but does not control the girth of $G$. The authors ask whether $G$ can be taken to have the same girth as $F$, conjecture that this is so, but are unable to prove it. As a partial result they prove Theorem 4, the weaker analogue where girth is replaced by odd girth.
rows.source_text: For every triangle-free graph $F$, there is a constant $c_F$ and graphs $G$ of arbitrarily large chromatic number and the same girth as $F$ such that every $F$-free induced subgraph of $G$ is $c_F$-colourable.
rows.class_notes: Standard vocabulary throughout (girth, chromatic number, F-free induced subgraphs); ∀F ∃c_F ∀k ∃G shape.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.6
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions IV. (Even hole, diamond, pyramid)-free graphs
rows.statement_text: For all $t > 0$ there exists $c_t \geq 0$ such that $\mathrm{tw}(G) \leq c_t$ for every $G \in \mathcal{C}^*_t$.
rows.context_text: Here $\mathcal{C}^*_t$ denotes the class of $(C_4, \text{diamond}, \text{theta}, \text{prism}, \text{even wheel}, K_t)$-free graphs — a relaxation of $\mathcal{C}_t$ in which pyramids are no longer excluded. The authors pose this as a slight generalization of Sintiari–Trotignon's Conjecture 1.5, noting that Theorem 1.4 (the main result of the paper) would serve as the base case if the conjecture could be proved by the same techniques.
rows.source_text: For all $t > 0$ there exists $c_t \geq 0$ such that $\mathrm{tw}(G) \leq c_t$ for every $G \in \mathcal{C}^*_t$.
rows.class_notes: Needs theta/prism/even-wheel induced-subgraph primitives (standard in even-hole literature, buildable); generalizes Sintiari-Trotignon Conjecture 1.5.

rows.title: Open Question (unit segment graphs delineation)
rows.paper_title: Twin-width VIII: delineation and win-wins
rows.statement_text: Are unit segment graphs delineated (by twin-width)?
rows.context_text: The paper establishes that axis-parallel $H_t$-free unit segment graphs have bounded twin-width, while axis-parallel $H_4$-free two-lengthed segment graphs have unbounded twin-width, and that (triangle-free) pure axis-parallel unit segment graphs have unbounded twin-width. This places unit segment graphs at the frontier between delineated and non-delineated intersection graph classes, and the authors explicitly leave their delineation status as an open question.
rows.source_text: Are unit segment graphs delineated (by twin-width)?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2204.00722 — Open Question (unit segment graphs delineation)
rows.status_semantics: The open question of whether unit segment graphs are delineated (by twin-width) remains unresolved for the general class, but significant partial progress has been made. Geniet, Kim, and Meijer (arXiv:2512.21896, 2025) prove that delineation holds for intersection graphs of non-degenerate axis-parallel unit segment graphs, while also showing that delineation fails for visibility graphs of 1.5D terrains; additionally the paper's body discusses non-delineation for more general unit segment variants (degenerate axis-parallel and arbitrary-slope unit segments). The non-degenerate axis-parallel subcase is thus settled positively, but the status of the full class of unit segment graphs — as posed in arXiv:2204.00722 — remains unresolved or at best partially answered negatively for broader subclasses.
rows.class_notes: 'Delineated' is a paper-defined notion (Twin-width VIII) not defined in the record; also needs geometric unit-segment-graph layer.

rows.title: Conjecture on r-free digraphs with r > 2n/3
rows.paper_title: Extremal results on feedback arc sets in digraphs
rows.statement_text: All $r$-free digraphs on $n$ vertices with $r > 2n/3$ are at most one edge away from being acyclic.
rows.context_text: The authors first construct a family of $r$-free digraphs $G$ on $n$ vertices with $\beta(G) \geq n^2/(r+1)^2$, implying that if every $r$-free digraph satisfies $\beta(G) = O(1)$ then $r = \Omega(n)$. In the converse direction they pose this conjecture, noting tightness: for $n$ a multiple of $3$ there exists an $(r-1)$-free digraph $G$ on $n$ vertices with $r = 2n/3$ and $\beta(G) = 2$.
rows.source_text: All $r$-free digraphs on $n$ vertices with $r > 2n/3$ are at most one edge away from being acyclic.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2204.01938 — Conjecture on r-free digraphs with r > 2n/3
rows.status_semantics: A January 2026 MIT PRIMES research paper by Kai Lum (mentored by Nitya Mani, one of the conjecture's authors) proves that β(G) ≤ 1 for all r-free digraphs on n vertices with r > ⌊2n/3⌋, directly resolving the conjecture. The source paper (arXiv:2204.01938) was subsequently published in Random Structures & Algorithms (2024). No peer-reviewed journal or arXiv paper proving this conjecture was found; the only identified proof is in an unreviewed PRIMES preprint whose PDF could not be fully read, hence medium confidence.
rows.class_notes: 'r-free digraph' is paper-local and not defined in context; once recovered (no directed cycle of length <= r) the statement beta(G)<=1 is clean. Solved.

rows.title: Suspected directed surplus of a random B-free orientation (Remark 1.6)
rows.paper_title: Extremal results on feedback arc sets in digraphs
rows.statement_text: A random orientation of a $\mathcal{B}$-free undirected graph on $n$ vertices with $m = \mathrm{ex}(n, \mathcal{B})$ edges likely satisfies $\beta(G) = m/2 - \Omega(\sqrt{mn})$.
rows.context_text: In Remark 1.6 the authors note that the proof of Theorem 1.5 uses the auxiliary condition $\mathrm{ex}(n,\mathcal{B}) = \Theta(n^{2-\varepsilon(\mathcal{B})})$ only for convenience, and that without it a random $\mathcal{B}$-free orientation is suspected to already achieve the stated directed-surplus bound.
rows.source_text: A random orientation of a $\mathcal{B}$-free undirected graph on $n$ vertices with $m = \mathrm{ex}(n, \mathcal{B})$ edges likely satisfies $\beta(G) = m/2 - \Omega(\sqrt{mn})$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2204.01938 — Suspected directed surplus of a random B-free orientation (Remark 1.6)
rows.class_notes: Depends on the family B and setup of Theorem 1.5 not defined in the record; 'likely satisfies' w.h.p. framing plus Omega asymptotics.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.3
rows.paper_title: Bipartite graphs with no $K_6$ minor
rows.statement_text: Every non-null 6-regular graph has a $K_6$ minor.
rows.context_text: The authors have found $K_6$-minor-free graphs with minimum degree six and maximum degree at most nine, and $K_6$-minor-free graphs with minimum degree five, maximum degree seven, and average degree arbitrarily close to $98/15$. Conjecture 1.3 asks whether 6-regularity alone forces a $K_6$ minor.
rows.source_text: Every non-null 6-regular graph has a $K_6$ minor.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture: min degree $\geq 6$, max degree $\leq 8$ forces $K_6$ minor
rows.paper_title: Bipartite graphs with no $K_6$ minor
rows.statement_text: Every non-null graph with minimum degree at least six and maximum degree at most eight has a $K_6$ minor.
rows.context_text: Stated parenthetically immediately after Conjecture 1.3: ``Indeed, as far as we know, every non-null graph with minimum degree at least six and maximum degree at most eight has a $K_6$ minor.'' It is a stronger open belief than Conjecture 1.3.
rows.source_text: Every non-null graph with minimum degree at least six and maximum degree at most eight has a $K_6$ minor.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2204.10119 — Informal Conjecture: min degree $\geq 6$, max degree $\leq 8$ forces $K_6$ minor
rows.class_notes: Strengthening of axv_2204_10119_00 (Conjecture 1.3), noted parenthetically by the authors.

rows.title: Open Question: minimum degree five in Theorem 1.6
rows.paper_title: Bipartite graphs with no $K_6$ minor
rows.statement_text: We do not know whether ``six'' can be replaced by ``five'' in 1.6 (every non-null bipartite graph with minimum degree at least six has a $K_6$ minor).
rows.context_text: Immediately after stating Theorem 1.6, the authors note that the analogous statement for minimum degree five in bipartite graphs is open. They also remark that minimum degree is more difficult than average degree to handle inductively.
rows.source_text: We do not know whether ``six'' can be replaced by ``five'' in 1.6 (every non-null bipartite graph with minimum degree at least six has a $K_6$ minor).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2204.10119 — Open Question: minimum degree five in Theorem 1.6
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no: every non-null bipartite graph with min degree >= 5 has a K6 minor.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture: average degree $\geq 5$ and max degree $\leq 6$ forces $K_5$ minor in bipartite graphs
rows.paper_title: Bipartite graphs with no $K_6$ minor
rows.statement_text: Perhaps average degree at least five and maximum degree at most six will guarantee a $K_5$ minor in a bipartite graph.
rows.context_text: After observing that there exist $n$-vertex bipartite graphs with average degree at least four and maximum degree at most five that have no $K_5$ minor, the authors suggest this stronger degree bound might guarantee a $K_5$ minor, but explicitly state they have not verified it.
rows.source_text: Perhaps average degree at least five and maximum degree at most six will guarantee a $K_5$ minor in a bipartite graph.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2204.10119 — Informal Conjecture: average degree $\geq 5$ and max degree $\leq 6$ forces $K_5$ minor in bipartite graphs
rows.class_notes: Hedged ('perhaps') but a single precise proposition; average degree is rational so still clean finite vocabulary.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (uniform twin-width strictly stronger than twin-width)
rows.paper_title: Twin-width VII: groups
rows.statement_text: Uniform twin-width is strictly stronger than twin-width, i.e. there are groups with finite twin-width and infinite uniform twin-width.
rows.context_text: Uniform twin-width requires a uniform bound on the permutation width of the action of every group element, whereas finite twin-width only requires each action to have finite width individually. Having finite uniform twin-width implies finite twin-width, but the converse is not established. The group of finitely supported permutations on $\mathbb{Z}$ is proposed as a candidate counterexample.
rows.source_text: Uniform twin-width is strictly stronger than twin-width, i.e. there are groups with finite twin-width and infinite uniform twin-width.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2204.12330 — Informal Conjecture (uniform twin-width strictly stronger than twin-width)
rows.class_notes: Twin-width and uniform twin-width of infinite groups; 'permutation width of the action' is paper-local and not fully defined in the record.

rows.title: Question (explicit group with infinite twin-width)
rows.paper_title: Twin-width VII: groups
rows.statement_text: Can we find a more explicit construction of a group with infinite twin-width, or one with a direct proof that twin-width is infinite?
rows.context_text: The group constructed in Theorem 1.1 relies on probabilistic methods and starts from a sequence of bounded degree graphs with unbounded twin-width; it does not yield an explicit bounded degree graph with unbounded twin-width. A more explicit or combinatorially direct construction would significantly advance understanding of twin-width for bounded degree graphs.
rows.source_text: Can we find a more explicit construction of a group with infinite twin-width, or one with a direct proof that twin-width is infinite?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2204.12330 — Question (explicit group with infinite twin-width)
rows.defer_reason: Asks for a 'more explicit construction' or a 'direct proof' — a meta request, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Open Problem (separating twin-width and queue number on sparse graphs)
rows.paper_title: Twin-width VII: groups
rows.statement_text: Separating twin-width and queue number on sparse graphs is an open problem.
rows.context_text: Finite queue number implies finite twin-width, so the two parameters are not equivalent in general. However, no example of a group (or sparse graph) with finite twin-width and infinite queue number is known, and the same gap exists for the corresponding uniform variants. Many results of the paper hold simultaneously for both parameters, making a separation elusive.
rows.source_text: Separating twin-width and queue number on sparse graphs is an open problem.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2204.12330 — Open Problem (separating twin-width and queue number on sparse graphs)
rows.defer_reason: 'Separating twin-width and queue number on sparse graphs' is a separation task with imprecise 'sparse'; not phrased as a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Could be recast as existence of a bounded-degree class with bounded twin-width and unbounded queue number.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.6
rows.paper_title: 11/4-colorability of subcubic triangle-free graphs
rows.statement_text: Every subcubic triangle-free graph avoiding $F^{(1)}_{14}$, $F^{(2)}_{14}$, $F_{11}$, and $F_{22}$ as subgraphs is fractionally $19/7$-colorable.
rows.context_text: The authors pose this as an intermediate conjecture between their main result (Theorem 1.4, giving $11/4$-colorability when only $F^{(1)}_{14}$ and $F^{(2)}_{14}$ are excluded) and Conjecture 1.3 (giving $8/3$-colorability when six graphs are excluded). It is noted that $F_{11}$ and $F_{22}$ both have fractional chromatic number exactly $11/4$, while $F^{(1)}_{19}$ and $F^{(2)}_{19}$ have fractional chromatic number $19/7$, motivating this intermediate bound.
rows.source_text: Every subcubic triangle-free graph avoiding $F^{(1)}_{14}$, $F^{(2)}_{14}$, $F_{11}$, and $F_{22}$ as subgraphs is fractionally $19/7$-colorable.
rows.class_notes: The forbidden graphs F14^(1), F14^(2), F11, F22 are paper-specific named graphs not defined in the record; fractional 19/7-colorability itself is bounded/rational.

rows.title: Problem 1.6
rows.paper_title: On density of $Z_3$-flow-critical graphs
rows.statement_text: What is the smallest constant $\alpha$ such that every $Z_3$-flow-critical graph $G$ satisfies $|E(G)| \leq \frac{5}{2}|V(G)| + \alpha g(G) + O(1)$?
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.5 establishes $\alpha \leq 5/2$, while the example of $K^+_{3,n-3}$ (which has Euler genus $\lceil\frac{n-5}{2}\rceil$) shows $\alpha \geq 1$, giving $1 \leq \alpha \leq 5/2$. The authors remark that the dependence on genus in Theorem 1.5 is likely not optimal.
rows.source_text: What is the smallest constant $\alpha$ such that every $Z_3$-flow-critical graph $G$ satisfies $|E(G)| \leq \frac{5}{2}|V(G)| + \alpha g(G) + O(1)$?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the smallest constant alpha' — asks for an optimal value (with O(1) slack), not a single proposition.

rows.title: Belief on 4-vertex-critical arrangement graphs
rows.paper_title: Coloring circle arrangements: New $4$-chromatic planar graphs
rows.statement_text: We believe that the corona construction results in infinitely many 4-vertex-critical arrangement graphs.
rows.context_text: The paper's corona construction maps $\triangle$-saturated arrangements with a pentagonal face to arrangements with 4-chromatic 4-regular arrangement graphs, yielding an infinite family. The authors believe these are all vertex-critical, though this is not proved.
rows.source_text: We believe that the corona construction results in infinitely many 4-vertex-critical arrangement graphs.
rows.class_notes: Relies on the paper's corona construction and circle-arrangement graph definitions, none defined in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture on Sufficiency of Conditions E, R, T for Generic Tuples
rows.paper_title: Counting Dope Matrices
rows.statement_text: When $\Lambda$ is generic, Conditions E, R, and T are necessary and sufficient for an $m \times (n+1)$ matrix to be the multiplicity matrix of some polynomial $P$ with respect to $\Lambda$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1 of the paper establishes that Conditions E, R, T are necessary and sufficient for a $2 \times (n+1)$ matrix to be a multiplicity matrix. The authors note that for $m > 2$ these three conditions are not sufficient in general (almost all matrices satisfying E, R, C for large $m = n$ are not multiplicity matrices), but conjecture sufficiency is restored when $\Lambda$ is generic. Further discussion is deferred to Section 8.
rows.source_text: When $\Lambda$ is generic, Conditions E, R, and T are necessary and sufficient for an $m \times (n+1)$ matrix to be the multiplicity matrix of some polynomial $P$ with respect to $\Lambda$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2205.09302 — Conjecture on Sufficiency of Conditions E, R, T for Generic Tuples
rows.status_semantics: Ankit Bisain (arXiv:2209.13811, September 2022; published BLMS 2024) resolves the conjecture by classifying dope matrices when the entries of Λ are algebraically independent (i.e., generic), establishing that Conditions E, R, T are necessary and sufficient for an m×(n+1) matrix to be the multiplicity matrix of a polynomial with respect to a generic tuple. The paper also provides asymptotic upper and lower bounds on the total number of m×(n+1) dope matrices.
rows.defer_reason: Multiplicity matrices of polynomials with respect to a tuple Lambda — polynomial algebra, not graph/hypergraph theory. Solved.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.3
rows.paper_title: Square roots of nearly planar graphs
rows.statement_text: The $\mathcal{H}$-square-root problem is NP-complete for the class $\mathcal{H}$ of planar graphs.
rows.context_text: The authors prove NP-completeness of the H-square-root problem for the class of 6-apex graphs (a proper minor-closed class), and note that 6-apex graphs lie at the bottom of the bounded-expansion hierarchy. They ask whether apex vertices are truly necessary or whether planarity alone already makes the problem hard, and state they believe the latter is the case.
rows.source_text: The $\mathcal{H}$-square-root problem is NP-complete for the class $\mathcal{H}$ of planar graphs.
rows.class_notes: NP-completeness of the planar-square-root recognition problem; precise but requires the complexity foundation.

rows.title: Informal conjecture on improving the $g(t)$ lower bound
rows.paper_title: Dense minors of graphs with independence number two
rows.statement_text: The factor $1/4$ in $g(t) \geq t(t+2)/8$ can likely be significantly improved. However it appears to be quite challenging, for example, to show that every graph with chromatic number at least $t$ contains a minor on $t$ vertices with at most one tenth of all possible edges missing.
rows.context_text: The authors define $g(t)$ as the maximum number of edges guaranteed in a $t$-vertex minor of any graph with chromatic number at least $t$. Mader's theorem yields $g(t) \geq t(t+2)/8$; the authors believe this is far from the true value, posing a density of $9/10$ as an example of a seemingly hard intermediate target for general graphs.
rows.source_text: The factor $1/4$ in $g(t) \geq t(t+2)/8$ can likely be significantly improved. However it appears to be quite challenging, for example, to show that every graph with chromatic number at least $t$ contains a minor on $t$ vertices with at most one tenth of all possible edges missing.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2206.00186 — Informal conjecture on improving the $g(t)$ lower bound
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that the factor $1/4$ in $g(t) \geq t(t+2)/8$ can be significantly improved has seen partial progress. Hendrey, Norin, Steiner, and Turcotte (arXiv:2307.01184, J. Graph Theory 2025) proved that graphs of average degree at least $t-1$ contain a $t$-vertex minor with at least $(\sqrt{2}-1-o(1))\binom{t}{2}$ edges — improving the density from $1/4$ to approximately $0.414$ — with an upper bound of $3/4+o(1)$. Since every graph with chromatic number $\geq t$ contains a $t$-critical subgraph of minimum degree $\geq t-1$, this result improves $g(t)$ accordingly. The conjecture's harder target of density $9/10$ (at most one tenth of edges missing) remains open.
rows.defer_reason: 'The factor 1/4 can likely be significantly improved' — improve-the-bound; the 9/10-density example is posed as an illustration of difficulty, not asserted as a conjecture.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.3
rows.paper_title: Sparse graphs with bounded induced cycle packing number have logarithmic treewidth
rows.statement_text: Maximum Independent Set is solvable in polynomial time in $\mathcal{O}_{k}$-free graphs.
rows.context_text: The paper proves that sparse $\mathcal{O}_k$-free graphs have logarithmic treewidth, making Maximum Independent Set polynomial in the sparse case (Corollary 1.2). A quasipolynomial algorithm is also given for general $\mathcal{O}_k$-free graphs, stopping just short of resolving this conjecture in full generality.
rows.source_text: Maximum Independent Set is solvable in polynomial time in $\mathcal{O}_{k}$-free graphs.
rows.class_notes: O_k-free = induced cycle packing number < k, adequately determined by title/context; poly-time MIS claim needs the computation model.

rows.title: Open Question: bounded twin-width for sparse $\mathcal{O}_k$-free graphs
rows.paper_title: Sparse graphs with bounded induced cycle packing number have logarithmic treewidth
rows.statement_text: Is there a function $f:\mathbb{N}\times\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ such that every $\mathcal{O}_{k}$-free graph that does not contain $K_{t,t}$ as a subgraph has twin-width at most $f(t,k)$?
rows.context_text: The paper shows that sparse $\mathcal{O}_k$-free graphs have logarithmic treewidth, but leaves open whether their twin-width is also bounded as a function of $t$ and $k$. The authors note that $\mathcal{O}_2$-free graphs without $K_{3,3}$ already yield a new family of counterexamples to bounded treewidth from forbidden induced subgraphs alone.
rows.source_text: Is there a function $f:\mathbb{N}\times\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ such that every $\mathcal{O}_{k}$-free graph that does not contain $K_{t,t}$ as a subgraph has twin-width at most $f(t,k)$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2206.00594 — Open Question: bounded twin-width for sparse $\mathcal{O}_k$-free graphs
rows.class_notes: Needs the twin-width primitive; otherwise a clean bounded-parameter statement over O_k-free K_{t,t}-subgraph-free graphs.

rows.title: Informal conjecture: MIS tractable in bounded-iocp graphs
rows.paper_title: Sparse graphs with bounded induced cycle packing number have logarithmic treewidth
rows.statement_text: Maximum Independent Set could be tractable in graphs with bounded $\mathrm{iocp}$ (induced odd cycle packing number).
rows.context_text: The paper establishes tractability of MIS for graphs with bounded induced cycle packing number ($\mathrm{icp}$, i.e., $\mathcal{O}_k$-free graphs) in the sparse case, and quasipolynomial time in general. The authors remark that extending this to bounded $\mathrm{iocp}$ would be a surprising and formidable generalisation of Conjecture 1.3 and of the known polynomial-time result for bounded $\mathrm{ocp}$ (odd cycle packing number).
rows.source_text: Maximum Independent Set could be tractable in graphs with bounded $\mathrm{iocp}$ (induced odd cycle packing number).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2206.00594 — Informal conjecture: MIS tractable in bounded-iocp graphs
rows.class_notes: Hedged ('could be tractable') but a single yes/no: MIS polynomial on bounded induced-odd-cycle-packing graphs; generalizes axv_2206_00594_00.

rows.title: Open problem on happy triples for $l < k/2$
rows.paper_title: Improved bounds for the triangle case of Aharoni's rainbow generalization of the Caccetta-Häggkvist conjecture
rows.statement_text: It would be interesting to consider the best upper bound on the number of happy triples in a graph with $k$ edges and maximum degree at most $l$, for the regime when $l < k/2$.
rows.context_text: Lemma 2 establishes a tight bound on happy triples when $l \geq k/2$, achieved by specific extremal graphs. The authors note that their algorithmic/dynamic-programming approach gives little insight into the $l < k/2$ regime; for example, for $l=3$ and $k=3t$ the extremal graph output is $K_{t,3}$, which does not actually have maximum degree 3 for $t \geq 4$.
rows.source_text: It would be interesting to consider the best upper bound on the number of happy triples in a graph with $k$ edges and maximum degree at most $l$, for the regime when $l < k/2$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2206.10733 — Open problem on happy triples for $l < k/2$
rows.defer_reason: 'It would be interesting to consider the best upper bound' in the l < k/2 regime — asks for an unknown extremal value, not a proposition.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture on R(n;r,s) vs R'(n;r,s) near Turán density
rows.paper_title: Set-coloring Ramsey numbers via codes
rows.statement_text: We clearly have $R(n; r, s) \geq R'(n; r, s)$ and we believe that this estimate should be close to an equality when $s/r$ is close to the Turán density $1 - \frac{1}{n-1}$.
rows.context_text: The authors introduce the variant $R'(n;r,s)$, defined as the minimum $N$ such that every $(r,s)$-coloring of $K_N$ yields a color class with chromatic number at least $n$. They observe that $R(n;r,s) \geq R'(n;r,s)$ always holds, and that for the special case $s = r-1$ near-equality is confirmed by prior work of Alon et al. [1]. The informal belief is stated in Section 4 while studying the fixed-$n$ regime.
rows.source_text: We clearly have $R(n; r, s) \geq R'(n; r, s)$ and we believe that this estimate should be close to an equality when $s/r$ is close to the Turán density $1 - \frac{1}{n-1}$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2206.11371 — Informal Conjecture on R(n;r,s) vs R'(n;r,s) near Turán density
rows.status_semantics: The informal conjecture that R(n;r,s) ≈ R'(n;r,s) when s/r is near the Turán density 1−1/(n−1) has not been explicitly resolved, but the same group of authors published a follow-up (arXiv:2305.14132) that studies R(n;r,s) precisely in the intermediate range where s/r is close to 1−1/(n−1) by connecting it to the maximum size of error-correcting codes near the zero-rate threshold — the same coding-theoretic quantity that defines R'. This provides partial progress towards the belief, but a precise comparison R(n;r,s) ≈ R'(n;r,s) has not been confirmed in the verified literature.
rows.defer_reason: 'Should be close to an equality when s/r is close to the Turan density' — 'close to' is not quantified; no single proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 6.2
rows.paper_title: Improved bounds for 1-independent percolation on $\mathbb{Z}^n$
rows.statement_text: For $p>0.55$, among models in $\mathcal{D}_{\geq p}(Q_{3})$ the signs model minimises the probability that $Q_{3}$ is connected.
rows.context_text: The signs model on $Q_3$ assigns each vertex independently '$+$' with probability $\theta$ and '$-$' otherwise, with an edge open iff both endpoints share the same sign (so each edge is open with probability $p=\theta^2+(1-\theta)^2$). Computational evidence strongly suggests this model minimises the connectivity probability of $Q_3$ for $p>0.55$; for smaller $p$ other models perform worse.
rows.source_text: For $p>0.55$, among models in $\mathcal{D}_{\geq p}(Q_{3})$ the signs model minimises the probability that $Q_{3}$ is connected.
rows.class_notes: Finite object (Q_3) but quantifies over all 1-independent measures with real edge probabilities >= p; 1-independence buildable, real-valued so rcfType idiom.

rows.title: Problem 6.4
rows.paper_title: Improved bounds for 1-independent percolation on $\mathbb{Z}^n$
rows.statement_text: What is the value of $p_{\mathrm{giant}}$?
rows.context_text: The paper establishes $p_{\mathrm{giant}}\leq 0.5847$ (when each edge of the hypercube $Q_n$ is open with probability at least $0.5847$ and $n$ is large, a giant component exists with high probability). The best lower bound remains $1/2$. Falgas-Ravry and Pfenninger conjectured $p_{\mathrm{giant}}=1/2$, but the exact value is unknown.
rows.source_text: What is the value of $p_{\mathrm{giant}}$?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the value of p_giant?' — asks for an exact threshold value, not a proposition (the Falgas-Ravry–Pfenninger p_giant = 1/2 conjecture is a separate attributable statement).

rows.title: Conjecture 2
rows.paper_title: Coloring hypergraphs with excluded minors
rows.statement_text: For every integer $t\geq 2$ we have $h(t)=\left\lceil\frac{3}{2}(t-1)\right\rceil$. In other words, if a hypergraph $H$ does not contain $K_{t}$ as a minor, then $\chi(H)\leq\left\lceil\frac{3}{2}(t-1)\right\rceil$.
rows.context_text: The function $h(t)$ is the smallest integer such that every $K_t$-minor-free hypergraph is $h(t)$-colorable. The paper proves the lower bound $h(t)\geq\left\lceil\frac{3}{2}(t-1)\right\rceil$ by an explicit construction, and conjectures this lower bound is tight. The conjecture remains open even for $t=3$, where the best known upper bound is $4$.
rows.source_text: For every integer $t\geq 2$ we have $h(t)=\left\lceil\frac{3}{2}(t-1)\right\rceil$. In other words, if a hypergraph $H$ does not contain $K_{t}$ as a minor, then $\chi(H)\leq\left\lceil\frac{3}{2}(t-1)\right\rceil$.
rows.class_notes: Needs a hypergraph-minor primitive (paper's notion of K_t minor in hypergraphs); given that, a clean h(t)=ceil(3(t-1)/2) bound.

rows.title: Problem 1
rows.paper_title: Coloring hypergraphs with excluded minors
rows.statement_text: Is every $K_{3}$-minor-free hypergraph $3$-colorable?
rows.context_text: Conjecture 2 predicts $h(3)=3$, but the best known upper bound for the chromatic number of $K_3$-minor-free hypergraphs is $4$ (from Corollary 2 of the paper). Problem 1 isolates the smallest open case of Conjecture 2.
rows.source_text: Is every $K_{3}$-minor-free hypergraph $3$-colorable?
rows.class_notes: Smallest open case (t=3) of axv_2206_13635_00; same hypergraph-minor primitive needed.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.5
rows.paper_title: Ramsey multiplicity and the Turán coloring
rows.statement_text: Every sufficiently large integer is polite.
rows.context_text: Conjecture 1.5 appears early in the paper with no surrounding context recovered. The term 'polite' is likely a graph-theoretic notion defined in the paper (possibly related to Ramsey multiplicity or the Turán coloring extremality), but the context was not captured in extraction.
rows.source_text: Every sufficiently large integer is polite.
rows.class_notes: 'Polite' is undefined in the record (extraction lost the context); in Ramsey-multiplicity setting it is an asymptotic notion, hence B3 pending recovery.

rows.title: Conjecture 5.3
rows.paper_title: Ramsey multiplicity and the Turán coloring
rows.statement_text: There exist a mixed blowup coloring of $K_{r(k)-1}[2]$ for infinitely many $k$.
rows.context_text: The conjecture arises from Lemma 5.2, which reduces finding a mixed blowup coloring to exhibiting two Ramsey colorings of $K_{r(k)-1}$ that differ on a single vertex. The authors note they cannot establish this for any $k$, partly because $r(k)$ is only known for $k=3,4$, and for both values the Ramsey coloring of $K_{r(k)-1}$ is unique with all vertices having the same red and blue degree.
rows.source_text: There exist a mixed blowup coloring of $K_{r(k)-1}[2]$ for infinitely many $k$.
rows.class_notes: 'Mixed blowup coloring' is a paper-local notion not defined in the record; involves Ramsey numbers r(k) and 2-blowups, finite once recovered.

rows.title: Conjecture 5.4
rows.paper_title: Ramsey multiplicity and the Turán coloring
rows.statement_text: If $k\geq 4$ and $t$ is sufficiently large in terms of $k$, then any $(k,t)$-generalized lollipop is a two-color bonbon.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.2 proves that adding $t\geq(1000kh)^{10}h^{10k}$ pendant edges to a $k$-critical graph $H_0$ with $h$ vertices yields a bonbon, but the proof requires $t$ at least exponentially large in $k$. Moreover, if $H_0=K_k$ then some lower bound on $t$ is necessary since adding $o(k^2/\log k)$ pendant edges to $K_k$ does not produce a bonbon. The conjecture proposes that sufficiently many pendant edges always suffice for any $k$-critical base graph.
rows.source_text: If $k\geq 4$ and $t$ is sufficiently large in terms of $k$, then any $(k,t)$-generalized lollipop is a two-color bonbon.
rows.class_notes: '(k,t)-generalized lollipop' and 'two-color bonbon' are paper-local; bonbon is an asymptotic Ramsey-multiplicity notion.

rows.title: Informal conjecture on the actual diameter
rows.paper_title: Small diameters and generators for arithmetic lattices in $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{R})$ and certain Ramanujan graphs
rows.statement_text: The bound (1.4) for the almost diameter is sharp and one may speculate whether the actual diameter is around the same length, i.e.\ $(2+o(1))\log 3V_\Gamma$.
rows.context_text: Having proved in Theorem 3 that for almost every $z\in\mathbb{H}$ one has $\min_{\gamma\in\Gamma}d(\gamma z,w)\leq(2+o(1))\log 3V_\Gamma$, the authors remark that this almost-diameter bound is sharp and speculate that the true diameter of the hyperbolic surface $\Gamma\backslash\mathbb{H}$ is of the same order. They note that this would imply the co-compact arithmetic lattice $\Gamma$ is generated by its elements of Frobenius norm $O_\varepsilon(V_\Gamma^{1+\varepsilon})$, bringing it into line with Theorem 1.
rows.source_text: The bound (1.4) for the almost diameter is sharp and one may speculate whether the actual diameter is around the same length, i.e.\ $(2+o(1))\log 3V_\Gamma$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2207.12684 — Informal conjecture on the actual diameter
rows.defer_reason: Diameter of hyperbolic surfaces / arithmetic lattices in SL2(R), not graph theory despite Ramanujan-graph paper title
rows.class_notes: Also vague ('one may speculate whether').

rows.title: Informal Conjecture on Property (*) for d = o(n/log n)
rows.paper_title: On random irregular subgraphs
rows.statement_text: The irregular random subgraph $H = H(G)$ satisfies Property $(*)$ — that is, $m(H,k) = (1+o(1))n/(d+1)$ for all $0 \leq k \leq d$ — with high probability for all $d = o(n/\log n)$.
rows.context_text: After proving Theorem 1.3 for $d = o(n/(\log n)^{12})$, the authors conjecture the range extends to $d = o(n/\log n)$. They also remark that for $d = \omega(n/\log n)$ the random subgraph model likely fails to satisfy Property $(*)$, and that new ideas would be required to establish Conjecture 1.2 in the full range.
rows.source_text: The irregular random subgraph $H = H(G)$ satisfies Property $(*)$ — that is, $m(H,k) = (1+o(1))n/(d+1)$ for all $0 \leq k \leq d$ — with high probability for all $d = o(n/\log n)$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2207.13651 — Informal Conjecture on Property (*) for d = o(n/log n)
rows.class_notes: Random subgraph model H(G) and m(H,k) are paper-local and not defined in the record.

rows.title: Problem 3
rows.paper_title: Perfect shuffling with fewer lazy transpositions
rows.statement_text: Does $U_t(n) = tn - \binom{t+1}{2}$?
rows.context_text: This generalises Problem 1 to the setting of shuffling $t$ distinguishable counters across $n$ positions using the fewest lazy transpositions. The upper bound $U_t(n)\leq tn-\binom{t+1}{2}$ is achieved by existing sweeping/divide-and-conquer constructions. When $t=n$ the problem reduces to Problem 1, which Theorem 2 answers negatively; the paper asks whether the trivial bound is tight for other values of $t$.
rows.source_text: Does $U_t(n) = tn - \binom{t+1}{2}$?
rows.status_semantics: Problem 3 asks whether U_t(n) = tn - C(t+1,2) holds for all t, where the upper bound is already established by sweeping/divide-and-conquer constructions and the t=n case is answered negatively by Theorem 2 of the source paper. A follow-up by Janzer, Johnson, and Leader (arXiv:2210.13286, October 2022) proves that U_2(n) = 2n-3, confirming the formula for t=2 and settling the corresponding conjecture of Groenland et al. Whether the formula holds for intermediate values of t remains open.
rows.defer_reason: Lazy-transposition shuffling networks for counters: permutation/sorting-network combinatorics, not graph theory

rows.title: Problem 1
rows.paper_title: Short reachability networks
rows.statement_text: What is the minimum number of transpositions needed in a $t$-reachable network? What if all transpositions are of the form $(1,\cdot)$?
rows.context_text: The authors determine the exact minimum for $t=2$ (Theorem 2) and give an upper bound of $(2+o_t(1))n$ transpositions for fixed $t\geq 3$, but the asymptotics remain open for large $t$. Lower bounds for star-transposition $t$-reachable networks are also given, but a gap between upper and lower bounds remains even in that restricted setting.
rows.source_text: What is the minimum number of transpositions needed in a $t$-reachable network? What if all transpositions are of the form $(1,\cdot)$?
rows.defer_reason: t-reachable transposition networks are permutation/sorting-network theory, not graph theory; also a vague 'what is the minimum' question

rows.title: Conjecture 1
rows.paper_title: Short reachability networks
rows.statement_text: For $n\geq 2$, the minimum number of lazy transpositions in a 2-uniformity network is $2n-3$.
rows.context_text: The authors construct a 2-uniformity network of length $2n-3$ using the sequence $(1,2,\tfrac{1}{2}),(1,3,\tfrac{2}{n}),(1,2,\tfrac{1}{2}),(1,4,\tfrac{2}{n-1}),\dots,(1,2,\tfrac{1}{2}),(1,n,\tfrac{2}{3}),(1,2,\tfrac{1}{2})$, and conjecture this is optimal. They remark that, if true, it would match nicely with selection networks.
rows.source_text: For $n\geq 2$, the minimum number of lazy transpositions in a 2-uniformity network is $2n-3$.
rows.defer_reason: 2-uniformity networks of lazy transpositions: randomized sorting-network combinatorics, not graph theory
rows.class_notes: Otherwise a precise exact-value conjecture (2n-3).

rows.title: Conjecture 2.1
rows.paper_title: The success probability in Levine's hat problem, and independent sets in graphs
rows.statement_text: $p_{\mathrm{intersecting}}(t)$ tends to $0$ as $t$ grows.
rows.context_text: This is a strengthening of Levine's Conjecture 1.1 in the setting where winning sets are intersecting families on $\{0,1\}^n$ rather than dictatorships. Since $p_{\mathrm{intersecting}}(t,n) \geq p_{\mathrm{dict}}(t,n)$, this conjecture is strictly stronger than Conjecture 1.1.
rows.source_text: $p_{\mathrm{intersecting}}(t)$ tends to $0$ as $t$ grows.
rows.defer_reason: Levine hat-game success probability over intersecting families on {0,1}^n: set systems/probability without graphs
rows.class_notes: Graph-theoretic equivalent appears as sibling row axv_2208_06858_02.

rows.title: Conjecture 2.2
rows.paper_title: The success probability in Levine's hat problem, and independent sets in graphs
rows.statement_text: $p_{\mathrm{monotone}}(t)$ tends to $0$ as $t$ grows.
rows.context_text: This is progressively stronger than Conjecture 2.1, as $p_{\mathrm{monotone}}(t) \geq p_{\mathrm{intersecting}}(t) \geq p_{\mathrm{dict}}(t)$, where winning sets are all balanced monotone families on $\{0,1\}^n$. The authors describe these two conjectures as 'progressively stronger than Conjecture 1.1'.
rows.source_text: $p_{\mathrm{monotone}}(t)$ tends to $0$ as $t$ grows.
rows.defer_reason: Hat-game probability over balanced monotone families on {0,1}^n: set systems/Boolean functions, not graph theory

rows.title: Conjecture 2.6
rows.paper_title: The success probability in Levine's hat problem, and independent sets in graphs
rows.statement_text: $$\lim_{t \to \infty} \lim_{n \to \infty} \bar{\alpha}\!\left(K(n)^{\square t}\right) = 0.$$
rows.context_text: This is a graph-theoretic restatement of Conjecture 2.1, using the observation that $p_{\mathrm{intersecting}}(t,n) = \bar{\alpha}(K(n)^{\square t})$, where $K(n)$ is the Kneser graph on $\{0,1\}^n$ (edge between $x$ and $y$ if their supports are disjoint) and $\square$ denotes the Hamming product of graphs.
rows.source_text: $$\lim_{t \to \infty} \lim_{n \to \infty} \bar{\alpha}\!\left(K(n)^{\square t}\right) = 0.$$
rows.class_notes: Independence ratio of Cartesian powers of Kneser graph K(n); context defines K(n), 'Hamming product' is the Cartesian product; double limit needs reals.

rows.title: Conjecture 2.8
rows.paper_title: The success probability in Levine's hat problem, and independent sets in graphs
rows.statement_text: $\varepsilon^*(\alpha) > 0$ for all $\alpha \in (0, 1/2)$.
rows.context_text: Here $\varepsilon^*(\alpha) = \inf_{G:\,\bar{\alpha}(G)\geq\alpha}(\bar{\alpha}(G) - \alpha^*(G))$, where $\alpha^*(G)$ is defined via an optimal partition of $V(G)^r$ and a positively-correlated random-subset distribution with Bernoulli$(1/2)$ marginals. This conjecture would imply Conjecture 2.6 and hence Levine's conjecture.
rows.source_text: $\varepsilon^*(\alpha) > 0$ for all $\alpha \in (0, 1/2)$.
rows.class_notes: alpha*(G) via 'optimal partition and positively-correlated random-subset distribution' is only sketched, not defined.

rows.title: Conjecture 2.9
rows.paper_title: The success probability in Levine's hat problem, and independent sets in graphs
rows.statement_text: $\varepsilon^{**}(\alpha) > 0$ for all $\alpha \in (0, 1/2)$. In other words, there exists a monotone non-decreasing function $\varepsilon^{**} : (0,1/2) \to (0,1/2)$ such that the following holds: if $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices with maximum independent set of size $\alpha n$, $W$ is a binomial random subset of $V(G)$, and $I_W$ is the maximal independent set contained in $W$, then $$\alpha^{**}(G) = E_W\!\left[|I_W|/n\right] \leq \alpha - \varepsilon^{**}(\alpha).$$
rows.context_text: This is the special case of Conjecture 2.8 where the random-subset distribution is simply binomial (each vertex included independently with probability $1/2$), which the authors describe as 'a fundamental problem in the study of independent sets in graphs'. The paper proves this for $\alpha > 1/4$ (Theorem 2.10) and for regular graphs with $\alpha > 1/8$ (Theorem 2.11).
rows.source_text: $\varepsilon^{**}(\alpha) > 0$ for all $\alpha \in (0, 1/2)$. In other words, there exists a monotone non-decreasing function $\varepsilon^{**} : (0,1/2) \to (0,1/2)$ such that the following holds: if $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices with maximum independent set of size $\alpha n$, $W$ is a binomial random subset of $V(G)$, and $I_W$ is the maximal independent set contained in $W$, then $$\alpha^{**}(G) = E_W\!\left[|I_W|/n\right] \leq \alpha - \varepsilon^{**}(\alpha).$$
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 2.9 asserts that for every graph $G$ on $n$ vertices with independence number $\alpha n$ ($\alpha < 1/2$), the expected size of the maximal independent set in a binomial random half-subset satisfies $\alpha^{**}(G) \leq \alpha - \varepsilon^{**}(\alpha)$ for some positive $\varepsilon^{**}(\alpha)$ depending only on $\alpha$. The source paper itself establishes the conjecture for $\alpha > 1/4$ (Theorem 2.10) and for regular graphs when $\alpha > 1/8$ (Theorem 2.11), leaving the full range $(0, 1/4]$ for general graphs open. Semantic Scholar lists five citing papers (to May 2026), none of which could be verified to make further progress on this specific conjecture; the closest follow-ups concern the hat-puzzle side of the paper or the related Bollobás–Erdős–Tuza conjecture.
rows.class_notes: Self-contained (binomial random subset, expected independence); minor ambiguity in 'maximal independent set contained in W' (should be maximum of G[W]).

rows.title: Conjecture (product structure with bounded tree-depth and tight clique size)
rows.paper_title: Product structure of graph classes with strongly sublinear separators
rows.statement_text: For any hereditary graph class $\mathcal{G}$ that admits $O(n^{1-\epsilon})$ separators, every $n$-vertex graph in $\mathcal{G}$ is a subgraph of the strong product of a graph $H$ with bounded tree-depth and a complete graph of size $O(n^{1-\epsilon})$.
rows.context_text: The abstract states that the paper investigates this conjecture. The proven results in the paper achieve the conclusion either with an extra $n^{\delta}$ factor in the clique size (for $H$ with constant tree-depth, any fixed $\delta\in(0,\epsilon)$) or with tree-depth $O(\log\log n)$ for $H$ when $\delta=0$; moreover, both limitations are shown to be best possible via isoperimetric inequalities for grid graphs. The conjecture in its strongest form — constant tree-depth of $H$ with clique size exactly $O(n^{1-\epsilon})$ — is investigated but not fully resolved.
rows.source_text: For any hereditary graph class $\mathcal{G}$ that admits $O(n^{1-\epsilon})$ separators, every $n$-vertex graph in $\mathcal{G}$ is a subgraph of the strong product of a graph $H$ with bounded tree-depth and a complete graph of size $O(n^{1-\epsilon})$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2208.10074 — Conjecture (product structure with bounded tree-depth and tight clique size)
rows.class_notes: Standard vocabulary (separators, tree-depth, strong product) but O(n^{1-eps}) quantification over a hereditary class needs an asymptotic wrapper.

rows.paper_title: Product structure of graph classes with strongly sublinear separators

rows.status_semantics: Open Problem 6 asks whether for any hereditary graph class \mathcal{G} with \operatorname{sep}(\mathcal{G}) \in O(n^{1-\varepsilon}), there exists a constant c = c(\mathcal{G}) such that every n-vertex graph G \in \mathcal{G} is contained in H \boxtimes K_m where \operatorname{tw}(H) \leq c and m \in O(n^{1-\varepsilon}). The source paper itself already confirms the answer affirmatively for graphs excluding a fixed minor (Theorem 7, citing Distel et al. 2022). The 2024 paper by Liu, Norin, and Wood (arXiv:2410.20333) further extends this to graphs excluding a fixed odd minor. The general hereditary case with strongly sublinear separators remains open.
