rows.title: Question 6.1
rows.paper_title: List-avoiding orientations
rows.statement_text: Does every graph $G$ have a spanning subgraph with an Alon-Tarsi orientation in which each vertex $v$ has out-degree at least $\frac{1}{2}(\deg_{G}(v)-1)$?
rows.context_text: This question asks whether Conjecture 1.1 could be proved for every graph via the paper's Theorem 1.4 by finding a suitable Alon-Tarsi orientation, which would yield a stronger result. An affirmative answer would further imply that every graph $G$ on $n$ vertices and maximum degree $\Delta$ has a subgraph $H$ with at least $|E(G)|-n/2$ edges satisfying $\operatorname{AT}(H)\leq\lfloor\frac{1}{2}\Delta+\frac{3}{2}\rfloor$.
rows.source_text: Does every graph $G$ have a spanning subgraph with an Alon-Tarsi orientation in which each vertex $v$ has out-degree at least $\frac{1}{2}(\deg_{G}(v)-1)$?
rows.class_notes: Precise yes/no; needs the Alon-Tarsi orientation primitive (even vs odd Eulerian subdigraph counts), which is buildable.

rows.title: Question 6.2
rows.paper_title: List-avoiding orientations
rows.statement_text: Does there exist a constant $C$ such that every graph $G$ of maximum degree $\Delta$ has a matching $M$ such that $\operatorname{AT}(G-M)\leq\frac{1}{2}\Delta+C$?
rows.context_text: This question is motivated by results showing that removing a matching from a graph can reduce upper bounds on coloring parameters such as the Alon-Tarsi number. It is noted that $C$ cannot be smaller than $\frac{1}{2}$, since for any odd $n$ and any maximum matching $M$ in $K_n$, $\operatorname{AT}(K_n-M)=\frac{n+1}{2}$. The authors ask specifically about matchings, inspired by Zhu's result on planar graphs and the result of Grytczuk and Zhu.
rows.source_text: Does there exist a constant $C$ such that every graph $G$ of maximum degree $\Delta$ has a matching $M$ such that $\operatorname{AT}(G-M)\leq\frac{1}{2}\Delta+C$?
rows.class_notes: Needs Alon-Tarsi number AT(G) as a primitive; otherwise a clean existential-constant statement.

rows.title: Open question on twin-width over infinite fields
rows.paper_title: Twin-width V: linear minors, modular counting, and matrix multiplication
rows.statement_text: An intriguing question concerns the existence of such results over infinite fields (starting with $\mathbb{Q}$). We do not have a direct definition of twin-width of matrices over $\mathbb{Q}$ based on contraction sequences. However linear-minor freeness naturally carries to infinite fields, and thus, it is natural to consider that a class of matrices over $\mathbb{Q}$ has bounded twin-width if its closure under linear minors is not the set of all matrices.
rows.context_text: After establishing FPT matrix multiplication algorithms for matrices over finite fields $\mathbb{F}_q$ with bounded twin-width (Theorems 5–7), the authors raise the question of whether analogous results hold for matrices over infinite fields such as $\mathbb{Q}$. The text is truncated at this point, so the precise formulation of the question (likely involving the grid rank of a matrix) is not fully visible.
rows.source_text: An intriguing question concerns the existence of such results over infinite fields (starting with $\mathbb{Q}$). We do not have a direct definition of twin-width of matrices over $\mathbb{Q}$ based on contraction sequences. However linear-minor freeness naturally carries to infinite fields, and thus, it is natural to consider that a class of matrices over $\mathbb{Q}$ has bounded twin-width if its closure under linear minors is not the set of all matrices.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2209.12023 — Open question on twin-width over infinite fields
rows.defer_reason: Informal and truncated musing about 'existence of such results' over infinite fields; no single proposition stated

rows.title: Grid Ramsey Problem (gr(G_{2x2}, K_n))
rows.paper_title: Hypergraph Ramsey numbers of cliques versus stars
rows.statement_text: What is the minimum $N$ such that any 2-edge-coloring of the Cartesian product $K_N \square K_N$ contains either a red rectangle or a blue $K_n$?
rows.context_text: The authors introduce this novel Ramsey problem on grid graphs as a key intermediate tool in the proof of the main result on $r(K^{(3)}_4, S^{(3)}_n)$. The minimum is denoted $\mathrm{gr}(G_{2\times 2}, K_n)$ and Theorem 1.3 establishes $2^{c\log^2 n} \le \mathrm{gr}(G_{2\times 2}, K_n) \le 2^{c' n^{2/3}\log n}$, but the exact growth rate remains open. The authors remark the problem may be of independent interest.
rows.source_text: What is the minimum $N$ such that any 2-edge-coloring of the Cartesian product $K_N \square K_N$ contains either a red rectangle or a blue $K_n$?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the minimum N' asks for the growth rate of gr(G_{2x2},K_n); no single proposition

rows.title: Informal Conjecture: r(K^(3)_4, S^(3)_n) and gr(G_{2x2}, K_n) have the same order
rows.paper_title: Hypergraph Ramsey numbers of cliques versus stars
rows.statement_text: We strongly believe that $r(K^{(3)}_4, S^{(3)}_n)$ and $\mathrm{gr}(G_{2\times 2}, K_n)$ are of roughly the same order.
rows.context_text: The paper shows $r(K^{(3)}_4, S^{(3)}_n) \le 2^{\mathrm{gr}(G_{2\times 2},K_n)}$, and the authors construct lower bounds for $r(K^{(3)}_4, S^{(3)}_n)$ by gluing together lower-bound constructions for $\mathrm{gr}(G_{2\times 2}, K_n)$, yielding comparable sizes. No direct implication between the lower bounds is known.
rows.source_text: We strongly believe that $r(K^{(3)}_4, S^{(3)}_n)$ and $\mathrm{gr}(G_{2\times 2}, K_n)$ are of roughly the same order.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2210.03545 — Informal Conjecture: r(K^(3)_4, S^(3)_n) and gr(G_{2x2}, K_n) have the same order
rows.defer_reason: 'Roughly the same order' is an imprecise belief, not a definite asymptotic proposition

rows.title: Informal Conjecture: r(K^(3)_{4-e}, S^(3)_n) = Theta(n^2 / log n)
rows.paper_title: Hypergraph Ramsey numbers of cliques versus stars
rows.statement_text: The close connection between $r(K^{(3)}_{4-e}, S^{(3)}_n)$ and $r(K_3, K_n)$ suggests that $r(K^{(3)}_{4-e}, S^{(3)}_n) = \Theta\!\left(\frac{n^2}{\log n}\right)$.
rows.context_text: Proposition 1.1 gives bounds $c\,\frac{n^2}{\log^2 n} \le r(K^{(3)}_{4-e}, S^{(3)}_n) \le c'\,\frac{n^2}{\log n}$. The authors note 'it seems likely that a proof of this may be possible through a careful analysis of the $(K^{(3)}_{4-e})$-free process', drawing an analogy with $r(K_3,K_n)=\Theta(n^2/\log n)$, but they do not pursue it.
rows.source_text: The close connection between $r(K^{(3)}_{4-e}, S^{(3)}_n)$ and $r(K_3, K_n)$ suggests that $r(K^{(3)}_{4-e}, S^{(3)}_n) = \Theta\!\left(\frac{n^2}{\log n}\right)$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2210.03545 — Informal Conjecture: r(K^(3)_{4-e}, S^(3)_n) = Theta(n^2 / log n)
rows.class_notes: Precise Theta(n^2/log n) claim for a 3-uniform hypergraph Ramsey number; K4^(3)-e and 3-uniform star are standard objects.

rows.title: Problem 4.1
rows.paper_title: A multidimensional Ramsey Theorem
rows.statement_text: Fix $r,d\geq 2$. Is $R_{2}(2,n)$ superexponential in $n$?
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 gives a doubly exponential upper bound on the $d$-dimensional Ramsey numbers $R_r(d,n)$, while the best known lower bound is only singly exponential (from a random colouring argument). Closing this gap—determining whether a simple exponential upper bound suffices or the true growth is faster—is identified as a key open problem.
rows.source_text: Fix $r,d\geq 2$. Is $R_{2}(2,n)$ superexponential in $n$?
rows.class_notes: d-dimensional Ramsey number R_r(d,n) never defined in the record; also 'Fix r,d>=2' preamble mismatches R_2(2,n).

rows.title: Problem 4.2
rows.paper_title: A multidimensional Ramsey Theorem
rows.statement_text: Does there exist $C>0$ such that $\log R_{2}(2,n)\leq(\log(M_{2}(n))^{C}$?
rows.context_text: Since $M_2(n)\leq R_2(2,n)$ for every $n$, the authors ask whether the two-dimensional Ramsey number $R_2(2,n)$ can be bounded above by a fixed power of $\log M_2(n)$, where $M_2(n)$ is the two-dimensional Erdős–Szekeres number. A singly-to-doubly exponential gap persists for both quantities.
rows.source_text: Does there exist $C>0$ such that $\log R_{2}(2,n)\leq(\log(M_{2}(n))^{C}$?
rows.class_notes: Both R_2(2,n) and the 2-dimensional Erdos-Szekeres number M_2(n) are paper-local, undefined here.

rows.title: Open problem: closing the gap for $d(s)$
rows.paper_title: Subdigraphs of prescribed size and outdegree
rows.statement_text: It remains an interesting open problem to close the gap between the lower and upper bounds: $\Omega(\log s) < \frac{s}{2} - d(s) < O(\sqrt{s\log s})$, where $d(s)$ denotes the largest integer such that every $2n$-vertex digraph of minimum out-degree at least $s$ contains an $n$-vertex subdigraph of minimum out-degree at least $d(s)$.
rows.context_text: After disproving Alon's problem, the authors note that the true asymptotic behaviour of $\frac{s}{2} - d(s)$ lies somewhere between $\Omega(\log s)$ (established by their construction) and $O(\sqrt{s\log s})$ (established by Alon's probabilistic argument). The precise asymptotics of $d(s)$ remain open.
rows.source_text: It remains an interesting open problem to close the gap between the lower and upper bounds: $\Omega(\log s) < \frac{s}{2} - d(s) < O(\sqrt{s\log s})$, where $d(s)$ denotes the largest integer such that every $2n$-vertex digraph of minimum out-degree at least $s$ contains an $n$-vertex subdigraph of minimum out-degree at least $d(s)$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2210.12699 — Open problem: closing the gap for $d(s)$
rows.defer_reason: 'Close the gap between lower and upper bounds' for s/2 - d(s); improve-the-bounds meta-problem
rows.class_notes: d(s) itself is well defined in the statement; only the goal is vague.

rows.title: Open Problem (characterizing edge probabilities for Theorem 1.1)
rows.paper_title: Largest subgraph from a hereditary property in a random graph
rows.statement_text: Characterize all edge probabilities $p = p(n)$ for which the assertion of Theorem 1.1 holds.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 is proved for fixed $p \in (0,1)$ and (via Proposition 2.1) for $p = p(n)$ satisfying $n^{-c} \leq p \leq 1-n^{-c}$. The authors note that hereditary (even monotone) properties exist for which the fraction of edges of $G(n,p)$ lying in a maximum in-$\mathcal{P}$ subgraph changes, whp, several times as $p$ increases from 0 to 1.
rows.source_text: Characterize all edge probabilities $p = p(n)$ for which the assertion of Theorem 1.1 holds.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2210.12754 — Open Problem (characterizing edge probabilities for Theorem 1.1)
rows.defer_reason: 'Characterize all edge probabilities' is a characterization request, not a single proposition

rows.title: Open Problem (accurate estimate in the bipartite case)
rows.paper_title: Largest subgraph from a hereditary property in a random graph
rows.statement_text: Provide a more accurate estimate for $\mathrm{ex}(G, \mathcal{P})$ when $\mathcal{P}$ misses a bipartite graph (i.e., when Theorem 1.1 gives only $\mathrm{ex}(G(n,p),\mathcal{P}) = o(n^2)$ whp).
rows.context_text: When the minimum chromatic number $k$ of a graph not in $\mathcal{P}$ equals 2, Theorem 1.1 yields $\mathrm{ex}(G(n,p),\mathcal{P}) = o(n^2)$ whp but does not determine the precise order of magnitude. The authors ask for a sharper asymptotic in this regime.
rows.source_text: Provide a more accurate estimate for $\mathrm{ex}(G, \mathcal{P})$ when $\mathcal{P}$ misses a bipartite graph (i.e., when Theorem 1.1 gives only $\mathrm{ex}(G(n,p),\mathcal{P}) = o(n^2)$ whp).
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2210.12754 — Open Problem (accurate estimate in the bipartite case)
rows.status_semantics: The open problem asks for a sharp asymptotic for ex(G(n,p),P) when the minimum chromatic number of a graph not in P is 2 (i.e., P misses a bipartite graph), a case where the main theorem of the source paper yields only o(n^2). Clifton, Liu, Mattos, and Zheng (arXiv:2405.09486, 2024) directly answer the explicit sub-question posed by Alon–Krivelevich–Samotij: they prove that ex(G(n,p),P) = O(n^{2-epsilon}) with high probability for some epsilon=epsilon(P)>0, establishing a polynomial improvement over the trivial o(n^2) bound. For excluded complete bipartite graphs K_{s,t} they obtain the more precise bound O(n^{2-1/s}(log n)^{1/s}). However, determining the precise order of magnitude for general hereditary properties missing an arbitrary bipartite graph remains open.
rows.defer_reason: 'Provide a more accurate estimate' is an improve-the-bounds request

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (polynomial saving in the bipartite case)
rows.paper_title: Largest subgraph from a hereditary property in a random graph
rows.statement_text: It seems plausible that in the bipartite case $\mathrm{ex}(G, \mathcal{P}) \leq n^{2-\varepsilon}$ for some $\varepsilon = \varepsilon(\mathcal{P}) > 0$.
rows.context_text: When $\mathcal{P}$ misses a bipartite graph, Theorem 1.1 gives only $\mathrm{ex}(G(n,p),\mathcal{P}) = o(n^2)$ whp. The authors speculate that the saving over the trivial $O(n^2)$ bound is in fact polynomial, not merely sub-polynomial.
rows.source_text: It seems plausible that in the bipartite case $\mathrm{ex}(G, \mathcal{P}) \leq n^{2-\varepsilon}$ for some $\varepsilon = \varepsilon(\mathcal{P}) > 0$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2210.12754 — Informal Conjecture (polynomial saving in the bipartite case)
rows.status_semantics: Clifton, Liu, Mattos, and Zheng (arXiv:2405.09486, 2024) fully resolve the conjecture by proving as Theorem 1.1 that for every non-trivial hereditary property $\mathcal{P}$ missing some bipartite graph $L$, we have $\mathrm{ex}(G(n,p),\mathcal{P}) \leq n^{2-\varepsilon}$ whp for some $\varepsilon = \varepsilon(\mathcal{P}) > 0$. Their paper explicitly states it answers a question of Alon, Krivelevich, and Samotij.
rows.class_notes: Solved; polynomial saving n^{2-eps} for ex(G(n,p),P) when hereditary P misses a bipartite graph — statable with whp wrapper.

rows.title: Conjecture 14
rows.paper_title: Graphs of Linear Growth have Bounded Treewidth
rows.statement_text: There exist functions $g : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{N}$ and $h : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that for any $c > 1$, every graph $G$ with growth $f_G(r) \leq cr$ is isomorphic to a subgraph of $T \boxtimes K_{g(c)}$ for some tree $T$ with growth $f_T(r) \leq h(c)r$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 13 shows that every graph $G$ with growth $f_G(r) \leq cr$ is a subgraph of $T \boxtimes K_{\lfloor 882c^3 \rfloor}$ for some tree $T$, but the tree $T$ itself may have exponential growth. Conjecture 14 proposes a rough characterisation of graphs of linear growth: if true, every subgraph $H$ of $T \boxtimes K_{g(c)}$ has growth $f_H(r) \leq g(c)h(c)r \in O(r)$, making the characterisation tight.
rows.source_text: There exist functions $g : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{N}$ and $h : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that for any $c > 1$, every graph $G$ with growth $f_G(r) \leq cr$ is isomorphic to a subgraph of $T \boxtimes K_{g(c)}$ for some tree $T$ with growth $f_T(r) \leq h(c)r$.
rows.class_notes: Growth function f_G(r) (ball sizes) is easy to define; statement is an existential-functions rough characterization of linear growth.

rows.title: Extension to H-free graphs
rows.paper_title: Turán graphs with bounded matching number
rows.statement_text: Determine the maximum possible number of edges of an $H$-free graph on $n$ vertices with matching number at most $s$, for general forbidden subgraphs $H$ (extending Theorem 1.1 beyond the case $H = K_{k+1}$).
rows.context_text: At the opening of Section 3, the authors note it may be interesting to extend Theorem 1.1 by replacing the forbidden clique $K_{k+1}$ by other forbidden subgraphs $H$. Proposition 3.1 settles this for color-critical $H$ when $s$ and $n$ are sufficiently large, but the problem for general $H$ (including non-color-critical graphs and small parameters) remains open.
rows.source_text: Determine the maximum possible number of edges of an $H$-free graph on $n$ vertices with matching number at most $s$, for general forbidden subgraphs $H$ (extending Theorem 1.1 beyond the case $H = K_{k+1}$).
rows.status_semantics: Gerbner (arXiv 2211.03272, J. Graph Theory 2024) directly took up this problem immediately after Alon–Frankl's paper, determining the maximum number of edges in n-vertex F-free graphs with matching number at most s apart from a constant additive term for all fixed F and s, with several exact results also obtained. The asymptotic form of the answer is thus settled for fixed parameters, but a complete exact determination for all H, n, s remains open. Ma and Hou (arXiv 2301.05625) subsequently extended the framework to the generalized Turán setting (counting copies of K_r).
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the maximum possible number of edges' for general H is an open-ended determination request, not one proposition

rows.title: Conjecture 1.4
rows.paper_title: A note on directed analogues of the Sidorenko and forcing conjectures
rows.statement_text: If $B$ is a bipartite oriented graph with a homomorphism $B \to \vec{K}_2$, then $B$ has the directed Sidorenko property.
rows.context_text: Observation 3.1 establishes that having a homomorphism to a directed edge is a necessary condition for the directed Sidorenko property. The authors conjecture this condition is also sufficient for bipartite oriented graphs, giving a directed analogue of Sidorenko's conjecture.
rows.source_text: If $B$ is a bipartite oriented graph with a homomorphism $B \to \vec{K}_2$, then $B$ has the directed Sidorenko property.
rows.class_notes: Directed analogue of Sidorenko's conjecture; 'directed Sidorenko property' is paper-local and undefined in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.7
rows.paper_title: A note on directed analogues of the Sidorenko and forcing conjectures
rows.statement_text: If $B$ is an oriented graph with a homomorphism to an edge and whose underlying undirected graph has a cycle, then $B$ has the directed forcing property.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.6 establishes that admitting a homomorphism to a directed edge and having a cycle in the underlying undirected graph are both necessary conditions for the directed forcing property. The authors conjecture these two conditions together are also sufficient, providing a directed analogue of the Skokan–Thoma forcing conjecture.
rows.source_text: If $B$ is an oriented graph with a homomorphism to an edge and whose underlying undirected graph has a cycle, then $B$ has the directed forcing property.
rows.class_notes: Directed analogue of the Skokan-Thoma forcing conjecture (Sidorenko-adjacent); 'directed forcing property' undefined here.

rows.title: Equivalence of asymmetric and classical Sidorenko properties
rows.paper_title: A note on directed analogues of the Sidorenko and forcing conjectures
rows.statement_text: It is conjectured that the asymmetric Sidorenko property and the classical Sidorenko property are equivalent for bipartite graphs.
rows.context_text: The asymmetric Sidorenko property is a priori stronger than the classical Sidorenko property. The authors note that essentially all bipartite graphs known to satisfy the classical Sidorenko property are also known to satisfy the asymmetric version, lending credence to the belief that the two properties coincide.
rows.source_text: It is conjectured that the asymmetric Sidorenko property and the classical Sidorenko property are equivalent for bipartite graphs.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2210.16971 — Equivalence of asymmetric and classical Sidorenko properties
rows.class_notes: Asymmetric-vs-classical Sidorenko equivalence; asymmetric Sidorenko property not defined in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.13
rows.paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic
rows.statement_text: Let $p = p(n)$ be the probability of edges in $G(n,p)$. The expected number of faces in a random embedding of a random graph $G \in G(n,p)$ is $(1 + o(1))\ln(pn^{2})$.
rows.context_text: In light of the paper's theorems for specific graph families and Monte Carlo experiments, the authors conjecture that a logarithmic bound holds for any usual model of random graphs. Extending the proof of Theorem 1.5 to arbitrary random graphs requires further ideas; Section 9 provides additional discussion.
rows.source_text: Let $p = p(n)$ be the probability of edges in $G(n,p)$. The expected number of faces in a random embedding of a random graph $G \in G(n,p)$ is $(1 + o(1))\ln(pn^{2})$.
rows.class_notes: Expected face count of a uniformly random rotation-system embedding of G(n,p); needs embedding layer plus (1+o(1)) asymptotics.

rows.title: Conjecture 9.1
rows.paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic
rows.statement_text: For any $p(n) : \mathbb{N} \rightarrow [0,1]$, almost all graphs in $G(n,p)$ satisfy $\mathbb{E}[F] = O(\log(n))$.
rows.context_text: Corollary 7.3 establishes a polylogarithmic bound for almost all dense graphs (when $p \geq 1/\mathrm{polylog}\, n$), and Section 8 gives a logarithmic bound for random sparse graphs. The authors conjecture that the logarithmic property holds without any density condition on edges. This conjecture would follow from the stronger Conjecture 1.13.
rows.source_text: For any $p(n) : \mathbb{N} \rightarrow [0,1]$, almost all graphs in $G(n,p)$ satisfy $\mathbb{E}[F] = O(\log(n))$.
rows.class_notes: Weaker O(log n) form implied by sibling Conjecture 1.13 (axv_2211_01032_00); needs embeddings + asymptotics.

rows.title: Conjecture 9.3
rows.paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices with minimum vertex degree $\Omega(n)$. Then $G$ satisfies $\mathbb{E}[F] = \Theta(\log(n))$.
rows.context_text: Families of sparse graphs with maximum degree $O(1)$ achieving $\mathbb{E}[F] = \Theta(n)$ are known, but no dense graph example with that many average faces has been found. Theorem 1.8 confirms this conjecture for the complete graph $K_n$; the general dense case remains open.
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices with minimum vertex degree $\Omega(n)$. Then $G$ satisfies $\mathbb{E}[F] = \Theta(\log(n))$.
rows.class_notes: Theta(log n) expected faces for min-degree Omega(n) graphs; needs embeddings + asymptotics.

rows.title: Conjecture 9.5
rows.paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic
rows.statement_text: The expected number of faces in a non-orientable random embedding of the complete graph $K_{n}$ is at most $\ln(n) + O(1)$.
rows.context_text: The authors extend their investigation to non-orientable surfaces, where a random embedding is defined by choosing a random rotation system together with a random signature assigning each edge a sign independently with probability $1/2$. Computer simulations support a logarithmic behaviour analogous to the orientable case.
rows.source_text: The expected number of faces in a non-orientable random embedding of the complete graph $K_{n}$ is at most $\ln(n) + O(1)$.
rows.class_notes: Random rotation-system+signature embedding is finite/combinatorial but face-count expectation with ln(n)+O(1) needs asymptotic wrapper.

rows.title: Conjecture 9.6
rows.paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic
rows.statement_text: Let $F^{-}$ be the random variable for the average number of faces in a non-orientable random embedding of some graph $G$. Then $\mathbb{E}[F^{-}] \leq \mathbb{E}[F]$.
rows.context_text: The authors conjecture that the expected face count in a non-orientable random embedding is always at most that of the orientable random embedding, for any graph $G$. The inequality is verified for toy models (including the dipole and chains of triangles joined by cut edges), and an analysis of Random Process A gives the bound $\mathbb{E}[F^{-}] \leq \frac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}[F] + 1$ for the dipole.
rows.source_text: Let $F^{-}$ be the random variable for the average number of faces in a non-orientable random embedding of some graph $G$. Then $\mathbb{E}[F^{-}] \leq \mathbb{E}[F]$.
rows.class_notes: Finite exact inequality E[F^-] <= E[F] over rotation systems; needs rotation-system/face-tracing primitive but no asymptotics.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (second phase transition for all radii)
rows.paper_title: Shotgun assembly of random graphs
rows.statement_text: It seems likely that for every radius $r$ there should be a second phase transition around some threshold $t=t(n)$. By which we mean that, if $p=\omega(t(n))$, then $G$ is with high probability reconstructible from its $r$-neighbourhoods, while if $p=o(t(n))$ and $p=\omega(n^{-\frac{2r+1}{2r}})$, then with high probability $G$ is not reconstructible from its $r$-neighbourhoods.
rows.context_text: The paper establishes the existence of this second phase transition for all $r\geq 3$, but the question remains open for $r=1$ and $r=2$, for which only improved (but not matching) bounds are given.
rows.source_text: It seems likely that for every radius $r$ there should be a second phase transition around some threshold $t=t(n)$. By which we mean that, if $p=\omega(t(n))$, then $G$ is with high probability reconstructible from its $r$-neighbourhoods, while if $p=o(t(n))$ and $p=\omega(n^{-\frac{2r+1}{2r}})$, then with high probability $G$ is not reconstructible from its $r$-neighbourhoods.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2211.14218 — Informal Conjecture (second phase transition for all radii)
rows.status_semantics: The source paper itself establishes the second phase transition for all r≥3, proving that if p=ω(t(n)) then G is reconstructible from its r-neighbourhoods and if p=o(t(n)) (and p=ω(n^{-(2r+1)/(2r)})) then G is not reconstructible. The conjecture remains open for r=1 and r=2, for which the paper provides improved but non-matching upper and lower bounds; an explicit open problem asks whether the threshold for r=2 is around n^{-3/4} up to polylogarithmic factors. No subsequent paper resolving either of these cases was found in the indexed literature.
rows.class_notes: Precise existential threshold statement (whp reconstructibility of G(n,p) from r-neighbourhoods); open cases r=1,2.
