rows.title: Question: finite families yielding clean H-free classes
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree-decompositions VII. Basic obstructions in $H$-free graphs
rows.statement_text: For which finite families $\mathcal{H}$ of graphs is the class of all $\mathcal{H}$-free graphs clean?
rows.context_text: After proving Theorem 1.5 — which characterizes all single graphs $H$ for which the class $\mathcal{F}_H$ of $H$-free graphs is clean (precisely the subdivided star forests) — the authors pose the next natural step. They observe that any finite set $\mathcal{H}$ containing a subdivided star forest yields a clean class, but note that the converse fails (e.g., $\mathcal{H}=\{H, K_3\}$ for the unique double star on six vertices is also clean). A full description is deferred to a companion paper [6] by four of the five authors.
rows.source_text: For which finite families $\mathcal{H}$ of graphs is the class of all $\mathcal{H}$-free graphs clean?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2212.02737 — Question: finite families yielding clean H-free classes
rows.status_semantics: The companion paper (arXiv:2311.05066, published in Advances in Combinatorics) by Alecu, Chudnovsky, Hajebi, and Spirkl proves Theorem 1.4: for a finite set $\mathcal{H}$ of graphs, the class of all $\mathcal{H}$-free graphs is clean if and only if there are only finitely many $n \in \mathbb{N}$ for which an $\mathcal{H}$-free $n$-array exists. This provides a complete necessary and sufficient condition in terms of $n$-arrays and reduces the question to understanding when $\mathcal{H}$-free arrays can be constructed for all large $n$. A direct structural characterization of exactly which families $\mathcal{H}$ satisfy this array condition — analogous to the single-graph case where $H$ must be a subdivided star forest — does not appear to follow immediately from these results.
rows.defer_reason: Characterization question ('for which finite families...'), not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: 'Clean' is paper-local; full description deferred to a companion paper.

rows.title: Conjecture on the asymptotic of n(2,d)
rows.paper_title: New bounds on the maximum number of neighborly boxes in R^d
rows.statement_text: A general conjecture is posed in the final section which, together with the lower bound $n(2,d) > (1-o(1))\frac{d^2}{2}$, would imply that $\lim_{d\to\infty} \frac{n(2,d)}{d^2} = \frac{1}{2}$.
rows.context_text: The authors establish the lower bound $n(2,d) > (1-o(1))\frac{d^2}{2}$, improving the previous bound $n(2,d) > \frac{d^2}{4}$. They remark that determining the precise asymptotic order of $n(2,d)$ seems to be a hard task (as noted earlier by Alon and by Huang–Sudakov), and refer to a formal conjecture in the final section concerning lamination-based constructions.
rows.source_text: A general conjecture is posed in the final section which, together with the lower bound $n(2,d) > (1-o(1))\frac{d^2}{2}$, would imply that $\lim_{d\to\infty} \frac{n(2,d)}{d^2} = \frac{1}{2}$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2212.05133 — Conjecture on the asymptotic of n(2,d)
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that $\lim_{d\to\infty} n(2,d)/d^2 = 1/2$ remains open. A 2024 follow-up by Grytczuk, Kisielewicz, and Przesławski (arXiv:2402.02199) gives new recursive constructions of 2-neighborly families improving the lower bound on $n(2,d)$, and poses an explicit conjectured formula for $n(2,d)$ based on their construction being optimal — this is partial progress but does not resolve the asymptotic limit. A 2025 paper (arXiv:2508.20648) on 'strings with jokers' addresses the regime $k$ close to $d$ (proving $n(d-s,d) \sim \frac{2^s+1}{2^{s+1}} \cdot 2^d$) but does not directly resolve the $k=2$ question.
rows.defer_reason: Neighborly boxes in R^d is discrete geometry, not graph/hypergraph theory.
rows.class_notes: Also non-self-contained (points to an unstated conjecture in the paper's final section).

rows.title: Open question on existence of lim n(2,d)/d^2
rows.paper_title: New bounds on the maximum number of neighborly boxes in R^d
rows.statement_text: Does $\lim_{d\to\infty} \frac{n(2,d)}{d^2}$ exist?
rows.context_text: The authors conjecture that if the limit exists it equals $\frac{1}{2}$, but explicitly remark that even the existence of the limit is currently unknown. The paper provides the lower bound $n(2,d) > (1-o(1))\frac{d^2}{2}$ via Theorem 2.
rows.source_text: Does $\lim_{d\to\infty} \frac{n(2,d)}{d^2}$ exist?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2212.05133 — Open question on existence of lim n(2,d)/d^2
rows.defer_reason: Discrete geometry (neighborly boxes in R^d), out of graph-theory scope.
rows.class_notes: Clean limit-existence question but about the geometric quantity n(2,d) for neighborly boxes.

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: Extremal Independent Set Reconfiguration
rows.context_text: The authors prove $D(n,3) = \Omega(n^2/e^{O(\sqrt{\log n})})$ and a general lower bound $D(n,k) = n^{2\lfloor k/3\rfloor}/e^{O_k(\sqrt{\log n})}$, but were unable to show that lower and upper bounds almost match for $k \geq 4$. In particular, it is open whether the 4-configuration graph can have super-quadratic diameter, while the upper bound is $o(n^3)$.
rows.class_notes: D(n,k) (diameter of the k-independent-set reconfiguration graph) is paper-local and not defined in the record.

rows.title: Question 8
rows.paper_title: Extremal Independent Set Reconfiguration
rows.statement_text: What is the asymptotic behavior of $\max_k D(n, k)$?
rows.context_text: The authors prove that for every $n$ and every $k$ with $3n/10 \leq k \leq 2n/5$, $D(n,k) = \Omega(2^{n/5})$, and remark that it is quite surprising that this exponential lower bound holds for such a wide range of values of $k$.
rows.source_text: What is the asymptotic behavior of $\max_k D(n, k)$?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the asymptotic behavior of max_k D(n,k)' asks for a value, not a proposition.
rows.class_notes: D(n,k) also undefined in record.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (chordal modulator characterization for even-hole-free graphs)
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions VIII. Excluding a forest in (theta, prism)-free graphs
rows.statement_text: Every chordal graph of clique number at most three modulates even-hole-free graphs.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.2 (Sintiari–Trotignon) shows that a graph $H$ can modulate even-hole-free graphs only if $H$ is a chordal graph of clique number at most three; the authors state that the converse may also be true. The paper proves the forest case (Theorem 1.6) but explicitly notes that extending to all chordal graphs of clique number at most three remains open.
rows.source_text: Every chordal graph of clique number at most three modulates even-hole-free graphs.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2301.02138 — Informal Conjecture (chordal modulator characterization for even-hole-free graphs)
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that every chordal graph of clique number at most three modulates even-hole-free graphs (the converse of Theorem 1.2 of Sintiari–Trotignon) remains open in full generality. The source paper itself proved the case when H is a forest (Theorem 1.6). Subsequently, Hajebi (2024/2025) proved the conjecture for H = cone(cone(F)) for any forest F and for H a 'crystal' (a graph built from coned double stars), substantially extending the known special cases, but the full statement for all K₄-free chordal H is still open.
rows.class_notes: 'Modulates' is paper-local (tree-decomposition modulator notion) and not defined in the record.

rows.title: Grassmannian Subvariety Construction Problem
rows.paper_title: The Kromatic Symmetric Function: A $K$-theoretic Analogue of $X_G$
rows.statement_text: For each claw-free incomparability graph $G$, there should exist a subvariety of the Grassmannian whose cohomology class is represented by $X_G$ and whose K-theoretic structure sheaf class is represented by $\overline{X}_G$. It would be very interesting to have an explicit construction of such subvarieties.
rows.context_text: The authors lift Gasharov's theorem to K-theory by showing that the Kromatic symmetric function of a claw-free incomparability graph is $\overline{s}$-positive. The existence of both a cohomological and a K-theoretic representative suggests a geometric interpretation in terms of Grassmannian subvarieties.
rows.source_text: For each claw-free incomparability graph $G$, there should exist a subvariety of the Grassmannian whose cohomology class is represented by $X_G$ and whose K-theoretic structure sheaf class is represented by $\overline{X}_G$. It would be very interesting to have an explicit construction of such subvarieties.
rows.defer_reason: Algebraic geometry / K-theory of Grassmannians, not graph theory.
rows.class_notes: Also vague ('it would be very interesting to have an explicit construction').

rows.title: Problem 3
rows.paper_title: Separating the edges of a graph by a linear number of paths
rows.statement_text: Does every graph on $n$ vertices admit a strongly-separating path system of size $2n$?
rows.context_text: The upper bound of $19n$ from Theorem 2 is likely far from optimal. Balogh et al. showed that the complete bipartite graph $K_{\varepsilon n,(1-\varepsilon)n}$ cannot be strongly separated by fewer than $2(1-2\varepsilon)n$ paths, but no graph with a larger lower bound is known, suggesting the optimal constant might be $2$.
rows.source_text: Does every graph on $n$ vertices admit a strongly-separating path system of size $2n$?
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no with explicit constant 2n; needs the (standard-in-area) strongly-separating path system primitive.

rows.title: Problem 4
rows.paper_title: Separating the edges of a graph by a linear number of paths
rows.statement_text: Does every properly edge-colored graph $G$ admit $O(|V(G)|)$ rainbow paths that cover $E(G)$?
rows.context_text: This question arose from initial attempts to solve Conjecture 1; a rainbow path is a path containing no two edges of the same color. An affirmative answer would yield an alternative proof of Conjecture 1 via a path decomposition argument, and the authors note that a version with 'trails' replacing 'paths' can already be verified.
rows.source_text: Does every properly edge-colored graph $G$ admit $O(|V(G)|)$ rainbow paths that cover $E(G)$?
rows.class_notes: Rainbow path defined in context; O(|V|) becomes an exists-constant wrapper.

rows.title: Informal conjecture on sharp threshold for linear-proportion reconstruction
rows.paper_title: Reconstructing a point set from a random subset of its pairwise distances
rows.statement_text: The correct threshold for the existence of a reconstructible set of size $\Omega(n)$ is $p = (1+\varepsilon)/n$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.2a is proved with constant 42, i.e., $p \geq 42/n$ suffices. The authors note in footnote 2 that the best their methods could give is $9/n$, and they believe the true threshold is $(1+\varepsilon)/n$, with further discussion deferred to Section 4 on open problems.
rows.source_text: The correct threshold for the existence of a reconstructible set of size $\Omega(n)$ is $p = (1+\varepsilon)/n$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2301.11019 — Informal conjecture on sharp threshold for linear-proportion reconstruction
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that $p=(1+\varepsilon)/n$ is the sharp threshold for the existence of a reconstructible set of size $\Omega(n)$ has been resolved by Zakharov (2026, arXiv:2604.09176), which proves that for every $\varepsilon>0$, with high probability there exists a reconstructible subset $U$ of the giant component $C$ of the 2-core satisfying $|U|=|V(C)|(1-o(1))$ when $p=(1+\varepsilon)/n$, establishing the conjectured threshold and in fact giving a stronger quantitative bound.
rows.defer_reason: Point-set distance reconstruction, out of graph-theory scope.
rows.class_notes: Reconstructing point sets from random pairwise distances is discrete geometry/probability, not graph theory; solved.

rows.title: Question 1.1
rows.paper_title: Graph-codes
rows.statement_text: Let $\mathcal{H}$ be a family of graphs closed under isomorphism. Is it true that $d_{\mathcal{H}}(n)$ tends to $0$ as $n$ tends to infinity if and only if $\mathcal{H}$ contains a graph with an even number of edges? Equivalently: is it true that for any fixed graph $H$ with an even number of edges, $d_H(n)$ tends to $0$ as $n$ tends to infinity?
rows.context_text: The authors define $d_H(n)$ as the maximum fraction of all graphs on $[n]$ forming an $H$-code (a family with no two members whose symmetric difference is a copy of $H$). If every member of $\mathcal{H}$ has an odd number of edges then $d_{\mathcal{H}}(n) \geq 1/2$, motivating the question of whether containing an even-edge graph is exactly the condition that forces $d_{\mathcal{H}}(n) \to 0$. The question is stated as the central open problem and noted in the concluding remarks to remain wide open.
rows.source_text: Let $\mathcal{H}$ be a family of graphs closed under isomorphism. Is it true that $d_{\mathcal{H}}(n)$ tends to $0$ as $n$ tends to infinity if and only if $\mathcal{H}$ contains a graph with an even number of edges? Equivalently: is it true that for any fixed graph $H$ with an even number of edges, $d_H(n)$ tends to $0$ as $n$ tends to infinity?
rows.status_semantics: The full Question 1.1 — whether $d_H(n)\to 0$ for every fixed graph $H$ with an even number of edges — remains open for general H-codes. Versteegen (arXiv:2310.19891, 2023) proved the analogous statement for the linear variant: if $H$ has an even number of edges then the density of a linear graph code avoiding $H$ is $O(2^{\binom{n}{2}}/\log n)$, and for almost all such $H$ it is at most $2^{\binom{n}{2}/n^{\varepsilon_H}}$ for some $\varepsilon_H>0$. This constitutes partial progress on Alon's question but does not settle it for non-linear codes.
rows.class_notes: H-code and d_H(n) fully defined in context_text; iff statement with limit-to-0 needs asymptotic wrapper.

rows.title: Informal question: is $d_{K_4}(n) = o(1)$?
rows.paper_title: Graph-codes
rows.statement_text: Is it true that $d_{K_4}(n) = o(1)$?
rows.context_text: In the concluding remarks the authors single this out as an interesting special case of Question 1.1. The companion question of whether $d_{K_4}(n) \geq 1/n^{o(1)}$ is answered affirmatively within the paper by appealing to an edge-coloring construction of Hunter and Mubayi [5] (modifying [11] and [4]); the upper-bound direction $d_{K_4}(n) = o(1)$ remains open.
rows.source_text: Is it true that $d_{K_4}(n) = o(1)$?
rows.status_semantics: The question whether $d_{K_4}(n) = o(1)$ for general graph codes remains open as of May 2026. Versteegen (arXiv:2310.19891, 2023) made partial progress by proving that any linear graph code on $n$ vertices containing no copy of a graph $H$ with an even number of edges has size $O(2^{\binom{n}{2}}/\log n)$, which settles the analogue for linear codes; however, the general (non-linear) case of the question from Conjecture 1.1 / Question 1.1 of arXiv:2301.13305 is not resolved by this work. Several further papers cite arXiv:2301.13305 (including Gishboliner–Jin–Sudakov 2312.06610, Conlon–Lee–Versteegen 2404.17467, and Rancourt–Dodos–Tyros 2602.03298) but none were verified to resolve the full question.
rows.class_notes: Special case (H=K_4) of Question 1.1 in the same paper (axv_2301_13305_00).

rows.title: Open problem: tighter bounds for linear graph-codes
rows.paper_title: Graph-codes
rows.statement_text: Establish tighter bounds for the maximum possible cardinality of a linear family of graphs on $[n]$ in which no symmetric difference is a copy of a fixed graph $H$ with an even number of edges.
rows.context_text: The paper notes that for any fixed graph $H$ with an even number of edges the maximum size of a linear $H$-code is $o\bigl(2^{\binom{n}{2}}\bigr)$, but the proof via Ramsey's Theorem yields very weak quantitative bounds. Theorem 1.6 gives a tight result for the family $\mathcal{K}$ of all cliques as a model for what such tight results could look like.
rows.source_text: Establish tighter bounds for the maximum possible cardinality of a linear family of graphs on $[n]$ in which no symmetric difference is a copy of a fixed graph $H$ with an even number of edges.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2301.13305 — Open problem: tighter bounds for linear graph-codes
rows.status_semantics: Versteegen (2023, arXiv:2310.19891) made significant progress on this problem: for any fixed graph H with an even number of edges, the maximum size of a linear H-code is O(2^{\binom{n}{2}} / \log n), and for almost all such H there exists \varepsilon_H > 0 giving the stronger bound 2^{\binom{n}{2}/n^{\varepsilon_H}}. These are substantially tighter than the original Ramsey-based bounds from the source paper. However, tight bounds matching a lower construction are not yet established in general, so the full problem remains open.
rows.defer_reason: 'Establish tighter bounds' is an improve-the-bounds task, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Open problem: binary vectors with cube-length forbidden symmetric difference
rows.paper_title: Graph-codes
rows.statement_text: What is the maximum possible cardinality of a collection of binary vectors whose coordinates are indexed by the elements of the ordered set $[n]$, where no symmetric difference of two distinct members of the collection forms an interval of length which is a cube of an integer?
rows.context_text: This is posed as an illustrative example of extending graph-code questions to other combinatorial structures. The corresponding Cayley graph has $2^n$ vertices and is triangle-free by Fermat's Last Theorem for cubes; its independence number is $o(2^n)$ by the Furstenberg–Sárközy Theorem and its extensions.
rows.source_text: What is the maximum possible cardinality of a collection of binary vectors whose coordinates are indexed by the elements of the ordered set $[n]$, where no symmetric difference of two distinct members of the collection forms an interval of length which is a cube of an integer?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2301.13305 — Open problem: binary vectors with cube-length forbidden symmetric difference
rows.defer_reason: Set-system/additive number theory question, not graph theory.
rows.class_notes: Binary-vector code with cube-length interval symmetric differences; additive/number-theoretic (Furstenberg-Sarkozy flavor), and also a determine-the-value question.

rows.title: Open problem: minimum edge-coloring with odd intersection property
rows.paper_title: Graph-codes
rows.statement_text: Determine or estimate the smallest number of colors in an edge coloring of $K_n$ in which every copy of a given graph $H$ (or every copy of any member of a prescribed family $\mathcal{H}$ of graphs) intersects at least one color class by an odd number of edges.
rows.context_text: The authors propose this in the concluding remarks as a natural variant of classical Ramsey Theory questions arising from the coloring method used throughout the paper to produce lower bounds on independence numbers of Cayley graphs.
rows.source_text: Determine or estimate the smallest number of colors in an edge coloring of $K_n$ in which every copy of a given graph $H$ (or every copy of any member of a prescribed family $\mathcal{H}$ of graphs) intersects at least one color class by an odd number of edges.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2301.13305 — Open problem: minimum edge-coloring with odd intersection property
rows.status_semantics: Bennett, Heath, and Zerbib (arXiv:2307.01314, July 2023) directly study the quantity g(G,H) — the minimum number of colors to edge-color G so that every copy of H has an odd color class — introduced as a tool for bounding graph-codes. They establish g(K_n, K_5) ≤ n^{o(1)} and g(K_{n,n}, C_4) = n/2 + o(n), providing the first non-trivial estimates for specific choices of H. The general problem for arbitrary H remains open, and subsequent 2025 papers continue developing the odd-Ramsey framework.
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine or estimate the smallest number of colors' asks for a value.
rows.class_notes: Edge-coloring of K_n with odd-intersection property is graph-theoretic but posed as an estimation task.

rows.title: Open Problem: sharpness of the treedepth bound in Theorem 1
rows.paper_title: Tight bound on treedepth in terms of pathwidth and longest path
rows.statement_text: Whether the bound $O(thb)$ in Theorem 1 — that every graph of treewidth less than $t$ with no complete binary tree of height $h$ as a minor and no $2^b$-vertex path has treedepth $O(thb)$ — can be improved remains an open problem.
rows.context_text: Theorems 2 and 3 together imply Theorem 1, and their individual bounds are each sharp up to constant factors. However, it is unknown whether the combined bound $O(thb)$ in Theorem 1 is optimal or can be reduced.
rows.source_text: Whether the bound $O(thb)$ in Theorem 1 — that every graph of treewidth less than $t$ with no complete binary tree of height $h$ as a minor and no $2^b$-vertex path has treedepth $O(thb)$ — can be improved remains an open problem.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2302.02995 — Open Problem: sharpness of the treedepth bound in Theorem 1
rows.defer_reason: 'Whether the bound can be improved' is an improve-the-bounds meta-question.
rows.class_notes: Underlying quantities (treewidth, treedepth, binary-tree minor) are standard, but the row only asks whether O(thb) can be improved.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.8
rows.paper_title: Hitting all maximum stable sets in $P_5$-free graphs
rows.statement_text: For every forest $H$, there exists a function $f:\mathbb{N}\rightarrow\mathbb{N}$ such that every $H$-free graph $G$ satisfies $\eta(G)\leq f(\omega(G))$.
rows.context_text: Proposed as an analogue of the Gyárfás–Sumner conjecture ($\chi$-boundedness) for $\eta$-boundedness: just as $H$-free graphs are conjectured to be $\chi$-bounded for every forest $H$, the authors conjecture the same for $\eta$-boundedness. The paper proves this conjecture for an assortment of special forests, including stars and paths $P_t$ with $t\leq 5$.
rows.source_text: For every forest $H$, there exists a function $f:\mathbb{N}\rightarrow\mathbb{N}$ such that every $H$-free graph $G$ satisfies $\eta(G)\leq f(\omega(G))$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.8 proposes that for every forest $H$, the class of $H$-free graphs is $\eta$-bounded (i.e., $\eta(G) \leq f(\omega(G))$), by analogy with the Gyárfás–Sumner conjecture for $\chi$-boundedness. The source paper proves it for stars, subdivided stars, and $P_t$ for $t \leq 5$. A subsequent paper in the Electronic Journal of Combinatorics (2025) extends $\eta$-boundedness to graphs without large induced matchings ($tK_2$-free), resolving a related conjecture from the source paper and adding another family of forests for which the conjecture holds. The full conjecture for all forests $H$ remains open, and the case of $P_t$-free graphs for $t \geq 6$ is explicitly listed as open.
rows.class_notes: eta(G) (min size of a set hitting all maximum stable sets) is paper-local and never defined in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.13
rows.paper_title: Hitting all maximum stable sets in $P_5$-free graphs
rows.statement_text: For every integer $t\geq 6$, the class of $P_{t}$-free graphs is $\eta$-bounded.
rows.context_text: After proving $\eta$-boundedness for $P_5$-free graphs (Theorem 1.12), the authors note that it remains open whether $P_t$-free graphs are $\eta$-bounded for $t\geq 6$, and state this as a conjecture paralleling the known $\chi$-boundedness of $P_t$-free graphs for all $t$.
rows.source_text: For every integer $t\geq 6$, the class of $P_{t}$-free graphs is $\eta$-bounded.
rows.class_notes: 'eta-bounded' undefined in record; otherwise a clean P_t-free class statement.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.17
rows.paper_title: Hitting all maximum stable sets in $P_5$-free graphs
rows.statement_text: There exists $d\in\mathbb{N}$ such that every $P_{5}$-free graph $G$ satisfies $\eta(G)\leq\omega(G)^{d}$.
rows.context_text: A special case of Conjecture 1.16 singled out because of its connection to the Erdős–Hajnal conjecture: by Theorem 1.6, a polynomial $\eta$-bound for $P_5$-free graphs would imply the Erdős–Hajnal conjecture for $P_5$-free graphs. The bound in the paper's main theorem (Theorem 1.12) is super-exponential.
rows.source_text: There exists $d\in\mathbb{N}$ such that every $P_{5}$-free graph $G$ satisfies $\eta(G)\leq\omega(G)^{d}$.
rows.class_notes: eta(G) undefined in record; polynomial eta-bound for P_5-free graphs, tied to Erdos-Hajnal.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.20
rows.paper_title: Hitting all maximum stable sets in $P_5$-free graphs
rows.statement_text: For every integer $t\geq 3$, $M_{t}$-free graphs are polynomially $\eta$-bounded.
rows.context_text: Proposed as a strengthening of Theorem 1.19; the authors note that it is not even known whether $M_3$-free graphs are $\eta$-bounded, making this a particularly bold conjecture even for the base case.
rows.source_text: For every integer $t\geq 3$, $M_{t}$-free graphs are polynomially $\eta$-bounded.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.20 asserts that for every integer $t \geq 3$, $M_t$-free graphs (graphs containing no induced matching of size $t$) are polynomially $\eta$-bounded. This was resolved by Ai, Liu, Xu, and Zhou in arXiv:2403.19737 (published in the Electronic Journal of Combinatorics, 2025), who proved that any graph $G$ without an induced matching of size $t$ satisfies $\eta(G) \leq \omega(G)^{3t-3+o(1)}$, establishing polynomial $\eta$-boundedness for all $t \geq 1$ and explicitly resolving the conjecture of Hajebi, Li, and Spirkl.
rows.class_notes: Both eta and the graph family M_t are paper-local and undefined in the record; marked solved.

rows.title: Open problem on $\eta$-boundedness for disjoint union of two stars
rows.paper_title: Hitting all maximum stable sets in $P_5$-free graphs
rows.statement_text: It remains open whether the disjoint union of two stars satisfies Conjecture 1.8, i.e., whether the class of $(S_{a}\cup S_{b})$-free graphs is $\eta$-bounded for all $a,b\geq 1$.
rows.context_text: For the Gyárfás–Sumner conjecture, $\chi$-boundedness of an $H$-free class reduces to $\chi$-boundedness of the component-free classes. The authors do not know whether the analogous reduction holds for Conjecture 1.8, and in particular whether the disjoint union of two stars satisfies $\eta$-boundedness.
rows.source_text: It remains open whether the disjoint union of two stars satisfies Conjecture 1.8, i.e., whether the class of $(S_{a}\cup S_{b})$-free graphs is $\eta$-bounded for all $a,b\geq 1$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2302.04986 — Open problem on $\eta$-boundedness for disjoint union of two stars
rows.class_notes: eta-boundedness undefined in record; instance (union of two stars) of Conjecture 1.8 (axv_2302_04986_00).

rows.title: Question 2
rows.paper_title: Maximum Independent Set when excluding an induced minor: $K_1 + tK_2$ and $tC_3 \uplus C_4$
rows.statement_text: Is it true that for every planar graph $H$, Max Independent Set can be solved in quasipolynomial time in the class of graphs excluding $H$ as an induced minor?
rows.context_text: Question 2 is the quasipolynomial-time relaxation of Question 1, for which substantially more positive evidence exists: quasipolynomial-time algorithms are known for $C_t$-induced-minor-free graphs and for $tC_3$-induced-minor-free graphs. The present paper contributes a quasipolynomial-time algorithm for $tC_3 \uplus C_4$-induced-minor-free graphs, running in $n^{O(t^{2}\log n)+f(t)}$.
rows.source_text: Is it true that for every planar graph $H$, Max Independent Set can be solved in quasipolynomial time in the class of graphs excluding $H$ as an induced minor?
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no (quasipolynomial MIS on H-induced-minor-free classes, H planar) but requires an algorithmic/complexity foundation.

rows.title: Polynomial bounds for path-induced copy theorem (1.2)
rows.paper_title: A note on the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture
rows.statement_text: Is there any hope for a comparable strengthening of 1.2, giving a polynomial bound on the chromatic number of graphs with no path-induced copy of a rooted tree $(T,r)$ and with clique number at most $t$?
rows.context_text: Scott, Seymour and Spirkl proved (Theorem 3.2) that excluding an induced tree and $K_{t,t}$ as a subgraph yields a polynomial bound $f(t)$ on average degree. The authors ask whether an analogous polynomial bound on chromatic number can be obtained for 1.2, which imposes the weaker hypothesis of bounded clique number at the cost of weakening the conclusion to a path-induced copy.
rows.source_text: Is there any hope for a comparable strengthening of 1.2, giving a polynomial bound on the chromatic number of graphs with no path-induced copy of a rooted tree $(T,r)$ and with clique number at most $t$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2302.08922 — Polynomial bounds for path-induced copy theorem (1.2)
rows.class_notes: References internal statement 1.2 and the paper-local 'path-induced copy of a rooted tree', not defined in the record.

rows.title: Polynomial bounds for the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture
rows.paper_title: A note on the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture
rows.statement_text: It is possible that the Gy\'{a}rf\'{a}s-Sumner conjecture holds with polynomial bounds; that is, 1.1 holds with a bound on the chromatic number that is polynomial in $t$.
rows.context_text: The authors note that polynomial bounds on the $\chi$-bounding function have recently been established for trees not containing $P_5$ as an induced subgraph, and suggest that the full Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture may admit a polynomial $\chi$-bounding function in $t$ for every fixed tree $T$.
rows.source_text: It is possible that the Gy\'{a}rf\'{a}s-Sumner conjecture holds with polynomial bounds; that is, 1.1 holds with a bound on the chromatic number that is polynomial in $t$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2302.08922 — Polynomial bounds for the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture holds with polynomial χ-bounding functions (chromatic number bounded by a polynomial in the clique number t) remains open in full generality. Significant partial progress has been made since 2023: polynomial χ-boundedness was established for P₅-free graphs (arXiv:2512.24907, Dec 2024), and the polynomial version of the conjecture was proved for intersection graphs of axis-aligned boxes of any fixed dimension (arXiv:2407.16882, Jul 2024). A further 2025 preprint (arXiv:2504.21127) improves bounds for P₅-free graphs and gives compositional results, but the full polynomial conjecture for all trees is still open.
rows.class_notes: Polynomial-chi-bounding-function version of Gyarfas-Sumner; standard vocabulary plus an exists-polynomial wrapper.

rows.title: Problem 41
rows.paper_title: Neighborhood complexity of planar graphs
rows.statement_text: Determine the profile and neighborhood complexities of planar graphs up to a constant factor. For profile complexity, it is known to be in $\Omega(r^{3})$ (Theorem 36) and in $\operatorname{\mathcal{O}}(r^{4})$ (Theorem 2). For neighborhood complexity, it is known to be in $\Omega(r^{2})$ (Theorem 36 and Lemma 8) and in $\operatorname{\mathcal{O}}(r^{4})$ (Theorem 2).
rows.context_text: The paper closes with two open problems. The first asks for tight bounds on the profile and neighborhood complexities of planar graphs; the existing lower bounds ($\Omega(r^3)$ for profile, $\Omega(r^2)$ for neighborhood) and the paper's new upper bound ($\mathcal{O}(r^4)$ for both) leave a small gap that the authors consider tantalizingly close.
rows.source_text: Determine the profile and neighborhood complexities of planar graphs up to a constant factor. For profile complexity, it is known to be in $\Omega(r^{3})$ (Theorem 36) and in $\operatorname{\mathcal{O}}(r^{4})$ (Theorem 2). For neighborhood complexity, it is known to be in $\Omega(r^{2})$ (Theorem 36 and Lemma 8) and in $\operatorname{\mathcal{O}}(r^{4})$ (Theorem 2).
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine ... up to a constant factor' asks for a value, not a proposition.
rows.class_notes: Profile/neighborhood complexity also not defined in the record.

rows.title: Problem 42
rows.paper_title: Neighborhood complexity of planar graphs
rows.statement_text: Are the profile and neighborhood complexities of $K_{t}$-minor-free graphs in $r^{\operatorname{\mathcal{O}}(t)}$?
rows.context_text: The second open problem asks whether the polynomial bound for proper minor-closed classes can be sharpened so that the degree of the polynomial grows only logarithmically (or polynomially) in the size $t$ of the excluded complete minor, rather than being a fixed polynomial independent of $t$.
rows.source_text: Are the profile and neighborhood complexities of $K_{t}$-minor-free graphs in $r^{\operatorname{\mathcal{O}}(t)}$?
rows.status_semantics: Beaudou, Bok, Foucaud, Quiroz, and Raymond (arXiv:2501.08895, 2025) prove that the r-profile complexity of $K_h$-minor-free graphs is in $O(h^{O(h)} \cdot r^{3h-3} \cdot k)$, improving Joret–Rambaud's earlier bound of $O_h(r^{h^2-1} k)$. Since the exponent $3h-3$ is $O(h)=O(t)$, this affirmatively answers Problem 42, confirming that both the profile and neighbourhood complexities of $K_t$-minor-free graphs lie in $r^{O(t)}$. The paper explicitly states it answers an open question of Joret and Rambaud.
rows.class_notes: Precise yes/no but 'profile complexity' and 'neighborhood complexity' are paper-local and undefined in the record; marked solved.

rows.title: Conjecture 2
rows.paper_title: Partitioning edges of a planar graph into linear forests and a matching
rows.statement_text: For every planar graph $G$ of odd maximum degree $\Delta \geq 7$ the edges of $G$ can be partitioned into $\frac{\Delta-1}{2}$ linear forests and one matching.
rows.context_text: This conjecture refines the known linear arboricity bound for planar graphs with odd maximum degree by replacing one linear forest with the weaker structure of a matching. The case $\Delta \geq 11$ follows from existing results; the paper resolves $\Delta = 9$ (Theorem 2), leaving $\Delta = 7$ as the sole remaining open case.
rows.source_text: For every planar graph $G$ of odd maximum degree $\Delta \geq 7$ the edges of $G$ can be partitioned into $\frac{\Delta-1}{2}$ linear forests and one matching.
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture holds for all odd Δ ≥ 9: the case Δ ≥ 11 follows from prior linear arboricity results, and the source paper (arXiv:2302.13312) resolves Δ = 9. The sole remaining open case is Δ = 7. A targeted web search and citation lookup (Semantic Scholar) found no post-2023 paper resolving the Δ = 7 case.
rows.class_notes: Fully standard vocabulary (planar, linear forest, matching); only Delta=7 case remains open.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.6
rows.paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VIII. Excluding a path and a complete multipartite graph
rows.statement_text: Every forest is multibounding.
rows.context_text: A graph $H$ is multibounding if for every $d \geq 1$ there is a polynomial $f(t)$ such that, for all $t \geq 1$, every $H$-free graph with no $K_d(t)$ subgraph satisfies $\chi(G) \leq f(t)$. Every multibounding graph must be a forest satisfying the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture, and any forest satisfying the polynomial-bound Conjecture 1.2 is multibounding. The paper establishes that paths, brooms, and disjoint unions of multibounding forests are multibounding, motivating the general conjecture.
rows.source_text: Every forest is multibounding.
rows.class_notes: 'Multibounding' is fully defined in context_text, so B2 despite recovery flag; needs exists-polynomial wrapper.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.3
rows.paper_title: The limit points of the top and bottom eigenvalues of regular graphs
rows.statement_text: For any $d \geq 3$ and any fixed $k$, the set of all limit points of the vectors $(\lambda_1(G_i), \lambda_2(G_i), \ldots, \lambda_k(G_i))$ for an infinite sequence $G_i$ of $d$-regular graphs is exactly the set $$B(d,k) = \{(\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_k) : d = \mu_1 \geq \mu_2 \geq \mu_3 \geq \ldots \geq \mu_k \geq 2\sqrt{d-1}\}.$$
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 establishes the case $k=2$ (the set of limit points of $\lambda_2$ for $d$-regular graphs is $[2\sqrt{d-1}, d]$). The authors conjecture this extends to vectors of the top $k$ eigenvalues for any fixed $k$, noting that the containment $\subseteq B(d,k)$ is already known but they have not been able to prove that every point of $B(d,k)$ is a limit point even for $k=3$. Theorem 1.5 shows every point of a slightly smaller set (with $2\sqrt{d-1}$ replaced by $2\sqrt{d-1} + 1/\sqrt{d-1}$) is indeed a limit point of $d$-regular graph sequences.
rows.source_text: For any $d \geq 3$ and any fixed $k$, the set of all limit points of the vectors $(\lambda_1(G_i), \lambda_2(G_i), \ldots, \lambda_k(G_i))$ for an infinite sequence $G_i$ of $d$-regular graphs is exactly the set $$B(d,k) = \{(\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_k) : d = \mu_1 \geq \mu_2 \geq \mu_3 \geq \ldots \geq \mu_k \geq 2\sqrt{d-1}\}.$$
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.3 from arXiv:2304.01281 has been fully resolved by Dingding Dong and Theo McKenzie in December 2024. Their paper 'Arbitrary Spectral Edge of Regular Graphs' (arXiv:2412.09570) proves that for any d >= 3 and k >= 2, the set of all limit points of the vectors (lambda_1(G_i), ..., lambda_k(G_i)) for infinite sequences of d-regular graphs is exactly B(d,k) = {(mu_1,...,mu_k) : d = mu_1 >= ... >= mu_k >= 2sqrt(d-1)}, confirming the conjecture in full. The authors explicitly state that the k=2 case was due to Alon and Wei and that their result confirms the conjecture of theirs.
rows.class_notes: Limit-point set of top-k eigenvalue vectors of d-regular graph sequences; solved, real/limit vocabulary.

rows.title: Problem 1
rows.paper_title: Temporalizing digraphs via linear-size balanced bi-trees
rows.statement_text: What is the value of $r_{t}$?
rows.context_text: The quantity $r_{t}$ denotes the best approximation ratio achievable for FCPP (Forward Connected Pairs Problem) in strongly connected digraphs. The paper establishes $r_{t}>0$ as its central result, but the exact value of $r_{t}$ remains open. The paper shows $r_{t}\geq 1/9$ via its bi-tree construction.
rows.source_text: What is the value of $r_{t}$?
rows.defer_reason: Asks for the exact value of the approximation ratio r_t, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Problem 2
rows.paper_title: Temporalizing digraphs via linear-size balanced bi-trees
rows.statement_text: What is the maximum $c_{b}$ for which every strongly connected directed graph on $n$ vertices has a balanced bi-tree of size at least $c_{b}.n$?
rows.context_text: The paper proves $c_{b}\geq 1/3$ (Theorem 4), where both the out-tree $|T^{+}|$ and in-tree $|T^{-}|$ of the bi-tree each have size at least $n/6$. The exact optimal constant $c_{b}$ is unknown.
rows.source_text: What is the maximum $c_{b}$ for which every strongly connected directed graph on $n$ vertices has a balanced bi-tree of size at least $c_{b}.n$?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the maximum c_b' asks for an exact extremal constant.
rows.class_notes: Determining the optimal constant c_b; the known bound c_b >= 1/3 would be a B2 fragment.

rows.title: Problem 3
rows.paper_title: Temporalizing digraphs via linear-size balanced bi-trees
rows.statement_text: Can we compute a left-maximal dfs-tree in $o(n^{2})$ time in minimally strongly connected digraphs? What about the complexity in the general case?
rows.context_text: The Left-DFS tree is a key algorithmic ingredient introduced in the paper for constructing balanced bi-trees. The current construction runs in $O(n^{2})$ time; whether this can be improved is left open, both for the minimally strongly connected special case and for general strongly connected digraphs.
rows.source_text: Can we compute a left-maximal dfs-tree in $o(n^{2})$ time in minimally strongly connected digraphs? What about the complexity in the general case?
rows.class_notes: Left-maximal dfs-tree is a paper-local construction not defined in the record; second half is open-ended.

rows.title: Problem 4
rows.paper_title: Temporalizing digraphs via linear-size balanced bi-trees
rows.statement_text: Is there a polytime constant approximation algorithm for RFCPP?
rows.context_text: RFCPP (Request Forward Connected Pairs Problem) is the variant of FCPP where only a specified set $R\subseteq\binom{V}{2}$ of pairs needs to be forward connected. Since FCPP (the case $R=\binom{V}{2}$) is in APX, the question is whether a constant-factor approximation also exists for RFCPP, and in particular whether a linear fraction of requests in $R$ can always be satisfied.
rows.source_text: Is there a polytime constant approximation algorithm for RFCPP?
rows.class_notes: Yes/no APX question but FCPP/RFCPP and forward-connectedness are paper-local and only sketched in context.

rows.title: Conjecture 1
rows.paper_title: Temporalizing digraphs via linear-size balanced bi-trees
rows.statement_text: Every strong digraph $D$ on $n$ vertices has a $O(\log n)$ size forward cover.
rows.context_text: A forward cover is a small set of vertex orderings such that every pair of vertices is forward connected in at least one ordering. The conjecture is verified for bi-oriented graphs (undirected graphs with each edge replaced by two arcs) using a centroid-decomposition argument. The general case for arbitrary strongly connected digraphs remains open.
rows.source_text: Every strong digraph $D$ on $n$ vertices has a $O(\log n)$ size forward cover.
rows.class_notes: 'Forward connected in an ordering' is never defined in the record; also O(log n) asymptotic wrapper needed.

rows.title: Problem 1
rows.paper_title: On the choosability of $H$-minor-free graphs
rows.statement_text: Characterize the class $\mathcal{H}$ of graphs $H$ such that $f_\ell(H') = v(H')-1$ for all $H' \subseteq H$.
rows.context_text: The authors ask for a structural characterization of graphs $H$ for which the list chromatic number of $H$-minor-free graphs matches the trivial lower bound $v(H)-1$. The problem is posed in the spirit of perfect graph theory, seeking the largest subgraph-closed class with this property; the paper's main results (Theorems 2 and 4) strongly limit the horizon for positive instances.
rows.source_text: Characterize the class $\mathcal{H}$ of graphs $H$ such that $f_\ell(H') = v(H')-1$ for all $H' \subseteq H$.
rows.defer_reason: Open-ended structural characterization of the class H, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture (Section 9, characterization of 2-extremal digraphs)
rows.paper_title: Digraph Colouring and Arc-Connectivity
rows.statement_text: [Statement unavailable — Section 9 content absent from PDF extraction]
rows.context_text: The paper's main theorem (Theorem 1.8) characterizes $k$-extremal digraphs for $k = 1$ and $k \geq 3$, but the authors were unable to handle the case $k = 2$ (i.e., digraphs $D$ with $\vec{\chi}(D) = \lambda(D)+1 = 3$). The introduction explicitly states: 'in the last section, we discuss the case of 2-extremal digraphs and propose a conjecture for their characterization.'
rows.source_text: [Statement unavailable — Section 9 content absent from PDF extraction]
rows.class_notes: Statement text absent from extraction; only known to be a characterization conjecture for 2-extremal digraphs.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.7
rows.paper_title: Flashes and rainbows in tournaments
rows.statement_text: For all positive integers $l$ and $k$, $t(l, k) = l^{k-1}$.
rows.context_text: Here $t(l,k)$ denotes the smallest integer such that every colouring of the edges of any tournament with more than $t(l,k)$ vertices contains an $l$-flash or a $k$-rainbow. Since $t(l,k) \geq f(l,k) \geq l^{k-1}$, this is a tournament strengthening of the Lefmann–Rödl–Thomas Conjecture 1.2. The paper proves two upper bounds on $t(l,k)$ confirming it asymptotically for $l = \omega(k^2)$, and also shows that some tournaments are strictly worse than transitive tournaments (Theorem 1.10).
rows.source_text: For all positive integers $l$ and $k$, $t(l, k) = l^{k-1}$.
rows.class_notes: t(l,k)=l^{k-1} is precise but l-flash and k-rainbow are not defined in the record.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (Equality in Theorem 1.1 near the zero-rate threshold)
rows.paper_title: Set-coloring Ramsey numbers and error-correcting codes near the zero-rate threshold
rows.statement_text: We suspect that there may even be equality in Theorem 1.1 when $s$ is sufficiently close to $(1 - 1/q)r$, i.e., $R(q+1; r, s) = A_q(r, s) + 1$, though our methods fall somewhat short of proving this.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 (from prior work [7]) establishes the lower bound $R(q+1; r, s) \geq A_q(r, s) + 1$ for all positive integers $q, r, s$ with $r > s$. The current paper proves Theorem 1.2, an upper bound showing $R(q+1; r, s) \leq (1+\epsilon)A_q(r, s - cj)$ when $s$ is close to $(1-1/q)r$, but the gap between lower and upper bounds remains. The authors conjecture the lower bound is essentially sharp in this intermediate regime.
rows.source_text: We suspect that there may even be equality in Theorem 1.1 when $s$ is sufficiently close to $(1 - 1/q)r$, i.e., $R(q+1; r, s) = A_q(r, s) + 1$, though our methods fall somewhat short of proving this.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2305.14132 — Informal Conjecture (Equality in Theorem 1.1 near the zero-rate threshold)
rows.class_notes: A_q(r,s) undefined in record and the regime 's sufficiently close to (1-1/q)r' is informal.

rows.title: Question 10
rows.paper_title: Chromatic number is not tournament-local
rows.statement_text: Does there exist, for each integer $d$, an integer $\chi$ such that for every graph $G$ with $\chi(G)\geqslant\chi$ and every tournament $T$ on the same vertex set, there is a vertex $v$ for which $G[N^{+}_{T}(v)]$ has degeneracy at least $d$?
rows.context_text: In the closing remarks the authors note they have been unable to determine whether high chromatic number forces an out-neighbourhood with high degeneracy. The paper's disproof of Conjecture 1 shows chromatic number is not preserved, but degeneracy is a weaker measure and may behave differently.
rows.source_text: Does there exist, for each integer $d$, an integer $\chi$ such that for every graph $G$ with $\chi(G)\geqslant\chi$ and every tournament $T$ on the same vertex set, there is a vertex $v$ for which $G[N^{+}_{T}(v)]$ has degeneracy at least $d$?
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no in standard vocabulary: chromatic number, tournament out-neighbourhood, degeneracy.

rows.title: Conjecture 11
rows.paper_title: Chromatic number is not tournament-local
rows.statement_text: For every graph $G$ with sufficiently large chromatic number, and every tournament $T$ on the same vertex set, there exists a vertex $v$ such that $G[N^{+}_{T}(v)]$ contains a cycle.
rows.context_text: The authors suspect that the case $d=2$ of Question 10 is true, i.e., high chromatic number should force some out-neighbourhood to contain a cycle. This is the weakest non-trivial instance of the degeneracy question, since bipartite graphs (which arise in their counterexamples) are acyclic-free but 1-degenerate.
rows.source_text: For every graph $G$ with sufficiently large chromatic number, and every tournament $T$ on the same vertex set, there exists a vertex $v$ such that $G[N^{+}_{T}(v)]$ contains a cycle.
rows.class_notes: d=2 case of Question 10 (axv_2305_15585_00): out-neighbourhood contains a cycle.

rows.title: Conjecture 12
rows.paper_title: Chromatic number is not tournament-local
rows.statement_text: For every positive integer $k$, there exists a $\chi$ such that if $T$ is the uniformly random tournament on vertex set $[N]$, then with high probability (as $N\to\infty$), for every graph $G$ on $[N]$ with $\chi(G)\geqslant\chi$ there is a vertex $v\in[N]$ for which $G[N^{+}_{T}(v)]\geqslant k$.
rows.context_text: The counterexamples to Conjecture 1 use very structured tournaments; the authors conjecture that a random tournament cannot be used as such a counterexample, i.e., that the Scott–Seymour phenomenon holds with high probability for the random tournament.
rows.source_text: For every positive integer $k$, there exists a $\chi$ such that if $T$ is the uniformly random tournament on vertex set $[N]$, then with high probability (as $N\to\infty$), for every graph $G$ on $[N]$ with $\chi(G)\geqslant\chi$ there is a vertex $v\in[N]$ for which $G[N^{+}_{T}(v)]\geqslant k$.
rows.class_notes: Statement garbled at the end: 'G[N+_T(v)] >= k' presumably means chi(G[N+_T(v)]) >= k; random tournament w.h.p.

rows.title: Conjecture 13
rows.paper_title: Chromatic number is not tournament-local
rows.statement_text: There exists $f(N)$ satisfying $f(N)=o(\log N)$ such that for every $N$-vertex graph $G$ with $\chi(G)\geqslant f(N)$, and every tournament $T$ on the same vertex set, there is a vertex $v$ for which $\chi(G[N^{+}_{T}(v)])\geqslant 3$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 2 shows a collection of at most $\lceil\log_2(N)/\lfloor\chi/2-1\rfloor\rceil$ out-neighbourhoods suffices to force chromatic number at least 3; the authors ask whether $o(\log(N)/\chi)$ out-neighbourhoods (or even a single vertex) suffice when the chromatic number threshold itself grows with $N$ as $o(\log N)$.
rows.source_text: There exists $f(N)$ satisfying $f(N)=o(\log N)$ such that for every $N$-vertex graph $G$ with $\chi(G)\geqslant f(N)$, and every tournament $T$ on the same vertex set, there is a vertex $v$ for which $\chi(G[N^{+}_{T}(v)])\geqslant 3$.
rows.class_notes: Existence of f(N)=o(log N) threshold; asymptotic quantification over N.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.6
rows.paper_title: Tree independence number I. (Even hole, diamond, pyramid)-free graphs
rows.statement_text: The class of (even hole, diamond)-free graph has bounded tree-$\alpha$.
rows.context_text: The paper's main result handles (even hole, diamond, pyramid)-free graphs; extending to (even hole, diamond)-free graphs (dropping the pyramid-free condition) is posed as the next natural step. No polynomial-time algorithm for MWIS is yet known for the larger (even hole, diamond)-free class, and the authors suggest that the methods developed here might be extendable to settle this conjecture.
rows.source_text: The class of (even hole, diamond)-free graph has bounded tree-$\alpha$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.6 asks for a constant (n-independent) bound on tree-α for the class of (even hole, diamond)-free graphs. The source paper itself established this for the smaller class also excluding pyramids. The follow-up paper arXiv:2407.08927 (Tree Independence Number IV, 2024) proves that the strictly larger class of all even-hole-free graphs has tree-α at most O(log^10 n), a polylogarithmic bound that does not imply the conjectured constant bound. No verified source found establishes the constant bound for the full (even hole, diamond)-free class.
rows.class_notes: Needs tree-independence-number (tree-alpha) primitive; even hole/diamond are standard forbidden-subgraph vocabulary.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.7
rows.paper_title: Tree independence number I. (Even hole, diamond, pyramid)-free graphs
rows.statement_text: The class of (even hole, $K_{t}$)-free graph has logarithmic treewidth.
rows.context_text: It is known that (even hole, $K_3$)-free graphs have treewidth at most 5, while (even hole, $K_4$)-free graphs do not have bounded treewidth due to layered-wheel constructions. Removing diamonds from the (even hole, $K_4$)-free class restores bounded treewidth; this conjecture posits that for general $t$ the (even hole, $K_t$)-free class has logarithmic treewidth, in line with an upcoming preprint of some of the same authors.
rows.source_text: The class of (even hole, $K_{t}$)-free graph has logarithmic treewidth.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.7 from arXiv:2305.16258, asserting that (even hole, $K_t$)-free graphs have logarithmic treewidth, was proved in arXiv:2402.14211 (Chudnovsky, Gartland, Hajebi, Lokshtanov, Spirkl, 2024). That paper establishes that for every integer $t\geq 1$ there exists a constant $c_t$ such that every $n$-vertex even-hole-free graph with no clique of size $t$ has treewidth at most $c_t \log n$, resolving Conjecture 1.2 of Sintiari and Trotignon and matching the 'upcoming preprint by some of the same authors' referenced in the source paper. The logarithmic bound is asymptotically best possible.
rows.class_notes: Solved: (even hole, K_t)-free graphs have treewidth O(log n).

rows.title: Question 1.1
rows.paper_title: On heroes in digraphs with forbidden induced forests
rows.statement_text: Which finite families $F$ of digraphs are $\vec{\chi}$-finite?
rows.context_text: A family $F$ is $\vec{\chi}$-finite if there is a constant $c$ such that all $F$-free digraphs have dichromatic number at most $c$. This is the overarching open question motivating the study of heroes in hereditary classes of digraphs, and all main results of the paper contribute to it.
rows.source_text: Which finite families $F$ of digraphs are $\vec{\chi}$-finite?
rows.defer_reason: 'Which finite families F are chi-vec-finite' is an open-ended classification, not a proposition.

rows.title: Open problem: hero status of $\Delta(1,m,m')$ for degree-4 oriented stars
rows.paper_title: On heroes in digraphs with forbidden induced forests
rows.statement_text: Determine for which integers $m, m' \geq 1$ the set $\{\Delta(1, m, m'), F\}$ is $\vec{\chi}$-finite when $F$ is an oriented star of degree $4$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.6 shows that if $F$ is an oriented star of degree 4 and $\{H, F\}$ is $\vec{\chi}$-finite, then either $H$ is a transitive tournament or $H = \Delta(1, m, m')$ for some $m, m' \geq 1$. The transitive tournament case is fully settled by Theorem 1.4 (Chudnovsky–Scott–Seymour), but the authors explicitly state that the case $H = \Delta(1, m, m')$ remains open.
rows.source_text: Determine for which integers $m, m' \geq 1$ the set $\{\Delta(1, m, m'), F\}$ is $\vec{\chi}$-finite when $F$ is an oriented star of degree $4$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2306.04710 — Open problem: hero status of $\Delta(1,m,m')$ for degree-4 oriented stars
rows.defer_reason: Classification request ('determine for which m,m'') rather than a single yes/no proposition.
rows.class_notes: Determine-for-which over integer parameters m,m'; no conjectured answer stated.

rows.title: Open problem: hero status of $\Delta(1,2,2)$
rows.paper_title: On heroes in digraphs with forbidden induced forests
rows.statement_text: Determine whether $\Delta(1,2,2)$ is a hero in $\{K_1 + \vec{P}_2\}$-free digraphs, i.e., whether $\{\Delta(1,2,2),\, K_1 + \vec{P}_2\}$ is $\vec{\chi}$-finite.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.11 (Aboulker, Aubian, Charbit) almost completely characterizes heroes in $\{K_1+\vec{P}_2\}$-free digraphs, and Theorem 1.12 of this paper extends the same structural characterization to $\{H, rK_1+\vec{P}_3\}$-free digraphs; in both settings the sole unresolved case is $H = \Delta(1,2,2)$.
rows.source_text: Determine whether $\Delta(1,2,2)$ is a hero in $\{K_1 + \vec{P}_2\}$-free digraphs, i.e., whether $\{\Delta(1,2,2),\, K_1 + \vec{P}_2\}$ is $\vec{\chi}$-finite.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2306.04710 — Open problem: hero status of $\Delta(1,2,2)$
rows.class_notes: B3-vs-B2 resolved to B2: hero, chi-vec-finite and Delta(1,2,2) are standard vocabulary of the heroes-in-digraphs literature (Berger et al.), so the yes/no question is self-contained; still needs dichromatic/hero primitives, so not clean.

rows.title: Question 1
rows.paper_title: The grid-minor theorem revisited
rows.statement_text: Can the upper bound on $\operatorname{utw}(\mathcal{G}_{X})$ in Equation 1 be improved? In particular, is $\operatorname{utw}(\mathcal{G}_{X})$ at most a polynomial function of $\operatorname{td}(X)$?
rows.context_text: The paper's main theorem (Theorem 1) gives an upper bound on the underlying treewidth of $X$-minor-free graph classes in terms of $\operatorname{td}(X)$. This question opens the concluding open-problems section and asks whether that bound can be tightened to a polynomial dependence on $\operatorname{td}(X)$.
rows.source_text: Can the upper bound on $\operatorname{utw}(\mathcal{G}_{X})$ in Equation 1 be improved? In particular, is $\operatorname{utw}(\mathcal{G}_{X})$ at most a polynomial function of $\operatorname{td}(X)$?
rows.class_notes: Underlying treewidth utw is paper-local and the statement references internal Equation 1; polynomial-in-td part is precise.

rows.title: Question 2
rows.paper_title: The grid-minor theorem revisited
rows.statement_text: Is there a function $f$ such that for every graph $X$ there exists a function $c$ such that for every positive integer $t$ and for every graph $G$ with $\operatorname{tw}(G)<t$ that does not contain $X$ as a topological minor, there exists a graph $H$ of treewidth at most $f(\operatorname{td}(X))$ such that $G\mathrel{\ooalign{\raise 0.75348pt\hbox{$\subset$}\cr\raise-3.87495pt\hbox{\scalebox{0.9}{$\sim$}}\cr}}H\boxtimes K_{c(t)}$?
rows.context_text: This question asks whether Theorem 2 can be extended from the excluded-minor setting to the excluded-topological-minor setting. Campbell et al. proved a weaker version with $\operatorname{tw}(H)\leqslant|V(X)|$, and that bound is tight for complete graphs.
rows.source_text: Is there a function $f$ such that for every graph $X$ there exists a function $c$ such that for every positive integer $t$ and for every graph $G$ with $\operatorname{tw}(G)<t$ that does not contain $X$ as a topological minor, there exists a graph $H$ of treewidth at most $f(\operatorname{td}(X))$ such that $G\mathrel{\ooalign{\raise 0.75348pt\hbox{$\subset$}\cr\raise-3.87495pt\hbox{\scalebox{0.9}{$\sim$}}\cr}}H\boxtimes K_{c(t)}$?
rows.class_notes: Needs strong product and tree-depth primitives; garbled LaTeX symbol is 'isomorphic to a subgraph of'.

rows.title: Question 3
rows.paper_title: The grid-minor theorem revisited
rows.statement_text: Is there a function $g$ such that for every graph $X$, there is a constant $c$ such that for every $X$-minor-free graph $G$, $\chi_{p}(G)\leqslant c\cdot p^{g(\operatorname{td}(X))}$ for every $p\geqslant 1$?
rows.context_text: This question concerns $p$-centered colorings of $X$-minor-free graphs. The paper gives a positive answer when $X$ is apex, but cannot extend the proof to arbitrary excluded minors because the technique using chordal partitions does not transfer to $p$-centered colorings.
rows.source_text: Is there a function $g$ such that for every graph $X$, there is a constant $c$ such that for every $X$-minor-free graph $G$, $\chi_{p}(G)\leqslant c\cdot p^{g(\operatorname{td}(X))}$ for every $p\geqslant 1$?
rows.status_semantics: Question 3 asks for a function $g$ such that every $X$-minor-free graph $G$ satisfies $\chi_p(G)\leqslant c\cdot p^{g(\operatorname{td}(X))}$. Hodor, La, Micek, and Rambaud (arXiv:2411.02122, 2024) prove that $K_t$-minor-free graphs satisfy $\chi_p(G)=O(p^{t-1})$; since tree-depth is minor-monotone, any graph $X$ with $\operatorname{td}(X)=h$ satisfies $|V(X)|\leqslant 2^h-1$, so $X$ is a minor of $K_{2^h-1}$, and hence every $X$-minor-free graph is $K_{2^h-1}$-minor-free, yielding $\chi_p(G)=O(p^{2^h-2})$ and answering Question 3 affirmatively. The same team further sharpens these bounds in arXiv:2603.13097 (2026), determining the maximum $q$-centered chromatic number of $K_t$-minor-free graphs within an $O(q)$-factor.
rows.class_notes: Solved; needs p-centered coloring primitive, otherwise standard minor vocabulary.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.8
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraph density. IV. New graphs with the Erdős-Hajnal property
rows.statement_text: For every graph $H$, there exists $d>0$ such that for every $\varepsilon\in(0,\frac{1}{2})$ and every graph $G$ with $\mathrm{ind}_{H}(G)\leq(\varepsilon^{d}\lvert G\rvert)^{\lvert H\rvert}$, there is an $\varepsilon$-restricted subset of $V(G)$ with size at least $\varepsilon^{d}\lvert G\rvert$.
rows.context_text: This 'polynomial Nikiforov' statement, introduced without external attribution, unifies Theorem 1.6 and Conjecture 1.7. A graph $H$ satisfying it is called viral; Bucić, Fox, and Pham [4] proved that having the Erdős-Hajnal property is equivalent to being viral.
rows.source_text: For every graph $H$, there exists $d>0$ such that for every $\varepsilon\in(0,\frac{1}{2})$ and every graph $G$ with $\mathrm{ind}_{H}(G)\leq(\varepsilon^{d}\lvert G\rvert)^{\lvert H\rvert}$, there is an $\varepsilon$-restricted subset of $V(G)$ with size at least $\varepsilon^{d}\lvert G\rvert$.
rows.class_notes: Quantitative 'viral'/polynomial-Nikiforov reformulation equivalent to Erdos-Hajnal (Bucic-Fox-Pham); epsilon-restricted subset not defined in record.

rows.title: Polynomial degree-bounding function for hereditary degree-bounded classes
rows.paper_title: Induced $C_4$-free subgraphs with large average degree
rows.statement_text: Is there always a polynomial degree-bounding function for every hereditary degree-bounded class of graphs? That is, for every hereditary degree-bounded class $\mathcal{F}$, does there exist a polynomial $g$ such that $d(G) \leq g(\tau(G))$ for every $G \in \mathcal{F}$?
rows.context_text: Corollary 1.4 establishes that every hereditary degree-bounded class admits an exponential degree-bounding function of the form $C^{s^3}$, which is the first bound of any type on the rate of growth. The paper notes explicitly in both the abstract and the introduction that it is open whether a polynomial always suffices. The analogous question for $\chi$-boundedness has a negative answer (Briański, Davies, and Walczak).
rows.source_text: Is there always a polynomial degree-bounding function for every hereditary degree-bounded class of graphs? That is, for every hereditary degree-bounded class $\mathcal{F}$, does there exist a polynomial $g$ such that $d(G) \leq g(\tau(G))$ for every $G \in \mathcal{F}$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2307.08361 — Polynomial degree-bounding function for hereditary degree-bounded classes
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture was answered affirmatively by Girão and Hunter in a 2025 paper in the International Mathematics Research Notices, which proves that every hereditary degree-bounded class has a polynomial degree-bounding function, fully resolving the question left open in arXiv:2307.08361. A related earlier result by Bourneuf, Bucćić, Cook, and Davies (arXiv:2311.03341, published in Advances in Combinatorics 2024) proved polynomial bounds for hereditary classes defined by excluding induced subdivisions of a fixed graph, establishing a key step toward the full result.
rows.class_notes: Solved; precise question but d(G) and tau(G) (biclique parameter) are not defined in the record.

rows.title: Polynomial bound in k for C4-free subgraphs
rows.paper_title: Induced $C_4$-free subgraphs with large average degree
rows.statement_text: Does there exist a polynomial $p(k)$ such that every graph with average degree at least $p(k)$ contains a $C_4$-free subgraph with average degree at least $k$?
rows.context_text: Montgomery, Pokrovskiy, and Sudakov proved that every graph with average degree at least $k^{Ck^2}$ contains a $C_4$-free subgraph of average degree at least $k$, and also gave a lower bound of $k^{3-o(1)}$. The paper describes determining whether a polynomial in $k$ suffices as 'a very interesting problem'.
rows.source_text: Does there exist a polynomial $p(k)$ such that every graph with average degree at least $p(k)$ contains a $C_4$-free subgraph with average degree at least $k$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2307.08361 — Polynomial bound in k for C4-free subgraphs
rows.class_notes: Exists-a-polynomial wrapper over average degree and C4-free subgraphs; otherwise standard vocabulary.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.4
rows.paper_title: Correspondence coloring of random graphs
rows.statement_text: For every fixed positive $p \leq 1$, Erdős-Rényi random graph $G(n, 1-p)$ a.a.s. has correspondence chromatic number $\Theta(n/\log n)$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 gives the upper bound $O(n/\sqrt{\log n})$, and both extreme cases of the correspondence assignment—matching the list-coloring setting and a fully random assignment for $K_n$ (Theorem 1.3)—only require $O(n/\log n)$ colors. The authors suspect the upper bound can be improved to match the lower bound $\Omega(n/\log n)$ coming from the independence number. They also note that a disproof would yield a new example separating list and correspondence chromatic numbers.
rows.source_text: For every fixed positive $p \leq 1$, Erdős-Rényi random graph $G(n, 1-p)$ a.a.s. has correspondence chromatic number $\Theta(n/\log n)$.
rows.class_notes: Theta(n/log n) for correspondence chromatic number of G(n,1-p) a.a.s.; random-graph plus asymptotic layer.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.4
rows.paper_title: Catching a robber on a random $k$-uniform hypergraph
rows.statement_text: Let $H$ be a connected $k$-graph on $n$ vertices. Then $c(H) = O\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{n}{k}}\right)$.
rows.context_text: The authors introduce this as a generalisation of Meyniel's conjecture to $k$-uniform hypergraphs, motivated by a blow-up construction that shows $c(H) = \Omega\!\left(\sqrt{n/k}\right)$ is achievable. They further observe that for any polynomial $k = n^\alpha$ with $\alpha < 1$, Conjecture 1.4 implies Meyniel's conjecture.
rows.source_text: Let $H$ be a connected $k$-graph on $n$ vertices. Then $c(H) = O\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{n}{k}}\right)$.
rows.class_notes: Meyniel-type bound c(H)=O(sqrt(n/k)); needs cop-number game primitive on hypergraphs plus O() wrapper.

rows.title: Question 1.2
rows.paper_title: Factoring Pattern-Free Permutations into Separable ones
rows.statement_text: Is the maximum separable index among permutations avoiding a pattern of size $k$ polynomial in $k$?
rows.context_text: The authors' main theorem gives a doubly exponential upper bound $2^{2^{O(k)}}$ on the separable index of permutations avoiding a pattern of size $k$. Lower bounds from Fox's results on growth of pattern-avoiding classes show the maximum separable index is at least $\Omega(k^{1/4-\varepsilon})$ for any $\varepsilon > 0$, leaving a large gap. The question asks whether a polynomial upper bound might hold.
rows.source_text: Is the maximum separable index among permutations avoiding a pattern of size $k$ polynomial in $k$?
rows.defer_reason: Permutation-pattern combinatorics (separable index), not graph/digraph/hypergraph theory.

rows.title: Question 1.3
rows.paper_title: Factoring Pattern-Free Permutations into Separable ones
rows.statement_text: What is the parameterised complexity of computing $\mathrm{sep}(\sigma)$, and of approximating it within a constant factor?
rows.context_text: The paper's main result implies an FPT approximation for the separable index $\mathrm{sep}(\sigma)$ via pattern recognition, but the approximation function is triple exponential in $k$. The authors ask whether the complexity of computing or approximating $\mathrm{sep}(\sigma)$ exactly can be improved, possibly to an exact algorithm.
rows.source_text: What is the parameterised complexity of computing $\mathrm{sep}(\sigma)$, and of approximating it within a constant factor?
rows.defer_reason: Parameterised complexity of a permutation-pattern parameter; not graph theory.
rows.class_notes: Also open-ended complexity question.

rows.title: Gap in $r$-dependence of sign-pattern bound
rows.paper_title: Ordering Candidates via Vantage Points
rows.statement_text: The bound in Theorem 2.2 must have at least a linear dependence on $r$ if it is to depend polynomially on $m$; the current bound is exponential in $r$. It would be interesting to close this gap.
rows.context_text: Proposition 2.5 demonstrates that $r = 2^{m+1}$ functions suffice to realise all $2^m$ proper sign patterns even when $N = 1$ and $\Delta = s = 2$ are fixed, yielding a linear lower bound on the $r$-dependence that is far below the exponential upper bound in Theorem 2.2. The paper notes this discrepancy and calls for closing it.
rows.source_text: The bound in Theorem 2.2 must have at least a linear dependence on $r$ if it is to depend polynomially on $m$; the current bound is exponential in $r$. It would be interesting to close this gap.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2308.05208 — Gap in $r$-dependence of sign-pattern bound
rows.defer_reason: Real algebraic sign-pattern bounds for sums of square roots; not graph theory.
rows.class_notes: Also 'it would be interesting to close this gap' phrasing.

rows.title: Gap in connected-component count for sums of square roots
rows.paper_title: Ordering Candidates via Vantage Points
rows.statement_text: For $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ a linear combination of $r$ square roots of everywhere-positive quadratic polynomials, the number of connected components of $\mathbb{R} \setminus V(f)$ is at most $2^{r-1}+1$, while at least $2r-1$ components are achievable. It would be interesting to narrow the gap between this linear lower bound and the exponential upper bound.
rows.context_text: As a simpler companion question to the sign-pattern gap, the authors fix $N = m = 1$ and $\Delta = s = 2$. The upper bound $2^{r-1}+1$ follows from the same conjugate-multiplication trick as Theorem 2.2; an explicit construction with parameters $0 < a_1 < \cdots < a_{r-1}$ growing extremely quickly achieves $2r - 1$ connected components, giving a linear lower bound versus an exponential upper bound.
rows.source_text: For $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ a linear combination of $r$ square roots of everywhere-positive quadratic polynomials, the number of connected components of $\mathbb{R} \setminus V(f)$ is at most $2^{r-1}+1$, while at least $2r-1$ components are achievable. It would be interesting to narrow the gap between this linear lower bound and the exponential upper bound.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2308.05208 — Gap in connected-component count for sums of square roots
rows.defer_reason: Real analysis/geometry of linear combinations of square roots; not graph theory.
rows.class_notes: Gap-narrowing request over real function component counts.

rows.title: Five-point protrusive-but-not-witnessed construction
rows.paper_title: Ordering Candidates via Vantage Points
rows.statement_text: Does there exist a 5-element set $C \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ such that some protrusive ordering of $C$ is not contained in $\Psi(C)$?
rows.context_text: The authors construct a 6-element set $C \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ for which $\Psi(C)$ is strictly smaller than the set of all protrusive orderings, showing that the equality $\Psi(C) = \{\text{protrusive orderings of } C\}$ (which holds for $d=1$, $n \le 4$, and vertex-transitive polytopes) fails in general. Whether 5 points suffice for a counterexample is explicitly left open.
rows.source_text: Does there exist a 5-element set $C \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ such that some protrusive ordering of $C$ is not contained in $\Psi(C)$?
rows.status_semantics: Adrian Beker answered the question positively in arXiv:2309.12809 (2023), constructing an explicit 5-element set in ℝ² that has a protrusive ordering not realised by any finite multiset of vantage points, i.e., not contained in Ψ(C). The paper explicitly states it answers a question of Alon, Defant, Kravitz, and Zhu, and was published in Archiv der Mathematik (DOI 10.1007/s00013-024-02020-x).
rows.defer_reason: Discrete geometry of planar point configurations; not graph theory.
rows.class_notes: Solved; clean yes/no but about point sets in R^2 and vantage-point orderings.

rows.title: Open Problem: m(C_t × C_s) for all t, s
rows.paper_title: Connectivity Graph-Codes
rows.statement_text: Determine $m(C_t \times C_s)$ for all $t, s$.
rows.context_text: Proposition 3.2 establishes that $m(C_3 \times C_s) \leq 8$ for all $s > 36$ (since $K_3 = C_3$), answering a question raised in [6] about whether the connectivity code for large tori is strictly smaller than the trivial edge-connectivity bound $2^4 = 16$. The paper explicitly notes that the broader problem of determining $m(C_t \times C_s)$ for all $t$ and $s$ remains open.
rows.source_text: Determine $m(C_t \times C_s)$ for all $t, s$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2308.07653 — Open Problem: m(C_t × C_s) for all t, s
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine m(C_t x C_s)' is a compute-the-value request, not a single proposition; m() is paper-local (connectivity graph-codes)

rows.title: Informal Problem: extending the $\sqrt{m}$ Ramsey bound to more than two colors for graphs
rows.paper_title: Ramsey numbers of hypergraphs of a given size
rows.statement_text: It would be interesting to extend the result $r_2(G; 2) \leq 2^{c\sqrt{m}}$ (for any graph $G$ with $m$ edges and no isolated vertices) to more than two colors.
rows.context_text: Sudakov [22] resolved Erdős's conjecture by showing $r_2(G; 2) \leq 2^{c\sqrt{m}}$, but the argument works only for two colors. The paper notes this gap immediately after mentioning the resolution of the conjecture.
rows.source_text: It would be interesting to extend the result $r_2(G; 2) \leq 2^{c\sqrt{m}}$ (for any graph $G$ with $m$ edges and no isolated vertices) to more than two colors.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.2
rows.paper_title: The power of many colours
rows.statement_text: For any $1\leq s\leq\frac{\sqrt{r}}{\log r}$ and $n$ large enough we have $f(n,r,s)=\frac{s^{2}(\log r)^{1-o_{r}(1)}}{r}\cdot n$.
rows.context_text: The paper determines $f(n,r,s)$ — the maximum number of vertices guaranteably connected using up to $s$ colours in any $r$-edge colouring of $K_n$ — up to a logarithmic factor. The upper and lower bounds in the regime $s\leq\sqrt{r}/\log r$ differ by an absolute constant between 4 and 8 depending on divisibility properties of $r$; this conjecture posits the precise asymptotic behaviour in that regime. The authors note the gap originates in the proof technique and that under appropriate divisibility assumptions the result can be sharpened to within a factor of $2+\varepsilon$.
rows.source_text: For any $1\leq s\leq\frac{\sqrt{r}}{\log r}$ and $n$ large enough we have $f(n,r,s)=\frac{s^{2}(\log r)^{1-o_{r}(1)}}{r}\cdot n$.
rows.class_notes: f(n,r,s) defined in context (largest vertex set connectable with <=s colours in any r-colouring of K_n); o_r(1) exponent needs asymptotic wrapper

rows.paper_title: The power of many colours

rows.title: Conjecture 2
rows.paper_title: Clustered Colouring of Odd-$H$-Minor-Free Graphs
rows.statement_text: For every graph $H$, $\chi_{\_{\Delta}}(\mathcal{G}_{\_{H}}^{\text{odd}}) = \chi_{\_{\star}}(\mathcal{G}_{\_{H}}^{\text{odd}}) = \overline{\operatorname{td}}(H)-1$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1 establishes the chain $\overline{\operatorname{td}}(H)-1=\chi_{\_{\Delta}}(\mathcal{G}_{\_{H}})\leqslant\chi_{\_{\Delta}}(\mathcal{G}_{\_{H}}^{\text{odd}})\leqslant\chi_{\_{\star}}(\mathcal{G}_{\_{H}}^{\text{odd}})\leqslant 3\cdot 2^{\overline{\operatorname{td}}(H)}-4$. Conjecture 2 posits that both the defective and clustered chromatic numbers of $H$-odd-minor-free graphs equal the lower bound $\overline{\operatorname{td}}(H)-1$, closing the exponential gap left by the main theorem.
rows.source_text: For every graph $H$, $\chi_{\_{\Delta}}(\mathcal{G}_{\_{H}}^{\text{odd}}) = \chi_{\_{\star}}(\mathcal{G}_{\_{H}}^{\text{odd}}) = \overline{\operatorname{td}}(H)-1$.
rows.class_notes: Needs defective/clustered chromatic number of a class, odd minors, and connected tree-depth as primitives; all standard in this literature

rows.title: Question 1
rows.paper_title: Boundary rigidity of 3D CAT(0) cube complexes
rows.statement_text: Under what conditions is a finite $k$-dimensional cube complex $X \hookrightarrow \mathbb{R}^k$ reconstructible up to combinatorial type from its boundary distances?
rows.context_text: This question arises as the natural higher-dimensional generalisation of Haslegrave's 2D result (Theorem 1), where a planar quadrangulation with all internal vertices of degree at least 4 is determined by its boundary distances. The paper answers it for $k=3$ via Theorem 2 under the CAT(0) condition, while the cases $k \geq 4$ are noted to involve non-trivial complications discussed in Section 10.
rows.source_text: Under what conditions is a finite $k$-dimensional cube complex $X \hookrightarrow \mathbb{R}^k$ reconstructible up to combinatorial type from its boundary distances?
rows.status_semantics: The CAT(0) case of Question 1 has been fully resolved: Chalopin and Chepoi (arXiv:2310.04223) proved that all finite CAT(0) cube complexes of any dimension are reconstructible up to isomorphism from their boundary distances (computed in the 1-skeleton), explicitly confirming the conjecture of Haslegrave–Scott–Tamitegama–Tan and in particular settling the open k≥4 cases. The broader question of what conditions beyond CAT(0) might suffice, and whether CAT(0) is in any sense necessary, remains open.
rows.defer_reason: Boundary rigidity of cube complexes in R^k is discrete geometry, and 'under what conditions is X reconstructible' is an open-ended characterization request, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Both classifiers deferred it; B5 chosen over B4 since scope exclusion dominates: k-dimensional CAT(0) cube complexes embedded in R^k are discrete/metric geometry, with graph content only via boundary distances.
