rows.title: Conjecture 1.4
rows.paper_title: On an induced version of Menger's theorem
rows.statement_text: For every $d, \Delta \in \mathbb{N}$ there exists a constant $C = C(d, \Delta) > 0$ such that the following holds. If $k \in \mathbb{N}$, $G$ is a graph with $\Delta(G) \leq \Delta$ and $X, Y \subseteq V(G)$, then there exists either (1) $k$ $X$-$Y$-paths pairwise at distance at least $d$ in $G$, or (2) a set of less than $Ck$ vertices in $G$ which separates $X$ and $Y$.
rows.context_text: This conjecture is derived as a consequence of the stronger (c independent of k) form of Conjecture 1.2 for graphs of bounded maximum degree $\Delta$: the ball $B_G(Z, cd)$ can be bounded in size by $\Delta^{cd+1}k$, yielding a linear separator of size $Ck$. The paper proves this conjecture in the first non-trivial case $d = 2$ as its first main result (Theorem 1.5).
rows.source_text: For every $d, \Delta \in \mathbb{N}$ there exists a constant $C = C(d, \Delta) > 0$ such that the following holds. If $k \in \mathbb{N}$, $G$ is a graph with $\Delta(G) \leq \Delta$ and $X, Y \subseteq V(G)$, then there exists either (1) $k$ $X$-$Y$-paths pairwise at distance at least $d$ in $G$, or (2) a set of less than $Ck$ vertices in $G$ which separates $X$ and $Y$.
rows.class_notes: Fully self-contained induced-Menger statement: k X-Y paths pairwise at distance >= d vs separator of size < Ck in bounded-degree graphs

rows.title: Question 3.5
rows.paper_title: Clique number of tournaments
rows.statement_text: Is it true that for every tournament $T$, there exists $\prec\in\mathfrak{S}(T)$ such that $\prec$ is both a $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}$-ordering and a $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-ordering?
rows.context_text: The paper establishes that for any $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}$-ordering $\prec$ of a tournament $T$, one has $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}(T)\leq\chi(T^{\prec})\leq\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}(T)^{2}$. The question asks whether a single ordering can simultaneously witness both the clique number and the dichromatic number.
rows.source_text: Is it true that for every tournament $T$, there exists $\prec\in\mathfrak{S}(T)$ such that $\prec$ is both a $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}$-ordering and a $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-ordering?
rows.class_notes: Relies on paper-local notions S(T), omega-vec-ordering and chi-vec-ordering, none defined in the record

rows.title: Question 3.10
rows.paper_title: Clique number of tournaments
rows.statement_text: Is it true that if a class of tournaments $\mathcal{T}$ is polynomially $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-bounded, then so is $\mathcal{T}^{subst}$.
rows.context_text: The hereditary closure of $\{\widetilde{S}_{n},n\in\mathbb{N}\}$ equals $\{TT_{1},TT_{2},\overrightarrow{C_{3}}\}^{subst}$, which satisfies an exponential bound, and it is open whether the order of magnitude $(3/2)^n$ of $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}(\widetilde{S}_n)$ can be reduced to a polynomial. The question asks whether polynomial $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-boundedness is preserved under substitution closure.
rows.source_text: Is it true that if a class of tournaments $\mathcal{T}$ is polynomially $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-bounded, then so is $\mathcal{T}^{subst}$.
rows.class_notes: 'Polynomially chi-vec-bounded' presupposes the paper's tournament clique number omega-vec, not defined in the record

rows.title: Conjecture 3.12
rows.paper_title: Clique number of tournaments
rows.statement_text: Let $k\geq 1$. The class of tournaments with twin-width at most $k$ is $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-bounded.
rows.context_text: For every integer $k\geq 2$, the tournament $S_k$ has twin-width $1$ and dichromatic number $k$, so tournaments with bounded twin-width can have arbitrarily large dichromatic number. Nevertheless, the authors conjecture that $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-boundedness holds. Conjecture 3.13 is presented as a sufficient condition implying this conjecture.
rows.source_text: Let $k\geq 1$. The class of tournaments with twin-width at most $k$ is $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-bounded.

rows.context_text: This conjecture is introduced as a strengthening implying Conjecture 3.12 on $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-boundedness of bounded-twin-width tournaments. It concerns the existence of a single ordering simultaneously witnessing relevant structural and coloring properties.

rows.paper_title: Clique number of tournaments
rows.statement_text: There exists a function $f$ such that, for every tournament $T$, there exists a $BST$-ordering $\prec$ of $T$ such:
rows.context_text: $BST$-orderings are identified as natural candidates for the ordering of Conjecture 3.13. This conjecture specialises Conjecture 3.13 to $BST$-orderings, which arise from the theory of binary search trees on tournaments.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.3 (Gyárfás-Sumner for Tournaments)
rows.paper_title: Clique number of tournaments
rows.statement_text: A tournament $H$ is $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-binding if and only if $H$ has a backedge graph which is a forest.
rows.context_text: The authors propose this as the directed analogue of the celebrated Gyárfás-Sumner Conjecture, where a tournament $H$ is $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-binding if the class of tournaments not containing $H$ as a subtournament is $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-bounded. The 'only if' direction is proved as Theorem 4.4, and it is shown that it suffices to prove the 'if' direction for trees.
rows.source_text: A tournament $H$ is $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-binding if and only if $H$ has a backedge graph which is a forest.
rows.status_semantics: Errata (Aboulker ranked list): refuted by Aubian arXiv:2401.07776 — forest backedge graph with bounded clique number and unbounded dichromatic number.
rows.class_notes: Directed Gyarfas-Sumner analogue; disproved, but backedge graphs and omega-vec-based chi-vec-boundedness are not defined in the record

rows.title: Conjecture 4.11
rows.paper_title: Clique number of tournaments
rows.statement_text: Let $(M,\prec)$ be an ordered graph with maximum degree $1$. Then the class of $(M,\prec)$-free ordered graphs is $\chi$-bounded.
rows.context_text: This conjecture on ordered graphs arises as a sufficient condition for proving that certain tournament classes are $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-bounded, via the correspondence between backedge graphs and $\chi$-boundedness established in the paper. It follows from Property 4.10 that Conjecture 4.11 would imply results about $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-binding tournaments.
rows.source_text: Let $(M,\prec)$ be an ordered graph with maximum degree $1$. Then the class of $(M,\prec)$-free ordered graphs is $\chi$-bounded.
rows.class_notes: Ordered graphs and (M,<)-free containment are standard buildable primitives; chi-boundedness here is the classical undirected one

rows.title: Conjecture 5.3 (Large dom implies an ω̄-cluster)
rows.paper_title: Clique number of tournaments
rows.statement_text: There exist two functions $f$ and $\ell$ such that, for every integer $k$, every tournament $T$ with $\operatorname{dom}(T)\geq f(k)$ contains a subtournament $X$ with $|X|\leq\ell(k)$ and $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(X)\geq k$.
rows.context_text: This conjecture is intermediate between Theorem 5.1 (large dom implies a $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-cluster) and Conjecture 5.2 (large dom implies a dom-cluster), since $\operatorname{dom}(T)\leq\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)\leq\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}(T)$. Conjecture 5.3 implies Conjecture 5.6 via Theorem 5.7.
rows.source_text: There exist two functions $f$ and $\ell$ such that, for every integer $k$, every tournament $T$ with $\operatorname{dom}(T)\geq f(k)$ contains a subtournament $X$ with $|X|\leq\ell(k)$ and $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(X)\geq k$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2310.04265 — Conjecture 5.3 (Large dom implies an ω̄-cluster)
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 5.3 asks whether large dom(T) forces a bounded-size subtournament with large clique number ω̄. Crew, Fan, Koerts, Moore, and Spirkl (arXiv:2602.09863, 2026) prove Corollary 7 of their paper, which is exactly Conjecture 5.8 of the source paper: every tournament T with ω̄(T) ≥ f(k) contains a subtournament X of size at most ℓ(k) with ω̄(X) ≥ k. Since Property 3.2 of the source paper establishes dom(T) ≤ ω̄(T) for every tournament T, Conjecture 5.3 follows immediately as a corollary: dom(T) ≥ f(k) implies ω̄(T) ≥ f(k), after which Corollary 7 applies.
rows.class_notes: Solved, but dom(T) and omega-vec are paper-local and undefined in the record

rows.title: Conjecture 5.6
rows.paper_title: Clique number of tournaments
rows.statement_text: There exists a function $g$ such that, for every integer $t$, if $T$ is a tournament such that for every $v\in V(T)$, $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(N^{+}(v))\leq t$, then $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)\leq g(t)$.
rows.context_text: This is the clique-number analogue of Theorem 4.8 (Harutyunyan-Le-Thomassé-Wu), which asserts that bounded local dichromatic number implies bounded global dichromatic number. Theorem 5.7 shows that Conjecture 5.3 implies Conjecture 5.6.
rows.source_text: There exists a function $g$ such that, for every integer $t$, if $T$ is a tournament such that for every $v\in V(T)$, $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(N^{+}(v))\leq t$, then $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)\leq g(t)$.
rows.status_semantics: Errata (Aboulker ranked list): implied by Crew–Fan–Koerts–Moore–Spirkl arXiv:2602.09863 plus dom(T) <= omega-bar(T).
rows.class_notes: Solved; depends on the paper-local tournament clique number omega-vec, undefined in the record

rows.title: Question 5.9
rows.paper_title: Clique number of tournaments
rows.statement_text: Is there a function $\ell$ such that, for every tournament $T$, if $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)\geq k$, then $T$ has a subtournament $A$ such that $|A|\leq\ell(k)$ and $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(A)\geq k$.
rows.context_text: This is a stronger form of Conjecture 5.8 in which the function $f$ is taken to be the identity. The authors could not disprove it, and note that Conjecture 5.10 (infinitely many $k$-$\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}$-critical tournaments) would answer it negatively.
rows.source_text: Is there a function $\ell$ such that, for every tournament $T$, if $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)\geq k$, then $T$ has a subtournament $A$ such that $|A|\leq\ell(k)$ and $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(A)\geq k$.
rows.status_semantics: Question 5.9 asks whether $\overrightarrow{\omega}(T)\geq k$ is always witnessed by a bounded-size subtournament (i.e., $f$ = identity). A 2026 paper by Crew, Fan, Koerts, Moore, and Spirkl (arXiv:2602.09863) proves a closely related weaker form (their Corollary 7): there exist two functions $f$ and $\ell$ such that $\overrightarrow{\omega}(T)\geq f(k)$ implies a subtournament of size $\leq\ell(k)$ with $\overrightarrow{\omega}\geq k$; this resolves the weaker Conjecture 5.8 of the source paper. Whether $f$ can be taken as the identity — the content of Question 5.9 — remains unclear from the available evidence.
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no shape but hinges on undefined omega-vec

rows.title: Conjecture 5.10
rows.paper_title: Clique number of tournaments
rows.statement_text: For every integer $k\geq 3$, there is an infinite number of $k$-$\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}$-critical tournaments.
rows.context_text: A tournament $T$ is $k$-$\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}$-critical if $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)=k$ and $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T-v)=k-1$ for every $v\in V(T)$. For $k=1,2$ there is exactly one such tournament; the conjecture asserts infinitely many exist for all $k\geq 3$. If true it answers Question 5.9 negatively; if false it answers it positively.
rows.source_text: For every integer $k\geq 3$, there is an infinite number of $k$-$\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}$-critical tournaments.
rows.class_notes: k-omega-vec-critical is defined in context but in terms of the undefined omega-vec

rows.title: Conjecture 6.1
rows.paper_title: Clique number of tournaments
rows.statement_text: If a class of digraphs $\mathcal{C}$ is $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-bounded, then so is its closure under substitution.
rows.context_text: In the concluding section the authors generalise Question 3.10 (which asked the same for tournaments) to all digraphs. They note that Theorem 3.3 does not hold for general digraphs, making the digraph setting harder, and discuss partial positive evidence from the tournament case.
rows.source_text: If a class of digraphs $\mathcal{C}$ is $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-bounded, then so is its closure under substitution.
rows.class_notes: Digraph generalisation of Question 3.10; chi-vec-boundedness of digraph classes not defined in the record

rows.title: Open case of Erdős's problem for fixed small k
rows.paper_title: Vertex-critical graphs far from edge-criticality
rows.statement_text: For every fixed $k \geq 4$ and arbitrarily large $r$, does there exist a $k$-chromatic vertex-critical graph $G$ such that $\chi(G - R) = k$ for every $R \subseteq E(G)$ with $|R| \leq r$?
rows.context_text: Theorem 1 resolves Erdős's problem for all sufficiently large $k$ (as a function of $r$), but leaves open the regime where $k \geq 4$ is held fixed as a small constant while $r$ grows. The authors explicitly flag this as an interesting remaining open case.
rows.source_text: For every fixed $k \geq 4$ and arbitrarily large $r$, does there exist a $k$-chromatic vertex-critical graph $G$ such that $\chi(G - R) = k$ for every $R \subseteq E(G)$ with $|R| \leq r$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2310.12891 — Open case of Erdős's problem for fixed small k
rows.status_semantics: Skottova and Steiner (arXiv:2508.08703, August 2025) resolve the conjecture affirmatively for all k ≥ 5: for every fixed k ≥ 5 and every r ≥ 1, a k-chromatic vertex-critical graph G with χ(G − R) = k for all R ⊆ E(G), |R| ≤ r, exists; they further establish f_k(n) = Ω(n^{1/3}) for k ≥ 5. The case k = 4 remains open, coinciding with the longstanding open case of Dirac's conjecture on 4-vertex-critical graphs with no critical edge.
rows.class_notes: Fixed-k regime of an Erdos problem on vertex-critical graphs far from edge-criticality; standard vocabulary, precise yes/no

rows.title: Complexity of Rotation Distance Between Binary Search Trees
rows.paper_title: Shortest paths on polymatroids and hypergraphic polytopes
rows.statement_text: What is the computational complexity of computing the rotation distance between two binary search trees (equivalently, the flip distance between two triangulations of a convex polygon)?
rows.context_text: The paper identifies the rotation distance problem as a central motivation for studying shortest paths on polymatroids. Despite a known polynomial-time 2-approximation and several FPT algorithms, the problem is neither known to be NP-hard nor known to admit a polynomial-time exact algorithm, making it a widely open question.
rows.source_text: What is the computational complexity of computing the rotation distance between two binary search trees (equivalently, the flip distance between two triangulations of a convex polygon)?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2311.00779 — Complexity of Rotation Distance Between Binary Search Trees
rows.status_semantics: The question of the computational complexity of rotation distance between binary search trees (equivalently, flip distance between triangulations of a convex polygon) has been resolved. Joseph Dorfer (arXiv:2602.22874, February 2026) proves that both problems are NP-complete, settling a question open since at least the 1980s. This rules out the possibility of a polynomial-time exact algorithm (unless P=NP), despite the known polynomial-time 2-approximation and several FPT algorithms.
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the computational complexity of rotation/flip distance' is an open-ended complexity question, not a single proposition
rows.class_notes: Status marked solved; a yes/no NP-hardness version would be B2 but is not what the record states

rows.title: Complexity of Flip Distance Between Rectangulations
rows.paper_title: Shortest paths on polymatroids and hypergraphic polytopes
rows.statement_text: What is the computational complexity of computing the flip distance between two rectangulations of a unit square?
rows.context_text: Rectangulations are tessellations of the unit square by axis-aligned rectangles, whose flip graphs are skeletons of polytopes that are polymatroids (and special cases of quotientopes). Since they fall under Problem 2 (shortest paths on a polymatroid), the authors note explicitly that the complexity of the flip distance problems between rectangulations remains open.
rows.source_text: What is the computational complexity of computing the flip distance between two rectangulations of a unit square?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2311.00779 — Complexity of Flip Distance Between Rectangulations
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the computational complexity of flip distance between rectangulations' is open-ended, not a single proposition

rows.title: Informal Conjecture on h(n)
rows.paper_title: Homogeneous structures in subset sums and non-averaging sets
rows.statement_text: The current best lower bound $h(n) = \Omega(n^{1/4})$ is tight, i.e., $h(n) = \Theta(n^{1/4})$.
rows.context_text: The bounds $\Omega(n^{1/4}) \leq h(n) \leq n^{1/2+o(1)}$ were both known by 1990, with the lower bound due to Bosznay [6] and the upper bound to Erdős and Sárközy [10]. The lower bound follows from a simple construction: fixing integer $q$, the set $\{n_i = iq^3 + i(i+1)/2 : i = 1,\ldots,q-1\}$ is a non-averaging subset of $[n]$ with $n = q^4$. The authors state 'which we suspect to be tight' immediately after citing this bound.
rows.source_text: The current best lower bound $h(n) = \Omega(n^{1/4})$ is tight, i.e., $h(n) = \Theta(n^{1/4})$.
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture that h(n) = \Theta(n^{1/4}) (i.e., that Bosznay's 1990 lower bound is tight) was resolved in October 2024 by Pham and Zakharov, who proved that the largest non-averaging subset of \{1,\ldots,n\} has size n^{1/4+o(1)}, matching the lower bound up to sub-polynomial factors. Their paper explicitly describes this as solving the Erdős–Straus problem on non-averaging sets. The journal version appeared in Geometric and Functional Analysis in 2025.
rows.defer_reason: Non-averaging subsets of [n] — additive combinatorics/number theory, no graph content

rows.title: Conjecture 10
rows.paper_title: Graphs with arbitrary Ramsey number and connectivity
rows.statement_text: For all functions $f(n) : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ with $n \leq f(n) \leq r(K_n)$ and for all $\varepsilon > 0$, there is a sequence of graphs $(G_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ satisfying $|V(G_n)| = n$ and $(1-\varepsilon)f(n) \leq r(G_n) \leq (1+\varepsilon)f(n)$ for all sufficiently large $n$.
rows.context_text: The paper's main theorem achieves every growth rate up to a multiplicative constant $C_k$; Conjecture 10 asks whether the multiplicative gaps in $\mathcal{R}^c_n$ can be made $1+o(1)$, i.e., whether any prescribed growth rate can be approximated arbitrarily closely. The authors suggest that edge-deletion results (such as a conjecture of Wigderson) might provide a route to this.
rows.source_text: For all functions $f(n) : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ with $n \leq f(n) \leq r(K_n)$ and for all $\varepsilon > 0$, there is a sequence of graphs $(G_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ satisfying $|V(G_n)| = n$ and $(1-\varepsilon)f(n) \leq r(G_n) \leq (1+\varepsilon)f(n)$ for all sufficiently large $n$.
rows.class_notes: Quantifies over all growth functions f between n and r(K_n); finite and expressible with an eventually-forall wrapper

rows.title: Question 8.3
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions XIII. Basic obstructions in $\mathcal{H}$-free graphs for finite $\mathcal{H}$
rows.statement_text: For which sets $\mathcal{S}$ of strings is it true that there is a constant $c=c(\mathcal{S})\in\mathbb{N}$ such that every $c$-padded string $S$ contains either a string in $\mathcal{S}$ or the reverse of a string in $\mathcal{S}$ as a consecutive substring?
rows.context_text: This question arises in the special case where every graph in $\mathcal{H}$ is a $c'$-tassel for some $c'\in\mathbb{N}$, where each tassel has a unique string $\mathcal{S}_{H,v}$ associated to each neck $v$. A set $\mathcal{S}$ satisfying the condition is called a $c$-unavoidable language, and identifying minimal such languages is key to characterising tasselled families. The only singleton $c$-unavoidable languages are $\mathcal{S}=\{0\cdots 01\}$ (up to reversal) and $\mathcal{S}=\{0\cdots 0\}$.
rows.source_text: For which sets $\mathcal{S}$ of strings is it true that there is a constant $c=c(\mathcal{S})\in\mathbb{N}$ such that every $c$-padded string $S$ contains either a string in $\mathcal{S}$ or the reverse of a string in $\mathcal{S}$ as a consecutive substring?
rows.defer_reason: 'For which sets S...' is a characterization request; c-padded strings and tassels are paper-local
rows.class_notes: Combinatorics-on-words question in service of the induced-subgraph/tree-decomposition series

rows.title: Conjecture 1.1
rows.paper_title: Universality for graphs with bounded density
rows.statement_text: For every $d\in\mathbb{Q}$, $d>1$, and $n\in\mathbb{N}$, there exists a graph $G$ with $$e(G)\leq Cn^{2-1/d}$$ edges which is $\mathcal{H}_{d}(n)$-universal, where $C=C(d)$.
rows.context_text: The authors initiate the study of universality for graphs with bounded density, unifying earlier results on bounded-degree graphs, forests, and degenerate graphs. They note that a simple counting argument shows $e(G)=o(n^{2-1/d})$ is insufficient, making the conjectured bound tight up to the constant $C$. The paper proves constructions achieving $O_d(n^{2-1/(\lceil d\rceil+1)})$ edges, approaching but not yet matching this conjecture.
rows.source_text: For every $d\in\mathbb{Q}$, $d>1$, and $n\in\mathbb{N}$, there exists a graph $G$ with $$e(G)\leq Cn^{2-1/d}$$ edges which is $\mathcal{H}_{d}(n)$-universal, where $C=C(d)$.
rows.class_notes: The family H_d(n) (which density notion, for rational d>1) is never defined in the record and the bound is sensitive to it

rows.title: Converse to Theorem 1.1 (3-Coloring dichotomy for $H$-free graphs)
rows.paper_title: List-$k$-Coloring $H$-free graphs for all $k>4$
rows.statement_text: If every component of $H$ is a path, then the $3$-Coloring Problem restricted to $H$-free graphs can be solved in polynomial time.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 (Holyer; Kamiński and Lozin) establishes that if $H$ has at least one component which is not a path then the $3$-Coloring Problem on $H$-free graphs is NP-hard. Immediately after stating this theorem the paper notes: 'The converse to Theorem 1.1 is wide open.'
rows.source_text: If every component of $H$ is a path, then the $3$-Coloring Problem restricted to $H$-free graphs can be solved in polynomial time.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2311.05713 — Converse to Theorem 1.1 (3-Coloring dichotomy for $H$-free graphs)
rows.class_notes: Precise converse of Holyer/Kaminski-Lozin: linear-forest H implies poly-time 3-Coloring on H-free graphs; needs P/NP layer

rows.title: Full dichotomy for $k$-Coloring on $H$-free graphs ($k\geq 3$)
rows.paper_title: List-$k$-Coloring $H$-free graphs for all $k>4$
rows.statement_text: For every integer $k \geq 3$, characterise the graphs $H$ for which the $k$-Coloring Problem restricted to $H$-free graphs is polynomial-time solvable versus NP-hard.
rows.context_text: The paper observes that, in contrast with the List-$k$-Coloring Problem (now settled for all $k>4$), 'for the $k$-Coloring Problem, no value of $k\geq 3$ is known for which the "easy" choices of $H$ are completely distinguished from the "hard" ones.' This identifies a broad open problem motivating the paper's List-$k$-Coloring results.
rows.source_text: For every integer $k \geq 3$, characterise the graphs $H$ for which the $k$-Coloring Problem restricted to $H$-free graphs is polynomial-time solvable versus NP-hard.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2311.05713 — Full dichotomy for $k$-Coloring on $H$-free graphs ($k\geq 3$)
rows.defer_reason: 'Characterise the graphs H...' full-dichotomy request, not a single proposition

rows.title: Conjecture 1.3
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions XIV. Non-adjacent neighbours in a hole
rows.statement_text: For every fixed $t \geq 1$, the family of $t$-clock-free graphs is clean.
rows.context_text: A $t$-clock is a clock consisting of a hole $C$ and a vertex $v$ such that there are two neighbours $x, y \in V(C)$ of $v$ where the distance between $x$ and $y$ along $C$ is at least $t$. The main result of the paper (Theorem 1.1) establishes cleanness for $t = 2$ (all clocks); Conjecture 1.3 proposes that cleanness persists even when clocks with close non-adjacent pairs are allowed, for every fixed threshold $t$.
rows.source_text: For every fixed $t \geq 1$, the family of $t$-clock-free graphs is clean.
rows.class_notes: t-clock is defined in context but the key predicate 'clean' (series-local: large treewidth forces a basic obstruction) is not

rows.title: Optimal disjoint-edges bound in complete simple topological graphs
rows.paper_title: A structure theorem for pseudo-segments and its applications
rows.statement_text: Is it true that every complete $n$-vertex simple topological graph contains $\Omega(n)$ pairwise disjoint edges?
rows.context_text: Aichholzer et al. [2] showed that every complete $n$-vertex simple topological graph always contains $\Omega(n^{1/2})$ pairwise disjoint edges. The paper notes that improving this lower bound to $\Omega(n)$ is an open problem, and Corollary 1.3 provides a new nearly-polynomial lower bound under a density assumption.
rows.source_text: Is it true that every complete $n$-vertex simple topological graph contains $\Omega(n)$ pairwise disjoint edges?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2312.01028 — Optimal disjoint-edges bound in complete simple topological graphs
rows.class_notes: Needs simple-topological-drawing vocabulary plus an exists-constant wrapper for Omega(n) disjoint edges

rows.title: Conjecture 1.2
rows.paper_title: Triangle Ramsey numbers of complete graphs
rows.statement_text: For all $s \leq t$, $r_{K_s}(K_t) = \binom{r(K_t)}{s}$.
rows.context_text: Motivated by a question of Sam Spiro asking whether an analogue of Chvatal's theorem holds for $r_{K_s}(K_t)$, the authors conjecture that the complete graph on $r(K_t)$ vertices minimises the number of $K_s$ copies among all $K_t$-Ramsey graphs. The paper verifies this conjecture for $s = 3$ and all sufficiently large $t$ as its main result (Theorem 1.3).
rows.source_text: For all $s \leq t$, $r_{K_s}(K_t) = \binom{r(K_t)}{s}$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.2 posits that for all $s \leq t$, $r_{K_s}(K_t) = \binom{r(K_t)}{s}$. The case $s = 2$ is classical (Chv\u00e1tal). The source paper itself resolves $s = 3$ for all sufficiently large $t$ as its main result (Theorem 1.3), via a chromatic-graph lemma: every $r$-chromatic graph in which every edge lies in at least $K$ triangles contains at least $\binom{r}{3}$ triangles. No subsequent work extending the result to $s \geq 4$ was found in the indexed literature as of May 2026.
rows.class_notes: r_{K_s}(K_t) recoverable from context as the minimum number of K_s copies over K_t-Ramsey (arrowing) graphs; recovery flag notwithstanding

rows.title: q-color generalization of Conjecture 1.2
rows.paper_title: Triangle Ramsey numbers of complete graphs
rows.statement_text: The $q$-color generalization of Conjecture 1.2 holds.
rows.context_text: After noting that the $q$-color generalizations of Theorems 1.1, 1.3, and 1.4 all hold by the same proofs, the authors conjecture that the $q$-color analogue of the main conjecture (Conjecture 1.2) likewise holds.
rows.source_text: The $q$-color generalization of Conjecture 1.2 holds.
rows.class_notes: Statement is only a pointer to the paper's Conjecture 1.2 (triangle Ramsey numbers), whose content is not given in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 3
rows.paper_title: Precoloring extension in planar near-Eulerian-triangulations
rows.statement_text: For every positive integer $\ell$, there is a polynomial-time algorithm that given a planar near-Eulerian-triangulation $G$ with the outer face of length at most $\ell$ and a precoloring $\varphi$ of the vertices incident with the outer face decides whether $\varphi$ extends to a 4-coloring of $G$.
rows.context_text: The paper provides only a necessary topological condition for precoloring extension in general, and resolves the problem completely only for outer faces of length at most five and in the case where all vertices of the outer face have odd degree and are colored with three colors. The authors conjecture that combining the topological condition with further ideas will resolve the disk case in full, and that a polynomial-time decision algorithm exists for any fixed outer face length $\ell$.
rows.source_text: For every positive integer $\ell$, there is a polynomial-time algorithm that given a planar near-Eulerian-triangulation $G$ with the outer face of length at most $\ell$ and a precoloring $\varphi$ of the vertices incident with the outer face decides whether $\varphi$ extends to a 4-coloring of $G$.
rows.class_notes: Precise, but 'planar near-Eulerian-triangulation' is paper-local and the claim is existence of a poly-time algorithm; also needs planarity layer.

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: Precoloring extension in planar near-Eulerian-triangulations
rows.statement_text: For every positive even integer $\ell$, there exists an integer $d$ such that the following claim holds. Let $G$ be a planar near-Eulerian-triangulation with the outer face bounded by a cycle $C$ of length $\ell$ and let $U$ be an independent set disjoint from $C$ such that $G-U$ is bipartite. Moreover, suppose that $G-U$ has a set $S$ of $d$ faces of length at least six such that there is no 4-cycle in $G-U$ separating a face of $S$ from the outer face, and for distinct faces $s_{1},s_{2}\in S$, the distance between $s_{1}$ and $s_{2}$ in $G-U$ is at least $d$ and there is no closed walk of length less than $\ell$ in $G-U$ separating both $s_{1}$ and $s_{2}$ from the outer face. Then any viable 4-coloring of $C$ extends to a 4-coloring of $G$.
rows.context_text: This conjecture captures a 'sufficiently generic' structural condition under which the necessary topological condition for precoloring extension is also sufficient. It is posed as an intermediate step toward Conjecture 3, asserting that when $G-U$ is bipartite and contains sufficiently many well-separated long faces, every viable precoloring of the outer face extends to a full 4-coloring.
rows.source_text: For every positive even integer $\ell$, there exists an integer $d$ such that the following claim holds. Let $G$ be a planar near-Eulerian-triangulation with the outer face bounded by a cycle $C$ of length $\ell$ and let $U$ be an independent set disjoint from $C$ such that $G-U$ is bipartite. Moreover, suppose that $G-U$ has a set $S$ of $d$ faces of length at least six such that there is no 4-cycle in $G-U$ separating a face of $S$ from the outer face, and for distinct faces $s_{1},s_{2}\in S$, the distance between $s_{1}$ and $s_{2}$ in $G-U$ is at least $d$ and there is no closed walk of length less than $\ell$ in $G-U$ separating both $s_{1}$ and $s_{2}$ from the outer face. Then any viable 4-coloring of $C$ extends to a 4-coloring of $G$.
rows.class_notes: Fully quantified statement but relies on paper-local 'near-Eulerian-triangulation' and 'viable 4-coloring' definitions.

rows.title: Problem 1.1
rows.paper_title: The growth rate of multicolor Ramsey numbers of $3$-graphs
rows.statement_text: Given a fixed $k$-uniform hypergraph $G$, determine the integer $h$ (if it exists) such that $r(G;q)$ grows as a tower of height $h$ as $q$ tends to infinity.
rows.context_text: In the graph case, $r(G;q)$ grows as a tower of height $1$ if $G$ is bipartite (with at least two edges) and as a tower of height $2$ otherwise. For 3-uniform hypergraphs, Abbott and Williams showed that $r(K_4^{(3)};q)$ grows as a tower of height $3$. The paper's main theorem (Theorem 1.4) resolves this problem for $k=3$ in full generality.
rows.source_text: Given a fixed $k$-uniform hypergraph $G$, determine the integer $h$ (if it exists) such that $r(G;q)$ grows as a tower of height $h$ as $q$ tends to infinity.
rows.status_semantics: The source paper's own Theorem 1.4 fully resolves Problem 1.1 for $k=3$: every 3-uniform hypergraph $G$ falls into one of three growth-rate regimes for $r(G;q)$ (polynomial, single-exponential, or double-exponential in $q$), giving tower heights $h\in\{1,2,3\}$ depending on structural properties of $G$. For $k\geq 4$ the general classification remains open; arXiv:2502.20863 (2025) establishes lower bounds on the tower height for $k$-uniform hypergraphs of bounded degree, showing the tower height can reach $k$ for $k\geq 3$, but a complete determination of $h$ for arbitrary $k$-uniform $G$ with $k\geq 4$ has not yet been achieved.
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the integer h' — determine-the-value meta problem, not a single proposition; resolved for k=3 by the paper.

rows.title: Problem 4.1
rows.paper_title: The growth rate of multicolor Ramsey numbers of $3$-graphs
rows.statement_text: Does there exist, for every $\ell\geq 1,$ a $3$-uniform hypergraph $G_{\ell}$ with $r(G_{\ell};q)=2^{q^{\ell+o(1)}}$?
rows.context_text: For 3-uniform hypergraphs in the exponential-growth class $\mathcal{U}$, the upper and lower bounds often have different powers of $q$ in the exponent. Known cases include $r(\mathrm{Star}^{(3)}(4);q)=2^{q^{1+o(1)}}$ (exponent $\ell=1$) and a construction from Proposition 3.3 achieving exponent $\ell=2$, but for $\ell\geq 3$ the best upper bound is only of the form $2^{q^{\ell+1}\mathrm{polylog}(q)}$.
rows.source_text: Does there exist, for every $\ell\geq 1,$ a $3$-uniform hypergraph $G_{\ell}$ with $r(G_{\ell};q)=2^{q^{\ell+o(1)}}$?
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no existence question about multicolor Ramsey growth 2^{q^{l+o(1)}}.

rows.title: Problem 1.1
rows.paper_title: Partitioning the hypercube into smaller hypercubes
rows.statement_text:
Determine or estimate the functions 
(i) $m(d)$,     (ii) $m^{\prime}(d)$,
    (iii) $f(d)$,
    (iv) $f_{\leq 2}(d).$
rows.context_text: $Q_d := \{0,1\}^d$ is the $d$-dimensional hypercube; $f(d)$ counts partitions of its vertex set into subhypercubes; $m(d)$ and $m^{\prime}(d)$ count perfect matchings and all matchings respectively. Problem (iii) was originally raised by Gadouleau at the 29th British Combinatorial Conference in 2022; problem (iv) is introduced by the authors as a closely related variant.
rows.source_text:
Determine or estimate the functions 
(i) $m(d)$,     (ii) $m^{\prime}(d)$,
    (iii) $f(d)$,
    (iv) $f_{\leq 2}(d).$
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine or estimate the functions' — open-ended estimation of four counting functions.

rows.title: Problem 1.2
rows.paper_title: Partitioning the hypercube into smaller hypercubes
rows.statement_text:
What is the order of magnitude of the
following ratios? In particular, which one of them is an
exponential function of $n$? 
(i) $m^{\prime}(d)/m(d)$,   (ii) $f_{\leq 2}(d)/m^{\prime}(d)$,
  (iii) $f(d)/f_{\leq 2}(d),$    (iv) $f(d)/m(d)$.
rows.context_text: The hierarchy $m(d) \leq m^{\prime}(d) \leq f_{\leq 2}(d) \leq f(d)$ holds by definition, where $n = 2^{d-1}$. The paper proves that $f(d)/m(d)$ (part iv) is exponential in $n$, and shows part (i) is subexponential; parts (ii) and (iii) remain open.
rows.source_text:
What is the order of magnitude of the
following ratios? In particular, which one of them is an
exponential function of $n$? 
(i) $m^{\prime}(d)/m(d)$,   (ii) $f_{\leq 2}(d)/m^{\prime}(d)$,
  (iii) $f(d)/f_{\leq 2}(d),$    (iv) $f(d)/m(d)$.
rows.status_semantics: Problem 1.2 asks which of the four ratios m'(d)/m(d), f_{\leq 2}(d)/m'(d), f(d)/f_{\leq 2}(d), and f(d)/m(d) is exponential in n=2^{d-1}. The source paper itself resolves two parts: part (iv) f(d)/m(d) is proved exponential in n, and part (i) m'(d)/m(d) is proved subexponential. Parts (ii) and (iii) remain explicitly open in the paper. No follow-up work specifically resolving parts (ii) or (iii) was found in the literature through May 2026.
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the order of magnitude' — multi-part value question, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Problem 1.9
rows.paper_title: Partitioning the hypercube into smaller hypercubes
rows.statement_text: Determine the asymptotic behaviour of the number $f_{2}(d).$
rows.context_text: It is natural to ask what happens if only $2$-dimensional subcubes are allowed in a partition of $Q_d$. The authors believe the same asymptotic bound as given by Proposition 1.7 (for general $r$) holds with $r=2$.
rows.source_text: Determine the asymptotic behaviour of the number $f_{2}(d).$
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the asymptotic behaviour of f_2(d)' — determine-the-value meta.
rows.class_notes: The precise conjectural answer is the companion row axv_2401_00299_03.

rows.title: Informal conjecture on $f_2(d)$
rows.paper_title: Partitioning the hypercube into smaller hypercubes
rows.statement_text: We believe that the same bound as in Proposition 1.7, with $r=2$, holds [for $f_2(d)$].
rows.context_text: Introduced immediately before Problem 1.9 as motivation for that problem. Proposition 1.7 provides asymptotic bounds for $f_S(d)$ for general fixed dimension $r$; the authors conjecture the $r=2$ case obeys the same estimate.
rows.source_text: We believe that the same bound as in Proposition 1.7, with $r=2$, holds [for $f_2(d)$].
rows.class_notes: Asserts the bound of the paper's Proposition 1.7 with r=2, but that bound is not reproduced in the record.

rows.title: Problem 1.10
rows.paper_title: Partitioning the hypercube into smaller hypercubes
rows.statement_text:
Is it true that
$f(d)/f_{\leq 2}(d)$ is subexponential in $n$?
rows.context_text: This addresses Problem 1.2 (iii), asking whether the gap between counting all subcube partitions and counting those restricted to subcubes of dimension at most 2 grows at most subexponentially in $n = 2^{d-1}$.
rows.source_text:
Is it true that
$f(d)/f_{\leq 2}(d)$ is subexponential in $n$?
rows.class_notes: Precise yes/no form of Problem 1.2(iii); f and f_{<=2} are adequately described in context (subcube-partition counts of Q_d).

rows.title: Conjecture 6.1
rows.paper_title: Ramsey and Turán numbers of sparse hypergraphs
rows.statement_text: For a 3-uniform $n$-vertex hypergraph $H$ with $d_{1}(H)=d$, [statement truncated in source]
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.5 gives $r(H;q)\leq\exp^{(k-1)}(O_{k,q}(d))n$ for $k\geq 4$-uniform hypergraphs with first skeletal degeneracy $d$. For $k=3$ the analogous bound from the proof is $\exp^{(2)}(O_{3,q}(d))n$; Conjecture 6.1 posits a stronger bound for 3-uniform hypergraphs, stronger than a result of Bradač–Fox–Sudakov [3] in which skeletal degeneracy is replaced by $\sqrt{e(H)}$. The inequality $d_{1}(H)\leq O(\sqrt{e(H)})$ shows that 6.1 would imply that result.
rows.source_text: For a 3-uniform $n$-vertex hypergraph $H$ with $d_{1}(H)=d$, [statement truncated in source]
rows.class_notes: Statement explicitly truncated in source; the conjectured bound is missing.

rows.title: Conjecture 6.2
rows.paper_title: Ramsey and Turán numbers of sparse hypergraphs
rows.statement_text: There is a constant $c_{k}>0$ such that for every $k$-uniform $k$-partite hypergraph $H$, [statement truncated in source]
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.4 gives $\Omega_{k}(n^{k-C_{k}/d_{1}(H)})\leq\operatorname{ex}(n,H)\leq O_{H}(n^{k-c_{k}/d_{1}(H)^{k-1}})$. The complete $k$-partite hypergraph $K^{(k)}_{d,\ldots,d}$ shows the upper bound is tight in $d_{1}$, while the bipartite hedgehog $H_{d}^{(k)}$ shows the lower bound is tight in $d_{1}$. This gap motivates replacing $d_{1}(H)$ by the finer quantity $d_{\mathsf{max}}(H)=\max_{1\leq i<k}d_{i}(H)$, and Conjecture 6.2 asserts that the Turán exponent is controlled by $d_{\mathsf{max}}(H)$.
rows.source_text: There is a constant $c_{k}>0$ such that for every $k$-uniform $k$-partite hypergraph $H$, [statement truncated in source]
rows.class_notes: Statement truncated in source; the d_max-controlled Turan exponent claim is not fully given.

rows.title: Conjecture 6.4
rows.paper_title: Ramsey and Turán numbers of sparse hypergraphs
rows.statement_text: Let $L$ be a $d\times d$ Latin square. Define $H_{L}$ to be the 3-uniform 3-partite hypergraph on vertex set $[d]\sqcup[d]\sqcup[d]$ where $(i,j,k)\in E(H_{L})$ if and only if $L(i,j)=k$. Then $\mathrm{ex}(n,H_{L})=n^{3-\Theta(1/d)}$.
rows.context_text: For every $d\times d$ Latin square one has $d_{1}(H_{L}),d_{2}(H_{L})=\Theta(d)$, so $d_{\mathsf{max}}(H_{L})=\Theta(d)$, yet Theorem 1.4 only yields $\mathrm{ex}(n,H_{L})\leq O(n^{3-c/d^{2}})$. The 1-skeleton of $H_{L}$ is $K_{d,d,d}$, the same as that of the complete 3-partite hypergraph $K^{(3)}_{d,d,d}$, so current techniques cannot distinguish $H_{L}$ from $K^{(3)}_{d,d,d}$ at the level of the Turán exponent. Conjecture 6.4 predicts that the exponent is $3-\Theta(1/d)$, matching $d_{\mathsf{max}}(H_{L})=\Theta(d)$ via Conjecture 6.2.
rows.source_text: Let $L$ be a $d\times d$ Latin square. Define $H_{L}$ to be the 3-uniform 3-partite hypergraph on vertex set $[d]\sqcup[d]\sqcup[d]$ where $(i,j,k)\in E(H_{L})$ if and only if $L(i,j)=k$. Then $\mathrm{ex}(n,H_{L})=n^{3-\Theta(1/d)}$.
rows.class_notes: Self-contained: H_L is fully defined in the statement; asserts ex(n,H_L)=n^{3-Theta(1/d)}.

rows.title: Conjecture 6.5
rows.paper_title: Ramsey and Turán numbers of sparse hypergraphs
rows.statement_text: There is a constant $c>0$ such that for all $d\geq 4$ the following holds. If $G$ is a (not necessarily linear) 3-uniform hypergraph on $n$ vertices with at least $n^{3-c/d}$ edges, then there is a linear subhypergraph of $G$ with $d$ vertices and at least $d^{2}/100$ edges.
rows.context_text: Proving that $\mathrm{ex}(n,H_{L})\leq O(n^{3-c/d})$ would require finding $H_{L}$ — a linear hypergraph — in any sufficiently dense host. The current barrier is that the techniques only find $K^{(3)}_{d,d,d}$, a non-linear structure. If Conjecture 6.5 holds, finding any dense enough linear subhypergraph (with quadratically many edges) would be the key missing step. The conjecture is known without the linearity constraint, since a hypergraph Kővári–Sós–Turán argument gives $K^{(3)}_{1,(d-1)/2,(d-1)/2}$ in any 3-uniform hypergraph with $n^{3-c/d}$ edges.
rows.source_text: There is a constant $c>0$ such that for all $d\geq 4$ the following holds. If $G$ is a (not necessarily linear) 3-uniform hypergraph on $n$ vertices with at least $n^{3-c/d}$ edges, then there is a linear subhypergraph of $G$ with $d$ vertices and at least $d^{2}/100$ edges.
rows.class_notes: Fully explicit finite statement about finding dense linear subhypergraphs; 'linear hypergraph' is standard.

rows.title: Question 1.1
rows.paper_title: On prime Cayley graphs
rows.statement_text: Given a graph $\Gamma$, can we decompose it as the joined union of smaller graphs?
rows.context_text: This question is motivated by the study of multilayer networks and phase oscillators, where decomposing a network as a joined union of smaller graphs allows solutions to be broadcast from a reduced representation system to the full network. The paper addresses this question specifically for Cayley graphs using tools from group theory and ring theory.
rows.source_text: Given a graph $\Gamma$, can we decompose it as the joined union of smaller graphs?
rows.status_semantics: Question 1.1 asks whether a given graph can be decomposed as the joined union of smaller graphs, which is equivalent to detecting non-trivial homogeneous sets (modules). The source paper itself provides a complete algebraic characterization for Cayley graphs: Cay(G,S) has a non-trivial homogeneous set if and only if there exists a non-trivial subgroup H such that every right coset Hc (for c in S minus H) is entirely contained in S (Theorem 3.4). The paper has since appeared in the Journal of Combinatorics (2025). A 2024 follow-up (arXiv:2403.05635) extends this line of research to p-unitary Cayley graphs over finite rings, providing necessary and sufficient conditions for primality (non-decomposability) in that more general setting.
rows.defer_reason: 'Can we decompose it' — vague program-level question; 'joined union' is paper-local and no criterion is asked for concretely.

rows.title: Question 4.28
rows.paper_title: On prime Cayley graphs
rows.statement_text: When is $X_{R}$ prime?
rows.context_text: $X_R$ denotes the Cayley graph constructed from a finite ring $R$ using its group of units and a connection set derived from the ring structure. This question is the central driving question of Section 4, and is reduced first to the semisimple case (showing non-semisimple $R$ gives non-prime $X_R$) and then to a combinatorial question about tensor products of complete graphs.
rows.source_text: When is $X_{R}$ prime?
rows.status_semantics: Question 4.28 asks when $X_R$ is prime, where $X_R$ is the Cayley graph on a finite ring $R$ built from its group of units. The source paper itself reduces the question to the semisimple case (non-semisimple $R$ yields non-prime $X_R$) and further to a combinatorial question about primality of tensor products of complete graphs. A March 2024 follow-up (arXiv:2403.05635) establishes necessary and sufficient conditions for primality in the related $p$-unitary Cayley graph setting, partially addressing the broader question for certain classes of finite commutative rings, but the full general question for arbitrary finite rings appears to remain open.
rows.defer_reason: 'When is X_R prime?' — open-ended characterization question over a paper-local construction.

rows.title: Question 4.31
rows.paper_title: On prime Cayley graphs
rows.statement_text: Let $n_{1},\ldots,n_{d}$ be positive integers. When is $\prod_{i=1}^{d}K_{n_{i}}$ prime?
rows.context_text: For a semisimple ring $R = \prod_{i=1}^d k_i$ (a product of finite fields of sizes $q_i$), the associated Cayley graph satisfies $X_R \cong \prod_{i=1}^d K_{q_i}$, reducing Question 4.28 for the semisimple case to this combinatorial question about tensor products (Kronecker products) of complete graphs. The vertex set of $\prod_{i=1}^d K_{n_i}$ consists of $d$-tuples $(s_1,\ldots,s_d)$ with two vertices adjacent iff they differ in every coordinate.
rows.source_text: Let $n_{1},\ldots,n_{d}$ be positive integers. When is $\prod_{i=1}^{d}K_{n_{i}}$ prime?
rows.defer_reason: 'When is the tensor product of complete graphs prime?' — characterization question; primality (join-indecomposability) not pinned to a proposition.
rows.class_notes: Combinatorially concrete objects (Kronecker products of K_n) but still a characterize-question.

rows.title: Problem 11
rows.paper_title: A note on graphs of $k$-colourings
rows.statement_text: For which graphs $H$ does there exist an $H^{\prime}$ such that $\pi_{G}^{(H^{\prime})}$ determines $\pi_{G}^{(H)}$ for every graph $G$?
rows.context_text: Asgarli et al. conjectured (their Conjecture 5.2) that $\pi_{G}^{(K_{2})}$ determines the chromatic polynomial $\pi_{G}^{(K_{1})}$, i.e., that equal edge-count polynomials of colouring graphs imply equal chromatic polynomials. This broader problem asks for which pairs $(H, H')$ such a determination holds, motivated by Remark 10 in the paper.
rows.source_text: For which graphs $H$ does there exist an $H^{\prime}$ such that $\pi_{G}^{(H^{\prime})}$ determines $\pi_{G}^{(H)}$ for every graph $G$?
rows.defer_reason: 'For which graphs H...' — characterization question, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Problem 12
rows.paper_title: A note on graphs of $k$-colourings
rows.statement_text: Is there a finite set of graphs $H_{1},\dotsc,H_{t}$ such that, for almost every $G\in\mathcal{G}(n,\frac{1}{2})$, if $\pi_{G^{\prime}}^{(H_{i})}=\pi_{G}^{(H_{i})}$ for all $i$ then $G^{\prime}\cong G$?
rows.context_text: Theorem 5 of the paper shows that no finite family of generalised chromatic polynomials can distinguish all graphs; this problem asks whether the situation is better for typical (Erdős–Rényi random) graphs. It is posed as a weakening of the Bollobás–Pebody–Riordan conjecture that almost every graph is determined by its chromatic polynomial.
rows.source_text: Is there a finite set of graphs $H_{1},\dotsc,H_{t}$ such that, for almost every $G\in\mathcal{G}(n,\frac{1}{2})$, if $\pi_{G^{\prime}}^{(H_{i})}=\pi_{G}^{(H_{i})}$ for all $i$ then $G^{\prime}\cong G$?
rows.class_notes: Precise yes/no question but the generalised chromatic polynomial pi_G^{(H)} is not defined in the record; also needs random-graph (a.a.s.) vocabulary.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.12
rows.paper_title: Variations on Sidorenko's conjecture in tournaments
rows.statement_text: For every undirected tree, there exists some orientation that has the tournament anti-Sidorenko property.
rows.context_text: After characterising exactly which orientations of stars are tournament anti-Sidorenko (Theorem 1.11), the authors note it is natural to ask whether every undirected tree admits at least one anti-Sidorenko orientation. They confirm the conjecture for all undirected trees with at most one vertex of even degree (Proposition 1.13).
rows.source_text: For every undirected tree, there exists some orientation that has the tournament anti-Sidorenko property.
rows.status_semantics: The conjecture remains open in full generality. The source paper (2402.08418) already confirms it for trees with at most one vertex of even degree. A follow-up paper (arXiv:2512.11222, He–Mani–Nie–Tung–Wei, 2025) proves the conjecture for all caterpillars (Theorem 1.4) and reduces the full conjecture to a restricted class: trees that are not caterpillars with at least 2 vertices of even degree and no isomorphic pair (their Conjecture 1.5). Specific open cases such as the '2-3-4 tree' are identified as Problem 1.6.
rows.class_notes: Tournament analogue inspired by Sidorenko's conjecture, not the OPG conjecture itself; 'tournament anti-Sidorenko property' is paper-defined and absent from the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.2
rows.paper_title: Finding forest-orderings of tournaments is NP-complete
rows.statement_text: There is a function $f$ such that for every integer $k$, there is a polynomial-time algorithm that, given a tournament $T$, correctly concludes that $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)\geq k$, or finds an order $\prec$ of $V(T)$ such that $\omega(T^{\prec})\leq f(k)$
rows.context_text: Aboulker et al. [2] proved an approximation version for $k=3$: there is a constant $c$ and a polynomial-time algorithm that either certifies $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)\geq 3$ or finds an ordering $\prec$ with $\omega(T^{\prec})\leq c$. The conjecture asks whether such an approximation scheme extends to all $k$.
rows.source_text: There is a function $f$ such that for every integer $k$, there is a polynomial-time algorithm that, given a tournament $T$, correctly concludes that $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)\geq k$, or finds an order $\prec$ of $V(T)$ such that $\omega(T^{\prec})\leq f(k)$
rows.class_notes: Algorithmic approximation conjecture; backedge-graph notation omega(T^prec) and directed clique number are paper-local here.

rows.title: Problem 4.4
rows.paper_title: Finding forest-orderings of tournaments is NP-complete
rows.statement_text: What is the complexity of the $\mathcal{C}$-FAS Problem when $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all paths? when $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of graphs with maximum degree $1$?
rows.context_text: Following the NP-completeness of the $\mathcal{C}$-FAS Problem for forests (Theorem 1.1), it is natural to ask for which classes $\mathcal{C}$ the problem is in P. Paths and matchings (graphs with maximum degree at most 1) are two natural candidates for tractable cases.
rows.source_text: What is the complexity of the $\mathcal{C}$-FAS Problem when $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all paths? when $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of graphs with maximum degree $1$?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the complexity of...' — open-ended complexity-determination question.

rows.title: Conjecture 3
rows.paper_title: Minimum acyclic number and maximum dichromatic number of oriented triangle-free graphs of a given order
rows.statement_text: $\vec{a}(n)=\Theta(\sqrt{n\log n})$.
rows.context_text: The paper establishes $\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-\varepsilon\right)\sqrt{n\log n}\leq\vec{a}(n)\leq\frac{107}{8}\sqrt{n}\log n$, a logarithmic gap between lower and upper bounds. The authors believe the upper bound can be sharpened to $C\sqrt{n\log n}$ by analysing a suitable orientation of the triangle-free process $G_{\triangle}$.
rows.source_text: $\vec{a}(n)=\Theta(\sqrt{n\log n})$.
rows.class_notes: B3-vs-B2 resolved to B2: a-vec(n) is fully determined by the paper title (minimum acyclic number over oriented triangle-free graphs of order n) plus the standard acyclic-number definition; Theta claim, open part is the sqrt(n log n) upper bound.

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: Minimum acyclic number and maximum dichromatic number of oriented triangle-free graphs of a given order
rows.statement_text: $\vec{t}(n)=\Theta\sqrt{\frac{n}{\log n}}$.
rows.context_text: Conjecture 4 follows from Conjecture 3, since $\vec{t}(n)\geq n/\vec{a}(n)$ and $\vec{t}(n)\leq t(n)$. The paper establishes $\frac{8}{107}\frac{\sqrt{n}}{\log n}\leq\vec{t}(n)\leq\left(\sqrt{2}+o(1)\right)\sqrt{\frac{n}{\log n}}$, leaving a logarithmic gap on the lower side.
rows.source_text: $\vec{t}(n)=\Theta\sqrt{\frac{n}{\log n}}$.
rows.class_notes: Same issue as Conjecture 3: t(n)-arrow (max dichromatic number) undefined in the record; follows from axv_2403_02298_00.

rows.title: Conjecture 10
rows.paper_title: Equivalence between Erdős-Hajnal and polynomial Rödl and Nikiforov conjectures
rows.statement_text: For each proper hereditary family $\mathcal{F}$ of graphs there are $C_{\mathcal{F}},d_{\mathcal{F}}>0$ such that if $0<\varepsilon<1/2$, every graph on $n$ vertices which is $\varepsilon^{d_{\mathcal{F}}}$-close to $\mathcal{F}$ contains an $\varepsilon$-homogeneous set of size at least $\varepsilon^{C_{\mathcal{F}}}n$.
rows.context_text: A graph on $n$ vertices is $\alpha$-close to a family $\mathcal{F}$ if one can add or delete at most $\alpha n^2/2$ edges to obtain a graph in $\mathcal{F}$. This conjecture, introduced as a natural extension for hereditary families, defines the polynomial close Rödl property; a family with this property also has the polynomial Rödl property.
rows.source_text: For each proper hereditary family $\mathcal{F}$ of graphs there are $C_{\mathcal{F}},d_{\mathcal{F}}>0$ such that if $0<\varepsilon<1/2$, every graph on $n$ vertices which is $\varepsilon^{d_{\mathcal{F}}}$-close to $\mathcal{F}$ contains an $\varepsilon$-homogeneous set of size at least $\varepsilon^{C_{\mathcal{F}}}n$.
rows.class_notes: Polynomial 'close Rodl' property for hereditary families — a strengthening/equivalent-form circle around Erdos-Hajnal; alpha-closeness defined in context, epsilon-homogeneous is standard.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.1
rows.paper_title: On off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers
rows.statement_text: There exists an absolute constant $a>0$ such that, for all $3$-graphs $G$ and $H$, $r^{p}(G,H)\geq r(G,H)^{a}$ whenever $p>r(G,H)^{o(1)}$.
rows.context_text: The authors introduce the notion of pair constructions $\chi_{f,g}$ of complexity $p$, observing that stepping-up and inducibility constructions both fit this paradigm. They conjecture that any Ramsey coloring of order $N$ yields one of order $N^{\Omega(1)}$ with much smaller complexity, which would explain the prevalence of low-complexity constructions in Ramsey theory and potentially illuminate the possible growth rates of $r(H, K_{n}^{(3)})$.
rows.source_text: There exists an absolute constant $a>0$ such that, for all $3$-graphs $G$ and $H$, $r^{p}(G,H)\geq r(G,H)^{a}$ whenever $p>r(G,H)^{o(1)}$.
rows.class_notes: Relies on the paper-local notion of pair-construction complexity p and r^p(G,H), not defined in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.3
rows.paper_title: On off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers
rows.statement_text: There exists a $3$-graph $H$ for which $r(H,K_{n}^{(3)})=2^{\Theta_{H}(n)}$.
rows.context_text: The paper establishes $r(H,K_{n}^{(3)})=2^{\Theta_{H}(n\log n)}$ for many $3$-graphs $H$, but no example achieving the purely exponential rate $2^{\Theta(n)}$ is known. The authors note that a specific constructed $3$-graph with $\frac{1}{3}<m_{\textnormal{pair}}(H)<\frac{1}{2}$, to which Theorem 1.1 does not apply yet $r(H,K_{n}^{(3)})\geq 2^{\Omega_H(n)}$ holds by Theorem 1.2, may be a promising candidate.
rows.source_text: There exists a $3$-graph $H$ for which $r(H,K_{n}^{(3)})=2^{\Theta_{H}(n)}$.
rows.class_notes: Standard vocabulary: existence of a 3-graph H with r(H,K_n^{(3)}) purely exponential.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.6
rows.paper_title: On off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers
rows.statement_text: For every $c>0$, there exists $s$ such that $r(K_{s}^{(3)},K_{n}^{(3)})\geq 2^{\Omega_{s}(n^{c})}$.
rows.context_text: The authors ask what growth rates are possible for off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers, noting that for the family $\mathcal{H}$ of links of nonbipartite graphs the rate $2^{\Theta_{\mathcal{H}}(n)}$ is achievable. They contrast this with the tripartite setting, where $r(H,K_{n,n,n}^{(3)})\leq 2^{O_H(n^2)}$ for all $H$, and note the conjecture fails with $K_{n,n,n}^{(3)}$ in place of $K_{n}^{(3)}$.
rows.source_text: For every $c>0$, there exists $s$ such that $r(K_{s}^{(3)},K_{n}^{(3)})\geq 2^{\Omega_{s}(n^{c})}$.
rows.class_notes: Standard off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey lower-bound claim.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.7
rows.paper_title: On off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers
rows.statement_text: There is an absolute constant $C>0$ such that $r(F,K_{n}^{(3)})\leq 2^{O_{F}(n^{C})}$ for every fixed linear $3$-graph $F$.
rows.context_text: Following Theorem 1.4, which disproves the polynomial upper bound for linear $3$-graphs, the authors propose the weaker conjecture that $r(F,K_{n}^{(3)})$ remains subexponential for linear $F$. They note possible further strengthenings (replacing $n^C$ by $n^{o(1)}$ or extending to $123$-inducible $3$-graphs) but are less certain about those variants.
rows.source_text: There is an absolute constant $C>0$ such that $r(F,K_{n}^{(3)})\leq 2^{O_{F}(n^{C})}$ for every fixed linear $3$-graph $F$.
rows.class_notes: 'Linear 3-graph' is standard; subexponential upper bound for r(F,K_n^{(3)}).

rows.title: Informal conjecture on tripartite off-diagonal Ramsey numbers
rows.paper_title: On off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers
rows.statement_text: It may be the case that $r(H,K_{n,n,n}^{(3)})\leq 2^{O_{H}(n\log n)}$ for all $H$.
rows.context_text: The authors note that $r(H,K_{n,n,n}^{(3)})\leq 2^{O_H(n^2)}$ for all $H$, and using the methods of [5] one can show a stronger bound $2^{O_H(n^{2-\epsilon})}$ for some $\epsilon>0$ depending on $H$. They suspect the true bound is $2^{O_H(n\log n)}$, which would help explain why the threshold $2^{\Theta(n\log n)}$ is so prevalent and hard to surpass in constructions for $K_n^{(3)}$.
rows.source_text: It may be the case that $r(H,K_{n,n,n}^{(3)})\leq 2^{O_{H}(n\log n)}$ for all $H$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2404.02021 — Informal conjecture on tripartite off-diagonal Ramsey numbers
rows.class_notes: Hedged ('may be the case') but a single precise proposition about tripartite off-diagonal Ramsey numbers.

rows.title: Problem 5.1 (cover-free families)
rows.paper_title: Lower bounds for graph reconstruction with maximal independent set queries
rows.statement_text: Are there $(1,r)$-cover-free families $\mathcal{F}\subseteq\mathcal{P}(t)$ with $t\in\mathcal{O}(r^{2}\log\lvert\mathcal{F}\rvert/\log r)$?
rows.context_text: The paper's lower bound for deterministic non-adaptive reconstruction ($\Omega(\Delta^{3}\log n/\log\Delta)$) is tied to a longstanding gap for cover-free families: $\Omega(r^{w+1}\log n/\log r)\leq t(n,w,r)\leq\mathcal{O}(r^{w+1}\log n)$. Closing this gap for $(1,r)$-cover-free families would directly improve the graph-reconstruction bounds.
rows.source_text: Are there $(1,r)$-cover-free families $\mathcal{F}\subseteq\mathcal{P}(t)$ with $t\in\mathcal{O}(r^{2}\log\lvert\mathcal{F}\rvert/\log r)$?
rows.defer_reason: Statement is pure extremal set theory (cover-free families in P(t)); the graph-reconstruction motivation lives only in context.

rows.title: Problem 5.2 (randomised non-adaptive optimality)
rows.paper_title: Lower bounds for graph reconstruction with maximal independent set queries
rows.statement_text: Is there a randomised non-adaptive algorithm which uses $\mathcal{O}(\Delta^{2}\log(n/\Delta))$ queries to reconstruct any graph of maximum degree $\Delta$ with high probability?
rows.context_text: The paper proves an $\Omega(\Delta^{2}\log(n/\Delta))$ lower bound for randomised non-adaptive algorithms, while the existing upper bound of Konrad, O'Sullivan, and Traistaru is $\mathcal{O}(\Delta^{2}\log n)$. The question asks whether the new lower bound is tight, i.e., whether the $\log\Delta$ gap in the $n$-dependence can be closed.
rows.source_text: Is there a randomised non-adaptive algorithm which uses $\mathcal{O}(\Delta^{2}\log(n/\Delta))$ queries to reconstruct any graph of maximum degree $\Delta$ with high probability?
rows.class_notes: Query-based graph reconstruction; the MIS-query model and 'reconstruct with high probability' semantics are not defined in the record.

rows.title: Problem 5.3 (adaptive vs non-adaptive gap)
rows.paper_title: Lower bounds for graph reconstruction with maximal independent set queries
rows.statement_text: Is there a deterministic adaptive algorithm that uses $o(\Delta^{3}\log(n/\Delta))$ queries to reconstruct any graph of maximum degree $\Delta$?
rows.context_text: For adaptive algorithms the best lower bound drops to $\Omega(\Delta^{2}\log(n/\Delta)/\log\Delta)$ in both the deterministic and randomised settings, while the upper bounds match the non-adaptive case. This gap suggests adaptive algorithms might outperform non-adaptive ones, motivating the question of whether adaptivity yields a provable saving.
rows.source_text: Is there a deterministic adaptive algorithm that uses $o(\Delta^{3}\log(n/\Delta))$ queries to reconstruct any graph of maximum degree $\Delta$?
rows.class_notes: Query model (MIS queries per paper title) is not specified in statement or context; also needs algorithm/query-complexity model.

rows.title: Open Problem (Introduction)
rows.paper_title: Big line or big convex polygon
rows.statement_text: Determine the correct dependence of $ES_{\ell}(n)$ on $\ell$.
rows.context_text: The paper establishes that $(3\ell-1)\cdot 2^{n-5} < ES_{\ell}(n) < \ell^{2}\cdot 2^{n+C\sqrt{n\log n}}$, leaving a gap in the dependence on $\ell$. In the regime where $n$ is fixed and $\ell\to\infty$, the cups-caps theorem gives $ES_{\ell}(n)=O(\ell)$, which is best possible up to constants, but the general joint dependence on both parameters remains open.
rows.source_text: Determine the correct dependence of $ES_{\ell}(n)$ on $\ell$.
rows.defer_reason: Discrete geometry (big line or convex polygon), not graph theory; and asks to determine a growth rate rather than stating a proposition.
rows.class_notes: Erdos-Szekeres-type function for points in the plane; also a 'determine the dependence' meta-question.

rows.title: Problem 1.1
rows.paper_title: The largest subgraph without a forbidden induced subgraph
rows.statement_text: Given a hereditary property $\mathcal{P}$ which misses a bipartite graph and a $(c,t)$-sparse graph $\Gamma$, determine $\mathrm{ex}(\Gamma,\mathcal{P})$.
rows.context_text: The authors introduce the notion of a $(c,t)$-sparse graph $\Gamma$ (one where the edge density between any two vertex subsets of size at least $t$ is at most $1-c$), which generalizes both dense random graphs and $K_{t,t}$-free graphs. This problem asks for a unified Turán-type theory covering both settings, extending a question of Alon, Krivelevich, and Samotij on hereditary properties in random graphs.
rows.source_text: Given a hereditary property $\mathcal{P}$ which misses a bipartite graph and a $(c,t)$-sparse graph $\Gamma$, determine $\mathrm{ex}(\Gamma,\mathcal{P})$.
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine ex(Gamma,P)' is a research program, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Asks for a unified Turan-type theory for (c,t)-sparse hosts.

rows.title: Conjecture 6.1
rows.paper_title: The largest subgraph without a forbidden induced subgraph
rows.statement_text: For every $c>0$ and integer $\ell$ there exists $C>1$, such that if $\Gamma$ is a $(c,t)$-sparse graph then $\mathrm{ex}(\Gamma,\mathcal{P}_{C_{2\ell}})\leq Ct^{1-1/\ell}n^{1+1/\ell}$.
rows.context_text: The conjecture concerns the induced Turán number for even cycles $C_{2\ell}$ in $(c,t)$-sparse graphs. It is motivated by the fact that the family $\mathcal{F}_{C_{2\ell}}$ of clique quotients consists of all cycles of length between $\ell$ and $2\ell$, and that graphs of girth larger than $2\ell$ on $n$ vertices have at most $O(n^{1+1/\ell})$ edges.
rows.source_text: For every $c>0$ and integer $\ell$ there exists $C>1$, such that if $\Gamma$ is a $(c,t)$-sparse graph then $\mathrm{ex}(\Gamma,\mathcal{P}_{C_{2\ell}})\leq Ct^{1-1/\ell}n^{1+1/\ell}$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 6.1 from arXiv:2405.05902, asserting that ex(Gamma, P_{C_{2l}}) <= C t^{1-1/l} n^{1+1/l} for every (c,t)-sparse graph Gamma, was resolved by Ding, Gao, Liu, Luan, and Sun in November 2024. Their paper arXiv:2411.12659 proves that if an n-vertex (c,t)-sparse graph has at least C t^{1-1/l} n^{1+1/l} edges then it must contain an induced copy of C_{2l}, establishing the conjectured bound.
rows.class_notes: Precise inequality but the induced Turan number ex(Gamma,P_{C_{2l}}) and the role of n are paper-local and not fully defined in the record; status solved.

rows.title: Conjecture 10
rows.paper_title: Strong log-convexity of genus sequences
rows.statement_text: The genus distribution of every graph $G$ is unimodal.
rows.context_text: After disproving the LCGD Conjecture, the author notes that the counterexamples constructed are still unimodal. The author suggests that the unimodal weakening of the LCGD Conjecture could, and actually should, still be true.
rows.source_text: The genus distribution of every graph $G$ is unimodal.
rows.class_notes: Genus distribution (embedding counts by genus) is standard topological graph theory vocabulary; needs an embedding-counting layer.

rows.title: Conjecture 11
rows.paper_title: Strong log-convexity of genus sequences
rows.statement_text: Every graph that triangulates some surface has log-concave genus distribution.
rows.context_text: After proposing unimodality as a positive replacement for the disproved LCGD Conjecture, the author notes this stronger statement as also looking plausible, restricting log-concavity to graphs that triangulate some surface.
rows.source_text: Every graph that triangulates some surface has log-concave genus distribution.
rows.class_notes: Log-concavity of genus distribution restricted to surface triangulations; embedding layer required.

rows.title: Problem 3.1
rows.paper_title: Rainbow Stackings of Random Edge-Colorings
rows.statement_text: Determine whether or not there exists a function $r_{0}\colon\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{R}$ such that the following holds. For each $n\geq 1$, let $\chi_{1},\ldots,\chi_{m}\colon\binom{[n]}{2}\to\mathcal{C}_{r}$ be independent uniformly random $r$-edge-colorings. If $r=r(n)$ satisfies $r(n)<r_{0}(n)$, then with high probability, there does not exist a rainbow stacking of $\chi_{1},\ldots,\chi_{m}$. If $r=r(n)$ satisfies $r(n)>r_{0}(n)$, then with high probability, there exists a rainbow stacking of $\chi_{1},\ldots,\chi_{m}$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 shows that the existence of rainbow stackings exhibits a sharp threshold, with the transition occurring within an interval of length roughly $(2m-1)/3$. The authors pose whether the transition is even sharper — i.e., whether a single threshold function $r_0$ governs the transition exactly. The critical value of $r$ is on the order of $n/\log n$, making such a sharp transition more dramatic than, for example, the 2-point concentration of the independence number of $G(n,1/2)$.
rows.source_text: Determine whether or not there exists a function $r_{0}\colon\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{R}$ such that the following holds. For each $n\geq 1$, let $\chi_{1},\ldots,\chi_{m}\colon\binom{[n]}{2}\to\mathcal{C}_{r}$ be independent uniformly random $r$-edge-colorings. If $r=r(n)$ satisfies $r(n)<r_{0}(n)$, then with high probability, there does not exist a rainbow stacking of $\chi_{1},\ldots,\chi_{m}$. If $r=r(n)$ satisfies $r(n)>r_{0}(n)$, then with high probability, there exists a rainbow stacking of $\chi_{1},\ldots,\chi_{m}$.
rows.class_notes: 'Rainbow stacking' is paper-local and never defined in the record; also whp/threshold asymptotics.

rows.title: Question 3.3
rows.paper_title: Rainbow Stackings of Random Edge-Colorings
rows.statement_text: Is it true that when $n$ is odd, every pair of proper edge-colorings of $K_{n}$ admits a rainbow stacking?
rows.context_text: Proposition 3.2 constructs, for each even $n\geq 4$, a pair of proper edge-colorings of $K_n$ with no rainbow stacking (based on a construction from transversals in Latin squares over $\mathbb{F}_2^k$). The question asks whether the odd case is categorically different. The answer is known to be affirmative for $n=3$ (by inspection) and $n=5$ (by computer search); a general resolution is expected to be difficult.
rows.source_text: Is it true that when $n$ is odd, every pair of proper edge-colorings of $K_{n}$ admits a rainbow stacking?
rows.class_notes: Both agree B3: 'rainbow stacking' is paper-local and absent from the record; once recovered it is a buildable finite primitive (hence needs-primitive, not clean); repo resolved to chromatic-theory (proper edge-colorings of K_n).

rows.title: Question 6.1
rows.paper_title: Sharing tea on a graph
rows.statement_text: For every finite graph $G$, every ${w\in\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^{V(G)}}$, and every ${v\in V(G)}$, is there always a ${(w,v)}$-optimal sequence whose length is at most $2^{{\left\lvert{V(G)}\right\rvert}}$?
rows.context_text: The authors prove that for finite graphs the maximum amount of tea at any vertex $v$ can always be achieved after a finite number of sharing moves, but the sharp upper bound on the length of a shortest optimal sequence is unknown. One natural approach—showing that an optimal sequence need not repeat any sharing move—is complicated by Proposition 6.2, which shows that there need not exist an optimal sequence in which no move is a subset of a later move.
rows.source_text: For every finite graph $G$, every ${w\in\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^{V(G)}}$, and every ${v\in V(G)}$, is there always a ${(w,v)}$-optimal sequence whose length is at most $2^{{\left\lvert{V(G)}\right\rvert}}$?
rows.class_notes: 'Sharing move' and '(w,v)-optimal sequence' are paper-local notions not defined in the record; the process itself is a buildable finite primitive over real weights.

rows.title: Open Problem: Polynomial-time MWIS on even-hole-free graphs
rows.paper_title: Tree Independence Number IV. Even-hole-free Graphs
rows.statement_text: Does there exist a polynomial-time algorithm for the Maximum Weight Independent Set problem on even-hole-free graphs?
rows.context_text: On even-hole-free graphs, Maximum Weight Clique is known to be polynomial-time solvable, but the question of whether a polynomial-time algorithm exists for Maximum Weight Independent Set remains open. This is in stark contrast to perfect graphs, for which polynomial-time algorithms have been known since 1981. The present paper gives a quasi-polynomial-time algorithm via a polylogarithmic tree independence number bound, but the polynomial-time question is not resolved.
rows.source_text: Does there exist a polynomial-time algorithm for the Maximum Weight Independent Set problem on even-hole-free graphs?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2407.08927 — Open Problem: Polynomial-time MWIS on even-hole-free graphs
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no: poly-time MWIS on even-hole-free graphs; needs a computation-model foundation.

rows.title: Open Problem: Polynomial-time Coloring on even-hole-free graphs
rows.paper_title: Tree Independence Number IV. Even-hole-free Graphs
rows.statement_text: Does there exist a polynomial-time algorithm for the Coloring problem (computing the chromatic number) on even-hole-free graphs?
rows.context_text: On even-hole-free graphs, the question of whether a polynomial-time algorithm for Coloring exists remains open, in stark contrast to perfect graphs where polynomial-time algorithms have been known since 1981. The paper's quasi-polynomial-time results via tree independence number do not resolve this question.
rows.source_text: Does there exist a polynomial-time algorithm for the Coloring problem (computing the chromatic number) on even-hole-free graphs?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2407.08927 — Open Problem: Polynomial-time Coloring on even-hole-free graphs
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no: poly-time chromatic number on even-hole-free graphs.

rows.title: Conjecture 2
rows.paper_title: Towards Characterization of 5-List-Colorability of Toroidal Graphs
rows.statement_text: A graph drawn on the torus is critical for 5-choosability if and only if it is $6$-critical.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1 establishes this result under the additional assumption that there exists a cyclic system of non-contractible triangles around the torus with consecutive triangles at distance at most four. The authors verified that $K_7$ is the only graph critical for 5-list coloring but not for 5-choosability satisfying the assumptions of Theorem 1, and believe the cyclic system assumption can be dropped entirely.
rows.source_text: A graph drawn on the torus is critical for 5-choosability if and only if it is $6$-critical.
rows.class_notes: Criticality for 5-choosability vs 6-criticality on the torus; standard list-coloring/criticality vocabulary plus torus embedding layer.

rows.title: Conjecture 3
rows.paper_title: Towards Characterization of 5-List-Colorability of Toroidal Graphs
rows.statement_text: A toroidal graph is $5$-choosable if and only if it is $5$-colorable.
rows.context_text: This conjecture is stated as equivalent to Conjecture 2 and represents the central goal of the paper: a complete choosability analogue of Thomassen's characterization of 5-colorability on the torus. The computational results of the paper, enumerating minimal obstructions under a cyclic-triangle-system condition, provide supporting evidence.
rows.source_text: A toroidal graph is $5$-choosable if and only if it is $5$-colorable.
rows.class_notes: Toroidal 5-choosable iff 5-colorable; stated as equivalent to the paper's Conjecture 2 (axv_2407_18800_00).

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: Towards Characterization of 5-List-Colorability of Toroidal Graphs
rows.statement_text: Every graph drawn on the torus with edge-width at least four is 5-choosable.
rows.context_text: This is proposed as an important intermediate step towards Conjecture 3, focusing on toroidal graphs with no non-contractible cycle of length less than four. Postle proved that the edge-width of a graph critical for 5-list coloring is at most logarithmic in its genus, so the conjecture holds when four is replaced by a sufficiently large constant.
rows.source_text: Every graph drawn on the torus with edge-width at least four is 5-choosable.
rows.class_notes: Edge-width (shortest non-contractible cycle) is standard for embedded graphs.

rows.title: Conjecture 8
rows.paper_title: Towards Characterization of 5-List-Colorability of Toroidal Graphs
rows.statement_text: There is no critical prism-canvas of spacing at least five.
rows.context_text: This conjecture arises from the paper's computer-assisted enumeration: using the classification of chordless critical cycle-canvases, the authors obtained 510, 2719, 300, and 5 candidates for critical prism-canvases of spacing at most four (after eliminating provably non-critical ones), and conjecture the list is empty at spacing five and beyond.
rows.source_text: There is no critical prism-canvas of spacing at least five.
rows.class_notes: 'Critical prism-canvas' and 'spacing' are heavy paper-local machinery not defined in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 7
rows.paper_title: Clustered Colouring of Graph Products
rows.statement_text: For any fixed integer $c \geq 2$, there exists an integer $t$ such that for infinitely many graphs $H$ with treewidth $t$ and paths $P$, every $c$-colouring of $H \boxtimes P$ has clustering $\Omega(|V(H \boxtimes P)|^{c/(c^2-c+1)})$.
rows.context_text: The upper bound in Theorem 4 gives that $H_1 \boxtimes H_2$ is $c$-colourable with clustering $O(|V(H_1 \boxtimes H_2)|^{c/(c^2-c+1)})$ when one graph has bounded degree. The authors believe this upper bound is asymptotically tight, and furthermore conjecture that there exists a matching construction where the bounded-degree graph is a path.
rows.source_text: For any fixed integer $c \geq 2$, there exists an integer $t$ such that for infinitely many graphs $H$ with treewidth $t$ and paths $P$, every $c$-colouring of $H \boxtimes P$ has clustering $\Omega(|V(H \boxtimes P)|^{c/(c^2-c+1)})$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 7 asserts that for every fixed integer c≥2 the optimal clustering exponent c/(c²−c+1) for c-colourings of H⊠P (with H of fixed treewidth and P a path) is achieved by a matching lower bound. The source paper itself establishes this lower bound for c=2 (Lemma 15, exponent 2/3) and c=3 (Theorem 2, exponent 3/7), confirming the conjecture for those two cases. For c≥4 only a weaker lower bound is known and the conjecture remains open. No follow-up paper resolving the general case was found in a search of arXiv and journal literature through May 2026.
rows.class_notes: Clustering (max monochromatic component) and strong product are standard; Omega lower bound over an infinite family.

rows.title: Conjecture 8
rows.paper_title: Clustered Colouring of Graph Products
rows.statement_text: There exists a positive integer $t$ such that for infinitely many graphs $H_1, H_2$ with treewidth $t$, every $3$-colouring of $H_1 \boxtimes H_2$ has clustering $\Omega(|V(H_1 \boxtimes H_2)|^{4/7})$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 3 gives an upper bound of $O(|V(H_1 \boxtimes H_2)|^{4/7})$ for 3-colourings of products of two bounded-treewidth graphs with no degree restriction, but only a lower bound of $\Omega(|V(H_1 \boxtimes H_2)|^{1/2})$ is proved. The authors conjecture the $4/7$ exponent in the upper bound is asymptotically tight.
rows.source_text: There exists a positive integer $t$ such that for infinitely many graphs $H_1, H_2$ with treewidth $t$, every $3$-colouring of $H_1 \boxtimes H_2$ has clustering $\Omega(|V(H_1 \boxtimes H_2)|^{4/7})$.
rows.class_notes: Matching-lower-bound conjecture for 3-colourings of products of bounded-treewidth graphs.
