rows.title: Problem 1.5
rows.paper_title: On the difference between the chromatic and cochromatic number
rows.statement_text: Let $k\geq 5$. Is there a graph $G_{k}$ with $\omega(G_{k})<5$, $\zeta(G_{k})=k$ and $\chi(G_{k})=\zeta(G_{k})+3$?
rows.context_text: After disproving Conjecture 1.2, the authors ask whether the gap $\chi(G)-\zeta(G)$ can reach $3$ for graphs with $\omega(G)<5$ and arbitrarily large cochromatic number $\zeta(G)$. Theorem 1.4 guarantees such graphs exist with gap at least $2$ for all large $k$, but gap $3$ remains open.
rows.source_text: Let $k\geq 5$. Is there a graph $G_{k}$ with $\omega(G_{k})<5$, $\zeta(G_{k})=k$ and $\chi(G_{k})=\zeta(G_{k})+3$?
rows.class_notes: Cochromatic number is a standard finite invariant; clean existence question per k>=5.

rows.title: Open question on strong 2-kernels in split digraphs
rows.paper_title: Distant digraph domination
rows.statement_text: Does every split digraph $G$ with no sources admit a strong 2-kernel of size at most $|G|/2$?
rows.context_text: The paper proves (Theorem 2.4) that every split digraph with no sources has a 2-kernel of size at most $|G|/2$. A 2-kernel $K$ is called strong if for every vertex $v$ in the tournament part $T$, either some vertex of $K$ 1-covers $v$, or some vertex of $K \cap T$ 2-covers $v$. The authors note in passing that they do not know whether the size bound survives under this strengthening.
rows.source_text: Does every split digraph $G$ with no sources admit a strong 2-kernel of size at most $|G|/2$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2409.05039 — Open question on strong 2-kernels in split digraphs
rows.class_notes: Split digraph, 2-kernel, and 1-/2-covering are not fully defined in the record (only 'strong' is); all are simple finite notions once recovered.

rows.title: Informal Conjecture (improved linear bound)
rows.paper_title: A Linear Lower Bound for the Square Energy of Graphs
rows.statement_text: We believe the above bound can be improved to $\frac{4n}{5}$ using more intricate partitioning of the graph $G$ and applying Theorem 2.
rows.context_text: After establishing $s(G) \geq \frac{3n}{4}$, the authors note that a more refined partitioning argument could push the constant to $\frac{4n}{5}$, but that fully resolving Conjecture 1 ($s(G) \geq n-1$) requires fundamentally new ideas beyond those used in this paper.
rows.source_text: We believe the above bound can be improved to $\frac{4n}{5}$ using more intricate partitioning of the graph $G$ and applying Theorem 2.
rows.class_notes: Informal 'we believe' improvement of 'the above bound'; square energy s(G) is not defined in the record; underlying claim s(G)>=4n/5 is concrete once recovered.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.2
rows.paper_title: When all directed cycles have length three
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a strongly 2-connected digraph drawn (possibly with crossings) in an annulus $\Sigma$, such that every directed cycle has winding number one around the annulus in the natural sense. Then $G$ can be drawn in $\Sigma$ without crossings, such that every directed cycle has winding number one.
rows.context_text: This conjecture arises from an analogy with Thomassen's theorem on acyclic digraphs: gluing the north and south sides of a rectangle produces an annulus, and the question becomes whether a drawing with crossings in the annulus can be continuously deformed to a crossing-free drawing while preserving winding numbers of directed cycles. The author states explicitly that 'we cannot prove it.'
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a strongly 2-connected digraph drawn (possibly with crossings) in an annulus $\Sigma$, such that every directed cycle has winding number one around the annulus in the natural sense. Then $G$ can be drawn in $\Sigma$ without crossings, such that every directed cycle has winding number one.
rows.class_notes: Needs drawings in an annulus and winding numbers; 'in the natural sense' is mildly informal but the statement is otherwise precise.

rows.title: Informal Question (NP-characterization of weightable digraphs)
rows.paper_title: When all directed cycles have length three
rows.statement_text: Can we give a construction for all weightable digraphs (i.e., an NP-characterization)?
rows.context_text: A digraph $G$ is weightable if one can assign a real weight $w(e)$ to each edge $e$ such that $\sum_{e\in E(C)}w(e)=1$ for each directed cycle $C$. The paper shows weightability is characterizable by excluded subdigraphs (giving a co-NP characterization), but an NP-characterization via explicit construction remains open. For strongly 2-connected digraphs, Conjecture 1.2 would provide such a construction.
rows.source_text: Can we give a construction for all weightable digraphs (i.e., an NP-characterization)?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2410.13008 — Informal Question (NP-characterization of weightable digraphs)
rows.status_semantics: Berger, Carter, and Seymour (arXiv:2601.12746, 2026) provide two hierarchical constructions: one that builds every planar weightable digraph from circular digraphs, and one that builds every weightable digraph from planar ones. Together these yield a structural construction for all weightable digraphs, substantially addressing the informal question, alongside a poly-time algorithm to test weightability. Whether this constitutes a full NP-characterization in the formal complexity sense (a polynomial-time verifiable certificate) is not explicitly confirmed in the abstract.
rows.defer_reason: 'Can we give a construction / NP-characterization' is a meta-request, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Asks for an explicit constructive characterization of weightable digraphs.

rows.title: Question 1.1
rows.paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions XVI. Complete bipartite induced minors
rows.statement_text: What are the unavoidable induced subgraphs of graphs with large treewidth?
rows.context_text: This question is the central goal of the series of papers on induced subgraphs and tree decompositions. The answer is known when 'induced subgraphs' is replaced by 'subgraphs' or 'minors' via Robertson and Seymour's grid theorem, but the induced setting is more complex, requiring all basic obstructions (complete graphs, complete bipartite graphs, subdivided walls, and their line graphs) as well as non-basic ones such as Pohoata-Davies graphs, occultations, and layered wheels.
rows.source_text: What are the unavoidable induced subgraphs of graphs with large treewidth?
rows.status_semantics: What the 'disproved' badge means here: the question is not being declared meaningless or trivially closed — rather, the clean answer it hoped for is now proven impossible. Question 1.1 asks for the unavoidable induced subgraphs of graphs with large treewidth: the induced-subgraph analogue of the Robertson–Seymour Grid Minor theorem (where subdivided walls are the unavoidable subgraphs) and the central programmatic goal of the Chudnovsky–Hajebi–Spirkl 'Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions' series. The hope was a single 'holy grail' family F* of unavoidable induced subgraphs that works for every hereditary class of unbounded treewidth. That hope is refuted by Alecu, Bonnet, Bureo Villafana and Trotignon, 'Every Graph is Essential to Large Treewidth' (arXiv:2502.14775, Feb 2025): their Theorem 1.1 shows that for every single graph H there is a hereditary (weakly sparse) class of unbounded treewidth whose H-free subclass has bounded treewidth — so every graph H is 'essential', and no F* can work except the class of all graphs. There is therefore no clean induced analogue of the Grid Minor theorem, and several explicit conjectures fall with it (Hajebi's Conjectures 1.14/1.15 and a conjecture of Trotignon). What remains alive is the restricted programme: for specific excluded-obstruction classes the CHS series still proves treewidth characterizations (e.g. paper XIX, arXiv:2506.05602). So: general clean characterization — provably does not exist; structure theory for restricted hereditary classes — ongoing.
rows.defer_reason: 'What are the unavoidable induced subgraphs' is a characterization program, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Grand characterization question driving the whole paper series; status recorded as disproved presumably via non-basic obstructions.

rows.title: Conjecture on $\lambda(d,1)$
rows.paper_title: On hypercube statistics
rows.statement_text: $\lambda(d,1)=(1+o(1))/e$ where the $o(1)$-term tends to $0$ as $d$ tends to infinity.
rows.context_text: The lower bound in Theorem 2, $(1-2^{-d})^{2^{d}-1}$, approaches $e^{-1}\approx 0.37$ as $d\to\infty$. The authors suspect this lower bound is asymptotically sharp, making $1/e$ the true limit of $\lambda(d,1)$.
rows.source_text: $\lambda(d,1)=(1+o(1))/e$ where the $o(1)$-term tends to $0$ as $d$ tends to infinity.
rows.class_notes: lambda(d,s) is paper-local and never defined in the record; asymptotic limit statement.

rows.title: Open problem on tight bounds for $\lambda(d,s)$
rows.paper_title: On hypercube statistics
rows.statement_text: Obtain tight bounds for $\lambda(d,s)$ for essentially all admissible values of $d$ and $s$.
rows.context_text: The paper proves several upper and lower bounds on $\lambda(d,s)$ and determines the exact set of $s$ for which $\lambda(d,s)=1$, but tight asymptotic characterisation of $\lambda(d,s)$ for general $s$ remains open.
rows.source_text: Obtain tight bounds for $\lambda(d,s)$ for essentially all admissible values of $d$ and $s$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2410.20498 — Open problem on tight bounds for $\lambda(d,s)$
rows.status_semantics: The open problem of obtaining tight bounds for $\lambda(d,s)$ for essentially all admissible $(d,s)$ remains open in general. Bodnár and Pikhurko (2025) made partial progress by determining exact values for the three specific pairs $(3,2)$, $(4,2)$, and $(4,4)$ using flag algebras, with lower bounds coming from blowups of small Hamming codes. The general asymptotic characterisation for all admissible $d$ and $s$ is unresolved.
rows.defer_reason: 'Obtain tight bounds for essentially all admissible values' is an open-ended program, not a proposition.
rows.class_notes: lambda(d,s) also undefined in the record.

rows.title: Problem 6
rows.paper_title: Blow-ups and extensions of trees in tournaments
rows.statement_text: Let $\alpha$ be a positive real number. Does there exist a polynomial $P_{\alpha}$ such that $\operatorname{unvd}(D)\leqslant P_{\alpha}(|V(D)|)$ for every digraph with maximum average degree at most $\alpha$?
rows.context_text: Fox, He, and Wigderson showed that acyclic digraphs with bounded maximum degree are not linearly unavoidable, ruling out linear bounds. This problem asks whether the weaker polynomial unavoidability still holds for digraphs with bounded maximum average degree, and is complemented by the second question of which families of such digraphs are linearly unavoidable.
rows.source_text: Let $\alpha$ be a positive real number. Does there exist a polynomial $P_{\alpha}$ such that $\operatorname{unvd}(D)\leqslant P_{\alpha}(|V(D)|)$ for every digraph with maximum average degree at most $\alpha$?
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no once the unavoidability number unvd (min tournament order containing D) primitive is built; maximum average degree is standard.

rows.title: Conjecture 7
rows.paper_title: Blow-ups and extensions of trees in tournaments
rows.statement_text: If $\cal F$ is linearly unavoidable and has bounded maximum average degree, then ${\cal F}[k]$ is also linearly unavoidable.
rows.context_text: This conjecture asks whether the $k$-blow-up operation preserves linear unavoidability. The bounded maximum average degree condition is necessary, as shown by an example using disjoint unions of transitive tournaments. The paper proves this conjecture holds for the family of oriented trees.
rows.source_text: If $\cal F$ is linearly unavoidable and has bounded maximum average degree, then ${\cal F}[k]$ is also linearly unavoidable.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 7 asks whether the $k$-blow-up operation preserves linear unavoidability under a bounded maximum average degree condition. The source paper itself proves the conjecture for the special case where $\mathcal{F}$ is the family of oriented trees, establishing that $k$-blow-ups of oriented trees are linearly unavoidable. No subsequent paper resolving the full conjecture has been found in the literature as of May 2026.
rows.class_notes: Needs unvd primitive plus standard k-blow-up of a digraph family; 'linearly unavoidable' is reconstructible (unvd linear in order over the family). Status partial (proved for oriented trees).

rows.title: Problem 8
rows.paper_title: Blow-ups and extensions of trees in tournaments
rows.statement_text: What is the infimum of all the constants $C$ such that for every large enough integer $n$, the $k$-blow-up of every oriented tree of order $n$ is $(C^{k}\cdot kn)$-unavoidable?
rows.context_text: The paper proves in Theorem 18 that the $k$-blow-up of an oriented tree of order $n$ is $(2^{10+18k}\cdot kn)$-unavoidable, but this upper bound is acknowledged to be far from tight. The maximum average degree of the $k$-blow-up of a tree provides a lower bound via Proposition 2.
rows.source_text: What is the infimum of all the constants $C$ such that for every large enough integer $n$, the $k$-blow-up of every oriented tree of order $n$ is $(C^{k}\cdot kn)$-unavoidable?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the infimum of all constants C' asks for a value, not a proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 9
rows.paper_title: Blow-ups and extensions of trees in tournaments
rows.statement_text: There exists a constant $C$ such that $\operatorname{unvd}(D)\leqslant C\cdot\operatorname{unvd}(D-v)$ for every acyclic digraph $D$ and for every vertex $v$ of $D$.
rows.context_text: This conjecture generalizes Proposition 1, which gives $\operatorname{unvd}(D)\leqslant 2\operatorname{unvd}(D-x)$ only when $x$ is a source or sink of $D$, to arbitrary vertices. Together with Sumner's Conjecture 4, it implies Conjecture 10 about $k$-extensions of oriented trees.
rows.source_text: There exists a constant $C$ such that $\operatorname{unvd}(D)\leqslant C\cdot\operatorname{unvd}(D-v)$ for every acyclic digraph $D$ and for every vertex $v$ of $D$.
rows.class_notes: Multiplicative stability of unvd under vertex deletion; clean once unvd primitive exists.

rows.title: Conjecture 10
rows.paper_title: Blow-ups and extensions of trees in tournaments
rows.statement_text: There is an absolute constant $C$ such that for every integer $k$, every $k$-extension of an oriented tree of order $n$ is $C^{k}(2n-2)$-unavoidable.
rows.context_text: This conjecture is implied by Conjecture 9 (multiplicative stability under vertex addition) together with Sumner's Conjecture 4 (that oriented trees are $(2n-2)$-unavoidable). The paper proves a weaker result in Corollary 25, establishing $(2\cdot 3^{\binom{2k+2}{2}}\cdot|V(F)|)$-unavoidability for $k$-extensions of sufficiently large oriented trees.
rows.source_text: There is an absolute constant $C$ such that for every integer $k$, every $k$-extension of an oriented tree of order $n$ is $C^{k}(2n-2)$-unavoidable.
rows.class_notes: Precise bound but the paper-local 'k-extension' operation is not defined in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 11
rows.paper_title: Blow-ups and extensions of trees in tournaments
rows.statement_text: If $\cal F$ is linearly unavoidable, then the family of $k$-extensions of digraphs in $\cal F$ is also linearly unavoidable.
rows.context_text: This conjecture, which would follow from Conjecture 9, asserts that the $k$-extension operation preserves linear unavoidability in general. The paper proves this for the specific family of oriented trees (and oriented forests) in Corollary 25.
rows.source_text: If $\cal F$ is linearly unavoidable, then the family of $k$-extensions of digraphs in $\cal F$ is also linearly unavoidable.
rows.class_notes: Same gap as Conjecture 10: 'k-extension' is paper-local and undefined in the record.

rows.title: Problem 12
rows.paper_title: Blow-ups and extensions of trees in tournaments
rows.statement_text: What is the minimum function $f$ such that every $k$-extension of every forest of order $n$ is $(2^{f(k)}\cdot n)$-unavoidable?
rows.context_text: This is the analogue of Problem 8 for extensions rather than blow-ups. Corollary 25 gives $f(k)\leqslant\binom{2k+2}{2}$ (quadratic upper bound), while Proposition 28 shows that the unavoidability of some $k$-extensions of the arcless digraph on $n$ vertices is $2^{k}n-o(n)$, giving a linear lower bound on $f$.
rows.source_text: What is the minimum function $f$ such that every $k$-extension of every forest of order $n$ is $(2^{f(k)}\cdot n)$-unavoidable?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the minimum function f' asks to determine a growth rate, not a proposition.
rows.class_notes: Also depends on the undefined k-extension operation.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.1
rows.paper_title: When are off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers polynomial?
rows.statement_text: For a $3$-graph $H$, there exists a constant $c$ depending only on $H$ such that $r(H,K_{n}^{(3)})\leq n^{c}$ for all $n$ if and only if $H$ is a subgraph of an iterated blowup of an edge.
rows.context_text: The paper proposes a full classification of those $3$-graphs $H$ for which the off-diagonal Ramsey number $r(H,K_{n}^{(3)})$ grows polynomially with $n$. The 'if' direction (polynomial upper bound when $H$ is iterated tripartite) was already known to Erdős and Hajnal; the 'only if' direction is new. The problem was first raised explicitly by the first author at an AIM workshop in 2015.
rows.source_text: For a $3$-graph $H$, there exists a constant $c$ depending only on $H$ such that $r(H,K_{n}^{(3)})\leq n^{c}$ for all $n$ if and only if $H$ is a subgraph of an iterated blowup of an edge.
rows.class_notes: Needs hypergraph Ramsey number wrapper plus 'iterated blowup of an edge' construction; iff classification stated as one proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.2
rows.paper_title: When are off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers polynomial?
rows.statement_text: If $k>s$ and $H$ is a $k$-graph which is $s$-tightly connected and not $k$-partite, then there exists a positive constant $c$ such that $r(H,K_{n}^{(k)})\geq t_{s}(n^{c})$, where the tower function $t_{i}(x)$ is defined by $t_{1}(x)=x$ and $t_{i}(x)=2^{t_{i-1}(x)}$ for all $i\geq 2$.
rows.context_text: The authors describe this as 'likely difficult' and note it would extend Theorem 4.1 (proved in the paper). It posits a tower-height lower bound of $t_s$ for the Ramsey number of any $s$-tightly connected $k$-graph that is not $k$-partite, generalising the $k=3, s=2$ case established in Theorem 4.1.
rows.source_text: If $k>s$ and $H$ is a $k$-graph which is $s$-tightly connected and not $k$-partite, then there exists a positive constant $c$ such that $r(H,K_{n}^{(k)})\geq t_{s}(n^{c})$, where the tower function $t_{i}(x)$ is defined by $t_{1}(x)=x$ and $t_{i}(x)=2^{t_{i-1}(x)}$ for all $i\geq 2$.
rows.class_notes: Tower function defined in-statement; 's-tightly connected' is standard hypergraph vocabulary but needs a new primitive.

rows.title: Conjecture 5.1
rows.paper_title: On the clique number of random Cayley graphs and related topics
rows.statement_text: There exists $C>0$ and a two-coloring of $\mathbb{F}_{2}^{n}\setminus\{0\}$ such that there is no subspace $H$ of size $Cn$ for which all nonzero elements of the subspace are monochromatic.
rows.context_text: Alon's conjecture remains wide open for $\mathbb{F}_{2}^{n}$ and $\mathbb{F}_{3}^{n}$. The finite unions theorem rules out a two-coloring with no monochromatic subspace at all over $\mathbb{F}_{2}^{n}$ for large $n$, so progress requires understanding the quantitative threshold. Establishing this conjecture would be an important step toward proving Alon's conjecture for $\mathbb{F}_{2}^{n}$, though the true bound on the minimum monochromatic subspace size may be considerably smaller.
rows.source_text: There exists $C>0$ and a two-coloring of $\mathbb{F}_{2}^{n}\setminus\{0\}$ such that there is no subspace $H$ of size $Cn$ for which all nonzero elements of the subspace are monochromatic.
rows.defer_reason: out-of-scope-v2: F_2^n subspace-coloring additive combinatorics — no graph object in the statement; plan explicitly parks F_p^n additive combinatorics under B5 (decision D1).
rows.class_notes: Resolution: sided with A on scope (plan lists F_p^n additive combinatorics as canonical B5) while keeping the agreed 'variant' adjudication — quantitative stepping stone toward ramsey_properties_of_cayley_graphs (future @EDGE; B also notes 'size Cn' should read size >= Cn).

rows.title: Conjecture 1.7
rows.paper_title: Local Shearer bound
rows.statement_text: Every triangle-free graph $G$ satisfies $\chi(G)\leq(1+o(1))\frac{\rho(G)}{\ln\rho(G)}$.
rows.context_text: Motivated by Harris's conjecture [27] that every triangle-free $d$-degenerate graph satisfies $\chi_f(G)\leq O(\frac{d}{\ln d})$ and by Molloy's bound $(1+o(1))\frac{\Delta(G)}{\ln\Delta(G)}$ in terms of the maximum degree. Since the spectral radius $\rho(G)$ is sandwiched between the degeneracy and the maximum degree, Theorem 1.6 of this paper (a new spectral upper bound on $\chi_f$) is presented as a first step toward interpolating between these two regimes.
rows.source_text: Every triangle-free graph $G$ satisfies $\chi(G)\leq(1+o(1))\frac{\rho(G)}{\ln\rho(G)}$.
rows.class_notes: chi vs spectral radius with (1+o(1)) factor; needs reals and asymptotic wrapper.

rows.title: Open question on Theorem 1.2 with $L=1$
rows.paper_title: Asymptotic structure. I. Coarse tree-width
rows.statement_text: We do not know whether 1.2 holds with $L=1$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.2 extends the result of Berger and Seymour (Theorem 1.1) from trees to graphs of bounded tree-width, establishing that a graph admitting a tree-decomposition whose bags are each contained in the union of a bounded number of balls of bounded radius admits an $(L,C)$-quasi-isometry to a graph of bounded tree-width. The authors note that unlike the tree case (where $L=1$ is achievable), it is unknown whether $L=1$ suffices in the general bounded-tree-width setting.
rows.source_text: We do not know whether 1.2 holds with $L=1$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2501.09839 — Open question on Theorem 1.2 with $L=1$
rows.status_semantics: The open question of whether Theorem 1.2 of Nguyen–Scott–Seymour holds with $L=1$ (i.e., whether an additive quasi-isometry to a graph of bounded tree-width always suffices) remains unresolved in full generality. The sequel paper arXiv:2509.09031 establishes $L=1$ for path-width but not tree-width. A partial result (arXiv:2503.00798) achieves additive quasi-isometry ($L=1$) to graphs of tree-width at most two for the special class of $K_{2,3}$-induced minor-free graphs, indicating partial progress on the conjecture for restricted graph classes.
rows.class_notes: References internal Theorem 1.2; (L,C)-quasi-isometry setup only paraphrased in context.

rows.title: Question 2
rows.paper_title: Shallow brambles
rows.statement_text: Is it true that for every graph class $\mathcal{C}$ with polynomial expansion, there exists a polynomial function $f$ such that for every integer $r\geqslant 0$ and every graph $G\in\mathcal{C}$, $$\mathrm{scol}_{r}(G)\leqslant f(r,\mathrm{bn}_{r}(G))?$$
rows.context_text: A positive answer to this question would be sufficient to answer Question 1 positively. The polynomial expansion assumption on $\mathcal{C}$ is essential: without it, $\mathrm{scol}_r(G)$ cannot be bounded by any function of $r$ and the depth-$r$ bramble number $\mathrm{bn}_r(G)$, even if the bramble number is replaced by $\mathrm{bn}_{g(r)}(G)$ for an arbitrary function $g$.
rows.source_text: Is it true that for every graph class $\mathcal{C}$ with polynomial expansion, there exists a polynomial function $f$ such that for every integer $r\geqslant 0$ and every graph $G\in\mathcal{C}$, $$\mathrm{scol}_{r}(G)\leqslant f(r,\mathrm{bn}_{r}(G))?$$
rows.class_notes: Depth-r bramble number bn_r is paper-introduced and not defined in context; scol_r and polynomial expansion also need primitives.

rows.title: Question 1.2
rows.paper_title: Lollipops, dense cycles and chords
rows.statement_text: Could it be that $f(\ell)=O(\ell\sqrt{\log\ell})$, matching the bound for standard minors from [Kos82, dlV83]?
rows.context_text: The authors define $f(\ell)$ as the smallest integer $\delta$ such that every graph of minimum degree at least $\delta$ contains $K_{\ell}$ as a cyclic minor. They prove $f(4)=3$, $6\leq f(5)\leq 8$, and more generally $f(\ell)=O(\ell^{2})$, and ask whether this quadratic bound can be improved to match the $O(\ell\sqrt{\log\ell})$ bound known for standard $K_\ell$-minors.
rows.source_text: Could it be that $f(\ell)=O(\ell\sqrt{\log\ell})$, matching the bound for standard minors from [Kos82, dlV83]?
rows.class_notes: f(l) is defined in context but 'cyclic minor' is a paper-local notion left undefined in the record.

rows.title: Question 4.1
rows.paper_title: Lollipops, dense cycles and chords
rows.statement_text: Is there a simple criterion for a sequence of vertices to be an active path of $K_{n}$?
rows.context_text: The authors introduce active paths as a key tool in the lollipop method. They note that not every Hamiltonian path starting at $c_1$ is active: in $K_6$ with Hamiltonian cycle $(c_1,\dots,c_6)$, the path $Q=c_1c_2c_5c_4c_3c_6$ is not active (there are 6 non-active paths among the 120 starting at $c_1$). The question asks for a simple characterisation of activity.
rows.source_text: Is there a simple criterion for a sequence of vertices to be an active path of $K_{n}$?
rows.defer_reason: Asks for a 'simple criterion' — characterization request, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: 'Active path' is also paper-local.

rows.title: Question 4.2
rows.paper_title: Lollipops, dense cycles and chords
rows.statement_text: Is there a simple way to compute all the active vertices in the cycle of an optimal lollipop?
rows.context_text: Active vertices determine the number of distinct chords produced by the lollipop argument. In the lollipop argument, the number of distinct active vertices (and thus chords) increases with the girth $g$ of the host graph, motivating the search for an efficient procedure to identify them.
rows.source_text: Is there a simple way to compute all the active vertices in the cycle of an optimal lollipop?
rows.defer_reason: Asks for a 'simple way to compute' — vague algorithmic meta-question.
rows.class_notes: Also relies on paper-local 'active vertices' and 'optimal lollipop'.

rows.title: Question 4.3
rows.paper_title: Lollipops, dense cycles and chords
rows.statement_text: Is there a constant $c>0$ such that every graph with minimum degree 3 contains a cycle of length $\ell$ with at least $c\ell$ chords?
rows.context_text: While high minimum degree guarantees cycles with many chords, the authors ask whether minimum degree 3 alone suffices to guarantee a cycle whose chord count is proportional to its length. The disjoint union of copies of $K_4$ shows one cannot simply require many chords without controlling cycle length, motivating the search for a linearly-chorded cycle.
rows.source_text: Is there a constant $c>0$ such that every graph with minimum degree 3 contains a cycle of length $\ell$ with at least $c\ell$ chords?
rows.status_semantics: Question 4.3 asks whether every graph with minimum degree 3 contains a cycle of length ℓ with at least cℓ chords for some constant c>0. Draganć and Girão (arXiv:2601.08769, 2026) proved that constant minimum degree suffices to guarantee a cycle of length ℓ with Ω(ℓ/log^C ℓ) chords, citing arXiv:2502.04726 directly. This achieves almost-linear chord count under constant minimum degree, but falls short of the conjectured exactly linear bound cℓ; the full statement of Question 4.3 remains open.
rows.class_notes: Self-contained: min degree 3 forces a cycle with linearly many chords.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.5
rows.paper_title: Induced Disjoint Paths Without an Induced Minor
rows.statement_text: For any subcubic graph $H$, $H$-\textsc{ISC} is in $\mathsf{P}$ if and only if $H$ is planar.
rows.context_text: The conjecture is proposed by the paper's authors as a natural open question following their proof that a specific non-planar subcubic graph yields NP-complete induced subdivision containment. It complements known polynomial-time algorithms for planar induced subdivision detection (e.g., for the net and $K_{4}$) and the new hardness results of the paper.
rows.source_text: For any subcubic graph $H$, $H$-\textsc{ISC} is in $\mathsf{P}$ if and only if $H$ is planar.
rows.class_notes: P-vs-NP dichotomy for induced subdivision containment; needs complexity foundation.

rows.title: Informal Question on Complexity Gap Between Induced Disjoint Paths Variants
rows.paper_title: Induced Disjoint Paths Without an Induced Minor
rows.statement_text: Is there a hereditary graph class in which Induced $k$-Disjoint Paths is NP-complete but Induced Disjoint $S$–$T$ Paths with $|S|=|T|=k$ is polynomial-time solvable? The case $k=2$ is of particular interest.
rows.context_text: The authors observe a structural asymmetry: polynomial-time algorithms in the literature tend to apply more broadly to Induced $k$-Disjoint Paths (the linkage variant), while hardness proofs tend to apply more broadly to Induced Disjoint $S$–$T$ Paths (the flow variant with $|S|=|T|=k$). This raises the question of whether the two problems can be separated in complexity on some hereditary class.
rows.source_text: Is there a hereditary graph class in which Induced $k$-Disjoint Paths is NP-complete but Induced Disjoint $S$–$T$ Paths with $|S|=|T|=k$ is polynomial-time solvable? The case $k=2$ is of particular interest.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2502.05289 — Informal Question on Complexity Gap Between Induced Disjoint Paths Variants
rows.class_notes: Clean existence question separating two induced disjoint paths variants on a hereditary class.

rows.title: Conjecture 3.4
rows.paper_title: Circular sorting
rows.statement_text: $t(n)=n-2$ if and only if $n$ is prime.
rows.context_text: The function $t(n)$ denotes the diameter of the swap graph on cyclic permutations when all swaps (not necessarily adjacent) are allowed. Observation 3.1 and Proposition 3.2 together establish that the condition holds whenever $n=2$ or $n$ is an odd prime, providing one direction of the biconditional.
rows.source_text: $t(n)=n-2$ if and only if $n$ is prime.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 3.4 asserts t(n)=n-2 iff n is prime; the source paper establishes the forward direction (n prime implies t(n)=n-2). A November 2025 follow-up (arXiv:2510.18529) proves the converse direction t(n)≤n-3 for all composite n divisible by 2 or 3, and for composite n whose permutations admit a polynomial representation over Z_n, giving a tight bound t(3p)=3p-3 for primes p. The conjecture remains open for composite n not divisible by 2 or 3 (e.g., n=25, 35, 49).
rows.defer_reason: Permutation sorting / symmetric group combinatorics, outside graph-theory scope.
rows.class_notes: Diameter of the all-swaps Cayley-type graph on cyclic permutations.

rows.title: Conjecture 3.6
rows.paper_title: Circular sorting
rows.statement_text: If $n>2$ and $\pi\in S_{n}$ satisfies [the condition that $\operatorname{cyc}(\sigma)=2$ for every $\sigma\in\pi C_{n}$], then $n$ is prime and [\pi = \pi_{n,a}$] for some $a\in{\mathbb{Z}}_{n}^{\times}$.
rows.context_text: Conjecture 3.6 is a structural strengthening of Conjecture 3.4: it characterises the permutations $\pi$ achieving $t([\pi])=n-2$ as exactly the permutations $\pi_{n,a}$ (defined via multiplication by $a$ in $\mathbb{Z}_n$) when $n$ is prime. The implication 'Conjecture 3.6 $\Rightarrow$ Conjecture 3.4' holds, and both have been verified for $n\leq 11$.
rows.source_text: If $n>2$ and $\pi\in S_{n}$ satisfies [the condition that $\operatorname{cyc}(\sigma)=2$ for every $\sigma\in\pi C_{n}$], then $n$ is prime and [\pi = \pi_{n,a}$] for some $a\in{\mathbb{Z}}_{n}^{\times}$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 3.6, which asserts that any permutation π satisfying cyc(σ)=2 for every σ∈πC_n must have n prime and be of the affine form π_{n,a}, is disproved by Bastide, Bishnoi, Groenland, Gijswijt, and Joshi (arXiv:2510.18529). The smallest counterexample occurs at n=23: a specific non-affine permutation satisfies t([π])=21=n−2 yet is not of the form π_{n,a}; all counterexamples found have the structure of quadratic orthomorphisms. The implied Conjecture 3.4 (t(n)=n−2 iff n is prime) remains open but is further supported.
rows.defer_reason: Permutation combinatorics, outside graph-theory scope.
rows.class_notes: Disproved structural strengthening of Conjecture 3.4; statement text also garbled.

rows.title: Question 3.9
rows.paper_title: Circular sorting
rows.statement_text: Is the function $t(n)$ monotone?
rows.context_text: The function $t(n)$ gives the diameter of the all-swaps graph on cyclic permutations of order $n$. Monotonicity would allow the bounds proved for prime powers to be transferred to composite integers; however, the authors note that adding a fixed point to a permutation can decrease its individual $t$-value, complicating a naive monotonicity argument.
rows.source_text: Is the function $t(n)$ monotone?
rows.defer_reason: Monotonicity of a permutation-sorting diameter function, outside graph-theory scope.

rows.title: Question 1.1
rows.paper_title: On graph classes with constant domination-packing ratio
rows.statement_text: Given a graph class $\mathcal{G}$, is there a constant $c_{\mathcal{G}}$ such that $$\frac{\gamma(G)}{\rho(G)}\leqslant c_{\mathcal{G}}\quad\text{ for each }\quad G\in\mathcal{G}~{}~{}?$$
rows.context_text: The paper studies the ratio between the dominating number $\gamma(G)$ and the packing number $\rho(G)$ of a graph $G$. A positive answer immediately gives the bound $c_{\mathcal{G}}$ on the integrality gap of both the dominating and the packing integer programs for any $G\in\mathcal{G}$.
rows.source_text: Given a graph class $\mathcal{G}$, is there a constant $c_{\mathcal{G}}$ such that $$\frac{\gamma(G)}{\rho(G)}\leqslant c_{\mathcal{G}}\quad\text{ for each }\quad G\in\mathcal{G}~{}~{}?$$
rows.status_semantics: Question 1.1 asks for which graph classes the domination-packing ratio $\gamma(G)/\rho(G)$ is bounded by a class-specific constant. The source paper itself gives positive answers for 2-degenerate, AT-free, and unit-disk graphs, with improved bounds for planar graphs and graphs of bounded treewidth or twin-width. A 2026 follow-up (arXiv:2602.18402) extends the positive answers further to chordal bipartite graphs and homogeneously orderable graphs, and provides an improved bound for planar graphs and a direct proof for trees. The general question — whether every natural graph class admits such a constant — remains open.
rows.defer_reason: 'Given a graph class, is there a constant' — parameterized meta-question over all classes.
rows.class_notes: Question schema quantified over an arbitrary graph class G, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Question 9.1
rows.paper_title: On graph classes with constant domination-packing ratio
rows.statement_text: Characterize those graph classes $\mathcal{G}$ for which there is a constant $c_{\mathcal{G}}$ such that $\dfrac{\gamma(G)}{\rho(G)}\leqslant c_{\mathcal{G}}$ for each $G\in\mathcal{G}$.
rows.context_text: In the conclusion, after extending both the list of graph classes with bounded $\gamma/\rho$ ratio and those with unbounded ratio (notably showing 2-degenerate graphs have bounded ratio), the authors note that Theorem 6.1 on 2-degenerate graphs rules out any characterization purely in terms of sparse graph classes.
rows.source_text: Characterize those graph classes $\mathcal{G}$ for which there is a constant $c_{\mathcal{G}}$ such that $\dfrac{\gamma(G)}{\rho(G)}\leqslant c_{\mathcal{G}}$ for each $G\in\mathcal{G}$.
rows.status_semantics: The full characterization of graph classes with bounded domination-packing ratio γ(G)/ρ(G) remains open. A follow-up paper (arXiv:2602.18402, Dúcz–Gujgiczer 2026) extends the list of classes with bounded ratio to chordal bipartite graphs and homogeneously orderable graphs (both with constant 2), and improves the planar bound from 10 to 7, but does not provide a characterization. The question of whether any structural characterization exists — especially in light of the 2-degenerate result ruling out purely sparse characterizations — is still unresolved.
rows.defer_reason: Explicit 'characterize those graph classes' request.
rows.class_notes: Grand-characterization form of Question 1.1 from the same paper.

rows.title: Conjecture 2
rows.paper_title: Vertex Partitioning and $p$-Energy of Graphs
rows.statement_text: For every connected graph $G$ of order $n$ and $p\geq 2$, $$\mathcal{E}^{-}_{p}(G)\geq\mathcal{E}^{-}_{p}(P_{n}).$$
rows.context_text: The paper's authors pose this as the natural negative $p$-energy analogue of Conjecture 1 of Tang, Liu, and Wang. Together, Conjectures 1 and 2 generalize Conjecture 3 of Elphick, Farber, Goldberg, and Wocjan, since at $p=2$ both give the bound $n-1$ by symmetry of the path spectrum. The paper settles this conjecture for $p\geq 4$ via Theorem 1.
rows.source_text: For every connected graph $G$ of order $n$ and $p\geq 2$, $$\mathcal{E}^{-}_{p}(G)\geq\mathcal{E}^{-}_{p}(P_{n}).$$
rows.status_semantics: The source paper itself settles the case $p \geq 4$ (Theorem 1), so the conjecture was already partially resolved upon publication. A subsequent paper by Chen, Wang, and Zhang (arXiv:2604.15656, April 2026) extends this to all integers $p \geq 3$ (Corollary 1.7), improving Theorem 1 of the source paper. The case $p = 2$ remains open. Note: the verified HTML of arXiv:2503.16882 shows that Conjecture 2 actually compares to the complete graph $K_n$ (i.e., $\mathcal{E}^{-}_{p}(G) \geq \mathcal{E}^{-}_{p}(K_n)$), not to $P_n$ as stated in the input; the two bounds coincide at $p = 2$ since $\mathcal{E}^{-}_{2}(P_n) = \mathcal{E}^{-}_{2}(K_n) = n-1$.
rows.class_notes: Negative p-energy is standard recent spectral vocabulary though not defined in the record; needs reals/eigenvalues.

rows.title: Question 1.1
rows.paper_title: Orientations of cycles in digraphs of high chromatic number and high minimum out-degree
rows.statement_text: For which $\varepsilon>0$ and $k>0$ does there exist a bound $c$ such that each simple graph $G$ either contains $C_{2k+1}$ as a subgraph or $\chi(G)\leq c$ (or both)?
rows.context_text: Erdős and Simonovits (1972) asked whether every graph with no fixed odd cycle and large minimum degree has bounded chromatic number, specifically for triangles and $C_5$. The paper authors present Question 1.1 as a generalization of those questions. Thomassen subsequently answered it affirmatively for $C_3$ at $\varepsilon>1/3$ and for all longer odd cycles at all $\varepsilon>0$.
rows.source_text: For which $\varepsilon>0$ and $k>0$ does there exist a bound $c$ such that each simple graph $G$ either contains $C_{2k+1}$ as a subgraph or $\chi(G)\leq c$ (or both)?
rows.status_semantics: Question 1.1 is the chromatic-threshold problem for odd cycles: for which ε>0 and k>0 does every graph with minimum degree at least ε|V(G)| that contains no C_{2k+1} have bounded chromatic number? This is fully resolved by pre-existing literature cited in the source paper itself. Thomassen proved the answer affirmative for k≥2 at all ε>0 and for k=1 at all ε>1/3; Erdős–Simonovits–Hajnal showed no such bound exists for k=1 when ε<1/3; and Allen, Böttcher, Griffiths, Kohayakawa, and Morris completely resolved the chromatic-threshold problem for all fixed graphs. The question is presented as known background motivation for the paper's novel digraph generalization.
rows.defer_reason: 'For which epsilon and k' — parameter-characterization question, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Statement is also garbled: epsilon is quantified but never used (min-degree hypothesis missing).

rows.title: Question 1.2
rows.paper_title: Orientations of cycles in digraphs of high chromatic number and high minimum out-degree
rows.statement_text: Which orientations of cycles are contained in every digraph with high minimum out-degree and high chromatic number?
rows.context_text: This is the central question of the paper, investigating a directed analogue of the chromatic threshold for cycles. The authors consider digraphs $D$ without loops or parallel arcs with minimum out-degree at least $\varepsilon|V(D)|$ for fixed $\varepsilon>0$. Theorem 1.3 in this paper fully answers Question 1.2.
rows.source_text: Which orientations of cycles are contained in every digraph with high minimum out-degree and high chromatic number?
rows.status_semantics: Question 1.2 is fully answered within the source paper itself by Theorem 1.3, which characterizes all orientations of cycles $C$ that must appear as subdigraphs in every digraph $D$ (without loops or parallel arcs) with $\chi(D) \geq c$ and minimum out-degree at least $\varepsilon|V(D)|$ for fixed $\varepsilon > 0$, generalizing a result of Thomassen. The paper has been peer-reviewed and published in Combinatorica 46 (2026).
rows.defer_reason: 'Which orientations of cycles' — characterization request.
rows.class_notes: Solved by Theorem 1.3 of the paper, but still a 'which orientations' characterization ask.

rows.title: Question 4.1
rows.paper_title: Orientations of cycles in digraphs of high chromatic number and high minimum out-degree
rows.statement_text: For which fixed digraphs $H$ does there exist a constant $c_{\varepsilon}\geq 1$ for all $\varepsilon>0$ such that every digraph $D$ without loops or parallel arcs with $\chi(D)\geq c_{\varepsilon}$ and minimum out-degree at least $\varepsilon|V(D)|$ contains $H$ as a subdigraph?
rows.context_text: This is a more general version of Question 1.2, replacing cycle orientations by arbitrary fixed digraphs $H$. Theorem 1.3 characterizes the orientations of cycles for which this question is answered affirmatively. The authors also note that all subdigraphs of directed complete bipartite digraphs (with uniform arc orientation between parts) give an affirmative answer via a Ramsey-type argument.
rows.source_text: For which fixed digraphs $H$ does there exist a constant $c_{\varepsilon}\geq 1$ for all $\varepsilon>0$ such that every digraph $D$ without loops or parallel arcs with $\chi(D)\geq c_{\varepsilon}$ and minimum out-degree at least $\varepsilon|V(D)|$ contains $H$ as a subdigraph?
rows.defer_reason: 'For which fixed digraphs H' — characterization request.

rows.title: Question 4.2
rows.paper_title: Orientations of cycles in digraphs of high chromatic number and high minimum out-degree
rows.statement_text: For a fixed digraph $H$, what is the minimum value $\varepsilon_{H}\geq 0$ such that there exists a constant $c\geq 1$ such that every digraph $D$ without loops or parallel arcs with $\chi(D)\geq c$ and minimum out-degree at least $\varepsilon_{H}|V(D)|$ contains $H$ as a subdigraph?
rows.context_text: The quantity $\varepsilon_H$ provides a directed analogue of the chromatic threshold studied for undirected graphs. Theorem 1.3 may be interpreted as characterizing the orientations of cycles for which the directed chromatic threshold $\varepsilon_H$ equals zero. The authors also suggest studying variants where high chromatic number is replaced by high dichromatic number.
rows.source_text: For a fixed digraph $H$, what is the minimum value $\varepsilon_{H}\geq 0$ such that there exists a constant $c\geq 1$ such that every digraph $D$ without loops or parallel arcs with $\chi(D)\geq c$ and minimum out-degree at least $\varepsilon_{H}|V(D)|$ contains $H$ as a subdigraph?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the minimum value' — determine-a-quantity question.
rows.class_notes: Directed chromatic threshold epsilon_H.

rows.title: Question 5.1
rows.paper_title: Two-block paths in oriented graphs of large semidegree
rows.statement_text: Is it true that every oriented graph $G$ with $\delta^{0}(G)\geq k/2$ contains every orientation of the $k$-edge path, save for the antidirected orientations?
rows.context_text: The tightness of Stein's conjecture for the antidirected orientation is witnessed by the $k/2$-blowup of the directed triangle. More generally, a regular tournament $T$ on $k+1$ vertices has $\delta^{0}(T)=k/2$ and, by Havet–Thomassé, contains every oriented $k$-arc path except (with three small exceptions) the antidirected one, suggesting that antidirected paths may be the only obstruction at the threshold $\delta^{0}(G)=k/2$.
rows.source_text: Is it true that every oriented graph $G$ with $\delta^{0}(G)\geq k/2$ contains every orientation of the $k$-edge path, save for the antidirected orientations?
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no: semidegree k/2 forces all non-antidirected orientations of the k-edge path.

rows.title: Question 1 (Intersectionwise χ-guarding)
rows.paper_title: Intersections of graphs and $χ$-boundedness
rows.statement_text: Which graph classes have the property that their graph-intersection with every $\chi$-bounded class of graphs is $\chi$-bounded?
rows.context_text: The authors introduce the term "intersectionwise $\chi$-guarding" for such classes. They prove that classes admitting a certain decomposition are intersectionwise $\chi$-guarding, characterize single-forbidden-induced-subgraph intersectionwise $\chi$-guarding classes, and provide necessary conditions on $\mathcal{H}$ for the class of $\mathcal{H}$-free graphs to be intersectionwise $\chi$-guarding.
rows.source_text: Which graph classes have the property that their graph-intersection with every $\chi$-bounded class of graphs is $\chi$-bounded?
rows.defer_reason: 'Which graph classes have the property' — characterization request over all classes.

rows.title: Problem 1.4
rows.paper_title: Defective coloring of blowups
rows.statement_text: Determine the smallest value of $c$ for which the inequality $\chi(G)\leq c\cdot\chi^{d}(G\boxtimes K_{d+1})$ always holds.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.2 (for $d=2$) provides a graph showing that $c\geq\frac{30}{29}$ is necessary, while Theorem 1.3 gives the upper bound $c\leq 2$. A bootstrapped version of Theorem 1.2 further shows that assuming $\chi(G)$ or $d$ to be large cannot reduce the constant factor arbitrarily close to $1$.
rows.source_text: Determine the smallest value of $c$ for which the inequality $\chi(G)\leq c\cdot\chi^{d}(G\boxtimes K_{d+1})$ always holds.
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the smallest value of c' — optimize-a-constant request.
rows.class_notes: Known window 30/29 <= c <= 2; a specific-value guess would be B2 but none is stated.

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: A note on extendable sets of colorings and rooted minors
rows.statement_text: For every integer $k\geq 3$, there exists a set $X$ of vertices and a set $C$ of $k$-colorings of $X$ such that no $X$-rooted-$K_{k}$-minor-free graph $G$ realizes $C$.
rows.context_text: The conjecture asserts that Theorem 3 (the generalization of the DeVos–Seymour result to $X$-rooted-$K_{k+1}$-minor-free $K_{k+2}$-minor-free graphs) does not further extend to $X$-rooted-$K_{k}$-minor-free graphs, i.e., reducible configurations cannot exploit the full global structure when the number of colors falls below what Hadwiger's conjecture postulates. Holroyd's conjecture — that if $G$ is $k$-colorable and every $k$-coloring uses all $k$ colors on $X$ then $K_k$ is an $X$-rooted minor of $G$ — would imply this conjecture if true in general.
rows.source_text: For every integer $k\geq 3$, there exists a set $X$ of vertices and a set $C$ of $k$-colorings of $X$ such that no $X$-rooted-$K_{k}$-minor-free graph $G$ realizes $C$.
rows.class_notes: X-rooted minors and 'realizes a set of colorings' are paper-local and not fully defined in the record.

rows.title: Question 1.4
rows.paper_title: $χ$-Boundedness and Neighbourhood Complexity of Bounded Merge-Width Graphs
rows.statement_text: Is the class $\{G:\operatorname{mw}_{1}(G)\leqslant k\}$, with $k$ arbitrary, $\chi$-bounded?
rows.context_text: The proof of $\chi$-boundedness (Theorem 1.2) uses merge-width at radius 2 in an essential way (specifically Lemma 4.1). It is unclear whether radius 1 suffices. A negative answer would require constructing a class of bounded radius-1 merge-width graphs with bounded clique number but unbounded chromatic number; such a class must contain arbitrarily large bicliques.
rows.source_text: Is the class $\{G:\operatorname{mw}_{1}(G)\leqslant k\}$, with $k$ arbitrary, $\chi$-bounded?
rows.class_notes: B2-vs-B3 resolved to B3: radius-1 merge-width is a brand-new (2025, this paper) parameter, not standard vocabulary and not defined in the record; buildable finitely once the definition is recovered.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.7
rows.paper_title: $χ$-Boundedness and Neighbourhood Complexity of Bounded Merge-Width Graphs
rows.statement_text: A class $\mathcal{C}$ has bounded merge-width if and only if every first-order transduction of $\mathcal{C}$ has linear neighbourhood complexity.
rows.context_text: Reidl, Villaamil, and Stavropoulos showed that linear neighbourhood complexity characterises bounded expansion among subgraph-closed classes; Theorem 1.5 of the present paper establishes the left-to-right direction for bounded merge-width. Proving this conjecture would also imply that flip-width and merge-width are equivalent, as conjectured by Dreier and Toruńczyk [DT25, Conjecture 1.17].
rows.source_text: A class $\mathcal{C}$ has bounded merge-width if and only if every first-order transduction of $\mathcal{C}$ has linear neighbourhood complexity.
rows.class_notes: Needs merge-width, FO transductions, and neighbourhood complexity, none defined in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 1
rows.paper_title: On a conjecture concerning 4-coloring of graphs with one crossing
rows.statement_text: Every 5-critical graph in ${\cal C}$ contains a vertex of degree four.
rows.context_text: The authors observe that 5-critical graphs have minimum degree at least four and that there is a natural way to handle degree-four vertices: if $G\neq K_5$ is 5-critical and $v$ has degree four, one can find non-adjacent neighbours $x,y$ of $v$ and contract them. Conjecture 1 is stated as a stepping stone toward the main Conjecture 2, which implies it because 5-critical graphs contain no separating cliques.
rows.source_text: Every 5-critical graph in ${\cal C}$ contains a vertex of degree four.
rows.class_notes: The class C (graphs with a one-crossing drawing per the paper) is not defined in this record.

rows.title: Conjecture 2
rows.paper_title: On a conjecture concerning 4-coloring of graphs with one crossing
rows.statement_text: If a graph $G$ does not contain separating triangles and has a drawing in the plane with one crossing such that all vertices not incident with the crossed edges have degree at least five, then $G$ is 4-colorable.
rows.context_text: This is the central conjecture of the paper. The authors verify it by computer enumeration for all graphs with at most 28 vertices, prove that it implies Conjecture 1, and note that the assumption on separating triangles is necessary. It generalises the Four Color Theorem to graphs drawable with one crossing under a minimum-degree condition.
rows.source_text: If a graph $G$ does not contain separating triangles and has a drawing in the plane with one crossing such that all vertices not incident with the crossed edges have degree at least five, then $G$ is 4-colorable.
rows.class_notes: Self-contained one-crossing 4-colorability conjecture generalizing 4CT; needs drawing/embedding layer.

rows.title: Conjecture 5
rows.paper_title: On a conjecture concerning 4-coloring of graphs with one crossing
rows.statement_text: Every non-4-colorable graph in ${\cal C}_{0}$ has an internal vertex of degree four.
rows.context_text: The class ${\cal C}_{0}\subseteq{\cal C}$ consists of graphs obtained from 4-connected plane triangulations by choosing two distinct facial triangles $xuv$ and $yuv$ sharing edge $uv$ and adding edge $xy$; vertices $x,y,u,v$ are external and all others internal. This conjecture is equivalent to Conjecture 2 (the main conjecture) and provides a more combinatorially explicit reformulation analogous to how the Four Color Theorem reduces to triangulations.
rows.source_text: Every non-4-colorable graph in ${\cal C}_{0}$ has an internal vertex of degree four.
rows.class_notes: Context fully defines the class C_0 (4-connected plane triangulations plus edge xy); equivalent to Conjecture 2 of same paper.

rows.title: Conjecture 30
rows.paper_title: On a conjecture concerning 4-coloring of graphs with one crossing
rows.statement_text: Every bichromatic-forbidding 4-candidate can be obtained from the diamond by a sequence of the operations described in Lemmas 27, 28, and 29.
rows.context_text: A plane graph with outer face bounded by a 4-cycle is bichromatic-forbidding if no 4-colouring assigns the same colour to opposite pairs of the bounding 4-cycle. A 4-candidate is a specific class of such graphs arising in the structural analysis of non-4-colourable graphs in $\mathcal{C}$. The conjecture proposes a complete recursive generation scheme for this class via three explicit expansion operations.
rows.source_text: Every bichromatic-forbidding 4-candidate can be obtained from the diamond by a sequence of the operations described in Lemmas 27, 28, and 29.
rows.class_notes: Generation operations are defined only via internal Lemmas 27-29; '4-candidate' only partially defined in context.

rows.title: Question 4.1
rows.paper_title: A Counterexample to a Conjecture of Lovász
rows.statement_text: Do there exist infinitely many $3$-edge colourable cubic graphs $G$ such that for all edges $(u,v),(x,y)\in E(G)$, [remainder of condition not captured in source]
rows.context_text: The authors verified computationally that the Biggs-Smith graph is the unique cubic edge-transitive graph on at most 166 vertices satisfying the key lemma's conclusion, and that F168D is the only other such graph on at most 300 vertices. This question asks whether infinitely many such graphs exist.
rows.source_text: Do there exist infinitely many $3$-edge colourable cubic graphs $G$ such that for all edges $(u,v),(x,y)\in E(G)$, [remainder of condition not captured in source]
rows.status_semantics: Question 4.1 asks whether infinitely many 3-edge-colourable cubic graphs exist such that for every pair of edges $(u,v),(x,y)\in E(G)$ the key lemma condition holds (i.e. the line hypergraph of $G$ is a counterexample to the Lovász conjecture for $r=3$). Abiad, Garbe, Povill, and Spiegel (arXiv:2506.21286, June 2025) answer this affirmatively by constructing a simple infinite family of such counterexamples based on generalized Petersen graphs, and additionally provide specific counterexamples for $r=4$.
rows.class_notes: Statement truncated ('[remainder of condition not captured]'); a question about Lovász-counterexample graphs, not a restatement of Ryser's conjecture.

rows.title: Question 4.2
rows.paper_title: A Counterexample to a Conjecture of Lovász
rows.statement_text: Does there exist a $d$-regular $d$-edge colourable graph $G$ where $d\geq 4$ such that for all edges $(u_{1},v_{1}),\dots,(u_{d-1},v_{d-1})\in E(G)$, [remainder of condition not captured in source]
rows.context_text: After finding only two counterexamples to Lovász's conjecture among line hypergraphs of cubic graphs, the authors ask whether counterexamples exist for higher regularity $d\geq 4$, generalising the $r=3$ construction.
rows.source_text: Does there exist a $d$-regular $d$-edge colourable graph $G$ where $d\geq 4$ such that for all edges $(u_{1},v_{1}),\dots,(u_{d-1},v_{d-1})\in E(G)$, [remainder of condition not captured in source]
rows.status_semantics: Question 4.2 from arXiv:2505.05339 asks whether a d-regular d-edge colourable graph witnessing a counterexample to Lovász's conjecture exists for d≥4. Abiad, Garbe, Povill, and Spiegel (arXiv:2506.21286) answer this affirmatively: they prove that there exist quartic (4-regular, 4-edge-colourable) graphs whose line hypergraphs are counterexamples to Lovász's conjecture for r=4, and they explicitly cite Question 4.2 from [CHM2025] as the question being resolved.
rows.class_notes: Statement truncated mid-condition; asks for counterexamples to Lovász's conjecture at regularity d>=4, distinct from Ryser's conjecture itself.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.3
rows.paper_title: A Counterexample to a Conjecture of Lovász
rows.statement_text: For every $r$-partite $r$-uniform hypergraph $\mathcal{H}$ there exists some $k$ with $1\leq k\leq r-1$ such that $\mathcal{H}$ contains $k(r-1)$ vertices whose deletion reduces the matching number by at least $k$.
rows.context_text: Proposed as a weakening of Lovász's (now disproved) conjecture that still implies Ryser's conjecture. The authors verified it holds for the line hypergraph of the Biggs-Smith graph, and note that only two counterexamples to Lovász's original conjecture are known, suggesting a slightly weaker statement may be true.
rows.source_text: For every $r$-partite $r$-uniform hypergraph $\mathcal{H}$ there exists some $k$ with $1\leq k\leq r-1$ such that $\mathcal{H}$ contains $k(r-1)$ vertices whose deletion reduces the matching number by at least $k$.
rows.class_notes: Weakening of Lovász's disproved conjecture that still implies Ryser's conjecture — strengthening-variant edge to rysers_conjecture.

rows.title: Question 4.4
rows.paper_title: A Counterexample to a Conjecture of Lovász
rows.statement_text: Is it true that if $\mathcal{L}$ is the line hypergraph of an $r$-regular $r$-edge colourable Cayley graph, then $\mathcal{L}$ contains $r-1$ vertices whose deletion reduces its matching number?
rows.context_text: Both known counterexamples to Lovász's conjecture (the Biggs-Smith graph and F168D) are non-Cayley graphs. The authors observe that while symmetry aids in identifying counterexamples, it is unclear why not being a Cayley graph is relevant, motivating this restricted version of Lovász's conjecture.
rows.source_text: Is it true that if $\mathcal{L}$ is the line hypergraph of an $r$-regular $r$-edge colourable Cayley graph, then $\mathcal{L}$ contains $r-1$ vertices whose deletion reduces its matching number?
rows.status_semantics: Question 4.4 asks whether the line hypergraph of every $r$-regular $r$-edge-colourable Cayley graph must contain $r-1$ vertices whose deletion reduces its matching number. This was answered negatively by Abiad, Garbe, Povill, and Spiegel (arXiv:2506.21286, June 2025), who found a cubic Cayley graph on 36 vertices and a quartic Cayley graph on 96 vertices whose line hypergraphs are counterexamples to Lovász's conjecture, thus disproving the conjecture even in the Cayley graph setting.
rows.class_notes: Cayley-graph-restricted special case of Lovász's conjecture (disproved); needs Cayley graph + line hypergraph primitives.

rows.title: Open Problem: polytime $f(\mathrm{OPT})$-approximation for largest complete minor
rows.paper_title: A Polynomial-Time Approximation Algorithm for Complete Interval Minors
rows.statement_text: It is open whether finding the size of a largest complete minor admits a polynomial-time $f(\mathrm{OPT})$-approximation algorithm.
rows.context_text: The best known polynomial-time approximation factor for the size of a largest complete minor is $O(\sqrt{n})$, due to Alon, Lingas and Wahlén [1]. No PTAS exists unless $\mathsf{P}=\mathsf{NP}$ (Wahlén [32]). The authors cite this open problem as direct motivation for studying the analogous question for complete interval minors in ordered graphs, where they obtain a polytime $f(t)$-approximation.
rows.source_text: It is open whether finding the size of a largest complete minor admits a polynomial-time $f(\mathrm{OPT})$-approximation algorithm.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2505.05997 — Open Problem: polytime $f(\mathrm{OPT})$-approximation for largest complete minor
rows.class_notes: Algorithmic yes/no question (polytime f(OPT)-approximation for largest clique minor); needs complexity foundation.

rows.title: Question 1.7
rows.paper_title: Halfway to induced saturation for even cycles
rows.statement_text: Let $t\geq 6$ be an integer. Does there exist a graph $G$ with $E(\overline{G})\neq\varnothing$ such that $G$ is $H$-free but $G+e$ has an induced subgraph isomorphic to $C_{2t-2}$ for every $e\in E(\overline{G})$?
rows.context_text: The authors prove Theorem 1.6 constructing graphs that are $C_{2t}$-free but where removing any edge creates an induced copy of $C_{2t}$ (half of the induced saturation condition). Question 1.7 asks about the other half: whether the edge-addition condition can also be satisfied, which would be a key step toward solving the induced saturation problem for all even cycles.
rows.source_text: Let $t\geq 6$ be an integer. Does there exist a graph $G$ with $E(\overline{G})\neq\varnothing$ such that $G$ is $H$-free but $G+e$ has an induced subgraph isomorphic to $C_{2t-2}$ for every $e\in E(\overline{G})$?
rows.class_notes: Statement garbled: '$H$-free' with H undefined in the record (should be an even cycle per context).

rows.title: Question 1.8
rows.paper_title: Halfway to induced saturation for even cycles
rows.statement_text: Does there exist a polynomial $f$ such that for every $t\geq 3$, there is a graph $G$ on at most $f(t)$ vertices with $E(G)\neq\varnothing$, such that $G$ is $H$-free but $G-e$ has an induced subgraph isomorphic to $C_{2t-2}$ for every $e\in E(G)$?
rows.context_text: The construction of $G_t$ in Theorem 1.6 has doubly exponential size in $t$. The authors remark they do not even have a rationale for expecting the number of vertices to be more than linear in $t$, making this a natural question about the efficiency of the construction.
rows.source_text: Does there exist a polynomial $f$ such that for every $t\geq 3$, there is a graph $G$ on at most $f(t)$ vertices with $E(G)\neq\varnothing$, such that $G$ is $H$-free but $G-e$ has an induced subgraph isomorphic to $C_{2t-2}$ for every $e\in E(G)$?
rows.class_notes: Same '$H$-free' undefined-H garbling as Question 1.7; otherwise a clean polynomial-size question.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.2
rows.paper_title: Refinement of a conjecture on positive square energy of graphs
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a connected graph of order $n$ and size $m$. If $m\geq n+1$, then $$s^{+}(G)\geq n.$$
rows.context_text: Motivated by the difficulty of proving Conjecture 1.1 inductively (because the bound $n-1$ is not preserved under subgraph removal), the authors ask when the strictly stronger bound $s^{+}(G)\geq n$ holds and propose this conjecture after computational investigation. It has been verified for all connected graphs up to 9 vertices and all graphs in the Mathematica database with $n\leq 100$ and $m\geq n+1$.
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a connected graph of order $n$ and size $m$. If $m\geq n+1$, then $$s^{+}(G)\geq n.$$
rows.class_notes: s^+ = sum of squares of positive adjacency eigenvalues (standard positive square energy); needs real eigenvalue vocabulary.

rows.title: Conjecture 9.1
rows.paper_title: Refinement of a conjecture on positive square energy of graphs
rows.statement_text: If $G$ is a unicyclic graph of order $n$ whose cycle has odd length $k$, then $s^{+}(G)>n>s^{-}(G)$, if $k\equiv 3\mod 4$. $s^{+}(G)<n<s^{-}(G)$, if $k\equiv 1\mod 4$.
rows.context_text: The authors compute $s^{+}$ and $s^{-}$ for odd cycles $C_n$ explicitly and observe a parity-of-$n$-mod-4 dependence. They extend this observation to all unicyclic graphs whose unique cycle has odd length and propose this conjecture.
rows.source_text: If $G$ is a unicyclic graph of order $n$ whose cycle has odd length $k$, then $s^{+}(G)>n>s^{-}(G)$, if $k\equiv 3\mod 4$. $s^{+}(G)<n<s^{-}(G)$, if $k\equiv 1\mod 4$.
rows.class_notes: Unicyclic parity-mod-4 dichotomy for s^+/s^-; real spectra needed.

rows.title: Conjecture 9.2
rows.paper_title: Refinement of a conjecture on positive square energy of graphs
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a connected graph of order $n$. $s^{+}(G)=n-1$ if and only if $G$ is a tree. $s^{-}(G)=n-1$ if and only if $G$ is a tree or a complete graph.
rows.context_text: After establishing Conjecture 1.1 for several families, the authors propose a characterisation of the equality case $s^{+}(G)=n-1$ (resp. $s^{-}(G)=n-1$) in Conjecture 1.1.
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a connected graph of order $n$. $s^{+}(G)=n-1$ if and only if $G$ is a tree. $s^{-}(G)=n-1$ if and only if $G$ is a tree or a complete graph.
rows.class_notes: Equality characterization s^+=n-1 iff tree; clean iff statement over real eigenvalues.

rows.title: Conjecture 9.3
rows.paper_title: Refinement of a conjecture on positive square energy of graphs
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a connected graph of order $n$ such that $s^{+}(G)=n$. Then $G$ is a bipartite unicyclic graph.
rows.context_text: The authors believe equality never holds in Conjecture 1.2 for non-unicyclic graphs, and propose this characterisation of the equality case $s^{+}(G)=n$ based on computational evidence and the structure of bipartite unicyclic graphs.
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a connected graph of order $n$ such that $s^{+}(G)=n$. Then $G$ is a bipartite unicyclic graph.

rows.title: Conjecture 9.4
rows.paper_title: Refinement of a conjecture on positive square energy of graphs
rows.statement_text: For any connected graph $G$ of order $n$ and $\omega(G)\geq 3$, we have $s^{+}(G)\geq n$.
rows.context_text: This conjecture refines Conjecture 1.1 for graphs with clique number at least 3, motivated by the expectation that a large clique forces a large value of $s^{+}$. It would follow from the truth of Conjectures 1.2 and 9.1; the authors identify unicyclic graphs with a triangle as the main obstacle.
rows.source_text: For any connected graph $G$ of order $n$ and $\omega(G)\geq 3$, we have $s^{+}(G)\geq n$.

rows.title: Conjecture 9.5
rows.paper_title: Refinement of a conjecture on positive square energy of graphs
rows.statement_text: The following hold: Let $G$ be a maximal planar graph of order $n\geq 10$. Then $s^{+}(G)\geq 3n$. Let $G$ be a maximal outerplanar graph of order $n\geq 8$. Then $s^{+}(G)\geq 2n$.
rows.context_text: Elphick and Linz noted that Conjecture 1.1 holds for maximal planar graphs and asked whether $s^{+}(G)\geq 3(n-2)$ for such graphs. The present authors propose the stronger bounds $3n$ and $2n$ for maximal planar and maximal outerplanar graphs respectively, showing the coefficients are asymptotically tight via explicit constructions.
rows.source_text: The following hold: Let $G$ be a maximal planar graph of order $n\geq 10$. Then $s^{+}(G)\geq 3n$. Let $G$ be a maximal outerplanar graph of order $n\geq 8$. Then $s^{+}(G)\geq 2n$.
rows.class_notes: Needs both planarity/outerplanarity layer and real eigenvalue vocabulary; planarity is the harder dependency.

rows.title: Problem 21
rows.paper_title: Infinite induced-saturated graphs
rows.statement_text: Is there an infinite family $\mathcal{G}$ of finite graphs, not containing cliques or independent sets, such that for all $H\in\mathcal{G}$, there is no finite $H$-induced-saturated graph?
rows.context_text: The paper establishes that every finite graph $H$ that is not a clique or independent set admits a countably infinite $H$-induced-saturated graph. In the finite setting the picture is far less clear: beyond $P_4$, cliques, and independent sets, no other finite graph $H$ is known to lack a finite $H$-induced-saturated graph, so it is open whether infinitely many such exceptions exist.
rows.source_text: Is there an infinite family $\mathcal{G}$ of finite graphs, not containing cliques or independent sets, such that for all $H\in\mathcal{G}$, there is no finite $H$-induced-saturated graph?
rows.class_notes: H-induced-saturated is standard saturation vocabulary; existence of an infinite family of finite exceptions is expressible finitely per H.

rows.title: Problem 22
rows.paper_title: Infinite induced-saturated graphs
rows.statement_text: For a finite graph $H$ and integer $n$, let $G_{H,n}$ denote the graph with the $H$-free graphs on vertex set $[n]$ as vertices, and with an edge between $G,G^{\prime}\in V(G_{H,n})$ if and only if $|E(G)\triangle E(G^{\prime})|=1$. For which $H$ is $G_{H,n}$ disconnected for all sufficiently large $n$?
rows.context_text: An $H$-induced-saturated graph on $n$ vertices corresponds to an isolated vertex in $G_{H,n}$; disconnectedness is a weaker but potentially more tractable property. The authors note that $G_{P_4,n}$ is connected for all $n\geq 1$, so disconnectedness is not universal.
rows.source_text: For a finite graph $H$ and integer $n$, let $G_{H,n}$ denote the graph with the $H$-free graphs on vertex set $[n]$ as vertices, and with an edge between $G,G^{\prime}\in V(G_{H,n})$ if and only if $|E(G)\triangle E(G^{\prime})|=1$. For which $H$ is $G_{H,n}$ disconnected for all sufficiently large $n$?
rows.defer_reason: 'For which H ...' characterization question, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Flip-graph G_{H,n} is fully defined in the statement.

rows.title: Problem 23
rows.paper_title: Infinite induced-saturated graphs
rows.statement_text: For which finite tournaments $H$, does there exist a countable tournament $G$ that does not contain $H$ as subtournament, yet any tournament obtained from $G$ by changing the direction of a single arc does contain $H$?
rows.context_text: The authors ask for a tournament analogue of induced saturation, where the natural perturbation operation is reversing arcs. They note that when $H$ is a directed triangle there is no finite $G$ but an infinite one exists (the rationals ordered by $<$), and that for the transitive tournament on 3 elements a finite example exists.
rows.source_text: For which finite tournaments $H$, does there exist a countable tournament $G$ that does not contain $H$ as subtournament, yet any tournament obtained from $G$ by changing the direction of a single arc does contain $H$?
rows.defer_reason: 'For which finite tournaments H ...' characterization question, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Conjecture 24
rows.paper_title: Infinite induced-saturated graphs
rows.statement_text: Let $T$ be a finite tournament. If $T$ is not transitive, then there exists a countably infinite $T$-free tournament $S_{T}$ such that every locally finite perturbation of $S_{T}$ has an induced copy of $T$.
rows.context_text: This is proposed as the tournament analogue of the paper's main Theorem 1. The non-transitivity condition is necessary because any tournament on $2^n$ elements contains a transitive subtournament on $n$ elements, so a transitive $T$ cannot be avoided in an infinite structure under locally finite perturbations.
rows.source_text: Let $T$ be a finite tournament. If $T$ is not transitive, then there exists a countably infinite $T$-free tournament $S_{T}$ such that every locally finite perturbation of $S_{T}$ has an induced copy of $T$.
rows.class_notes: Countably infinite tournament with locally-finite-perturbation saturation; digraph flavor but infinite structure dominates.

rows.title: Conjecture 25
rows.paper_title: Infinite induced-saturated graphs
rows.statement_text: Let $k\geq 3$ be an integer, and let $H$ be a finite $k$-uniform hypergraph. If $H$ has both an edge and a non-edge, then there exists a countably infinite $k$-uniform hypergraph $G_{H}$ such that every locally finite perturbation of $G_{H}$ has an induced copy of $H$.
rows.context_text: This is the strongest proposed extension of Theorem 1 to $k$-uniform hypergraphs, where a single perturbation consists of adding or removing a single hyperedge. The condition that $H$ has both an edge and a non-edge is the natural analogue of $H$ not being a clique or independent set.
rows.source_text: Let $k\geq 3$ be an integer, and let $H$ be a finite $k$-uniform hypergraph. If $H$ has both an edge and a non-edge, then there exists a countably infinite $k$-uniform hypergraph $G_{H}$ such that every locally finite perturbation of $G_{H}$ has an induced copy of $H$.
rows.class_notes: Countably infinite k-uniform hypergraph version of Theorem 1.

rows.title: Conjecture 26
rows.paper_title: Infinite induced-saturated graphs
rows.statement_text: Let $k\geq 2$ be an integer, and let $c$ be a $k$-colouring of the edges of a finite clique $K$ such that there is an edge of every colour. Then there is a colouring $\chi$ of the edges of the complete countably infinite graph such that every locally finite perturbation contains a copy of $K$ with colouring $c$.
rows.context_text: This is the proposed analogue of Theorem 1 for edge-coloured complete graphs, where a locally finite perturbation changes the colours on a nonempty locally finite subgraph. The condition that every colour appears in $c$ is the natural analogue of $H$ not being a clique or independent set.
rows.source_text: Let $k\geq 2$ be an integer, and let $c$ be a $k$-colouring of the edges of a finite clique $K$ such that there is an edge of every colour. Then there is a colouring $\chi$ of the edges of the complete countably infinite graph such that every locally finite perturbation contains a copy of $K$ with colouring $c$.
rows.class_notes: Edge-coloured countably infinite complete graph analogue of Theorem 1.

rows.title: Informal conjecture on sun-free $\chi$-boundedness without net exclusion
rows.paper_title: Suns in triangle-free graphs of large chromatic number
rows.statement_text: It could be true that the class of all sun-free graphs is already $\chi$-bounded.
rows.context_text: After stating Problem 1.4, the authors remark that they are not sure why the net needs to be excluded, suggesting that sun-freeness alone might suffice for $\chi$-boundedness without additionally requiring net-freeness.
rows.source_text: It could be true that the class of all sun-free graphs is already $\chi$-bounded.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2506.10227 — Informal conjecture on sun-free $\chi$-boundedness without net exclusion
rows.class_notes: 'Sun' graph family is paper-local and not defined in the record; proposition itself (sun-free class is chi-bounded) is otherwise standard.

rows.title: Problem 1.4
rows.paper_title: Extended VC-dimension, and Radon and Tverberg type theorems for unions of convex sets
rows.statement_text: Determine or estimate the least integer $f=f_{r}(d,s_{1},\ldots,s_{r})$ such that for any set $P$ of $f$ points in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ there is a partition into $r$ pairwise disjoint sets $P=\bigcup_{i=1}^{r}P_{i}$ such that for any family of sets $C_{1},\ldots,C_{r}$ with $P_{i}\subset C_{i}$ where $C_{i}$ is an $s_{i}$-convex set for every $i\in[r]$ we have that $\bigcap_{i=1}^{r}C_{i}\neq\emptyset$.
rows.context_text: This is a Tverberg-type generalization of Problem 1.3 to $r$ parts and $r$ families of $s_i$-convex sets. Radon's theorem is equivalent to $f(d,1,1)=d+2$ and Tverberg's theorem is equivalent to $f_{r}(d,1,\ldots,1)=(r-1)(d+1)+1$. The paper provides an upper bound via Theorem 1.6 but the exact determination remains open.
rows.source_text: Determine or estimate the least integer $f=f_{r}(d,s_{1},\ldots,s_{r})$ such that for any set $P$ of $f$ points in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ there is a partition into $r$ pairwise disjoint sets $P=\bigcup_{i=1}^{r}P_{i}$ such that for any family of sets $C_{1},\ldots,C_{r}$ with $P_{i}\subset C_{i}$ where $C_{i}$ is an $s_{i}$-convex set for every $i\in[r]$ we have that $\bigcap_{i=1}^{r}C_{i}\neq\emptyset$.
rows.status_semantics: Problem 1.4 asks to determine or estimate f_r(d,s_1,...,s_r), the minimum number of points guaranteeing a Tverberg-type partition with s_i-convex containers. The source paper supplies an upper bound via Theorem 1.6. A follow-up by Chen, Wang, Ge, Shu, and Xu (arXiv:2510.20770, October 2025) answers two related questions of Alon and Smorodinsky negatively, proving f_r(d,s,...,s) > s^r for all r >= 2, s >= 1, d >= 2r-2, disproving polynomial boundedness in s and nearly matching the upper bound up to a log s factor. The exact determination of f_r(d,s_1,...,s_r) remains open.
rows.defer_reason: Discrete geometry (Radon/Tverberg-type partition numbers for s-convex sets in R^d), not graph theory; also 'determine or estimate' framing.

rows.title: Conjecture 13
rows.paper_title: On Modular Edge Colourings of Graphs
rows.statement_text: Let $k$ be a positive integer. For every $0_{k}$-graph $G$ we have $\chi^{\prime}_{k}(G)\leq k+o(k)$.
rows.context_text: A $0_k$-graph is a graph whose relevant subgraph structure satisfies a $0\pmod{k}$ degree condition. If Conjecture 13 holds, the proof strategy of Theorem 2 can be adapted so that a maximal $0_k$-subgraph $H$ of any graph $G$ is coloured with $\leq k+o(k)$ colours and the remainder (which would then be $(2q(k)-2)$-degenerate) is coloured via Lemma 9, yielding an overall bound approaching $\chi^{\prime}_{k}(G)\leq k+o(k)$ for all graphs.
rows.source_text: Let $k$ be a positive integer. For every $0_{k}$-graph $G$ we have $\chi^{\prime}_{k}(G)\leq k+o(k)$.
rows.class_notes: Paper-local notions 0_k-graph and chi'_k only vaguely glossed in context; bound is k+o(k) asymptotic.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.4
rows.paper_title: Covering Complete Geometric Graphs by Monotone Paths
rows.statement_text: For some (possibly, for every) dense $n$-element point set $A$ in general position in the plane, covering the edge set of $K_{n}[A]$ requires a superlinear number of monotone paths.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.3 shows that $O(n^{3/2})$ monotone paths suffice to cover $K_n[A]$ for any $\alpha$-dense point set $A$. The authors suspect this upper bound is not tight and that a superlinear lower bound holds. This is contrasted with the worst-case arbitrary point sets, for which a quadratic number of monotone paths is required.
rows.source_text: For some (possibly, for every) dense $n$-element point set $A$ in general position in the plane, covering the edge set of $K_{n}[A]$ requires a superlinear number of monotone paths.
rows.defer_reason: out-of-scope-v2: combinatorial geometry — monotone (coordinate-dependent) path covers of complete geometric graphs on planar point sets.
rows.class_notes: Resolution: sided with A (discrete geometry is B5 per plan); B's B3 concerns (paper-local 'alpha-dense', hedged 'for some (possibly, for every)' quantifier) would apply anyway if scope ever expands.

rows.title: Problem 5.1
rows.paper_title: Covering Complete Geometric Graphs by Monotone Paths
rows.statement_text: Does there exist a constant $c>1/2$ with the property that, for infinitely many values of $n$, there are $n$-element point sets $A$ in the plane such that every covering of the edge set of $K_{n}[A]$ by crossing-free paths requires at least $cn$ paths?
rows.context_text: Pach, Saghafian, and Schnider proved that every complete convex geometric graph can be decomposed into $n/2 + o(n)$ plane subgraphs. It is open whether a linear lower bound strictly above $n/2$ holds for crossing-free path coverings over general point sets.
rows.source_text: Does there exist a constant $c>1/2$ with the property that, for infinitely many values of $n$, there are $n$-element point sets $A$ in the plane such that every covering of the edge set of $K_{n}[A]$ by crossing-free paths requires at least $cn$ paths?
rows.defer_reason: out-of-scope-v2: crossing-free path covers of straight-line-drawn K_n on planar point sets require real point-set geometry, not a combinatorial embedding layer.
