rows.class_notes: Resolution: sided with A; B is right that it is a clean exists-c yes/no, making it a good carve-out candidate if a real-plane geometry layer ever lands.

rows.title: Problem 5.2
rows.paper_title: Covering Complete Geometric Graphs by Monotone Paths
rows.statement_text: What is the smallest number $z=z(n)$ such that the edge set of every complete geometric graph on $n$ vertices can be covered by $z$ plane zig-zag paths?
rows.context_text: The authors show that for any finite point set $A$ in general position in the plane, one can cover the edge set of $K_n[A]$ by plane Hamiltonian zig-zag paths, but the minimum number of such paths needed in the worst case is left open.
rows.source_text: What is the smallest number $z=z(n)$ such that the edge set of every complete geometric graph on $n$ vertices can be covered by $z$ plane zig-zag paths?
rows.defer_reason: out-of-scope-v2: combinatorial geometry (plane zig-zag path covers of complete geometric graphs); additionally a determine-z(n) value question with a paper-local zig-zag notion (B's B4 point).
rows.class_notes: Resolution: A vs B was B5 vs B4 — out-of-scope dominates since a value-determination question about an out-of-scope object stays parked either way; both defects recorded in defer_reason.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.3
rows.paper_title: Improved Decomposition Bounds for Partition Polytopes and Odd-Covers
rows.statement_text: There exists a constant $c$ such that for any positive integers $n$ and $\kappa_1 \geq \cdots \geq \kappa_n$, the partition polytope $\mathrm{PP}(\kappa_1, \ldots, \kappa_n)$ has diameter at most $\lceil 4\kappa_1/3 \rceil + c$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.1 establishes an upper bound of $\lceil 3\kappa_1/2 \rceil$ on the diameter of $\mathrm{PP}(\kappa_1,\ldots,\kappa_n)$, while the known lower bound (generalizing a construction from [6]) is $\lceil 4\kappa_1/3 \rceil$. The authors suspect the true diameter is closer to the lower bound, motivating this conjecture. They also note that the natural proof strategy—showing diameter at most 4 when $\kappa_1 = 3$—fails, so new techniques will be required.
rows.source_text: There exists a constant $c$ such that for any positive integers $n$ and $\kappa_1 \geq \cdots \geq \kappa_n$, the partition polytope $\mathrm{PP}(\kappa_1, \ldots, \kappa_n)$ has diameter at most $\lceil 4\kappa_1/3 \rceil + c$.
rows.defer_reason: Polytope diameter bound, not graph/digraph/hypergraph theory.
rows.class_notes: Diameter of partition polytopes — polyhedral combinatorics/geometry rather than graph theory.

rows.title: Problem 5.2
rows.paper_title: Critical edge sets in vertex-critical graphs
rows.statement_text: Does there exist a $6$-regular $(4,1)$-graph?
rows.context_text: This question is posed in relation to the still-open case $k=4$ of Erdős' problem. A $(4,1)$-graph is a $4$-vertex-critical graph containing no critical edge; the question asks whether a $6$-regular example of such a graph exists, motivated by structural properties of $4$-vertex-critical graphs established in the paper.
rows.source_text: Does there exist a $6$-regular $(4,1)$-graph?
rows.class_notes: (4,1)-graph is defined in context (4-vertex-critical graph with no critical edge), so self-contained despite recovery flag.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.5
rows.paper_title: Distinct Directions and Distinct Distances in $\mathbb{R}^d$
rows.statement_text: For every fixed $d$ the following holds for all $d$-norms $\|\cdot\|$ but a meagre set. For all $n>n_{0}(d)$, any set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ that do not all lie in an affine hyperplane determine at least $(d-o(1))n$ distinct distances with respect to $\|\cdot\|$, where the $o(1)$-term tends to $0$ as $n$ tends to infinity.
rows.context_text: The conjecture concerns distinct distances in typical $d$-dimensional norms; the space of $d$-norms is a Baire space under the Hausdorff metric on unit balls, so 'all but a meagre set' is a strong genericity condition. The paper notes this is open already for $d=2$, as the result in [1] only ensures $(1-o(1))n$ distinct distances; the paper proves a weaker version (Theorem 1.6) by combining Theorem 1.2 with the arguments of [1].
rows.source_text: For every fixed $d$ the following holds for all $d$-norms $\|\cdot\|$ but a meagre set. For all $n>n_{0}(d)$, any set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ that do not all lie in an affine hyperplane determine at least $(d-o(1))n$ distinct distances with respect to $\|\cdot\|$, where the $o(1)$-term tends to $0$ as $n$ tends to infinity.
rows.defer_reason: Distinct distances in normed spaces — discrete/distance geometry, not graph theory.
rows.class_notes: Also needs Baire-category genericity over the space of d-norms.

rows.title: Open case: Coarse Menger Conjecture for c=2
rows.paper_title: Asymptotic structure. IV. A counterexample to the weak coarse Menger conjecture
rows.statement_text: It is unknown whether Conjecture 1.1 holds when $c = 2$: for all integers $k \geq 1$ there exists $\ell > 0$ such that for every graph $G$ and $S, T \subseteq V(G)$, either there are $k$ paths between $S, T$ pairwise at distance at least $2$, or there is a set $X \subseteq V(G)$ with $|X| \leq k-1$ such that every $S$-$T$ path contains a vertex within distance $\ell$ of some member of $X$.
rows.context_text: The authors note that the case $c = 3$ is of special interest because a positive result for $c = 3$ would imply one for all $c \geq 3$ (via taking the $c$-th power of $G$). They explicitly remark that 'the conjecture remains open when $c = 2$, and we have nothing to say about that case in this paper.'
rows.source_text: It is unknown whether Conjecture 1.1 holds when $c = 2$: for all integers $k \geq 1$ there exists $\ell > 0$ such that for every graph $G$ and $S, T \subseteq V(G)$, either there are $k$ paths between $S, T$ pairwise at distance at least $2$, or there is a set $X \subseteq V(G)$ with $|X| \leq k-1$ such that every $S$-$T$ path contains a vertex within distance $\ell$ of some member of $X$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2508.14332 — Open case: Coarse Menger Conjecture for c=2
rows.status_semantics: The Coarse Menger Conjecture for c=2 was explicitly left open in the source paper, which disproves the weak coarse Menger conjecture for c≥3. Subsequent work (arXiv:2509.08762) proves the coarse Menger conjecture for all graphs of bounded path-width, giving a partial positive result that applies to c=2 in that restricted class. A further paper (arXiv:2605.11112) establishes a coarse Menger theorem for planar and bounded-genus graphs. The c=2 case for general graphs remains open.
rows.class_notes: The c=2 case is fully spelled out in the statement despite the 'Informal' kind and recovery flag.

rows.title: Conjecture 15
rows.paper_title: Random Cayley graphs and random sumsets
rows.statement_text: There exist collections of sets $\mathcal{F}_{\ell}$ such that [size/complexity conditions] and such that for every $|A|=s$ with $|A+A|\leq K|A|$, there exists $\ell\leq\log_{2}K$ and $F\in\mathcal{F}_{\ell}$ such that $A+A\supseteq F$.
rows.context_text: The authors believe the main covering lemma — showing sumsets of sets with small doubling contain dense subsets of low complexity — will have further applications beyond those discussed in the paper. This conjecture proposes an optimal or strengthened form of that covering lemma, and is motivated in part by a version of Lovett's question (from Green's list of open problems [14]) asking whether sumsets $A+A$ of dense sets contain large subsets that are iterated sumsets $B+B+B+B$.
rows.source_text: There exist collections of sets $\mathcal{F}_{\ell}$ such that [size/complexity conditions] and such that for every $|A|=s$ with $|A+A|\leq K|A|$, there exists $\ell\leq\log_{2}K$ and $F\in\mathcal{F}_{\ell}$ such that $A+A\supseteq F$.
rows.defer_reason: Additive combinatorics (sumsets, small doubling), not graph theory.
rows.class_notes: Also truncated ('[size/complexity conditions]').

rows.title: Conjecture 1.2 (Coarse Menger conjecture for surfaces)
rows.paper_title: Asymptotic structure. VI. Distant paths across a disc
rows.statement_text:
Let $\mathcal{S}$ be a fixed surface. For all integers $k,c\geq 0$ there exists $\ell>0$ with the following property. Let $G$ be a graph that embeds on $\mathcal{S}$ and let $S,T\subseteq V(G)$. Then either

$\bullet$ there are $k+1$ paths between $S,T$, pairwise $c$-distant; or

$\bullet$ there is a set $X\subseteq V(G)$ with $|X|\leq k$ such that every path between $S,T$ contains a vertex with distance at most $\ell$ from some member of $X$.
rows.context_text: The original Coarse Menger conjecture (attributed to Albrechtsen, Huynh, Jacobs, Knappe and Wollan, and independently to Georgakopoulos and Papasoglu) was shown to be false for general graphs by the same authors (Nguyen–Scott–Seymour) in [11], with counterexamples that are highly non-planar (unbounded genus). Since the counterexamples rely on non-planarity, the authors propose that a surface-restricted version may still hold.
rows.source_text:
Let $\mathcal{S}$ be a fixed surface. For all integers $k,c\geq 0$ there exists $\ell>0$ with the following property. Let $G$ be a graph that embeds on $\mathcal{S}$ and let $S,T\subseteq V(G)$. Then either

$\bullet$ there are $k+1$ paths between $S,T$, pairwise $c$-distant; or

$\bullet$ there is a set $X\subseteq V(G)$ with $|X|\leq k$ such that every path between $S,T$ contains a vertex with distance at most $\ell$ from some member of $X$.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2509.07174 — Conjecture 1.2 (Coarse Menger conjecture for surfaces)
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.2 (the strong Coarse Menger conjecture for surfaces) remains open but has partial progress. Blažej, Pilipczuk, and Protopapas (arXiv:2605.11112, 2026) prove the weak form for graphs embeddable on any fixed surface: if fewer than k pairwise d-apart S-T paths exist, then there is a vertex set X with |X| ≤ f(k,d) such that every S-T path is within distance d of some vertex of X. This weak form allows |X| to grow with d, whereas Conjecture 1.2 requires the stronger bound |X| ≤ k independent of the distance parameter; the full conjecture remains open.
rows.class_notes: Fully stated; needs surface-embedding layer for the fixed-surface hypothesis.

rows.title: Informal conjecture: coarse Menger for bounded genus
rows.paper_title: Asymptotic structure. V. The coarse Menger conjecture in bounded path-width
rows.statement_text: It might be true that the coarse Menger conjecture holds for graphs of bounded genus.
rows.context_text: The counterexamples to the coarse Menger conjecture constructed in the authors' earlier paper have unbounded genus. The present paper proves the conjecture for bounded path-width; the authors note that the bounded-genus case is open and mention [13] for some progress in that direction.
rows.source_text: It might be true that the coarse Menger conjecture holds for graphs of bounded genus.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2509.08762 — Informal conjecture: coarse Menger for bounded genus
rows.status_semantics: The informal conjecture that the coarse Menger conjecture holds for graphs of bounded genus was confirmed in May 2026 by Blažej, Pilipczuk, and Protopapas (arXiv:2605.11112), which proves that for every surface Σ there is a function f(d,k) such that in any Σ-embeddable graph, the absence of k pairwise d-distant S–T paths implies a set of at most f(d,k) vertices covering all S–T paths within distance d. Independently, Liu (arXiv:2605.10068) establishes the weak coarse Menger property for all minor-closed families, which subsumes bounded-genus graphs.
rows.class_notes: References the coarse Menger conjecture without restating it; bounded-genus version essentially matches the fixed-surface Conjecture 1.2 of 2509.07174.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.2
rows.paper_title: Asymptotic structure. II. Path-width and additive quasi-isometry
rows.statement_text: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a class of connected graphs, closed under contracting edges and subdividing edges. For all $L,C$ there exists $C^{\prime}$ such that if there is an $(L,C)$-quasi-isometry from a graph $G$ to a member of $\mathcal{C}$, then there is a $(1,C^{\prime})$-quasi-isometry from $G$ to a member of $\mathcal{C}$.
rows.context_text: Motivated by a theorem of Chepoi, Dragan, Newman, Rabinovich, and Vaxès showing that $(L,C)$-quasi-isometry to a tree implies $(1,C')$-quasi-isometry to a tree, the authors ask for which graph classes $\mathcal{C}$ such a multiplicative-to-additive distortion improvement holds. Closure under edge subdivision and edge contraction is identified as a necessary condition, and the conjecture proposes it is also sufficient. The paper proves a special case: when $\mathcal{C}$ is the class of graphs of bounded path-width.
rows.source_text: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a class of connected graphs, closed under contracting edges and subdividing edges. For all $L,C$ there exists $C^{\prime}$ such that if there is an $(L,C)$-quasi-isometry from a graph $G$ to a member of $\mathcal{C}$, then there is a $(1,C^{\prime})$-quasi-isometry from $G$ to a member of $\mathcal{C}$.
rows.class_notes: Needs (L,C)-quasi-isometry primitive; the graph classes quantified over may include infinite graphs.

rows.title: Informal question: validity of Conjecture 1.2 for trees and planar graphs
rows.paper_title: Asymptotic structure. III. Excluding a fat tree
rows.statement_text: Is Conjecture 1.2 (Georgakopoulos–Papasoglu) true when $H$ is a tree, or more generally when $H$ is a planar graph?
rows.context_text: After noting that Conjecture 1.2 was disproved in general with a counterexample $H$ containing a clique of size 15, the authors remark that 'there remains hope that 1.2 is true for some interesting graphs $H$', and parenthetically ask '(Trees? Planar graphs?)'. The paper's own main result handles trees but with a weaker conclusion (bounded line-width rather than no $H$ minor), so the original formulation of Conjecture 1.2 for trees remains open.
rows.source_text: Is Conjecture 1.2 (Georgakopoulos–Papasoglu) true when $H$ is a tree, or more generally when $H$ is a planar graph?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2509.09035 — Informal question: validity of Conjecture 1.2 for trees and planar graphs
rows.class_notes: References the external Georgakopoulos-Papasoglu Conjecture 1.2 (fat-minor/coarse-minor), whose statement is not given in this record.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.3
rows.paper_title: On cuts of small chromatic number in sparse graphs
rows.statement_text: For every integer $k$ and every graph $G$ on at least $k$ vertices, if $$|E(G)|<k|V(G)|-\tfrac{k(k+1)}{2},$$ then $G$ admits a cut $X$ with $\chi(G[X])<k$. In particular, $\ell_{k}=k$.
rows.context_text: Motivated by the confirmed cases $\ell_1=1$, $\ell_2=2$ (Theorem 1.1), and the conjectured $\ell_3=3$ (Conjecture 1.2), the paper's authors propose this bold generalization asserting that $\ell_k=k$ for all $k$. The main purpose of the paper is then to show that this conjecture is far from correct.
rows.source_text: For every integer $k$ and every graph $G$ on at least $k$ vertices, if $$|E(G)|<k|V(G)|-\tfrac{k(k+1)}{2},$$ then $G$ admits a cut $X$ with $\chi(G[X])<k$. In particular, $\ell_{k}=k$.
rows.status_semantics: Conjecture 1.3 is disproved in the same paper that states it. The authors prove that the conjecture 'fails dramatically': whereas Conjecture 1.3 asserts ℓ_k = k for all k, the paper establishes that for every ε > 0 and all sufficiently large k, ℓ_k ≤ (1+ε)k/2, showing that the trivial lower bound ℓ_k ≥ k/2 is asymptotically tight. The paper is to appear in Discrete Mathematics. No subsequent follow-up papers addressing Conjecture 1.3 were found in the indexed literature.
rows.class_notes: Disproved; main inequality is self-contained reading 'cut' as vertex cut, drop the paper-local 'in particular l_k=k' clause.

rows.title: Conjecture 21
rows.paper_title: Short circuit walks in fixed dimension
rows.statement_text: Circuit Distance is $\mathsf{NP}$-hard for polygons.
rows.context_text: The authors focus on monotone circuit walks as most directly relevant to circuit augmentation schemes, but note the non-monotone variant is natural too. The Circuit Distance problem asks whether there is a circuit walk from $\mathbf{s}$ to $\mathbf{t}$ of length at most $k$ in a polytope $P=\{\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{n}\colon Ax\leq\mathbf{b}\}$. The authors remark that their proof techniques seem likely to extend to this non-monotone setting.
rows.source_text: Circuit Distance is $\mathsf{NP}$-hard for polygons.
rows.defer_reason: NP-hardness of circuit walks in polytopes/polygons: polyhedral geometry and LP complexity, not graph theory.

rows.title: Conjecture 6.1
rows.paper_title: Extending Thomassen's conjecture to directed graphs
rows.statement_text: All orientations of odd cycles are avoidable.
rows.context_text: In light of Theorem 1.1 (which proves all orientations of $C_3$ and $C_5$ are avoidable), the authors conjecture that the result extends to all odd cycles. A positive resolution would yield that every digraph of large minimum out-degree has a subdigraph with large minimum out-degree whose underlying graph has large odd girth, providing a directed analog to the existence of bipartite subgraphs of high minimum degree.
rows.source_text: All orientations of odd cycles are avoidable.
rows.class_notes: 'Avoidable' is a paper-local notion (min out-degree function d_F), only sketched, not defined in this record.

rows.title: Question 6.2
rows.paper_title: Extending Thomassen's conjecture to directed graphs
rows.statement_text: Are all digraphs which allow a height function not avoidable?
rows.context_text: All digraphs for which the authors proved non-avoidability (including one-directed complete bipartite graphs and oriented trees) allow a height function. This observation motivates asking whether the existence of a height function fully characterizes non-avoidable digraphs.
rows.source_text: Are all digraphs which allow a height function not avoidable?
rows.class_notes: Both 'height function' and 'avoidable' are paper-local and undefined in this record.

rows.title: Question 6.3
rows.paper_title: Extending Thomassen's conjecture to directed graphs
rows.statement_text: Which orientations of $C_{4}$ are avoidable?
rows.context_text: The simplest digraph allowing a height function for which avoidability is unknown is the orientation of $C_4$ consisting of two parallel directed paths of length two. The authors believe this is the hardest orientation of $C_4$ and expect its resolution to settle the question for all orientations.
rows.source_text: Which orientations of $C_{4}$ are avoidable?
rows.defer_reason: Open-ended 'which orientations are avoidable' characterization question, not a single proposition; also relies on paper-local 'avoidable'.
rows.class_notes: Only finitely many orientations of C4; the key open instance (two parallel directed 2-paths) could be a B2 extract.

rows.title: Eulerian-avoidability characterization
rows.paper_title: Extending Thomassen's conjecture to directed graphs
rows.statement_text: Which digraphs are Eulerian-avoidable?
rows.context_text: A digraph $F$ is Eulerian-avoidable if there exists $d_F:\mathbb{N}\rightarrow\mathbb{N}$ such that every Eulerian digraph (where each vertex has equal out- and in-degree) with minimum out-degree at least $d_F(k)$ contains an $F$-free subdigraph with minimum out-degree at least $k$. This setting is highlighted as potentially yielding new insights complementing the regular digraph characterization proved in the paper.
rows.source_text: Which digraphs are Eulerian-avoidable?
rows.defer_reason: 'Which digraphs are Eulerian-avoidable?' is an open-ended characterization request, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Eulerian-avoidability is fully defined in context, so extracted instances would be formalizable.

rows.title: Question 6.4
rows.paper_title: Extending Thomassen's conjecture to directed graphs
rows.statement_text: Is every orientation of $C_{4}$ Eulerian-avoidable?
rows.context_text: Restricting to Eulerian digraphs (where each vertex has equal out- and in-degree) may make avoidability questions more tractable. The authors ask whether the specific case of $C_4$, which is open in the general setting, becomes resolvable in the Eulerian host digraph setting.
rows.source_text: Is every orientation of $C_{4}$ Eulerian-avoidable?
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no, but 'Eulerian-avoidable' is fully defined only in the sibling record axv_2510_11311_03, not here.

rows.title: Problem 1.1
rows.paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings
rows.statement_text: Determine the smallest $m$ such that in every $2$-coloring of the edges of the ordered complete graph $K_{m}$, there is a monochromatic non-nested matching of size $n$.
rows.context_text: Two independent edges $ij$ and $st$ in an ordered complete graph are nested if $i<s<t<j$ or $s<i<j<t$. The answer is known to lie between $3n-1$ and $4n-2$; the presenting author conjectured (in prior work [2]) that the lower bound $3n-1$ is optimal. A Turán-type approach recently improved the upper bound to $(2+\sqrt{3})n$.
rows.source_text: Determine the smallest $m$ such that in every $2$-coloring of the edges of the ordered complete graph $K_{m}$, there is a monochromatic non-nested matching of size $n$.
rows.status_semantics: Problem 1.1 asks for the smallest $m$ guaranteeing a monochromatic non-nested matching of size $n$ in every 2-coloring of ordered $K_m$. The answer lies between $3n-1$ (the lower bound, conjectured by Barát to be exact) and $(2+\sqrt{3})n \approx 3.73n$ (a Turán-type upper bound established in arXiv:2512.15461 by Barát, Freschi, and Tóth). The conjecture that $3n-1$ is the precise threshold remains open.
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the smallest m' is a determine-the-value problem, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: The conjectured value 3n-1 (lower bound optimal) would make a clean B2 extract.

rows.title: Problem 2.1
rows.paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a random 4-regular graph. Is it true that the coefficient of $P_{G}$ at $\prod_{v\in V(G)}v^{2}$ is a.a.s. non-zero?
rows.context_text: The graph polynomial $P_G$ is uniform and every monomial appearing with a non-zero coefficient has total degree $|E(G)|$; for a 4-regular graph the only relevant monomial is $\prod_{v\in V(G)}v^{2}$. The expected value of the coefficient is zero in the two Hamiltonian cycles model, so a proof would require an anti-concentration inequality.
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a random 4-regular graph. Is it true that the coefficient of $P_{G}$ at $\prod_{v\in V(G)}v^{2}$ is a.a.s. non-zero?
rows.class_notes: P_G is the standard graph polynomial prod (x_i - x_j); needs random 4-regular model and a.a.s. asymptotics.

rows.title: Problem 3.1
rows.paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a disjoint union of cycles. Let $H$ be a disjoint union of $K_{4}$'s. Is $G\cup H$ 4-colourable?
rows.context_text: This is a special case of the general strong chromatic number question: if $G$ has maximum degree $d$ and $H$ is a disjoint union of $K_{2d}$'s, is $G\cup H$ $(2d)$-colourable? The quantity $2d$ would be best possible, and it is known that $G\cup H$ is $(3d-1)$-colourable when $H$ is a disjoint union of $K_{3d-1}$'s.
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a disjoint union of cycles. Let $H$ be a disjoint union of $K_{4}$'s. Is $G\cup H$ 4-colourable?
rows.class_notes: Strong-chromatic-number special case; G and H share a vertex set and the union is edge union.

rows.title: Problem 4.1
rows.paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings
rows.statement_text:
Is it true that every diamond-free claw-free cubic graph is strongly 6-edge-colorable?
In other words, is it true that $\chi^{\prime}_{s}(T(G))=6$ for every cubic graph $G$?
rows.context_text: Lv, Li, and Zhang proved that every claw-free subcubic graph other than the triangular prism satisfies $\chi^{\prime}_{s}(G)\leq 8$. A partial solution by Han and Cui establishes strong 6-edge-colorability for truncated prisms.
rows.source_text:
Is it true that every diamond-free claw-free cubic graph is strongly 6-edge-colorable?
In other words, is it true that $\chi^{\prime}_{s}(T(G))=6$ for every cubic graph $G$?
rows.class_notes: First formulation (diamond-free claw-free cubic strongly 6-edge-colorable) is self-contained; drop the T(G) rephrasing.

rows.title: Problem 5.1
rows.paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings
rows.statement_text:
If a cubic graph admits a $2$-homogeneous coloring,
then $4$ colors are always sufficient.
rows.context_text: A $k$-homogeneous coloring is a proper vertex coloring in which exactly $k$ colors appear in the open neighborhood of every vertex. For bipartite cubic graphs it is known that at most 6 colors suffice for a 2-homogeneous coloring; the conjecture asks whether 4 always suffice whenever a 2-homogeneous coloring exists.
rows.source_text:
If a cubic graph admits a $2$-homogeneous coloring,
then $4$ colors are always sufficient.
rows.class_notes: 2-homogeneous coloring fully defined in context.

rows.title: Problem 5.2
rows.paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings
rows.statement_text: There is a finite number of connected bridgeless cubic graphs that do not admit a $2$-homogeneous coloring.
rows.context_text: Not every cubic graph admits a 2-homogeneous coloring; for example, a graph containing a triangular prism with one edge of a triangle subdivided does not. The conjecture claims the set of exceptions is finite.
rows.source_text: There is a finite number of connected bridgeless cubic graphs that do not admit a $2$-homogeneous coloring.
rows.class_notes: Finiteness of the exception set formalizes as an order bound wrapper.

rows.title: Conjecture 6.1
rows.paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings
rows.statement_text: Each bridgeless graph admits a $2$-flow $\varphi_{2}$ and a $4$-flow $\varphi_{4}$ such that for any edge $e$, if $\varphi_{2}(e)=0$, then $|\varphi_{4}(e)|\geq 2$.
rows.context_text: The conjecture implies the 5-flow conjecture since $(5\varphi_{2}+\varphi_{4})/2$ is a circular 5-flow, and generalises Seymour's approach in the proof of the 6-flow theorem. The resulting pair is called a $1/2$-flow-pair, and the conjecture has been verified computationally for all cyclically 4-edge-connected snarks up to 34 vertices.
rows.source_text: Each bridgeless graph admits a $2$-flow $\varphi_{2}$ and a $4$-flow $\varphi_{4}$ such that for any edge $e$, if $\varphi_{2}(e)=0$, then $|\varphi_{4}(e)|\geq 2$.
rows.class_notes: 1/2-flow-pair conjecture implying the 5-flow conjecture.

rows.title: Problem 7.1
rows.paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings
rows.statement_text: Every bicritical snark is almost-$\mathbb{Z}_{2}^{2}$-connected and almost-$\mathbb{Z}_{4}$-connected.
rows.context_text: A graph is almost-$A$-connected if all boundaries are admissible for group $A$ except $\partial f=0$. The property has been verified for the Petersen graph and both Blanuša snarks on 18 vertices. The conjecture emerged from exploration of the House of Graphs snark database.
rows.source_text: Every bicritical snark is almost-$\mathbb{Z}_{2}^{2}$-connected and almost-$\mathbb{Z}_{4}$-connected.
rows.class_notes: 'Bicritical snark' and almost-A-connectivity are standard snark/group-connectivity vocabulary; context defines the 'almost' relaxation.

rows.title: Question 5.2
rows.paper_title: Induced matching treewidth and tree-independence number, revisited
rows.statement_text: Is is true that for every positive integer $\mu$ there exists a polynomial $\mathsf{p}_{\mu}$ such that every $K_{t,t}$-free graph $G$ with $\operatorname{tree\textnormal{-}\mu}(G)\leqslant\mu$ satisfies $\operatorname{tree\textnormal{-}\alpha}(G)\leqslant\mathsf{p}_{\mu}(t)$?
rows.context_text: Lemma 5.1 shows that the smallest integer $\mathsf{N}(s,s,2)$ appearing in the bound from Abrishami et al. is not bounded by any polynomial in both parameters, motivating the question of whether a polynomial bound in $t$ alone (for fixed $\mu$) is achievable. The question asks specifically whether tree-independence number can be bounded polynomially in the induced biclique exclusion parameter $t$ for graphs of bounded induced matching treewidth.
rows.source_text: Is is true that for every positive integer $\mu$ there exists a polynomial $\mathsf{p}_{\mu}$ such that every $K_{t,t}$-free graph $G$ with $\operatorname{tree\textnormal{-}\mu}(G)\leqslant\mu$ satisfies $\operatorname{tree\textnormal{-}\alpha}(G)\leqslant\mathsf{p}_{\mu}(t)$?
rows.class_notes: Needs tree-independence-number and induced-matching-treewidth primitives (buildable finite width parameters).

rows.title: Question 5.3
rows.paper_title: Induced matching treewidth and tree-independence number, revisited
rows.statement_text: Is it true that for classes of graphs with bounded induced matching treewidth, tree-independence number is bounded from above by a polynomial function of the induced biclique number?
rows.context_text: The induced biclique number of $G$ is the largest nonnegative integer $t$ such that $G$ contains an induced subgraph isomorphic to $K_{t,t}$. This question is an equivalent restatement of Question 5.2 using the induced biclique number, and is noted as closely related to recent work bounding tree-independence number in terms of clique-like parameters.
rows.source_text: Is it true that for classes of graphs with bounded induced matching treewidth, tree-independence number is bounded from above by a polynomial function of the induced biclique number?
rows.class_notes: Context states this is an equivalent restatement of Question 5.2 (axv_2511_03864_00) via the induced biclique number.

rows.title: Conjecture 1.5
rows.paper_title: Infinite Schnyder Woods
rows.statement_text: The unique maximal Schnyder wood of the UIHPT is the weak limit of the maximal Schnyder wood of a uniformly random triangulation from $T^m_n$ as $n \to \infty$ and then $m \to \infty$.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.2 establishes that the maximal Schnyder wood of the UIPT with boundary length $m$ is the weak limit of maximal Schnyder woods of uniformly random triangulations from $T^m_n$ as $n \to \infty$. The authors were unable to extend this convergence result to the UIHPT (letting $m \to \infty$ after $n \to \infty$), and leave it as a conjecture. They discuss potential consequences for geodesic paths and the winding behaviour of monochromatic trees in large finite triangulations.
rows.source_text: The unique maximal Schnyder wood of the UIHPT is the weak limit of the maximal Schnyder wood of a uniformly random triangulation from $T^m_n$ as $n \to \infty$ and then $m \to \infty$.
rows.class_notes: Relies on paper-constructed objects (maximal Schnyder wood of UIHPT, local weak-limit topology on wooded triangulations) not defined in this record.

rows.title: Open question on Schnyder woods for general half-plane triangulations
rows.paper_title: Infinite Schnyder Woods
rows.statement_text: It would be interesting to know whether Schnyder woods exist on all half-plane triangulations, and if not, to characterise the ones for which they do exist.
rows.context_text: The paper proves existence and uniqueness of the maximal Schnyder wood for all infinite triangulations with finite boundary (Theorem 1.1) and for the UIHPT specifically (Theorem 1.3), but does not treat arbitrary triangulations of the half-plane (infinite boundary). The authors note that whenever a maximal Schnyder wood of the half-plane exists it is unique, leaving existence for the general case open.
rows.source_text: It would be interesting to know whether Schnyder woods exist on all half-plane triangulations, and if not, to characterise the ones for which they do exist.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2511.07601 — Open question on Schnyder woods for general half-plane triangulations
rows.defer_reason: 'It would be interesting to know ... and if not, to characterise' is a meta/characterization request, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: The existence half (do all half-plane triangulations admit a Schnyder wood?) could be a B2/B3 extract.

rows.title: Open question on local limit of uniformly random wooded triangulations
rows.paper_title: Infinite Schnyder Woods
rows.statement_text: It would be interesting to understand the local limit of uniformly random wooded triangulations as the boundary length tends to infinity, and whether such wooded triangulations have a local limit, which would be a random wooded triangulation of the half-plane different from the one constructed in this work.
rows.context_text: The authors remark that uniformly random wooded triangulations (triangulations paired with an arbitrary Schnyder wood) are quite different from maximal Schnyder woods of uniformly random triangulations: tree heights in the former are $\Theta(\sqrt{n})$ in probability, while the expected height of a uniformly random vertex in the latter is of order $n^{3/4}$. Whether these wooded triangulations possess a local limit as the boundary length grows is left open.
rows.source_text: It would be interesting to understand the local limit of uniformly random wooded triangulations as the boundary length tends to infinity, and whether such wooded triangulations have a local limit, which would be a random wooded triangulation of the half-plane different from the one constructed in this work.
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2511.07601 — Open question on local limit of uniformly random wooded triangulations
rows.defer_reason: 'It would be interesting to understand the local limit' is a vague research direction, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Problem 1
rows.paper_title: Spectrally symmetric orientations of graphs
rows.statement_text: Find the minimal constant $\widehat{\rho}\in[0,1]$ such that each spectrally symmetric graph satisfies $\frac{2|E(G)|}{|V(G)|^{2}}\leq\widehat{\rho}$.
rows.context_text: Motivated by the classical fact that a graph of order $n$ with more than $\frac{n^2}{4}$ edges is not bipartite (and hence its adjacency spectrum is not symmetric), the authors ask for an analogous density threshold for spectrally symmetric orientations. The paper establishes $\frac{13}{18}\leq\widehat{\rho}\leq\frac{10}{11}$ but the exact value remains open.
rows.source_text: Find the minimal constant $\widehat{\rho}\in[0,1]$ such that each spectrally symmetric graph satisfies $\frac{2|E(G)|}{|V(G)|^{2}}\leq\widehat{\rho}$.
rows.defer_reason: 'Find the minimal constant' is a determine-the-value problem; known bounds 13/18 <= rho <= 10/11 but no conjectured value.
rows.class_notes: 'Spectrally symmetric' also not precisely defined in this record.

rows.title: Problem 5.1
rows.paper_title: Color-avoiding directed paths in tournaments
rows.statement_text: For some integers $q\geqslant 4$ and $N$, does there exist a non-transitive $q$-edge-colored $N$-vertex tournament whose longest color-avoiding path has strictly fewer than $f_{q,q-1}(N)$ vertices?
rows.context_text: For $q\geqslant 4$, Proposition 1.5 shows $F_{q,q-1}(n)=G_{q,q-1}(n)$, meaning the largest $(q-1)$-comparable set is always a $(q-1)$-increasing sequence and hence corresponds to a transitive tournament. Problem 5.1 asks whether a non-transitive coloring can do strictly worse than the transitive extremum. The answer is affirmative for $q=3$ via the Hamaker–Stein construction, but Proposition 1.5 rules out any vector-based construction for $q\geqslant 4$, leaving the problem open in that range.
rows.source_text: For some integers $q\geqslant 4$ and $N$, does there exist a non-transitive $q$-edge-colored $N$-vertex tournament whose longest color-avoiding path has strictly fewer than $f_{q,q-1}(N)$ vertices?
rows.class_notes: Depends on the paper-local extremal functions f_{q,q-1} and G_{q,q-1}, not defined in this record.

rows.title: Conjecture 6
rows.paper_title: An Induced $A$-Path Theorem
rows.statement_text: For every $k\in\mathbb{N}$, every graph $G$ and $X,Y\subseteq V(G)$ satisfies one of the following: $G$ contains $k$ pairwise anti-complete $(X,Y)$-paths; or there exists a set $Z\subseteq V(G)$ with $|Z|\leqslant k-1$ such that $G-N_{G}[Z]$ has no $(X,Y)$-path.
rows.context_text: The authors pose this $(X,Y)$-path strengthening of their main theorem (Theorem 3), seeking to reduce the separator size from $78(k-1)$ to the optimal $k-1$. If true, it would have immediate application to the study of induced minors, in particular implying an Induced Grid Minor Theorem for $K_{1,t}$-induced-subgraph-free graphs conjectured by Dallard et al.
rows.source_text: For every $k\in\mathbb{N}$, every graph $G$ and $X,Y\subseteq V(G)$ satisfies one of the following: $G$ contains $k$ pairwise anti-complete $(X,Y)$-paths; or there exists a set $Z\subseteq V(G)$ with $|Z|\leqslant k-1$ such that $G-N_{G}[Z]$ has no $(X,Y)$-path.
rows.class_notes: Induced Menger-type dichotomy with standard vocabulary (anticomplete paths, closed neighborhood); optimal-separator strengthening of the paper's Theorem 3.

rows.title: Problem 1.1
rows.paper_title: Nowhere-zero flow reconfiguration
rows.statement_text: Is it true that for every 2-edge-connected graph $G$, the reconfiguration graph $\mathcal{F}(G,5)$ is connected?
rows.context_text: This is the natural reconfiguration version of Tutte's 5-flow conjecture. The paper notes that Problem 1.1 is incomparable to Tutte's 5-flow conjecture itself, since connectedness of $\mathcal{F}(G,k)$ does not imply non-emptiness. The group-flow analogue is posed separately as Problem 1.3.
rows.source_text: Is it true that for every 2-edge-connected graph $G$, the reconfiguration graph $\mathcal{F}(G,5)$ is connected?
rows.class_notes: The adjacency (reconfiguration step) of F(G,k) is paper-defined and not given in this record.

rows.title: Problem 2.6
rows.paper_title: Nowhere-zero flow reconfiguration
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph and $k$ be an integer. Prove that if $\mathcal{F}(G,\mathbb{Z}_{k})$ is connected, then $\mathcal{F}(G,k)$ is also connected.
rows.context_text: The paper establishes the easier direction (connectivity of $\mathcal{F}(G,k)$ implies connectivity of $\mathcal{F}(G,\mathbb{Z}_k)$) and provides examples showing the group structure matters. The converse direction is open; the authors note they cannot prove it even for $k=4$.
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph and $k$ be an integer. Prove that if $\mathcal{F}(G,\mathbb{Z}_{k})$ is connected, then $\mathcal{F}(G,k)$ is also connected.
rows.class_notes: Same missing definition of the reconfiguration graphs F(G,k) and F(G,Z_k) as axv_2512_17342_00.

rows.title: Open Question on Circular Drawing Characterisation
rows.paper_title: When all directed cycles have the same weight
rows.statement_text: Is there a theorem that says ``every appropriately connected digraph $G$ contains no thing of type $X$ if and only if $G$ admits a circular drawing''?
rows.context_text: Thomassen's theorem gives a characterisation of circular drawings in the presence of a specific 2-vertex separator. The authors generalise this into a motivating question and, despite fully characterising weightable digraphs in the paper, explicitly state: ``We still have not come up with the characterization we hoped for of the digraphs with circular drawings.''
rows.source_text: Is there a theorem that says ``every appropriately connected digraph $G$ contains no thing of type $X$ if and only if $G$ admits a circular drawing''?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2601.12746 — Open Question on Circular Drawing Characterisation
rows.defer_reason: Meta-question asking whether some characterization theorem with an unspecified obstruction 'X' exists; no concrete proposition.

rows.title: Problem 1
rows.paper_title: Faster 3-colouring algorithm for graphs of diameter 3
rows.statement_text: Can 3-colouring be solved on diameter-2 graphs in quasi-polynomial time?
rows.context_text: The paper improves the algorithm for 3-colouring diameter-3 graphs, leaving the complexity of 3-colouring diameter-2 graphs as the main outstanding challenge. The authors note this is one of the two 'notorious' open cases identified by Paulusma, and that the polynomial-time solvability of 3-colouring on diameter-2 graphs is the most intriguing open problem in the area. The quasi-polynomial formulation is highlighted as an explicit intermediate target.
rows.source_text: Can 3-colouring be solved on diameter-2 graphs in quasi-polynomial time?
rows.class_notes: Clean yes/no algorithmic question but needs a computation model (quasi-polynomial time).

rows.title: Problem 1
rows.paper_title: Characterization of sparse monotone graph classes with bounded domination-to-2-independence ratio
rows.statement_text: Which graph classes have bounded domination-to-2-independence ratio?
rows.context_text: The domination-to-2-independence ratio of a graph class $\mathcal{G}$ is defined as $\sup_{G\in\mathcal{G}}\tfrac{\gamma(G)}{\alpha_{2}(G)}$. Since the fractional relaxations of domination number and $2$-independence number coincide, a bounded ratio would allow both parameters to be approximated via their common fractional relaxation, computable in polynomial time. The problem has been studied for trees, strongly chordal graphs, dually chordal graphs, outerplanar graphs, and other sparse graph classes.
rows.source_text: Which graph classes have bounded domination-to-2-independence ratio?
rows.defer_reason: 'Which graph classes ...' is an open-ended characterization over all graph classes, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Problem 2
rows.paper_title: Characterization of sparse monotone graph classes with bounded domination-to-2-independence ratio
rows.statement_text: Which graph classes have domination tied to 2-independence?
rows.context_text: A graph class $\mathcal{G}$ has domination tied to 2-independence if there exists a function $h$ such that $\gamma(G)\leq h(\alpha_{2}(G))$ holds for every graph $G\in\mathcal{G}$. This is a relaxation of Problem 1, which requires $h$ to be linear; the authors remark that even this relaxed version seems non-trivial.
rows.source_text: Which graph classes have domination tied to 2-independence?
rows.defer_reason: 'Which graph classes have domination tied to 2-independence?' is an open-ended characterization, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Relaxation of Problem 1 (axv_2601_15082_00) from linear to arbitrary tying function.

rows.title: Problem 4.1
rows.paper_title: Coloring small locally sparse degenerate graphs and related problems
rows.statement_text: What is the maximum fractional chromatic number of a $d$-degenerate, $K_{r}$-free graph?
rows.context_text: Given the main theorem of Martinsson [26] on triangle-free $d$-degenerate graphs and its many applications, the authors seek a generalization of Harris' conjecture for $K_{r}$-free graphs. One might guess the answer is $O_{r}\left(\frac{d\log\log d}{\log d}\right)$ by analogy with Johansson's bound for $K_r$-free graphs of maximum degree $\Delta$, but Theorem 1.6 shows this is not the case.
rows.source_text: What is the maximum fractional chromatic number of a $d$-degenerate, $K_{r}$-free graph?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the maximum fractional chromatic number ...' is a determine-the-value problem with no conjectured answer (the natural guess is refuted in the paper).

rows.title: Problem 6.1
rows.paper_title: Coloring small locally sparse degenerate graphs and related problems
rows.statement_text: Can the upper bound $2^{cd}$ on the number of vertices assumed in Theorems 1.1 and 1.2 be weakened to $e^{\omega(d)}$ (with a corresponding improvement of the upper bound on $\chi$ when $n=e^{\omega(d)}$)? What if we additionally assume that the graph has large (constant) girth?
rows.context_text: The perhaps most important open problem left by the paper is whether the exponential requirement on the number of vertices in Theorems 1.1 and 1.2 can be further relaxed. Even improving the constant $c$ in Theorem 1.1 could resolve Erdős's problem on graph Ramsey numbers for all triangle-free graphs by combining with the trivial lower bound $r(G)\geq|V(G)|$.
rows.source_text: Can the upper bound $2^{cd}$ on the number of vertices assumed in Theorems 1.1 and 1.2 be weakened to $e^{\omega(d)}$ (with a corresponding improvement of the upper bound on $\chi$ when $n=e^{\omega(d)}$)? What if we additionally assume that the graph has large (constant) girth?
rows.class_notes: Refers to Theorems 1.1/1.2 not stated in context; also partly meta (improve-the-bound flavor).

rows.title: Problem 6.2
rows.paper_title: Coloring small locally sparse degenerate graphs and related problems
rows.statement_text: Is the following statement true for all $\varepsilon>0$? If $G$ is a $d$-degenerate triangle-free graph with maximum degree $\Delta(G)\leq e^{d^{1-\varepsilon}}$, then we have [statement truncated in source]
rows.context_text: Resolving this problem would likely require localizing the random coloring process used in the paper so that the union bound can be replaced by an application of the Lovász Local Lemma. An affirmative answer would imply results on immersion analogues of Hadwiger's conjecture, as conjectured by Lescure and Meyniel in 1988.
rows.source_text: Is the following statement true for all $\varepsilon>0$? If $G$ is a $d$-degenerate triangle-free graph with maximum degree $\Delta(G)\leq e^{d^{1-\varepsilon}}$, then we have [statement truncated in source]
rows.class_notes: Conclusion of the implication is truncated in source; hypothesis side is fully standard.

rows.title: Problem 6.3
rows.paper_title: Coloring small locally sparse degenerate graphs and related problems
rows.statement_text: Is it true that if the chromatic number of a $K_{r}$-free $d$-degenerate graph is $d+1$, then the graph has $e^{\Omega_{r}(d)}$ vertices?
rows.context_text: The main results of the paper establish that triangle-free $d$-degenerate graphs with at most $2^{cd}$ vertices have chromatic number strictly less than $d+1$; this problem asks whether the exponential lower bound on the order of extremal examples extends from the triangle-free case ($r=3$) to all $r$.
rows.source_text: Is it true that if the chromatic number of a $K_{r}$-free $d$-degenerate graph is $d+1$, then the graph has $e^{\Omega_{r}(d)}$ vertices?
rows.class_notes: Self-contained: K_r-free d-degenerate with chi=d+1 forces e^{Omega_r(d)} vertices.

rows.title: Problem 6.4
rows.paper_title: Coloring small locally sparse degenerate graphs and related problems
rows.statement_text: Let $r\geq 4$. Is it true that the maximum fractional chromatic number of $K_{r}$-free $d$-degenerate graphs is $o_{r}(d)$?
rows.context_text: Theorem 1.6 gives a lower bound of $\Omega(d/\log^{(r-2)}d)$ on the fractional chromatic number of $K_r$-free $d$-degenerate graphs, which approaches linear in $d$ as $r$ increases, leaving open whether the maximum is truly sub-linear for each fixed $r\geq 4$.
rows.source_text: Let $r\geq 4$. Is it true that the maximum fractional chromatic number of $K_{r}$-free $d$-degenerate graphs is $o_{r}(d)$?
rows.class_notes: Fractional chromatic number is standard; o_r(d) needs asymptotic wrapper.

rows.title: Problem 9
rows.paper_title: Cops and robber in graphs with bounded vertex cover number
rows.statement_text: Is it true that for connected graphs $G$, $\operatorname{cop}(G)=o(\mathrm{td}(G))$?
rows.context_text: The authors ask whether a sublinear bound on the cop number in terms of treedepth holds, viewing treedepth as a natural intermediate parameter between vertex cover number and treewidth. They note that the bound $\operatorname{cop}(G)\leqslant\tfrac{1}{2}(\mathrm{td}(G)+1)$ is easily deducible from existing results on treewidth, but improving significantly on it seems difficult.
rows.source_text: Is it true that for connected graphs $G$, $\operatorname{cop}(G)=o(\mathrm{td}(G))$?
rows.class_notes: Needs cop-number (pursuit game) and treedepth primitives plus little-o wrapper.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.1
rows.paper_title: Long cycles in vertex transitive digraphs
rows.statement_text: There exists an $\varepsilon>0$ and infinitely many values of $n$ for which there exists a connected vertex transitive digraph of order $n$, whose perimeter gap is at least $\varepsilon n$.
rows.context_text: The paper proves the perimeter gap grows at least as fast as $(1-o(1))\ln n$ and that circumference is always at least $\Omega(n^{1/3})$. Determining the best possible bounds seems far out of reach; this conjecture is proposed as a natural intermediate target, asserting that the logarithmic lower bound on the gap is far from tight.
rows.source_text: There exists an $\varepsilon>0$ and infinitely many values of $n$ for which there exists a connected vertex transitive digraph of order $n$, whose perimeter gap is at least $\varepsilon n$.
rows.class_notes: 'Perimeter gap' is paper-local and not defined in the record.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.2
rows.paper_title: Long cycles in vertex transitive digraphs
rows.statement_text: Let $c(n)$ denote the minimum circumference of a connected vertex transitive graph on $n$ vertices. Let $d(n)$ denote the minimum circumference of a connected vertex transitive digraph on $n$ vertices. Then $d(n)=\Theta(c(n))$.
rows.context_text: The paper's bound of $\Omega(n^{1/3})$ on the circumference of connected vertex transitive digraphs is slightly weaker than the best known $\Omega(n^{9/14})$ undirected bound. This conjecture asserts that the directed and undirected cases are asymptotically the same, proposed as a natural intermediate target.
rows.source_text: Let $c(n)$ denote the minimum circumference of a connected vertex transitive graph on $n$ vertices. Let $d(n)$ denote the minimum circumference of a connected vertex transitive digraph on $n$ vertices. Then $d(n)=\Theta(c(n))$.
rows.class_notes: d(n)=Theta(c(n)) fully defined in the statement; circumference and vertex transitivity are standard.

rows.title: Question 4.3
rows.paper_title: Long cycles in vertex transitive digraphs
rows.statement_text: Does vertex transitivity guarantee that every two longest directed cycles in a connected vertex transitive digraph share a common vertex?
rows.context_text: A major obstacle to translating results from graphs to digraphs is that, unlike 2-connected undirected graphs, strongly 2-connected digraphs may contain vertex-disjoint longest cycles. The authors ask whether vertex transitivity could be a condition guaranteeing intersection of all pairs of longest cycles.
rows.source_text: Does vertex transitivity guarantee that every two longest directed cycles in a connected vertex transitive digraph share a common vertex?

rows.title: Question 4.4
rows.paper_title: Long cycles in vertex transitive digraphs
rows.context_text: Appears as a labelled question environment in the concluding remarks section alongside Question 4.3, but its full statement was not included in the provided paper content.
rows.class_notes: Statement entirely missing from the record; recovery required.

rows.title: Conjecture 1
rows.paper_title: Packing arithmetic progressions
rows.statement_text: $m(n)\sim\frac{4}{3}\cdot\frac{n^{3/2}}{\ln n}$.
rows.context_text: The paper proves $m(n)=\Theta(n^{3/2}/\ln n)$ with matching constants $4/3$ (lower) and $5/3$ (upper) in Theorem 1. The authors state that closing this gap and pinning down the exact constant requires additional ideas, and suspect $4/3$ is correct.
rows.source_text: $m(n)\sim\frac{4}{3}\cdot\frac{n^{3/2}}{\ln n}$.
rows.defer_reason: Packing arithmetic progressions in [n]: additive combinatorics, not graph theory.

rows.title: Conjecture 4
rows.paper_title: Packing arithmetic progressions
rows.statement_text: $M_{\mathbb{P}(n)}(n)\sim\frac{1}{6}\cdot\frac{n^{3}}{\ln n}$.
rows.context_text: Closely related to Conjecture 3. The lower bounds in Conjectures 3 and 4 follow from the proofs of the lower bounds in Theorems 1 and 5, respectively. The upper bounds remain open.
rows.source_text: $M_{\mathbb{P}(n)}(n)\sim\frac{1}{6}\cdot\frac{n^{3}}{\ln n}$.
rows.defer_reason: Arithmetic-progression packing asymptotics; additive combinatorics, not graph theory.

rows.title: Conjecture 5
rows.paper_title: Packing arithmetic progressions
rows.statement_text: For every $k$ satisfying $\Omega(\sqrt{n})=k<\sqrt{n}$, we have [asymptotic formula for $m_k(n)$ — mathematical conclusion truncated in source].
rows.context_text: If Conjecture 1 holds, it also resolves $m_k(n)$ asymptotically for $k\geqslant\sqrt{n}$ via Theorem 4. The only remaining open case is $k<\sqrt{n}$ with $k\not=o(\sqrt{n})$, which this conjecture addresses.
rows.source_text: For every $k$ satisfying $\Omega(\sqrt{n})=k<\sqrt{n}$, we have [asymptotic formula for $m_k(n)$ — mathematical conclusion truncated in source].
rows.defer_reason: AP packing (also truncated); additive combinatorics, not graph theory.

rows.title: Conjecture 6
rows.paper_title: Packing arithmetic progressions
rows.statement_text: If $k\ll n$, then [formula for $M_k(n)$], while otherwise, [formula for $M_k(n)$] — mathematical conclusions truncated in source.
rows.context_text: Conjectured asymptotic behaviour of $M_k(n)$ for $k<n$ in view of Theorem 5, splitting into two regimes according to whether $k\ll n$ or not.
rows.source_text: If $k\ll n$, then [formula for $M_k(n)$], while otherwise, [formula for $M_k(n)$] — mathematical conclusions truncated in source.
rows.defer_reason: AP packing (also truncated); additive combinatorics, not graph theory.

rows.title: Conjecture 7
rows.paper_title: Packing arithmetic progressions
rows.statement_text: For every fixed $n$ and $k$ tending to infinity, we have [asymptotic formula — mathematical conclusion truncated in source].
rows.context_text: Concerns packing of $k$ arithmetic progressions of the form $B_d=\{d,2d,\ldots,nd\}$ for $d=1,2,\ldots,k$ with $n$ fixed. The authors suspect the trivial lower bound of $nk$ is asymptotically correct as $k\to\infty$.
rows.source_text: For every fixed $n$ and $k$ tending to infinity, we have [asymptotic formula — mathematical conclusion truncated in source].
rows.defer_reason: AP packing (also truncated); additive combinatorics, not graph theory.

rows.title: Question 5
rows.paper_title: On Borodin-Kostochka conjecture for correspondence coloring
rows.statement_text: What is the smallest number $\Delta_{0}$ such that every graph $G$ with $\Delta(G)\geq\Delta_{0}$ and $\omega(G)=\Delta(G)-1$ satisfies $\chi(G)=\chi_{\ell}(G)=\chi_{DP}(G)$?
rows.context_text: Theorem 4 of this paper gives an upper bound $\Delta_0 \leq 3\cdot 10^9$ for the correspondence coloring analogue. The equality $\chi(G)=\chi_\ell(G)=\chi_{DP}(G)$ clearly holds for all graphs $G$ with $\omega(G)=\Delta(G)+1$, and the parameters also coincide for graphs with $\omega(G)=\Delta(G)$; the smallest threshold $\Delta_0$ for the case $\omega(G)=\Delta(G)-1$ remains unknown.
rows.source_text: What is the smallest number $\Delta_{0}$ such that every graph $G$ with $\Delta(G)\geq\Delta_{0}$ and $\omega(G)=\Delta(G)-1$ satisfies $\chi(G)=\chi_{\ell}(G)=\chi_{DP}(G)$?
rows.defer_reason: Asks for the exact smallest threshold Delta_0, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: DP/list-coloring threshold variant of Borodin-Kostochka (omega=Delta-1 regime), not the conjecture itself.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.1
rows.paper_title: Anticoncentration of random spanning trees in graphs with large minimum degree
rows.statement_text: Let $d$ be sufficiently large, and let $n$ be sufficiently large relative to $d$. Suppose that $G$ is a connected graph with $n$ vertices and minimum degree at least $d$, and let $\mathcal{T}$ be a uniformly random spanning tree of $G$. Then, for every tree $T$ it holds that $$\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{T}\cong T)\leq n^{-(1/2-o_n(1))(d-1)}.$$
rows.context_text: The authors conjecture that the optimal constant factor in the exponent of the anticoncentration bound should be $1/2$, matching the anticoncentration of a uniformly random spanning tree of $K_{d,n-d}$. For $K_{d,n-d}$, a spanning tree up to isomorphism is determined by a minimal subtree connecting the $d$ vertices in the small class and the number of leaves attached to each of these $d$ vertices.
rows.source_text: Let $d$ be sufficiently large, and let $n$ be sufficiently large relative to $d$. Suppose that $G$ is a connected graph with $n$ vertices and minimum degree at least $d$, and let $\mathcal{T}$ be a uniformly random spanning tree of $G$. Then, for every tree $T$ it holds that $$\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{T}\cong T)\leq n^{-(1/2-o_n(1))(d-1)}.$$
rows.class_notes: Uniform random spanning tree anticoncentration; needs probability over finite trees plus o_n(1) exponent.

rows.title: Conjecture 4.2
rows.paper_title: Anticoncentration of random spanning trees in graphs with large minimum degree
rows.statement_text: Let $d$ be sufficiently large, and let $n$ be sufficiently large relative to $d$. Suppose that $G$ is a connected graph with $n$ vertices and minimum degree at least $d$. Then, the number of non-isomorphic spanning trees of $G$ is at least $\Omega(n^{d-1})$.
rows.context_text: The anticoncentration property of Conjecture 4.1 would imply only $n^{(1/2-o_n(1))(d-1)}$ non-isomorphic spanning trees, but $K_{d,n-d}$ has $\Omega(n^{d-1})$ non-isomorphic spanning trees. The authors conjecture that this larger quantity gives an essentially optimal lower bound, with $K_{d,n-d}$ being an extremal example.
rows.source_text: Let $d$ be sufficiently large, and let $n$ be sufficiently large relative to $d$. Suppose that $G$ is a connected graph with $n$ vertices and minimum degree at least $d$. Then, the number of non-isomorphic spanning trees of $G$ is at least $\Omega(n^{d-1})$.
rows.class_notes: Counting non-isomorphic spanning trees; Omega(n^{d-1}) needs asymptotic wrapper.

rows.title: Problem 1
rows.paper_title: A Gray code for arborescences of tournaments
rows.statement_text: Does every graph $G$ admit a pivot Gray code on its spanning trees?
rows.context_text: A pivot Gray code for spanning trees requires that consecutive spanning trees differ by an edge exchange sharing a common endpoint (the strong revolving door property). Although the corresponding reconfiguration graph is connected for any graph $G$, it remains open whether all graphs admit such a code. Pivot Gray codes have been constructed for fan graphs and more generally for outerplanar graphs.
rows.source_text: Does every graph $G$ admit a pivot Gray code on its spanning trees?
rows.class_notes: Pivot Gray code = Hamilton path in spanning-tree exchange graph with common-endpoint pivots; defined in context.

rows.title: Conjecture 6.1
rows.paper_title: Colour-balanced subgraphs
rows.statement_text: Let $h\colon E(K_{n,n})\to\mathbb{R}^{k}$ such that $\|h(e)\|_{1}\leq 1$ for all $e\in E(K_{n,n})$. Then there is a perfect matching $M$ of $K_{n,n}$ satisfying [a bound of order $O(\sqrt{k})$ on $\|\sum_{e\in M}h(e)\|_1$].
rows.context_text: There is a gap between the $O(k^2)$ upper bound for representative matchings in bipartite complete graphs (Section 1.1) and the worst-case lower bound of $\sqrt{k/2}$ constructed in Theorem 5.1. The authors conjecture that the lower bound gives the correct order, so that the true bound is $\Theta(\sqrt{k})$.
rows.source_text: Let $h\colon E(K_{n,n})\to\mathbb{R}^{k}$ such that $\|h(e)\|_{1}\leq 1$ for all $e\in E(K_{n,n})$. Then there is a perfect matching $M$ of $K_{n,n}$ satisfying [a bound of order $O(\sqrt{k})$ on $\|\sum_{e\in M}h(e)\|_1$].
rows.class_notes: Bound O(sqrt k) paraphrased in brackets but content clear: exists C with ||sum_M h(e)||_1 <= C sqrt(k).

rows.title: Conjecture 6.2
rows.paper_title: Colour-balanced subgraphs
rows.statement_text: There exists some absolute constant $C$ such that for all positive integers $k$ and $t$, every colour-balanced $k$-edge-colouring of $K_{2kt}$ admits a perfect matching $M$ satisfying $f_{c}(M)<C$.
rows.context_text: There is a gap between the $O(k^2)$ upper bound from Theorem 1.2 and the constant lower bound constructed in Theorem 5.3 for matchings in complete graphs. The authors conjecture the constant lower bound is tight. They note that a $\sqrt{k}$ lower bound for perfect matchings of $K_{kt,kt}$ implies that new ideas would be necessary to prove this conjecture.
rows.source_text: There exists some absolute constant $C$ such that for all positive integers $k$ and $t$, every colour-balanced $k$-edge-colouring of $K_{2kt}$ admits a perfect matching $M$ satisfying $f_{c}(M)<C$.
rows.class_notes: Paper-local f_c and 'colour-balanced' not defined in the record.

rows.title: Problem 6.3
rows.paper_title: Colour-balanced subgraphs
rows.statement_text: Improve the bounds on $f_{h}(F)$ for bounded-degree spanning forests $F$ in complete graphs when $k\geq 3$.
rows.context_text: For $k=2$, error bounds sharp up to an additive constant are known for spanning forests of maximum degree $\Delta$. For $k>2$ the paper shows the error remains linear in $\Delta$, but no attempt is made to optimise the leading coefficient.
rows.source_text: Improve the bounds on $f_{h}(F)$ for bounded-degree spanning forests $F$ in complete graphs when $k\geq 3$.
rows.defer_reason: 'Improve the bounds' — no single proposition stated.

rows.title: Problem 6.4
rows.paper_title: Colour-balanced subgraphs
rows.statement_text: Improve the bounds on $f_{h}(H)$ for a Hamilton cycle $H$ in complete or complete bipartite graphs.
rows.context_text: The general upper bound from Theorem 1.2 gives $O(k^2)$ for Hamilton cycles in complete graphs, and the same bound holds for complete bipartite graphs by the paper's methods. A necklace-splitting argument shows any improvement to the bipartite matching bound yields an equal improvement here, so progress on either problem benefits the other.
rows.source_text: Improve the bounds on $f_{h}(H)$ for a Hamilton cycle $H$ in complete or complete bipartite graphs.
rows.defer_reason: 'Improve the bounds' — no single proposition stated.

rows.title: Problem 2
rows.paper_title: Openly disjoint cycles and directed tree-width of regular digraphs
rows.statement_text: Determine $\lim_{r\rightarrow\infty}\frac{c_{r}}{r}$.
rows.context_text: The paper proves that the sequence $(c_r/r)_{r\in\mathbb{N}}$ converges to some limit $L\in[\frac{3}{22},1]$; Problem 2 asks for the exact value of this limit. The problem is stated immediately after the proof of convergence.
rows.source_text: Determine $\lim_{r\rightarrow\infty}\frac{c_{r}}{r}$.
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine the limit' — asks for a value; c_r also paper-local.

rows.title: Informal Question on optimal directed tree-width constant for regular digraphs
rows.paper_title: Openly disjoint cycles and directed tree-width of regular digraphs
rows.statement_text: What is the best-possible constant $\varepsilon>0$ such that every $r$-regular digraph $D$ has directed tree-width at least $\varepsilon r$?
rows.context_text: Motivated by Theorem 7 of the paper, which establishes that every $r$-regular digraph has directed tree-width growing with $r$, the authors pose this question about the optimal linear constant in such a lower bound.
rows.source_text: What is the best-possible constant $\varepsilon>0$ such that every $r$-regular digraph $D$ has directed tree-width at least $\varepsilon r$?
rows.source_locator: arXiv:2604.13700 — Informal Question on optimal directed tree-width constant for regular digraphs
rows.defer_reason: Asks for the best-possible constant epsilon, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Problem 8
rows.paper_title: A note on arithmetic progressions with restricted differences
rows.statement_text: Improve the range of $|S|$ in Theorem 1.
rows.context_text: Theorem 1 establishes that if $q$ is an odd prime power and $S\subseteq\mathbb{F}_{q}$ with $0\in S$ and $|S|>(q+1)/2$, then any $A\subseteq\mathbb{F}_{q}^{n}$ containing no three-term arithmetic progression with common difference in $S^{n}$ satisfies $|A|\leq q^{(1-\varepsilon_{q})n}$. The problem asks whether the condition $|S|>(q+1)/2$ can be weakened.
rows.source_text: Improve the range of $|S|$ in Theorem 1.
rows.defer_reason: 3-APs with restricted differences in F_q^n: additive combinatorics; also 'improve the range' meta.

rows.title: Problem 9
rows.paper_title: A note on arithmetic progressions with restricted differences
rows.statement_text: Is there an arithmetic triangle removal lemma with restricted differences?
rows.context_text: The multicolor sum-free result was used to prove an arithmetic triangle removal lemma in $\mathbb{F}_{q}^{n}$. In line with this, it is natural to wonder if there is a restricted differences version extending Theorem 2.
rows.source_text: Is there an arithmetic triangle removal lemma with restricted differences?
rows.defer_reason: Arithmetic triangle removal lemma in F_q^n: additive combinatorics, not graph theory; also vague.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1006
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph with girth $>4$ (that is, it contains no cycles of length $3$ or $4$). Can the edges of $G$ always be directed such that there is no directed cycle, and reversing the direction of any edge also creates no directed cycle?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph with girth $>4$ (that is, it contains no cycles of length $3$ or $4$). Can the edges of $G$ always be directed such that there is no directed cycle, and reversing the direction of any edge also creates no directed cycle?
rows.class_notes: Solved; acyclic orientation robust to any single edge reversal for girth>4 graphs.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1007
rows.prize: SOLVED (LEAN) This has been resolved in some other way than a proof or disproof, and that resolution verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: The dimension of a graph $G$ is the minimal $n$ such that $G$ can be embedded in $\mathbb{R}^n$ such that every edge of $G$ is a unit line segment. What is the smallest number of edges in a graph with dimension $4$?
rows.source_text: The dimension of a graph $G$ is the minimal $n$ such that $G$ can be embedded in $\mathbb{R}^n$ such that every edge of $G$ is a unit line segment. What is the smallest number of edges in a graph with dimension $4$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #1009

rows.source_text: Is it true that, for every $c>0$, there exists $f(c)$ such that every graph on $n$ vertices with at least $\lfloor n^2/4\rfloor+k$ edges, where $k<c n$, contains at least $k-f(c)$ many edge disjoint triangles?

rows.title: Erdős problem #1010

rows.statement_text: Let $t<\lfloor n/2\rfloor$. Does every graph on $n$ vertices with $\lfloor n^2/4\rfloor+t$ edges contain at least $t\lfloor n/2\rfloor$ triangles?
rows.source_text: Let $t<\lfloor n/2\rfloor$. Does every graph on $n$ vertices with $\lfloor n^2/4\rfloor+t$ edges contain at least $t\lfloor n/2\rfloor$ triangles?

rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1014
rows.prize: PROVED (LEAN) This has been solved in the affirmative and the proof verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: Let $R(k,l)$ be the Ramsey number, so the minimal $n$ such that every graph on at least $n$ vertices contains either a $K_k$ or an independent set on $l$ vertices. Prove, for fixed $k\geq 3$, that\[\lim_{l\to \infty}\frac{R(k,l+1)}{R(k,l)}=1.\]

rows.title: Erdős problem #1019
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: A planar graph on $n$ vertices with $3n-6$ edges (the maximum possible) is called saturated. Does every graph on $n$ vertices with $\lfloor n^2/4\rfloor+\lfloor \frac{n+1}{2}\rfloor$ edges contain a saturated planar graph with $>3$ vertices?
rows.source_text: A planar graph on $n$ vertices with $3n-6$ edges (the maximum possible) is called saturated. Does every graph on $n$ vertices with $\lfloor n^2/4\rfloor+\lfloor \frac{n+1}{2}\rfloor$ edges contain a saturated planar graph with $>3$ vertices?
rows.class_notes: Saturated planar graph is defined in the statement; exact edge threshold, finite.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1020
rows.prize: FALSIFIABLE Open, but could be disproved with a finite counterexample.
rows.statement_text: Let $f(n;r,k)$ be the maximal number of edges in an $r$-uniform hypergraph which contains no set of $k$ many independent edges. For all $r\geq 3$,\[f(n;r,k)=\max\left(\binom{rk-1}{r}, \binom{n}{r}-\binom{n-k+1}{r}\right).\]
rows.source_text: Let $f(n;r,k)$ be the maximal number of edges in an $r$-uniform hypergraph which contains no set of $k$ many independent edges. For all $r\geq 3$,\[f(n;r,k)=\max\left(\binom{rk-1}{r}, \binom{n}{r}-\binom{n-k+1}{r}\right).\]
rows.class_notes: This is the Erdos matching conjecture; exact finite formula.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1021
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Is it true that, for every $k\geq 3$, there is a constant $c_k>0$ such that\[\mathrm{ex}(n,G_k) \ll n^{3/2-c_k},\]where $G_k$ is the bipartite graph between $\{y_1,\ldots,y_k\}$ and $\{z_1,\ldots,z_{\binom{k}{2}}\}$, with each $z_j$ joined to a unique pair of $y_i$?
rows.source_text: Is it true that, for every $k\geq 3$, there is a constant $c_k>0$ such that\[\mathrm{ex}(n,G_k) \ll n^{3/2-c_k},\]where $G_k$ is the bipartite graph between $\{y_1,\ldots,y_k\}$ and $\{z_1,\ldots,z_{\binom{k}{2}}\}$, with each $z_j$ joined to a unique pair of $y_i$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #1024
rows.prize: SOLVED This has been resolved in some other way than a proof or disproof.
rows.statement_text: Let $f(n)$ be such that every $3$-uniform linear hypergraph on $n$ vertices contains an independent set on $f(n)$ vertices. Estimate $f(n)$.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1028
rows.prize: SOLVED (LEAN) This has been resolved in some other way than a proof or disproof, and that resolution verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: Let\[H(n)=\min_f \max_{X\subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}} \left\lvert \sum_{x\neq y\in X} f(x,y)\right\rvert,\]where $f$ ranges over all functions $f:X^2\to \{-1,1\}$. Estimate $H(n)$.
rows.source_text: Let\[H(n)=\min_f \max_{X\subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}} \left\lvert \sum_{x\neq y\in X} f(x,y)\right\rvert,\]where $f$ ranges over all functions $f:X^2\to \{-1,1\}$. Estimate $H(n)$.
rows.defer_reason: Pure 'estimate H(n)' with no concrete conjectured bound
rows.class_notes: Edge +-1-weighting of K_n (graph discrepancy), so in scope, but the ask is open-ended.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1029
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation. - $100
rows.statement_text: If $R(k)$ is the Ramsey number for $K_k$, the minimal $n$ such that every $2$-colouring of the edges of $K_n$ contains a monochromatic copy of $K_k$, then\[\frac{R(k)}{k2^{k/2}}\to \infty.\]

rows.title: Erdős problem #1030
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $R(k,l)$ be the usual Ramsey number: the smallest $n$ such that if the edges of $K_n$ are coloured red and blue then there exists either a red $K_k$ or a blue $K_l$. Prove the existence of some $c>0$ such that\[\lim_{k\to \infty}\frac{R(k+1,k)}{R(k,k)}> 1+c.\]

rows.title: Erdős problem #1031
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: If $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices which contains no trivial (empty or complete) subgraph on $\geq 10\log n$ many vertices, then must $G$ contain an induced non-trivial regular subgraph on $\gg \log n$ many vertices?

rows.title: Erdős problem #1032
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: We say that a graph is $4$-chromatic critical if it has chromatic number $4$, and removing any edge decreases the chromatic number to $3$. Is there, for arbitrarily large $n$, a $4$-chromatic critical graph on $n$ vertices with minimum degree $\gg n$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #1033
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $h(n)$ be such that every graph on $n$ vertices with $>n^2/4$ many edges contains a triangle whose vertices have degrees summing to at least $h(n)$. Estimate $h(n)$. In particular, is it true that\[h(n)\geq (2(\sqrt{3}-1)-o(1))n?\]

rows.class_notes: 'Estimate' framing, but the named sub-question h(n)>=(2(sqrt3-1)-o(1))n is precise.

rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices with $>n^2/4$ many edges. Must there be a triangle $T$ in $G$ and vertices $y_1,\ldots,y_t$, where $t>(\frac{1}{2}-o(1))n$, such that every vertex is joined to at least two vertices of $T$?

rows.class_notes: Wording slightly garbled ('every vertex' vs the role of y_1..y_t); intended meaning recoverable.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1035
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Is there a constant $c>0$ such that every graph on $2^n$ vertices with minimum degree $>(1-c)2^n$ contains the $n$-dimensional hypercube $Q_n$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #1036

rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices which does not contain a trivial (empty or complete) graph on more than $c\log n$ vertices. Must $G$ contain at least $2^{\Omega_c(n)}$ many induced subgraphs which are not pairwise isomorphic?

rows.title: Erdős problem #1037
rows.prize: DISPROVED (LEAN) This has been solved in the negative and the proof verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices in which every degree occurs at most twice, and the number of distinct degree is $>(\frac{1}{2}+\epsilon)n$. Must $G$ contain a trivial (empty or complete) subgraph of size 'much larger' than $\log n$?

rows.defer_reason: Threshold 'much larger' than log n is informal, not a single proposition
rows.class_notes: Could be read as omega(log n), but the quotes signal deliberate vagueness.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1066
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph given by $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, where any two distinct points are at least distance $1$ apart, and we draw an edge between two points if they are distance $1$ apart. Let $g(n)$ be maximal such that any such graph always has an independent set on at least $g(n)$ vertices. Estimate $g(n)$, or perhaps $\lim \frac{g(n)}{n}$.
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph given by $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, where any two distinct points are at least distance $1$ apart, and we draw an edge between two points if they are distance $1$ apart. Let $g(n)$ be maximal such that any such graph always has an independent set on at least $g(n)$ vertices. Estimate $g(n)$, or perhaps $\lim \frac{g(n)}{n}$.
rows.defer_reason: out-of-scope-v2: separated unit-distance point sets in R^2 (discrete geometry); additionally 'estimate g(n), or perhaps lim g(n)/n' fixes no single proposition (B's B4 point).
rows.class_notes: Resolution: B5 over B's B4 — the object itself is real-plane geometric, so it is parked on scope grounds regardless of the estimation phrasing.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1077

If $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices with at least $n^{1+\alpha}$ edges, then must $G$ contain a $D$-balanced subgraph on $m>n^{1-\alpha}$ vertices with at least $\epsilon m^{1+\alpha}$ edges?

rows.title: Erdős problem #108

rows.statement_text: For every $r\geq 4$ and $k\geq 2$ is there some finite $f(k,r)$ such that every graph of chromatic number $\geq f(k,r)$ contains a subgraph of girth $\geq r$ and chromatic number $\geq k$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #1091
rows.prize: SOLVED This has been resolved in some other way than a proof or disproof.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a $K_4$-free graph with chromatic number $4$. Must $G$ contain an odd cycle with at least two diagonals? More generally, is there some $f(r)\to \infty$ such that every graph with chromatic number $4$, in which every subgraph on $\leq r$ vertices has chromatic number $\leq 3$, contains an odd cycle with at least $f(r)$ diagonals?

Is it true that $f_2(n) \gg n$? More generally, is $f_r(n)\gg_r n$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #1105
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: The anti-Ramsey number $\mathrm{AR}(n,G)$ is the maximum possible number of colours in which the edges of $K_n$ can be coloured without creating a rainbow copy of $G$ (i.e. one in which all edges have different colours). Let $C_k$ be the cycle on $k$ vertices. Is it true that\[\mathrm{AR}(n,C_k)=\left(\frac{k-2}{2}+\frac{1}{k-1}\right)n+O(1)?\]Let $P_k$ be the path on $k$ vertices and $\ell=\lfloor\frac{k-1}{2}\rfloor$. If $n\geq k\geq 5$ then is $\mathrm{AR}(n,P_k)$ equal to\[\max\left(\binom{k-2}{2}+1, \binom{\ell-1}{2}+(\ell-1)(n-\ell+1)+\epsilon\right)\]where $\epsilon=1$ if $k$ is odd and $\epsilon=2$ otherwise?
rows.source_text: The anti-Ramsey number $\mathrm{AR}(n,G)$ is the maximum possible number of colours in which the edges of $K_n$ can be coloured without creating a rainbow copy of $G$ (i.e. one in which all edges have different colours). Let $C_k$ be the cycle on $k$ vertices. Is it true that\[\mathrm{AR}(n,C_k)=\left(\frac{k-2}{2}+\frac{1}{k-1}\right)n+O(1)?\]Let $P_k$ be the path on $k$ vertices and $\ell=\lfloor\frac{k-1}{2}\rfloor$. If $n\geq k\geq 5$ then is $\mathrm{AR}(n,P_k)$ equal to\[\max\left(\binom{k-2}{2}+1, \binom{\ell-1}{2}+(\ell-1)(n-\ell+1)+\epsilon\right)\]where $\epsilon=1$ if $k$ is odd and $\epsilon=2$ otherwise?
rows.class_notes: Two precise sub-questions; cycle part has an O(1) error term, path part is exact for n>=k>=5.

rows.title: Erdős problem #111
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: If $G$ is a graph let $h_G(n)$ be defined such that any subgraph of $G$ on $n$ vertices can be made bipartite after deleting at most $h_G(n)$ edges. What is the behaviour of $h_G(n)$? Is it true that $h_G(n)/n\to \infty$ for every graph $G$ with chromatic number $\aleph_1$?
rows.source_text: If $G$ is a graph let $h_G(n)$ be defined such that any subgraph of $G$ on $n$ vertices can be made bipartite after deleting at most $h_G(n)$ edges. What is the behaviour of $h_G(n)$? Is it true that $h_G(n)/n\to \infty$ for every graph $G$ with chromatic number $\aleph_1$?
rows.class_notes: First ask (behaviour of h_G(n)) is vague; the yes/no question h_G(n)/n -> infinity for chromatic number aleph_1 graphs is the target and needs infinite graphs.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1111
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: If $G$ is a finite graph and $A,B$ are disjoint sets of vertices then we call $A,B$ anticomplete if there are no edges between $A$ and $B$. If $t,c\geq 1$ then there exists $d\geq 1$ such that if $\chi(G)\geq d$ and $\omega(G)<t$ then there are anticomplete sets $A,B$ with $\chi(A)\geq \chi(B)\geq c$.
rows.source_text: If $G$ is a finite graph and $A,B$ are disjoint sets of vertices then we call $A,B$ anticomplete if there are no edges between $A$ and $B$. If $t,c\geq 1$ then there exists $d\geq 1$ such that if $\chi(G)\geq d$ and $\omega(G)<t$ then there are anticomplete sets $A,B$ with $\chi(A)\geq \chi(B)\geq c$.
rows.class_notes: Anticomplete pairs with large chromatic number in clique-bounded graphs; fully finite standard vocabulary.

rows.statement_text: Let $k=k(n,m)$ be minimal such that any directed graph on $k$ vertices must contain either an independent set of size $n$ or a transitive tournament of size $m$. Determine $k(n,m)$.
