rows.title: Erdős problem #1155
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Construct a random graph on $n$ vertices in the following way: begin with the complete graph $K_n$. At each stage, choose uniformly a random triangle in the graph and delete all the edges of this triangle. Repeat until the graph is triangle-free. Describe the typical parameters and structure of such a graph. In particular, if $f(n)$ is the number of edges remaining, then is it true that\[\mathbb{E}f(n)\asymp n^{3/2}\]and that $f(n) \ll n^{3/2}$ almost surely?
rows.source_text: Construct a random graph on $n$ vertices in the following way: begin with the complete graph $K_n$. At each stage, choose uniformly a random triangle in the graph and delete all the edges of this triangle. Repeat until the graph is triangle-free. Describe the typical parameters and structure of such a graph. In particular, if $f(n)$ is the number of edges remaining, then is it true that\[\mathbb{E}f(n)\asymp n^{3/2}\]and that $f(n) \ll n^{3/2}$ almost surely?
rows.class_notes: 'Describe typical parameters' is vague, but the E f(n) asymp n^{3/2} question for the triangle removal process is precise; needs probability layer.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1156
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a random graph on $n$ vertices, in which every edge is included independently with probability $1/2$. Is there some constant $C$ such that that chromatic number $\chi(G)$ is, almost surely, concentrated on at most $C$ values? Is it true that, if $\omega(n)\to \infty$ sufficiently slowly, then for every function $f(n)$\[\mathbb{P}(\lvert\chi(G)-f(n)\rvert<\omega(n))<1/2\]if $n$ is sufficiently large?

rows.class_notes: Non-concentration of chi(G(n,1/2)); needs probability/asymptotics vocabulary.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1178
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: For $r\geq 3$ let $d_r(e)$ be the minimal $d$ such that\[\mathrm{ex}_r(n,\mathcal{F})=o(n^2),\]where $\mathcal{F}$ is the family of $r$-uniform hypergraphs on $d$ vertices with $e$ edges. Prove that\[d_r(e)=(r-2)e+3\]for all $r,e\geq 3$.

rows.class_notes: Brown-Erdos-Sos type threshold d_r(e)=(r-2)e+3; o(n^2) condition needs asymptotics.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1182
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $f(n)$ be maximal such that there is a connected graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and $f(n)$ edges such that\[R(K_3,G)= 2n-1.\]Let $F(n)$ be maximal such that every connected graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and $\leq F(n)$ edges has\[R(K_3,G)= 2n-1.\]Estimate $f(n)$ and $F(n)$. In particular, is it true that $F(n)/n\to \infty$?
rows.source_text: Let $f(n)$ be maximal such that there is a connected graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and $f(n)$ edges such that\[R(K_3,G)= 2n-1.\]Let $F(n)$ be maximal such that every connected graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and $\leq F(n)$ edges has\[R(K_3,G)= 2n-1.\]Estimate $f(n)$ and $F(n)$. In particular, is it true that $F(n)/n\to \infty$?
rows.class_notes: 'Estimate f,F' is vague but F(n)/n -> infinity is a precise yes/no target; R(K_3,G)=2n-1 goodness question.

rows.title: Erdős problem #1216
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: A tournament is a complete directed graph. Let $f(n)$ be such that every tournament on $n$ vertices contains a transitive tournament on $f(n)$ vertices (i.e. one such that if $i\to j\to k$ then $i\to k$). Is it true that $f(n)=\lfloor \log_2 n\rfloor +1$?
rows.source_text: A tournament is a complete directed graph. Let $f(n)$ be such that every tournament on $n$ vertices contains a transitive tournament on $f(n)$ vertices (i.e. one such that if $i\to j\to k$ then $i\to k$). Is it true that $f(n)=\lfloor \log_2 n\rfloor +1$?
rows.class_notes: Solved (disproved); exact conjecture f(n)=floor(log2 n)+1 for largest guaranteed transitive subtournament, fully finite.

rows.title: Erdős problem #127
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $f(m)$ be maximal such that every graph with $m$ edges must contain a bipartite graph with\[\geq \frac{m}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{8m+1}-1}{8}+f(m)\]edges. Is there an infinite sequence of $m_i$ such that $f(m_i)\to \infty$?
rows.source_text: Let $f(m)$ be maximal such that every graph with $m$ edges must contain a bipartite graph with\[\geq \frac{m}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{8m+1}-1}{8}+f(m)\]edges. Is there an infinite sequence of $m_i$ such that $f(m_i)\to \infty$?
rows.class_notes: Max-cut surplus beyond m/2 + sqrt term; asks for a sequence with f(m_i) -> infinity.

rows.title: Erdős problem #128
rows.prize: FALSIFIABLE Open, but could be disproved with a finite counterexample. - $250
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices such that every induced subgraph on $\geq \lfloor n/2\rfloor$ vertices has more than $n^2/50$ edges. Must $G$ contain a triangle?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices such that every induced subgraph on $\geq \lfloor n/2\rfloor$ vertices has more than $n^2/50$ edges. Must $G$ contain a triangle?
rows.class_notes: Concrete n^2/50 local-density triangle question; fully finite.

rows.title: Erdős problem #129
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $R(n;k,r)$ be the smallest $N$ such that if the edges of $K_N$ are $r$-coloured then there is a set of $n$ vertices which does not contain a copy of $K_k$ in at least one of the $r$ colours. Prove that there is a constant $C=C(r)>1$ such that\[R(n;3,r) < C^{\sqrt{n}}.\]
rows.source_text: Let $R(n;k,r)$ be the smallest $N$ such that if the edges of $K_N$ are $r$-coloured then there is a set of $n$ vertices which does not contain a copy of $K_k$ in at least one of the $r$ colours. Prove that there is a constant $C=C(r)>1$ such that\[R(n;3,r) < C^{\sqrt{n}}.\]
rows.class_notes: Size-Ramsey-type quantity R(n;3,r) < C^sqrt(n); existential constant wrapper, otherwise finite.

rows.title: Erdős problem #130
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $A\subset\mathbb{R}^2$ be an infinite set which contains no three points on a line and no four points on a circle. Consider the graph with vertices the points in $A$, where two vertices are joined by an edge if and only if they are an integer distance apart. How large can the chromatic number and clique number of this graph be? In particular, can the chromatic number be infinite?
rows.source_text: Let $A\subset\mathbb{R}^2$ be an infinite set which contains no three points on a line and no four points on a circle. Consider the graph with vertices the points in $A$, where two vertices are joined by an edge if and only if they are an integer distance apart. How large can the chromatic number and clique number of this graph be? In particular, can the chromatic number be infinite?
rows.defer_reason: out-of-scope-v2: integer-distance graph on infinite planar point sets with no-3-collinear/no-4-concyclic constraints — real-plane geometry (lines, circles, distances).
rows.class_notes: Resolution: sided with A on scope; B's observation stands that the 'in particular, can chi be infinite?' sub-question is a clean yes/no carve-out if a real-plane plus infinite-graph layer ever lands.

rows.title: Erdős problem #133
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: Let $f(n)$ be minimal such that every triangle-free graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and diameter $2$ contains a vertex with degree $\geq f(n)$. What is the order of growth of $f(n)$? Does $f(n)/\sqrt{n}\to \infty$?
rows.source_text: Let $f(n)$ be minimal such that every triangle-free graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and diameter $2$ contains a vertex with degree $\geq f(n)$. What is the order of growth of $f(n)$? Does $f(n)/\sqrt{n}\to \infty$?
rows.class_notes: 'Order of growth' is vague but f(n)/sqrt(n) -> infinity is a precise target; solved.

rows.title: Erdős problem #134
rows.prize: PROVED (LEAN) This has been solved in the affirmative and the proof verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: Let $\epsilon,\delta>0$ and $n$ be sufficiently large in terms of $\epsilon$ and $\delta$. Let $G$ be a triangle-free graph on $n$ vertices with maximum degree $<n^{1/2-\epsilon}$. Can $G$ be made into a triangle-free graph with diameter $2$ by adding at most $\delta n^2$ edges?
rows.source_text: Let $\epsilon,\delta>0$ and $n$ be sufficiently large in terms of $\epsilon$ and $\delta$. Let $G$ be a triangle-free graph on $n$ vertices with maximum degree $<n^{1/2-\epsilon}$. Can $G$ be made into a triangle-free graph with diameter $2$ by adding at most $\delta n^2$ edges?
rows.class_notes: Solved; epsilon/delta quantifiers with real exponent n^{1/2-eps} need the asymptotics idiom.

rows.title: Erdős problem #136
rows.prize: SOLVED This has been resolved in some other way than a proof or disproof.
rows.statement_text: Let $f(n)$ be the smallest number of colours required to colour the edges of $K_n$ such that every $K_4$ contains at least 5 colours. Determine the size of $f(n)$.
rows.source_text: Let $f(n)$ be the smallest number of colours required to colour the edges of $K_n$ such that every $K_4$ contains at least 5 colours. Determine the size of $f(n)$.
rows.defer_reason: Asks to 'determine the size of f(n)' with no stated conjectured value; not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Local (4,5)-coloring number of K_n; solved as a determination problem.

rows.title: Erdős problem #146
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation. - $500
rows.statement_text: If $H$ is bipartite and is $r$-degenerate, that is, every induced subgraph of $H$ has minimum degree $\leq r$, then\[\mathrm{ex}(n;H) \ll n^{2-1/r}.\]
rows.source_text: If $H$ is bipartite and is $r$-degenerate, that is, every induced subgraph of $H$ has minimum degree $\leq r$, then\[\mathrm{ex}(n;H) \ll n^{2-1/r}.\]
rows.class_notes: Erdos degenerate bipartite Turan conjecture: ex(n;H) << n^{2-1/r} for r-degenerate bipartite H.

rows.title: Erdős problem #147
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative. - $500
rows.statement_text: If $H$ is bipartite with minimum degree $r$ then there exists $\epsilon=\epsilon(H)>0$ such that\[\mathrm{ex}(n;H) \gg n^{2-\frac{1}{r-1}+\epsilon}.\]
rows.source_text: If $H$ is bipartite with minimum degree $r$ then there exists $\epsilon=\epsilon(H)>0$ such that\[\mathrm{ex}(n;H) \gg n^{2-\frac{1}{r-1}+\epsilon}.\]
rows.class_notes: Solved; lower-bound counterpart to erdos_146 with existential epsilon(H).

rows.title: Erdős problem #149
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: The strong chromatic index of a graph $G$, denoted by $\mathrm{sq}(G)$, is the minimum $k$ such that the edges of $G$ can be partitioned into $k$ sets of 'strongly independent' edges, that is, such that the subgraph of $G$ induced by each set is the union of vertex-disjoint edges. Is it true that, for any graph $G$ with maximum degree $\Delta$,\[\mathrm{sq}(G)\leq\frac{5}{4}\Delta^2?\]
rows.source_text: The strong chromatic index of a graph $G$, denoted by $\mathrm{sq}(G)$, is the minimum $k$ such that the edges of $G$ can be partitioned into $k$ sets of 'strongly independent' edges, that is, such that the subgraph of $G$ induced by each set is the union of vertex-disjoint edges. Is it true that, for any graph $G$ with maximum degree $\Delta$,\[\mathrm{sq}(G)\leq\frac{5}{4}\Delta^2?\]
rows.class_notes: Erdos-Nesetril strong chromatic index conjecture sq(G) <= (5/4)Delta^2; strong independence is defined in the statement.

rows.title: Erdős problem #150
rows.prize: PROVED (LEAN) This has been solved in the affirmative and the proof verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: A minimal cut of a graph is a minimal set of vertices whose removal disconnects the graph. Let $c(n)$ be the maximum number of minimal cuts a graph on $n$ vertices can have. Does $c(n)^{1/n}\to \alpha$ for some $\alpha <2$?
rows.source_text: A minimal cut of a graph is a minimal set of vertices whose removal disconnects the graph. Let $c(n)$ be the maximum number of minimal cuts a graph on $n$ vertices can have. Does $c(n)^{1/n}\to \alpha$ for some $\alpha <2$?
rows.class_notes: Limit of c(n)^{1/n} for counting minimal vertex cuts; needs real limits.

rows.title: Erdős problem #151
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: For a graph $G$ let $\tau(G)$ denote the minimal number of vertices that include at least one from each maximal clique of $G$ on at least two vertices (sometimes called the clique transversal number). Let $H(n)$ be maximal such that every triangle-free graph on $n$ vertices contains an independent set on $H(n)$ vertices. If $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices then is\[\tau(G)\leq n-H(n)?\]
rows.source_text: For a graph $G$ let $\tau(G)$ denote the minimal number of vertices that include at least one from each maximal clique of $G$ on at least two vertices (sometimes called the clique transversal number). Let $H(n)$ be maximal such that every triangle-free graph on $n$ vertices contains an independent set on $H(n)$ vertices. If $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices then is\[\tau(G)\leq n-H(n)?\]
rows.class_notes: tau(G) <= n - H(n) with H(n) the triangle-free independence guarantee; all quantities finite and defined in the statement.

rows.title: Erdős problem #159
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation. - $100
rows.statement_text: There exists some constant $c>0$ such that $$R(C_4,K_n) \ll n^{2-c}.$$
rows.source_text: There exists some constant $c>0$ such that $$R(C_4,K_n) \ll n^{2-c}.$$
rows.class_notes: R(C_4,K_n) << n^{2-c} for some c>0.

rows.title: Erdős problem #163
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: For any $d\geq 1$ if $H$ is a graph such that every subgraph contains a vertex of degree at most $d$ then $R(H)\ll_d n$.
rows.source_text: For any $d\geq 1$ if $H$ is a graph such that every subgraph contains a vertex of degree at most $d$ then $R(H)\ll_d n$.

rows.title: Erdős problem #165

rows.source_text: Give an asymptotic formula for $R(3,k)$.

rows.title: Erdős problem #166
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative. - $250
rows.statement_text: Prove that\[R(4,k) \gg \frac{k^3}{(\log k)^{O(1)}}.\]
rows.source_text: Prove that\[R(4,k) \gg \frac{k^3}{(\log k)^{O(1)}}.\]
rows.class_notes: Solved (Mattheus-Verstraete); R(4,k) >> k^3/(log k)^{O(1)}.

rows.title: Erdős problem #167
rows.prize: FALSIFIABLE Open, but could be disproved with a finite counterexample.
rows.statement_text: If $G$ is a graph with at most $k$ edge disjoint triangles then can $G$ be made triangle-free after removing at most $2k$ edges?
rows.source_text: If $G$ is a graph with at most $k$ edge disjoint triangles then can $G$ be made triangle-free after removing at most $2k$ edges?
rows.class_notes: This is Tuza's conjecture (triangle cover <= 2 x triangle packing); fully finite.

rows.title: Erdős problem #180
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: If $\mathcal{F}$ is a finite set of finite graphs then $\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})$ is the maximum number of edges a graph on $n$ vertices can have without containing any subgraphs from $\mathcal{F}$. Note that it is trivial that $\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})\leq \mathrm{ex}(n;G)$ for every $G\in\mathcal{F}$. Is it true that, for every $\mathcal{F}$, there exists $G\in\mathcal{F}$ such that\[\mathrm{ex}(n;G)\ll_{\mathcal{F}}\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})?\]
rows.source_text: If $\mathcal{F}$ is a finite set of finite graphs then $\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})$ is the maximum number of edges a graph on $n$ vertices can have without containing any subgraphs from $\mathcal{F}$. Note that it is trivial that $\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})\leq \mathrm{ex}(n;G)$ for every $G\in\mathcal{F}$. Is it true that, for every $\mathcal{F}$, there exists $G\in\mathcal{F}$ such that\[\mathrm{ex}(n;G)\ll_{\mathcal{F}}\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})?\]

rows.title: Erdős problem #181
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $Q_n$ be the $n$-dimensional hypercube graph (so that $Q_n$ has $2^n$ vertices and $n2^{n-1}$ edges). Prove that\[R(Q_n) \ll 2^n.\]
rows.source_text: Let $Q_n$ be the $n$-dimensional hypercube graph (so that $Q_n$ has $2^n$ vertices and $n2^{n-1}$ edges). Prove that\[R(Q_n) \ll 2^n.\]
rows.class_notes: Burr-Erdos hypercube Ramsey conjecture R(Q_n) << 2^n.

rows.title: Erdős problem #182
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative. - $100
rows.statement_text: Let $k\geq 3$. What is the maximum number of edges that a graph on $n$ vertices can contain if it does not have a $k$-regular subgraph? Is it $\ll n^{1+o(1)}$?
rows.source_text: Let $k\geq 3$. What is the maximum number of edges that a graph on $n$ vertices can contain if it does not have a $k$-regular subgraph? Is it $\ll n^{1+o(1)}$?
rows.class_notes: Solved; 'what is the maximum' framing but the n^{1+o(1)} yes/no sub-question is the precise target.

rows.title: Erdős problem #183
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation. - $250
rows.statement_text: Let $R(3;k)$ be the minimal $n$ such that if the edges of $K_n$ are coloured with $k$ colours then there must exist a monochromatic triangle. Determine\[\lim_{k\to \infty}R(3;k)^{1/k}.\]
rows.source_text: Let $R(3;k)$ be the minimal $n$ such that if the edges of $K_n$ are coloured with $k$ colours then there must exist a monochromatic triangle. Determine\[\lim_{k\to \infty}R(3;k)^{1/k}.\]
rows.defer_reason: Asks to 'determine' lim R(3;k)^{1/k} with no conjectured value; not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Multicolour triangle Ramsey growth rate; even existence of the limit is open.

rows.title: Erdős problem #184
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Any graph on $n$ vertices can be decomposed into $O(n)$ many edge-disjoint cycles and edges.
rows.source_text: Any graph on $n$ vertices can be decomposed into $O(n)$ many edge-disjoint cycles and edges.
rows.class_notes: Erdos-Gallai decomposition conjecture: O(n) edge-disjoint cycles and edges; needs only an existential constant wrapper.

rows.title: Erdős problem #19

rows.source_text: If $G$ is an edge-disjoint union of $n$ copies of $K_n$ then is $\chi(G)=n$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #22
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $\epsilon>0$ and $n$ be sufficiently large depending on $\epsilon$. Is there a graph on $n$ vertices with $\geq n^2/8$ many edges which contains no $K_4$ such that the largest independent set has size at most $\epsilon n$?
rows.source_text: Let $\epsilon>0$ and $n$ be sufficiently large depending on $\epsilon$. Is there a graph on $n$ vertices with $\geq n^2/8$ many edges which contains no $K_4$ such that the largest independent set has size at most $\epsilon n$?
rows.class_notes: Solved Ramsey-Turan question: K_4-free, n^2/8 edges, independence at most eps*n; eps/large-n quantifier wrapper.

rows.title: Erdős problem #23

rows.class_notes: Erdos' triangle-free bipartization conjecture: 5n vertices, delete at most n^2 edges; fully finite exact statement.

rows.source_text: Does every triangle-free graph on $5n$ vertices contain at most $n^5$ copies of $C_5$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #426
rows.prize: DISPROVED (LEAN) This has been solved in the negative and the proof verified in Lean. - $25
rows.statement_text: We say $H$ is a unique subgraph of $G$ if there is exactly one way to find $H$ as a subgraph (not necessarily induced) of $G$. Is there a graph on $n$ vertices with\[\gg \frac{2^{\binom{n}{2}}}{n!}\]many distinct unique subgraphs?
rows.source_text: We say $H$ is a unique subgraph of $G$ if there is exactly one way to find $H$ as a subgraph (not necessarily induced) of $G$. Is there a graph on $n$ vertices with\[\gg \frac{2^{\binom{n}{2}}}{n!}\]many distinct unique subgraphs?
rows.class_notes: Existence of graphs with >> 2^{C(n,2)}/n! unique subgraphs; solved, feeds right-polarity proof.

rows.title: Erdős problem #500
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation. - $500
rows.statement_text: What is $\mathrm{ex}_3(n,K_4^3)$? That is, the largest number of $3$-edges which can placed on $n$ vertices so that there exists no $K_4^3$, a set of 4 vertices which is covered by all 4 possible $3$-edges.
rows.source_text: What is $\mathrm{ex}_3(n,K_4^3)$? That is, the largest number of $3$-edges which can placed on $n$ vertices so that there exists no $K_4^3$, a set of 4 vertices which is covered by all 4 possible $3$-edges.
rows.defer_reason: 'What is ex_3(n,K_4^3)?' — determination question, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Turan's K_4^3 problem; underlying ex_3 vocabulary is finite but the record only asks to determine the value.

rows.title: Erdős problem #518

rows.statement_text: Is it true that, in any two-colouring of the edges of $K_n$, there exist $\sqrt{n}$ monochromatic paths, all of the same colour, which cover all vertices?
rows.source_text: Is it true that, in any two-colouring of the edges of $K_n$, there exist $\sqrt{n}$ monochromatic paths, all of the same colour, which cover all vertices?

rows.title: Erdős problem #544
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Show that\[R(3,k+1)-R(3,k)\to\infty\]as $k\to \infty$. Similarly, prove or disprove that\[R(3,k+1)-R(3,k)=o(k).\]

rows.title: Erdős problem #545
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph with $m$ edges and no isolated vertices. Is the Ramsey number $R(G)$ maximised when $G$ is 'as complete as possible'? That is, if $m=\binom{n}{2}+t$ edges with $0\leq t<n$ then is\[R(G)\leq R(H),\]where $H$ is the graph formed by connecting a new vertex to $t$ of the vertices of $K_n$?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph with $m$ edges and no isolated vertices. Is the Ramsey number $R(G)$ maximised when $G$ is 'as complete as possible'? That is, if $m=\binom{n}{2}+t$ edges with $0\leq t<n$ then is\[R(G)\leq R(H),\]where $H$ is the graph formed by connecting a new vertex to $t$ of the vertices of $K_n$?
rows.class_notes: Exact comparison R(G) <= R(H) with H explicitly constructed; fully finite.

rows.title: Erdős problem #546

rows.title: Erdős problem #550
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $m_1\leq\cdots\leq m_k$ and $n$ be sufficiently large. If $T$ is a tree on $n$ vertices and $G$ is the complete multipartite graph with vertex class sizes $m_1,\ldots,m_k$ then prove that\[R(T,G)\leq (\chi(G)-1)(R(T,K_{m_1,m_2})-1)+m_1.\]
rows.source_text: Let $m_1\leq\cdots\leq m_k$ and $n$ be sufficiently large. If $T$ is a tree on $n$ vertices and $G$ is the complete multipartite graph with vertex class sizes $m_1,\ldots,m_k$ then prove that\[R(T,G)\leq (\chi(G)-1)(R(T,K_{m_1,m_2})-1)+m_1.\]

rows.title: Erdős problem #551
rows.prize: DECIDABLE Resolved up to a finite check.
rows.statement_text: Prove that\[R(C_k,K_n)=(k-1)(n-1)+1\]for $k\geq n\geq 3$ (except when $n=k=3$).
rows.source_text: Prove that\[R(C_k,K_n)=(k-1)(n-1)+1\]for $k\geq n\geq 3$ (except when $n=k=3$).
rows.class_notes: Exact cycle-vs-clique Ramsey formula; solved.

rows.title: Erdős problem #552
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation. - $100
rows.statement_text: Determine the Ramsey number\[R(C_4,S_n),\]where $S_n=K_{1,n}$ is the star on $n+1$ vertices. In particular, is it true that, for any $c>0$, there are infinitely many $n$ such that\[R(C_4,S_n)\leq n+\sqrt{n}-c?\]
rows.source_text: Determine the Ramsey number\[R(C_4,S_n),\]where $S_n=K_{1,n}$ is the star on $n+1$ vertices. In particular, is it true that, for any $c>0$, there are infinitely many $n$ such that\[R(C_4,S_n)\leq n+\sqrt{n}-c?\]
rows.class_notes: Anchor on the precise 'in particular' question (infinitely many n with R(C_4,S_n) <= n+sqrt(n)-c); the 'determine' framing alone would be B4.

rows.title: Erdős problem #553
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $R(3,3,n)$ denote the smallest integer $m$ such that if we $3$-colour the edges of $K_m$ then there is either a monochromatic triangle in one of the first two colours or a monochromatic $K_n$ in the third colour. Define $R(3,n)$ similarly but with two colours. Show that\[\frac{R(3,3,n)}{R(3,n)}\to \infty\]as $n\to \infty$.
rows.source_text: Let $R(3,3,n)$ denote the smallest integer $m$ such that if we $3$-colour the edges of $K_m$ then there is either a monochromatic triangle in one of the first two colours or a monochromatic $K_n$ in the third colour. Define $R(3,n)$ similarly but with two colours. Show that\[\frac{R(3,3,n)}{R(3,n)}\to \infty\]as $n\to \infty$.
rows.class_notes: Ratio tends to infinity; multicolour Ramsey defined in the record.

rows.title: Erdős problem #559
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: Let $\hat{R}(G)$ denote the size Ramsey number, the minimal number of edges $m$ such that there is a graph $H$ with $m$ edges that is Ramsey for $G$. If $G$ has $n$ vertices and maximum degree $d$ then prove that\[\hat{R}(G)\ll_d n.\]

rows.title: Erdős problem #561
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $\hat{R}(G)$ denote the size Ramsey number, the minimal number of edges $m$ such that there is a graph $H$ with $m$ edges such that in any $2$-colouring of the edges of $H$ there is a monochromatic copy of $G$. Let $F_1$ and $F_2$ be the union of stars. More precisely, let $F_1=\cup_{i\leq s} K_{1,n_i}$ and $F_2=\cup_{j\leq t} K_{1,m_j}$ with $n_1\geq \cdots \geq n_s\geq 1$ and $m_1\geq \cdots \geq m_t\geq 1$. Prove that\[\hat{R}(F_1,F_2) = \sum_{2\leq k\leq s+t}l_k\]where\[l_k=\max\{n_i+m_j-1 : i+j=k\}.\]
rows.source_text: Let $\hat{R}(G)$ denote the size Ramsey number, the minimal number of edges $m$ such that there is a graph $H$ with $m$ edges such that in any $2$-colouring of the edges of $H$ there is a monochromatic copy of $G$. Let $F_1$ and $F_2$ be the union of stars. More precisely, let $F_1=\cup_{i\leq s} K_{1,n_i}$ and $F_2=\cup_{j\leq t} K_{1,m_j}$ with $n_1\geq \cdots \geq n_s\geq 1$ and $m_1\geq \cdots \geq m_t\geq 1$. Prove that\[\hat{R}(F_1,F_2) = \sum_{2\leq k\leq s+t}l_k\]where\[l_k=\max\{n_i+m_j-1 : i+j=k\}.\]
rows.class_notes: Exact size-Ramsey formula for star forests; all quantities finite and defined in the record.

rows.title: Erdős problem #562
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $R_r(n)$ denote the $r$-uniform hypergraph Ramsey number: the minimal $m$ such that if we $2$-colour all edges of the complete $r$-uniform hypergraph on $m$ vertices then there must be some monochromatic copy of the complete $r$-uniform hypergraph on $n$ vertices. Prove that, for $r\geq 3$,\[\log_{r-1} R_r(n) \asymp_r n,\]where $\log_{r-1}$ denotes the $(r-1)$-fold iterated logarithm. That is, does $R_r(n)$ grow like\[2^{2^{\cdots n}}\]where the tower of exponentials has height $r-1$?
rows.source_text: Let $R_r(n)$ denote the $r$-uniform hypergraph Ramsey number: the minimal $m$ such that if we $2$-colour all edges of the complete $r$-uniform hypergraph on $m$ vertices then there must be some monochromatic copy of the complete $r$-uniform hypergraph on $n$ vertices. Prove that, for $r\geq 3$,\[\log_{r-1} R_r(n) \asymp_r n,\]where $\log_{r-1}$ denotes the $(r-1)$-fold iterated logarithm. That is, does $R_r(n)$ grow like\[2^{2^{\cdots n}}\]where the tower of exponentials has height $r-1$?
rows.class_notes: Tower-growth asymp_r with iterated logarithms.

rows.title: Erdős problem #563
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $F(n,\alpha)$ denote the smallest $m$ such that there exists a $2$-colouring of the edges of $K_n$ so that every $X\subseteq [n]$ with $\lvert X\rvert\geq m$ contains more than $\alpha \binom{\lvert X\rvert}{2}$ many edges of each colour. Prove that, for every $0\leq \alpha< 1/2$,\[F(n,\alpha)\sim c_\alpha\log n\]for some constant $c_\alpha$ depending only on $\alpha$.
rows.source_text: Let $F(n,\alpha)$ denote the smallest $m$ such that there exists a $2$-colouring of the edges of $K_n$ so that every $X\subseteq [n]$ with $\lvert X\rvert\geq m$ contains more than $\alpha \binom{\lvert X\rvert}{2}$ many edges of each colour. Prove that, for every $0\leq \alpha< 1/2$,\[F(n,\alpha)\sim c_\alpha\log n\]for some constant $c_\alpha$ depending only on $\alpha$.
rows.class_notes: F(n,alpha) ~ c_alpha log n: real parameter alpha and asymptotic equivalence.

rows.title: Erdős problem #565
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $R^*(G)$ be the induced Ramsey number: the minimal $m$ such that there is a graph $H$ on $m$ vertices such that any $2$-colouring of the edges of $H$ contains an induced monochromatic copy of $G$. Is it true that\[R^*(G) \leq 2^{O(n)}\]for any graph $G$ on $n$ vertices?
rows.source_text: Let $R^*(G)$ be the induced Ramsey number: the minimal $m$ such that there is a graph $H$ on $m$ vertices such that any $2$-colouring of the edges of $H$ contains an induced monochromatic copy of $G$. Is it true that\[R^*(G) \leq 2^{O(n)}\]for any graph $G$ on $n$ vertices?
rows.class_notes: 2^{O(n)} unpacks to exists C: R*(G) <= 2^{Cn}; solved.

rows.title: Erdős problem #575
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: If $\mathcal{F}$ is a finite set of finite graphs then $\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})$ is the maximum number of edges a graph on $n$ vertices can have without containing any subgraphs from $\mathcal{F}$. Note that it is trivial that $\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})\leq \mathrm{ex}(n;G)$ for every $G\in\mathcal{F}$. Is it true that, for every $\mathcal{F}$, if there is a bipartite graph in $\mathcal{F}$ then there exists some bipartite $G\in\mathcal{F}$ such that\[\mathrm{ex}(n;G)\ll_{\mathcal{F}}\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})?\]
rows.source_text: If $\mathcal{F}$ is a finite set of finite graphs then $\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})$ is the maximum number of edges a graph on $n$ vertices can have without containing any subgraphs from $\mathcal{F}$. Note that it is trivial that $\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})\leq \mathrm{ex}(n;G)$ for every $G\in\mathcal{F}$. Is it true that, for every $\mathcal{F}$, if there is a bipartite graph in $\mathcal{F}$ then there exists some bipartite $G\in\mathcal{F}$ such that\[\mathrm{ex}(n;G)\ll_{\mathcal{F}}\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})?\]
rows.class_notes: Family-vs-single-graph Turan comparability; precise up to <<_F constant.

rows.title: Erdős problem #584
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices and $\delta n^{2}$ edges. Are there subgraphs $H_1,H_2\subseteq G$ such that $H_1$ has $\gg \delta^3n^2$ edges and every two edges in $H_1$ are contained in a cycle of length at most $6$, and furthermore if two edges share a vertex they are on a cycle of length $4$, and $H_2$ has $\gg \delta^2n^2$ edges and every two edges in $H_2$ are contained in a cycle of length at most $8$.
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices and $\delta n^{2}$ edges. Are there subgraphs $H_1,H_2\subseteq G$ such that $H_1$ has $\gg \delta^3n^2$ edges and every two edges in $H_1$ are contained in a cycle of length at most $6$, and furthermore if two edges share a vertex they are on a cycle of length $4$, and $H_2$ has $\gg \delta^2n^2$ edges and every two edges in $H_2$ are contained in a cycle of length at most $8$.
rows.class_notes: Compound yes/no question (H1 and H2 parts) but each is precise.

rows.title: Erdős problem #585
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: What is the maximum number of edges that a graph on $n$ vertices can have if it does not contain two edge-disjoint cycles with the same vertex set?
rows.source_text: What is the maximum number of edges that a graph on $n$ vertices can have if it does not contain two edge-disjoint cycles with the same vertex set?
rows.defer_reason: 'What is the maximum number of edges' — open-ended determination, no conjectured answer.

rows.title: Erdős problem #59
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: Is it true that the number of graphs on $n$ vertices which do not contain $G$ is\[\leq 2^{(1+o(1))\mathrm{ex}(n;G)}?\]
rows.source_text: Is it true that the number of graphs on $n$ vertices which do not contain $G$ is\[\leq 2^{(1+o(1))\mathrm{ex}(n;G)}?\]
rows.class_notes: Counting G-free graphs vs 2^{ex(n;G)}; solved (false for bipartite G).

rows.title: Erdős problem #593

rows.statement_text: Characterize those finite 3-uniform hypergraphs which appear in every 3-uniform hypergraph of chromatic number $>\aleph_0$.
rows.source_text: Characterize those finite 3-uniform hypergraphs which appear in every 3-uniform hypergraph of chromatic number $>\aleph_0$.
rows.defer_reason: 'Characterize' request — grand characterization, not a single proposition.

rows.title: Erdős problem #594
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Does every graph $G$ with chromatic number $\geq \aleph_1$ contain all sufficiently large odd cycles?
rows.source_text: Does every graph $G$ with chromatic number $\geq \aleph_1$ contain all sufficiently large odd cycles?
rows.class_notes: Solved; uncountable chromatic number forces all large odd cycles.

rows.title: Erdős problem #595

rows.statement_text: Is there an infinite graph $G$ which contains no $K_4$ and is not the union of countably many triangle-free graphs?
rows.source_text: Is there an infinite graph $G$ which contains no $K_4$ and is not the union of countably many triangle-free graphs?
rows.class_notes: Precise yes/no about countable unions of triangle-free graphs.

rows.title: Erdős problem #596
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: For which graphs $G_1,G_2$ is it true that for every $n\geq 1$ there is a graph $H$ without a $G_1$ but if the edges of $H$ are $n$-coloured then there is a monochromatic copy of $G_2$, and yet for every graph $H$ without a $G_1$ there is an $\aleph_0$-colouring of the edges of $H$ without a monochromatic $G_2$.
rows.source_text: For which graphs $G_1,G_2$ is it true that for every $n\geq 1$ there is a graph $H$ without a $G_1$ but if the edges of $H$ are $n$-coloured then there is a monochromatic copy of $G_2$, and yet for every graph $H$ without a $G_1$ there is an $\aleph_0$-colouring of the edges of $H$ without a monochromatic $G_2$.
rows.defer_reason: 'For which graphs G1,G2' — characterization request, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Mixes finite-color Ramsey with aleph_0-colourings.

rows.title: Erdős problem #597
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph on at most $\aleph_1$ vertices which contains no $K_4$ and no $K_{\aleph_0,\aleph_0}$ (the complete bipartite graph with $\aleph_0$ vertices in each class). Is it true that\[\omega_1^2 \to (\omega_1\omega, G)^2?\]What about finite $G$?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph on at most $\aleph_1$ vertices which contains no $K_4$ and no $K_{\aleph_0,\aleph_0}$ (the complete bipartite graph with $\aleph_0$ vertices in each class). Is it true that\[\omega_1^2 \to (\omega_1\omega, G)^2?\]What about finite $G$?
rows.class_notes: Ordinal partition calculus (omega_1^2 arrow relation) — set-theoretic machinery well beyond finite graph vocabulary.

rows.title: Erdős problem #599
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a (possibly infinite) graph and $A,B$ be disjoint independent sets of vertices. Must there exist a family $P$ of disjoint paths between $A$ and $B$ and a set $S$ which contains exactly one vertex from each path in $P$, and such that every path between $A$ and $B$ contains at least one vertex from $S$?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a (possibly infinite) graph and $A,B$ be disjoint independent sets of vertices. Must there exist a family $P$ of disjoint paths between $A$ and $B$ and a set $S$ which contains exactly one vertex from each path in $P$, and such that every path between $A$ and $B$ contains at least one vertex from $S$?
rows.class_notes: Erdos-Menger conjecture, solved by Aharoni-Berger.

rows.title: Erdős problem #60
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Does every graph on $n$ vertices with $>\mathrm{ex}(n;C_4)$ edges contain $\gg n^{1/2}$ many copies of $C_4$?

rows.class_notes: Supersaturation just above ex(n;C_4).

rows.title: Erdős problem #600
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $e(n,r)$ be minimal such that every graph on $n$ vertices with at least $e(n,r)$ edges, each edge contained in at least one triangle, must have an edge contained in at least $r$ triangles. Let $r\geq 2$. Is it true that\[e(n,r+1)-e(n,r)\to \infty\]as $n\to \infty$? Is it true that\[\frac{e(n,r+1)}{e(n,r)}\to 1\]as $n\to \infty$?
rows.source_text: Let $e(n,r)$ be minimal such that every graph on $n$ vertices with at least $e(n,r)$ edges, each edge contained in at least one triangle, must have an edge contained in at least $r$ triangles. Let $r\geq 2$. Is it true that\[e(n,r+1)-e(n,r)\to \infty\]as $n\to \infty$? Is it true that\[\frac{e(n,r+1)}{e(n,r)}\to 1\]as $n\to \infty$?
rows.class_notes: Two closely related limit questions on e(n,r); each precise.

rows.title: Erdős problem #601
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation. - $500
rows.statement_text: For which limit ordinals $\alpha$ is it true that if $G$ is a graph with vertex set $\alpha$ then $G$ must have either an infinite path or independent set on a set of vertices with order type $\alpha$?
rows.source_text: For which limit ordinals $\alpha$ is it true that if $G$ is a graph with vertex set $\alpha$ then $G$ must have either an infinite path or independent set on a set of vertices with order type $\alpha$?
rows.defer_reason: 'For which limit ordinals' — characterization request over ordinals.
rows.class_notes: Ordinal-indexed vertex sets and order types.

rows.title: Erdős problem #608
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices and $>n^2/4$ many edges. Are there at least $\frac{2}{9}n^2$ edges of $G$ which are contained in a $C_5$?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices and $>n^2/4$ many edges. Are there at least $\frac{2}{9}n^2$ edges of $G$ which are contained in a $C_5$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #609

rows.statement_text: Let $f(n)$ be the minimal $m$ such that if the edges of $K_{2^n+1}$ are coloured with $n$ colours then there must be a monochromatic odd cycle of length at most $m$. Estimate $f(n)$.
rows.source_text: Let $f(n)$ be the minimal $m$ such that if the edges of $K_{2^n+1}$ are coloured with $n$ colours then there must be a monochromatic odd cycle of length at most $m$. Estimate $f(n)$.

rows.title: Erdős problem #61
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: For any graph $H$ is there some $c=c(H)>0$ such that every graph $G$ on $n$ vertices that does not contain $H$ as an induced subgraph contains either a complete graph or independent set on $\geq n^c$ vertices?

rows.title: Erdős problem #610
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: For a graph $G$ let $\tau(G)$ denote the minimal number of vertices that include at least one from each maximal clique of $G$ (sometimes called the clique transversal number). Estimate $\tau(G)$. In particular, is it true that if $G$ has $n$ vertices then\[\tau(G) \leq n-\omega(n)\sqrt{n}\]for some $\omega(n)\to \infty$, or even\[\tau(G) \leq n-c\sqrt{n\log n}\]for some absolute constant $c>0$?
rows.source_text: For a graph $G$ let $\tau(G)$ denote the minimal number of vertices that include at least one from each maximal clique of $G$ (sometimes called the clique transversal number). Estimate $\tau(G)$. In particular, is it true that if $G$ has $n$ vertices then\[\tau(G) \leq n-\omega(n)\sqrt{n}\]for some $\omega(n)\to \infty$, or even\[\tau(G) \leq n-c\sqrt{n\log n}\]for some absolute constant $c>0$?
rows.class_notes: Leads with 'Estimate' but the 'in particular' inequalities are concrete propositions.

rows.title: Erdős problem #611
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: For a graph $G$ let $\tau(G)$ denote the minimal number of vertices that include at least one from each maximal clique of $G$ (sometimes called the clique transversal number). Is it true that if all maximal cliques in $G$ have at least $cn$ vertices then $\tau(G)=o_c(n)$? Similarly, estimate for $c>0$ the minimal $k_c(n)$ such that if every maximal clique in $G$ has at least $k_c(n)$ vertices then $\tau(G)<(1-c)n$.
rows.source_text: For a graph $G$ let $\tau(G)$ denote the minimal number of vertices that include at least one from each maximal clique of $G$ (sometimes called the clique transversal number). Is it true that if all maximal cliques in $G$ have at least $cn$ vertices then $\tau(G)=o_c(n)$? Similarly, estimate for $c>0$ the minimal $k_c(n)$ such that if every maximal clique in $G$ has at least $k_c(n)$ vertices then $\tau(G)<(1-c)n$.
rows.class_notes: Primary yes/no (tau=o_c(n)) is precise; trailing 'estimate k_c(n)' part is vague.

rows.title: Erdős problem #612
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a connected graph with $n$ vertices, minimum degree $d$, and diameter $D$. Show if that $G$ contains no $K_{2r}$ and $(r-1)(3r+2)\mid d$ then\[D\leq \frac{2(r-1)(3r+2)}{2r^2-1}\frac{n}{d}+O(1),\]and if $G$ contains no $K_{2r+1}$ and $3r-1 \mid d$ then\[D\leq \frac{3r-1}{r}\frac{n}{d}+O(1).\]
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a connected graph with $n$ vertices, minimum degree $d$, and diameter $D$. Show if that $G$ contains no $K_{2r}$ and $(r-1)(3r+2)\mid d$ then\[D\leq \frac{2(r-1)(3r+2)}{2r^2-1}\frac{n}{d}+O(1),\]and if $G$ contains no $K_{2r+1}$ and $3r-1 \mid d$ then\[D\leq \frac{3r-1}{r}\frac{n}{d}+O(1).\]
rows.class_notes: Explicit diameter bounds with O(1) additive terms under clique-freeness and divisibility conditions.

rows.title: Erdős problem #613
rows.prize: DISPROVED (LEAN) This has been solved in the negative and the proof verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: Let $n\geq 3$ and $G$ be a graph with $\binom{2n+1}{2}-\binom{n}{2}-1$ edges. Must $G$ be the union of a bipartite graph and a graph with maximum degree less than $n$?
rows.source_text: Let $n\geq 3$ and $G$ be a graph with $\binom{2n+1}{2}-\binom{n}{2}-1$ edges. Must $G$ be the union of a bipartite graph and a graph with maximum degree less than $n$?
rows.class_notes: Solved; exact finite edge-count threshold statement.

rows.title: Erdős problem #614
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $f(n,k)$ be minimal such that there is a graph with $n$ vertices and $f(n,k)$ edges where every set of $k+2$ vertices induces a subgraph with maximum degree at least $k$. Determine $f(n,k)$.
rows.source_text: Let $f(n,k)$ be minimal such that there is a graph with $n$ vertices and $f(n,k)$ edges where every set of $k+2$ vertices induces a subgraph with maximum degree at least $k$. Determine $f(n,k)$.
rows.defer_reason: 'Determine f(n,k)' — no conjectured value or bound stated.

rows.title: Erdős problem #615
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: Does there exist some constant $c>0$ such that if $G$ is a graph with $n$ vertices and $\geq (1/8-c)n^2$ edges then $G$ must contain either a $K_4$ or an independent set on at least $n/\log n$ vertices?
rows.source_text: Does there exist some constant $c>0$ such that if $G$ is a graph with $n$ vertices and $\geq (1/8-c)n^2$ edges then $G$ must contain either a $K_4$ or an independent set on at least $n/\log n$ vertices?
rows.class_notes: Solved Ramsey-Turan question; existential constant c plus n/log n independence bound.

rows.title: Erdős problem #616
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $r\geq 3$. For an $r$-uniform hypergraph $G$ let $\tau(G)$ denote the covering number (or transversal number), the minimum size of a set of vertices which includes at least one from each edge in $G$. Determine the best possible $t$ such that, if $G$ is an $r$-uniform hypergraph $G$ where every subgraph $G'$ on at most $3r-3$ vertices has $\tau(G')\leq 1$, we have $\tau(G)\leq t$.
rows.source_text: Let $r\geq 3$. For an $r$-uniform hypergraph $G$ let $\tau(G)$ denote the covering number (or transversal number), the minimum size of a set of vertices which includes at least one from each edge in $G$. Determine the best possible $t$ such that, if $G$ is an $r$-uniform hypergraph $G$ where every subgraph $G'$ on at most $3r-3$ vertices has $\tau(G')\leq 1$, we have $\tau(G)\leq t$.
rows.defer_reason: Asks to determine the best possible constant t, not a single proposition.
rows.class_notes: Each fixed t gives a clean B2 fragment about r-uniform transversal numbers.

rows.title: Erdős problem #617
rows.prize: FALSIFIABLE Open, but could be disproved with a finite counterexample.
rows.statement_text: Let $r\geq 3$. If the edges of $K_{r^2+1}$ are $r$-coloured then there exist $r+1$ vertices with at least one colour missing on the edges of the induced $K_{r+1}$.

rows.title: Erdős problem #618
rows.prize: PROVED (LEAN) This has been solved in the affirmative and the proof verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: For a triangle-free graph $G$ let $h_2(G)$ be the smallest number of edges that need to be added to $G$ so that it has diameter $2$ and is still triangle-free. Is it true that if $G$ has maximum degree $o(n^{1/2})$ then $h(G)=o(n^2)$?
rows.source_text: For a triangle-free graph $G$ let $h_2(G)$ be the smallest number of edges that need to be added to $G$ so that it has diameter $2$ and is still triangle-free. Is it true that if $G$ has maximum degree $o(n^{1/2})$ then $h(G)=o(n^2)$?
rows.class_notes: Statement writes h(G) but clearly means h_2(G); o() wrappers needed. Solved.

rows.title: Erdős problem #619
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: For a triangle-free graph $G$ let $h_r(G)$ be the smallest number of edges that need to be added to $G$ so that it has diameter $r$ (while preserving the property of being triangle-free). Is it true that there exists a constant $c>0$ such that if $G$ is a connected graph on $n$ vertices then $h_4(G)<(1-c)n$?
rows.source_text: For a triangle-free graph $G$ let $h_r(G)$ be the smallest number of edges that need to be added to $G$ so that it has diameter $r$ (while preserving the property of being triangle-free). Is it true that there exists a constant $c>0$ such that if $G$ is a connected graph on $n$ vertices then $h_4(G)<(1-c)n$?
rows.class_notes: Exists c>0 with h_4(G)<(1-c)n for all connected G; quantified-constant wrapper suffices.

rows.title: Erdős problem #621

rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices, $\alpha_1(G)$ be the maximum number of edges that contain at most one edge from every triangle, and $\tau_1(G)$ be the minimum number of edges that contain at least one edge from every triangle. rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices, $\alpha_1(G)$ be the maximum number of edges that contain at most one edge from every triangle, and $\tau_1(G)$ be the minimum number of edges that contain at least one edge from every triangle. rows.title: Erdős problem #622

rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a regular graph with $2n$ vertices and degree $n+1$. Must $G$ have $\gg 2^{2n}$ subsets that are spanned by a cycle?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a regular graph with $2n$ vertices and degree $n+1$. Must $G$ have $\gg 2^{2n}$ subsets that are spanned by a cycle?

rows.title: Erdős problem #625
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation. - $1000
rows.statement_text: The cochromatic number of $G$, denoted by $\zeta(G)$, is the minimum number of colours needed to colour the vertices of $G$ such that each colour class induces either a complete graph or empty graph. Let $\chi(G)$ denote the chromatic number. If $G$ is a random graph with $n$ vertices and each edge included independently with probability $1/2$ then is it true that almost surely\[\chi(G) - \zeta(G) \to \infty\]as $n\to \infty$?
rows.source_text: The cochromatic number of $G$, denoted by $\zeta(G)$, is the minimum number of colours needed to colour the vertices of $G$ such that each colour class induces either a complete graph or empty graph. Let $\chi(G)$ denote the chromatic number. If $G$ is a random graph with $n$ vertices and each edge included independently with probability $1/2$ then is it true that almost surely\[\chi(G) - \zeta(G) \to \infty\]as $n\to \infty$?
rows.class_notes: Almost-sure divergence of chi minus cochromatic number for G(n,1/2); needs probability layer.

rows.title: Erdős problem #626
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $k\geq 4$ and $g_k(n)$ denote the largest $m$ such that there is a graph on $n$ vertices with chromatic number $k$ and girth $>m$ (i.e. contains no cycle of length $\leq m$). Does\[\lim_{n\to \infty}\frac{g_k(n)}{\log n}\]exist? Conversely, if $h^{(m)}(n)$ is the maximal chromatic number of a graph on $n$ vertices with girth $>m$ then does\[\lim_{n\to \infty}\frac{\log h^{(m)}(n)}{\log n}\]exist, and what is its value?
rows.source_text: Let $k\geq 4$ and $g_k(n)$ denote the largest $m$ such that there is a graph on $n$ vertices with chromatic number $k$ and girth $>m$ (i.e. contains no cycle of length $\leq m$). Does\[\lim_{n\to \infty}\frac{g_k(n)}{\log n}\]exist? Conversely, if $h^{(m)}(n)$ is the maximal chromatic number of a graph on $n$ vertices with girth $>m$ then does\[\lim_{n\to \infty}\frac{\log h^{(m)}(n)}{\log n}\]exist, and what is its value?
rows.class_notes: B2-vs-B4 resolved to B2: the two limit-existence questions are each a clean yes/no proposition (exists c with g_k(n)/log n -> c); only the trailing 'what is its value' is B4-flavored and is dropped from the formal target.

rows.title: Erdős problem #627
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $\omega(G)$ denote the clique number of $G$ and $\chi(G)$ the chromatic number. If $f(n)$ is the maximum value of $\chi(G)/\omega(G)$, as $G$ ranges over all graphs on $n$ vertices, then does\[\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{f(n)}{n/(\log_2n)^2}\]exist?
rows.source_text: Let $\omega(G)$ denote the clique number of $G$ and $\chi(G)$ the chromatic number. If $f(n)$ is the maximum value of $\chi(G)/\omega(G)$, as $G$ ranges over all graphs on $n$ vertices, then does\[\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{f(n)}{n/(\log_2n)^2}\]exist?
rows.class_notes: Limit existence for max chi/omega normalized by n/(log n)^2.

rows.title: Erdős problem #628
rows.prize: FALSIFIABLE Open, but could be disproved with a finite counterexample.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $k$ containing no $K_k$. If $a,b\geq 2$ and $a+b=k+1$ then must there exist two disjoint subgraphs of $G$ with chromatic numbers $\geq a$ and $\geq b$ respectively?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $k$ containing no $K_k$. If $a,b\geq 2$ and $a+b=k+1$ then must there exist two disjoint subgraphs of $G$ with chromatic numbers $\geq a$ and $\geq b$ respectively?
rows.class_notes: This is the Erdos-Lovasz Tihany conjecture; clean finite statement.

rows.title: Erdős problem #629
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: The list chromatic number $\chi_L(G)$ is defined to be the minimal $k$ such that for any assignment of a list of $k$ colours to each vertex of $G$ (perhaps different lists for different vertices) a colouring of each vertex by a colour on its list can be chosen such that adjacent vertices receive distinct colours. Determine the minimal number of vertices $n(k)$ of a bipartite graph $G$ such that $\chi_L(G)>k$.
rows.source_text: The list chromatic number $\chi_L(G)$ is defined to be the minimal $k$ such that for any assignment of a list of $k$ colours to each vertex of $G$ (perhaps different lists for different vertices) a colouring of each vertex by a colour on its list can be chosen such that adjacent vertices receive distinct colours. Determine the minimal number of vertices $n(k)$ of a bipartite graph $G$ such that $\chi_L(G)>k$.
rows.defer_reason: Asks to determine the minimal n(k), a value question rather than a proposition.

rows.title: Erdős problem #63
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Does every graph with infinite chromatic number contain a cycle of length $2^n$ for infinitely many $n$?
rows.source_text: Does every graph with infinite chromatic number contain a cycle of length $2^n$ for infinitely many $n$?
rows.class_notes: Infinite chromatic number graphs; solved, infinite-graph vocabulary required.

rows.title: Erdős problem #630
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: The list chromatic number $\chi_L(G)$ is defined to be the minimal $k$ such that for any assignment of a list of $k$ colours to each vertex of $G$ (perhaps different lists for different vertices) a colouring of each vertex by a colour on its list can be chosen such that adjacent vertices receive distinct colours. Does every planar bipartite graph $G$ have $\chi_L(G)\leq 3$?
rows.source_text: The list chromatic number $\chi_L(G)$ is defined to be the minimal $k$ such that for any assignment of a list of $k$ colours to each vertex of $G$ (perhaps different lists for different vertices) a colouring of each vertex by a colour on its list can be chosen such that adjacent vertices receive distinct colours. Does every planar bipartite graph $G$ have $\chi_L(G)\leq 3$?
rows.class_notes: Planar bipartite choosability <=3; solved (Alon-Tarsi).

rows.title: Erdős problem #631
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: The list chromatic number $\chi_L(G)$ is defined to be the minimal $k$ such that for any assignment of a list of $k$ colours to each vertex of $G$ (perhaps different lists for different vertices) a colouring of each vertex by a colour on its list can be chosen such that adjacent vertices receive distinct colours. Does every planar graph $G$ have $\chi_L(G)\leq 5$? Is this best possible?
rows.source_text: The list chromatic number $\chi_L(G)$ is defined to be the minimal $k$ such that for any assignment of a list of $k$ colours to each vertex of $G$ (perhaps different lists for different vertices) a colouring of each vertex by a colour on its list can be chosen such that adjacent vertices receive distinct colours. Does every planar graph $G$ have $\chi_L(G)\leq 5$? Is this best possible?

rows.title: Erdős problem #632
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: A graph is $(a,b)$-choosable if for any assignment of a list of $a$ colours to each of its vertices there is a subset of $b$ colours from each list such that the subsets of adjacent vertices are disjoint. If $G$ is $(a,b)$-choosable then $G$ is $(am,bm)$-choosable for every integer $m\geq 1$.
rows.source_text: A graph is $(a,b)$-choosable if for any assignment of a list of $a$ colours to each of its vertices there is a subset of $b$ colours from each list such that the subsets of adjacent vertices are disjoint. If $G$ is $(a,b)$-choosable then $G$ is $(am,bm)$-choosable for every integer $m\geq 1$.
rows.class_notes: (a,b)-choosable implies (am,bm)-choosable; solved (disproved), finite vocabulary.

rows.title: Erdős problem #636
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Suppose $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices which contains no complete graph or independent set on $\gg \log n$ many vertices. Must $G$ contain $\gg n^{5/2}$ induced subgraphs which pairwise differ in either the number of vertices or the number of edges?
rows.source_text: Suppose $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices which contains no complete graph or independent set on $\gg \log n$ many vertices. Must $G$ contain $\gg n^{5/2}$ induced subgraphs which pairwise differ in either the number of vertices or the number of edges?
rows.class_notes: Ramsey-graph induced-subgraph diversity with >> bounds; solved.

rows.title: Erdős problem #637
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: If $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices which contains no complete graph or independent set on $\gg \log n$ vertices then $G$ contains an induced subgraph on $\gg n$ vertices which contains $\gg n^{1/2}$ distinct degrees.
rows.source_text: If $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices which contains no complete graph or independent set on $\gg \log n$ vertices then $G$ contains an induced subgraph on $\gg n$ vertices which contains $\gg n^{1/2}$ distinct degrees.
rows.class_notes: Ramsey graphs contain linear induced subgraph with >> n^{1/2} distinct degrees; solved.

rows.title: Erdős problem #638
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $S$ be a family of finite graphs such that for every $n$ there is some $G_n\in S$ such that if the edges of $G_n$ are coloured with $n$ colours then there is a monochromatic triangle. Is it true that for every infinite cardinal $\aleph$ there is a graph $G$ of which every finite subgraph is in $S$ and if the edges of $G$ are coloured with $\aleph$ many colours then there is a monochromatic triangle.
rows.source_text: Let $S$ be a family of finite graphs such that for every $n$ there is some $G_n\in S$ such that if the edges of $G_n$ are coloured with $n$ colours then there is a monochromatic triangle. Is it true that for every infinite cardinal $\aleph$ there is a graph $G$ of which every finite subgraph is in $S$ and if the edges of $G$ are coloured with $\aleph$ many colours then there is a monochromatic triangle.

rows.title: Erdős problem #639
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Is it true that if the edges of $K_n$ are 2-coloured then there are at most $n^2/4$ many edges which do not occur in a monochromatic triangle?
rows.source_text: Is it true that if the edges of $K_n$ are 2-coloured then there are at most $n^2/4$ many edges which do not occur in a monochromatic triangle?
rows.class_notes: At most n^2/4 edges outside monochromatic triangles in any 2-colouring of K_n; solved.

rows.title: Erdős problem #64
rows.prize: FALSIFIABLE Open, but could be disproved with a finite counterexample. - $1000
rows.statement_text: Does every finite graph with minimum degree at least 3 contain a cycle of length $2^k$ for some $k\geq 2$?
rows.source_text: Does every finite graph with minimum degree at least 3 contain a cycle of length $2^k$ for some $k\geq 2$?
rows.class_notes: Erdos-Gyarfas conjecture: min degree 3 forces a cycle of length a power of two.

rows.title: Erdős problem #640
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $k\geq 3$. Does there exist some $f(k)$ such that if a graph $G$ has chromatic number $\geq f(k)$ then $G$ must contain some odd cycle whose vertices span a graph of chromatic number $\geq k$?
rows.source_text: Let $k\geq 3$. Does there exist some $f(k)$ such that if a graph $G$ has chromatic number $\geq f(k)$ then $G$ must contain some odd cycle whose vertices span a graph of chromatic number $\geq k$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #641
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: Is there some function $f$ such that for all $k\geq 1$ if a finite graph $G$ has chromatic number $\geq f(k)$ then $G$ has $k$ edge disjoint cycles on the same set of vertices?
rows.source_text: Is there some function $f$ such that for all $k\geq 1$ if a finite graph $G$ has chromatic number $\geq f(k)$ then $G$ has $k$ edge disjoint cycles on the same set of vertices?
rows.class_notes: Existence of a function f(k) forcing k edge-disjoint cycles on the same vertex set; solved.

rows.title: Erdős problem #642
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $f(n)$ be the maximal number of edges in a graph on $n$ vertices such that all cycles have more vertices than chords. Is it true that $f(n)\ll n$?
rows.source_text: Let $f(n)$ be the maximal number of edges in a graph on $n$ vertices such that all cycles have more vertices than chords. Is it true that $f(n)\ll n$?
rows.class_notes: f(n) << n needs an asymptotic (linear-bound) wrapper.

rows.title: Erdős problem #643
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $f(n;t)$ be minimal such that if a $t$-uniform hypergraph on $n$ vertices contains at least $f(n;t)$ edges then there must be four edges $A,B,C,D$ such that\[A\cup B= C\cup D\]and\[A\cap B=C\cap D=\emptyset.\]Estimate $f(n;t)$ - in particular, is it true that for $t\geq 3$\[f(n;t)=(1+o(1))\binom{n}{t-1}?\]
rows.source_text: Let $f(n;t)$ be minimal such that if a $t$-uniform hypergraph on $n$ vertices contains at least $f(n;t)$ edges then there must be four edges $A,B,C,D$ such that\[A\cup B= C\cup D\]and\[A\cap B=C\cap D=\emptyset.\]Estimate $f(n;t)$ - in particular, is it true that for $t\geq 3$\[f(n;t)=(1+o(1))\binom{n}{t-1}?\]
rows.class_notes: Lead-in says 'estimate' but the particular (1+o(1))binom(n,t-1) question is a precise proposition.

rows.title: Erdős problem #65
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices and $kn$ edges, and $a_1<a_2<\cdots $ be the lengths of cycles in $G$. Is it true that\[\sum\frac{1}{a_i}\gg \log k?\]Is the sum $\sum\frac{1}{a_i}$ minimised when $G$ is a complete bipartite graph?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices and $kn$ edges, and $a_1<a_2<\cdots $ be the lengths of cycles in $G$. Is it true that\[\sum\frac{1}{a_i}\gg \log k?\]Is the sum $\sum\frac{1}{a_i}$ minimised when $G$ is a complete bipartite graph?
rows.class_notes: Sum of reciprocals of cycle lengths >> log k; second (minimiser) question is softer.

rows.title: Erdős problem #666
rows.prize: DISPROVED (LEAN) This has been solved in the negative and the proof verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: Let $Q_n$ be the $n$-dimensional hypercube graph (so that $Q_n$ has $2^n$ vertices and $n2^{n-1}$ edges). Is it true that, for every $\epsilon>0$, if $n$ is sufficiently large, every subgraph of $Q_n$ with\[\geq \epsilon  n2^{n-1}\]many edges contains a $C_6$?
rows.source_text: Let $Q_n$ be the $n$-dimensional hypercube graph (so that $Q_n$ has $2^n$ vertices and $n2^{n-1}$ edges). Is it true that, for every $\epsilon>0$, if $n$ is sufficiently large, every subgraph of $Q_n$ with\[\geq \epsilon  n2^{n-1}\]many edges contains a $C_6$?
rows.class_notes: C_6 Turan problem in the hypercube with epsilon/sufficiently-large quantifiers; solved.

rows.title: Erdős problem #667
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $p,q\geq 1$ be fixed integers. We define $H(n)=H(N;p,q)$ to be the largest $m$ such that any graph on $n$ vertices where every set of $p$ vertices spans at least $q$ edges must contain a complete graph on $m$ vertices. Is\[c(p,q)=\liminf \frac{\log H(n)}{\log n}\]a strictly increasing function of $q$ for $1\leq q\leq \binom{p-1}{2}+1$?
rows.source_text: Let $p,q\geq 1$ be fixed integers. We define $H(n)=H(N;p,q)$ to be the largest $m$ such that any graph on $n$ vertices where every set of $p$ vertices spans at least $q$ edges must contain a complete graph on $m$ vertices. Is\[c(p,q)=\liminf \frac{\log H(n)}{\log n}\]a strictly increasing function of $q$ for $1\leq q\leq \binom{p-1}{2}+1$?
rows.class_notes: Strict monotonicity in q of the liminf exponent c(p,q); needs liminf/real layer.

rows.title: Erdős problem #70
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $\mathfrak{c}$ be the ordinal of the real numbers, $\beta$ be any countable ordinal, and $2\leq n<\omega$. Is it true that $\mathfrak{c}\to (\beta, n)_2^3$?
rows.source_text: Let $\mathfrak{c}$ be the ordinal of the real numbers, $\beta$ be any countable ordinal, and $2\leq n<\omega$. Is it true that $\mathfrak{c}\to (\beta, n)_2^3$?
rows.defer_reason: Ordinal partition calculus on the continuum (set theory), not finite/infinite graph theory proper.

rows.title: Erdős problem #704
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $G_n$ be the unit distance graph in $\mathbb{R}^n$, with two vertices joined by an edge if and only if the distance between them is $1$. Estimate the chromatic number $\chi(G_n)$. Does it grow exponentially in $n$? Does\[\lim_{n\to \infty}\chi(G_n)^{1/n}\]exist?
rows.source_text: Let $G_n$ be the unit distance graph in $\mathbb{R}^n$, with two vertices joined by an edge if and only if the distance between them is $1$. Estimate the chromatic number $\chi(G_n)$. Does it grow exponentially in $n$? Does\[\lim_{n\to \infty}\chi(G_n)^{1/n}\]exist?
rows.defer_reason: out-of-scope-v2: Hadwiger-Nelson-type chromatic number of the unit-distance graph of R^n (discrete geometry); additionally 'estimate chi(G_n)' bundles several sub-questions with no single proposition (B's B4 point).
rows.class_notes: Resolution: B5 over B's B4 — out-of-scope object dominates; the lim chi(G_n)^{1/n} existence sub-question is the natural carve-out if scope expands.

rows.title: Erdős problem #705
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a finite unit distance graph in $\mathbb{R}^2$ (i.e. the vertices are a finite collection of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ and there is an edge between two points if and only if the distance between them is $1$). Is there some $k$ such that if $G$ has girth $\geq k$ (i.e. $G$ contains no cycles of length $<k$) then $\chi(G)\leq 3$?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a finite unit distance graph in $\mathbb{R}^2$ (i.e. the vertices are a finite collection of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ and there is an edge between two points if and only if the distance between them is $1$). Is there some $k$ such that if $G$ has girth $\geq k$ (i.e. $G$ contains no cycles of length $<k$) then $\chi(G)\leq 3$?
rows.defer_reason: out-of-scope-v2: finite unit-distance graphs in R^2 need a real-plane point/distance layer.
rows.class_notes: Resolution: sided with A on scope; B correctly notes it is otherwise a clean, solved yes/no (girth >= k forces chi <= 3) that would make a good right-polarity target if a geometry layer ever lands.

rows.title: Erdős problem #706
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $L(r)$ be such that if $G$ is a graph formed by taking a finite set of points $P$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$ and some set $A\subset (0,\infty)$ of size $r$, where the vertex set is $P$ and there is an edge between two points if and only if their distance is a member of $A$, then $\chi(G)\leq L(r)$. Estimate $L(r)$. In particular, is it true that $L(r)\leq r^{O(1)}$?
rows.source_text: Let $L(r)$ be such that if $G$ is a graph formed by taking a finite set of points $P$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$ and some set $A\subset (0,\infty)$ of size $r$, where the vertex set is $P$ and there is an edge between two points if and only if their distance is a member of $A$, then $\chi(G)\leq L(r)$. Estimate $L(r)$. In particular, is it true that $L(r)\leq r^{O(1)}$?
rows.defer_reason: out-of-scope-v2: r-distance-set graphs on finite planar point sets (discrete geometry); headline 'estimate L(r)' is also vague.
rows.class_notes: Resolution: sided with A on scope; B's carved target L(r) <= r^{O(1)} is a concrete sub-question worth revisiting if a real-plane layer ever lands.

rows.title: Erdős problem #71
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Is it true that for every infinite arithmetic progression $P$ which contains even numbers there is some constant $c=c(P)$ such that every graph with average degree at least $c$ contains a cycle whose length is in $P$?
rows.source_text: Is it true that for every infinite arithmetic progression $P$ which contains even numbers there is some constant $c=c(P)$ such that every graph with average degree at least $c$ contains a cycle whose length is in $P$?
rows.class_notes: For every AP containing even numbers there is c(P) forcing a cycle with length in P; solved (Bollobas).

rows.title: Erdős problem #712
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation. - $500
rows.statement_text: Determine, for any $k>r>2$, the value of\[\frac{\mathrm{ex}_r(n,K_k^r)}{\binom{n}{r}},\]where $\mathrm{ex}_r(n,K_k^r)$ is the largest number of $r$-edges which can placed on $n$ vertices so that there exists no set of $k$ vertices which is covered by all $\binom{k}{r}$ possible $r$-edges.
rows.source_text: Determine, for any $k>r>2$, the value of\[\frac{\mathrm{ex}_r(n,K_k^r)}{\binom{n}{r}},\]where $\mathrm{ex}_r(n,K_k^r)$ is the largest number of $r$-edges which can placed on $n$ vertices so that there exists no set of $k$ vertices which is covered by all $\binom{k}{r}$ possible $r$-edges.
rows.defer_reason: Asks to determine the hypergraph Turan density value for all k>r>2 — a value question, not a proposition.
rows.class_notes: This is the general Turan problem for complete r-uniform hypergraphs.

rows.title: Erdős problem #713
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation. - $500
rows.statement_text: Is it true that, for every bipartite graph $G$, there exists some $\alpha\in [1,2)$ and $c>0$ such that\[\mathrm{ex}(n;G)\sim cn^\alpha?\]Must $\alpha$ be rational?
rows.source_text: Is it true that, for every bipartite graph $G$, there exists some $\alpha\in [1,2)$ and $c>0$ such that\[\mathrm{ex}(n;G)\sim cn^\alpha?\]Must $\alpha$ be rational?
rows.class_notes: Rational-exponents conjecture for bipartite Turan numbers; ~ cn^alpha needs limit/real layer.

rows.title: Erdős problem #714
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Is it true that\[\mathrm{ex}(n; K_{r,r}) \gg n^{2-1/r}?\]
rows.source_text: Is it true that\[\mathrm{ex}(n; K_{r,r}) \gg n^{2-1/r}?\]
rows.class_notes: Zarankiewicz-type lower bound ex(n;K_{r,r}) >> n^{2-1/r}; open.

rows.title: Erdős problem #715
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Does every regular graph of degree $4$ contain a regular subgraph of degree $3$? Is there any $r$ such that every regular graph of degree $r$ must contain a regular subgraph of degree $3$?
rows.source_text: Does every regular graph of degree $4$ contain a regular subgraph of degree $3$? Is there any $r$ such that every regular graph of degree $r$ must contain a regular subgraph of degree $3$?
rows.class_notes: Berge-Sauer-type question; two clean finite propositions.

rows.title: Erdős problem #716
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $\mathcal{F}$ be the family of all $3$-uniform hypergraphs with $6$ vertices and $3$ $3$-edges. Is it true that\[\mathrm{ex}_3(n,\mathcal{F})=o(n^2)?\]
rows.source_text: Let $\mathcal{F}$ be the family of all $3$-uniform hypergraphs with $6$ vertices and $3$ $3$-edges. Is it true that\[\mathrm{ex}_3(n,\mathcal{F})=o(n^2)?\]
rows.class_notes: Ruzsa-Szemeredi (6,3) theorem; o(n^2) needs asymptotic wrapper.

rows.title: Erdős problem #717
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices with chromatic number $\chi(G)$ and let $\sigma(G)$ be the maximal $k$ such that $G$ contains a subdivision of $K_k$. Is it true that\[\chi(G) \ll \frac{n^{1/2}}{\log n}\sigma(G)?\]
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices with chromatic number $\chi(G)$ and let $\sigma(G)$ be the maximal $k$ such that $G$ contains a subdivision of $K_k$. Is it true that\[\chi(G) \ll \frac{n^{1/2}}{\log n}\sigma(G)?\]
rows.class_notes: chi vs largest clique subdivision with n^{1/2}/log n factor.

rows.title: Erdős problem #718
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Is there some constant $C>0$ such that any graph on $n$ vertices with $\geq Cr^2n$ edges contains a subdivision of $K_r$?
rows.source_text: Is there some constant $C>0$ such that any graph on $n$ vertices with $\geq Cr^2n$ edges contains a subdivision of $K_r$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #719
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $\mathrm{ex}_r(n;K_{r+1}^r)$ be the maximum number of $r$-edges that can be placed on $n$ vertices without forming a $K_{r+1}^r$ (the $r$-uniform complete graph on $r+1$ vertices). Is every $r$-hypergraph $G$ on $n$ vertices the union of at most $\mathrm{ex}_{r}(n;K_{r+1}^r)$ many copies of $K_r^r$ and $K_{r+1}^r$, no two of which share a $K_r^r$?
rows.source_text: Let $\mathrm{ex}_r(n;K_{r+1}^r)$ be the maximum number of $r$-edges that can be placed on $n$ vertices without forming a $K_{r+1}^r$ (the $r$-uniform complete graph on $r+1$ vertices). Is every $r$-hypergraph $G$ on $n$ vertices the union of at most $\mathrm{ex}_{r}(n;K_{r+1}^r)$ many copies of $K_r^r$ and $K_{r+1}^r$, no two of which share a $K_r^r$?
rows.class_notes: Finite decomposition statement; ex_r fully defined in the record.

rows.title: Erdős problem #72
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative. - $100
rows.statement_text: Is there a set $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ of density $0$ and a constant $c>0$ such that every graph on sufficiently many vertices with average degree $\geq c$ contains a cycle whose length is in $A$?
rows.source_text: Is there a set $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ of density $0$ and a constant $c>0$ such that every graph on sufficiently many vertices with average degree $\geq c$ contains a cycle whose length is in $A$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #720
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative. - $100
rows.statement_text: Let $\hat{R}(G)$ denote the size Ramsey number, the minimal number of edges $m$ such that there is a graph $H$ with $m$ edges such that in any $2$-colouring of the edges of $H$ there is a monochromatic copy of $G$. Is it true that, if $P_n$ is the path of length $n$, then\[\hat{R}(P_n)/n\to \infty\]and\[\hat{R}(P_n)/n^2 \to 0?\]Is it true that, if $C_n$ is the cycle with $n$ edges, then\[\hat{R}(C_n) =o(n^2)?\]
rows.source_text: Let $\hat{R}(G)$ denote the size Ramsey number, the minimal number of edges $m$ such that there is a graph $H$ with $m$ edges such that in any $2$-colouring of the edges of $H$ there is a monochromatic copy of $G$. Is it true that, if $P_n$ is the path of length $n$, then\[\hat{R}(P_n)/n\to \infty\]and\[\hat{R}(P_n)/n^2 \to 0?\]Is it true that, if $C_n$ is the cycle with $n$ edges, then\[\hat{R}(C_n) =o(n^2)?\]
rows.class_notes: Size Ramsey of paths/cycles; limits of ratios.

rows.title: Erdős problem #73
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $k\geq 0$. Let $G$ be a graph such that every subgraph $H$ contains an independent set of size $\geq (n-k)/2$, where $n$ is the number of vertices of $H$. Must $G$ be the union of a bipartite graph and $O_k(1)$ many vertices?
rows.source_text: Let $k\geq 0$. Let $G$ be a graph such that every subgraph $H$ contains an independent set of size $\geq (n-k)/2$, where $n$ is the number of vertices of $H$. Must $G$ be the union of a bipartite graph and $O_k(1)$ many vertices?
rows.class_notes: O_k(1) conclusion needs an existential constant wrapper.

rows.title: Erdős problem #736

rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$. Is there, for every cardinal number $m$, some graph $G_m$ of chromatic number $m$ such that every finite subgraph of $G_m$ is a subgraph of $G$?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$. Is there, for every cardinal number $m$, some graph $G_m$ of chromatic number $m$ such that every finite subgraph of $G_m$ is a subgraph of $G$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #737
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$. Must there exist an edge $e$ such that, for all large $n$, $G$ contains a cycle of length $n$ containing $e$?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$. Must there exist an edge $e$ such that, for all large $n$, $G$ contains a cycle of length $n$ containing $e$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #738
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: If $G$ has infinite chromatic number and is triangle-free (contains no $K_3$) then must $G$ contain every tree as an induced subgraph?
rows.source_text: If $G$ has infinite chromatic number and is triangle-free (contains no $K_3$) then must $G$ contain every tree as an induced subgraph?
