rows.title: Erdős problem #739
rows.prize: NOT PROVABLE Open in general, but there exist models of set theory where the result is false.
rows.statement_text: Let $\mathfrak{m}$ be an infinite cardinal and $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $\mathfrak{m}$. Is it true that, for every infinite cardinal $\mathfrak{n}< \mathfrak{m}$, there exists a subgraph of $G$ with chromatic number $\mathfrak{n}$?
rows.source_text: Let $\mathfrak{m}$ be an infinite cardinal and $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $\mathfrak{m}$. Is it true that, for every infinite cardinal $\mathfrak{n}< \mathfrak{m}$, there exists a subgraph of $G$ with chromatic number $\mathfrak{n}$?
rows.class_notes: Subgraphs of every smaller infinite chromatic cardinal.

rows.title: Erdős problem #74
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation. - $500
rows.statement_text: Let $f(n)\to \infty$ (possibly very slowly). Is there a graph of infinite chromatic number such that every finite subgraph on $n$ vertices can be made bipartite by deleting at most $f(n)$ edges?
rows.source_text: Let $f(n)\to \infty$ (possibly very slowly). Is there a graph of infinite chromatic number such that every finite subgraph on $n$ vertices can be made bipartite by deleting at most $f(n)$ edges?
rows.class_notes: Infinite chromatic graph with nearly bipartite finite subgraphs.

rows.title: Erdős problem #740
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $\mathfrak{m}$ be an infinite cardinal and $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $\mathfrak{m}$. Let $r\geq 1$. Must $G$ contain a subgraph of chromatic number $\mathfrak{m}$ which does not contain any odd cycle of length $\leq r$?
rows.source_text: Let $\mathfrak{m}$ be an infinite cardinal and $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $\mathfrak{m}$. Let $r\geq 1$. Must $G$ contain a subgraph of chromatic number $\mathfrak{m}$ which does not contain any odd cycle of length $\leq r$?
rows.class_notes: Infinite cardinal chromatic number with odd-girth subgraph.

rows.title: Erdős problem #742
rows.prize: DECIDABLE Resolved up to a finite check.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices with diameter $2$, such that deleting any edge increases the diameter of $G$. Is it true that $G$ has at most $n^2/4$ edges?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices with diameter $2$, such that deleting any edge increases the diameter of $G$. Is it true that $G$ has at most $n^2/4$ edges?
rows.class_notes: Murty-Simon conjecture: diameter-2-critical graphs have at most n^2/4 edges.

rows.title: Erdős problem #743
rows.prize: FALSIFIABLE Open, but could be disproved with a finite counterexample.
rows.statement_text: Let $T_2,\ldots,T_n$ be a collection of trees such that $T_k$ has $k$ vertices. Can we always write $K_n$ as the edge disjoint union of the $T_k$?
rows.source_text: Let $T_2,\ldots,T_n$ be a collection of trees such that $T_k$ has $k$ vertices. Can we always write $K_n$ as the edge disjoint union of the $T_k$?
rows.class_notes: Gyarfas-Lehel tree packing conjecture; fully finite.

rows.title: Erdős problem #744
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: Let $k$ be a large fixed constant. Let $f_k(n)$ be the minimal $m$ such that there exists a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices with chromatic number $k$, such that every proper subgraph has chromatic number $<k$, and $G$ can be made bipartite by deleting $m$ edges. Is it true that $f_k(n)\to \infty$ as $n\to \infty$? In particular, is it true that $f_4(n) \gg \log n$?
rows.source_text: Let $k$ be a large fixed constant. Let $f_k(n)$ be the minimal $m$ such that there exists a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices with chromatic number $k$, such that every proper subgraph has chromatic number $<k$, and $G$ can be made bipartite by deleting $m$ edges. Is it true that $f_k(n)\to \infty$ as $n\to \infty$? In particular, is it true that $f_4(n) \gg \log n$?
rows.class_notes: f_k(n) -> infinity and >> log n growth claims.

rows.title: Erdős problem #745
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Describe the size of the second largest component of the random graph on $n$ vertices, where each edge is included independently with probability $1/n$.
rows.source_text: Describe the size of the second largest component of the random graph on $n$ vertices, where each edge is included independently with probability $1/n$.
rows.defer_reason: Asks to 'describe' the second largest component of G(n,1/n) — not a single proposition.

rows.title: Erdős problem #746
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Is it true that, almost surely, a random graph on $n$ vertices with $\geq (\tfrac{1}{2}+\epsilon)n\log n$ edges is Hamiltonian?
rows.source_text: Is it true that, almost surely, a random graph on $n$ vertices with $\geq (\tfrac{1}{2}+\epsilon)n\log n$ edges is Hamiltonian?
rows.class_notes: Hamiltonicity threshold for random graphs; needs probability/limit layer.

rows.title: Erdős problem #75
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Is there a graph of chromatic number $\aleph_1$ with $\aleph_1$ vertices such that for all $\epsilon>0$ if $n$ is sufficiently large and $H$ is a subgraph on $n$ vertices then $H$ contains an independent set of size $>n^{1-\epsilon}$? What about an independent set of size $\gg n$?
rows.source_text: Is there a graph of chromatic number $\aleph_1$ with $\aleph_1$ vertices such that for all $\epsilon>0$ if $n$ is sufficiently large and $H$ is a subgraph on $n$ vertices then $H$ contains an independent set of size $>n^{1-\epsilon}$? What about an independent set of size $\gg n$?
rows.class_notes: Chromatic number aleph_1 with large independent sets in finite subgraphs.

rows.title: Erdős problem #750
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $f(m)$ be some function such that $f(m)\to \infty$ as $m\to \infty$. Does there exist a graph $G$ of infinite chromatic number such that every subgraph on $m$ vertices contains an independent set of size at least $\frac{m}{2}-f(m)$?
rows.source_text: Let $f(m)$ be some function such that $f(m)\to \infty$ as $m\to \infty$. Does there exist a graph $G$ of infinite chromatic number such that every subgraph on $m$ vertices contains an independent set of size at least $\frac{m}{2}-f(m)$?
rows.class_notes: Infinite chromatic number with near-half independent sets; companion to erdos_73/erdos_74.

rows.title: Erdős problem #751
rows.prize: DISPROVED (LEAN) This has been solved in the negative and the proof verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $\chi(G)=4$. If $m_1<m_2<\cdots$ are the lengths of the cycles in $G$ then can $\min(m_{i+1}-m_i)$ be arbitrarily large? Can this happen if the girth of $G$ is large?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $\chi(G)=4$. If $m_1<m_2<\cdots$ are the lengths of the cycles in $G$ then can $\min(m_{i+1}-m_i)$ be arbitrarily large? Can this happen if the girth of $G$ is large?
rows.class_notes: Arbitrarily-large gap phrased as forall-C-exists-G; finite and clean.

rows.title: Erdős problem #752
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph with minimum degree $k$ and girth $>2s$ (i.e. $G$ contains no cycles of length $\leq 2s$). Must there be $\gg k^s$ many distinct cycle lengths in $G$?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph with minimum degree $k$ and girth $>2s$ (i.e. $G$ contains no cycles of length $\leq 2s$). Must there be $\gg k^s$ many distinct cycle lengths in $G$?
rows.class_notes: >> k^s becomes an existential-constant lower bound.

rows.title: Erdős problem #753
rows.prize: DISPROVED (LEAN) This has been solved in the negative and the proof verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: The list chromatic number $\chi_L(G)$ is defined to be the minimal $k$ such that for any assignment of a list of $k$ colours to each vertex of $G$ (perhaps different lists for different vertices) a colouring of each vertex by a colour on its list can be chosen such that adjacent vertices receive distinct colours. Does there exist some constant $c>0$ such that\[\chi_L(G)+\chi_L(G^c)> n^{1/2+c}\]for every graph $G$ on $n$ vertices (where $G^c$ is the complement of $G$)?
rows.source_text: The list chromatic number $\chi_L(G)$ is defined to be the minimal $k$ such that for any assignment of a list of $k$ colours to each vertex of $G$ (perhaps different lists for different vertices) a colouring of each vertex by a colour on its list can be chosen such that adjacent vertices receive distinct colours. Does there exist some constant $c>0$ such that\[\chi_L(G)+\chi_L(G^c)> n^{1/2+c}\]for every graph $G$ on $n$ vertices (where $G^c$ is the complement of $G$)?
rows.class_notes: Nordhaus-Gaddum for list chromatic; constant c can be taken rational.

rows.title: Erdős problem #758
rows.prize: SOLVED This has been resolved in some other way than a proof or disproof.
rows.statement_text: The cochromatic number of $G$, denoted by $\zeta(G)$, is the minimum number of colours needed to colour the vertices of $G$ such that each colour class induces either a complete graph or empty graph. Let $z(n)$ be the maximum value of $\zeta(G)$ over all graphs $G$ with $n$ vertices. Determine $z(n)$ for small values of $z(n)$. In particular is it true that $z(12)=4$?

Let $z(n)$ be the maximum value of $\zeta(G)$ over all graphs $G$ with $n$ vertices. Determine $z(n)$ for small values of $z(n)$. In particular is it true that $z(12)=4$?
rows.class_notes: Target the concrete sub-question z(12)=4; the 'determine z(n)' framing is meta.

rows.title: Erdős problem #759
rows.prize: SOLVED This has been resolved in some other way than a proof or disproof.
rows.statement_text: The cochromatic number of $G$, denoted by $\zeta(G)$, is the minimum number of colours needed to colour the vertices of $G$ such that each colour class induces either a complete graph or empty graph. Let $z(S_n)$ be the maximum value of $\zeta(G)$ over all graphs $G$ which can be embedded on $S_n$, the orientable surface of genus $n$. Determine the growth rate of $z(S_n)$.

rows.title: Erdős problem #76

rows.statement_text: Is it true that in any $2$-colouring of the edges of $K_n$ there must exist at least\[(1+o(1))\frac{n^2}{12}\]many edge-disjoint monochromatic triangles?

rows.title: Erdős problem #761
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: The cochromatic number of $G$, denoted by $\zeta(G)$, is the minimum number of colours needed to colour the vertices of $G$ such that each colour class induces either a complete graph or empty graph. The dichromatic number of $G$, denoted by $\delta(G)$, is the minimum number $k$ of colours required such that, in any orientation of the edges of $G$, there is a $k$-colouring of the vertices of $G$ such that there are no monochromatic oriented cycles. Must a graph with large chromatic number have large dichromatic number? Must a graph with large cochromatic number contain a graph with large dichromatic number?

Must a graph with large chromatic number have large dichromatic number? Must a graph with large cochromatic number contain a graph with large dichromatic number?

Is it true that if $G$ has no $K_5$ and $\zeta(G)\geq 4$ then $\chi(G) \leq \zeta(G)+2$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #766
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $f(n;k,l)=\min \mathrm{ex}(n;G)$, where $G$ ranges over all graphs with $k$ vertices and $l$ edges. Give good estimates for $f(n;k,l)$ in the range $k<l\leq k^2/4$. For fixed $k$ and large $n$ is $f(n;k,l)$ a strictly monotone function of $l$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #767
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $g_k(n)$ be the maximal number of edges possible on a graph with $n$ vertices which does not contain a cycle with $k$ chords incident to a vertex on the cycle. Is it true that\[g_k(n)=(k+1)n-(k+1)^2\]for $n$ sufficiently large?

rows.title: Erdős problem #77
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation. - $250
rows.statement_text: If $R(k)$ is the Ramsey number for $K_k$, the minimal $n$ such that every $2$-colouring of the edges of $K_n$ contains a monochromatic copy of $K_k$, then find the value of\[\lim_{k\to \infty}R(k)^{1/k}.\]

rows.statement_text: Is there a $3$-uniform hypergraph on $n$ vertices which contains at least $n-O(1)$ different sizes of cliques (maximal complete subgraphs)

rows.title: Erdős problem #777
rows.prize: SOLVED This has been resolved in some other way than a proof or disproof.
rows.statement_text: If $\mathcal{F}$ is a family of subsets of $\{1,\ldots,n\}$ then we write $G_{\mathcal{F}}$ for the graph on $\mathcal{F}$ where $A\sim B$ if $A$ and $B$ are comparable - that is, $A\subseteq B$ or vice versa. Is it true that, if $\epsilon>0$ and $n$ is sufficiently large, whenever $m\leq (2-\epsilon)2^{n/2}$ the graph $G_\mathcal{F}$ has $<2^{n}$ many edges? Is it true that if $G_{\mathcal{F}}$ has $\geq cm^2$ edges then $m\ll_c 2^{n/2}$? Is it true that, for any $\epsilon>0$, there exists some $\delta>0$ such that if there are $>m^{2-\delta}$ edges then $m<(2+\epsilon)^{n/2}$?
rows.source_text: If $\mathcal{F}$ is a family of subsets of $\{1,\ldots,n\}$ then we write $G_{\mathcal{F}}$ for the graph on $\mathcal{F}$ where $A\sim B$ if $A$ and $B$ are comparable - that is, $A\subseteq B$ or vice versa. Is it true that, if $\epsilon>0$ and $n$ is sufficiently large, whenever $m\leq (2-\epsilon)2^{n/2}$ the graph $G_\mathcal{F}$ has $<2^{n}$ many edges? Is it true that if $G_{\mathcal{F}}$ has $\geq cm^2$ edges then $m\ll_c 2^{n/2}$? Is it true that, for any $\epsilon>0$, there exists some $\delta>0$ such that if there are $>m^{2-\delta}$ edges then $m<(2+\epsilon)^{n/2}$?
rows.class_notes: Comparability graph of a set family; three precise yes/no sub-questions with epsilon/delta asymptotics; borderline set-system flavor but the graph is the central object.

rows.title: Erdős problem #778
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Alice and Bob play a game on the edges of $K_n$, alternating colouring edges by red (Alice) and blue (Bob). Alice goes first, and wins if at the end the largest red clique is larger than any of the blue cliques. Does Bob have a winning strategy for $n\geq 3$? (Erdős believed the answer is yes.) If we change the game so that Bob colours two edges after each edge that Alice colours, but now require Bob's largest clique to be strictly larger than Alice's, then does Bob have a winning strategy for $n>3$? Finally, consider the game when Alice wins if the maximum degree of the red subgraph is larger than the maximum degree of the blue subgraph. Who wins?
rows.source_text: Alice and Bob play a game on the edges of $K_n$, alternating colouring edges by red (Alice) and blue (Bob). Alice goes first, and wins if at the end the largest red clique is larger than any of the blue cliques. Does Bob have a winning strategy for $n\geq 3$? (Erdős believed the answer is yes.) If we change the game so that Bob colours two edges after each edge that Alice colours, but now require Bob's largest clique to be strictly larger than Alice's, then does Bob have a winning strategy for $n>3$? Finally, consider the game when Alice wins if the maximum degree of the red subgraph is larger than the maximum degree of the blue subgraph. Who wins?
rows.class_notes: Needs a finite combinatorial-game/strategy primitive over edge colourings of K_n; three related precise game questions.

rows.title: Erdős problem #79
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: We say $G$ is Ramsey size linear if $R(G,H)\ll m$ for all graphs $H$ with $m$ edges and no isolated vertices. Are there infinitely many graphs $G$ which are not Ramsey size linear but such that all of its subgraphs are?
rows.source_text: We say $G$ is Ramsey size linear if $R(G,H)\ll m$ for all graphs $H$ with $m$ edges and no isolated vertices. Are there infinitely many graphs $G$ which are not Ramsey size linear but such that all of its subgraphs are?
rows.class_notes: Ramsey-size-linear uses uniform implied constants over all H; infinitude quantifier is fine.

rows.title: Erdős problem #797

Estimate $f(d)$. In particular is it true that $f(d)=o(d^2)$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #80

rows.statement_text: Is it true that any $K_r$-free graph on $n$ vertices with average degree $t$ contains an independent set on\[\gg_r \frac{\log t}{t}n\]many vertices?

rows.class_notes: Constant depends on r; exists-c_r wrapper over Kr-free graphs.

rows.title: Erdős problem #803
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: We call a graph $H$ $D$-balanced (or $D$-almost-regular) if the maximum degree of $H$ is at most $D$ times the minimum degree of $H$. Is it true that for every $m\geq 1$, if $n$ is sufficiently large, any graph on $n$ vertices with $\geq n\log n$ edges contains a $O(1)$-balanced subgraph with $m$ vertices and $\gg m\log m$ edges (where the implied constants are absolute)?
rows.source_text: We call a graph $H$ $D$-balanced (or $D$-almost-regular) if the maximum degree of $H$ is at most $D$ times the minimum degree of $H$. Is it true that for every $m\geq 1$, if $n$ is sufficiently large, any graph on $n$ vertices with $\geq n\log n$ edges contains a $O(1)$-balanced subgraph with $m$ vertices and $\gg m\log m$ edges (where the implied constants are absolute)?
rows.class_notes: O(1)-balanced and absolute implied constants unfold to a nested exists-constants statement.

rows.title: Erdős problem #804
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: Let $f(m,n)$ be maximal such that any graph on $n$ vertices in which every induced subgraph on $m$ vertices has an independent set of size at least $\log n$ must contain an independent set of size at least $f(n)$. Estimate $f(n)$. In particular, is it true that $f((\log n)^2,n) \geq n^{1/2-o(1)}$? Is it true that $f((\log n)^3,n)\gg (\log n)^3$?
rows.source_text: Let $f(m,n)$ be maximal such that any graph on $n$ vertices in which every induced subgraph on $m$ vertices has an independent set of size at least $\log n$ must contain an independent set of size at least $f(n)$. rows.class_notes: Statement text slightly garbled (f(m,n) vs f(n)) but the two particular sub-questions are precise; n^{1/2-o(1)} needs asymptotics.

rows.title: Erdős problem #805
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: For which functions $g(n)$ with $n>g(n)\geq (\log n)^2$ is there a graph on $n$ vertices in which every induced subgraph on $g(n)$ vertices contains a clique of size $\geq \log n$ and an independent set of size $\geq \log n$? In particular, is there such a graph for $g(n)=(\log n)^3$?
rows.source_text: For which functions $g(n)$ with $n>g(n)\geq (\log n)^2$ is there a graph on $n$ vertices in which every induced subgraph on $g(n)$ vertices contains a clique of size $\geq \log n$ and an independent set of size $\geq \log n$? In particular, is there such a graph for $g(n)=(\log n)^3$?
rows.class_notes: 'For which g(n)' part is B4-flavored, but the particular question g(n)=(log n)^3 is a precise proposition.

rows.title: Erdős problem #807
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: The bipartition number $\tau(G)$ of a graph $G$ is the smallest number of pairwise edge disjoint complete bipartite graphs whose union is $G$. The independence number $\alpha(G)$ is the size of the largest independent subset of $G$. Is it true that, if $G$ is a random graph on $n$ vertices with edge probability $1/2$, then\[\tau(G)=n-\alpha(G)\]almost surely?

The independence number $\alpha(G)$ is the size of the largest independent subset of $G$. Is it true that, if $G$ is a random graph on $n$ vertices with edge probability $1/2$, then\[\tau(G)=n-\alpha(G)\]almost surely?
rows.class_notes: Random graph G(n,1/2) almost-surely statement needs probability-limit vocabulary.

rows.title: Erdős problem #808
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: Let $c,\epsilon>0$ and $n$ be sufficiently large. If $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ has $\lvert A\rvert=n$ and $G$ is any graph on $A$ with at least $n^{1+c}$ edges then\[\max(\lvert A+_GA\rvert,\lvert A\cdot_G A\rvert) \geq \lvert A\rvert^{1+c-\epsilon},\]where\[A+_GA = \{ a+b : (a,b)\in G\}\]and similarly for $A\cdot_GA$.

rows.defer_reason: Sum-product estimate over integer sets; the graph is only an index set for which pairs are summed - core content is additive combinatorics/number theory

rows.title: Erdős problem #809
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Define the anti-Ramsey number $\chi_S(n,e,G)$ as the smallest $r$ such that there is a graph with $n$ vertices and $e$ edges with an $r$-colouring of its edges in which every copy of $G$ has entirely distinct edge colours. Is it true that, for all $k\geq 3$,\[\chi_S(n, \lfloor n^2/4\rfloor+1,C_{2k+1})\sim n^2/8?\]
rows.source_text: Define the anti-Ramsey number $\chi_S(n,e,G)$ as the smallest $r$ such that there is a graph with $n$ vertices and $e$ edges with an $r$-colouring of its edges in which every copy of $G$ has entirely distinct edge colours. Is it true that, for all $k\geq 3$,\[\chi_S(n, \lfloor n^2/4\rfloor+1,C_{2k+1})\sim n^2/8?\]
rows.class_notes: Asymptotic equivalence ~ n^2/8 needs limit vocabulary.

rows.title: Erdős problem #81
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a chordal graph on $n$ vertices - that is, $G$ has no induced cycles of length greater than $3$. Can the edges of $G$ be partitioned into $n^2/6+O(n)$ many cliques?

rows.title: Erdős problem #810
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Does there exist some $\epsilon>0$ such that, for all sufficiently large $n$, there exists a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices with at least $\epsilon n^2$ many edges such that the edges can be coloured with $n$ colours so that every $C_4$ receives $4$ distinct colours?

rows.title: Erdős problem #811
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Suppose $n\equiv 1\pmod{m}$. We say that an edge-colouring of $K_n$ using $m$ colours is balanced if every vertex sees exactly $\lfloor n/m\rfloor$ many edges of each colours. For which graphs $G$ is it true that, if $m=e(G)$, for all large $n\equiv 1\pmod{m}$, every balanced edge-colouring of $K_n$ with $m$ colours contains a rainbow copy of $G$? (That is, a subgraph isomorphic to $G$ where each edge receives a different colour.)

rows.class_notes: Individual instances (fixed G) would be clean finite statements.

rows.title: Erdős problem #812
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Is it true that\[\frac{R(n+1)}{R(n)}\geq 1+c\]for some constant $c>0$, for all large $n$? Is it true that\[R(n+1)-R(n) \gg n^2?\]

rows.title: Erdős problem #813
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $h(n)$ be minimal such that every graph on $n$ vertices where every set of $7$ vertices contains a triangle (a copy of $K_3$) must contain a clique on at least $h(n)$ vertices. Estimate $h(n)$ - in particular, do there exist constants $c_1,c_2>0$ such that\[n^{1/3+c_1}\ll h(n) \ll n^{1/2-c_2}?\]

rows.statement_text: Let $F(n)$ be maximal such that every graph on $n$ vertices contains a regular induced subgraph on at least $F(n)$ vertices. Prove that $F(n)/\log n\to \infty$.

rows.title: Erdős problem #832

rows.statement_text: Does there exist an absolute constant $c>0$ such that, for all $r\geq 2$, in any $r$-uniform hypergraph with chromatic number $3$ there is a vertex contained in at least $(1+c)^r$ many edges?

rows.title: Erdős problem #837
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $k\geq 2$ and $A_k\subseteq [0,1]$ be the set of $\alpha$ such that there exists some $\beta(\alpha)>\alpha$ with the property that, if $G_1,G_2,\ldots$ is a sequence of $k$-uniform hypergraphs with\[\liminf \frac{e(G_n)}{\binom{\lvert G_n\rvert}{k}} >\alpha\]then there exist subgraphs $H_n\subseteq G_n$ such that $\lvert H_n\rvert \to \infty$ and\[\liminf \frac{e(H_n)}{\binom{\lvert H_n\rvert}{k}} >\beta,\]and further that this property does not necessarily hold if $>\alpha$ is replaced by $\geq \alpha$. What is $A_3$?

rows.title: Erdős problem #84
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: The cycle set of a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices is a set $A\subseteq \{3,\ldots,n\}$ such that there is a cycle in $G$ of length $\ell$ if and only if $\ell \in A$. Let $f(n)$ count the number of possible such $A$. Prove that $f(n)=o(2^n)$. Prove that $f(n)/2^{n/2}\to \infty$.
rows.source_text: The cycle set of a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices is a set $A\subseteq \{3,\ldots,n\}$ such that there is a cycle in $G$ of length $\ell$ if and only if $\ell \in A$. Let $f(n)$ count the number of possible such $A$. Prove that $f(n)=o(2^n)$. Prove that $f(n)/2^{n/2}\to \infty$.
rows.class_notes: Two asymptotic propositions on the count of achievable cycle-length sets; both o()/divergence statements.

rows.title: Erdős problem #883
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: For $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}$ let $G(A)$ be the graph with vertex set $A$, where two integers are joined by an edge if they are coprime. Is it true that, for every $\ell\geq 1$, if $n$ is sufficiently large and\[\lvert A\rvert >\lfloor\tfrac{n}{2}\rfloor+\lfloor\tfrac{n}{3}\rfloor-\lfloor\tfrac{n}{6}\rfloor\]then $G(A)$ must contain a complete $(1,\ell,\ell)$ triparite graph on $2\ell+1$ vertices?

Is it true that, for every $\ell\geq 1$, if $n$ is sufficiently large and\[\lvert A\rvert >\lfloor\tfrac{n}{2}\rfloor+\lfloor\tfrac{n}{3}\rfloor-\lfloor\tfrac{n}{6}\rfloor\]then $G(A)$ must contain a complete $(1,\ell,\ell)$ triparite graph on $2\ell+1$ vertices?

rows.title: Erdős problem #900

rows.title: Erdős problem #904
rows.prize: PROVED (LEAN) This has been solved in the affirmative and the proof verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: Let $r\geq 2$ and let $t_r(n)$ be the Turán number (the maximal number of edges in a graph on $n$ vertices with no $K_{r+1}$). If $G$ is a graph with $n$ vertices and $m\geq t_r(n)$ edges there exists a clique on $r$ vertices, say $x_1,\ldots,x_r$, such that\[d(x_1)+\cdots+d(x_r)\geq \frac{2rm}{n}.\]
rows.source_text: Let $r\geq 2$ and let $t_r(n)$ be the Turán number (the maximal number of edges in a graph on $n$ vertices with no $K_{r+1}$). If $G$ is a graph with $n$ vertices and $m\geq t_r(n)$ edges there exists a clique on $r$ vertices, say $x_1,\ldots,x_r$, such that\[d(x_1)+\cdots+d(x_r)\geq \frac{2rm}{n}.\]
rows.class_notes: Solved; Turan-threshold graphs contain an r-clique of large degree sum, fully finite.

rows.title: Erdős problem #905
rows.prize: PROVED (LEAN) This has been solved in the affirmative and the proof verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: Every graph with $n$ vertices and $>n^2/4$ edges contains an edge which is in at least $n/6$ triangles.
rows.source_text: Every graph with $n$ vertices and $>n^2/4$ edges contains an edge which is in at least $n/6$ triangles.
rows.class_notes: Solved book-number statement: >n^2/4 edges forces an edge in >= n/6 triangles.

rows.title: Erdős problem #911
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $\hat{R}(G)$ denote the size Ramsey number, the minimal number of edges $m$ such that there is a graph $H$ with $m$ edges that is Ramsey for $G$. Is there a function $f$ such that $f(x)/x\to \infty$ as $x\to \infty$ such that, for all large $C$, if $G$ is a graph with $n$ vertices and $e\geq Cn$ edges then\[\hat{R}(G) > f(C) e?\]
rows.source_text: Let $\hat{R}(G)$ denote the size Ramsey number, the minimal number of edges $m$ such that there is a graph $H$ with $m$ edges that is Ramsey for $G$. Is there a function $f$ such that $f(x)/x\to \infty$ as $x\to \infty$ such that, for all large $C$, if $G$ is a graph with $n$ vertices and $e\geq Cn$ edges then\[\hat{R}(G) > f(C) e?\]
rows.class_notes: Existence of a superlinear function lower-bounding size Ramsey numbers of dense graphs; f(x)/x -> infinity needs limits.

rows.title: Erdős problem #914
rows.prize: PROVED (LEAN) This has been solved in the affirmative and the proof verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: Let $r\geq 2$ and $m\geq 1$. Every graph with $rm$ vertices and minimum degree at least $m(r-1)$ contains $m$ vertex disjoint copies of $K_r$.
rows.source_text: Let $r\geq 2$ and $m\geq 1$. Every graph with $rm$ vertices and minimum degree at least $m(r-1)$ contains $m$ vertex disjoint copies of $K_r$.
rows.class_notes: Solved (Hajnal-Szemeredi form): min degree m(r-1) on rm vertices forces m disjoint K_r copies.

rows.title: Erdős problem #915
rows.prize: SOLVED This has been resolved in some other way than a proof or disproof.
rows.statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph with $1+n(m-1)$ vertices and $1+n\binom{m}{2}$ edges. Must $G$ contain two points which are connected by $m$ disjoint paths?
rows.source_text: Let $G$ be a graph with $1+n(m-1)$ vertices and $1+n\binom{m}{2}$ edges. Must $G$ contain two points which are connected by $m$ disjoint paths?
rows.class_notes: Solved; edge count forcing two vertices joined by m disjoint paths, fully finite.

rows.title: Erdős problem #916
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Does every graph with $n$ vertices and $2n-2$ edges contain a cycle and another vertex adjacent to three vertices on the cycle?
rows.source_text: Does every graph with $n$ vertices and $2n-2$ edges contain a cycle and another vertex adjacent to three vertices on the cycle?
rows.class_notes: Solved; 2n-2 edges force a cycle plus an outside vertex with three neighbors on it.

rows.title: Erdős problem #917
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $k\geq 4$ and $f_k(n)$ be the largest number of edges in a graph on $n$ vertices which has chromatic number $k$ and is critical (i.e. deleting any edge reduces the chromatic number). Is it true that\[f_k(n) \gg_k n^2?\]Is it true that\[f_6(n)\sim n^2/4?\]More generally, is it true that, for $k\geq 6$,\[f_k(n) \sim \frac{1}{2}\left(1-\frac{1}{\lfloor k/3\rfloor}\right)n^2?\]
rows.source_text: Let $k\geq 4$ and $f_k(n)$ be the largest number of edges in a graph on $n$ vertices which has chromatic number $k$ and is critical (i.e. deleting any edge reduces the chromatic number). Is it true that\[f_k(n) \gg_k n^2?\]Is it true that\[f_6(n)\sim n^2/4?\]More generally, is it true that, for $k\geq 6$,\[f_k(n) \sim \frac{1}{2}\left(1-\frac{1}{\lfloor k/3\rfloor}\right)n^2?\]
rows.class_notes: Three quantitative questions on max edges of k-critical graphs; the ~n^2/4 parts need asymptotic vocabulary.

rows.title: Erdős problem #918
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Is there a graph with $\aleph_2$ vertices and chromatic number $\aleph_2$ such that every subgraph on $\aleph_1$ vertices has chromatic number $\leq\aleph_0$? Is there a graph with $\aleph_{\omega+1}$ vertices and chromatic number $\aleph_1$ such that every subgraph on $\aleph_\omega$ vertices has chromatic number $\leq\aleph_0$?
rows.source_text: Is there a graph with $\aleph_2$ vertices and chromatic number $\aleph_2$ such that every subgraph on $\aleph_1$ vertices has chromatic number $\leq\aleph_0$? Is there a graph with $\aleph_{\omega+1}$ vertices and chromatic number $\aleph_1$ such that every subgraph on $\aleph_\omega$ vertices has chromatic number $\leq\aleph_0$?
rows.class_notes: Precise set-theoretic questions about uncountable chromatic number; infinite cardinals throughout.

rows.title: Erdős problem #919
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Is there a graph $G$ with vertex set $\omega_2^2$ and chromatic number $\aleph_2$ such that every subgraph whose vertices have a lesser type has chromatic number $\leq \aleph_0$? What if instead we ask for $G$ to have chromatic number $\aleph_1$?
rows.source_text: Is there a graph $G$ with vertex set $\omega_2^2$ and chromatic number $\aleph_2$ such that every subgraph whose vertices have a lesser type has chromatic number $\leq \aleph_0$? What if instead we ask for $G$ to have chromatic number $\aleph_1$?
rows.class_notes: Ordinal-type version of #918 with vertex set of order type omega_2^2; infinite/set-theoretic.

rows.title: Erdős problem #920
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $f_k(n)$ be the maximum possible chromatic number of a graph with $n$ vertices which contains no $K_k$. Is it true that, for $k\geq 4$,\[f_k(n) \gg \frac{n^{1-\frac{1}{k-1}}}{(\log n)^{c_k}}\]for some constant $c_k>0$?
rows.source_text: Let $f_k(n)$ be the maximum possible chromatic number of a graph with $n$ vertices which contains no $K_k$. Is it true that, for $k\geq 4$,\[f_k(n) \gg \frac{n^{1-\frac{1}{k-1}}}{(\log n)^{c_k}}\]for some constant $c_k>0$?
rows.class_notes: Lower bound n^{1-1/(k-1)}/(log n)^{c_k} for max chromatic number of K_k-free graphs.

rows.title: Erdős problem #921
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $k\geq 4$ and let $f_k(n)$ be the largest $m$ such that there is a graph on $n$ vertices with chromatic number $k$ in which every odd cycle has length $> m$. Is it true that\[f_k(n) \asymp n^{\frac{1}{k-2}}?\]
rows.source_text: Let $k\geq 4$ and let $f_k(n)$ be the largest $m$ such that there is a graph on $n$ vertices with chromatic number $k$ in which every odd cycle has length $> m$. Is it true that\[f_k(n) \asymp n^{\frac{1}{k-2}}?\]
rows.class_notes: Solved; order-of-magnitude f_k(n) asymp n^{1/(k-2)} for chromatic-k graphs with large odd girth.

rows.title: Erdős problem #922
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $k\geq 0$. Let $G$ be a graph such that every subgraph $H$ contains an independent set of size $\geq (n-k)/2$, where $n$ is the number of vertices of $H$. Must $G$ have chromatic number at most $k+2$?
rows.source_text: Let $k\geq 0$. Let $G$ be a graph such that every subgraph $H$ contains an independent set of size $\geq (n-k)/2$, where $n$ is the number of vertices of $H$. Must $G$ have chromatic number at most $k+2$?
rows.class_notes: Solved; hereditary independence-ratio hypothesis implies chromatic number <= k+2, fully finite.

rows.title: Erdős problem #923
rows.prize: PROVED (LEAN) This has been solved in the affirmative and the proof verified in Lean.
rows.statement_text: Is it true that, for every $k$, there is some $f(k)$ such that if $G$ has chromatic number $\geq f(k)$ then $G$ contains a triangle-free subgraph with chromatic number $\geq k$?
rows.source_text: Is it true that, for every $k$, there is some $f(k)$ such that if $G$ has chromatic number $\geq f(k)$ then $G$ contains a triangle-free subgraph with chromatic number $\geq k$?
rows.class_notes: Solved (Rodl); high chromatic number forces triangle-free subgraph of chromatic number >= k.

rows.title: Erdős problem #924
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $k\geq 2$ and $l\geq 3$. Is there a graph $G$ which contains no $K_{l+1}$ such that every $k$-colouring of the edges of $G$ contains a monochromatic copy of $K_l$?
rows.source_text: Let $k\geq 2$ and $l\geq 3$. Is there a graph $G$ which contains no $K_{l+1}$ such that every $k$-colouring of the edges of $G$ contains a monochromatic copy of $K_l$?
rows.class_notes: Solved (Folkman/Nesetril-Rodl): K_{l+1}-free graphs that are k-edge-Ramsey for K_l.

rows.title: Erdős problem #925
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: Is there a constant $\delta>0$ such that, for all large $n$, if $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices which is not Ramsey for $K_3$ (i.e. there exists a 2-colouring of the edges of $G$ with no monochromatic triangle) then $G$ contains an independent set of size $\gg n^{1/3+\delta}$?
rows.source_text: Is there a constant $\delta>0$ such that, for all large $n$, if $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices which is not Ramsey for $K_3$ (i.e. there exists a 2-colouring of the edges of $G$ with no monochromatic triangle) then $G$ contains an independent set of size $\gg n^{1/3+\delta}$?
rows.class_notes: Solved; non-K_3-Ramsey graphs have independence number >> n^{1/3+delta}.

rows.title: Erdős problem #926
rows.prize: PROVED This has been solved in the affirmative.
rows.statement_text: Let $k\geq 4$. Is it true that\[\mathrm{ex}(n;H_k) \ll_k n^{3/2},\]where $H_k$ is the graph on vertices $x,y_1,\ldots,y_k,z_1,\ldots,z_{\binom{k}{2}}$, where $x$ is adjacent to all $y_i$ and each pair of $y_i,y_j$ is adjacent to a unique $z_i$.
rows.source_text: Let $k\geq 4$. Is it true that\[\mathrm{ex}(n;H_k) \ll_k n^{3/2},\]where $H_k$ is the graph on vertices $x,y_1,\ldots,y_k,z_1,\ldots,z_{\binom{k}{2}}$, where $x$ is adjacent to all $y_i$ and each pair of $y_i,y_j$ is adjacent to a unique $z_i$.
rows.class_notes: Solved; ex(n;H_k) << n^{3/2} with H_k fully defined in the statement.

rows.title: Erdős problem #927
rows.prize: DISPROVED This has been solved in the negative.
rows.statement_text: Let $g(n)$ be the maximum number of different sizes of cliques that can occur in a graph on $n$ vertices. Estimate $g(n)$ - in particular, is it true that\[g(n)=n-\log_2n-\log_*(n)+O(1),\]where $\log_*(n)$ is the number of iterated logarithms such that $\log\cdots \log n <1$.
rows.source_text: Let $g(n)$ be the maximum number of different sizes of cliques that can occur in a graph on $n$ vertices. Estimate $g(n)$ - in particular, is it true that\[g(n)=n-\log_2n-\log_*(n)+O(1),\]where $\log_*(n)$ is the number of iterated logarithms such that $\log\cdots \log n <1$.
rows.class_notes: 'Estimate g(n)' but the 'in particular' clause gives a precise n - log_2 n - log_*(n) + O(1) proposition; classified on that.

rows.title: Erdős problem #934
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: Let $h_t(d)$ be minimal such that every graph $G$ with $h_t(d)$ edges and maximal degree $\leq d$ contains two edges whose shortest path between them has length $\geq t$. Estimate $h_t(d)$.
rows.source_text: Let $h_t(d)$ be minimal such that every graph $G$ with $h_t(d)$ edges and maximal degree $\leq d$ contains two edges whose shortest path between them has length $\geq t$. Estimate $h_t(d)$.
rows.defer_reason: asks to 'Estimate h_t(d)' — no single proposition
rows.class_notes: h_t(d) precisely defined (distant pairs of edges under degree bound) but only estimation is requested.

rows.title: Erdős problem #944
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: A critical vertex, edge, or set of edges, is one whose deletion lowers the chromatic number. Let $k\geq 4$ and $r\geq 1$. Must there exist a graph $G$ with chromatic number $k$ such that every vertex is critical, yet every critical set of edges has size $>r$?
rows.source_text: A critical vertex, edge, or set of edges, is one whose deletion lowers the chromatic number. Let $k\geq 4$ and $r\geq 1$. Must there exist a graph $G$ with chromatic number $k$ such that every vertex is critical, yet every critical set of edges has size $>r$?
rows.class_notes: Existence of vertex-critical chromatic-k graphs with no small critical edge set; finite for each k,r.

rows.title: Erdős problem #986
rows.prize: OPEN This is open, and cannot be resolved with a finite computation.
rows.statement_text: For any fixed $k\geq 3$,\[R(k,n) \gg \frac{n^{k-1}}{(\log n)^c}\]for some constant $c=c(k)>0$.
rows.source_text: For any fixed $k\geq 3$,\[R(k,n) \gg \frac{n^{k-1}}{(\log n)^c}\]for some constant $c=c(k)>0$.
rows.class_notes: Off-diagonal Ramsey lower bound R(k,n) >> n^{k-1}/(log n)^c.

rows.title: Erdős problem #993
rows.prize: FALSIFIABLE Open, but could be disproved with a finite counterexample.
rows.statement_text: The independent set sequence of any tree or forest is unimodal. In other words, if $i_k(G)$ counts the number of independent sets of vertices of size $k$ in a graph $G$, and $T$ is any tree or forest, then for some $m\geq 0$ $$i_{0}(T)\leq i_{1}(T)\leq\cdots\leq i_{m}(T)\geq i_{m+1}(T)\geq i_{m+2}(T)\geq\cdots.$$
rows.source_text: The independent set sequence of any tree or forest is unimodal. In other words, if $i_k(G)$ counts the number of independent sets of vertices of size $k$ in a graph $G$, and $T$ is any tree or forest, then for some $m\geq 0$ $$i_{0}(T)\leq i_{1}(T)\leq\cdots\leq i_{m}(T)\geq i_{m+1}(T)\geq i_{m+2}(T)\geq\cdots.$$
rows.class_notes: Both agree B2/clean (Alavi-Malde-Schwenk-Erdos unimodality for trees/forests, fully finite standard vocabulary); repo resolved to packing-theory since independent sets are its core object.
